question.text: Energy Analyst: Given the amount of sunlight in our area of the world, even if the roof of the average house in our area of the world were completely covered with solar panels, the panels would provide only about two-thirds of the electrical power used by the appliances a house typically has.So, in our area of the world, rooftop solar will never be the sole source of electrical power for houses.Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument is based?

answers.text: Electrically powered household appliances will not in general be redesigned to use around half the power that such appliances use today.
A household would not save money by generating two-thirds of the electrical power it uses by using rooftop solar panels.
People in the energy analyst’s area of the world are not concerned about the effects on the environment of using fossil fuels to generate electricity.
In the future, solar panels will generate much more power per unit of area than solar panels available today generate.
A roof covered with solar panels is not as attractive as a roof without solar panels.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Given the amount of sunlight in our area of the world, even if the roof of the average house in our area of the world were completely covered with solar panels, the panels would provide only about two-thirds of the electrical power used by the appliances a house typically has.
Conclusion: So, in our area of the world, rooftop solar will never be the sole source of electrical power for houses.

The answer choices to this question include some that support conclusions related to the conclusion of this argument.So, in order to avoid choosing one of those choices, we have to pay careful attention to the conclusion of the argument.

If we have a vague idea of the conclusion in our minds, such as “something about rooftop solar not being good,” we could choose one of the incorrect answers that seems to be something that one would have to assume to conclude “something about rooftop solar not being good.”
On the other hand, if we are completely clear about what the conclusion is, we’ll likely get this question correct.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Electrically powered household appliances will not in general be redesigned to use around half the power that such appliances use today.

The energy analyst concludes that, in that area of the world, rooftop solar will never be the sole source of electrical power for houses.He bases that conclusion on the fact that, currently, even a roof full of solar panels generates only two-thirds of the power used by the appliances a house typically has.
Of course, in basing that conclusion on that evidence, he must be assuming what this choice says.

After all, if, contrary to what this choice says, electrically powered household appliances WILL in general be redesigned to use half the power that such appliances use today, then it’s quite possible that, in the future, the average house will use only half the power it uses today.

In that case, it’s quite possible that, since a roof full of solar panels can generate two-thirds of the power a house’s appliances use today, and since two-thirds is significantly greater than half, rooftop solar will be able to provide plenty of power for the average house.
Of course, in that case, the energy analyst’s argument falls apart, because, in that case, his evidence no longer supports his conclusion.

In that case, even though, currently, rooftop solar is unable to provide as much power as the average house uses, it would be possible that, in the future, rooftop solar will be the sole source of power for houses in that area of the world.
Thus, the energy analyst must be assuming that, as this choice says, appliances WILL NOT in general be redesigned to use half the power appliances use today.
(B) A household would not save money by generating two-thirds of the electrical power it uses by using rooftop solar panels.

This choice is tempting because it supports a conclusion related to the energy analyst’s conclusion.This choice supports the conclusion that rooftop solar won’t become widespread, or another similar conclusion.
Notice, however, that, in arriving at his conclusion, the energy analyst doesn’t have to assume that a household would not save money by using rooftop solar.

After all, even if every household in that area of the world WOULD save loads of money by using rooftop solar to generate two-thirds of the energy the household uses, the fact that rooftop solar produces only about two-thirds of the power used by the appliances a house typically has would still support the conclusion that rooftop solar will not be the “sole source of electrical power” for households in that area of the world.
So, clearly, this choice does not state an assumption upon which the energy analyst’s argument depends.
(C) People in the energy analyst’s area of the world are not concerned about the effects on the environment of using fossil fuels to generate electricity.

This choice is tempting because it supports a conclusion related to the energy analyst’s conclusion.This choice supports the conclusion that people in the energy analyst’s area of the world won’t be quick to adopt rooftop solar, or another similar conclusion.
Notice, however, that, in arriving at his conclusion, the energy analyst doesn’t have to assume what this choice says.

After all, even if people in the energy analyst’s area of the world WERE VERY concerned about the effects on the environment of using fossil fuels to generate electricity, the fact that rooftop solar produces only about two-thirds of the power used by the appliances a house typically has would still support the conclusion that rooftop solar will not be the “sole source of electrical power” for households in that area of the world.
So, clearly, this choice does not state an assumption upon which the energy analyst’s argument depends.
(D) In the future, solar panels will generate much more power per unit of area than solar panels available today generate.

This choice is extremely tempting.It sounds so relevant to the argument.After all, the argument is based on how much power rooftop solar produces, and this choice is about a change in how much power rooftop solar can produce.
There’s just one problem.This choice goes in completely the wrong direction.This choice weakens the energy analyst’s argument.

After all, if what this choice says is true, then even though, currently, in the energy analyst’s area of the world, rooftop solar generates less power than a household’s appliances use, in the future, rooftop solar will generate much more power and, thus, might be able to generate all of the power that a household’s appliances use.
Of course, in that case, it’s possible that, in that area of the world, rooftop solar will someday be the sole source of electrical power for houses.
So, this choice is a weakener that destroys the energy analyst’s argument.
(E) A roof covered with solar panels is not as attractive as a roof without solar panels.

This choice could be tempting if we are not clear about the conclusion of the argument.If we have a vague idea that the conclusion is “something about rooftop solar not being good,” then we might perceive this choice to be an assumption made by the energy analyst.
Notice however that, in arriving at his conclusion, the energy analyst doesn’t have to assume what this choice says.

After all, even if, contrary to what this choice says, a roof covered with solar panels were the most attractive roof ever, the fact that rooftop solar produces only about two-thirds of the power used by the appliances a house typically has would still support the conclusion that rooftop solar will not be the “sole source of electrical power” for households in that area of the world.
So, clearly, this choice does not state an assumption upon which the energy analyst’s argument depends.

question.text: Retail Chain CEO: For years, we have been artificially inflating the prices of the merchandise that we sell, and then enticing customers to buy by offering merchandise at prices that we present as discounted from the inflated, “regular” prices.People generally wait for the times when prices are discounted to make purchases.Our new strategy will dispense with using such discounts.Instead, we will make our regular, everyday prices on all of our merchandise similar to what were previously our discounted prices.Because we will be more consistently providing reasonable prices to potential customers, we will achieve increased sales.Upon which of the following does the success of the new strategy depend?

answers.text: The retail chain’s customers would not shift to shopping at competing stores were the chain to continue using its current strategy.
The retail chain’s artificially inflated prices have deterred people from visiting the chain’s stores.
The strategy of generating sales by offering merchandise at discounts from artificially inflated prices is not successfully used by many retail stores.
By consistently pricing merchandise at reasonable levels rather than regularly offering discounts from higher prices, the chain will reduce administrative expenses.
The chain’s customers are not more inspired to make purchases by seeing discounts from inflated prices than they are by consistently seeing reasonable prices.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: We have been artificially inflating the prices of our merchandise.
Premise: We entice customers to buy by offering prices that we present as discounted from the inflated, “regular” prices.
Premise: People generally wait for the times when prices are discounted to make purchases.
Premise: Our new strategy will be to dispense with using discounts and instead make our regular prices similar to what were previously our discounted prices.
Conclusion: We will achieve increased sales.

The passage presents a scenario in which a retail chain has been artificially inflating prices on merchandise and then driving sales by regularly offering discounts from the artificially inflated prices.Possibly because customers tend to defer making purchases until the discounts are offered, the CEO announces a new strategy of offering merchandise at reasonable prices all the time.

While the question does not use the word “assumption,” in asking us to determine which choice the strategy’s success depends upon, the question essentially asks for an assumption upon which the CEO’s conclusion depends.
In other words, we are seeking an answer that expresses something that must be true in order for “more consistently providing reasonable prices to potential customers” to result in increased sales.
(A) The retail chain’s customers would not shift to shopping at competing stores were the chain to continue using its current strategy.

This choice supports the wrong conclusion, the conclusion that the chain would continue to see similar levels of sales if it continued to use its current strategy.We are seeking an assumption upon which a different conclusion depends, the conclusion that the chain will achieve increased sales by using the new strategy.
(B) The retail chain’s artificially inflated prices have deterred people from visiting the chain’s stores.

This answer could be a tempting trap because the fact that the old strategy of inflating prices has deterred customers from visiting the stores provides some support for the conclusion that the new strategy will result in increased sales.

However, although this choice providessome supportfor the conclusion, it is notan assumptionupon which the conclusion depends.Even if using the old strategy has not deter customers from visiting the stores, switching to the new strategy could still result in an increase in sales.So, what this choice says does not have to be true for the new strategy to work.
(C) The strategy of generating sales by offering merchandise at discounts from artificially inflated prices is not successfully used by many retail stores.

The CEO’s rationale for switching to the new strategy is not that the old strategy did not work but that the new strategy will work better.In other words, the conclusion that switching to the new strategy will result in increased sales does not require the that it be the case that the old strategy does not work.
(D) By consistently pricing merchandise at reasonable levels rather than regularly offering discounts from higher prices, the chain will reduce administrative expenses.

This choice is about the new strategy’s effect on administrative expenses, while the conclusion about the new strategy’s effect on sales.Since increasing sales is different from and does not require reducing expenses, this choice is out of contention.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The chain’s customers are not more inspired to make purchases by seeing discounts from inflated prices than they are by consistently seeing reasonable prices.
In order for switching to the new strategy to result in increased sales, switching to the new strategy has to result in customers’ making more purchases.

The only significant difference between the two strategies is the pricing methodology.So, the only factor that will determine the success of the new strategy is customer response to the new type of pricing.

Therefore, the conclusion that switching to the new strategy will result in increased sales depends upon the assumption that discounts from inflated prices, the key aspect of the old strategy, do not inspire purchasing more than do consistently reasonable prices, the key aspect of the new strategy.

question.text: For some time, the Green Corporation has been considering purchasing some solar-powered electric scooters for its campus, but it has not done so because the prices of such scooters have typically been about twice as high as it is willing to pay.However, the City Bike Company is going to run a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half the typical selling price of such scooters.Clearly, the Green Corporation will purchase some solar-powered electric scooters from the City Bike Company.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the support for the above conclusion depends?

answers.text: The average price of solar-powered electric scooters is expected to fall significantly over the coming months.
Someone at the Green Corporation will be aware that the City Bike Company is running a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half the typical price.
Most employees of the Green Corporation know how to operate a solar-powered electric scooter.
Other companies that sell solar-powered electric scooters are also going to offer their scooters at half price.
The Green Corporation does not yet own any solar-powered electric scooters.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: The Green Corporation has been considering purchasing some solar-powered electric scooters for its campus.
Premise: It has not yet done so because the prices of such scooters have typically been about twice as high as it is willing to pay.
Premise: The City Bike Company is going to run a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half the typical selling price of such scooters.
Conclusion: The Green Corporation will purchase some solar-powered electric scooters from the City Bike Company.
Because this is an Assumption question, we’re seeking an answer choice that presents an unwritten premise that must be true in order for the argument to work.

Do the premises provided by the author necessarily lead us to the conclusion that the Green Corporation will purchase some solar-powered electric scooters from the City Bike Company? What must the author assume in making her argument?
(A) The average price of solar-powered electric scooters is expected to fall significantly over the coming months.
The author certainly does not need to assume that the average price of solar-powered electric scooters is expected to fall significantly over the coming months.

Even if the average price of such scooters is not expected to fall significantly, the author has presented as fact that the City Bike Company is going to run a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half the typical selling price of such scooters.

That fact, in conjunction with the fact that the Green Corporation has been considering purchasing some solar-powered electric scooters, but has not done so because the prices of such scooters have typically been about twice as high as the corporation is willing to pay, supports the author’s conclusion even if the average price of such scooters is not expected to fall.
So, this choice does not state an assumption upon which the argument depends.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Someone at the Green Corporation will be aware that the City Bike Company is running a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half the typical price.

The argument’s conclusion is that the Green Corporation will purchase some solar-powered electric scooters from the City Bike Company.Of course, for this to occur, at least one person at the Green Corporation will need to be aware that the City Bike Company is running a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half the typical price for such scooters.

After all, what would happen to the argument if NO ONE at the Green Corporation will be aware that the City Bike Company is running a promotion involving selling scooters at about half the typical price? In that case, the argument would fall apart because, even though it is true that the City Bike Company is going to run a promotion involving selling solar-powered electric scooters at about half price, if no one at the corporation will be aware of the promotion, it would not make sense to conclude that the Green Corporation will purchase some scooters from the City Bike Company.
Therefore, what this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(C) Most employees of the Green Corporation know how to operate a solar-powered electric scooter.
What this choice says may slightly strengthen the argument, but it is not an assumption necessary in order to make the argument.

If most employees of the Green Corporation know how to operate a solar-powered electric scooter, then we have a little bit more faith that the Green Corporation will purchase some solar-powered electric scooters from the City Bike Company.

However, we are not seeking a choice that strengthens the argument; we’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument depends, and the evidence provided would still support the conclusion that the Green Corporation will buy some scooters from the City Bike Company even if it were not the case that most employees of the Green Corporation know how to operate a solar-powered electric scooter.
(D) Other companies that sell solar-powered electric scooters are also going to offer their scooters at half price.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument.

After all, if other companies that sell solar-powered electric scooters are also going to offer their scooters at half price, then the Green Corporation could very well decide to purchase scooters from one of these other companies, rather than from the City Bike Company.
Of course, we are not seeking a choice that weakens the argument; we’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(E) The Green Corporation does not yet own any solar-powered electric scooters.
Notice that what this answer choice says is a conclusion that is supported by the passage.

After all, information presented by the passage, that the Green Corporation has not purchased some solar-powered electric scooters, supports the conclusion that the Green Corporation does not yet own any solar-powered electric scooters.
However, what this choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument relies.

Regardless of whether it is the case that the Green Corporation does not yet own any solar-powered electric scooters, the passage states that the Green Corporation has been considering purchasing some solar-powered electric scooters but has not done so because the prices of such scooters have typically been about twice as high as the corporation is willing to pay.That fact, in conjunction with the other facts provided, supports the author’s conclusion.

In order to correctly answer an Assumption question, we have to be sure to find a choice that states a necessary assumption, not a choice that does something else interesting, such as state a conclusion supported by the passage.

question.text: Member of Congress: The amount of money donated each year to the campaigns of candidates for elected office has been increasing for decades.Furthermore, in the vast majority of elections held in recent years, the candidate who has received the most donated money has won.Only after we change campaign finance laws so that they better combat the money-driven nature of today’s electoral process will elections generally result in the most qualified candidates’ winning.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the member of Congress relies in supporting the above conclusion?

answers.text: Voters would be better off if the government were the only entity allowed to provide financing to the campaigns of candidates for elected office.
In an election, it is not usually the case that the candidate most qualified for the office sought receives the most in financial donations.
In the vast majority of past elections, it was not the case that the candidate who received the most donated money won.
Current campaign finance laws were designed with the intention of giving advantages to candidates who most effectively solicit campaign donations.
There is currently no practical way for the average voter to determine which of the candidates in an election is most qualified for the office being sought.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: The amount of money donated each year to electoral campaigns has been increasing for decades.
Premise: In the vast majority of elections in recent years, the candidate who has received the most donated money has won.

Conclusion: Only after we change campaign finance laws so that they better combat the money-driven nature of today’s electoral process will elections generally result in the most qualified candidates’ winning.

There is a gap in the argument.The premises are focused on the effect of money donated to campaigns but do not say anything about candidate qualifications.Meanwhile, the conclusion is about changing the system so that the most qualified candidates win.

So, in order for the argument to work, the points about campaign donations have to be somehow connected to the conclusion about candidate qualifications.The correct answer will contain an assumption that makes that connection.
(A) Voters would be better off if the government were the only entity allowed to provide financing to the campaigns of candidates for elected office.

While this choice could be tempting because what it expresses may seem to be supported by the argument, we are not looking for something supported by the argument.Rather, we are looking for an assumption that is required for making the argument.

We don’t have to assume that the government should be the sole source of campaign financing in order to conclude that a change in campaign finance laws is necessary in order to make elections more merit-based.In other words, even if voters would NOT be better off if the government were the only entity allowed to provide financing to campaigns, the argument could still work.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In an election, it is not usually the case that the candidate most qualified for the office sought receives the most in financial donations.

In order for the argument to work, what this choice says has to be true.If it were not true, then the candidates who receive the most donated money, and win elections, would often be those who were most qualified for office.Thus, changing the campaign finance laws would not be necessary, and the conclusion would fall apart.
(C) In the vast majority of past elections, it was not the case that the candidate who received the most donated money won.

While the member of congress does mention that the amount of money donated to campaigns has been increasing over time, the argument is not based on trends in campaign financing.Rather, the argument is focused on the current situation in campaign financing.So, assuming that things were different in the past is not necessary for arriving at the conclusion.

We are given the premise that “in the vast majority of elections heldin recent years, the candidate who has received the most donated moneyhaswon,” and that premise supports the argument regardless of whether that pattern is different from how things went in the past or the same.
(D) Current campaign finance laws were designed with the intention of giving advantages to candidates who most effectively solicit campaign donations.

This choice could be tempting because it does seem that, under current campaign finance laws, the candidates who are best at raising money have advantages in elections.However, concluding that the structure must be changed in order for the most qualified candidates to win does not require assuming that the structure waspurposelyset up so as to give advantages to adept fundraisers.

In other words, intention and effect are two separate issues here.The finance laws could have been designed with the intention of creating an even playing field, and the congressperson’s argument would still hold.
(E) There is currently no practical way for the average voter to determine which of the candidates in an election is most qualified for the office being sought.

This choice is a trap.Since the conclusion of the argument is that the most qualified candidates won’t win unless the finance laws are changed, one might think that arriving at the conclusion requires the assumption that voters have no practical way to determine which candidates are most qualified.

Notice, however, that the point of the argument is not that there is currentlyno wayfor the most qualified candidates to be elected.The point is simply that the current system is not conducive to merit-based results.Even if voters could determine which candidates were most qualified, the system could still favor candidates who receive the most donations, perhaps because those candidates have more money to spend on advertising.

question.text: The board of the Infinity Corporation is seeking to promote a company director to the position of Vice President of Marketing.Augustina is the only director at the Infinity Corporation who has an MBA degree.Clearly, then, she will be the one promoted to the position of Vice President of Marketing.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the support for the above conclusion depends?

answers.text: Many of the other directors at the Infinity Corporation have only bachelor’s degrees.
The board of directors will consider people’s educational qualifications when choosing whom to promote to the Vice President of Marketing position.
None of the other vice presidents at the Infinity Corporation have MBA degrees.
Augustina has not earned any master’s degrees except for the MBA degree.
Most of the other directors working at the Infinity Corporation have significantly more experience in marketing than Augustina has.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background:The board of the Infinity Corporation is seeking to promote a company director to the position of Vice President of Marketing.
Premise: Augustina is the only director at the Infinity Corporation who has an MBA degree.
Conclusion: Clearly, then, she will be the one promoted to the position of Vice President of Marketing.
Because this is an Assumption question, we’re seeking an answer choice that presents an unwritten premise that must be true in order for the argument to work.
Notice that there is a large gap in the argument.

The author provides us with only one premise, that Augustina is the only director at the Infinity Corporation who has an MBA degree.From that one premise, she concludes that Augustina will be the one promoted to the position of Vice President of Marketing.
What else must be true in order for the author’s argument to be valid?
(A) Many of the other directors at the Infinity Corporation have only bachelor’s degrees.
The author does not need to assume that many of the other directors at the Infinity Corporation have only bachelor’s degrees.
What if the other directors did not have only bachelor’s degrees? What if they had an array of different degrees? Would the argument be affected?

Even if many of the other directors have degrees other than bachelor’s degrees, it is still the case that Augustina is the only director who has an MBA, as the passage states.That evidence could still support the conclusion that Augustina will be the one promoted to the position of Vice President of Marketing.
So, what this choice says does not have to be true for the argument to be sound.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The board of directors will consider people’s educational qualifications when choosing whom to promote to the Vice President of Marketing position.

We know that the board of the Infinity Corporation is seeking to promote a company director to the position of Vice President of Marketing.We also know that Augustina is the only director at the Infinity Corporation who has an MBA degree.
On the basis of those facts, the author concludes that Augustina will be the one promoted to the position of Vice President of Marketing.
Of course, this argument works only if the board of directors WILL consider people’s educational qualifications when choosing whom to promote to the Vice President of Marketing position.

After all, if it were true that the board of directors WILL NOT consider people’s educational qualifications when choosing whom to promote to the Vice President of Marketing position, then this argument would fall apart because the fact that Augustina has an MBA would not even be considered by the board of directors.
So, what this choice says must be true in order for the evidence, that Augustina has an MBA, to support the conclusion that she will be the one promoted.
(C) None of the other vice presidents at the Infinity Corporation have MBA degrees.
Notice that the board of the Infinity Corporation is seeking to promote a company director to the position of Vice President of Marketing.The author bases her conclusion on this fact.

Therefore, the author certainly would not need to assume that none of the other vice presidents at the Infinity Corporation have MBA degrees.After all, it is not a vice president who will be promoted; it is a company director.
If you choose this answer choice as correct, you may have missed the key detail that a company director, not a vice president, will be promoted.
(D) Augustina has not earned any master’s degrees except for the MBA degree.

The argument is based on the fact that Augustina is the only director at the Infinity Corporation who has an MBA degree.To make this argument, the author certainly does not need to assume that Augustina does not have other degrees.

Even if Augustina has earned many other master’s degrees, it could still make sense to conclude that she will be the one promoted.In fact, what this choice says may even provide further support for the conclusion that she will be the one promoted.
Of course, we are not looking for an answer choice that provides further support for the conclusion; we are looking for an assumption upon which the support for the conclusion depends.
(E) Most of the other directors working at the Infinity Corporation have significantly more experience in marketing than Augustina has.
Notice that what this answer choice says weakens the case for the author’s conclusion.

After all, if most of the other directors working at the Infinity Corporation have significantly more experience in marketing than Augustina has, then we have less faith that Augustina will be the one promoted.If most of the other directors have significantly more experience in marketing than Augustina has, then one of those other directors could very well be the person who is promoted, regardless of whether Augustina is the only director who has an MBA degree.
Of course, we’re not seeking an answer choice that weakens the argument.We’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument depends.

question.text: Teaching schoolchildren is considered by many a particularly stressful occupation.However, studies have found that people engaging in a variety of non-teaching occupations have higher incidences of stress-related illnesses than teachers have.Therefore, it is clear that teaching is not one of the most stressful occupations.Upon which of the following does the argument depend in arriving at its conclusion?

answers.text: Schoolteachers are better at handling job stress in ways that safeguard their health than are people in other occupations.
People in non-teaching occupations who experience high levels of work-related stress are not easily able to leave their occupations to work in less stressful occupations.
Teachers are less likely than are people in other occupations to seek the services of physicians when they experience stress-related health issues.
The factors underlying the manifestation of stress-related illnesses in people engaging in non-teaching occupations are work related.
Stress-related illnesses are the most significant health problems people experience.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Teaching is considered a stressful occupation.
Premise: People engaging in a variety of non-teaching occupations have higher incidences of stress-related illnesses than teachers have.
Conclusion: It is clear that teaching is not one of the most stressful occupations.

There is clear gap in this argument.The supporting evidence is based on the incidences of stress-related illnesses, while the conclusion is about the relative stress levels of occupations.Going from the premise to the conclusion requires an assumption that somehow connects incidences of stress-related illnesses directly to stress associated with the occupations.
(A) Schoolteachers are better at handling job stress in ways that safeguard their health than are people in other occupations.

This choice could be tempting because what it says could explain how the incidence of stress-related illness in teachers could be lower than that in other workers even if teaching were one of the most stressful occupations.

However, we are not looking for an explanation.The question asks us to find an assumption upon which the argument depends.While this answer choice provides an explanation for the observed events, an explanation for the observed events is not the same as an assumption upon which the argument rests.
(B) People in non-teaching occupations who experience high levels of work-related stress are not easily able to leave their occupations to work in less stressful occupations.
To avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to remember what the conclusion of the argument is, that teaching is not one of the most stressful occupations.

While one could argue that not being able to leave stressful, non-teaching jobs could add to the stress of those jobs, that fact would not bring us to the conclusion that teaching is not one of the most stressful occupations.
Arriving at that conclusion does not require assuming anything about how easy it is for people in stressful occupations to change occupations.
(C) Teachers are less likely than are people in other occupations to seek the services of physicians when they experience stress-related health issues.

This answer choice could be tempting because it could be interpreted as explaining the discrepancy between the perception that teaching is stressful and the lower incidence of stress-related illnesses in teachers.Perhaps teachers do get sick, but nobody knows when teachers are sick because they don’t go to doctors!

Even if this information did explain the discrepancy, it would not be the correct answer, because we are not looking for an explanation.We are looking for an assumption upon which the argument depends.A possible explanation of the observation is not the same as an assumption upon which the argument rests.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The factors underlying the manifestation of stress-related illnesses in people engaging in non-teaching occupations are work related.

In order to use the incidence of stress-related illness as an indicator of levels of job stress, one has to assume that the factors causing the illnesses are actually work related.If the factors causing people to experience stress-related illnesses were not work related, then experiencing a stress-related illness would indicate nothing about the stress levels associated with those people’s occupations.
If the foregoing is hard to see, we can negate the statement:
The factors underlying the manifestation of stress-related illnesses in people engaging in non-teaching occupations are NOT work related.
Notice that this negated statement causes the argument to fall apart.
(E) Stress-related illnesses are the most significant health problems people experience.

While the focus of the argument is stress-related illness, the logic of the argument does not depend on the assumption that stress-related illnesses are significant health problems.What this answer choice says does not lead us to the argument’s conclusion.

question.text: Ecologist: Bees all over the world are being poisoned by neonicotinoid pesticides used on plants from which bees feed.Compounding this threat to bee survival is the destruction of bee habitat.Bees need flowering plants to feed from; as a result of habitat destruction, in many areas, the number of such plants is much lower than the number optimal for bee survival.So, by simply buying some flowering plants at your local garden center and planting them in your garden, you can help bees.In making the above recommendation, the ecologist assumes which of the following?

answers.text: At least some people are willing to plant flowering plants in their gardens in order to help bees.
The threats to bees’ survival posed by neonicotinoid pesticide poisoning and habitat destruction will continue to grow for the foreseeable future.
Plants for sale at local garden centers have not been treated with neonicotinoid pesticides.
People cannot do more for bees by purchasing only food grown without the use of neonicotinoid pesticides than they can by planting flowering plants in their gardens.
Bee habitat destruction is a significant threat to bee survival only because of the use of neonicotinoid pesticides.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Bees all over the world are being poisoned by neonicotinoid pesticides used on plants from which bees feed.
Premise: Bees need flowering plants to feed from; as a result of habitat destruction, in many areas, the number of such plants is much lower than the number optimal for bee survival.
Conclusion: So, by simply buying some flowering plants at your local garden center and planting them in your garden, you can help bees.

The ecologist makes some pretty sweeping claims, even going as far as saying that the pesticide poisoning is affecting “bees all over the world.” However, she then concludes that “by simply buying some flowering plants at your local garden center and planting them in your garden, you can help bees.” Although we’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument is based, it’s always helpful to be skeptical of the claims made by authors.In being skeptical, we’ll be better able to find the assumptions made.
Also, remember that whenever an author proposes a plan, she assumes that her plan will work as expected, and there will not be unexpected variables that spoil the plan.
(A) At least some people are willing to plant flowering plants in their gardens in order to help bees.

To not be trapped by what this answer choice says, we need to be clear on what the argument’s conclusion is: “by simply buying some flowering plants at your local garden center and planting them in your garden, you can help bees.” Notice that the conclusion says, “you CAN help bees.” The conclusion does not say, “you WILL help bees.” Therefore, assuming that at least some people are willing to plant flowering plants in their gardens in order to help bees is not an necessary to this argument, which is about how bees CAN be helped, not about how bees WILL be helped.
(B) The threats to bees’ survival posed by neonicotinoid pesticide poisoning and habitat destruction will continue to grow for the foreseeable future.

The premises in the passage are considered factual, and the ecologist’s argument is based upon the fact that “bees all over the worldare being poisonedby neonicotinoid pesticides used on plants from which bees feed.” In other words, bees currently are in bad shape because of these pesticides.In order for her argument to work, the ecologist does not need to assume that this threat will continue to grow; she is concerned with how to help bees now.
If you have a hard time seeing this, we can negate what this answer choice says:
The threats to bees’ survival posed by neonicotinoid pesticide poisoning and habitat destruction will NOT continue to grow for the foreseeable future.
This negation of the statement has no effect on the argument because, as we know from the premise stated above, the argument relates to the present situation, not a future one.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Plants for sale at local garden centers have not been treated with neonicotinoid pesticides.

As we said, an author assumes that there are not reasons that her plan will fail; otherwise, she would not recommend the plan.In this case, the ecologist recommends buying flowering plants from local garden centers and planting them in home gardens in order to help bees being poisoned by neonicotinoid pesticides.What is there to make us think that the plants at local garden centers have not also been treated with neonicotinoid pesticides?

If the plants at garden centers were in fact treated with neonicotinoids, then the ecologist’s plan would certainly fail.So, in making her argument, the ecologist is assuming that the plants at local garden centers have not been treated with neonicotinoids.If you have a hard time seeing this, we can negate what this answer choice says:
The plants for sale at local garden centers HAVE been treated with neonicotinoid pesticides.

The argument that buying and planting plants from garden centers will help bees clearly falls apart.Therefore, what this answer choice says is an assumption the ecologist makes in order to reach her conclusion.
(D) People cannot do more for bees by purchasing only food grown without the use of neonicotinoid pesticides than they can by planting flowering plants in their gardens.

We need to be on the lookout for the “better plan” trap answer choice.The ecologist has not made the claim that her plan is the best possible plan.Rather, she says that her plan is a one valid plan.Therefore, she does not need to assume that there is not a better plan out there.There could be many plans that are better than the one she proposes, and her plan could still work perfectly, meaning that her argument would still be valid.
If you have a hard time seeing this, we can negate what this answer choice says:
People CAN do more for bees by eating food grown without the use of neonicotinoid pesticides than they can by planting flowering plants in their gardens.

This negation does not change anything about the ecologist’s argument, which is simply that people can dosomethingto help bees by planting in their gardens.Thus, what this answer choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument rests.
(E) Bee habitat destruction is a significant threat to bee survival only because of the use of neonicotinoid pesticides.

Notice that the ecologist never makes the claim that neonicotinoid pesticides are the driving forces behind bee habitat destruction.The premises presented are simply that neonicotinoids used on plants are poisoning bees and that habitat destruction has lowered the number of plants available to bees.Given the premises, it does not matter what primarily caused habitat destruction to threaten bee survival.The threats are there regardless, and the ecologist presents a way to mitigate those threats.Thus, what this answer choice says is not an assumption required by the ecologist’s argument.
If you have a hard time seeing this, we can negate what this statement says:
Bee habitat destruction is a significant threat to bee survival NOT only because of the use of neonicotinoid pesticides (but also because of…).
There could be a wide range of reasons why habitat destruction is a significant threat to bee survival, but the ecologist’s recommendation for how to mitigate the existing problems still stands.

question.text: Without a sufficient dietary intake of vitamin B12, humans will develop vitamin B12 deficiency.Vitamin B12 is found in high concentrations in red meat.Therefore, to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency, humans must eat red meat.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the support for the above conclusion depends?

answers.text: Humans who do not consume red meat could not prevent vitamin B12 deficiency by using vitamin B12 supplements.
Some plants, such as shitake mushrooms and seaweed, contain high concentrations of vitamin B12.
In humans, vitamin B12 is degraded by the acid found in the stomach.
Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause a variety of health problems in humans.
Because consumption of red meat can cause many health problems, many people avoid consumption of red meat.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Without a sufficient dietary intake of vitamin B12, humans will develop vitamin B12 deficiency.
Premise: Vitamin B12 is found in high concentrations in red meat.
Conclusion: Therefore, to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency, humans must eat red meat.
The author presents us with two premises.From those premises, she concludes that, to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency, humans must eat red meat.
Do those two premises alone provide solid support for the author’s conclusion? What must the author assume in order to draw that conclusion from her two premises?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Humans who do not consume red meat could not prevent vitamin B12 deficiency by using vitamin B12 supplements.

The author states that, without a sufficient dietary intake of vitamin B12, humans will develop vitamin B12 deficiency, and that vitamin B12 is found in high concentrations in red meat.From those two facts, the author concludes that, to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency, humansmusteat red meat.

Notice that, in arriving at this conclusion, she assumes that eating meat is the only way for humans to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency; she assumes that there could not be another way that humans could prevent vitamin B12 deficiency.

What would happen to the argument if humans who do not consume red meat COULD prevent vitamin B12 deficiency by using vitamin B12 supplements? Would the conclusion that, to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency, humans must eat red meat still be supported by the evidence provided by the author? In that case, even though red meat contains B12, humans would not have to eat red meat in order to prevent B12 deficiency.

So, if what the negated version of this choice says is true, the argument falls apart.Therefore, the author must be assuming that, as this choice says, humans who do not consume red meat could not prevent vitamin B12 deficiency by using vitamin B12 supplements.
(B) Some plants, such as shitake mushrooms and seaweed, contain high concentrations of vitamin B12.
Notice that what this answer choice says weakens the argument.

If some plants, such as shitake mushrooms and seaweed, contain high concentrations of vitamin B12, then we have less faith in the author’s conclusion that humans must eat red meat to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency.After all, if these plants contain high concentrations of vitamin B12, couldn’t humans eat them to prevent vitamin B12 deficiency?
Of course, we’re not seeking something that weakens the argument; we’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument relies.
In general, be careful not to choose an answer choice that weakens the argument when answering an Assumption question.
(C) In humans, vitamin B12 is degraded by the acid found in the stomach.
Remember, we are seeking an unwritten fact that must true in order for the conclusion to follow from the premises (an assumption).

Of course, the author need not assume that vitamin B12 is degraded by the acid found in the human stomach.Regardless of whether B12 is degraded by acid, it is the case that, without a sufficient dietary intake of vitamin B12, humans will develop vitamin B12 deficiency and that vitamin B12 is found in high concentrations in red meat, and so the author’s conclusion may be valid.
Still, this choice could be tempting, and here is one reason why:

If it is true that vitamin B12 is degraded by the acid found in the stomach, that fact could seem to explain why humans develop B12 deficiency without sufficient dietary intake of B12.In other words, we could get the impression that the reason humans need to take in B12 is that acid degrades the B12 they have already.

Of course, even if this choice does explain why humans develop B12 deficiency without sufficient dietary intake of B12 (it doesn’t explain why, by the way), we aren’t looking for such an explanation.We are looking for an assumption necessary for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.

In order to correctly answer an Assumption question, we have to be sure to find a choice that states a necessary assumption, not a choice that does something else related to the argument, such as explain something mentioned in the passage.
(D) Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause a variety of health problems in humans.
In order to avoid choosing this tempting trap choice, we have to be careful to keep the exact conclusion in mind.
Notice that the conclusion is aboutwhat humans must do in order to avoidB12 deficiency.
The conclusion is not thatit is important to avoidB12 deficiency.

Therefore, the author does not have to provide any reasons, such health issues, for avoiding B12 deficiency, because such reasons would provide support for the wrong conclusion, the conclusion that it is important to avoid B12 deficiency, rather than support for the conclusion of the argument presented in the passage.
(E) Because consumption of red meat can cause many health problems, many people avoid consumption of red meat.
Remember, we are seeking an unwritten fact that must true in order for the conclusion to follow from the premises.

Of course, the author need not assume that, because consumption of red meat can cause many health problems, many people avoid the consumption of red meat.Even if people do not avoid the consumption of red meat, her argument could still work.

It can be tempting to choose an answer choice that sounds good because we know that it is true in the real world.However, we must resist the urge to choose answers simply because they sound true to life.

question.text: Brightstar CEO: The most effective way to increase the productivity of office employees is to limit their distractions.The main source of distraction of our office employees is the internet.Starting next week, we will no longer provide internet access to employees working in our offices.Once that change takes effect, we will see an increase in the productivity of our office employees.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the support for the above conclusion depends?

answers.text: Some Brightstar office employees are not distracted by the internet.
Brightstar does not provide any of its employees with laptops that can be used at their homes.
Office employees at Brightstar are, on average, very productive.
It is not impossible for Brightstar office employees to be productive without using the internet.
Brightstar could not increase the productivity of its office employees by providing better training.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: The most effective way to increase the productivity of office employees is to limit their distractions.
Premise: The main source of distraction of our office employees is the internet.
Premise: Starting next week, we will no longer provide internet access to employees working in our offices.
Conclusion: Once that change takes effect, we will see an increase in the productivity of our office employees.
Because this is an Assumption question, we’re seeking an answer choice that presents an unwritten premise that must be true in order for the argument to work.

The CEO has presented a plan.Any time an author presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work.In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will spoil the plan.In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, he assumes that there are no malicious variables.
The correct answer to this question states an assumption that the CEO has made about a particular malicious variable.
(A) Some Brightstar office employees are not distracted by the internet.
The conclusion of the argument is that Brightstar’s ceasing to provide internet access will result in a reduction in employee distraction and thus an increase in employee productivity.

Of course, then, the plan will work best ifallof the office employees are distracted by the internet; in that case, ceasing to provide internet access will reduce distraction for all of the employees.

However, even if distraction is reduced for only some employees—those whoaredistracted by the internet—that reduced distraction could still lead to an increase in productivity, and the CEO’s conclusion would still be sound.

Notice also that the argument states as fact that the main source of distraction of Brightstar’s office employees is the internet.Since that premise is true, the CEO’s plan could still work, regardless of whether some employees are not distracted by the internet.
(B) Brightstar does not provide any of its employees with laptops that can be used at their homes.

This choice could be tempting, because we could get the impression that, if employees have laptops they can use at home, the employees will access and be distracted by the internet even though Brightstar will no longer provide internet access in its offices.
However, we have to be careful to pay attention to the details of the plan, so that we don’t get fooled by a tempting choice.

The success of the plan depends on making the employeesin the officesmore productive.So, the employees could have laptops they use at home and be distracted by the internet at home, yet ceasing to provide internet accessin the officescould still result in an increase in office employee productivity.
So, the CEO need not assume that Brightstar does not provide any of its employees with laptops that can be used at their homes.
(C) Office employees at Brightstar are, on average, very productive.
In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to pay attention to what the conclusion of the argument is.
The conclusion is that, once the company ceases providing internet access to employees who are working in its offices, there will be an increase in the productivity of the office employees.

So, we see that the CEO’s conclusion, his plan, is to improve upon the productivity of his office employees,whatever that current level of productivity is.In other words, whether the office employees are some of the most productive employees in the world or some of the least productive, his plan is simply to improve that productivity by removing something that is distracting the employees.
So, the CEO does not need to assume that the office employees at Brightstar are, on average, very productive.These office employees could be very unproductive, and his argument could still be sound.
YOUR CHOICE(D) It is not impossible for Brightstar office employees to be productive without using the internet.
The CEO concludes that, once the company ceases providing internet access to employees working in its offices, the company will see an increase in the productivity of its office employees.

However, what if the Brightstar office employees require the internet to be productive? What if it IS impossible for Brightstar office employees to be productive without using the internet? In that case, ceasing to provide internet access won’t make the employees more productive.It will cause them to become unproductive.
Therefore, the CEO must be assuming that it is NOT impossible for Brightstar office employees to be productive without using the internet.Without this assumption, the argument falls apart.
(E) Brightstar could not increase the productivity of its office employees by providing better training.

In arriving at the conclusion that removing internet access will increase office employee productivity, the CEO need not assume that there are no other ways to increase the productivity of the office employees.Even if there are other ways that Brightstar could increase the productivity of its office employees, the plan of ceasing to provide internet service could work.
It’s important to remember that, when a person presents a plan, he does not need to assume that there is not another plan that would serve to achieve the same aim.

question.text: In a survey, cyclists indicated that the bike path in the car-free pedestrian zone is the bike path in Boselton most dangerous for cycling.However, cyclists’ perceptions of which path is most dangerous must not match reality.Police records of crashes involving cyclists contain just three reports of serious crashes that occurred on the pedestrian zone path, and there are bike paths in Boselton for which there are significantly more reports of serious crashes involving cyclists than there are for the bike path in the pedestrian zone.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument relies?

answers.text: Of crashes involving cyclists in the pedestrian zone, the proportion reported is not much smaller than the proportion of such crashes on other Boselton bike paths reported.
The reported crashes involving cyclists that occurred in Boselton’s car-free pedestrian zone did not also involve one or more pedestrians.
Crashes between pedestrians and cyclists are less dangerous for cyclists than crashes involving cars and cyclists are.
The city of Boselton should do more to separate pedestrians from cyclists traveling through the car-free pedestrian zone.
The survey results do not represent the opinions of the majority of cyclists in Boselton.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In a survey, cyclists indicated that the bike path in the car-free pedestrian zone is the bike path in Boselton most dangerous for cycling.
Premise: Police records of crashes involving cyclists contain just three reports of serious crashes that occurred on the pedestrian zone path.
Premise: There are bike paths in Boselton for which there are significantly more reports of serious crashes involving cyclists than there are for the bike path in the pedestrian zone.
Conclusion: Cyclists’ perceptions of which path is most dangerous must not match reality.

The conclusion of the argument is based on an apparent discrepancy between what cyclists indicated in a survey and what is indicated by the numbers of crashes reported for various bike paths.The evidence used to support the conclusion is the number of reported crashes, while the cyclists say simply that a particular area is “most dangerous.” Notice that these two criteria are not synonymous by any stretch.It could be the level of danger present in an area is not accurately represented by the number of crashes reported for that area.What assumption must the author be making?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Of crashes involving cyclists in the pedestrian zone, the proportion reported is not much smaller than the proportion of such crashes on other Boselton bike paths reported.

The conclusion is based on the numbers of crashesreportedfor various bike paths in Boselton.Basing the argument on those numbers requires assuming that those numbers accurately represent the relative levels of danger of the paths.

In order for the numbers of reported crashes to be accurate indicators of the paths’ relative levels of danger, the proportion of crashes involving cyclists on the pedestrian zone path reported has to be similar to the proportion of such crashes on other bike paths in Boselton reported.

Otherwise, it could be that a relatively small proportion of the crashes in the pedestrian zone have been reported.In such a case, it could be that, even though the records seem to indicate that there were fewer crashes on the pedestrian zone path, there were actually more crashes on the path in the pedestrian zone than there were on other paths.In that case, the evidence, numbers of crashes reported, would not be good support for the conclusion that the cyclists’ perceptions that the path through the pedestrian zone is the most dangerousmustnot match reality.

So, the author must be assuming that the proportion of crashes involving cyclists on the pedestrian zone path reported is not much smaller than the proportion of such crashes on other bike paths in Boselton reported.
(B) The reported crashes involving cyclists that occurred in Boselton’s car-free pedestrian zone did not also involve one or more pedestrians.

This choice could be tempting if we fail to note the exact conclusion of the argument.If we have the impression that the conclusion is essentially that the bike path through the pedestrian zone is not particularly dangerous for anyone, including pedestrians, then we might decide that the author must be assuming that no pedestrians were involved in the reported crashes in the pedestrian area.

Notice, however, that the conclusion is specifically about which area is most dangerousfor cycling.Lack of pedestrian involvement in the reported crashes is not necessary for going from the evidence – that there were more reports of serious crashes involving cyclists in other areas – to the conclusion – that cyclists’ perception of which path is most dangerous for cycling must not match reality.
(C) Crashes between pedestrians and cyclists are less dangerous for cyclists than crashes involving cars and cyclists are.

This answer choice could be tempting, because it states something that is both related to the argument and likely to be true.However, we aren’t looking for something likely to be true.We’re looking for an assumption integral to the argument.
(D) The city of Boselton should do more to separate pedestrians from cyclists traveling through the car-free pedestrian zone.

This choice could be tempting, because what it states could be taken to be a conclusion loosely based on what cyclists said about how dangerous the bicycle path through the pedestrian zone is for cycling.
To avoid choosing an answer like this one, we must keep in mind what type of choice is needed for answering the question.In this case, we are looking for an assumption, not a conclusion.
(E) The survey results do not represent the opinions of the majority of cyclists in Boselton.

This answer could conceivably serve as anexplanationfor the discrepancy between what cyclists indicated in the survey and the number of accidents reported for the areas.The explanation would be that the survey results somehow did not match the opinions of most cyclists, and that the majority of cyclists do in fact agree that the path in the pedestrian zone is not the most dangerous.
However, we are not looking for an explanation; we are looking for an assumption integral to the argument.

question.text: Last month, the Lotus Yoga Studio hired a new yoga instructor to teach its Monday morning yoga classes.It must be the case that the hiring of the new yoga instructor has made the Monday morning classes more appealing to students, because, since the new instructor started teaching the classes, the number of students taking yoga classes at the studio on Monday mornings has doubled.Which of the following is an assumption logically necessary for coming to the conclusion of the above argument?

answers.text: Last month, the Lotus Yoga Studio reduced by half the cost students must pay to attend Monday morning yoga classes at the studio.
Attending yoga class on Monday mornings has not since last month become generally more popular than it was in the city in which the Lotus Yoga Studio is located.
Many other yoga studios located on the same city block as the Lotus Yoga Studio also hired new yoga instructors last month.
Last month, Lotus Yoga Studio began heavily promoting its Monday morning yoga classes to local residents who do not go to work on Mondays.
Since last month, student attendance of yoga classes taught by all other yoga instructors at the studio has sharply decreased.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Last month, the Lotus Yoga Studio hired a new yoga instructor to teach its Monday morning yoga classes.
Premise: Since the new instructor started teaching the classes, the number of students taking yoga classes at the studio on Monday mornings has doubled.
Conclusion: It must be the case that the hiring of the new yoga instructor has made the Monday morning classes more appealing to students

The scenario described in the passage involves two occurrences: the studio hired a new instructor to teach the Monday morning classes, and the number of students taking classes on Monday mornings doubled.The author concludes that, since the second occurrence followed the first, the first must have caused the second.

Of course, in arriving at this cause-and-effect conclusion, the author has assumed that there is not an alternative explanation for the increase in the number of students attending Monday morning classes.
(A) Last month, the Lotus Yoga Studio reduced by half the cost students must pay to attend Monday morning yoga classes at the studio.
Rather than state an assumption necessary for the premises to support the argument’s conclusion, this choice undermines the case for arriving at that conclusion.

If it’s true that, as this choice says, last month, the Lotus Yoga Studio reduced by half the cost students must pay to attend Monday morning yoga classes at the studio, then there’s reason to question whether the hiring of the new instructor caused the increase in attendance of the studio’s Monday morning classes.It could be the case that the reduction in cost rather than the hiring of the new instructor is the cause of the increase in attendance.
So, what this choice says is a reason to question the validity of the argument’s conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Attending yoga class on Monday mornings has not since last month become generally more popular than it was in the city in which the Lotus Yoga Studio is located.

This choice is certainly an assumption necessary for going from the fact that Monday morning class attendance has increased since the instructor was hired last month to the conclusion that the hiring of the instructor is the cause of that increase, because it has to be true that Monday morning yoga has not become generally more popular since last month in order for the conclusion to follow from the premises.
Let’s negate the choice:
Attending yoga class on Monday mornings HAS since last month become generally more popular than it was in the city in which the Lotus Yoga Studio is located.

We can see that the negated version of the choice weakens the author’s case, because, if it’s true that Monday morning yoga HAS become generally more popular since last month, then it could be the case that that general increase in popularity, rather than the hiring of the instructor, is what caused the increase in attendance of the studio’s Monday morning classes.

So, if what this choice says were not true, then concluding that the hiring of the instructor is the cause of the attendance increase would not make sense.So, what this choice says is an assumption necessary for this argument’s premises to support its conclusion.
(C) Many other yoga studios located on the same city block as the Lotus Yoga Studio also hired new yoga instructors last month.

Regardless of whether other studios hired instructors, the hiring of the new instructor could be the cause of the increase in attendance that has occurred since the Lotus Yoga Studio hired that instructor.So, this choice is certainly not an assumption required for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.
(D) Last month, Lotus Yoga Studio began heavily promoting its Monday morning yoga classes to local residents who do not go to work on Mondays.
Rather than state an assumption necessary for the premises to support the argument’s conclusion, this choice weakens the argument.

If it’s true that, as this choice says, last month, Lotus Yoga Studio began heavily promoting its Monday morning yoga classes to local residents who do not go to work on Mondays, then there’s reason to question whether the hiring of the new instructor caused the increase in attendance of the studio’s Monday morning classes.It could be the case that the promotion rather than the hiring of the new instructor is the cause of the increase in attendance.
So, what this choice says is a reason to question the validity of the argument’s conclusion.
(E) Since last month, student attendance of yoga classes taught by all other yoga instructors at the studio has sharply decreased.

Regardless of whether student attendance of yoga classes taught by all other yoga instructors at the studio has sharply decreased, the hiring of the new instructor could be the cause of the increase in attendance that has occurred since the Lotus Yoga Studio hired that instructor.So, this choice is certainly not an assumption required for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.
To more easily see why this choice is not a necessary assumption let’s negate it:
Since last month, student attendance of yoga classes taught by all other yoga instructors at the studio has NOT sharply decreased.

Would that statement mean that the hiring of the new instructor is not the cause of the increased attendance of Monday morning classes? Of course not.Attendance of the other classes does not have to decrease in order for it to be the case that hiring the new instructor caused an increase in attendance of the Monday morning classes.

question.text: BenoSolar proposes to sell solar electric power systems to people who live in rural Benoa, to whom BenoSolar would offer the option of spreading the cost of installed systems over time by paying in monthly installments.Most rural Benoans believe that they would benefit from having increased access to electrical power for lights and other electrical devices.However, it is clear that few rural Benoans would be able to afford monthly payments for solar electric power systems since the vast majority of them have little income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument relies in supporting its conclusion?

answers.text: Rural Benoans generally do not spend significant portions of their incomes on candles for lighting and on batteries for powering electrical devices.
In rural Benoa, the installation of solar electric power systems on a house-by-house basis would not cost less than connecting the houses to Benoa’s central power grid.
Most rural Benoans work in occupations generally associated with relatively low earnings.
The solar electric power systems provided by BenoSolar would not have the capacity to store power that could be used at night.
With increased access to electricity, rural Benoans could better utilize the internet and other technological resources that they could use to increase their incomes.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Background: BenoSolar proposes to sell solar electric power systems to people who live in rural Benoa, to whom BenoSolar would offer the option of spreading the system cost over time by paying in monthly installments.
Background: Most rural Benoans believe that they would benefit from having increased access to electrical power for lights and other electrical devices.
Premise: The vast majority of rural Benoans have little income in excess of what they need to cover their current expenses.
Conclusion: It is clear that few rural Benoans would be able to afford monthly payments for solar electric power systems.

While the conclusion follows logically from the premise, arriving at the conclusion requires making any of a variety of assumptions, such as that most rural Benoans don’t have money hidden in mattresses, or that the Benoan government doesn’t offer tax breaks to people who purchase solar systems.Let’s see what assumption is highlighted by an answer choice.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Rural Benoans generally do not spend significant portions of their incomes on candles for lighting and on batteries for powering electrical devices.

If this statement were not true, and it were the case that rural Benoans generally spend significant portions of their incomes on candles for lighting and batteries for powering electrical devices, then it would be likely that most rural Benoans could simply switch from spending money on candles and batteries to paying for solar systems in monthly installments.In such a case, it would be quite possible that they could afford the monthly payments.

So, the conclusion that it is clear that few rural Benoans could afford monthly payments depends on the assumption that most rural Benoans do not spend significant portions of their incomes on candles and batteries.
(B) In rural Benoa, the installation of solar electric power systems on a house-by-house basis would not cost less than connecting the houses to Benoa’s central power grid.

This choice could be tempting, since the cost of the solar electric systems is an aspect of the support for the conclusion.However, even if what this answer choice says weren’t true, and installation of the solar electric systems costlessthan connecting houses to the power grid, the installation could still be unaffordable for most rural Benoans.Thus, this choice is not an assumption upon which the conclusion depends.

We need to be careful of answer choices that mention a different or better plan (in this case, connecting to the central grid).After all, the author is not claiming that the proposed plan is the best possible plan; it is simply one possible plan.
(C) Most rural Benoans work in occupations generally associated with relatively low earnings.

This choice seems to explain a fact stated in the passage.It seems to explain why rural Benoans have little income in excess of what they need to cover their current expenses: they work in occupations associated with relatively low earnings.
However, we are not looking for an explanation for why a fact stated in the passage is true.We are looking for an assumption necessary in order for the evidence to support the conclusion.

Even if rural Benoans did NOT work in occupations associated with relatively low earnings, the fact that they have little excess income could still support the conclusion that it is clear that few would be able to afford monthly payments for solar electric power systems.
So, this choice is not an assumption upon which the argument relies.
(D) The solar electric power systems provided by BenoSolar would not have the capacity to store power that could be used at night.

Since the sun doesn’t shine at night, and since solar systems need sun for generating electricity, there is an issue with solar power that would be resolved by the systems’ having the capacity to store power that could be used at night.

However, there is no clear reason to believe that providing a way to use solar electricity at night would somehow make solar power more affordable.So, one doesn’t have to assume that the systems would not provide electricity at night in order to conclude that rural Benoans don’t have the income to make the required monthly payments.
(E) With increased access to electricity, rural Benoans could better utilize the internet and other technological resources that they could use to increase their incomes.

This choice could be tempting, because what it says is clearly relevant to the argument.If, by having increased access to electricity, rural Benoans could increase their incomes, then perhaps the solar power would serve as a means for Benoans to generate the income needed to make monthly payments.

Of course, how would they afford the initial costs of attaining solar power service in order to generate the income that would allow them to afford the service? We can see that the logic here is somewhat circular.

More importantly, if what this choice says is true, then it weakens the argument, by indicating that there may ways for rural Benoans to afford the monthly payments.Thus, this choices has an effect that is basically the opposite of the effect we’re looking for.

question.text: Soda Company Executive: Many people feel that people’s heavy consumption of sugar-sweetened soda is to blame for the high rate of obesity seen in people today.However, even as the percentage of people who are obese has been climbing, the total amount of soda consumed per year has been declining.Therefore, it is clear that soda is not the culprit.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?

answers.text: The decrease in soda consumption is not largely the result of a reduction in consumption of soda by people who were already consuming little soda.
The results of some studies have indicated that consumption of diet soda, which contains no sugar, can contribute to excessive weight gain.
A person cannot regularly consume large amounts of soda without eventually becoming obese.
It can take years of regular consumption of sugar for a person to become obese as a result of sugar consumption.
Lack of exercise, rather than intake of foods that contain relatively large quantities of sugar, is the primary factor underlying the high rate of obesity in people.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: People blame heavy consumption of soda for the increase in percentage of people who are obese.
Premise: While the percentage of people who are obese has been climbing, soda consumption has been declining.
Conclusion: It is clear that soda is not the culprit (the cause of the increase in percentage of obese people).

Since the percentage of people who are obese has been climbing even as the total amount of soda consumed has been declining, it seems logical to conclude that soda consumption must not be the cause of the increase in obesity and that something else must be the cause.

However, if we consider the situation further, we can come up with ways that soda consumption could decrease while still being a key factor in the increase in obesity.For example, it could be that some non-obese people reduced their consumption of soda while other non-obese people continued to heavily consume soda and eventually became obese.
