So, in making the argument, the soda company executive assumes that there is no way that soda consumption could decrease and still be the cause of the increase in the rate of obesity.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The decrease in soda consumption is not largely the result of a reduction in consumption of soda by people who were already consuming little soda.
This answer choice outlines a scenario in which total soda consumption decreases but soda consumption still underlies the increase in the rate of obesity.

Imagine a scenario in which the people consuming less and less soda are the people who were not drinking enough soda to become obese anyway.At the same time, the group of people drinking enough soda to become obese continue to drink that amount of soda.In such a case, the total amount of soda consumed per year could decline even as the percentage of obese people increases.

Thus, the executive must be assuming that the decline in total soda consumption was not the result of a reduction in consumption by people who already consumed little soda.If you have a hard time seeing this, try negating what this answer choice says:
The decrease in soda consumption IS largely the result of a reduction in consumption of soda by people who were already consuming little soda.
We see that the argument falls apart because the people who have decreased their soda consumption have not been the people drinking enough to become obese.
(B) The results of some studies have indicated that consumption of diet soda, which contains no sugar, can contribute to excessive weight gain.

If we don’t pay attention to what the question asks, this answer could be tempting in that what it says is somewhat relevant to the argument.However, while relevant to the argument, this choice does not have the effect we need.

The executive’s conclusion is that soda consumption is clearly not to blame for the increase in the obesity rate, but this choice provides a reason to believe that soda consumptiondoesin fact result in obesity.
Of course, we are not looking for a choice that conflicts with or weakens the support for the conclusion.We are looking for an assumption upon which the support for the conclusion depends.
(C) A person cannot regularly consume large amounts of soda without eventually becoming obese.
What this choice says is plausible.However, we are looking not for a plausible statement but for an assumption integral to the argument.

Since the executive’s conclusion is that the increase in the obesity rate isnotthe result of soda consumption, the executive does not have to assume that consuming large quantities of sodadoesresult in obesity.If anything, what this answer choice says weakens the executive’s conclusion, and we are not looking for a weakener.
(D) It can take years of regular consumption of sugar for a person to become obese as a result of sugar consumption.

This choice could seem to support the executive’s conclusion by indicating that something else must be to blame for the obesity increase, since becoming obese from sugar consumption takes a long time.However, note that the argument does not put a timeline on the decrease in soda consumption or the increase in obesity.We merely know that “as the percentage of people who are obese has been climbing, the total amount of soda consumed per year has been declining.” These simultaneous events could be happening over the course of five years or five decades.
In other words, the length of time it takes for sugar consumption to result in obesity does not affect the argument one way or another.Thus, it is not an assumption on which the argument relies.

What if we assume that what this choice says isnottrue, in which case it does NOT take years for sugar to make a person obese? It would still be the case that soda consumption is declining while the obesity rate is increasing.Therefore, it would still be reasonable to conclude that soda consumption is not the cause of the increase in obesity.
(E) Lack of exercise, rather than intake of foods that contain relatively large quantities of sugar, is the primary factor underlying the high rate of obesity in people.

What this choice says might explain how it could be that the obesity rate has increased while soda consumption has decreased.However, we are not looking for an explanation.We are looking for an assumption integral to the argument, and the executive does not have to assume that this particular explanation matches reality in order to come to the argument’s conclusion.

The executive’s argument works as long as there is any cause other than soda consumption for the increase in the obesity rate.That other cause does not have to be lack of exercise in order for the argument to work.

question.text: Some biologists claim that swan genetics are such that there can exist swans with white feathering and swans with black feathering.However, this claim must not be accurate, since, to date, not a single black swan has been observed in nature by biologists.Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies?

answers.text: There are no birds with black feathering that look sufficiently similar to swans that they could be confused for swans.
If a bird was observed with black feathering, it must not have been a swan.
No swan with black feathering exists that has not yet been observed.
Biologists have had a difficult time locating swans with black feathering.
Swans do not share a black-feathered, common ancestor with other birds that have both black and white feathering.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Not a single black swan has been observed in nature by biologists.
Conclusion: The claim that swan genetics are such that there can exist swans with white feathering and swans with black feathering must not be accurate.

It’s important to remember that the absence of evidence does not mean that the evidence does not exist.We simply might not yet have found that evidence.So, the author is making a big assumption in moving from the premise to the conclusion.As we look at the answer choices, we need to determine what “unstated premise” must be true in order for the conclusion to be properly drawn from the stated premise.
(A) There are no birds with black feathering that look sufficiently similar to swans that they could be confused for swans.

A bird thatlooks likea black swan isn’t a black swan, so the information in this answer choice tells us nothing about the existence of black swans or the validity of the argument.Let’s try negating the statement:
There ARE birds with black feathering that look sufficiently similar to swans that they could be confused for swans.

The fact remains that biologists have not observed a single, actual black swan in nature, so the author’s conclusion could still be valid.Since we are given no reason to believe that the existence of birds that look like black swans proves or disproves the existence of actual black swans, what this answer choice says is not an assumption on which the argument relies.
(B) If a bird was observed with black feathering, it must not have been a swan.

This answer choice essentially just repeats the premise that has already been given to us, that not a single black swan has been observed to date.So, in order to make the argument, the author does not have toassumethat any birds with black feathering that were observed must not have been swans; we are given that fact.Remember, a given premise must be taken as fact.So, a gap remains in the argument, and confirming something already told to us by a premise does not fill that gap.
YOUR CHOICE(C) No swan with black feathering exists that has not yet been observed.

Let’s look again at the argument’s structure: We know that the fact that we have yet to find the evidence we seek (a black swan, in this case) does not mean that that evidence is not out there.So, to say that the fact that we have never seen a black swan means that they don’t exist is not logical, unless the author assumes that no swan with black feathering exists that has not yet been observed.
Notice that this assumption connects the argument’s premise with its conclusion:
Premise: Not a single black swan has been observed in nature by biologists.
Assumption: No swan with black feathering exists that has not yet been observed.
Conclusion: The claim that swan genetics are such that there can exist swans with white feathering and swans with black feathering must not be accurate.
We could negate the assumption to see its effect more clearly:
There IS a swan with black feathering that has not yet been observed.
In that case, the author’s argument falls apart.Thus, this is an assumption that is necessary in order for the argument to work.
(D) Biologists have had a difficult time locating swans with black feathering.

Whether biologists have had a difficult time locating a swan with black feathering or they simply haven’t been looking for one (or there is any other explanation for their not finding one), the fact remains that not a single black swan has been observed.

Remember, we are not looking for an explanation for the fact that black swans haven’t been observed.We are looking for an assumption that would get the author from that fact to the conclusion that black swans don’t exist.
(E) Swans do not share a black-feathered, common ancestor with other birds that have both black and white feathering.

This choice may be tempting because it could be perceived as making the author’s conclusion that black swans don’t exist more plausible, and the passage’s mention of genetics could make this information about common ancestry seem relevant to the argument.Remember, however, that we’re looking for something that must be true in order for the argument to be valid.
Let’s try negating the statement:
Swans DO share a black-feathered, common ancestor with other birds that have both black and white feathering.
This negation does not change the fact presented or weaken the author’s argument.The fact remains that not a single black swan has been observed in nature by biologists.

Since what this answer choice says, that swans do not share a black-feathered ancestor with other birds, does nothave to be truein order for the author’s argument to work, the statement is not a necessary assumption.

question.text: Community Leader: Increasing the presence of police in our neighborhood may result in decreased levels of crime and, at the same time, residents of our neighborhood may feel uncomfortable living under the constant watch of police officers.An alternative to increasing the police presence in our neighborhood is constructing green spaces that are conducive to social and recreational activities.In coming out of their houses to congregate in green spaces, the residents themselves would provide watchful eyes that would serve to reduce crime.The success of the alternative strategy outlined above depends on which of the following?

answers.text: Neighborhoods with parks and other green spaces have no more crime than neighborhoods without parks but with significant police presences have.
The cost of constructing and maintaining green spaces in a neighborhood is equal to or less than the cost of increasing the police presence in that neighborhood.
The residents who would congregate in green spaces in the neighborhood in question would not be only those who are involved in criminal activity.
Most residents of the neighborhood in question would prefer an increase in green spaces over an increase in police presence.
The city in which the neighborhood in question is located does not already have the resources necessary for increasing the police presence in the neighborhood in question.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Background: Increasing the presence of police in our neighborhood may result in decreased levels of crime, and at the same time, residents of our neighborhood may feel uncomfortable living constantly under the watch of police officers.

Conclusion: An alternative to increasing the police presence in our neighborhood is constructing green spaces that are conducive to social and recreational activities.In coming out of their houses to congregate in green spaces, the residents themselves would provide watchful eyes that would serve to reduce crime.
While the passage in this question is not structured as a clearly defined set of premises that support a conclusion, we can tell the following:

The community leader has a goal, crime reduction.The community leader indicates that crime reduction could be achieved via increasing the police presence in the neighborhood, but because of a disadvantage of that strategy, proposes an alternative strategy of providing green spaces to encourage residents to come outdoors.The community leader believes that this strategy will result in decreased levels of crime because, when they congregate in green spaces, the residents will provide watchful eyes that serve to reduce crime.

To correctly answer this question, we have to find a choice that outlines something necessary for the alternative strategy’s success, which we can define as an increase in watchful eyes that would serve to reduce crime.

Remember that whenever a speaker presents us with a plan, the speaker assumes that the plan will work as intended and that there will not be unforeseen variables or events that derail it.Of course, if that assumption is found to be incorrect, the plan will fall apart.
(A) Neighborhoods with parks and other green spaces have no more crime than neighborhoods without parks but with significant police presences have.

This choice is a trap, as it seems to support the idea that neighborhoods with green spaces are just as safe as neighborhoods with significant police presences.However, for the alternative strategy to work, it does not have to be the case that, in general, neighborhoods with green spaces have the same amount of crime as neighborhoods with significant police presences.The success of the community leader’s strategy depends solely on whether providing green spaces in this particular neighborhood would provide watchful eyes that would serve to reduce crime.
(B) The cost of constructing and maintaining green spaces in a neighborhood is equal to or less than the cost of increasing the police presence in that neighborhood.

The conclusion is not that providing green spaces costs less than increasing police presence.For the strategy to be successful, providing green spaces simply has to result in there being watchful eyes that serve to reduce crime.So, what this answer choice says does not have to be true in order for the strategy to be successful.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The residents who would congregate in green spaces in the neighborhood in question would not be only those who are involved in criminal activity.

The success of the strategy depends on its resulting in “watchful eyes that would serve to reduce crime.” Of course, if the residents congregating in green spaces were only those involved in criminal activity, then those residents would not aid in crime reduction.So, in order for the strategy to succeed, what this choice says has to be true.
(D) Most residents of the neighborhood in question would prefer an increase in green spaces over an increase in police presence.

This answer is a trap, as the passage mentions that residents may feel “uncomfortable living under the constant watch of police officers”; one could get the impression that, in order for the strategy to be successful, people would have to prefer having more green spaces over having more police.However, although the alternative strategy may have been influenced by residents’ perceived preferences, the success of the strategy does not depend on people’s preferences.Regardless of whether people prefer more green space or more police, as long as providing more green spaces would result in there being watchful eyes that would serve to reduce crime, the strategy will succeed.
(E) The city in which the neighborhood in question is located does not already have the resources necessary for increasing the police presence in the neighborhood in question.

To answer the question, we need a statement that has to be true in order for the alternative strategy to work.The availability of resources to increase the police presence does not affect how well the strategy of providing green spaces would work to reduce crime.What this answer choice says does not affect the argument.

question.text: Cats are considered obligate carnivores, since their health deteriorates if their diets do not contain taurine, an amino acid found in meat but not in plants.In order for a cat to live on a plant-based diet, the food the cat eats must be supplemented with synthetic taurine.Of course, this supplementation process does not occur in nature.Therefore, it is not healthy for a cat to live on a plant-based diet.Which of the following is an assumption necessary for arriving at the above conclusion?

answers.text: Without human intervention, cats never eat plant material.
When commercial cat food is made with meat, no synthetic taurine is added.
Cats are the only animals that evolved in a way such that taurine became an essential component of their diets.
There are no cats that live on plant-based diets.
It is not healthy to live on food created via a process that does not occur in nature.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: The health of cats deteriorates if their diets do not contain taurine.
Premise: In order for a cat to live on a plant-based diet, the food the cat eats must be supplemented with synthetic taurine.
Premise: The process of supplementing food with synthetic taurine does not occur in nature.
Conclusion: It is not healthy for a cat to live on a plant-based diet.

There is a clear gap in this argument.The conclusion is that eating plant-based food is not healthy for a cat.The evidence provided says nothing about health or about the effects eating plant-based food would have on a cat but rather presents the fact that the process of supplementing food with synthetic taurine does not occur in nature.
So, in order for this argument to work, we need an assumption that connects the premise “does not occur in nature” to the conclusion “is not healthy for a cat.”
(A) Without human intervention, cats never eat plant material.

This choice could be tempting because it seems to indicate that eating plant material is not a natural behavior of cats, and, therefore, this choice seems to support the conclusion that a plant-based diet must not be healthy for cats.However, concluding thatliving onplant-based food is not healthy for a cat does not require assuming that catsnevereat plant material on their own.

Remember, there is a difference betweenliving ona plant-based diet and sometimes eating plants.Without human intervention, cats could eat plant material as a small part of their diets, and doing so could even be a healthy practice for cats, and those facts would not negate the fact that living on a plant-based diet is not healthy for cats.Thus, this answer choice does not provide an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion.
(B) When commercial cat food is made with meat, no synthetic taurine is added.

This choice could be tempting if we lose track of the conclusion of the argument and mistakenly decide that the conclusion is that meat-based, commercial-made cat food is healthier for cats than plant-based food is.

The conclusion is simply that living on a plant-based diet is not healthy for a cat, and the evidence provided could still support that conclusion even if synthetic taurine is added when commercial cat food is made with meat.Perhaps eating commercial meat-based cat food is also not heathy for cats.
(C) Cats are the only animals that evolved in a way such that taurine became an essential component of their diets.

What this choice says seems to support the conclusion by emphasizing the importance of taurine for cats; after all, if cats are the only animals that have evolved to need taurine, then it seems to be vital to their health, right? However, given the information stated in the passage, we already know that taurine is essential in a cat’s diet, so what this choice says is not an assumption needed to reach the conclusion.

Furthermore, coming to a conclusion about what is healthy for cats does not require assuming that cats are theonlyanimals for which a certain thing is healthy.So what if other animals have also evolved to need taurine? That fact doesn’t make taurine any more or less necessary in a cat’s diet.
(D) There are no cats that live on plant-based diets.

This choice could be tempting because it seems to be supported by what is said in the passage.However, we are not looking for a conclusion supported by what is said in the passage.We are looking for an assumption upon which the argument relies.

Even if some cats live on plant-based diets, perhaps out of a lack of access to other foods, doing so could be unhealthy for a cat.It could be possible for a cat to survive on a plant-based diet, but surviving is not the same as being healthy.
YOUR CHOICE(E) It is not healthy to live on food created via a process that does not occur in nature.
Here we have a statement that directly addresses the gap between the premises and the conclusion of the argument.

Going from the premise that the process of supplementing food with synthetic taurine does not occur in nature to the conclusion that living on plant-based foods is not healthy for cats requires assuming that it is not healthy to eat food created via a process that does not occur in nature.If itwerehealthy to eat food created via a process that does not occur in nature, then there would be no reason to conclude from that premise that it is not healthy for a cat to live on a plant-based diet.

question.text: Mayor: Many of our citizens are concerned by the number of locusts, which feed upon and often destroy farmers’ crops, present in our town.Effective immediately, all farmers in our town are required to plant only genetically engineered crops that produce BT biotoxin, which kills locusts that feed upon crops that produce it.Therefore, over the coming growing season, we will begin to see the number of locusts in our town decrease.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the support for the above conclusion depends?

answers.text: The town could not find a more effective method for controlling locust populations.
BT biotoxin also kills insects other than locusts, such as ants and bees.
Locusts gather in large swarms when they feed, quickly destroying acres of crops.
Large numbers of birds that eat locusts will not come to the town and wipe out the town’s locust population.
Not all crops to be planted during the coming growing season have already been planted.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many of our citizens are concerned by the number of locusts, which feed upon and often destroy farmers’ crops, present in our town.
Premise: Effective immediately, all farmers in our town are required to plant only genetically engineered crops that produce BT biotoxin, which kills locusts that feed upon crops that produce it.
Conclusion: Over the coming growing season, we will begin to see the number of locusts in our town decrease.
Because this is an Assumption question, we’re seeking an answer choice that presents an unwritten premise that must be true in order for the argument to work.

The mayor has presented a plan.Any time an author presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work.In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will spoil the plan.In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, he assumes that there are no malicious variables.

In order to answer this question correctly, we have to notice a key detail of the mayor’s conclusion.The key detail is that the mayor expects the number of locusts to decrease “over the coming growing season.”
What possible malicious variable is the mayor making an assumption about in concluding that the number of locusts will decrease “over the coming growing season”?
(A) The town could not find a more effective method for controlling locust populations.
The mayor has presented a plan for reducing the number of locusts in the town, a plan that involves all farmers’ planting only genetically engineered crops that produce BT biotoxin.
In concluding that that plan will work, he need not assume that there is not a better plan out there.
In other words, the town might be able to find a more effective method for controlling locust populations, and at the same time, the mayor’s plan could still be sound.
It’s important to remember that when a person concludes that a plan will work, he does not need to assume that there is not a better plan out there.
(B) BT biotoxin also kills insects other than locusts, such as ants and bees.

In order to avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to keep in mind the conclusion of the argument, which is that, because the farmers will be planting only crops that produce BT biotoxin, the people of the town will begin to see the number oflocustsin the town decrease.
We can see that the conclusion is about the number of locusts only.
Thus, regardless of whether BT biotoxin kills insects other than locusts, such as ants and bees, the plan could still work as the mayor concludes it will.
So, the mayor need not assume that BT biotoxin also kills insects other than locusts, such as ants and bees.
(C) Locusts gather in large swarms when they feed, quickly destroying acres of crops.

In order to avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to keep in mind what we are looking for, because what this choice says is certainly pertinent to what the mayor is saying, but this choice does not have the effect we need.

Notice that what this choice says is a reason for implementing the plan.In other words, the fact that locusts gather in large swarms when they feed, quickly destroying acres of crops, is a reason to implement the mayor’s plan.

However, we are not seeking a reason to implement the plan.We are seeking an assumption necessary in order for the evidence to support the mayor’s conclusion, and what this answer choice says is not an assumption necessary for concluding that the plan will work.After all, even if locusts DO NOT gather in large swarms when they feed, the mayor’s argument could still be perfectly sound.
(D) Large numbers of birds that eat locusts will not come to the town and wipe out the town’s locust population.
As always, we must pay attention to the conclusion, which, in this case, is that the number of locusts will decrease.

In arriving at that conclusion, the mayor does not have to assume that the number of locusts will not be reduced in an alternative way.After all, if birds will come to the town and wipe out the locust population, the conclusion that the number of locusts in the town will decrease will still hold.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Not all crops to be planted during the coming growing season have already been planted.

The mayor’s plan is to mandate that all farmers in the town plant only genetically engineered crops that produce BT biotoxin, which kills locusts that feed upon crops that produce it.In his mind, because of that plan’s being implemented, the number of locusts in the town will decrease over the coming growing season.

However, what if the farmers have already planted all of the crops that they will plant during the coming growing season? In that case, the mayor would have missed a crucial piece of information.He would have overlooked a malicious variable.

If the farmers have already planted all of the crops that they will plant during the coming growing season, then, even though they are required to plant only crops that produce BT biotoxin, there is no reason to believe that they will plant such crops during the coming growing season.The planting has already been completed.
In that case, concluding that the number of locusts will decrease over the coming growing season does not make sense.
Therefore, the mayor must be assuming that not all crops to be planted during the coming growing season have already been planted.

question.text: When either corn-based ethanol or gasoline is burned in car engines, carbon dioxide is produced.However, the amount of carbon dioxide added to the atmosphere when corn-based ethanol is burned is equivalent to the amount that was removed from the atmosphere as that corn grew.Therefore, by switching to using corn-based ethanol instead of gasoline, we can power cars without increasing the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.Which of the following is an assumption required for arriving at the above conclusion?

answers.text: Cars designed to use gasoline for fuel would not have to be retrofitted to run on ethanol.
Growing corn to produce ethanol does not require a process that would increase atmospheric carbon dioxide.
The equipment used in the process of farming corn burns fuels such that operating the equipment results in increased atmospheric carbon dioxide.
More carbon dioxide is produced via the burning of a gallon of gasoline than is produced via the burning of a gallon of ethanol.
In the long run, it would not be less expensive to power cars by using electricity generated by solar panels than to power cars by using ethanol.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: When corn-based ethanol is burned, the amount of carbon dioxide released into the atmosphere is equivalent to the amount carbon dioxide that the corn from which the ethanol was made removed from the atmosphere as that corn grew.
Conclusion: By switching to using corn-based ethanol instead of gasoline, we can power cars without increasing the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.

The logic of the argument seems pretty tight.When the ethanol is burned, no more carbon dioxide is released than was removed from the atmosphere by the corn used in making the ethanol.So, the conclusion that burning ethanol to power cars would result in no net increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide seems well supported.

However, the question asks for an assumption required for arriving at the conclusion.Thus, it must be the case that one of the choices will serve to both highlight a gap in the argument and fill that gap.
Remember, whenever an author proposes a plan, the author assumes that the plan will work as intended.
(A) Cars designed to use gasoline for fuel would not have to be retrofitted to run on ethanol.

The argument’s conclusion is that using ethanol to power cars would result in no net increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide.Arriving at that conclusion does not require assuming that all cars are currently ready to run on ethanol.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Growing corn to produce ethanol does not require a process that would increase atmospheric carbon dioxide.

This choice highlights an issue not addressed in the argument and expresses an assumption upon which the argument depends.If growing corn to produce ethanoldoesrequire a process that would increase atmospheric carbon dioxide, then the argument falls apart.For example, if clearing forests to create fields to grow corn increases atmospheric carbon dioxide, then the argument that we can power cars without increasing carbon dioxide in the atmosphere does not hold.

Growing corn and burning ethanol are just two stages of the process of using ethanol to power cars.An increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide resulting from any other stage in the process could invalidate the conclusion.
Thus, by saying that no process involved increases atmospheric carbon dioxide, this answer conveys a necessary assumption.
(C) The equipment used in the process of farming corn burns fuels such that operating the equipment results in increased atmospheric carbon dioxide.

While this answer affects the conclusion, the effect that it has is the opposite of what we need.If what this choice says is true, then the process of making ethanol results in increased atmospheric carbon dioxide.So, this choice weakens the support for the conclusion rather than adds a necessary component to the support.
(D) More carbon dioxide is produced via the burning of a gallon of gasoline than is produced via the burning of a gallon of ethanol.
This answer is a trap, as the way it connects parts of the argument makes it seem to be an assumption upon which the conclusion depends.

While the argument does compare the burning of corn-based ethanol and the burning of gasoline, the conclusion is not based on the idea that there is a difference in the amount of carbon dioxide released when each fuel is burned.Rather, the conclusion is based on the fact that no net increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide results from burning ethanol in cars, because growing corn to produce ethanol offsets the subsequent carbon dioxide release.

So, while this comparison of the two fuels may provide good reason for the author to propose switching to ethanol, this comparison is not an assumption necessary in order for the evidence already provided to support the argument’s conclusion.
(E) In the long run, it would not be less expensive to power cars by using electricity generated by solar panels than to power cars by using ethanol.
This answer is tempting, because it mentions the expense involved in powering cars, something that likely would be considered in a real-world discussion of how best to power cars.

However, the question is not asking for something worth considering.It asks for an assumption required for arriving at the conclusion, and the argument’s conclusion is not that powering cars by burning ethanol is the least expensive way to power cars.The conclusion is concerned with levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide.Arriving at the conclusion does not require assuming that there is not a way to power cars that is less expensive than burning ethanol.
Beware of the better plan / different plan answer choice, particularly when that other plan provides a solution to a problem that the plan outlined by the passage doesn’t seek to solve.

question.text: In general, the older a car is, the more pollution per mile traveled it produces.Often, when it becomes more expensive for someone in New Zealand to maintain a relatively old car than to purchase a newer, imported used car, the person opts to swap the relatively old car for a newer imported used car.To positively impact the pollution reduction achieved through the swapping of relatively old cars for newer imported used cars, the government of New Zealand changed regulations.Now, as a result, only the newest of used cars can legally be imported into New Zealand.In order for the government’s plan to have the desired effect, which of the following must be true?

answers.text: Because of the new regulations, more people in New Zealand will swap their older cars for newer used cars than would have were the new regulations not in place.
The reasons why newer cars produce less pollution than older cars are that newer cars have improved designs and that, as engines wear, cars run less efficiently.
The typical cost of the newest of imported used cars is not significantly greater than the typical cost of maintaining one of the oldest, least reliable cars in New Zealand.
The cost of maintaining older cars in New Zealand will not decrease from its current level.
People in New Zealand do not prefer to import less-new used cars rather than the newest of used cars.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: In general, the older a car is, the more pollution per mile traveled it produces.

Premise: Often, when it becomes more expensive for someone in New Zealand to maintain a relatively old car than to purchase a newer, imported used car, the person opts to swap the relatively old car for a newer, imported used car.
Premise: The government has changed regulations, so that only the newest of used cars can legally be imported into New Zealand.
Conclusion (desired effect of the plan): To positively impact the pollution reduction achieved through the swapping of relatively old cars for newer imported used cars.
(A) Because of the new regulations, more people in New Zealand will swap their older cars for newer used cars than would have were the new regulations not in place.

The government has a plan “to positively impact the pollution reduction achieved through the swapping of relatively old cars for newer imported used cars.” For the plan to work, all that needs to happen is for New Zealanders to continue doing what they already have been doing: swapping their relatively old cars for newer, imported used cars, which will be those of only the newest variety, thanks to the changed regulations.

In other words, it is not necessary formorepeople to swap their cars.Given the background presented in the passage, even if the exact same number of people swap their cars as were doing so before the regulation change, there will still be a reduction in pollution produced by automobiles.What this answer choice says does not have to be true in order for the government’s plan to work, and thus the statement is not a necessary assumption.
(B) The reasons why newer cars produce less pollution than older cars are that newer cars have improved designs and that, as engines wear, cars run less efficiently.
The passage already states that “the older a car is, the more pollution per mile traveled it produces.”
Now, this answer choiceexplains whynewer cars produce less pollution than older cars.

However, we’re not seeking an explanation for information provided in the passage.Rather, we’re seeking something that must be true in order for the plan to work, and it does not have to be the case that newer cars produce less pollution for the reasons presented by this choice in order for the plan to work.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The typical cost of the newest of imported used cars is not significantly greater than the typical cost of maintaining one of the oldest, least reliable cars in New Zealand.

Notice that one of the premises provided in the passage is that “when it becomes more expensive for someone in New Zealand to maintain a relatively old car than to purchase a newer, imported used car, the person opts to swap the relatively old car for a newer, imported used car.”

So, what if maintaining old cars costs less than purchasing one of the newest of used cars? In that case, the government’s plan would fail, because cost-conscious New Zealanders would not swap their old cars for newer, imported used cars, since only the newest of used cars would be available for them to purchase.
If you’re having trouble seeing this, you can negate what this answer choice says:
The typical cost of the newest of imported used cars IS significantly greater than the typical cost of maintaining one of the oldest, least reliable cars in New Zealand.
We see that what this answer choice says, that the newest of used carsis notcostlier, must be true for this argument to work.
(D) The cost of maintaining older cars in New Zealand will not decrease from its current level.
Let’s negate what this answer choice says:
The cost of maintaining older cars in New Zealand WILL decrease from its current level.

If this negation were true, we probably would see people holding onto their older cars for longer than they hold onto their older cars currently.However, when the time came for them to switch from a relatively old car to a newer, imported used cars, they still would be switching to only the newest of used cars.Therefore, the plan would still work.
Since the negation of this answer choice does not cause the argument to fall apart, what this answer choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.
YOUR CHOICE(E) People in New Zealand do not prefer to import less-new used cars rather than the newest of used cars.

Regardless of what New Zealanders prefer, the government has limited their choice of imported used cars to only the newest ones.The conclusion assumes not that people will prefer what is available but that the available cars will produce less pollution than was produced by the used cars that were available before the changing of the regulations.That could be true whether people prefer the cars available as a result of the regulations or don’t.Thus, what this answer choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.

question.text: Sartania State Governor: People have criticized the policies of my administration, saying that the policies have resulted in poor job growth.This criticism is misguided, as businesses in Sartania increased the total number of people employed in the state by 50,000 over the past year.Compare that result with the average rate of job growth seen in neighboring states for the past year, a poor one percent.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the governor’s argument depends?

answers.text: No businesses in Sartania laid off significant numbers of workers during the past year.
Twelve months ago, there were fewer than 5 million people employed in Sartania.
The policies of the governor’s administration were material factors causing the increase in the number of jobs in Sartania.
During the current year, the number of jobs in Sartania will increase by 50,000 again.
The rate of unemployment in Sartania is now lower than that of any neighboring state.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background information: People have based criticism of the administration’s policies on the idea that the policies have resulted in poor job growth.
Premise: Neighboring states have had poor job growth, defined as one percent growth over the past year.
Premise: Over the past year, businesses in Sartania increased the number of jobs in the state by 50,000.
Conclusion: The criticism is misguided.

The governor’s conclusion is based on a comparison, one percent job growth in neighboring states versus an increase of 50,000 jobs in Sartania.There is a clear issue in this argument; there is a gap in the comparison, as the governor compares apercentage increasein neighboring states with anincrease in the absolute numberof jobs in Sartania.The governor’s argument requires assuming that the absolute number increase in Sartania represents a percentage increase greater than the percentage increase in neighboring states.
(A) No businesses in Sartania laid off significant numbers of workers during the past year.

This answer is a classic Assumption question trap, a statement that, if untrue, could seem to undermine the governor’s argument that Sartania’s job growth has not been poor and that, therefore, the criticism is misguided.After all, significant layoffs could result in poor job growth.

However, the argument includes the premise that the total number of people employed in Sartania increased by 50,000 over the year.In other words, as a result of all the hiring and firing that went on during the year, the number employed increased by 50,000.So, the 50,000 change already includes the effects of any layoffs that occurred.Thus, regardless of whether this answer is true, the premise holds and the argument works.Remember, any premise provided in an argument must be considered factual.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Twelve months ago, there were fewer than 5 million people employed in Sartania.

The connection between this answer choice and the argument may not be clear at first glance.Notice, however, the relationship between 50,000, the increase in people employed that is mentioned in the argument, and 5 million, the number mentioned in this choice: 50,000 is one percent of 5 million.

So, if 5 million people were employed in Sartania at the beginning of the year, and the number employed increased by 50,000, then the number employed in Sartania would have increased by one percent, a change the governor termed “poor.” In other words, if there were 5 million or more people employed at the beginning of the year, then the governor’s argument would not be supported.

In order for the governor’s comparison to serve as support for the argument, job growth in Sartania had to be greater than one percent, and it was greater than one percent only if 50,000 is more than one percent of the number of people who were employed in Sartania at the beginning of last year.
Thus, for the argument to work, it has to be the case that fewer than 5 million people were employed in Sartania at the beginning of last year.
(C) The policies of the governor’s administration were material factors causing the increase in the number of jobs in Sartania.

While the governor’s argument is a response to criticism of the administration’s policies, the argument is not based on the idea that the policies were material factors causing the increase in the number employed in Sartania.Rather, the governor’s point is thathis administration’s policiesdid not result in poor job growth, because the job growth experienced by Sartania was not poor in comparison with that experienced by neighboring states.So, it does not have to be the case that the policies were the cause of the job growth in order for the argument to work.The argument could workeven if the policiesdidn’t cause anything, as long as they didn’t result in poor job growth.
(D) During the current year, the number of jobs in Sartania will increase by 50,000 again.

Both the criticism of the administration and the governor’s argument are based on past results, not future results.Further, we have no solid basis for concluding that the number of people employed in Sartania has to increase by 50,000 per year in order for job growth to be considered “good.” Therefore, there is no solid basis for saying that another 50,000 increase in jobs has to occur in order for the governor’s argument to work.
(E) The rate of unemployment in Sartania is now lower than that of any neighboring state.

This choice could be tempting, because the governor’s argument is based on a comparison between job creation in Sartania and job creation in neighboring states.However, the argument is not about unemployment rates but, rather, about job growth, and while job growth affects unemployment rates, job growth in Sartania could have been relatively strong even if the resulting unemployment rate is not relatively low.So, the comparison between unemployment rates expressed by this choice does not affect the conclusion of the argument.

question.text: When, in recent years, large pieces have broken off Antarctic ice shelves and become floating icebergs, people have voiced concerns that disintegration of the ice shelves will lead to rises in ocean water levels.These concerns are unfounded.When ice shelves break apart into icebergs, ice is not introduced into the oceans, as the ice shelves themselves are floating in the ocean.Even when icebergs melt, the release of water from melting icebergs does not add to the water in the oceans, as the icebergs’ water is already in the oceans, in the form of floating ice.Which of the following, if true, is an assumption upon which the above argument depends?

answers.text: Icebergs that are floating free in the ocean do not melt faster than ice that is part of an ice shelf.
Over time, ice shelves that have broken up will be replaced by newly formed ice shelves.
The presence of Antarctic ice shelves does not prevent large ice formations on land from breaking down and thus releasing water into the ocean.
The rate at which ice shelves have been breaking apart in recent years is not faster than the rate at which they were breaking apart previously.
The breaking apart of ice shelves does not result in significant habitat changes that could disturb the biological balance of the oceans.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: People have voiced concerns that disintegration of the Antarctic ice shelves will lead to rises in ocean water levels.
Premise: When ice shelves break apart into icebergs, ice is not introduced into the oceans.
Premise: When icebergs melt, the release of water from melting icebergs does not add to the water in the oceans.
Conclusion: The concerns are unfounded.

While the conclusion about the concerns about ice shelf disintegration seems well supported by the presented facts about ice shelf disintegration and melting of icebergs, there could be consequences of ice shelf disintegration that are not mentioned in the passage, consequences that somehow cause ocean water levels to rise.So, in supporting the conclusion by using the presented facts, the argument relies on the assumption that there aren’t unmentioned consequences of ice shelf disintegration thatcouldlead to rises in ocean water levels.
(A) Icebergs that are floating free in the ocean do not melt faster than ice that is part of an ice shelf.

This answer could seem correct if one disregarded or missed the fact that the passage has already stated that the melting of the icebergs does not add water to the oceans.The argument does not rely on the assumption that free-floating icebergs melt at the same speed as ice that is part of an ice shelf; the argument is based on the fact that the melting of icebergs, whether slow or fast, does not affect ocean water levels.
(B) Over time, ice shelves that have broken up will be replaced by newly formed ice shelves.

Coming to the argument’s conclusion does not require assuming that new ice shelves will form, as the argument is supported by information showing that ocean water levels would not change even if the ice shelves completely disintegrated and melted.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The presence of Antarctic ice shelves does not prevent large ice formations on land from breaking down and thus releasing water into the ocean.

The conclusion of the argument relies on the assumption that there is not some other consequence of ice shelf disintegration such that the disintegration will result in ocean water level rises.This answer mentions a specific consequence.

If the presence of ice shelves prevents large ice formations on land from breaking down and, thus, releasing water into the ocean, then ice shelf disintegration would allow for that release of water, a consequence that would result in rises in ocean water levels.

The argument therefore rests on the assumption that this secondary process wouldnotoccur as a consequence of the ice shelf disintegration.In other words, the argument relies on the assumption that the presence of Antarctic ice shelves does not prevent ice formations on land from breaking down and releasing water into the ocean.
(D) The rate at which ice shelves have been breaking apart in recent years is not faster than the rate at which they were breaking apart previously.

If, as the passage says, ice shelf disintegration does not cause water to be added to the oceans, then the rate at which ice shelf disintegration occurs makes no difference in ocean water levels.What this answer choice says has no bearing on the argument.
(E) The breaking apart of ice shelves does not result in significant habitat changes that could disturb the biological balance of the oceans.

This answer choice could be tempting if we were to lose track of the argument’s conclusion.If we were to think that the argument’s conclusion is that there is no reason to be concerned about ice shelf disintegration, then we might reasonably think that the argument relies on the assumption that such disintegration does not disturb the biological balance of the oceans.

However, the argument’s conclusion is not that there is no reason to be concerned in general about the disintegration but, rather, that concerns that ice shelf disintegration will result in rises in ocean water levels are unfounded.What this answer choice says has no bearing on the argument.

question.text: During times of natural disaster when telephone wires are severed, citizens generally seek to contact emergency services by using their cell phones to make voice calls.However, there is a limit to the number of calls that cell towers can process.So, when citizens most need to reach such services, their calls may not go through.A solution is to implement an emergency text messaging system, because there is almost no limit to the number of text messages that cell towers can process.With such a system in place, citizens could be sure to reach emergency services during natural disasters.Each of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends, EXCEPT:

answers.text: During a natural disaster, emergency services personnel would be able to effectively respond in a timely manner to most, if not all, of the text messages they received.
Citizens who would seek to reach emergency services are aware of how to use the text messaging feature of their cell phones.
During a natural disaster, citizens’ cell phones would have sufficient battery power to send text messages.
Cell phone towers would not be damaged in ways such that the towers would cease to operate during a natural disaster.
Natural disasters do not significantly interfere with the signals that cell phones transmit in sending text messages.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: During times of natural disaster when telephone wires are severed, citizens generally seek to contact emergency services by using their cell phones to make voice calls.
Background: Because, there is a limit to the number of calls that cell towers can process, when citizens most need to reach such services, their calls may not go through.
Premise: There is almost no limit to the number of text messages that cell towers can process.
Conclusion: If an emergency text messaging system were in place, citizens could be sure to reach emergency services during natural disasters.

To correctly answer this question, we need to pay close attention to the details of the argument’s conclusion that “if an emergency text messaging system were implemented, citizens could be suretoreachemergency services during natural disasters.” Notice that this conclusion is only about the ability of citizens to “reach” emergency services during natural disasters.No claims are made about what will happen after citizens reach emergency services.

Because this is an “EXCEPT” question, four of the answer choices WILL be assumptions on which the argument depends; these answers will be incorrect.The correct answer will NOT be an assumption on which the argument relies.
YOUR CHOICE(A) During a natural disaster, emergency services personnel would be able to effectively respond in a timely manner to most, if not all, of the text messages they received.

Let’s revisit the conclusion, which is that “citizens could be sure toreachemergency services during natural disasters.” Notice that the conclusion is not about emergency services personnel’s being able to effectivelyrespondto the text messages that reach them.The conclusion is merely that citizens’ text messages will reliably reach emergency services personnel.Therefore, what this answer choice says is not an assumption on which this argument depends.
If you have a hard time seeing this, you could always negate the statement to see whether this negation has any impact on the argument:
During a natural disaster, emergency services personnel would NOT be able to effectively respond in a timely manner to most, if not all, of the text messages they received.
Does this negated statement affect the strength of the argument? No, because the author does not make any claims about emergency services’ ability to respond to the text messages they receive.
Assumption upon which the argument relies? No.
(B) Citizens who would seek to reach emergency services are aware of how to use the text messaging feature of their cell phones.

What this answer choice says is an assumption on which the argument relies.After all, if the argument makes the claim that “citizens couldbe sure to reachemergency services during natural disasters” if a text messaging system were implemented, then the author is certainly assuming that citizens who would seek to reach emergency services are aware of how to use the text messaging feature of their cell phones.If citizens werenotaware of how to use text messaging, then the plan of implementing a text messaging system would fail, right?
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.
(C) During a natural disaster, citizens’ cell phones would have sufficient battery power to send text messages.

What this answer choice says is an assumption on which the argument relies.After all, if the argument makes the claim that “citizens couldbe sure toreachemergency services during natural disasters” if a text messaging system were implemented, then the author is certainly assuming that citizens’ cell phones would have sufficient battery power to send text messages.If citizens’ cell phones would NOT have sufficient battery power to send text messages, then the plan of implementing a text messaging system would fail, right?
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.
(D) Cell phone towers would not be damaged in ways such that the towers would cease to operate during a natural disaster.

What this answer choice says is an assumption on which the argument relies.After all, if the argument makes the claim that “citizens couldbe sure toreachemergency services during natural disasters” if a text messaging system were implemented, then the author is certainly assuming that cell phone towers would not be damaged in ways such that the towers would cease to operate during a natural disaster.If cell phone towers WOULD be damaged in ways such that the towers would cease to operate during a natural disaster, then the plan of implementing a text messaging system would fail, right?
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.
(E) Natural disasters do not significantly interfere with the signals that cell phones transmit in sending text messages.

What this answer choice says is an assumption on which the argument relies.After all, if the argument makes the claim that “citizens could besure toreachemergency services during natural disasters” if a text messaging system were implemented, then the author is certainly assuming that natural disasters do not significantly interfere with the signals that cell phones transmit in sending text messages.If natural disasters DID significantly interfere with the signals that cell phones transmit in sending text messages, then the plan of implementing a text messaging system would fail, right?
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.

question.text: In order to give fish populations an opportunity to recover, the government of Rhensland plans to designate roughly 25 percent of the waters off the coast of Rhensland fish sanctuary areas, in which fishing is not allowed.Officials theorize that, in doing so, the government will protect at least 25 percent of the fish currently inhabiting the waters off Rhensland from fishing operations.Which of the following is an assumption made by the officials in theorizing about the results of designating fish sanctuary areas?

answers.text: Growth of fish populations in the sanctuary areas will not cause the fish inhabiting sanctuary areas to exceed 25 percent of all fish inhabiting waters off Rhensland.
The areas not designated fish sanctuary areas will not be composed of habitat that is, on average, significantly more attractive to fish than habitat found in the sanctuary areas is.
Fish that currently inhabit non-sanctuary areas will not learn to avoid fishing boats by moving to sanctuary areas.
Most Rhensland-based fishing boat operators will be able to continue to operate profitably even though the waters available for them to fish in will have been significantly reduced.
Those fishing in waters off Rhensland will not seek to compensate for the inability to fish in sanctuary areas by increasing the intensity with which they fish in non-sanctuary areas.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: In order to give fish populations an opportunity to recover, the government of Rhensland plans to designate 25 percent of the waters off the coast of Rhensland fish sanctuary areas, in which fishing is not allowed.
Conclusion: Environmental officials theorize that, in doing so, the government will protect at least 25 percent of the fish currently inhabiting the waters off Rhensland from fishing operations.

Notice that the government of Rhensland is proposing a plan.In proposing this plan, the government is assuming that the plan will work as intended and that there will not be un anticipated things that derail the plan.
(A) Growth of fish populations in the sanctuary areas will not cause the fish inhabiting sanctuary areas to exceed 25 percent of all fish inhabiting waters off Rhensland.

As long as the government protects at least 25 percent of the fish currently inhabiting the waters off Rhensland, then the plan will work, regardless of what percentage the fish inhabiting sanctuary areas become of the total fish inhabiting waters off Rhensland.To see this more clearly, let’s negate what this answer choice says:
Growth of fish populations in the sanctuary areas WILL cause the fish that inhabit sanctuary areas to exceed 25 percent of all fish inhabiting waters off Rhensland.

Does this negated statement invalidate the argument? No.In fact, itstrengthensthe argument because the conclusion is that “the government will protectat least25 percent of the fish currently inhabiting the waters off Rhensland.” Therefore, if the plan protects more than 25 percent of all fish, then the plan is still a success.
Thus, in order for the argument to remain valid, we need not assume that fish inhabiting sanctuary areas will not exceed 25 percent of all fish inhabiting waters off Rhensland.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The areas not designated fish sanctuary areas will not be composed of habitat that is, on average, significantly more attractive to fish than habitat found in the sanctuary areas is.
Remember that the government believes that its plan will work and that it has not missed something that will adversely affect its plan.

What if the areas not designated fish sanctuaries ARE composed of habitat that is, on average, more attractive to fish than habitat in the sanctuaries is? In that case, there may not be that many fish that inhabit the sanctuary areas.As a result, the government may not actually protect at least 25 percent of the fish, as theorized.Therefore, what this answer choice says is an assumption on which the officials’ theory depends, because if this assumption isn’t true, the argument falls apart.
(C) Fish that currently inhabit non-sanctuary areas will not learn to avoid fishing boats by moving to sanctuary areas.
If we negate what this answer choice says, we see that we actually strengthen the argument:
Fish that currently inhabit non-sanctuary areas WILL learn to avoid fishing boats by moving to sanctuary areas.

If more fish move to the sanctuary areas, then the government’s prediction that it will protect at least 25 percent of the fish becomes more plausible.Thus, what this answer choice says does not need to be true in order for the argument to work, and so, this choice is not an assumption upon which this argument depends.
(D) Most Rhensland-based fishing boat operators will be able to continue to operate profitably even though the waters available for them to fish in will have been significantly reduced.

As always, it’s important to focus on the conclusion, which is that“the government will protect at least 25 percent of the fish currently inhabiting the waters off Rhensland from fishing operations.” This goal could be accomplished even if most boat operators willnotbe able to continue to operate profitably.In other words, what this answer choice says does not have to be true in order for the conclusion to remain valid.Thus, this choice is not an assumption upon which this argument depends.
(E) Those fishing in waters off Rhensland will not seek to compensate for the inability to fish in sanctuary areas by increasing the intensity with which they fish in non-sanctuary areas.

To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to be clear about the effects of intensifying fishing in non-sanctuary areas and see that, while depletion of fish populations in non-sanctuary areas may not be in line with the government's aims, that depletion may not directly conflict with the stated goal of protecting 25 percent of fish.

The government's aim is to protect 25 percent of the fish inhabiting the waters off Rhensland.While, because of increased fishing in the non-sanctuary areas, the plan may result in the depletion of fish in non-sanctuary areas, the fish in sanctuary areas will be protected.So, to the degree that 25 percent of the fish inhabit the 25 percent of the waters designated sanctuary areas, 25 percent of the fish will be protected regardless of the intensity of fishing in non-sanctuary areas.Thus, what this answer choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument relies.

question.text: Newly Elected President: In running for president, I said that I would take a variety of actions, which included cutting taxes, reducing regulations, and increasing spending on infrastructure.Given that a majority of the voters voted to elect me president rather than to reelect the previous president, it must be the case that most voters are in favor of my taking the actions that I talked about during my campaign.Which of the following is an assumption that the newly elected president made in drawing her conclusion?

answers.text: There are not actions that are more important to voters than are the ones the newly elected president included in her platform.
A survey of voters found that most feel that cutting taxes, reducing regulations, and increasing spending on infrastructure make sense.
Voters were not in favor of the actions that made up the previous president’s platform.
If the newly elected president’s platform had included a set of actions different from the set it included, the opposing candidate would have won the election.
Using their votes to express dissatisfaction with the previous president’s policies was not the only concern of the vast majority of people who voted for the new president.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In running for president, I said that I would take a variety of actions, which included cutting taxes, reducing regulations, and increasing spending on infrastructure.
Premise: A majority of the voters voted to elect me president rather than to reelect the previous president.
Conclusion: It must be the case that most voters are in favor of my taking the actions that I talked about during my campaign.
(A) There are not actions that are more important to voters than are the ones the newly elected president included in her platform.

This answer is a tempting trap because the conclusion is that voters must be in favor of the president’s taking the actions that she said she would, and this choice is about how important those actions are to voters.

However, the president’s conclusion is not that the actions she promised are the onesmostimportant to voters but simply that the voters are in favor of her taking the actions.Concluding that the voters are in favor of her taking the actions does not require assuming that the actions are more important to voters than are any other actions.
(B) A survey of voters found that most feel that cutting taxes, reducing regulations, and increasing spending on infrastructure make sense.
This choice adds support for the newly elected president’s conclusion but is not an assumption the new president would need to make to reach her conclusion.
(C) Voters were not in favor of the actions that made up the previous president’s platform.

While the conclusion is based in part on the premise that voters selected the newly elected president over the previous president, concluding that the voters are in favor of the actions that the new president promised does not require assuming that voters were not in favor of the actions included in the previous president’s platform.
YOUR CHOICE(D) If the newly elected president’s platform had included a set of actions different from the set it included, the opposing candidate would have won the election.

A test-taker who has already eliminated choices A, B, and C might be tempted to choose this answer because it seems to make sense that, if voters elected the new president because she promised certain actions, then they would not have voted for her had her platform included a different set of actions.

However, the conclusion is simply that the voters are in favor of the actions that the new president promised while campaigning.Even if they are in favor of the actions she promised, they might have elected her had she mentioned an entirely different set of actions.
So, concluding that voters are in favor of the actions does not require assuming that they wouldn’t have elected her had her platform included a different set of actions.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Using their votes to express dissatisfaction with the previous president’s policies was not the only concern of the vast majority of people who voted for the new president.

This statement has to be true in order for the argument to work.If the vast majority of voters voted for the newly elected president because they were concerned solely with using their votes to express dissatisfaction with the previous president’s policies, then the fact that the voters elected the new president would not indicate that they are in favor of the actions she promised.

So, going from the premises to the conclusion requires assuming that voters voted for the new president for reasons other than using their votes to express dissatisfaction with the previous president’s policies.

question.text: Politician: Clearly, there has been an increase in the percentage of people who are willing to fraudulently receive disability benefits rather than work.Over the last 30 years, the percentage of people living on government disability benefits has increased significantly.However, over the same period of time, medical statistics have shown little change in the incidences of health issues that would render people unable to work.Which of the following isnotan assumption upon which the politician’s argument depends?

answers.text: The legal definition of disability has not been expanded.
Technological advances have not made it possible for people previously considered disabled to work full time.
The percentage of people who could legally receive disability benefits but who remain employed instead has not decreased.
It is not the case that a greater percentage of people are entering the work force and thus becoming eligible for disability benefits.
The maximum age at which a person can be eligible to receive disability benefits has not been increased.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Over the last 30 years, the percentage of people living on government disability benefits has increased significantly.
Premise: Over the same period of time, medical statistics have shown little change in the incidences of health issues that would render people unable to work.
Conclusion: Clearly, there has been an increase in the percentage of people who are willing to fraudulently receive disability benefits rather than work.

The author makes the bold conclusion that there has been an increase in the percentage of people who are willing to fraudulently receive disability benefits rather than work.Of course, in arriving at that conclusion, she is making number of assumptions that are integral to her argument.For example, isn’t she assuming that there is no other reason for the observed increase in the percentage of people living on government disability benefits?
Remember, since this question asks which choice isnotan assumption upon which the argument depends, any choice that states such an assumption will not be correct.
(A) The legal definition of disability has not been expanded.
What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument relies.

What if the legal definition of disability HAS been expanded? Wouldn’t this fact support an alternative explanation for why, over the last 30 years, the percentage of people living on government disability benefits has increased significantly, while medical statistics have shown little change in the incidences of health issues that would render people unable to work.

If the definition of disability has been expanded, then we would expect to see more people who are considered disabled.With more people considered disabled, we’d expect to see a higher percentage of people living on government disability benefits, even if the percentage who were willing to do so fraudulently has not increased.
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Technological advances have not made it possible for people previously considered disabled to work full time.

Concluding that there has been an increase in the percentage of people who are willing to fraudulently receive disability benefits rather than work does not require assuming that technological advances haven’t made working full time possible for people who were previously considered disabled.
To clearly see why this choice does not state an assumption integral to the argument, let’s negate this choice:
Technological advances HAVE made it possible for people previously considered disabled to work full time.

Clearly, this negation would not weaken the case for believing the argument’s conclusion.The fact that people previously considered disabled can now work certainly does not conflict with the conclusion that the percentage of people who are willing to live on disability benefits fraudulently has increased.
We can see that what this choice says doesnothave to be assumed to be true in order for the argument to work, and therefore, this choice is our correct answer.
Assumption upon which the argument relies? No.
(C) The percentage of people who could legally receive disability benefits but who remain employed instead has not decreased.
What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument relies.
What if the percentage of people who could legally receive disability benefits but who remain employed instead HAS decreased?

Wouldn’t this fact support an alternative explanation for why, over the last 30 years, the percentage of people living on government disability benefits has increased significantly, while medical statistics have shown little change in the incidences of health issues that would render people unable to work.

In that situation, it could be the case that the reason for the increase in the percentage of people living on government disability benefits is not that the percentage who were willing to do so fraudulently has increased but rather that the percentage of those who can do so legally who are deciding to do so has increased.
So, the author must be assuming that the percentage of people who could legally receive disability benefits but who remain employed instead has NOT decreased.
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes
(D) It is not the case that a greater percentage of people are entering the work force and thus becoming eligible for disability benefits.
What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument relies.

What if it were the case that a greater percentage of people ARE entering the work force and thus becoming eligible for disability benefits? In that situation, we’d expect to see an increase in the percentage of people living on government disability benefits.In other words, in that situation we’d have an alternative explanation for the phenomenon observed.

So, in arguing that there has been an increase in the percentage of people who are willing to fraudulently receive disability benefits rather than work, the author must be assuming that it is NOT the case that a greater percentage of people are entering the work force and thus becoming eligible for disability benefits.
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.
(E) The maximum age at which a person can be eligible to receive disability benefits has not been increased.
What this answer choice says is an assumption upon which the argument relies.

What if the maximum age at which a person can be eligible to receive disability benefits HAS been increased? In that situation, we’d expect to see an increase in the percentage of people living on government disability benefits.In other words, in that situation we’d have an alternative explanation for the phenomenon observed.

Thus, in arguing that there has been an increase in the percentage of people who are willing to fraudulently receive disability benefits rather than work, the author is making the assumption that the maximum age at which a person can be eligible to receive disability benefits has NOT been increased.
Assumption upon which the argument relies? Yes.

question.text: The town of Oceanside plans to incinerate its citizens’ trash and use the energy produced in the process to generate electricity.Engineers who have analyzed the amount and composition of the trash that Oceanside’s citizens discarded during the last three years have estimated that, through incinerating that trash, in each of those years, the town would have saved at least 4 million dollars that it would have otherwise spent on energy used to light classrooms, cool offices, and power equipment.The town predicts, therefore, that by implementing the plan, it will save at least 4 million dollars per year.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the accuracy of the prediction depends?

answers.text: The amount of energy that the town uses in lighting classrooms, cooling offices, and powering equipment will not increase substantially over time.
After the trash incineration plan has been implemented, citizens will not begin putting out greater amounts of trash than they are putting out now.
Currently, the town spends a significant amount of money on disposal of trash that could be instead incinerated.
The unit cost that the town faces in purchasing energy in the open market will not decrease significantly.
There is no way for the town to save at least 4 million dollars per year on its energy bills that would be more environmentally friendly than a trash incineration program.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Oceanside plans to incinerate its citizens’ trash and use the energy produced in the process to generate electricity.

Premise: Analysis of the trash that Oceanside’s citizens discarded during the last three years indicates that, through incinerating that trash, in each of those years, the town would have saved at least 4 million dollars that it would have otherwise spent.
Conclusion: By implementing the plan, the town will save at least 4 million dollars per year.
So, we have a plan.The town, of course, assumes the plan will work.Why?
(A) The amount of energy that the town uses in lighting classrooms, cooling offices, and powering equipment will not increase substantially over time.
That the amount of energy that the town uses in lighting classrooms, cooling offices, and powering equipment will not increase substantially over time is not relevant to the argument.
