The argument’s conclusion is that, by implementing the plan, the town will save at least 4 million dollars per year.The amount the town expects to save is based on the amount of trash incinerated, not on the amount of energy used.Thus, regardless of whether the town increases its energy use, as long as people do not decrease the amount or change the composition of trash they put out, the town can incinerate that trash to save at least 4 million dollars per year.In other words, the plan will still work.
(B) After the trash incineration plan has been implemented, citizens will not begin putting out greater amounts of trash than they are putting out now.

The plan is to saveat least4 million dollars per year, which is an estimate based on amount and composition of trash from the past three years.Presumably, if trash levels increase, that 4 million dollar target will be even easier to meet.Thus, in order for the argument to work, it does not have to be true that citizens willnotbegin putting out greater amounts of trash than they are putting out now.After all, if plan works at the current level of trash disposal, then it will work at higher levels of trash disposal as well.
(C) Currently, the town spends a significant amount of money on disposal of trash that could be instead incinerated.

Notice that the plan is not based on reducing what is spent on trash disposal.Instead, the plan is based on saving money by generating energy via incinerating trash.Since the amount of savings that the town predicts does not depend on what the town currently spends, what this answer choice says is not an assumption upon which the accuracy of the prediction depends.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The unit cost that the town faces in purchasing energy in the open market will not decrease significantly.

The conclusion is that, by implementing the plan, the town will save at least 4 million dollars per year.But, imagine if the town implemented the plan and the unit cost of energy dropped 90 percent? In this situation, couldn’t the town end up saving a lot less than 4 million dollars? Definitely.
Let’s negate the statement:
The unit cost that the town faces in purchasing energy in the open market WILL decrease significantly.

This information would destroy the case for the prediction about saving 4 million dollars per year, because, if the market cost of energy decreases, the town won’t, by generating energy by incinerating trash, save as much as it would have had the cost of energy remained at its current level.In other words, if the cost of buying energy decreases, the savings the town will achieve by buying less energy will be smaller than expected.
Thus, what this answer choice says has to be true in order for the prediction to be accurate.
(E) There is no way for the town to save at least 4 million dollars per year on its energy bills that would be more environmentally friendly than a trash incineration program.
Beware of the “better or different plan” trap.The author does not claim that no better plans exist.The author simply claims that the proposed plan will save the town at least 4 million dollars.
Let’s negate the statement to more clearly see this trap:
There IS a way for the town to save at least 4 million dollars per year on its energy bills that would be more environmentally friendly than a trash incineration program.
This negation does not affect the case for the prediction about reducing costs.Thus, this answer choice is not an assumption upon which the accuracy of the prediction depends.

question.text: It must be the case that eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates is beneficial for health.Data generated by a recently completed study indicate that people who live on low carbohydrate diets tend to have lifespans shorter than those of people who consistently eat moderate amounts of carbohydrates.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument above depends?

answers.text: Most people on low carbohydrate diets do not compensate for their low carbohydrate consumption by consuming greater than average amounts of animal products known to be detrimental to health.
People who follow low carbohydrate diets would not be able to extend their lifespans by exercising regularly and refraining from engaging in unhealthy activities.
People who follow low carbohydrate diets do not live longer on average than people whose diets are unusually high in carbohydrates do.
Few people whose diets are low in carbohydrates live longer than the average lifespan of people who regularly consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates.
People who regularly consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates would not shorten their life expectancies were they to reduce the amounts of carbohydrates that they regularly consume.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Data generated by a recently completed study indicate that people who live on low carbohydrate diets tend to have lifespans shorter than those of people who consistently eat moderate amounts of carbohydrates.
Conclusion: It must be the case that eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates is beneficial for health.

We see that the author is making a cause-and-effect claim.As we know, we must be skeptical of any such claim, asking ourselves whether the supposed cause is really the reason for the effect.Could there be another explanation for the given phenomenon? The author is assuming that there is not.

YOUR CHOICE(A) Most people on low carbohydrate diets do not compensate for their low carbohydrate consumption by consuming greater than average amounts of animal products known to be detrimental to health.

The author makes the cause-and-effect claim that eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates is beneficial for health, since people on low carbohydrate diets have lifespans shorter than those of people who eat moderate amounts of carbohydrates.Essentially, he assumes that the moderate amounts of carbohydrates are the cause of the longer lifespans, meaning that he assumes that there is not an alternative explanation for observation he presents.If we negate this answer choice, we can see the plausible alternative that the author overlooks.

If itweretrue that most people on low carbohydrate diets compensate by consuming greater than average amounts of animal products known to be detrimental to health, then we could attribute those people’s shorter lifespans to their animal product consumption rather than to their low carbohydrate consumption.Thus, the author must be assuming that people do not compensate for low carbohydrate consumption by consuming more animal products known to be detrimental to health.
(B) People who follow low carbohydrate diets would not be able to extend their lifespans by exercising regularly and refraining from engaging in unhealthy activities.

We must resist the urge to choose this classic type of trap, which we often refer to as “the better / different plan trap.” After all, the author never makes the claim that people could not extend their lifespans by engaging in other healthy activities; he simply says that it must be the case that eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates is beneficial for health.He need not assume that this “plan,” eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates, is the only way or the best way to be healthier in order to conclude that eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates is beneficial for health.
If you have a hard time seeing this, we can negate what this answer choice says:
People who follow low carbohydrate diets WOULD be able to extend their lifespans by exercising regularly and refraining from engaging in unhealthy activities.
The argument is still perfectly valid because the author never makes the claim that eating moderate amounts of carbohydrates is the only way to be healthy.It is simply one way.
(C) People who follow low carbohydrate diets do not live longer on average than people whose diets are unusually high in carbohydrates do.
The passage does not address the health of people whose diets are “unusually high” in carbohydrates, nor does it make claims about the longevity of such people.
What happens if we negate the statement?
People who follow low carbohydrate diets DO live longer on average than do people whose diets are unusually high in carbohydrates.

We can see that this negated statement does not affect the validity of the argument, which concludes that “eatingmoderateamounts of carbohydrates is beneficial for health.” The effect of eating unusually high amounts of carbohydrates is not relevant to the argument.
(D) Few people whose diets are low in carbohydrates live longer than the average lifespan of people who regularly consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates.

This answer choice is tempting because what it says seems similar to the information that the argument uses to support the conclusion.However, this choice is not an assumption necessary for supporting the conclusion.

The author supports the conclusion via the use of data indicating that people on low carbohydrate dietstend tohave lifespans shorter than those of people who consistently consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates.That general information will support the conclusion regardless of whether there are cases don’t match the overall pattern.

For instance, even though,in general, people on low carbohydrate diets have lifespans shorter than those of people who consistently consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates, it could be the case thatsome,or even many, people on low carbohydrate diets have lifespans much longer than those of most people who consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates.In that hypothetical situation, it would still be the case that theaveragelifespans of those who consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates are longer than the lifespans of people on low carbohydrate diets.Every person need not follow the pattern in order for there to be an overall tendency.

So, using the given evidence to support the argument does not require assuming that few people whose diets are low in carbohydrates live longer than the average lifespan of people who regularly consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates.
(E) People who regularly consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates would not shorten their life expectancies were they to reduce the amounts of carbohydrates that they regularly consume.
What this choice says goes in entirely the wrong direction, as it is in conflict with the argument’s conclusion.

The point of the argument is that consuming moderate amounts of carbohydrates makes people live longer.Implied by the argument is that peoplewouldshorten their life expectancies if they were to cease to consume moderate amounts of carbohydrates.Since that implication is the opposite of what this choice says, that people who regularly consume moderate amounts of carbohydrateswould notshorten their life expectancies if they were to reduce their carbohydrate consumption, clearly this answer choice is not an assumption upon which the argument depends.

question.text: Bernie: In our country, the current minimum wage of $7.25 per hour should be raised to $14.50 per hour.Only with such an increase could workers earning minimum wage begin to earn enough money each month to stay out of poverty.Jane: What you propose would have an effect opposite that which you seek; such an increase would surely result in a reduction in the average monthly income of people currently working for minimum wage, because to offset the wage increase you propose, businesses in our country would lay off workers earning minimum wage.In responding to Bernie, Jane makes which of the following assumptions?

answers.text: Bernie is incorrect in believing that workers earning the increased minimum wage he proposes could begin to earn enough each month to stay out of poverty.
Small businesses could effectively employ automation to keep operation levels constant while reducing the number of people they employ at an increased minimum wage.
If the current minimum wage of $7.25 per hour were raised to $14.50 per hour, businesses in the country would lay off more than 50 percent of workers earning minimum wage.
Businesses in the country do not have ways other than laying off workers to significantly reduce costs.
It is not the case that workers currently earning minimum wage will experience significant wage increases even if the minimum wage is not increased to $14.50 per hour.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of Jane’s argument:
Premise: To offset the $7.25 to $14.50 wage increase Bernie proposes, businesses in our country would lay off workers earning minimum wage.

Conclusion: What Bernie proposes would have an effect opposite that which he seeks; such an increase would surely result in a reduction in the average monthly income of people currently working for minimum wage.

Correctly answering an Assumption question typically requires noticing exactly what the author of the argument has concluded.In this case, Jane has concluded that the increase that Bernie has proposed would “result in a reduction in the average monthly income of people currently working for minimum wage.”
Jane’s conclusion abouta reduction in average monthly incomeis rather specific.What does she have to be assuming in order to arrive at that conclusion?
(A) Bernie is incorrect in believing that workers earning the increased minimum wage he proposes could begin to earn enough each month to stay out of poverty.

This choice is tempting, because Jane obviously disagrees with what Bernie has said.So, we could easily fall into the trap of deciding that Jane must be assuming that a key part of Bernie’s argument, the idea that people earning the proposed higher minimum wage would begin to earn enough to stay out of poverty, is incorrect.

However, the truth is that this choice completely misses the point of what Jane is saying.She’s not claiming thatpeople who would earnthat new, higher minimum wage would not earn enough to stay out of poverty.She’s claiming that some people would be laid off and, therefore,wouldn’t earn any wage at all, and that, therefore, the proposed increase in the minimum wage would result in a reduction in average earnings.

Going from the evidence, that people would be laid off, to the conclusion, that increasing the minimum wage would be counterproductive, does not require assuming that it is not true that someone earning the higher minimum wage would earn enough to begin to stay out of poverty.
(B) Small businesses could effectively employ automation to keep operation levels constant while reducing the number of people they employ at an increased minimum wage.
What this choice says could explain how or why businesses would lay off employees if the minimum wage were increased as Bernie proposes.Businesses could automate.

However, we are not looking for an explanation.We are looking for an assumption integral to Jane’s argument, and concluding that, because businesses would lay off employees, increasing the minimum wage would be counterproductive, does not require assuming that businesses could automate.

Jane has already stated as fact that businesses would offset the increase in the minimum wage by laying off employees.She does not have to assume that they could go about doing so by automating to use that already stated fact to support her conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) If the current minimum wage of $7.25 per hour were raised to $14.50 per hour, businesses in the country would lay off more than 50 percent of workers earning minimum wage.
Jane has stated a specific conclusion: the increase in the minimum wage proposed by Bernie would surely result in a reduction in theaverage monthly incomeof people currently working for minimum wage.

She supports that conclusion by saying that layoffs will occur.Notice, though, that, in order for layoffs to have the effect that Jane says they will have, a reduction in the average monthly income of people currently working for minimum wage, the layoffs have to affect at least a certain percentage of people.
What percentage would have to be laid off?

Well Bernie has proposed doubling the minimum wage from $7.25 to $14.50.If nothing else were to change, that increase in the minimum wage would result ina doubling of the average earningsof people earning minimum wage.

Now, in order for layoffs to offset that doubling and make the average lower, the layoffs would have to cause the total earnings of people earning the minimum wage to be reduced by more than in half.In order for layoffs to cause that reduction, more than half of the employees currently employed at minimum wage would have to be laid off.

So, in order for Jane to go from the premise that Bernie’s proposed doubling of the minimum wage would result in layoffs to the conclusion that the average income of people currently earning minimum wage would decrease, Jane has to assume that, to offset the wage increase, businesses in the country would lay off more than 50 percent of workers earning minimum wage.
(D) Businesses in the country do not have ways other than laying off workers to significantly reduce costs.

Jane has stated as fact that, to offset the wage increase Bernie has proposed, businesses in our country would lay off workers earning minimum wage.Having stated that fact, she can arrive at the conclusion that increasing the minimum wage would be counterproductive without assuming that businesses have no other way to reduce costs.

Even if businesses DO have other ways to significantly reduce costs, it could still be the case that, because businesses would lay off workers to offset the increase in costs associated with the minimum wage increase, increasing the minimum wage would be counterproductive.
YOUR CHOICE(E) It is not the case that workers currently earning minimum wage will experience significant wage increases even if the minimum wage is not increased to $14.50 per hour.

This choice is tempting, because one could think something along the lines of, “Jane must be assuming that wages won’t increase without a minimum wage increase, because, if they would, then increasing the minimum wage wouldn’t make a difference.Wages would have increased anyway.”

However, the truth is that “significant” wage increases over time are not the same as a sudden doubling of the minimum wage.A “significant” increase could be a 20 percent increase, which is much smaller than a 100 percent increase.So, clearly Jane does not have to be assuming that, without a doubling of the minimum wage, workers’ wages won’t increase significantly.

More generally, even if workers’ wages WILL increase significantly without the proposed minimum wage increase, it could still be the case that a sudden doubling of the minimum wage would be counterproductive.

question.text: Hedge Fund Manager: I propose that we short sell the stock of the company that makes Super Fitness Drink.According to the company’s advertising, people consuming Super Fitness Drink find that doing so helps them to attain their optimal weights.However, when we talked with people who regularly consume Super Fitness Drink, we found that only a small percentage of them had seen any significant changes in their weights over the past year.Super Fitness Drink must be a fraudulent product.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the hedge fund manager’s argument depends?

answers.text: Most people who regularly consume Super Fitness Drink feel that doing so is, by itself, sufficient to cause them to attain their optimal weights.
It is not possible to make a drink such that people who consume it regularly find that doing so helps them to attain their optimal weights.
Consuming Super Fitness Drink is not as effective in helping people attain their ideal weights as consuming other fitness-related drinks is.
Most people who consume Super Fitness Drink do not continue to do so regularly once they have attained their optimal weights.
Often, companies that make fitness-related drinks are fraudulent operations.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: According to the company’s advertising, people consuming Super Fitness Drink find that doing so helps them to attain their optimal weights.
Premise: We found that only a small percentage of people who regularly consume Super Fitness Drink had seen any significant changes in their weights over the past year.
Conclusion: Super Fitness Drink must be a fraudulent product.

In the scenario presented, the hedge fund manager proposes short selling the stock of the company that makes Super Fitness Drink.The hedge fund manager’s reasoning is that Super Fitness Drink’s advertising claims are unsupported, and thus the drink must be a fraudulent product.

The correct answer will make a statement that must be true in order for the hedge fund manager’s findings – that only a small percentage of people who consumed the drink saw significant changes in weight over the past year – to support the conclusion that the drink is a fraudulent product.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Most people who regularly consume Super Fitness Drink feel that doing so is, by itself, sufficient to cause them to attain their optimal weights.

The fact that most people believe that consuming Super Fitness Drink will cause them to attain their ideal weights while, according to the passage, research indicates that most people who consume the drink have seen no significant change in weight for a year may seem to confirm that the product is fraudulent.However, we are not looking for information that confirms the conclusion.We are looking for an assumption upon which the argument depends.

This choice is not an assumption upon which the argument depends, because the evidence – the advertising claim and the results reported by consumers – provide the hedge fund manager with a case for concluding that the drink is a fraudulent product regardless of what consumers of the drink believe the effects of consuming the drink to be.

Further, the belief that consuming Super Fitness Drink is,by itself, sufficient to causepeople to attain their optimal weights goes beyond the company’s advertising claim, which is that consuming Super Fitness Drinkhelpspeople to attain their ideal weights.Concluding that the drink is a fraudulent product does not require assuming that people believe that the product has effects that surpass the company’s claims.
(B) It is not possible to make a drink such that people who consume it regularly find that doing so helps them to attain their optimal weights.

While it may be that the hedge fund manager believes that it is not possible to make a drink that helps people to attain their ideal weights, the hedge fund manager does not have to assume that it is not possible to make such a drink in order to conclude that one particular drink of that type, Super Fitness Drink, is a fraudulent product.
(C) Consuming Super Fitness Drink is not as effective in helping people attain their ideal weights as consuming other fitness-related drinks is.

While this statement might add additional support for the conclusion that Super Fitness Drink is a fraudulent product, we are not looking for additional support for the conclusion.We are looking for information necessary for using the evidence already provided to support the conclusion.

It does not have to be true that Super Fitness Drink is less effective for the proposed purpose than other fitness-related drinks are in order for the evidence provided to support the conclusion.In fact, it could be that all fitness-related drinks, including Super Fitness Drink, are fraudulent products, and therefore all such drinks are equally ineffective, in which case Super Fitness drink would be both no less effective than other fitness-related drinks and fraudulent.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Most people who consume Super Fitness Drink do not continue to do so regularly once they have attained their optimal weights.

The hedge fund manager jumped from the fact that most of the Super Fitness Drink consumers surveyed didn’t experience significant weight changes to the conclusion that the product must be fraudulent, but what if most people who consume the drink are already at their optimal weights? Then, of course, for most people, consuming Super Fitness Drink would not result in significant weight changes.
How could it be that most people consuming the drink are already at their optimal weights? It could be that people continue to consume the drink even after they attain their optimal weights.

So, concluding that the drink must be a fraudulent product because most people consuming the drink experienced no significant weight change over the past year requires assuming that most people who consume the drink do not continue to consume it after they attain their optimal weights.
(E) Often, companies that make fitness-related drinks are fraudulent operations.

While this answer choice could seem to support the hedge fund manager’s conclusion, remember, we are not looking for support for the conclusion.We are looking for an assumption upon which the argument depends.

The conclusion that Super Fitness Drink is a fraudulent product does not depend on the assumption that companies that make such drinks are often fraudulent operations.Super Fitness Drink could be a fraudulent product even if it is very rare for a company that makes such drinks to be a fraudulent operation.

question.text: Raptors, birds that scan the ground for prey while soaring through the air, are particularly vulnerable to being hit by the blades of wind turbines used for power generation.As a result, every year, thousands of raptors are killed in collisions with wind turbines.Thus, while the increasing use of wind turbines for power generation may help to prevent further climate change, the increasing use of wind turbines is clearly detrimental to raptor populations.Which of the following must be true in order for the above conclusion to be correct?

answers.text: Most areas where winds are optimal for propelling turbines are prime areas of raptor habitat.
More raptors are killed each year in collisions with wind turbines than are killed in collisions with building windows.
Estimates of the number of raptors that collide with wind turbines each year do not vary widely.
Degradation of habitats that is caused by climate change does not pose a great threat to raptor populations.
The number of collisions between raptors and wind turbines can be greatly reduced via strategic placement of wind turbines and the use of new technology.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background (key detail of what the passage says): The increasing use of wind turbines helps to prevent further climate change.
Premise: Raptors are particularly vulnerable to being hit by the blades of wind turbines; as a result, thousands of raptors are killed in collisions with wind turbines every year.
Conclusion: Increasing use of wind turbines is clearly detrimental to raptor populations.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since many raptors are killed in collisions with wind turbines, the increasing use of wind turbines is clearly detrimental to raptor populations.While that reasoning sounds logical, maybe things aren’t actually so clear.The argument relies on some key assumptions, including the one stated by the correct answer.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Most areas where winds are optimal for propelling turbines are prime areas of raptor habitat.

While what this choice says may seem necessary for the argument to work, the truth is that this choice goes beyond what is necessary.In order for the conclusion to be correct, it does nothave to betrue thatmostareas optimal for turbine use are alsoprimeareas of raptor habitat.As long as some turbines are in areas where some raptors will collide with them, the argument works.

Remember, we are already given the premise that raptors are vulnerable to turbines and that thousands of raptors are killed by turbines every year, and we must take a given premise to be true.Thus, regardless of whether turbine placement often overlaps with raptor habitat or hardly ever does, the conclusion remains well supported.
(B) More raptors are killed each year in collisions with wind turbines than are killed in collisions with building windows.

The support for the conclusion of the argument does not depend on the assumption that more raptors are killed in collisions with wind turbines than are killed in any other way.The conclusion is based on the premise that thousands of raptors are killed by wind turbines.Whether that number is more or less than the number killed in other types of collisions does not affect the conclusion that turbines, in particular, pose a threat to the raptor population.
(C) Estimates of the number of raptors that collide with wind turbines each year do not vary widely.

While this choice is related to the facts presented in the argument, it does not state an assumption upon which the conclusion depends.The estimates could vary while still indicating that thousands of raptors collide with wind turbines each year.For example, if the estimates vary from 10,000 to 10 million per year, thousands of raptors still collide with wind turbines each year.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Degradation of habitats that is caused by climate change does not pose a great threat to raptor populations.

Among the statements in the argument is one that indicates that increasing use of wind turbines for power generation may help to prevent further climate change.While this statement about turbines and climate change seems to serve merely as nonessential background for the argument, as it turns out, this statement expresses something integral to arriving at the correct answer.

In order to conclude that the increasing use of wind turbines is “clearly detrimental” to raptor populations, one has to assume that there is no reason to believe that the turbine use may benefit raptor populations more than it hurts them.

However, if increasing use of wind turbines may help to prevent further climate change, and if climate change poses a great threat to raptor populations, then we have a reason to believe that the benefits of turbine use may outweigh the drawbacks.
Thus, the conclusion that turbines are “clearly detrimental” to raptor populations depends on the assumption that climate change does not pose a great threat to raptors.
(E) The number of collisions between raptors and wind turbines can be greatly reduced via strategic placement of wind turbines and the use of new technology.

We have to be careful not to be fooled into choosing this choice by the fact that this choice provides information pertinent to what is discussed in the passage, because the effect of this choice is basically the opposite of what we need.

By indicating that there are methods that can be used to significantly reduce the number of collisions between raptors and turbines, this choice does not express an assumption upon which the conclusion depends.Rather, this choice serves to call the conclusion into question.

question.text: An extensive study involved analyzing the lifestyle choices of people who have experienced cancer, and the results of the study indicate that, in approximately 42 percent of the cases examined, the people who experienced cancer had been making lifestyle choices known to be unhealthy.It must be that, in approximately 42 percent of cases, the manifestation of cancer results from people’s lifestyle choices, and, in the remaining cases, it does not.Which of the following is an assumption made in arriving at the above conclusion?

answers.text: People make unhealthy lifestyle choices even if they are aware that making such choices may result in their experiencing cancer.
Through getting people to make healthier lifestyle choices, it would be possible to prevent approximately 42 percent of cancer cases.
There is no way to prevent the manifestation of cancer in cases in which it is not caused by unhealthy lifestyle choices.
There are not numerous cases in which cancer manifests solely because of people’s making lifestyle choices that are not known to be unhealthy.
It is always possible to determine whether a lifestyle choice is unhealthy before determining whether the manifestation of cancer results from making that lifestyle choice.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In approximately 42 percent of cases examined, people experiencing cancer had been making lifestyle choices known to be unhealthy.
Conclusion: In 42 percent of cases, cancer manifests because of lifestyle choices.
An assumption fills a gap in an argument.In this case, seeing the gap in the argument requires noticing a key detail.

The key detail to see in this argument is the phrase “known to be unhealthy” in the premise.Note that this phrase does not also appear in the conclusion.The argument jumps from “lifestyle choicesknown to be unhealthy” in the premise to simply “lifestyle choices” in the conclusion.

The assumption necessary for making the argument will bridge the gap between the premise about a specific category of lifestyle choices (those known to be unhealthy) and the conclusion about lifestyle choices in general.
YOUR CHOICE(A) People make unhealthy lifestyle choices even if they are aware that making such choices may result in their experiencing cancer.

The argument is simply about the cause-and-effect relationship between lifestyle choices and the manifestation of cancer.Making the argument does not require assuming anything about what people are aware of when they make lifestyle choices that result in the manifestation of cancer.In other words, the “cause” with which the argument is concerned relates to the actions people take, not their mental states or levels of knowledge.
(B) Through getting people to make healthier lifestyle choices, it would be possible to prevent approximately 42 percent of cancer cases.

This choice could be tempting, as what it says seems to follow logically from what the passage says.However, we are not looking for something that we can infer from what the passage says.We are looking for an assumption integral to the argument, and making an argument about how cancer is caused does not require assuming anything about how cancer can be prevented.
In a way, this answer choice provides an alternate conclusion to the argument, rather than provides an assumption necessary for reaching the conclusion that is already there.
(C) There is no way to prevent the manifestation of cancer in cases in which it is not caused by unhealthy lifestyle choices.

This choice is tempting, as what it says seems plausible and is connected to what is said in the passage.However, the passage is about one thing only, the cause-and-effect relationship between lifestyle choices and the manifestation of cancer.The question of whether cancer not caused by lifestyle choices could be prevented is a matter separate from what the argument discusses.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) There are not numerous cases in which cancer manifests solely because of people’s making lifestyle choices that are not known to be unhealthy.

The premise of the argument is that, in 42 percent of cancer cases examined, people had been making “lifestyle choices known to be unhealthy.” From that premise, the argument concludes that 42 percent of cancer cases manifest because of lifestyle choices.Getting from the premise to the conclusion requires assuming that,in all casesin which cancer manifests because of lifestyle choices, the lifestyle choices underlying the manifestation of cancer are “known to be unhealthy.”

But what if there were another category of lifestyle choices that result in cancer manifestation? In that case, the argument would fall apart.Thus, the argument requires assuming that there is not another category of lifestyle choices that result in cancer, for instance, lifestyle choicesnotknown to be unhealthy.
Let’s try negating the statement:
There ARE numerous cases in which cancer manifests solely because of people’s making lifestyle choices that are not known to be unhealthy.
If that statement is true, the argument no longer makes sense.

Sure, it is still the case that, in 42 percent of cases, people who have experienced cancer were making lifestyle choices known to be unhealthy, but that information no longer supports the conclusion that cancer manifests because of lifestyle choices in 42 percent of cases.

Rather, if the negated version of this answer choice is true, then the logical conclusion that follows from the facts presented is that, inmorethan 42 percent of cases cancer manifests because of lifestyle choices, but in many cases, the lifestyle choices underlying the manifestation of cancer are not “known to be unhealthy.”

Thus, what this answer choice says, that there are not numerous cases in which cancer manifests because of people’s making lifestyle choices not known to be unhealthy, must be true in order for the argument to work.We’ve found the assumption necessary to fill the argument’s gap in logic.
(E) It is always possible to determine whether a lifestyle choice is unhealthy before determining whether the manifestation of cancer results from making that lifestyle choice.

This choice is tempting because what it says seems to match the flow of what is described in the passage: The lifestyle choices mentioned were “known to be unhealthy” before it was found that the people experiencing cancer had been making those lifestyle choices.However, although the argument describes a sequence of events, as this answer choice does, the argument is not actually about the order in which events occur; it is about cause and effect.

The information presented in this answer choice perhaps provides some interesting background to the argument, but must it be true in order for the argument to remain valid? Let’s try negating the statement:
It is NOT always possible to determine whether a lifestyle choice is unhealthy before determining whether the manifestation of cancer results from making that lifestyle choice.

The fact remains that, in approximately 42 percent of cases examined, people experiencing cancer had been making lifestyle choices known to be unhealthy.When exactly it was determined that those choices were unhealthy makes no difference in arriving at the conclusion that 42 percent of cancer cases result from lifestyle choices.

question.text: A recent study determined that MBA candidates who engaged experienced admissions consultants to help in the development of their applications were significantly more likely to be accepted into MBA programs than candidates who did not engage such consultants.Therefore, when applying to MBA programs, relatively wealthy applicants must have an advantage over non-wealthy applicants, because experienced admissions consultants are costly, and not all applicants can afford to utilize their services.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?

answers.text: MBA candidates willing to make significant financial investments in their applications are not generally more motivated to earn high grades and scores than candidates unwilling to make such investments.
Experienced admissions consultants help candidates applying to MBA programs develop stronger applications than those candidates would ever be able to develop on their own.

MBA admissions officers who review candidates’ applications are not able to determine whether a candidate has engaged an experienced admissions consultant to help in the development of an application.
Experienced admissions consultants have valuable insider information regarding what MBA programs are and are not seeking in applicants, which they pass along to their clients.
Experienced admissions consultants often engage in pro-bono work involving their helping applicants to MBA programs develop their applications at little to no cost.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: A recent study determined that MBA candidates who engaged experienced admissions consultants to help in the development of their applications were significantly more likely to be accepted into MBA programs than candidates who did not engage such consultants.
Premise: Experienced admissions consultants are costly, and not all applicants can afford to utilize their services.
Conclusion: When applying to MBA programs, relatively wealthy applicants must have an advantage over non-wealthy applicants.
The author of the argument has made a cause and effect related claim.

In concluding that relatively wealthy applicants have an advantage, the author has implicitly concluded that the engaging of experienced admissions consultants is the cause of the candidates who engaged the consultants’ being significantly more likely to be accepted into MBA programs than candidates who did not engage such consultants.

Of course, in making that cause and effect related claim, the author is assuming that engaging consultants is the actual cause and that there is not an alternative explanation for the observed pattern.

YOUR CHOICE(A) MBA candidates willing to make significant financial investments in their applications are not generally more motivated to earn high grades and scores than candidates unwilling to make such investments.

In using the evidence that MBA candidates who engaged experienced admissions consultants were significantly more likely to be accepted into MBA programs than other candidates to arrive at the conclusion that relatively wealthy applicants must have an advantage over non-wealthy applicants, the author is assuming that the engaging of the consultants is thecauseof the candidates who engaged consultants’ being significantly more likely to be accepted into MBA programs than other candidates.

So, the author must be assuming that there is for the observed pattern no alternative explanation, such as that the reason that candidates who engage consultants are more likely than others to be accepted is actually that candidates who engage consultants aremore motivated.

So, the author must be assuming what this choice says about candidates who make significant financial investments in their applications, such as investments in engaging admissions consultants, because, if those candidates ARE more motivated, then it could be motivation, rather than engaging consultants, that really makes the difference, in which case the evidence provided would no longer support the conclusion that wealthy applicants have an advantage over other applicants.
(B) Experienced admissions consultants help candidates applying to MBA programs develop stronger applications than those candidates would ever be able to develop on their own.

The argument is based on the idea that admissions consultants are helpful.However, the argument does not rely on the assumption that admissions consultants are so helpful that applications they help candidates develop are stronger than any applications the candidates couldeverdevelop on their own.As long as the consultants are helpful in any significant way, the conclusion could be valid.

(C) MBA admissions officers who review candidates’ applications are not able to determine whether a candidate has engaged an experienced admissions consultant to help in the development of an application.
This choice could trap us into incorrectly choosing it if we make up an unsupported story.

If we make up the story that admissions officers are so biased against candidates who use consultants that any help that consultants provide is offset by admissions officers’ bias against candidates who use consultants, then we could convince ourselves that the author must be assuming that admissions officers can’t determine whether a candidate has used an admissions consultant.

We could convince ourselves that the author must be assuming that because, if admissions officers were biased in that way and COULD DETERMINE which candidates have used admissions consultants, then having the financial means to engage an admissions consultant would not be advantageous.It wouldn’t help at all.In that case, the support for the author’s conclusion wouldn’t even matter.The conclusion would be clearly invalid.

So, if we were to make up that story, we could decide that the author must be assuming that admissions officers are NOT able to determine whether a candidate has engaged an experienced admissions consultant to help in the development of an application.In the context of that story, the argument would work only with that assumption.

However, that story about admissions officers being biased in that way is not supported by either information presented anywhere in this question or by common knowledge.So, the author does not have to assume that admissions officers are not able to determine whether a candidate has engaged an experienced admissions consultant to help in the development of an application, because even if admissions officers CAN determine whether a candidate has engaged an admissions consultant, engaging such a consultant might be helpful.Therefore, candidates with the financial means to engage consultants could still have an advantage over others.
(D) Experienced admissions consultants have valuable insider information regarding what MBA programs are and are not seeking in applicants, which they pass along to their clients.

This choice is tempting because it is certainly pertinent to the topic of the argument, because this choice seems to explain why candidates who engage admissions consultants are more like to be accepted into MBA programs: the consultants have insider information.

Of course, we are not looking for an explanation.We’re looking for an assumption integral to the argument, and the author does not have to assume that consultants have insider information to conclude that they are helpful and that, therefore, wealthy MBA candidates have an advantage over other candidates.
Even if admissions consultants did NOT have insider information, they could still be helpful and wealthy candidates could, therefore, still have an advantage over others.
(E) Experienced admissions consultants often engage in pro-bono work involving their helping applicants to MBA programs develop their applications at little to no cost.
This choice could be tempting, because what it says does affect the argument.
However, this choice is incorrect because it affects the argument in the wrong way.It’s not an assumption integral to the argument, it weakens the support for the conclusion.

The author concludes that wealth confers an advantage to MBA applicants.By saying that admissions consultants are willing to help applicants at little to no cost, this choice provides a reason to question the validity of that conclusion, because this choice indicates that non-wealthy applicants also can get help from admissions consultants.That fact indicates that wealth may not confer the advantage the author claims it does.

question.text: Longview Prep Board Member: Over the last five years, the school's administrators have noticed signs of an increase in student drug use, and there has been, simultaneously, a drop in the averages of the scores our students have been getting on state-issued tests of general knowledge.Schools that offer drug use awareness classes have experienced significant reductions in student drug use.Clearly, if we can reduce student drug use by incorporating drug use awareness classes into the Longview Prep curriculum, we will increase the averages of Longview Prep students' state-issued test scores to the levels at which they were five years ago.Which of the following is an assumption on which the support for the board member's conclusion depends?

answers.text: The students who use drugs the most would not leave Longview Prep and enroll in other schools because they prefer not to attend drug use awareness classes.
Over the past five years, Longview Prep has not changed its curriculum by shifting the primary focus from imparting knowledge to the development of critical thinking and research skills.
Longview Prep students who use drugs are not able to hide their drug use from teachers and administrators.
Test-focused review sessions would not be effective in increasing Longview Prep students' scores on state-issued tests of general knowledge.
Most students who use drugs will not eventually reduce their drug use on their own, without having attended drug use awareness classes.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Schools that offer drug use awareness classes have experienced significant reductions in drug use by students.

Premise: Over the last five years at Longview Prep, there have been signs of an increase in student drug use and a drop in the scores students have been getting on state-issued tests of general knowledge.

Conclusion: Clearly, if we can reduce student drug use by incorporating drug use awareness classes into the curriculum, we will increase the students’ scores on state-issued tests to the levels at which they were five years ago.

In making the argument, the board member takes a leap from seeing a correlation between lower scores and signs of drug use to concluding that drug usecausedthe decrease in test scores.Of course, we must always be skeptical when speakers make claims about cause-and-effect relationships.

There could be an alternative cause for the decreasing test scores; however, the board member believes that she has correctly identified the cause of the decrease.In other words, she must be assuming that there is not another cause.As we go through the answer choices, we’ll be seeking such an assumption.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The students who use drugs the most would not leave Longview Prep and enroll in other schools because they prefer not to attend drug use awareness classes.

This choice could be tempting, as students have to attend the awareness classes in order for the classes to affect student drug use.If the students who use drugs the most were to leave the school rather than attending the classes, the strategy of having students take such classes wouldn’t work, because the classes would not have any impact on students who use drugs the most.

However, the stated goal of implementing the awareness classes is to bring up the averages of student test scores.Drug-using students’ leaving the school does not conflict with that goal and may even facilitate the achievement of the goal.Thus, this answer choice does not present an assumption necessary for reaching the conclusion.

CORRECT ANSWER(B) Over the past five years, Longview Prep has not changed its curriculum by shifting the primary focus from imparting knowledge to the development of critical thinking and research skills.

In order for the plan to work, it has to be the case that the test scores will increase if drug use is reduced.If drug use is not the cause of the decrease in scores, and there is some other cause, then the plan will not work.So, the board member’s argument depends on the assumption that something else was not the cause of the decrease in scores.

This choice mentions something else that could be the cause of the decrease in test scores: a change in the school’s curriculum.If, five years ago, the primary focus of the curriculum shifted from imparting knowledge to the development of critical thinking and research skills, that change could be the reason why students started scoring lower on tests of general knowledge.They weren’t learning as much general knowledge as they were previously.

So, the board member’s conclusion depends upon the assumption that the curriculum’s focus was not changed to deemphasize knowledge acquisition, because it’s quite possible that such a change could have caused the score decrease.In such a case, it would not be clear that reducing student drug use would result in an increase in test scores.
(C) Longview Prep students who use drugs are not able to hide their drug use from teachers and administrators.

While the passage mentions that the school’s administration has noticed signs of drug use, the success of the plan does not depend on teachers and administrators’ knowing which students use drugs.Under the plan, the awareness classes would be part of the school’s curriculum, so students would take the classes regardless of whether their drug use was obvious.Thus, the classes would have the intended effect even if teachers and administrators weren’t sure which students were using drugs.

Even more importantly, the board member’s conclusion is that the scores will increaseif drug use is reduced.So, the board member is not assuming that adding the classes to the curriculum will result in decreased drug use.The board member is merely concluding that, the scores will increaseifadding the classesdoesresult in decreased drug use.Note the language in the passage: “if we canreduce student drug use by incorporating drug use awareness classes … we will increase the averages….”
(D) Test-focused review sessions would not be effective in increasing Longview Prep students' scores on state-issued tests of general knowledge.

This choice is a classic “alternative plan” trap.“Alternative plan” traps appear in some GMAT Critical Reasoning questions that contain arguments supporting conclusions that plans will work.An “alternative plan” trap can be tempting because it presents a plan that is either better than or different from the plan that the argument lays out.For example, this answer choice discusses test-focused review sessions as an alternative to incorporating awareness classes.

An “alternative plan” choice could be tempting because one might think that, if the speaker is proposing one plan instead of another, then the speaker must be assuming that the other plan would not be as effective as the plan being proposed.

However, what this choice says misses the point of the argument, as the board member is not asserting that there is no other plan that would work.There could be several different ways to increase test scores, but the board member’s conclusion is about the effectiveness of one particular way.
(E) Most students who use drugs will not eventually reduce their drug use on their own, without having attended drug use awareness classes.

This choice is tempting because it does support a conclusion related to the argument.However, the conclusion it supports is not the board member’s conclusion.Remember, the conclusion of the argument is not that awareness classes arenecessaryfor reducing student drug use but rather that reduced drug use will result in increased test scores.The plan could work even if there are other paths to reduction in drug use, such as students’ eventually reducing drug use on their own.

question.text: Geologist: Our work indicates that, near the beginning of Earth’s formation, the entire planet was composed of hot molten rock.We can calculate with some accuracy the rate at which Earth loses heat into space.Therefore, by determining how long it would take for Earth to lose enough heat into space to go from being molten rock to being in the form of Earth today, we can calculate a fairly accurate estimate of the age of Earth.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the geologist’s conclusion depends?

answers.text: The decay of radioactive isotopes abundantly found in the rock that makes up much of Earth does not constantly generate huge amounts of heat.
All of the different types of material that make up Earth lose heat into space at the same rate.
Analyzing geological formations to determine the length of time required to create them would not be the most effective method of estimating the age of Earth.
The rate at which Earth loses heat into space is similar to the heat loss rates of other similarly sized planets.
It would not be possible to accurately estimate the age of Earth if the planet were not losing heat into space.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Near beginning of its formation, Earth was hot molten rock.
Premise: We can calculate the rate at which Earth loses heat into space.

Conclusion: By determining how long it would take for Earth to lose enough heat into space to go from being molten rock to being in the form of Earth today, we can calculate a fairly accurate estimate of the age of Earth.

The geologist presents us with a method for calculating Earth’s age.Whenever a speaker presents us with a plan, the speaker assumes that the plan will work as intended and that there will not be unforeseen variables or events that derail the plan.Of course, if that assumption is found to be incorrect, the plan will fall apart.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The decay of radioactive isotopes abundantly found in the rock that makes up much of Earth does not constantly generate huge amounts of heat.

The geologist says that Earth’s age can be estimated by determining how long it would take Earth’s molten form to cool into Earth’s present form, but what if Earth were not only losing heat into space but also being heated somehow? In that case, the planet would cool more slowly than it would were it only losing, and not gaining, heat, and an estimate arrived at via a process that took into account only the loss but not the gain of heat would understate Earth’s age.Therefore, in proposing that we can estimate Earth’s age by considering only how long it took Earth to lose enough heat to go from being molten rock to being in its current form, the geologist must be assuming that Earth is not being heated by the decay of radioactive isotopes.
If you have trouble seeing this, try negating the statement in this answer choice:
The decay of radioactive isotopes abundantly found in the rock that makes up much of the Earth DOES constantly generate huge amounts of heat.
In this case, we see that the plan will fail because Earth is losing heat and being heated at the same time, so the simple calculation using heat loss is no longer valid.
(B) All of the different types of material that make up Earth lose heat into space at the same rate.

What this choice says could be perceived as supporting the geologist’s statement that “we can calculate with some accuracy the rate at which Earth loses heat into space.” However, premises in GMAT Critical Reasoning arguments are taken to be statements of fact that do not have to be confirmed by answer choices.

Meanwhile, even if the various materials that make up Earth lose do not lose heat at the same rate, the rate at which the planet as a whole loses heat could still be calculated by someone taking those various rates into account.Therefore, this choice does not convey an assumption necessary for connecting the premises to the conclusion.
(C) Analyzing geological formations to determine the length of time required to create them would not be the most effective method of estimating the age of Earth.

We need to be wary of the “better / different plan” answer choice.Notice that the argument is about just one plan that the geologist thinks will work; the geologist is not making the claim that the proposed plan is the best possible plan or that there are no other plans out there.Thus, the effectiveness of another method of calculating Earth’s age has no impact on the effectiveness of the method described in the argument.What this answer choice says does not affect the argument.
(D) The rate at which Earth loses heat into space is similar to the heat loss rates of other similarly sized planets.

The process for estimating Earth’s age that is described in the argument involves considering the rate at which Earth loses heat into space, and the results of the process would be exactly the same whether that rate is unique or similar to the heat loss rates of other planets.What this answer choice says has no effect on the argument.
(E) It would not be possible to accurately estimate the age of Earth if the planet were not losing heat into space.

This choice could be tempting because what it says may seem plausible, given what is stated in the argument.However, to correctly answer this question, we don’t need a plausible statement; we need an assumption on which the conclusion depends.The support for the conclusion of this argument, which is simply that the estimation method described would work, does not depend on the idea that there would be no way to estimate Earth’s age if Earth were not losing heat into space.

question.text: Commerce Secretary: Though, currently, many material needs of our nation’s citizens are not being met, with each year of strong economic growth, the citizens have access to and are able to afford more products and services.So, as long as we have strong economic growth, the material needs of our nation’s citizens will be increasingly met.Which of the following is an assumption upon which the Commerce Secretary’s argument depends?

answers.text: As the nation’s economy grows, the citizens who are the least financially secure will become increasingly able to move into relatively high-paying jobs.
In some future years, the nation’s rate of economic growth will be greater than it has been in any year so far.
Not all of the nation’s economic growth will result from people’s selling to each other unneeded products and services.
There will not be future years in which the nation’s rate of economic growth will be lower than its rate of population growth.
Competition from businesses based in other nations will not hamper wage growth and drive down the prices of many products.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Currently, many material needs of our nation’s citizens are not being met.
Premise: With each year of strong economic growth, citizens have access to and are able to afford more products and services.
Conclusion: As long as we have strong economic growth, the material needs of our nation’s citizens will be increasingly met.

In order to correctly answer an Assumption question, we have to clearly identify the conclusion.Notice that the conclusion to the argument made is not that there will be strong economic growth, and the conclusion is not that the material needs of the citizens will be met.The conclusion involves a cause-and-effect scenario, which is that, as long as there is strong economic growth (the cause), the material needs of the citizens will be increasingly met (the effect).

Another interesting aspect of the passage is that the premise mentions “more products and services,” while the conclusion mentions “material needs.” This difference in wording is related to a gap in the argument, and the correct answer fills that gap.
YOUR CHOICE(A) As the nation’s economy grows, the citizens who are the least financially secure will become increasingly able to move into relatively high-paying jobs.

This choice is tempting, because it mentions what could be seen as a requirement for people’s material needs to be met.The truth is, however, that this very specific development, those who are “the least financially secure” moving into “high-paying jobs,” is not necessary in order for people’s needs to be increasingly met through strong economic growth.
(B) In some future years, the nation’s rate of economic growth will be greater than it has been in any year so far.

If we aren’t clear about exactly what the conclusion is and, instead, mistakenly perceive the conclusion to be, for example, that people’s material needs will be increasingly met, we could easily choose this answer.However, the truth is that the commerce secretary does not have to assume anything about the rate of economic growth.

The commerce secretary’s conclusion is that,ifthere is strong economic growth, people’s needs will be increasingly met.The rate at which either of these developments occurs has no bearing on the conclusion, which is about the impact of one occurrence (economic growth) on the other (meeting needs).
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Not all of the nation’s economic growth will result from people’s selling to each other unneeded products and services.

Since it does not mention anything that obviously would affect economic growth or material needs, this choice could seem to be unrelated to the argument.However, often the choice that seems the least likely to affect an argument turns out to be the correct answer.Let’s take a closer look at this answer choice.

In concluding that strong economic growth will result in the citizens’ needs being increasingly met, the commerce secretary must be assuming thatsomeof the nation’s economic growth will result from people’s selling to each other needed products and services (which would meet people’s needs).Ifallof the economic growth were to result from the sale ofunneededproducts and services, then the conclusion that the growth would result in people’s needs being increasingly met would not make sense.

So, the commerce secretary must be assuming that “not allof the nation’s economic growth will result from people’s selling to each otherunneededproducts and services” (which, by definition, would not meet people’s needs).
(D) There will not be future years in which the nation’s rate of economic growth will be lower than its rate of population growth.

This choice could tempt a test-taker who notices that, if economic growth does not exceed population growth, then per capita income will not increase.However, the truth is that economic growth does not have to exceed population growthevery yearin order for per capita income to increase.Citizens’ material needs could be increasingly met even if,in some years, the economy grows at a lower rate than the population.So, the secretary’s argument does not depend on the assumption that, in every future year, economic growth will exceed population growth.
(E) Competition from businesses based in other nations will not hamper wage growth and drive down the prices of many products.

This choice could be very tempting because it seems to express an assumption necessary for concluding that there will be economic growth and/or that people’s needs will be increasingly met.It is, however, wrong on two levels.

For one thing, strong economic growth could occur even if competition hampered wage growth and drove down product prices.Similarly, people’s needs could be increasingly met even if wage growth were hampered.Furthermore, driving down prices actually could result in people’s needs being met even more than they would have been otherwise.

More importantly, the conclusion is not that there will be strong economic growth or that needs will be met.The conclusion is that,ifsuch growth occurs, people’s needs will be increasingly met.Thus, we are looking for an assumption necessary for arriving at this cause-and-effect conclusion.

question.text: Genwash, a producer of washing machines, has found that the greater the number of years one of its machines has been in use, the more likely it is that that machine will suffer a serious breakdown.To monitor product quality, at the end of every year, Genwash conducts a survey to determine the percentage of the machines sold by the company in the preceding 10 years that have operated without a serious breakdown.The survey results indicate that that percentage was much greater five years ago than it is this year.The average quality of machines produced by Genwash must have declined in recent years.Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies?

answers.text: The number of washing machines sold annually by Genwash has not steadily and significantly decreased over the past five years.
Over the past five years, Genwash has not made significant changes to the components that it uses to build its washing machines.
The percentage of owners of Genwash machines that have suffered serious breakdowns who have chosen to discard those machines has not decreased significantly over the past five years.
Over the past five years, Genwash has steadily reduced the number of quality control checks that each of its washing machines undergoes before being sold.
Over the past five years, the average cost of repairing a Genwash washing machine that has suffered a serious breakdown has not decreased significantly.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Genwash has found that the greater the number of years one of its washing machines has been in use, the more likely it is that the machine will suffer a serious breakdown.

Background: To monitor product quality, at the end of every year, Genwash conducts a survey to determine the percentage of washing machines sold by the company in the preceding 10 years that have operated without a serious breakdown.
Premise: The survey results indicate that the percentage of washing machines operating without a serious breakdown was much greater five years ago than it is this year.
Conclusion: The average quality of washing machines produced by Genwash must have declined in recent years.

Yes, the percentage of Genwash washing machines sold over the trailing 10 years that are operating without a serious breakdown has declined, but is a decline in quality the only possible reason for the decline in the percentage operating without a serious breakdown? What has the author assumed?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The number of washing machines sold annually by Genwash has not steadily and significantly decreased over the past five years.
To see why this choice states an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion, let’s negate it:
The number of washing machines sold annually by Genwash HAS steadily and significantly decreased over the past five years.
If that statement were true, then what would be going on is that, in each year of the past five years, fewer new Genwash machines would have been sold than were sold in previous years.
For instance, we could say that, before five years ago, Genwash sold 1,000 machines per year.So, 10 years’ sales would have been 10,000 machines.

Then, if the number sold was declining, five years ago, the number sold in a year could have declined to, for instance, 900 machines.In that case, the trailing 10-year total of machines sold would have been 9,900 machines, 9 x 1,000 and 1 x 900.

Then, four years ago, the number sold in a year could have declined to 700 machines.In that case, the trailing 10-year total of machines sold would have been 9,600 machines, 8 x 1,000, 1 x 900, and 1 x 700.

Now, notice what’s going on here.Not only is the trailing 10-year total decreasing but also the proportion of newer machines is declining.For instance, among the 10,000 machines, there were 1,000 brand new machines and 1,000 machines from the year before.So, 2,000, or 1/5, of the machines sold within the ten-year period would have been around one year old or brand new.However, four years ago, only 900 + 700 = 1,600, or 1/6, of the 9,600 machines would have been one year old or brand new.

Of course, if such a pattern of decreasing sales were to have continued, newer machines would have represented smaller and smaller proportions of the machines sold over the trailing ten years, and older machines would have represented greater and greater proportions of the machines sold over the trailing ten years.
The passage says that Genwash has found that the greater the number of years one of its machines has been in use, the more likely it is that that machine will suffer a serious breakdown.

So, of course, if newer machines were representing smaller and smaller proportions of the machines sold over the trailing ten years, and if older machines are more likely to experience serious breakdowns, we would expect that, even if nothing else changed, the percentage of machines sold over the trailing 10 years that are operating without a breakdown would decrease.

So, the negated version of this choice provides an alternative explanation for the decline in the percentage of machines operating without a breakdown.The alternative explanation is that newer machines are representing smaller and smaller percentages of the machines sold over the preceding 10 years.

Since the negation of this choice would provide an alternative explanation for the observed change, the author must be assuming that what this choice saysistrue, because if it were not true, then the author’s conclusion that the changemustbe the result of a decline in product quality would not be supported by the evidence.
(B) Over the past five years, Genwash has not made significant changes to the components that it uses to build its washing machines.

What does “significant changesto the components” it uses to build its washing machines mean? Has Genwash significantly improve the components? Made the components significantly worse? Notice that this statement is a vague one.

In any case, to go from the fact that the percentage of Genwash machines operating without a serious breakdown has declined to the conclusion that the quality of the machines has declined does not require assuming that Genwash has not made significant changes to components used in building the machines.A decline in product quality and changes to components are not mutually exclusive occurrences.In fact, a decline in product quality could be the result of changes to components.
(C) The percentage of owners of Genwash machines that have suffered serious breakdowns who have chosen to discard those machines has not decreased significantly over the past five years.

This choice could be tempting, because one might wonder whether a decrease in the percentage of machines discarded after breakdowns could somehow be related to the decrease in the percentage of machines operating without breakdowns.For one thing, maybe these machines kept after breakdowns are more likely to break down than other machines.

Notice, however, that machines kept after breakdowns have already broken down.So, at that point, there’s no way for these machines to affect the percentage in question, the percentage of machines that havenotbroken down.

Since there’s no clear reason to believe that people’s continuing to use machines that have already broken down would have any effect on the percentage of machines operating without a serious breakdown, this choice must not be an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion.
(D) Over the past five years, Genwash has steadily reduced the number of quality control checks that each of its washing machines undergoes before being sold.

This statement could explain why the decline in quality the author claims has occurred would have occurred.The decline in quality could have occurred as a result of Genwash’s having reduced the number of quality control checks that each of its washing machines undergoes before being sold.

This statement is not, however, an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion that the quality of machines produced by Genwash has declined.That conclusion could be supported by the evidence even if Genwash has not reduced the number of quality control checks.Quality could have declined for a different reason.
(E) Over the past five years, the average cost of repairing a Genwash washing machine that has suffered a serious breakdown has not decreased significantly.
This choice could be tempting because it serves to bring up something relevant to a discussion of breakdowns, the costs of repairs.
However, it is not an assumption necessary for arriving at the conclusion, because there’s no clear reason to believe that a decline in costs of repairs would affect the reasons why breakdowns occur.

question.text: Deforestation in Peru has caused the number of jaguars living there to decline precipitously.Most of the deforestation that occurs in Peru is done illegally.Therefore, by increasing the severity of the punishment for engaging in illegal deforestation in Peru, we can stop the decline in the number of jaguars living there.Which of the following, if discovered to be true, would most seriously undermine the support for the above conclusion?

answers.text: Deforestation often leads to a decline in the presence of the main sources of food upon which jaguars depend.
Jaguar populations have declined rapidly in other countries in which deforestation is occurring.
Almost all the deforestation that occurs in Peru occurs in remote regions where almost no law enforcement is present.
When nearby Bolivia increased the severity of penalties for illegal drug trafficking, smaller amounts of illegal drugs were produced and sold there.
Numerous conservation groups are currently working in Peru to help rebuild its declining jaguar population.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Deforestation in Peru has caused the number of jaguars living there to decline precipitously.
Premise: Most of the deforestation that occurs in Peru is done illegally.
Conclusion: Therefore, by increasing the severity of the punishment for engaging in illegal deforestation in Peru, we can stop the decline in the number of jaguars living there.

The author has presented a plan.Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work.In other words, she assumes that there are not variables that will enter the equation that will spoil the plan.In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What might the author have failed to consider? What malicious variables might have gone unnoticed?
(A) Deforestation often leads to a decline in the presence of the main sources of food upon which jaguars depend.
This choice has the wrong effect.Rather than weaken the argument, this choice simply explains a part of the scenario.

The fact that deforestation often leads to a decline in the presence of the main sources of food upon which jaguars depend explains why deforestation in Peru has caused the number of jaguars living there to decline precipitously.
However, this information explaining the decline in the number of jaguars does not indicate that the plan will not work and, therefore, does not weaken the argument.
(B) Jaguar populations have declined rapidly in other countries in which deforestation is occurring.

The fact that jaguar populations have declined rapidly in other countries in which deforestation is occurring tends to confirm that it is indeed the deforestation that is causing the decline in the number of jaguars in Peru.

However, since the passage has already stated as fact that deforestation is causing the decline in the number of jaguars in Peru, what this answer choice says does not change what we know and, therefore, has no effect on the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Almost all the deforestation that occurs in Peru occurs in remote regions where almost no law enforcement is present.
The fact that almost all the deforestation that occurs in Peru occurs in remote regions where almost no law enforcement is present serves to call into question the conclusion that the plan will work.
The plan is based on the expectation that increasing the severity of the punishment for engaging in illegal deforestation will serve to reduce the amount of illegal deforestation going on.

Of course, if there is almost no law enforcement where the deforestation occurs, then increasing the severity of the punishment for engaging in illegal deforestation isn’t likely to make much difference, because regardless of how severe a punishment is, it is not much of a disincentive if there is almost nobody around to impose that punishment.
Notice that this answer choice highlights a malicious variable that the author has failed to consider.
(D) When nearby Bolivia increased the severity of penalties for illegal drug trafficking, smaller amounts of illegal drugs were produced and sold there.
What this answer choice says goes in the same direction as the argument.
