After all, if, when nearby Bolivia imposed more severe penalties for illegal drug trafficking, smaller amounts of illegal drugs were produced and sold there, then we have a bit more reason to think that, in general, increasing the severity of a punishment can in fact stop unwanted actions associated with that punishment.
Of course, we’re not seeking an answer choice that goes in the same direction as the argument; we’re seeking one that weakens it.
(E) Numerous conservation groups are currently working in Peru to help rebuild its declining jaguar population.
The fact that numerous conservation groups are currently working in Peru to help rebuild its declining jaguar population is a wonderful thing.

However, the fact that people are taking other measures to address the declines in the jaguar population does not indicate that increasing the severity of the punishment for engaging in illegal deforestation will not serve to stop the decline in the number of jaguars living in Peru.
So, this choice does not weaken the argument.

question.text: Strength and Conditioning Coach: I recently put 100 people through my six-month exercise program.By the program’s end, every one of the participants was at least ten pounds closer to his or her ideal weight than he or she was before starting the program.Therefore, my six-month exercise program must cause people’s weights to move toward their ideal weights.Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

answers.text: Some people will drop out of a six-month exercise program before their weights have had a chance to move toward their ideal weights.
Soon after starting to participate in the exercise program, all the program participants also started for the first time to work with nutritionists to optimize their diets.
Not all of the participants in the strength and conditioning coach's program felt that they had attained their ideal weights by the time they completed the program.
None of the people who participated in the six-month exercise program had prior experience with exercise programs designed to help people attain their ideal weights.
Some of the people who participated in the six-month exercise program experienced weight changes of only ten pounds.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: I recently put 100 people through my six-month exercise program.
Premise: By the program’s end, every one of the participants was at least ten pounds closer to his or her ideal weight than he or she was before starting the program.
Conclusion: Therefore, my six-month exercise program must cause people’s weights to move toward their ideal weights.

Notice that the strength and conditioning coach is making a cause-and-effect claim.As always, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim made.Is her six-month exercise program really the cause of people’s weights moving toward their ideal weights?
Could there be an alternative explanation? What might the strength and conditioning coach have missed?
(A) Some people will drop out of a six-month exercise program before their weights have had a chance to move toward their ideal weights.

This answer choice attacks the support for the wrong conclusion.The fact that some people will drop out of a six-month exercise program calls into question a variety of conclusions, such as the conclusion that all people in will attain their ideal weights because the strength and conditioning coach’s six-month program is available.
However, it does not undermine the support for the conclusion of this argument, because the conclusion of this argument is about what happens when people do not drop out of the program.

Since the conclusion is about the effect the program has when people do stick with it for the entire six months, not about whether people stick with the program, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Soon after starting to participate in the exercise program, all the program participants also started for the first time to work with nutritionists to optimize their diets.
Notice that what this answer choice says weakens the argument by highlighting that there may be an alternative explanation for the why the people’s weights moved toward their ideal weights.

The strength and conditioning coach is making that claim that it must be her six-month exercise program that caused people’s weights to move toward their ideal weights.However, if, soon after starting to participate in the exercise program, all the program participants also started for the first time to work with nutritionists to optimize their diets, then we are less sure that it is the actually the six-month exercise program that is responsible for the people’s changes in weight.
After all, perhaps it is instead the optimization of their diets that is responsible for the people’s changes in weight.
(C) Not all of the participants in the strength and conditioning coach's program felt that they had attained their ideal weights by the time they completed the program.
This choice is a pseudo-weakener that may seem to conflict with or attack the support for the strength and conditioning coach's conclusion but does not affect the argument at all.
To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be clear about what the conclusion of the argument is.
The conclusion is that the six-month exercise program must cause people’s weights to move toward their ideal weights.

Notice thatmoving towardtheir ideal weights is not the same asattainingtheir ideal weights.Clearly, people could participate in the program and see their weights move toward their ideal weights even if they don't attain their ideal weights by the time the program is complete.So, the program could have the effects the coach says it has even if not all of the participants felt they had attained their ideal weights by the time they completed the program.
(D) None of the people who participated in the six-month exercise program had prior experience with exercise programs designed to help people attain their ideal weights.

If this choice does anything, it strengthens the argument a little by indicating that the participants in the strength and conditioning coach's six-month program didn't experience changes in weight as a result of their using things they had learned through participating in other exercise programs.

The fact that the participants did not have prior experience with such programs certainly does not weaken the support for the conclusion that this program causes people’s weights to move toward their ideal weights.
(E) Some of the people who participated in the six-month exercise program experienced weight changes of only ten pounds.
This choice is a pseudo-weakener that doesn’t really change what we know.

The strength and conditioning coach presents as fact that 100 people went through her six-month exercise program and that, at the program’s end, every one of the participants was at least ten pounds closer to his or her ideal weight than he or she was before starting the program.Her cause-and-effect conclusion is supported by that evidence.

Notice that what this answer choice says, that some of the people who participated in the six-month exercise program experienced weight changes of only ten pounds, does not change the evidence she provides to support her conclusion.

question.text: Global warming is caused by increased levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide, much of which comes from use of fossil fuels.Ocean waters in a highly alkaline state are capable of absorbing huge amounts of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, and the oceans can be made highly alkaline by adding to them powdered olivine rock, which can be mined in large quantities.Therefore, adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans is an effective approach to combating global warming.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the likelihood that the plan described will be effective?

answers.text: Emissions of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere can be dramatically reduced via changing methods of producing food for human consumption.
While measures have been taken to reduce emissions of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, atmospheric carbon dioxide levels have continued to increase.
Technology that could be used for mining and powdering olivine rock has been available for many years.
There are minerals, such as peridotite, that could be used for absorbing atmospheric carbon dioxide without mining or powdering.
The process of mining and powdering of rock currently requires extensive use of heavy machinery powered by fossil fuels.

explanation_parts.text: The argument works as follows:
Premise: Global warming is caused by increased levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide.
Background: Much of atmospheric carbon dioxide comes from use of fossil fuels.
Premise: Highly alkaline ocean waters absorb carbon dioxide.
Premise: Ocean waters can be made alkaline by adding powdered olivine rock to them.
Premise: Olivine rock can be mined in large quantities.
Conclusion: Adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans is an effective approach to combating global warming.

The author has presented a plan.Whenever a plan is presented, the author assumes that there are not things that he or she failed to consider that will interfere with the plan.As we analyze the answer choices, we’ll be on the lookout for information that raises doubts that the plan will work.What important detail might the author have failed to consider?
(A) Emissions of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere can be dramatically reduced via changing methods of producing food for human consumption.
This choice could be tempting because it offers an alternative way to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide, one that might be superior to the plan outlined in the argument.

However, the fact that there is an alternative method for achieving a reduction in atmospheric carbon dioxide does not affect the conclusion of the argument, which is simply that a particular plan would work.We must be watchful for the “better plan / different plan” trap answer.The fact that there is a plan other than the one an author proposes does not mean that the author’s plan is not sound.So, we can’t weaken the support for a conclusion that a plan will work by claiming that there is a different or better plan available.
(B) While measures have been taken to reduce emissions of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, atmospheric carbon dioxide levels have continued to increase.

This choice could be tempting because it seems to indicate that measures taken to reduce carbon dioxide levels have not worked, and thus that the measure outlined in the argument would not work either.However, this choice can be ruled out for multiple reasons.

For one, notice that this choice does not state that measures taken have not been effective at all.Rather, it indicates that measures taken have not been effective in a certain way.For all we know, the measures taken have been effective in reducing carbon dioxides levels to less than what they would have been were those measures not taken.

Even more importantly, this choice can be eliminated because it discusses measures taken to reduce carbon dioxideemissions, while the argument is about a plan to absorb carbon dioxide already in the atmosphere.So, this choice and the argument are discussing two different things.

Basically, the fact that a completely different approach to solving the problem did not have a particular effect does not weaken the support for the conclusion that adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans would work.
(C) Technology that could be used for mining and powdering olivine rock has been available for many years.

The fact that technology needed for undertaking one aspect of the plan has been available for many years does nothing to affect the outcome of pursuing the plan.Knowing that a technology has been available for a long time does not tell us how effective or efficient that technology is.Furthermore, the technology mentioned is for “mining and powdering” olivine rock, a necessary step in pursuing the plan, but this fact is not very helpful in predicting the effectiveness of adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans in combating global warming.If anything, the existence of this technology could slightly strengthen, not weaken, the author’s argument, although we can see that many questions remain.
(D) There are minerals, such as peridotite, that could be used for absorbing atmospheric carbon dioxide without mining or powdering.

Like choice (A), this choice discusses an alternate plan, and the plan discussed in this choice seems better than the plan mentioned in the argument.Once again, the fact that there is a better way to achieve reduced carbon dioxide levels and, thus, combat global warming does not weaken the conclusion that the plan outlined in the argument would work.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The process of mining and powdering of rock currently requires extensive use of heavy machinery powered by fossil fuels.

What this choice says calls into question the conclusion of the argument by indicating that, while adding powdered olivine rock to ocean water may itself serve to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide, the process of creating that powdered rock would increase atmospheric carbon dioxide.How can we tell? The passage says that much of atmospheric carbon dioxide comes from use of fossil fuels, and this choice indicates that fossil fuels are used in the process of creating powdered olivine rock.So, that one detail of the passage is key in answering this question.

Can we be sure from what this choice says that the reduction in carbon dioxide levels resulting from adding the powdered rock to ocean waters would not be much greater than the increase resulting from the use of heavy machinery? No.Even given what this choice says, the net effect of the plancouldbe a decrease in carbon dioxide levels.

All the same, the fact that a process involved in implementing the plan would result in addition of carbon dioxide to the atmosphere calls into question the idea that implementing the plan would result in a reduction in atmospheric carbon dioxide and, thus, be effective in combating global warming.Thus, this choice weakens the author’s argument, doing all the correct answer has to do.

question.text: Overconsumption of sugar and caffeine leads to many of the diet-related health problems we see people experiencing today, and much of people’s intake of sugar and caffeine occurs when they consume soda.Adding a tax of one cent per ounce to the price of soda will cause people to significantly reduce their consumption of soda.Therefore, imposing such a tax will result in people’s experiencing fewer diet-related health problems.Which of the following, if true, would be most detrimental to the likelihood that the above conclusion is correct?

answers.text: Health problems related to eating food contaminated with heavy metals are generally more debilitating than those related to overconsumption of sugar or caffeine.
The results of some studies indicate that consumption of moderate amounts of caffeine may result in improved ability to concentrate.
Some people consume soda regularly for many years without experiencing any obvious adverse effects.
There is evidence indicating that consuming diet soda, which contains artificial sweetener instead of sugar, may be more deleterious to health than consuming regular soda.
When faced with increases in soda prices, many people opt to drink instead significant quantities of alcoholic beverages.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Overconsumption of sugar and caffeine leads to many of the diet-related health problems we see people experiencing today, and much of people’s intake of sugar and caffeine is in the form of soda.
Premise: Adding a tax of one cent per ounce to the price of soda will cause people to significantly reduce their soda consumption.
Conclusion: Therefore, imposing such a tax will result in people’s experiencing fewer diet-related health problems.
(A) Health problems related to eating food contaminated with heavy metals are generally more debilitating than those related to overconsumption of sugar or caffeine.

To avoid being tempted into choosing this choice, we have to remember what the conclusion of the argument is.The argument does not make the claim that health problems related to overconsumption of sugar and caffeine are the most debilitating health problems that exist.Even if there are other, more debilitating, health problems that people will continue to experience if people reduce their consumption of sugar and caffeine, a reduction in people’s consumption of sugar and caffeine would still likely result in people’s experiencingfewerhealth problems.So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
(B) The results of some studies indicate that consumption of moderate amounts of caffeine may result in improved ability to concentrate.

People may improve concentration by consuming moderate amounts of caffeine and, at the same time, still experience the diet-related health issues the occurrence of which the author’s plan is intended to reduce.The fact that there may be a beneficial effect of caffeine consumption does not negate the fact that caffeine could cause health problems.Notice also that this answer choice makes no mention of the effects of sugar consumption.In any case, the information in this answer choice does not weaken the argument’s conclusion, which is about the impact of a tax on the occurrence of certain health problems.
(C) Some people consume soda regularly for many years without experiencing any obvious adverse effects.

In just about every situation, there will be “some” members of a group who do not exhibit the qualities that many, or most, members of the group exhibit.For instance, some people may smoke cigarettes for decades and never develop lung cancer.Does that mean that cigarettes do not negatively affect people’s health? Of course not.It’s also important to keep in mind that we don’t know what “some” means.Is “some” two people or two million people?

Also, the fact that someone doesn’t experience adverse health effects for “many years” doesn’t mean that that personneverexperiences adverse health effects, and, further, the fact that adverse effects aren’t “obvious” doesn’t mean that adverse effects aren’t present.
(D) There is evidence indicating that consuming diet soda, which contains artificial sweetener instead of sugar, may be more deleterious to health than consuming regular soda.

If you choose this incorrect answer, you may have reasoned that, if people are dissuaded from drinking soda sweetened with sugar, they will switch to drinking diet soda, which is even worse for people than regular soda.Notice, however, that the tax proposed would be on soda of all forms, including diet soda.So, there’s no reason to believe that people would make such a switch.In fact, imposing the tax should cause a reduction in consumption of diet soda, which fact is another reason to believe the argument’s conclusion, that imposing such a tax will result in people’s experiencing fewer diet-related health problems.
YOUR CHOICE(E) When faced with increases in soda prices, many people opt to drink instead significant quantities of alcoholic beverages.

By saying that, when faced with increases in soda prices, many people opt to drink significant quantities of alcoholic beverages instead, this choice provides a reason to question the validity of the argument’s conclusion that “imposing such a tax will result in people’s experiencing fewer diet-related health problems.” After all, drinking alcohol in significant quantities is likely to, or at least very well may, result in health problems.So, a shift from drinking soda to drinking alcohol is quite likely not to result in people’s experiencing fewer diet-related health problems.What this answer choice says is a reason to question the validity of the conclusion of the argument.

question.text: Epidemiologist: Many of the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa have been sickened by a pathogenic soil bacterium, a situation resulting in a decline in the sheep population there.My suggestion is that, to halt the decline in the sheep population, we put in place sheep feeding stations filled with antibiotic-fortified food.Which of the following, if true, most seriously serves to call into question that implementing the proposed plan will have the desired result?

answers.text: The sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa dislike the taste of antibiotic-fortified food.
The antibiotic with which the food will be fortified is highly effective at killing the type of pathogenic soil bacteria sickening the sheep.
Most sheep have voracious appetites, eating a wide variety of foods throughout the day.
The soil bacteria that have infected the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa have also infected other animals that live in the province.
The size of population of the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa is larger today than it was five years ago.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many of the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa have been sickened by a pathogenic soil bacterium, a situation resulting in a decline in the sheep population there.
Conclusion: My suggestion is that, to halt the decline in the sheep population, we put in place sheep feeding stations filled with antibiotic-fortified food.

The epidemiologist has presented a plan.Any time someone presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work.In other words, he assumes that there are not variables that will enter the equation that will spoil the plan.In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When someone presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What might the epidemiologist have failed to consider? What malicious variables might have gone unnoticed?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa dislike the taste of antibiotic-fortified food.

If the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa dislike the taste of antibiotic-fortified food, then they are likely not going to eat the antibiotic-fortified food placed in the sheep feeding stations.If the sheep do not eat the antibiotic-fortified food placed in the sheep feeding stations, then the antibiotic will not kill the bacteria that is making the sheep sick, and the plan will not work.

So, this choice serves to call into question the conclusion that the plan will work by bringing up a malicious variable, the sheep’s not liking the taste of the food, that the epidemiologist has failed to take into account.
(B) The antibiotic with which the food will be fortified is highly effective at killing the type of pathogenic soil bacteria sickening the sheep.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by eliminating a possible weakness in the plan.

Notice that the epidemiologist never presents us with evidence that the antibiotic to be used will kill the bacteria that is infecting the sheep.He may assume that it will, but he never states as fact that it will.
Therefore, the plan is open to the criticism that perhaps the antibiotic will not actually kill the bacteria that is infecting the sheep.

Now, what this answer choice says, that the antibiotic with which the food will be fortified is highly effective at killing the type of pathogenic soil bacteria sickening the sheep, serves to rule out the possibility that the antibiotic does not kill the bacteria, strengthening the case for expecting that the plan will work.
Any time we can rule out a possible objection to an argument by taking an assumption made by an author and turning that assumption into a stated fact (premise), the argument will be stronger.
(C) Most sheep have voracious appetites, eating a wide variety of foods throughout the day.
This answer choice provides support for the conclusion that the plan will work.
After all, if most sheep have voracious appetites, eating a wide variety of foods throughout the day, then we have more reason to believe that these sheep will eat the antibiotic-fortified food.
Of course, we’re not seeking something that strengthens the argument; we’re seeking something that weakens the argument.
(D) The soil bacteria that have infected the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa have also infected other animals that live in the province.
What this answer choice says makes no difference.

The fact that the soil bacteria that have infected the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa have also infected other animals that live in the province shows that these bacteria infect a wide range of organisms, but that fact does not erode our faith in the plan.
The fact of the matter is that the bacteria could infect every animal living there and the plan could still work.
(E) The size of population of the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa is larger today than it was five years ago.

If you chose this choice as correct, you likely did something that we should never do in answering Weaken the Argument questions: you attempted to argue with one of the premises provided in the argument.

You said something like the following.“If the size of population of the sheep living wild in the remote province of Tangaroa is larger today than it was five years ago, then the author is wrong, and there hasn’t really been a decline in the sheep population.So, putting out antibiotic-fortified food won’t halt a decline, because there’s no decline to halt.”
However, that logic is unsound.The argument has already established as fact that the sheep’s being sickened by the bacterium is “a situation resulting in a decline in the sheep population there.”
We can’t argue with that fact.

Also, notice that the fact that the size of population of the sheep is larger today than it was five years ago could be completely true, and at the same time, it could also be completely true that, currently, the sheep population is declining.

The bottom line is that the fact that the sheep population is larger than it was five years ago does not affect the likelihood that putting out antibiotic-fortified food will halt the current decline in the sheep population.So, this choice has no effect on the argument.

question.text: City Council Member: Much of the air pollution in Goldenton is produced via the burning of fuel by automobiles.Meanwhile, most people using automobiles in Goldenton do not need the full capacity of such a vehicle and could easily accomplish their travel goals by using motorbikes instead of automobiles.Since a motorbike burns less fuel than an automobile traveling the same distance, by getting people to switch from using automobiles to using motorbikes, we can reduce air pollution in Goldenton.Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest reason to question the conclusion?

answers.text: Because motorbikes are smaller than automobiles, if a certain number of automobiles is replaced by the same number of motorbikes in a city, street congestion may be reduced.
Some of the people who currently use automobiles in Goldenton do not know how to operate motorbikes and would refuse to use them.
The average total number of miles traveled each day on the roads of Goldenton by people using automobiles has not increased significantly over the past 10 years.
Because the pollution control devices on motorbikes are less sophisticated than those on automobiles, motorbikes emit more pollution per unit of fuel burned than automobiles emit.
The amount of air pollution created via automobile use in Goldenton could be significantly reduced via improving traffic flow and thus reducing road congestion.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Much of the air pollution in Goldenton is produced via the burning of fuel by automobiles.
Background: People could accomplish their travel goals by using motorbikes instead of automobiles.
Premise: In traveling a distance, a motorbike burns less fuel than is burned by an automobile traveling that distance.
Conclusion: By getting people to switch from using automobiles to using motorbikes, we can reduce air pollution.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since pollution is produced via the burning of fuel, and motorbikes burn less fuel than automobiles do, if people used motorbikes rather than automobiles to travel around Goldenton, there would be less air pollution in Goldenton.However, while the use of a motorbike instead of an automobile may result in a reduction in the fuel burned when a person makes a given trip, replacing automobiles with motorbikes could introduce of a variety of new factors into the situation, some of which could cause air pollution to increase rather than decrease.The correct answer choice will discuss one such factor and, thus, serve to call into question the conclusion of the argument.
(A) Because motorbikes are smaller than automobiles, if a certain number of automobiles is replaced by the same number of motorbikes in a city, street congestion may be reduced.

This answer choice could be spun to either undermine or support the conclusion that the switch from automobiles to motorbikes will reduce air pollution.For example, one could suppose that, with reduced street congestion, people will decide to travel around the city more, and therefore create more pollution.On the other hand, if people mainly travel around Goldenton for practical purposes, such as getting to and from work, and not for pleasure, perhaps travel would not increase, and the roads would simply be less congested, with the result that air pollution resulting from congestion would be reduced.We have no way to know what the effect of what this answer choice says would be, and thus this choice does not clearly serve to call into question the conclusion of the argument.
(B) Some of the people who currently use automobiles in Goldenton do not know how to operate motorbikes and would refuse to use them.
This choice brings up a factor that could constrain the number of people who could be convinced to switch from driving automobiles to using motorbikes.

However, the fact that some people do not know how to operate motorbikes and would refuse to use them does not mean that convincing people to switch from using automobiles to using motorbikes would not result in a reduction in air pollution.
After all, if other people are convinced to make the switch, then a reduction in air pollution could still be achieved.
(C) The average total number of miles traveled each day on the roads of Goldenton by people using automobiles has not increased significantly over the past 10 years.

If a test-taker read this answer quickly without really considering what it says, it could appear to undermine the argument, perhaps because it could be taken as somehow indicating that automobiles’ burning fuel is not a significant cause of air pollution in Goldenton.However, neither automobile use nor pollution produced by automobiles has to be increasing in order for such pollution to be reduced by replacing automobiles with motorbikes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Because the pollution control devices on motorbikes are less sophisticated than those on automobiles, motorbikes emit more pollution per unit of fuel burned than automobiles emit.

If motorbikes emit more pollution per unit of fuel burned than automobiles emit, it could be that, even though a motorbike burns less fuel than an automobile over a given distance, a motorbike might produce the same or more pollution than an automobile does.Therefore, replacing automobiles with motorbikes might not result in a reduction in air pollution, and thus this choice serves to call the conclusion of the argument into question.
(E) The amount of air pollution created via automobile use in Goldenton could be significantly reduced via improving traffic flow and thus reducing road congestion.

The conclusion is not that there is no way to reduce air pollution in Goldenton other than by replacing automobiles with motorbikes.Therefore, the existence of an alternative way to reduce air pollution in Goldenton does not affect the conclusion.We must be careful of answer choices that present a different or better plan.That a better plan is out there does not weaken the support for the conclusion that the plan presented by the author will work.

question.text: Governor: This past year alone, hundreds of our citizens have been injured by non-lethal rubber bullets fired at them by police officers during mass confrontations.In response, we have passed a new law prohibiting the use of non-lethal rubber bullets by police officers.Thus, we can be sure that, going forward, our citizens will not be injured in mass confrontations with our police officers.Which of the following, if true, provides the most compelling reason to call into question the above conclusion?

answers.text: All of the police officers will carefully follow the new law.
Some police officers will refuse to get involved in mass confrontations if they are prohibited from using non-lethal rubber bullets.
Social workers who are trained to respond to public disturbances can diffuse volatile situations much better than police officers.
Many confrontations between police officers and citizens are peacefully and respectfully handled.
Without the ability to use non-lethal rubber bullets in confrontations with citizens, police officers will be forced to use wooden sticks, which can cause broken bones.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: This past year alone, hundreds of our citizens have been injured by non-lethal rubber bullets fired at them by police officers during mass confrontations.
Premise: In response, we have passed a new law prohibiting the use of non-lethal rubber bullets by police officers.
Conclusion: Thus, we can be sure that, going forward, our citizens will not be injured in mass confrontations with our police officers.

The Governor has presented a plan.Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work.In other words, she assumes that there are not variables that will enter the equation that will spoil the plan.In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What might the author have failed to consider? What malicious variables might have gone unnoticed? Any malicious variable will weaken the argument.
(A) All of the police officers will carefully follow the new law.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
After all, if all the police officers will carefully follow the new law, then we have more faith that the plan will work.
The reverse of this statement would be the statement that none of the police officers will carefully follow the new law.Of course, that statement would weaken the argument.
(B) Some police officers will refuse to get involved in mass confrontations if they are prohibited from using non-lethal rubber bullets.
This choice serves to call into question the wrong conclusion.It serves to call into question a conclusion along the lines of that prohibiting the use of rubber bullets won’t cause any issues.

Notice, however, that this choice does not undermine the support for the conclusion of this argument, which is that, as a result of the passage of the new law prohibiting the use of rubber bullets, citizens will not be injured in mass confrontations with our police officers.

The fact that some police officers will refuse to get involved in such confrontations is not a reason to question that conclusion.The police officers who do not get involved certainly will not injure citizens, and the police officers who do get involved won’t be using rubber bullets.

To avoid choosing an incorrect choice like this one, we have to be careful not to choose a choice just because it seems to say something negative about a plan.We have to find a choice that undermines the support for or serves call into question a specific conclusion, not one that merely seems to say something negative about what has been proposed.
(C) Social workers who are trained to respond to public disturbances can diffuse volatile situations much better than police officers.

This choice weakens the support for the wrong conclusion.If the conclusion were that police officers are the best at handling volatile situations, of course this choice would weaken the support for that conclusion.

However, the conclusion is not about how well police officers or anyone else handles volatile situations.It’s about the effects of prohibiting the use of non-lethal rubber bullets by police officers, and regardless of whether social workers can diffuse volatile situations better than police officers, prohibiting the use of non-lethal rubber bullets may prevent citizens’ being injured.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(D) Many confrontations between police officers and citizens are peacefully and respectfully handled.

The fact that many confrontations between police officers and citizens are peacefully and respectfully handled is fine, but the governor’s argument centers on the times when citizenswould beinjured by non-lethal rubber bullets fired at them by police officers during mass confrontations.

The fact that many confrontations are handled in ways that probably would not result in citizens being injured does not weaken the case for believing that, in cases in which confrontations are not peacefully handled, the new law will keep citizens from being injured.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Without the ability to use non-lethal rubber bullets in confrontations with citizens, police officers will be forced to use wooden sticks, which can cause broken bones.

The governor just passed a new law prohibiting the use of non-lethal rubber bullets by police officers.As a result of that law, he concludes that “we can be sure that, going forward, our citizens will not be injured in mass confrontations with our police officers.”

However, if it is true that without the ability to use non-lethal rubber bullets in confrontations with citizens, police officers will be forced to use wooden sticks, which can cause broken bones, then we have less faith in the governor’s argument.
After all, if police move to using wooden sticks, which can cause broken bones, then it very well might be the case that the citizens WILL be injured in mass confrontations with the police officers.
Notice that this answer choice presents a malicious variable that the governor has failed to account for.

question.text: The unique, fruit heavy diets of people who live on the island of Xetilios must cause people to live longer than normal, since government records of the ages of people who have lived on Xetilios indicate that, on average, people living on Xetilios live to ages that are significantly older than the average age to which people live worldwide.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the conclusion presented?

answers.text: Many people who do not live on Xetilios live to the age that is the average age to which people living on Xetilios live.
People who retire to Xetilios from the mainland and, therefore, have not followed fruit-heavy diets for most of their lives, live no longer, on average, than people do worldwide.
On average, inhabitants of the island of Zytelios, which is near to Xetilios, who follow nut-heavy diets, live longer than inhabitants of Xetilios do.
The fruits typically eaten by inhabitants of Xetilios are not as nutritious as fruits of many types found elsewhere but not on Xetilios.
To receive certain benefits early, inhabitants of Xetilios often inform the government that they have reached age 65 before they actually have reached that age.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Government records indicate that, on average, people living on Xetilios live significantly longer than people do worldwide.
Conclusion: The unique, fruit heavy diets of people who live on the island of Xetilios must cause people to live longer than normal.
(A) Many people who do not live on Xetilios live to the age that is the average age to which people living on Xetilios live.

Notice that what this answer choice says does not change the facts provided to us.In the passage, we are told that government records indicate that, on average, people living on Xetilios live to ages that are significantly older than the average age to which people live worldwide.The argument’s conclusion rests upon that statistic.We know that within a group, some people will live to an age greater than the average age, just as some people will live to an age less than the average age.So, what this choice says does not change anything about the evidence presented nor the support for the conclusion.
(B) People who retire to Xetilios from the mainland and, therefore, have not followed fruit-heavy diets for most of their lives, live no longer, on average, than people do worldwide.

What this answer choice says adds support for the conclusion.After all, the statement in this choice indicates that when the cause, a fruit-heavy diet, is removed, so too is the effect, greater-than-average longevity.Whenever we can show that when the cause is removed, so too is the effect, the case for believing that the cause-and-effect link exists becomes strengthened.However, we’re seeking an answer choice that weakens, not strengthens, the argument.
(C) On average, inhabitants of the island of Zytelios, which is near to Xetilios, who follow nut-heavy diets, live longer than inhabitants of Xetilios do.

This answer choice essentially tells us that there is an even better diet to follow than a fruit-heavy one.However, the fact that there is a better diet (or a better plan) does not weaken the support for the conclusion that the diet in question is better than average.Thus, what this answer choice says has no effect on the argument or the conclusion.
(D) The fruits typically eaten by inhabitants of Xetilios are not as nutritious as fruits of many types found elsewhere but not on Xetilios.

Notice that what this answer choice says, that the fruits typically eaten by inhabitants of Xetilios are not as nutritious as fruits of many types found elsewhere but not on Xetilios, does not change the facts that people who live on Xetilios eat unique, fruit-heavy diets, and that people who live on Xetilios live to ages significantly older than the average age to which people live worldwide.In other words, even given what this answer choice says, the facts upon which the author’s conclusion rests are still true and still support the conclusion.What this answer choice says has no affect.
YOUR CHOICE(E) To receive certain benefits early, inhabitants of Xetilios often inform the government that they have reached age 65 before they actually have reached that age.

This choice may seem off topic and, therefore, irrelevant, because the argument is about the effect of a fruit-heavy diet on longevity, while this choice discusses fraudulent behavior of inhabitants of Xetilios.Notice, though, that the argument’s conclusion that the fruit-heavy diets of Xetilios inhabitants cause people to live longer than normal is based on the fact thatgovernment age recordsindicate that Xetilios inhabitants live to ages that are significantly older than the average age to which people live worldwide.

If people on Xetilios often tell the government that they have reached age 65 before they actually have, then it follows that government records often indicate that residents of Xetilios are older than they actually are and, therefore, often indicate that these people live to ages older than the ages to which they actually live.Thus, this choice undermines the support for the conclusion by indicating that the government records upon which the conclusion is based overstate how long people on Xetilios live.

question.text: After receiving complaints that certified organic bakeries, which are allowed to use only organic ingredients, in the City of Moss Hills were using non-organic ingredients in their products, the Moss Hills city council passed a new rule allowing food inspectors to stop into any certified organic bakery unannounced and fine any such bakery caught using non-organic ingredients.Clearly, the new rule will prevent the certified organic bakeries in the City of Moss Hills from using non-organic ingredients in their products.Which of the following, if true, most seriously calls into question the expectation that the new rule will have the desired effect?

answers.text: Food inspectors do not have the authority to revoke the business license of a certified organic bakery caught using non-organic ingredients in its products.
The City of Moss Hills will double the number of food inspectors tasked with certifying that organic bakeries are not using non-organic ingredients in their products.
Not all certified organic bakeries in the City of Moss Hill have been using non-organic ingredients in their products.
The money a bakery saves by using non-organic rather than organic ingredients can quickly exceed the cost of the fine for using non-organic ingredients.
The Moss Hills Health Department does not respond to a certified organic bakery’s being caught using non-organic ingredients by reducing the health rating of that bakery.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: After receiving complaints that certified organic bakeries, which are allowed to use only organic ingredients, in the City of Moss Hills were using non-organic ingredients in their products, the Moss Hills city council passed a new rule allowing food inspectors to stop into any certified organic bakery unannounced and fine any such bakery caught using non-organic ingredients.
Conclusion: Clearly, the new rule will prevent the certified organic bakeries in the City of Moss Hills from using non-organic ingredients in their products.

The author has presented a plan.Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work.In other words, she assumes that there are not variables that will enter the equation that will spoil the plan.In the TTP Course, we refer to such variables as “malicious variables.” When an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables.
What might the author have failed to consider? What malicious variables might have gone unnoticed? Any malicious variable will weaken the argument.
(A) Food inspectors do not have the authority to revoke the business license of a certified organic bakery caught using non-organic ingredients in its products.

The plan is that food inspectors will be able to fine organic bakeries using non-organic ingredients.Of course, it is common knowledge that businesses do not enjoy paying fines.Thus, the author believes that the threat of the fine will be sufficient to affect the behavior of the certified organic bakeries and will prevent the certified organic bakeries from using non-organic ingredients in their products.

This answer choice brings up another form of punishment, revoking a bakery’s business license, that the food inspectors do not have the authority to use.The fact that there are other forms of punishment that cannot be used by the food inspectors does not weaken the case for believing that the threat of the fine will affect the bakeries’ behavior.

There could be millions of forms of punishment that the food inspectors cannot use, and the author’s argument could still be sound, because she’s basing her conclusion on the one punishment that the food inspectors CAN use – the fine.
(B) The City of Moss Hills will double the number of food inspectors tasked with certifying that organic bakeries are not using non-organic ingredients in their products.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument.

After all, if the City of Moss Hills will double the number of food inspectors tasked with certifying that organic bakeries are not using non-organic ingredients in their products, then we have more faith that the plan will work, because more inspectors equates to more robust enforcement of the rule.
Of course, we’re not seeking an answer choice that strengthens the argument; we’re seeking one that weakens it.
(C) Not all certified organic bakeries in the City of Moss Hill have been using non-organic ingredients in their products.
The fact that not all certified organic bakeries in the City of Moss Hills have been using non-organic ingredients in their products does not weaken the argument.

The argument is about the effect of the fine on the ones that have been using non-organic ingredients.The fact that not all have been doing so is not a reason to question whether the plan will change the behavior of the ones that have.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The money a bakery saves by using non-organic rather than organic ingredients can quickly exceed the cost of the fine for using non-organic ingredients.

Let’s think about it.What’s the driving force behind the city’s plan? It’s the fine, right? Of course, the idea is that fines are costly, and businesses do not enjoy paying fines.Therefore, the author expects that the fine will be a deterrent sufficient to prevent organic bakeries from using non-organic ingredients.

However, what if, as this choice says, the money a bakery saves by using non-organic rather than organic ingredients can quickly exceed the cost of the fine for using non-organic ingredients? Then maybe the fine won’t change the bakeries’ behavior, because, in that case, the bakeries might actually make more money by taking the risk and using non-organic ingredients, because, even if they are caught and pay a fine, they may still be better off from a dollars and cents point of view.Sure, the fine will cost them, but they may save more than the fine costs by using non-organic ingredients.
Notice that this answer choice presents us with a malicious variable that the author failed to account for.
(E) The Moss Hills Health Department does not respond to a certified organic bakery’s being caught using non-organic ingredients by reducing the health rating of that bakery.
This choice is a tempting pseudo-weakener that has no effect on the argument.
The conclusion of the argument is specifically about the effect of the new rule involving the fine.
This choice brings up another possible consequence of an organic bakery’s getting caught using non-organic ingredients, a reduction in its health rating.

The fact that the Health Department does not respond to a certified organic bakery’s being caught using non-organic ingredients by reducing the health rating of that bakery does not mean that the threat of the fine won’t change the behavior of the bakeries.All it means is that there is not a certain additional factor that could affect the behavior of the bakeries.
To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing a choice just because it says something that seems to attack the conclusion in some vague way.

question.text: At the beginning of this past year, Rambutan, the iconic Asian restaurant located in the heart of Asherton’s theater district, shifted most of its advertising spending from Internet-based providers to local radio stations.It is clear that, for Rambutan, advertising on local radio is more effective than advertising on the Internet, as Rambutan’s revenues during this past year were 8 percent greater than its revenues during the year before.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call the conclusion into question?

answers.text: Over 50 percent of Rambutan customers surveyed during the past two years said that they had chosen to dine there because of what they had heard from other customers.
The Asherton Chamber of Commerce estimates that about 19 percent more people went to shows in Asherton's theater district this past year than went the year before.
This past year, more restaurants in Asherton’s theater district shifted some advertising from radio to the Internet than shifted advertising from the Internet to radio.
A survey of theatergoers in Asherton found that, on average, they spend as much time using the Internet as they spend listening to the radio.
Using the Internet, restaurants can get a lot of publicity without spending any money on Internet-based advertising.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Rambutan is an iconic Asian restaurant located in the heart of Asherton’s theater district.
Premise: At the beginning of this past year, Rambutan shifted most of its advertising spending from Internet-based providers to local radio stations.
Premise: Rambutan’s revenues during this past year were 8 percent greater than its revenues during the year before.
Conclusion: For Rambutan, advertising on local radio is more effective than advertising on the Internet.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since Rambutan’s revenues increased 8 percent after it shifted its advertising from the Internet to local radio stations, advertising on local radio is more effective for Rambutan than advertising on the Internet.The argument relies on the assumption that, since the increase in revenue followed the shift from Internet advertising to radio advertising, the revenue increase was caused by the change in advertising method.An answer choice that indicates that a factor other than the shift in advertising method was likely the cause of the revenue increase would weaken the argument.Remember, we must be skeptical of cause-and-effect claims.The fact that one change occurs before another change does not mean that the first change is the cause of the second change.
(A) Over 50 percent of Rambutan customers surveyed during the past two years said that they had chosen to dine there because of what they had heard from other customers.

The conclusion is specifically about the value to Rambutan of radio advertising and Internet advertising.The fact that most of Rambutan’s customers dine there because of factors other than advertising does not affect this conclusion.

Notice also that this choice mentions survey information from“the past two years.”This information on what has occurred over the entirety of the past two years does not indicate why Rambutan's revenues changed from one of those two years to the next.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The Asherton Chamber of Commerce estimates that about 19 percent more people went to shows in Asherton's theater district this past year than went the year before.

If 19 percent more people went to shows in Asherton's theatre district this past year than went the year before, it could be the case that Rambutan’s revenues increased not because of the switch from Internet to radio advertising but because there were more people eating in the theater district in general.So, by showing that there was another factor that could very well have been the cause of the increase in Rambutan’s revenues, this choice serves to call into question the conclusion that the change in advertising methods caused the revenue increase.In other words, what this answer choice says is that there is another plausible explanation for the observed effect.
(C) This past year, more restaurants in Asherton’s theater district shifted some advertising from radio to the Internet than shifted advertising from the Internet to radio.

The fact that more restaurants shifted some advertising from radio to the Internet than shifted advertising from the Internet to radio may seem to indicate that advertising on the Internet is preferred and, therefore, must be better than advertising on the radio.However, we don’t have enough information to confidently come to that conclusion; there could be another reason for last year’s trend toward using the Internet for advertising.Furthermore, the conclusion of the argument is about Rambutan specifically, which could be better off using radio even if other restaurants are not.So, this choice does not affect the argument about which of the two advertising methods is more effective for Rambutan.
(D) A survey of theatergoers in Asherton found that, on average, they spend as much time using the Internet as they spend listening to the radio.

Regardless of the fact that theatergoers spend as much time using the Internet as they spend listening to the radio, it could still be the case that advertising on local radio is more effective for Rambutan than advertising on the Internet.

For instance, it could be the case that customers pay more attention to radio advertising than they do to Internet advertising.In that case, even though they spend as much time using the Internet as they spend listening to the radio, radio advertising would be more effective than Internet advertising.
(E) Using the Internet, restaurants can get a lot of publicity without spending any money on Internet-based advertising.

The conclusion is specifically about which advertising method is more effective for Rambutan, not which one costs less.The fact that restaurants in general can use the Internet to get publicity without paying for Internet advertising does not affect whether radio advertising is more effective for Rambutan than Internet advertising is.

question.text: Goji berries are becoming more popular as the nutritional benefits of eating them become more widely known.Some people in rural communities in Asia who once lived in poverty are now much better off financially as a result of their farming of goji berries, which often sell for $25 a pound or more.Since the bushes that bear goji berries grow in a variety of environments, by encouraging people living in impoverished communities to farm goji berries, we can substantially reduce poverty worldwide.Which of the following, if true, most strongly indicates that the suggested plan would not work as intended?

answers.text: Because of the high per-pound cost of goji berries, many people who consume large amounts of goji berries prefer to grow their own goji berries.
Unlike grains, which are harvested within months of planting, goji berries do not become available for harvesting until three years after the seeds have been planted.
When the supply of a commodity increases, the price of that commodity can decrease precipitously.
Many people worldwide have never eaten or heard of goji berries.
Most health-conscious consumers prefer to eat fruit that has been grown without the use of toxic synthetic pesticides.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Goji berries are becoming more popular as the nutritional benefits of eating them become more widely known.
Premise: Some people in rural communities in Asia who once lived in poverty are now much better off financially as a result of their farming of goji berries, which often sell for $25 a pound or more.

Conclusion: Since the bushes that bear goji berries grow in a variety of environments, by encouraging people living in impoverished communities to farm goji berries, we can substantially reduce poverty worldwide.

Think about it.At the moment, there are some people growing and selling goji berries, which often sell for $25 a pound or more.The author concludes that, by encouraging people living in impoverished communities to farm goji berries, we can substantially reduce poverty worldwide.Has the author missed something important? What variable would derail this plan? Remember, when a speaker proposes a plan, he or she assumes that the plan will work as expected and that other factors will not derail the plan.
(A) Because of the high per-pound cost of goji berries, many people who consume large amounts of goji berries prefer to grow their own goji berries.
First, how many people are “many” people? “Many” people could be, for example, 10,000 people, out of billions.In such a case, “many” would not be a significant number.

Second, notice that what this answer choice says discusses a unique population of people: those who consume large amounts of goji berries.Many of the people in this unique population could very well grow their own goji berries, and their doing so would not affect the argument.The fact is, people are already making money selling goji berries, so there are obviously people buying the berries as opposed to growing their own supply.The fact that people who consume large amounts of the berries grow their own does not mean that there is no viable market for goji berries.The passage tells us that there is.
(B) Unlike grains, which are harvested within months of planting, goji berries do not become available for harvesting until three years after the seeds have been planted.

Notice that the argument never makes the claim that the change in people’s financial circumstances would happen overnight or in any particular time frame.So, the fact the goji berries are not available for harvesting until three years after the seeds have been planted does not mean that farmers in impoverished communities wouldn’t, eventually, become less impoverished as a result of their growing and selling the berries.What this answer choice says does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) When the supply of a commodity increases, the price of that commodity can decrease precipitously.

If it is true that, when the supply of a commodity increases, the price of that commodity can decrease precipitously, then we have a very good reason to believe that the author’s plan will not work as anticipated.Imagine what would occur if the number of people growing and selling goji berries doubled, or tripled, or went up 500 percent.In any of these cases, the price at which goji berries could be sold could drop substantially.If the price were to drop substantially, the goal of substantially reducing poverty might not be reached, since farming goji berries would no longer be such a profitable endeavor.
(D) Many people worldwide have never eaten or heard of goji berries.

That there are many people worldwide who have neither eaten nor heard of goji berries does not affect the strength of the argument.This fact could coexist with the fact that millions of people regularly consume goji berries.In fact, this fact could strengthen the support for the conclusion, by indicating that there are many potential purchasers of goji berries who have not even heard of the berries yet.
(E) Most health-conscious consumers prefer to eat fruit that has been grown without the use of toxic synthetic pesticides.

We have noreason to believe that goji berries are grown or would be grown via the use of toxic synthetic pesticides.So, what this answer choice says is not clearly connected to the argument and does nothing to make the conclusion any less plausible.

question.text: In order to improve the bone health of the citizens of Verdantia, the Verdantian Ministry of Health has a goal of significantly increasing Verdantians’ average daily intake of calcium.In order to achieve this goal, the Ministry of Health has proposed promoting eating kale and snap beans.Since both vegetables are high in calcium, implementing the proposed plan would result in Verdantians’ consuming more calcium than they do currently.Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the conclusion above?

answers.text: Verdantian citizens have already increased their consumption of almonds and dates, both of which contain significant amounts of calcium.
Many older Verdantian citizens do not use the internet, which is the medium via which the ministry would promote eating kale and green beans.
The finance director of the Ministry of Health has indicated that, currently, the ministry may not have funds available for undertaking any new projects.
Because neither kale nor snap beans are produced in large quantities in Verdantia, both would have to be imported from neighboring countries.
Verdantians choosing to eat kale and snap beans would reduce their consumption of collard greens and okra, both of which contain large amounts of calcium.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In order to improve the bone health of the citizens of Verdantia, the Verdantian Ministry of Health has a goal of significantly increasing Verdantians’ average daily intake of calcium.
Premise: In order to achieve this goal, the Ministry of Health has proposed promoting eating kale and snap beans.
Conclusion: Since both vegetables are high in calcium, implementing the proposed plan would result in Verdantians’ consuming more calcium than they do currently.

Notice that the ministry has proposed a plan.We must remember that, whenever a plan is proposed, the plan’s creator assumes that the plan will work as proposed.Might that assumption be faulty in this case? Might there be a variable that the ministry has not considered that could derail its plan?
(A) Verdantian citizens have already increased their consumption of almonds and dates, both of which contain significant amounts of calcium.

That Verdantian citizens have increased their consumption of almonds and dates, both of which contain significant amounts of calcium, does not have an impact on the effectiveness of the ministry’s plan, and thus does not weaken the argument.Despite Verdantians’ increased consumption of almonds and dates, which are high in calcium, the author’s conclusion that “implementing the proposed plan would result in Verdantians’ consuming more calciumthan they do currently” is not undermined.The phrase “consuming more calcium than they do currently” is key here because it takes into account the calcium from almonds and dates.Regardless of the fact that “citizens have increased their consumption of almonds and dates, both of which have lots of calcium,” the citizens could still consumemorecalcium via the addition of kale and snap beans to their diets.Thus, what this answer choice says does not affect the argument or weaken the conclusion.
(B) Many older Verdantian citizens do not use the internet, which is the medium via which the ministry would promote eating kale and green beans.

The argument’s conclusion is that “implementing the proposed plan would result in Verdantians’ consuming more calcium than they do currently.” The conclusion isnotthat “all Verdantians will increase their consumption.”

Even if no older people end up eating more kale and snap beans, people who use the internet, and thus are privy to the ministry’s online campaign, still may.So, even if a whole segment of the population is left out, overall calcium intake still may increase.

Anotherthing to notice about this answer choice is that it says “many.” Is “many” 1,000 people? Two million people? We don’t know, and “many” may not be a significant number of people relative to the entire population.[LG1]
(C) The finance director of the Ministry of Health has indicated that, currently, the ministry may not have funds available for undertaking any new projects.

Notice that what this answer choice says serves to call into question the wrong conclusion; it serves to call into question the conclusion that the ministry is in a position to implement the plan, not the argument’s conclusion, which is that the plan, if implemented, would have certain results.Thus, this answer choice has no bearing on the argument or the conclusion.
(D) Because neither kale nor snap beans are produced in large quantities in Verdantia, both would have to be imported from neighboring countries.

Regardless of whetherkale and snap beans have to be imported from neighboring countries, as long as Verdantians have some way to access those foods, the plan could be successful.Thus, what this answer choice says does not weaken the argument or serve to call into question the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Verdantians choosing to eat kale and snap beans would reduce their consumption of collard greens and okra, both of which contain large amounts of calcium.

The fact that Verdantians’ choosing to eat kale and snap beans would result in a reduction in their consumption of collard greens and okra, both of which contain large amounts of calcium is a reason to question the author’s conclusion.Remember, the argument is that, since kale and snap beans are both high in calcium, “implementing the proposed plan would result in Verdantians’ consuming more calcium than they do currently.” However, if Verdantians simply replace one calcium source with another, then the net effect might be no significant change in calcium intake, rather than an increase.While this choice does not prove for sure that implementing the plan will not result in an increase in calcium intake, it does provide a reason to question the conclusion.

question.text: Head Chef: Heavy rainstorms in the Midwest have damaged corn crops, and the reduction in expected corn supply has resulted in an increase in the price of corn and, therefore, the price of corn syrup.However, we use beet sugar rather than corn syrup in our cookie recipes.So, the rainstorms in the Midwest should not affect the cost of our cookie ingredients.Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the head chef’s conclusion?

answers.text: In areas where sugar beets are grown, frost can severely damage plants, reducing the volume of harvested sugar beets.
When corn syrup is in short supply, many commercial users of corn syrup temporarily switch to using other forms of sugar.
Most wholesale buyers of sugar feel that beet sugar is a form of sugar higher in quality than corn syrup.
The damage to the wheat crop caused by a late spring snowstorm is expected to result in an increase in the price of wheat flour, a key ingredient in cookies.
Even under normal market conditions, beet sugar is usually more expensive than corn syrup.

explanation_parts.text: Here is a breakdown of the argument.
Background: Heavy rainstorms in the Midwest have resulted in a reduction in the expected supply of corn and, therefore, in an increase in the price of corn syrup.
Premise: We use beet sugar, not corn syrup, in cookie recipes.
Conclusion: The rainstorms in the Midwest should not affect the cost of our cookie ingredients.
The conclusion is specific.It is thatthe rainstorms in the Midwestshould not affect thecostof the chef’s cookie ingredients.
An answer choice that somehow indicates that the rainstorms in the Midwest will affect the cost of cookie ingredients will weaken the argument.
(A) In areas where sugar beets are grown, frost can severely damage plants, reducing the volume of harvested sugar beets.

What this choice says indicates thatweathercan affect beet crops in ways similar to how the rainstorms in the Midwest affected the corn crop.However, the argument mentionsrainstorms, while this choice mentionsfrostas the weather phenomenon that can damage beet plants.Therefore, this choice does not connect the rainstorms to the beet crops or to the price of beet sugar, and thus it does not affect the conclusion.Also, note that this choice does not even tell us whether frost actually occurred or did any damage to the current beet crop; it simply makes a statement of fact that frost can damage beet crops.
YOUR CHOICE(B) When corn syrup is in short supply, many commercial users of corn syrup temporarily switch to using other forms of sugar.

Given what this choice says, it seems likely that, because of the rainstorms’ affecting the corn crop, commercial users of corn syrup will switch to using other forms of sugar.Beet sugar is a form of sugar.So, it could be that because of the storms, commercial users of corn syrup will switch to using beet sugar, thus increasing the demand for beet sugar and driving up its price.Since this choice connects the effects of the rainstorms to a potential, or even likely, increase in the price of beet sugar, it is damaging to the conclusion that the storms will not affect the price of the ingredients that the head chef uses in making cookies.Notice that what this answer choice says underscores a key point that the author of the argument has failed to consider.
(C) Most wholesale buyers of sugar feel that beet sugar is a form of sugar higher in quality than corn syrup.

While this choice compares beet sugar with corn syrup, it does not give us any reason to believe that the price of beet sugar will be affected by the rainstorms in the Midwest.In fact, this choice does not tell us anything that affects the validity of the argument one way or the other.
(D) The damage to the wheat crop caused by a late spring snowstorm is expected to result in an increase in the price of wheat flour, a key ingredient in cookies.
This choice is tempting, because it gives us a reason to believe that the cost of cookie ingredients will increase: the price of wheat flour is expected to increase.

However, the conclusion the correct answer will damage is that the rainstorms in the Midwest will not cause the cost of the chef’s cookie ingredients to increase.This answer choice is about the effects of a snowstorm, not the rainstorms in the Midwest.So, this choice does not damage the conclusion that the rainstorms in the Midwest will not affect the cost of cookie ingredients.
(E) Even under normal market conditions, beet sugar is usually more expensive than corn syrup.
This answer choice could be tempting, because it discusses a relationship between the price of beet sugar and the price of corn syrup.

However, what it says does not indicate that this relationship in price always holds, as it says that the relationship holds “in normal market conditions.” Further, it does not provide information on the current relationship between the price of corn syrup and the price of beet sugar.For all we know from what this choice says, even at its current increased level, the price of corn syrup is well below that of beet sugar.

So, this answer choice does not give us a clear reason to believe that either the price of corn or the storms in the Midwest will affect the price of beet sugar and, therefore, does not damage the support for the conclusion.

question.text: Concerned Citizen: Colony collapse disorder involves entire colonies of honeybees becoming disoriented and flying off and dying.Much of the blame for this disorder has been placed on the effects that neonicotinoid pesticides used in agriculture have on bees.That placement of blame is clearly incorrect, as honeybee colonies in our town, where there are no farms, are experiencing colony collapse disorder at the same rate as honeybee colonies in neighboring towns that are agricultural communities.Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the argument?

answers.text: Neonicotinoid pesticides have been found in pollen, which honeybees use for food, in the neighboring towns referred to by the concerned citizen.
In searching for food, honeybees often travel several miles from the location of the hive that serves as the home of their colony.
Neonicotinoid pesticides are designed to interfere with the nervous systems of insects.
The practice of feeding honeybees high fructose corn syrup has resulted in a weakening of the immune systems of honeybees, which are therefore susceptible to infection.
Agricultural production has been negatively affected by the colony collapse disorder-caused reduction in the population of honeybees, which serve a key function in the growth of crops.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Much of the blame for colony collapse disorder has been placed on the effects that neonicotinoid pesticides used in agriculture have on bees.
Premise: Honeybee colonies in our town, where there are no farms, are experiencing colony collapse disorder at the same rate as colonies in agricultural towns.
Conclusion: Placement of blame for colony collapse disorder on neonicotinoid pesticides is incorrect.

The argument concludes that colony collapse disorder is not caused by neonicotinoid pesticides by relying on the assumption that, because there are no farms in a particular town, the bees in that town are not affected by neonicotinoid pesticides.Is this assumption a sound one? Might the author have missed something? The argument can be weakened by information that indicates that the assumption is false.
(A) Neonicotinoid pesticides have been found in pollen, which honeybees use for food, in the neighboring towns referred to by the concerned citizen.

A test-taker might perceive this choice as confirming that neonicotinoid pesticides are harming bees and thus causing colony collapse disorder.However, what this choice says does not affect the support for the conclusion, which is that, even in a town where neonicotinoid pesticides are not used, colony collapse disorder is occurring.

If this choice had said that neonicotinoid pesticides had been found in pollen in the town where they are not used, it would have weakened the support for the conclusion by providing reason to believe that the pesticides were causing colony collapse disorder even in the town where they are not used.However, the information in this choice is basically in line with what has already been stated, that neonicotinoid pesticides are used in the neighboring towns.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In searching for food, honeybees often travel several miles from the location of the hive that serves as the home of their colony.

If bees fly several miles from their hives in searching for food, then bees from a town without farms could search for food in neighboring towns that do have farms and thus come into contact with neonicotinoid pesticides.That this is the case weakens the argument, which is based on the assumption that bees in the town without farms are not affected by neonicotinoid pesticides.
(C) Neonicotinoid pesticides are designed to interfere with the nervous systems of insects.

While this answer choice explains how neonicotinoid pesticides could harm bees, it does not affect the argument, which does not dispute that the pesticides could somehow harm bees but maintains only that colony collapse disorder is not caused by the pesticides.
(D) The practice of feeding honeybees high fructose corn syrup has resulted in a weakening of the immune systems of honeybees, which are therefore susceptible to infection.

What this choice says could perhaps serve as an alternate explanation for colony collapse disorder.However, to answer this question we do not need an alternate explanation for colony collapse disorder; we need a weakener, and the fact that feeding honeybees corn syrup results in their having weakened immune systems does not weaken this argument.

Rather, by providing an alternative explanation for colony collapse disorder, this choice provides some support for the conclusion that the use of neonicotinoid pesticides is not to blame for colony collapse disorder.So, if anything, the effect of this choice is to provide additional support for the conclusion.
(E) Agricultural production has been negatively affected by the colony collapse disorder-caused reduction in the population of honeybees, which serve a key function in the growth of crops.

This choice could be tempting in that what it says sounds plausible, is related to the topic of the argument, and connects colony collapse disorder and agriculture.However, the fact that colony collapse disorder is negatively affecting agricultural production does not conflict with the conclusion that neonicotinoid pesticides used in agriculture are not to blame for colony collapse disorder.
