We have to be careful not to choose a question’s choice (E) just because we didn’t see any way in which one of the other choices affects the argument, and because choice (E) at least seems related to the passage.In other words, we have to be careful not to just give up and choose choice (E).

question.text: Mining Industry Representative: The chemicals that we have been using in extracting metals from the ground are making their way to rivers and streams, causing damage to the DNA of vertebrates that inhabit these aquatic environments.The companies in our industry care about the health of these organisms.So, all of the companies are switching to extraction methods that use organic chemicals that are shown to have no impact on the DNA of aquatic vertebrates.Once this switch is complete, the damage occurring to the DNA of these vertebrates will cease.Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

answers.text: After the switch to the new chemicals has taken place, large amounts of the old chemicals will linger in rivers and streams.
Because the new chemicals are less effective than the old chemicals, they must be used in quantities twice as large as the quantities of the old chemicals used.
The definition of “organic” used by chemists is not the same as the definition of “organic” associated with farming methods that involve the use of naturally occurring substances.
The new chemicals remain in the environment for much longer periods of time than the old chemicals.
Environmental studies have shown that the new chemicals enter rivers and streams and cause substantial damage to many of the smaller organisms on which aquatic vertebrates feed.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: The chemicals that we have been using in extracting metals from the ground are making their way to rivers and streams, causing damage to the DNA of vertebrates that inhabit these aquatic environments.
Premise: The companies in our industry are switching to extraction methods that use organic chemicals that are shown to have no impact on the DNA of aquatic vertebrates.
Conclusion: Once this switch is complete, the damage occurring to the DNA of these vertebrates will cease.

The mining representative has proposed a plan to stop the damage occurring to the DNA of aquatic vertebrates living in rivers and streams.Whenever someone proposes a plan, an assumption made is that there are no variables that have not been considered that will derail the plan or render it ineffective.Could the mining representative have failed to consider a factor that would hinder the success of the plan?
YOUR CHOICE(A) After the switch to the new chemicals has taken place, large amounts of the old chemicals will linger in rivers and streams.

The mining representative makes the argument that, once the switch to organic chemicals is complete, the damage occurring to the DNA of the aquatic vertebrates will cease.If, even after the switch has taken place, the old chemicals will linger in the rivers and streams, then the old chemicals may continue to cause damage.Notice that what this answer choice says weakens the argument by pointing out that an assumption made by the author (that simply switching chemicals would be enough to stop the damage) was not a sound assumption.
(B) Because the new chemicals are less effective than the old chemicals, they must be used in quantities twice as large as the quantities of the old chemicals used.

The argument states that the new organic chemicals do not have any impact on the DNA of the aquatic vertebrates. If we take that claim to be true–and we must, since all premises are considered factual–then using double the amount of these organic chemicals should not cause any harm to aquatic vertebrates’ DNA. What this answer choice says has no effect on the argument.
(C) The definition of “organic” used by chemists is not the same as the definition of “organic” associated with farming methods that involve the use of naturally occurring substances.

The definition of “organic” is not relevant to the argument, which is concerned with the impact of chemicals on aquatic vertebrates’ DNA.The new chemicals may go by any definition or name that chemists ultimately choose, as long as the chemicals meet the condition of having “no impact on the DNA of aquatic vertebrates.”
(D) The new chemicals remain in the environment for much longer periods of time than the old chemicals.

The argument makes the claim that the new organic chemicals do not have any impact on the DNA of the aquatic vertebrates.Since we must take that claim to be true, we know that, even if the new chemicals remain in the environment for much longer periods of time than the old chemicals, the new chemicals still are not harmful to the vertebrates’ DNA.Thus, what this answer choice says does not weaken the argument.
(E) Environmental studies have shown that the new chemicals enter rivers and streams and cause substantial damage to many of the smaller organisms on which aquatic vertebrates feed.

This is a tempting choice because we can easily imagine a scenario in which damage to smaller organisms that are eaten by the aquatic vertebrates would somehow result in harm to the aquatic vertebrates.Remember, however, that we have to focus on the exact conclusion of the argument presented, which is that damage to the DNA of the aquatic vertebrates would cease.

Since we have no clear reason to believe that damage to smaller organisms down the food chain would result in damage to aquatic vertebrates’ DNA, this choice does not affect the scenario in a way that undermines the case for the author’s conclusion.

question.text: Silver carp, a type of fish imported into North America in the 1970s, have become prevalent in waterways leading to the Great Lakes.Fortunately, before the carp reached the Great Lakes, where they would cause damage to the ecosystems, electrodes creating fields of electrical current in small areas of water were placed in the waterways, between where the carp are located and the lakes.Since it has been found that silver carp do not swim through water through which such electric current flows, they will not reach the Great Lakes.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the conclusion above?

answers.text: The electrical current used to block the progress of silver carp generally works by repelling fish, which upon sensing the current swim away from it without being harmed.
While importation of silver carp to the Americas has been banned, the carp can increase in number through reproduction.
No silver carp have been found swimming in any waters within miles of the areas in which the electrodes create fields of electrical current.
While silver carp occasionally jump from the water into passing boats, because they have little value to people, carp that do so are generally returned to the water.
Fish of some species tend to swim closer to water through which electrical current flows than do other fish.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Silver carp have become prevalent in waterways leading to the Great Lakes.
Premise: Electrodes creating fields of electrical current in small areas of water were placed in the waterways, between where the carp are located and the lakes.
Premise: Silver carp do not swim through water through which such electric current flows.
Conclusion: The silver carp in the waterways will not reach the Great Lakes.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since there are electrical current barriers through which carp will not swim, the carp will not reach the lakes.Could the author be missing something? Might there be another way for the carp to make their way to the Great Lakes? Of course, the argument can be weakened by any information that indicates that the carp could reach the lakes even though the barriers exist.
(A) The electrical current used to block the progress of silver carp generally works by repelling fish, which upon sensing the current swim away from it without being harmed.

While this choice indicates that it is likely that the carp are not harmed by the barriers, their not being harmed does not change the fact that they don’t swim through the barriers.So, this choice does not affect the argument.
(B) While importation of silver carp to the Americas has been banned, the carp can increase in number through reproduction.

The fact that the carp can increase in number through reproduction may indicate that the number of carp that will attempt to reach the Great Lakes will increase.However, no matter how many carp there are, as long as the carp don’t swim through electrical current barriers and don’t have another way to get to the Great Lakes, the carp will not reach the lakes.So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
(C) No silver carp have been found swimming in any waters within miles of the areas in which the electrodes create fields of electrical current.

A test-taker might perceive what this choice says as indicating that, since there are no carp near the electrical current barriers, the barriers are somehow not placed where they need to be in order to block the carp.

However, the fact that the carp are not near the barriers does not change the premise, which we must consider factual, that the barriers are between the carp and the lakes.So, this choice does not change the key characteristics of the scenario described and does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) While silver carp occasionally jump from the water into passing boats, because they have little value to people, carp that do so are generally returned to the water.
At first glance, what this answer choice says may seem completely irrelevant to the argument.However, often a choice that looks completely irrelevant turns out to be the correct answer.

This choice is such a choice, as it indicates that, while carp may not swim through the barriers, there is a way that at least a few carp could get past the barriers.Carp could jump into boats on non-lake side of the barriers and be returned to the water on the lake-side of the barriers.Thus, this choice provides a reason to question the conclusion of the argument.
(E) Fish of some species tend to swim closer to water through which electrical current flows than do other fish.

While what this choice says indicates that some fish may be less affected by fields of electrical current than are other fish, the argument has stated as fact that carp do not swim through the fields of electrical current, and that fact remains unchanged by the new information given in this choice.So, this choice does not affect the argument.

question.text: Water Contamination Specialist: In every one of the past thirty years, in testing bodies of fresh water, such as lakes and rivers, for industrial pollutants, we have found one or more such pollutants in well over 50 percent of the bodies of water that we have tested.Given the huge number of data points we have amassed, one can say with confidence that most bodies of fresh water are contaminated with industrial pollutants.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines confidence in the validity of the specialist’s conclusion?

answers.text: The specialist’s company usually tests for a limited group of contaminants that does not include many industrial pollutants that have been found in bodies of fresh water.
The hiring of a specialist to test a body of water for contamination is much more likely to occur when there is reason to believe that body of water to be contaminated.
Often, contaminants detected in water through testing appear in such low concentrations that they present little danger to human health.
Over the past thirty years, government regulation of the release of industrial pollutants into the environment has been strict.
The movement toward sustainability by industry is expected to result in a significant reduction in water pollution.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Every year for thirty years, the specialist’s company has found industrial pollutants in the majority of the bodies of fresh water tested by the company.
Conclusion: Most bodies of fresh water are contaminated with industrial pollutants.

This argument uses evidence gained via testing some bodies of fresh water to reach a conclusion about most bodies of fresh water.Doing so requires assuming that there is not a difference between the tested and untested bodies of water that would indicate that the conclusion may not be correct.
(A) The specialist’s company usually tests for a limited group of contaminants that does not include many industrial pollutants that have been found in bodies of fresh water.

This choice is a pseudo-weakener, since it could be perceived as indicating that the testing performed by the specialist’s company is not thorough, and that, therefore, a conclusion based on the results of that testing is possibly not valid.

However, the truth is that what this choice says suggests that bodies of fresh water containeven more contaminantsthan have been detected through the specialist’s company’s testing.Therefore, this choice certainly does not weaken the support for the conclusion that most bodies of fresh water are contaminated.If anything, this choice strengthens the support for that conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The hiring of a specialist to test a body of water for contamination is much more likely to occur when there is reason to believe that body of water to be contaminated.

What this choice says indicates that the process of choosing bodies of water to test is not random but, rather, often influenced by reasons to believe that bodies of water are contaminated.The implication is that the sample cited by the specialist is biased; most of the bodies of water tested are bodies of water such that, even before they are tested, there is some reason to believe them to be contaminated.Therefore, the proportion of bodies of water tested that are contaminated is likely greater than the proportion of bodies of water not tested that are contaminated.Therefore, what this choice says reduces support for the conclusion, because the conclusion is based on the generalization that the proportion ofallbodies of water that are contaminated is about the same as the proportion of tested bodies of water that are contaminated.
(C) Often, contaminants detected in water through testing appear in such low concentrations that they present little danger to human health.

This choice could be tempting because one might assume that contamination only matters if it is harmful to human health, and therefore the contamination in bodies of fresh water is often not significant.But would this reasoning lead to a reason to doubt the conclusion?

We must remember that the conclusion is not about thelevelsof contamination in the bodies of water tested or whether the contamination will have a significant impact.The conclusion is simply that most bodies of fresh water are contaminated.What this answer choice says does not in any way refute that conclusion.
(D) Over the past thirty years, government regulation of the release of industrial pollutants into the environment has been strict.

We cannot contradict a premise of the argument.Even though this choice may seem to indicate that strict government regulation would prevent industrial pollutants from entering bodies of fresh water, the fact remains that the water contamination specialist did indeed find contaminants in well over 50 percent of the bodies of water tested.So, even with strict regulation, pollutants have entered bodies of fresh water.Therefore, what this choice says has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
(E) The movement toward sustainability by industry is expected to result in a significant reduction in water pollution.

This choice discusses a reason why water pollution would decrease in the future, while the argument is about contamination already present in bodies of water.Thus, what this choice says does not affect the argument.

question.text: Since powering electric race cars requires that electric race cars carry heavy battery packs, gasoline-powered race cars have a maximum power-to-weight ratio advantage over electric race cars, meaning that a gasoline-powered race car can generate a given maximum engine power while weighing less than an electric race car that generates the same maximum engine power.Power-to-weight ratio is a key factor affecting which car will win a race.Therefore, electric race cars will not be competitive with gasoline-powered race cars in the Pikes Peak Climb, which involves racing to the top of a mountain, until lighter weight batteries have been developed.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the conclusion?

answers.text: A team has developed an electric car that generates more engine power than does any gasoline-powered car ever used in the Pikes Peak Climb.
The batteries used in electric cars today weigh significantly less than did similarly powerful batteries used only a few years ago.
Unlike electric motors, to produce maximum power, gasoline engines require abundant oxygen, which is not available at high altitudes.
As the charge in an electric race car’s battery pack is consumed over the course of a race, the power available to that electric race car decreases.
In the straight portions of the Pikes Peak Climb racecourse, power-to-weight ratio has less effect on car performance than it has in the curved portions.

explanation_parts.text: Here is a breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Because electric race cars use heavy battery packs, gasoline-powered race cars have a maximum power-to-weight ratio advantage over electric cars.
Premise: Power-to-weight ratio is a key factor affecting which car will win a race.

Conclusion: Electric race cars will not be competitive with gasoline-powered race cars in the Pikes Peak Climb, which involves racing to the top of a mountain, until lighter weight batteries have been developed.
Notice that the argument is based on one factor affecting which car wins in the Pikes Peak Climb.Might there be more to the story? What might the author have missed?

The argument could be weakened by information indicating that there is another key factor such that, as a result of the presence of that other factor, electric cars have an advantage over gasoline-powered cars.
(A) A team has developed an electric car that generates more engine power than does any gasoline-powered car ever used in the Pikes Peak Climb.

This is a tricky answer, because it seems to indicate that the electric car mentioned will have an advantage over gasoline-powered cars.Notice, however, that the argument is based not on the power of cars, but on the maximum power-to-weight ratio of cars.Since maximum power-to-weight ratio, rather than power itself, is what confers an advantage to a gasoline-powered car in the Pikes Peak Climb, what this answer says does not weaken the argument.As always, we must pay careful attention to the details presented to us in the passage.
(B) The batteries used in electric cars today weigh significantly less than did similarly powerful batteries used only a few years ago.

This answer choice could be tempting, as what it says could be perceived as contradicting the premise that, because batteries are heavy, gasoline-powered cars have a maximum power-to-weight ratio advantage over electric cars.However, that premise is stated as fact, and the fact that the batteries used in electric cars are lighter now than such batteries were in the past does not change the fact that gasoline-powered cars currently have a maximum power-to-weight ratio advantage over electric cars.Remember that the premises provided to us in the passage are considered factual.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Unlike electric motors, to produce maximum power, gasoline engines require abundant oxygen, which is not available at high altitudes.

This choice brings up another factor affecting which car wins in the Pikes Peak Climb.At high altitudes, such as the altitude at the top of a mountain, electric cars can generate maximum power, but gasoline-powered cars cannot, because they lack the oxygen that they need in order to do so.While it is not clear that the fact that electric cars have this advantage at high altitudes means that electric cars are competitive in the Pikes Peak Climb, this new information is nevertheless a clear reason to question the conclusion.
(D) As the charge in an electric race car’s battery pack is consumed over the course of a race, the power available to that electric race car decreases.

While what this choice says may be relevant to a discussion of whether electric cars can be competitive in the Pikes Peak Climb, we are not looking for a choice that is merely relevant, and the fact that the power available to electric cars decreases over the course of a race clearly does notweakenan argument concluding that electric cars cannot be competitive in a race.If anything, this choice provides additional support for the author’s conclusion, by providing an additional reason why electric cars would have trouble competing with gasoline powered cars.
YOUR CHOICE(E) In the straight portions of the Pikes Peak Climb racecourse, power-to-weight ratio has less effect on car performance than it has in the curved portions.

What this answer choice says could be perceived as contradicting the premise that power-to-weight ratio is a key factor determining which car wins in the Pikes Peak Climb.However, that power-to-weight ratio is a key factor has been stated as fact, and the fact that power-to-weight ratio has less effect on performance in certain portions of the racecourse than it has in others does not change the fact that power-to-weight ratio affects performance in the race overall.

question.text: Politician: To achieve our aim of decreased fuel use, we could increase fuel taxes, thus discouraging fuel use by increasing the prices people operating vehicles pay for fuel.However, increasing fuel taxes would not be popular with voters.An alternative to increasing fuel taxes is requiring vehicle manufacturers to produce vehicles that are, on average, more fuel-efficient than those currently produced.Requiring manufacturers to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles would be just as effective in reducing fuel use as would be taking the unpopular route of increasing fuel taxes.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call the politician’s final position into question?

answers.text: In producing vehicles that are more fuel efficient on average than vehicles currently produced are, manufacturers could still produce some vehicles that have relatively poor fuel efficiency.
When fuel expense associated with operating motor vehicles decreases, people tend to increase their use of motor vehicles.
Many people prefer to drive relatively large vehicles, which tend to burn more fuel per mile traveled than relatively small vehicles burn per mile traveled.
In order to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles, manufactures would have to make major investments in both product development and plant retooling.
People can avoid paying fuel taxes by using public transportation rather than operating their own vehicles.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: To achieve our aim of decreased fuel use, we could increase fuel taxes, thus discouraging fuel use by increasing the prices people operating vehicles pay for fuel.
Background: However, increasing fuel taxes would not be popular with voters.
Premise: An alternative to increasing fuel taxes is requiring vehicle manufacturers to produce vehicles that are more fuel efficient on average than those currently produced.

Conclusion (the politician’s final position): Requiring manufacturers to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles would be just as effective in reducing fuel use as would be taking the unpopular route of increasing fuel taxes.
(A) In producing vehicles that are more fuel efficient on average than vehicles currently produced are, manufacturers could still produce some vehicles that have relatively poor fuel efficiency.

Even if manufacturers could still producesomevehicles that have relatively poor fuel efficiency,” the author’s plan could work well, becauseon average, vehicles produced would be more fuel-efficient than vehicles currently produced areon average.Thus, this answer choice is not an effective weakener.
YOUR CHOICE(B) When fuel expense associated with operating motor vehicles decreases, people tend to increase their use of motor vehicles.

If, when fuel expense associated with operating motor vehicles decreases, people tend to increase their motor vehicle use, then, requiring vehicle manufacturers to produce vehicles that are on average more fuel efficient than those currently produced could result in an overall increase, rather than a reduction, in fuel usage.After all, people’s responding to greater fuel efficiency, and the resulting decrease in fuel expense associated with operating motor vehicles, by using their motor vehicles more often and perhaps to travel greater distances could more than make up for better average fuel efficiency.What this choice says is certainly a reason to question the politician’s final position.
(C) Many people prefer to drive relatively large vehicles, which tend to burn more fuel per mile traveled than relatively small vehicles burn per mile traveled.

That many people prefer to drive larger vehicles, which tend to burn more fuel per mile traveled than is burned per mile traveled by smaller vehicles does not weaken the argument.Remember, one of the argument’s premises is that “an alternative to increasing fuel taxes is requiring vehicle manufacturers to produce vehicles that areon averagemore fuel-efficient than those currently produced.” So, if vehicles (all vehicles) are on average more fuel-efficient than those currently produced, then it will not matter that many people prefer to drive larger vehicles, because vehicles in general will be more fuel-efficient than they are now.
(D) In order to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles, manufactures would have to make major investments in both product development and plant retooling.

Remember that this argument’s conclusion is that “requiring manufacturers to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles would be just as effective in reducing fuel use as would be taking the unpopular route of increasing fuel taxes.” The investments that vehicle manufacturers would have to make in order to produce more fuel-efficient vehicles does not affect the amount of fuel people use when operating motor vehicles.What this answer choice says does not affect the argument.
(E) People can avoid paying fuel taxes by using public transportation rather than operating their own vehicles.

What this answer choice says could be seen as indicating that, if fuel taxes increased, at least some people would choose to avoid such taxes by taking public transportation rather than driving their vehicles, and thus fuel use would decrease.All the same, the politician has already acknowledged that increasing fuel taxes would result in a decrease in fuel use, and this choice simply explainshowpeople could reduce fuel use.The fact that this choice provides information on how people would do something that the politician has already acknowledged they would do certainly is not a reason to call the politician’s final position into question.

question.text: Nonprofit Organization Administrator: In past years, attending our mid-year celebration event cost only a nominal amount approximately equal to the organization’s cost per attendee.Going forward, we will be charging more for attendance; attending the event will cost a relatively substantial amount that is the equivalent of a smaller medium-sized donation.Making fees for attending the event into a source of donations will serve to increase the funds available to our organization each year.Which of the following, if true, does the most to call into question the administrator’s projection?

answers.text: The expenses involved in holding the event will increase slightly as a result of the organization’s having to account for the donations made as payment for attendance.
The primary purpose of the event is not fundraising but, rather, celebrating the successes that the organization has recently had.
Many people who would consider making large donations to the organization see their giving to the organization for the year as complete once they have made one donation.
Some people who would have attended the event will not attend because they don’t want to pay the new, higher rate.
Some nonprofit organizations set relatively low prices for event attendance, using event sponsorships rather than attendance fees as key sources of funds.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In past years, attending the mid-year celebration event cost only a nominal amount approximately equal to the organization’s cost per attendee.
Premise: Going forward, we will be charging more for attendance; attending the event will cost a relatively substantial amount that is the equivalent of a smaller medium-sized donation.
Conclusion (the administrator’s projection): Making fees for attending the event into a source of donations will serve to increase the funds available to the organization each year.

The reasoning of the argument seems straightforward.By increasing fees for attending the event, so that each attendee pays the equivalent of a smaller medium-sized donation, the organization will generate, through holding the event, a net inflow of funds that was not generated when the price of attendance was approximately what the organization spent to have an attendee at the event.Therefore, because of the change, the organization will have each year more funds available than it had in previous years.

In this argument, a gap exists between the evidence that there will be an increase in the price of attending the event and the conclusion that there will be an increase in funds available to the organization each year.It’s likely that the weakening answer choice will attack an assumption necessary for bridging that gap.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The expenses involved in holding the event will increase slightly as a result of the organization’s having to account for the donations made as payment for attendance.

There is no clear reason to believe that what seems likely to be a significant increase in event attendance fee revenue would be offset by a slight increase in accounting expenses.So, this choice does not clearly weaken the argument.
(B) The primary purpose of the event is not fundraising but, rather, celebrating the successes that the organization has recently had.

This answer choice seems to imply that, by charging more for attendance, the organization could be somehow deviating from the primary purpose of holding the event, and one might question whether it makes sense to charge what seems to be a relatively high price for attendance at an event meant for celebrating the organization’s successes.

However, even if charging more to attend the event were to somehow undermine the event’s primary purpose, it is not clear how such undermining would negate the increase in funds available to the organization each year as a result of increased attendance fees.So, this choice does not provide a reason to call into question the administrator’s projection.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Many people who would consider making large donations to the organization see their giving to the organization for the year as complete once they have made one donation.

The implication of what this choice says is that, having made "relatively substantial" smaller medium-sized donations in order to attend the event, some people who otherwise would have made large donations will make no further donations for a given year.Therefore, in going from charging a "nominal amount" to getting smaller medium-sized donations from event attendees, the organization could hamper its success in getting large donations.While it is not certain that the organization’s missing out on some large donations would result in its ending up with less rather than more funds available each year, that such an outcome could occur does serve to call into question the conclusion that, by getting smaller medium-sized donations from every event attendee, the organization will increase the funds it has available each year.
(D) Some people who would have attended the event will not attend because they don’t want to pay the new, higher rate.
This choice could be tempting, because it implies that the organization will see reduced attendance at the event as a result of the increased attendance fees.

Notice, however, that the conclusion is about the funds available to the organization and that, before increasing the attendance fees, the organization received from each attendee only around enough in fees to cover the expense of having that attendee at the event.So, before increasing the fees, the organization was not experiencing any direct financial gain from having an attendee at the event.Therefore, since having an attendee at the event at the old rate did not benefit the organization financially, there is no clear reason to believe that losing some attendees will make any financial difference to the organization.Could there be indirect financial effects of losing attendees? Perhaps, but it is not clear that there are.So, this choice does not serve as a clear weakener.
(E) Some nonprofit organizations set relatively low prices for event attendance, using event sponsorships rather than attendance fees as key sources of funds.

This choice merely describes a method of fundraising that is an alternative to charging event attendance fees that are the equivalent of smaller medium-sized donations.The conclusion is not that charging such fees is the only or best method for fundraising.The conclusion is simply that, as a result of charging such fees, the organization will have more funds available each year than it has had in the past.So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.

question.text: Among the possible ways to treat kidney stones are methods using open surgery and methods using small punctures.A study of the two methods found that the success rate of methods using open surgery was 78 percent, while the success rate of methods using small punctures was 83 percent.Clearly, if faced with a choice between these two methods, a person seeking to maximize the likelihood of being successfully treated for kidney stones should opt for a method that uses a small puncture.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the support for the above conclusion?

answers.text: The time needed for recovery from open surgery is somewhat longer than that needed for recovery from a small puncture.
In most cases, kidney stones can be treated via the use of non-invasive methods, such as using sound waves to break the stones into pieces.
When a kidney stone is removed via open surgery, the stone remains intact, an outcome facilitating analysis of the stone for the purpose of determining why it formed.
The formation of kidney stones can be prevented via drinking water consistently and following certain dietary guidelines.
Physicians treating people suffering with kidney stones generally choose open surgery over small punctures only when handling cases that are particularly problematic.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Among the possible ways to treat kidney stones are methods using open surgery and methods using small punctures.
Premise: A study of the two methods found that the success rate of methods using open surgery was 78 percent, while the success rate of methods using small punctures was 83 percent.

Conclusion: Clearly, if faced with a choice between these two methods, a person seeking to maximize the likelihood of being successfully treated for kidney stones should opt for a method that uses a small puncture.

As we know, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect argument.The argument makes the cause-and-effect related claim that, because the success rate of kidney stone treatments using small punctures, 83 percent, is higher than that of treatments using open surgery, 78 percent, a person seeking to maximize the likelihood of being successfully treated for kidney stones should opt for a method that uses a small puncture.What might the author have missed?
(A) The time needed for recovery from open surgery is somewhat longer than that needed for recovery from a small puncture.

The amount of time needed for recovery from a type of treatment is not clearly connected by any information provided to whether that treatment will be successful.Thus, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion, which is about how one can maximize the likelihood of being successfully treated, not about how one can minimize recovery time.
(B) In most cases, kidney stones can be treated via the use of non-invasive methods, such as using sound waves to break the stones into pieces.

To avoid choosing this incorrect answer, we must focus carefully on the conclusion, which says, “if faced with a choice between these two methods ….” The fact that, as this choice indicates, generally, neither of those methods has to be used in the treatment of kidney stones, does not affect the validity of the conclusion that, if faced with a choice between those two methods, a person seeking to maximize the likelihood of being successfully treated for kidney stones should opt for one of those two methods rather than the other.
(C) When a kidney stone is removed via open surgery, the stone remains intact, an outcome facilitating analysis of the stone for the purpose of determining why it formed.

To avoid choosing this incorrect answer, we must focus carefully on the conclusion, which is “a person seeking tomaximize the likelihood of being successfully treatedfor kidney stones should opt for a method that uses a small puncture.” The person is seeking to maximize the likelihood of successful treatment, not to maximize the potential for further analysis of kidney stones. The fact that a kidney stone can be analyzed after open surgery does not change the facts provided–open surgery has a 78 percent success rate, while the method using small punctures has an 83 percent success rate–or undermine the support those facts provide for the conclusion.
(D) The formation of kidney stones can be prevented via drinking water consistently and following certain dietary guidelines.

The argument is not about thepreventionof kidney stones.Rather, it is abouttreatmentonce they have formed.Thus, information about preventing kidney stone formation via drinking water and following dietary guidelines does not affect the support for the conclusion.The kidney stones in question have already formed.Now, how best to treat them? That is the concern addressed by the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Physicians treating people suffering with kidney stones generally choose open surgery over small punctures only when handling cases that are particularly problematic.

The argument makes a cause-and-effect related claim that, because the success rate of methods using small punctures is 83 percent, while that of methods involving open surgery is 78 percent, a person seeking to maximize the likelihood of being successfully treated for kidney stones should opt for a method that uses a small puncture.

However, if physicians generally choose open surgery over small punctures only when handling cases that are particularly problematic, then we have strong reason to believe that the lower success rate of methods using open surgery could be that the cases in which open surgery is used are more problematic, not that methods that involve open surgery are less effective.In other words, any method is likely to produce poorer outcomes when used for particularly problematic cases.So, if one method tends to be chosen only for problematic cases, and the other method is used for simpler cases, the success rates will be skewed.

So, given what this choice says, the observed difference in success rates may not be the result small puncture methods’ actually being more effective for kidney stone treatment, a fact undermining the support the evidence provides for the conclusion.

question.text: Many of the employees at a certain company would be more productive were they to take more days off than they have had available under company policy.To address this issue, the company’s management has decided to change its vacation policy, making it such that, for many of the company’s employees, deciding how many days to take off each year is completely up to the employee.With the new policy in place, employees who would be more productive were they to take more days off will take as many days off as they need.Which of the following, if true, most calls into question the above conclusion?

answers.text: For many of the company’s employees, work and recreation are so integrated that those employees can be very productive without taking many days off.
For many of the company’s employees, having a company-assigned number of vacation days provides psychological justification for taking days off from work.
Under the company’s previous vacation policy, some employees had more vacation days available than they needed to take off to maximize their productivity.
Though some of the company’s employees feel better when they take entire weeks off, others generally gain the most from taking only one or two days off at a time.
None of the company’s employees had planned to leave the company because of dissatisfaction with the company’s previous vacation policy.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many of the employees at a certain company would be more productive were they to take more days off than they have had available under company policy.

Premise: The company’s management has decided to change its vacation policy, making it such that, for many of the company’s employees, deciding how many days to take off each year is completely up to the employee.
Conclusion: With the new policy in place, employees who would be more productive were they to take more days off will take as many days off as they need.

The reasoning of the argument is straightforward.With company policy changed so that employees can decide how many days to take off, the employees will take off as many days as they need.However, the conclusion relies on the assumption that company policy is the only thing that keeps employees from taking the number of days off that is optimal for them.
One detail worth noting is that the argument states that some, rather than all, employees would work better with more days off than they have had available under company policy.
(A) For many of the company’s employees, work and recreation are so integrated that those employees can be very productive without taking many days off.

The fact that many employees don’t need many days off does not affect the argument, which is about what those employees who do need more days off will do given the new policy.In other words, the group of people with whom we’re concerned are those who do need more days off than they had available under the company policy.Thus, the information in this answer choice does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) For many of the company’s employees, having a company-assigned number of vacation days provides psychological justification for taking days off from work.

The implication of this choice is that the company’s doing away with assigning a specific number of days off to each employee will not necessarily result in employees’ taking more days off.Instead, the new policy may result in some employees taking fewer days off, because without company-assigned time off, some employees will feel that they lack justification for taking days off.While what this choice says does not indicate whether the employees who need more days off are also the employees who use assigned time off for psychological justification for taking days off, the fact that no longer assigning specific numbers of days off would eliminate the psychological justification some employees need in order to take days off does call the conclusion of the argument into question.
(C) Under the company’s previous vacation policy, some employees had more vacation days available than they needed to take off to maximize their productivity.

What this choice says could be perceived as weakening the argument, because it indicates that some of the company’s employees won’t benefit from the new policy in terms of getting additional vacation days that they need in order to be more productive.

However, the fact that some employees do not need more vacation days to maximize their productivity does not affect the conclusion of the argument.After all, the conclusion is about what employees who DO NEED more days off than they have had available will benefit from a policy that allows them to take off as many days as they need.

Note also that the fact that "some" employees had more vacation days available than they needed does not in any way call into question the argument that "many" of the employees would be more productive were they to take more days off than they have had available, and thus those "many" employees could benefit from the policy.After all, "some" could be a small proportion of employees.
(D) Though some of the company’s employees feel better when they take entire weeks off, others generally gain the most from taking only one or two days off at a time.

The fact that different employees have different preferences for how many days off they take in succession does have any clear effect on the total number of days off employees will take given a policy that allows them to take off as many days as they need.This answer choice has no effect on the argument.
(E) None of the company’s employees had planned to leave the company because of dissatisfaction with the company’s previous vacation policy.

The conclusion is not that the policy will affect employee decisions regarding whether to remain with the company but rather that the policy will result in employees’ taking as many days off as they need.Information indicating that changing the policy is not necessary for keeping employees from leaving the company does not affect the argument.

question.text: The currently proposed 15 percent increase in the minimum wage is being touted as good for people employed at minimum wage.However, such an increase in the minimum wage would not be good but, rather, bad overall for people employed at minimum wage, because businesses would lay off employees in response to the increased labor costs that would result from the increase in the minimum wage.Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

answers.text: For someone laid off, finding a new job can take months and may require acquiring new skills.
For many businesses that pay the minimum wage, labor costs represent the majority of their operating expenses.
Recruiting, hiring, and training employees can involve significant costs for a business.
It has been estimated that layoffs expected to result from the proposed increase in the minimum wage would involve 3 percent of people currently paid minimum wage.
Even with a significant increase in the minimum wage, some businesses would continue to employ as many people as they do now.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: The proposed 15 percent increase in the minimum wage is being touted as good for people employed at minimum wage.
Premise: Businesses would lay off employees in response to the increase in the minimum wage.
Conclusion: The increase in the minimum wage would be bad overall for people employed at minimum wage.

There is a key detail to notice in the conclusion, the use of the word “overall.” In GMAT critical reasoning questions, what the premises say is taken to be true.Therefore, the correct answer to the question does not seek to contradict the premise that businesses would lay off employees in response to the increase in the minimum wage.Rather, the correct answer attacks the idea that the effects of the increase in the minimum wage would be badoverallfor people employed at minimum wage.
(A) For someone laid off, finding a new job can take months and may require acquiring new skills.

What this choice says could be taken as confirmation that being laid off is bad for those who are laid off.Therefore, it slightly strengthens, rather than weakens, the conclusion that increasing the minimum wage would be bad overall for people employed at minimum wage.We must be careful not to choose as correct an answer choice that says something that strengthens the argument when we’re seeking a weakener.
(B) For many businesses that pay the minimum wage, labor costs represent the majority of their operating expenses.

While what this choice says may seem to support the idea that businesses would respond to an increase in the minimum wage by laying off workers, the truth is that the argument has already stated as fact that they would do so.Therefore, this choice does not affect the argument.
(C) Recruiting, hiring, and training employees can involve significant costs for a business.

Since it indicates that businesses’ personnel-related costs include many expenses other than wages, what this choice says could be taken as downplaying the effect on personnel-related expenses of an increase in the minimum wage.Thus, this choice could be seen as somehow attacking the idea that businesses would respond to an increase in the minimum wage by laying off workers.However, the argument has already stated as fact that businesses would lay off workers in response to an increase in the minimum wage, and therefore this choice does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) It has been estimated that layoffs expected to result from the proposed increase in the minimum wage would involve 3 percent of people currently paid minimum wage.

While this choice says that the proposed increase in the minimum wage would result in layoffs of 3 percent of people currently paid minimum wage, the argument states that the minimum wage would increase by 15 percent.Since, the combination of the effect of the layoffs and the effect of the wage increase would result in a significant increase, approximately 12 percent, in the aggregate earnings of people employed at minimum wage, what this choice says weakens the support for the conclusion that the increase in the minimum wage would be, as the conclusion says, badoverallfor people employed at minimum wage.
(E) Even with a significant increase in the minimum wage, some businesses would continue to employ as many people as they do now.

Like what choice (C) says, what this choice says could be perceived as somehow attacking the premise that businesses would respond to an increase in the minimum wage by laying off workers.However, in GMAT Critical Reasoning, the premises are taken to be statements of fact, and so answer choices that seem to contradict premises are generally not correct answers.

In this case, the fact that some businesses would not lay off employees in response to an increase in the minimum wage does not negate the premise that some would.Thus, what this choice says does not weaken the support for the conclusion that the minimum wage increase would be bad overall for people employed at minimum wage.

question.text: Because air conditioners consume significant amounts of electricity, each summer, the electrical power generation systems in Hothumidia are strained.Fortunately, recent innovations have led to the design of air conditioners that use 30 percent less power per unit of air cooled than is used by any air conditioner in use in Hothumidia today.Once the new air conditioners become widely available, the strain on Hothumidia’s electrical power generation systems will be relieved.Which of the following, if true, is most detrimental to the above prediction?

answers.text: Regulators in Hothumidia have made clear that, in order to protect Hothumidia’s fragile ecology, they will not approve the construction of any new power plants in Hothumidia.
Of the air conditioners currently in operation in Hothumidia, approximately 60 percent have been in operation for 10 years or longer.
Most people in Hothumidia do not use air conditioners because the monthly cost to power an air conditioner is typically about 20 percent more than they are willing to spend.
At times of peak electricity use, approximately 35 percent of the electricity used in Hothumidia is used by air conditioners.
On average, it will take three years for a building owner in Hothumidia to recoup the costs of replacing an air conditioner with one of the new, more efficient ones.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Because air conditioners consume significant amounts of electricity, the electrical power generation systems in Hothumidia are strained each summer.
Premise: Recent innovations have led to the design of air conditioners that use 30 percent less power than do air conditioners in use in Hothumidia today.
Conclusion: Once the new air conditioners become widely available, the strain on Hothumidia’s electrical power generation systems will be relieved.

Because the new air conditioners use less power than is used by ones currently in use, the author has concluded that strain on power generation systems will relieved once the new air conditioners become widely available.What factor could the author have missed?
(A) Regulators in Hothumidia have made clear that, in order to protect Hothumidia’s fragile ecology, they will not approve the construction of any new power plants in Hothumidia.

The argument is supported by the fact that an air conditioner that uses less power has been designed, and the conclusion is that, once that air conditioner becomes widely available, the strain on the electrical power generation systems will be relieved.While the construction of new power plants might further relieve that strain, the fact that no new power plants will be constructed does not change the relief on the electrical power systems that air conditioners using less power will provide.What this answer choice says does not affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Of the air conditioners currently in operation in Hothumidia, approximately 60 percent have been in operation for 10 years or longer.

The information in this answer choice could strengthen or weaken the argument.For example, if we assume that, because approximately 60 percent of the air conditioners have been in operation for 10 years or longer, many people are ready for new air conditioners, then the argument is stronger.However, if we take what this answer choice says as indicating that people hold on to their air conditioners for a long time and, thus, will not purchase the new air conditioners when they become widely available, then the argument is weaker.We have no reason to presume that either story is more valid than the other.Therefore, the information in this answer choice is not an effective weakener.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Most people in Hothumidia do not use air conditioners because the monthly cost to power an air conditioner is typically about 20 percent more than they are willing to spend.

Currently, most people in Hothumidia do not use an air conditioner because the monthly cost to power one is about 20 percent more than they are willing to spend.However, the new air conditioners require 30 percent less power than the current ones.So, we can see that, once the new air conditioners become available, the monthly cost to power an air conditioner will be reduced to a level that people in Hothumidia are willing to pay, and most people in Hothumidia will begin to use air conditioners.This huge expected increase in air conditioner use certainly would cast doubt on the conclusion that the strain on Hothumidia’s electrical power generation systems will be relieved.
To clearly see why the foregoing makes sense, let’s take a closer look at the math.

Most Hothumidians do not use an air conditioner because the monthly cost to power one is about 20 percent more than they are willing to spend, meaning that the monthly cost is 120 percent of what people are willing to pay.

From the passage, we see that newly air conditioners use 30 percent less power per unit of air cooled than do air conditioners in use today.Therefore, we can see that the monthly cost to power a new air conditioner would be 70 percent of 120 percent = 84 percent of what people are willing to pay.(0.70 x 1.20 = 0.84)
In other words, this new cost is 16 percentlessthan what most people (i.e., more than half of the people) in Hothumidia people would be willing to pay. (1.00–0.84 = 0.16)
Thus, at the reduced cost, more than half of the people in Hothumidia can be expected to begin using air conditioners.Wow.The strain on the power system will be off the charts.
(D) At times of peak electricity use, approximately 35 percent of the electricity used in Hothumidia is used by air conditioners.

What this answer choice says, that at times of peak electricity use, approximately 35 percent of the electricity used in Hothumidia is used by air conditioners, confirms that air conditions are using a considerable percentage of the total available electricity.Since this is in line with the information that supports the conclusion, this information certainly does not weaken the argument.
(E) On average, it will take three years for a building owner in Hothumidia to recoup the costs of replacing an air conditioner with one of the new, more efficient ones.

What this answer choice says, that recouping the costs of replacing an old air conditioner with a new one will take a building owner an average of three years, is a trap, a tempting shiny object, but the truth is that we don’t know how people in Hothumidia make business decisions.If the savings over 10 years is significant enough, then perhaps three years to recoup the costs seems well worth the investment.On the other hand, perhaps building owners won’t replace the air conditioners unless they can recoup the costs in one year.We have no reason to presume one way or the other, and thus this answer choice is not an effective weakener.

question.text: Members of the legislature in Feronia have proposed putting into place a system such that many people who lose their jobs could, for up to a year, receive from the government close to 100 percent of the amount of money they were getting paid while in their jobs.Adopting such a system would be detrimental to economic growth in Feronia, because people’s being aware that they could receive such compensation would serve to reduce their incentives to quickly find new jobs.Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the above conclusion?

answers.text: Because of ongoing strong economic growth in Feronia, the government would likely run a budget surplus even if the proposed system were to be adopted.
Under the proposed system, the compensation received by people who were the most highly paid while in jobs would still be substantially less than what their pay was.
Some unemployed individuals would take advantage of the compensation available under the system to take extended breaks from working and spend time with their families.
For some people, much of the incentive for quickly finding new jobs after losing jobs derives from their desire to minimize gaps in their employment histories.
People who would start new businesses often do not do so because they are either too busy at their current jobs or not financially positioned to wait for a new business to generate income.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Background: Members of the legislature in Feronia have proposed putting into place a system such that many people who lose their jobs could, for up to a year, receive from the government close to 100 percent of the amount of money they were getting paid while in their jobs.
Premise: People’s being aware that they could receive such compensation would serve to reduce their incentives to quickly find new jobs.
Conclusion: Adopting such a system would be detrimental to economic growth in Feronia.

In GMAT questions, evidential statements are taken to be true.So, the premise that people’s being aware that they would receive compensation under the proposed system would serve to reduce their incentives to quickly find new jobs must be considered factual, and coming to the argument’s conclusion seems reasonable.It makes sense to conclude that something that reduces people’s incentive to find jobs when they are unemployed would be detrimental to economic growth.So, to correctly answer the question, we have to find a choice that provides information with some unexpected angle that undermines the support for what seems to be a well-supported conclusion.
(A) Because of ongoing strong economic growth in Feronia, the government would likely run a budget surplus even if the proposed system were to be adopted.
This choice could be tempting because it uses wording similar to wording used in the passage, such as “economic growth,” “proposed system,” and “adopted.”

However, GMAT Critical Reasoning is not a word matching game.So, there is no real basis for choosing this choice, which undermines the support for a conclusion other than the conclusion that we have to weaken, such as the conclusion that adopting the proposed system would cause the government of Feronia to experience budget-related problems.Notice that having abudget surplusis not the same as experiencingeconomicgrowth.
(B) Under the proposed system, the compensation received by people who were the most highly paid while in jobs would still be substantially less than what their pay was.

This answer is a tempting trap, because it could be taken as indicating that the people who were the most highly paid while in jobs would still have an incentive to quickly find new jobs, since the compensation they would receive under the system would not come close to matching their former pay.

While, given what this choice says, the disincentive to find new jobs would not be the same for all people, it is still the case that “many people” would have exactly the disincentive described in the argument.Further, even people who would under the system receive much less compensation than they were receiving while in jobs, would, under the system, still beless incentivizedto find new jobs than they would be were the system not to be adopted.So, what this choice says does not undermine the support for the conclusion.
(C) Some unemployed individuals would take advantage of the compensation available under the system to take extended breaks from working and spend time with their families.

What this choice says sounds plausible and tends to support the reasoning of the argument by indicating that some people indeed would choose to engage in relatively economically unproductive activities if they were to receive generous compensation from the government.
To avoid making the mistake of choosing a choice like this one, we must keep in mind what the question is asking and look for a weakener, rather than a plausible statement or a strengthener.
YOUR CHOICE(D) For some people, much of the incentive for quickly finding new jobs after losing jobs derives from their desire to minimize gaps in their employment histories.

This choice could be tempting, because it indicates that, even if the system were to be adopted, some people still would have incentives for quickly finding new jobs.Thus, what this choice says seems to conflict with the premise that people’s being aware that they would receive the proposed compensation would serve to reduce their incentives to quickly find new jobs.

As we are aware, however, in GMAT Critical Reasoning, an answer choice cannot negate a factual premise, and this choice does not change the facts presented.Although, as this choice states, some people want to minimize gaps in their employment histories, people’s awareness of the availability of the proposed compensation would still reduce their incentives to find new jobs quickly.While some people might still have some incentives to find new jobs quickly, people in general wouldn’t have all the incentives they would have without the system's having been adopted.

CORRECT ANSWER(E) People who would start new businesses often do not do so because they are either too busy at their current jobs or not financially positioned to wait for a new business to generate income.

We have to use a little reasoning to see why this choice weakens the argument.We already know from the argument that the generous compensation would serve as a disincentive for people to find new jobs.So, how could offering that compensation possibly not negatively impact economic growth? This choice provides an answer to that question.

If, as this choice says, people who would start new businesses often do not do so because they are either too busy at their current jobs or not financially positioned to wait for a new business to generate income, then, it seems likely that providing people with compensation that would allow them to remain out of jobs for a year without a substantial decrease in income would give people the time and financial support that they need in order to start new businesses.In other words, because people would not feel the need to find jobs, some would choose to start new businesses, thereby spurring economic growth.While it is not clear whether things would work in this way, or whether the positive effects those new businesses would have on economic growth would exceed the negative effects of some people’s choosing simply not to work as a result of their receiving the compensation, what this choice says does give us reason to question the conclusion of the argument.

question.text: The practice of shipping recyclable waste to other countries for processing seems to be a convenient way to dispose of such waste.However, once such material is out of our country, we have no reliable way of monitoring what happens to the material, and our country’s waste disposal regulations no longer apply.Therefore, we have no practical way of knowing whether the material is actually being recycled and sold rather than simply dumped somewhere.Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest evidence that the concern outlined above is unwarranted?

answers.text: Municipalities in the country shipping recyclable waste to other countries receive from those taking the material a minimum of $6 per ton of such material.
In virtually every country, there are government officials who want to adopt waste disposal regulations.
While, in many countries, the fees for dumping waste are quite high, in other countries, the cost of dumping is negligible.
Generally, in order for waste to be transported from ships to areas where it can be dumped, it has to be transferred to trucks.
In countries where facilities that process recyclable waste are located, there are many applicants for jobs in those facilities.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Shipping recyclable waste to other countries for processing seems to be a convenient way to dispose of such waste.
Premise: We have no way to reliably monitor what happens to material once it has left our country.
Premise: Our country’s waste disposal regulations do not apply to material shipped to other countries.
Conclusion: We have no practical way of knowing whether the material is actually being recycled and sold rather than simply dumped somewhere.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Municipalities in the country shipping recyclable waste to other countries receive from those taking the material a minimum of $6 per ton of such material.

The argument’s conclusion is that we have no practical way of knowing whether the material is actually being recycled and sold rather than simply dumped somewhere.However, if municipalities in the country shipping recyclable waste to other countries receive from those taking the material a minimum of $6 per ton of such material, then we actually DO have a practical way of knowing that the material is NOT simply dumped somewhere.After all, why would anyone pay for the material only to dump it somewhere? What this answer choice says clearly weakens the argument.
(B) In virtually every country, there are government officials who want to adopt waste disposal regulations.

The statement that there are government officials in virtually every country who want to adopt waste disposal regulations implies that such regulations have not yet been implemented.Waste disposal regulations that have not yet been implemented cannot prevent waste from being dumped.Thus, if anything, what this answer choice says slightly strengthens the argument.
(C) While, in many countries, the fees for dumping waste are quite high, in other countries, the cost of dumping is negligible.

We really don’t know how what this answer choice says effects the argument.For example, we might assume that, if there are locations where the cost of dumping is negligible, then the argument is strengthened because the we might feel that the waste is almost certainly being brought to those locations and dumped.However, we must be careful not to make unwarranted assumptions.The truth is that we really don’t know the direction in which this answer choice takes us.
(D) Generally, in order for waste to be transported from ships to areas where it can be dumped, it has to be transferred to trucks.
In all likelihood, waste needs to be transferred to trucks regardless of whether it is recycled or dumped.Thus, what this answer choice says has no clear effect on the validity of the conclusion.
(E) In countries where facilities that process recyclable waste are located, there are many applicants for jobs in those facilities.

While facilities that process recyclable waste may benefit from there being many applicants for jobs in those facilities, the existence of these many job applicants does nothing to invalidate the author’s concern that there is no way to know whether waste is being recycled or dumped.What this choice says does not affect the argument.

question.text: Analysis of commercial and government data reveals that, the more craft beer breweries there are in a town, the greater is the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town.Clearly, the presence of craft beer breweries in a town causes property owners to pay more in real estate taxes.Which of the following, if true, provides the most compelling reason to call into question the above conclusion?

answers.text: Craft beer breweries generally sell beer at prices considerably higher than those at which beer is sold by other sellers of beer.
Generally, the greater the number of craft beer breweries in a town is, the greater the number of property owners in that town is.
In some cases, newly opened craft beer breweries are exempt from paying real estate taxes.
The profits of craft beer breweries would be higher if craft beer breweries did not have to pay real estate taxes.
It is generally the case that, the greater the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town is, the more people the government of that town employs.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Analysis of commercial and government data reveals that, the more craft beer breweries there are in a town, the greater is the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town.
Conclusion: Clearly, the presence of craft beer breweries in a town causes property owners to pay more in real estate taxes.

Notice that the author is making a cause and effect claim.As always, we must be skeptical of any cause and effect claim made.Are the presence of craft beer breweries in a town really the cause of the property owners paying more in real estate taxes.Could there be an alternative explanation? What might the author have missed?
(A) Craft beer breweries generally sell beer at prices considerably higher than those at which beer is sold by other sellers of beer.

The author’s conclusion is that the presence of craft beer breweries in a town causes property owners to pay more in real estate taxes.The fact that craft beer breweries sell beer at prices considerably higher than those at which beer is sold by other sellers of beer would not weaken that argument because there is no clear, logical connection between the price of craft beers sold at craft beer breweries and the real estate taxes that property owners pay.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Generally, the greater the number of craft beer breweries in a town is, the greater the number of property owners in that town is.

The author presents the fact that the more craft beer breweries there are in a town, the greater is the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town.From that fact she concludes that the presence of craft beer breweries in a town causes property owners to pay more in real estate taxes.Of course, she’s making a cause-and-effect claim.

What this answer choice says, that, generally, the greater the number of craft beer breweries in a town is, the greater the number of property owners in that town is, weakens the argument by bringing up an alternative explanation for the observed correlation between the number of craft beer breweries in a town and the total amount paid in real estate taxes by property owners.

In other words, what if the presence of craft beer breweries in a town does not at all cause property owners to pay more in real estate taxes? What if instead the correlation exists because towns with a higher number of craft beer breweries also have greater a number of property owners? This fact would for sure weaken the argument.

After all, in a town with more property owners, those property owners would likely pay a total amount of real estate taxes greater than the total paid by property owners in a town with fewer property owners.So, in that case, the total amounts paid in real estate taxes would be higher not because of the presence of craft beer breweries but because of greater numbers of property owners, which just happen to be associated with the presence of greater numbers of craft beer breweries.
Notice that what this answer choice says highlights the fact that the author has likely misinterpreted the data.She has concluded that a cause-and-effect relationship exists when likely one does not.
(C) In some cases, newly opened craft beer breweries are exempt from paying real estate taxes.

This choice could be tempting if we were to perceive it as attacking the premise that the more craft beer breweries there are in a town, the greater is the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town.

We might think something along the lines of “If newly-opened craft beer breweries sometimes don’t have to pay property taxes, then maybe the total amount of property taxes paid in a town isn’t really higher when there are more craft beer breweries in that town.”

That line of thinking is faulty, however.The passage has stated as fact that the more craft beer breweries there are in a town, the greater is the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town, and what this choice says does not change that fact.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(D) The profits of craft beer breweries would be higher if craft beer breweries did not have to pay real estate taxes.
This choice is a pseudo-weakener that does not really affect the strength of the argument.

This choice is tempting because it seems to indicate that owners of craft beer breweries would prefer to pay relatively little in real estate taxes.So, we might be tempted to believe that that fact somehow indicates that the presence of craft beer breweries doesn’t cause property owners to pay more in real estate taxes.

However, the truth is that, regardless of what owners of craft beer breweries may prefer, the fact remains that the more craft beer breweries there are in a town, the greater is the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town, and that fact still supports the argument’s conclusion that the presence of craft beer breweries causes property owners to pay more in real estate taxes.
(E) It is generally the case that, the greater the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town is, the more people the government of that town employs.

Although what this answer choice says is probably true in reality, the fact that it is generally the case that, the greater the total amount of money paid in real estate taxes by property owners in that town is, the more people the government of that town employs certainly does not weaken the argument.
What impact would this fact possibly have on the argument?

If you chose this choice as correct, probably you created a convoluted story about the number people the government of the town employs and the number of craft beer breweries in that town that is not clearly supported by any information in the passage or in this choice.

question.text: MaxiLook, a web portal site that generates revenue by selling advertising space, recently changed the format of its home page.However, to maintain profitability, MaxiLook should immediately revert to using the previous format before it loses any more users.In surveys of MaxiLook’s users, most of the users have expressed that they prefer the format the site used previously, and that format is a factor in their decisions regarding which web portal sites to use.Which of the following, if true, would most undermine the support for the conclusion of the argument?

answers.text: Some website users who respond to surveys quickly click through the questions without paying much attention to the answers they are choosing.
The new format appeals to the category of users known to be the most likely to respond to advertising on a website.
MaxiLook’s users provided a range of responses on the survey, some expressing a strong preference for the previous format and others preferring the new one.
Users who respond to surveys about websites they use tend to be the users who use the sites the most.
The team that designed the survey does not have any members in common with the team that designed the new format.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Maxilook recently changed the format of its home page.
Premise: Users surveyed have expressed that they prefer the previous format.
Premise: Users surveyed have expressed that they consider format in deciding which sites to use.
Conclusion: To maintain profitability, MaxiLook should revert to using the previous format.

Notice, there is a gap in the argument.The premises are the results of a survey of users.The conclusion is about what Maxilook should do in order to maintain profitability.So, the argument depends on the assumption that the information provided by the user survey correctly indicates what MaxiLook should do in order to maintain profitability.After all, what if it were true that the user survey does not, for some reason, correctly indicate what MaxiLook should do to maintain profitability? The argument would be considerably weaker…
