(A) Some website users who respond to surveys quickly click through the questions without paying much attention to the answers they are choosing.

This answer may seem to downgrade the value of the survey and thus call into question the conclusion, which is based on the survey results.However, this choice is a pseudo-weakener that does not really weaken the support for the conclusion.

Saying that “some” respondents click through the survey is not a strong condemnation of the value of the survey and does not show any bias one way or the other in the survey.So, this choice does not effectively weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The new format appeals to the category of users known to be the most likely to respond to advertising on a website.

This choice is a clear weakener, because what it says indicates that the new format appeals to a particular audience, one that may not include the majority of MaxiLook users, but one that perhaps MaxiLook should target in order to maximize advertising revenue and thus profitability.

For maintaining profitability, basing choice of format on the results of a survey of current users may not make as much sense as basing choice of format on what attracts users whose use of the site would result in the generation of relatively high advertising revenue.So, this choice serves to call the conclusion of the argument into question.
(C) MaxiLook’s users provided a range of responses on the survey, some expressing a strong preference for the previous format and others preferring the new one.

This choice could be tempting, because, by indicating that some users expressed that they prefer the new format, this choice may seem to reduce the weight of the premise that most of the users expressed that they prefer the previous format.
However, that some users prefer the new format does not change the fact that most of them prefer the previous format, and so, this choice does not change the facts presented or weaken the argument.
(D) Users who respond to surveys about websites they use tend to be the users who use the sites the most.

The effect of what this choice says is unclear, but this choice indicates that the users who responded to MaxiLook’s survey likely are the users who use the site most.Thus, if this choice has any effect, the effect is to make the information provided by the survey results seem more valuable, because the results came from important users of the site.Therefore, if anything, the argument is strengthened rather than weakened by this choice.
(E) The team that designed the survey does not have any members in common with the team that designed the new format.

What this choice says could be perceived as somehow widening the gap between the implications of the survey results and the choice of which format MaxiLook should use in order to maintain profitability.However, the truth is that, regardless of who designed the survey or the format, the relationship between the results of the survey and the decision regarding which format to use remains the same.Therefore, this choice does not affect the argument.

question.text: The Tony Toes shoe manufacturing company has a new plan for both boosting its business and fighting poverty.For every pair of Tony Toes shoes sold, the company will donate a pair of shoes to someone living in a less affluent area of a major city.Since Tony Toes sells many thousands of pairs of shoes every year, in adopting this plan, Tony Toes will make lower-income communities better off.Which of the following, if true, provides the best reason to question the above conclusion?

answers.text: Many of the people currently experiencing the greatest financial challenges live not in major cities but, rather, in rural areas.
When lower-income people residing in cities are asked to list their most pressing needs, many do not mention shoes.
The prices Tony Toes charges for its shoes are some of the highest in the industry, making the shoes unaffordable for most people with lower incomes.
Often, people who buy Tony Toes shoes donate to charities their old shoes, which could be worn for years to come.
In many less affluent communities, shoe stores located there are key sources both of training for entry-level employees and of income.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: For every pair of Tony Toes shoes sold, the company will donate a pair of shoes to someone living in a less affluent area of a major city.
Premise: Tony Toes sells many thousands of pairs of shoes each year.
Conclusion: In adopting this plan, Tony Toes will make lower-income communities better off.

The conclusion of the argument is based on the assumption that donating shoes to people in lower income communities makes people in lower-income communities better off.Additional information that serves as reason to believe that such donations do not necessarily make people in lower-income communities better off would attack that assumption and weaken the support for the conclusion that the plan will work as expected.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Many of the people currently experiencing the greatest financial challenges live not in major cities but, rather, in rural areas.

While this answer choice indicates that people with lower incomes who live in rural areas would not be helped by Tony Toes’ shoe donation program, the fact remains that many lower-income people living in major cities–would receive donated shoes from Tony Toes. So, while the program would not help all lower-income people, the conclusion that the program would help lower-income communities is not truly weakened by what this choice says.
(B) When lower-income people residing in cities are asked to list their most pressing needs, many do not mention shoes.

This choice may seem to weaken the argument by reducing the perceived value that a donated pair of shoes represents to a person with a lower income.However, even if shoes are not among the most pressing needs of many people in lower-income communities, as long as a donated pair of shoes has any value to a person with a lower income, then donating shoes to lower-income people could help them.
(C) The prices Tony Toes charges for its shoes are some of the highest in the industry, making the shoes unaffordable for most people with lower incomes.

This choice could be taken as indicating that the way that Tony Toes operates its business is not helpful in general to lower-income people.The conclusion of the argument is not, however, that Tony Toes helps lower-income people in the course of operating its business.Rather, it is that Tony Toes will help lower-income people by donating shoes.So, this choice does not affect the argument.People who can afford the shoes would buy them, and Tony Toes would donate shoes to people who couldn’t afford to buy them.
(D) Often, people who buy Tony Toes shoes donate to charities their old shoes, which could be worn for years to come.

Because it mentions shoe donations, this choice may seem to have some relevance to the argument.The truth is, however, that, while the statement in this choice indicates that, in many cases, a purchase of Tony Toes shoes would result in a donation of shoes even if Tony Toes itself did not donate shoes, this information does not provide a clear reason to believe that Tony Toes’ donating shoes also would not help people with lower incomes.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) In many less affluent communities, shoe stores located there are key sources both of training for entry-level employees and of income.

While this choice does not mention shoe donations, it does describe a relevant dynamic in the economies of many less affluent areas.If we consider how the plan to donate shoes might affect that dynamic, we can see that donating many thousands of pairs of shoes to people in lower-income communities could cause shoe stores in those communities to lose significant amounts of business.If those shoe stores are sources of training for entry-level employees and of income, then Tony Toes’ donations may disrupt beneficial aspects of the local economies in those communities.While it is not clear whether the effect of donating shoes would be, on balance, beneficial or detrimental to less affluent communities, given the information in this choice, it is quite possible that thousands of shoe donations could have a negative impact on the local economies.So, this choice provides a reason to question the conclusion of the argument.

question.text: For gourmet restaurant L’Experience, it tends to be the case that, the more expensive a menu item is, the greater is the difference between the selling price of that item and the cost of the ingredients used in making the item.However, the most expensive items on L’Experience’s menu are rarely ordered.So, in order to increase profitability, the owners of the restaurant plan to remove the most expensive items from the menu and thus improve the efficiency of the operation.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the idea that the plan will have the desired effect?

answers.text: The ingredients in the most expensive items on the menu at L’Experience are not always more costly than ingredients in less expensive items.
Sometimes people perceive the most expensive items on a menu to be the most desirable.
Most of the people who patronize L’Experience tend to shy away from ordering the most expensive items on any menu.
Improved efficiency in the kitchen would result in faster service times without any increase in staff or equipment.
L’Experience has both menus that include prices, meant for patrons planning to pay, and menus that do not include prices, meant for guests of patrons planning to pay.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Generally, the greater the price of a menu item at L’Experience, the greater the difference between the cost of the ingredients in and the price of the item.
Premise: The most expensive items on the menu are rarely ordered.
Premise: Increase efficiency by removing the most expensive items from the menu.
Conclusion (the desired effect of the plan): Doing so will result in increased profitability.

While removing the most expensive items from the menu may indeed result in increased efficiency, increased efficiency may not be the only effect of removing the items.Even though the most expensive items are rarely ordered, removing them could have effects that would serve to reduce rather than increase profitability.What might the author have missed?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The ingredients in the most expensive items on the menu at L’Experience are not always more costly than ingredients in less expensive items.

The argument has already established that the difference between the cost of ingredients in and the selling prices of the more expensive items tends to be greater than that difference for less expensive items.So, by indicating that the ingredients in more expensive items sometimes cost less than the ingredients in less expensive items, what this choice says merely reinforces what we already know to be the case.It remains true that the most expensive items are rarely ordered.So, what this choice says does not materially affect the expected outcome of going through with the plan.
(B) Sometimes people perceive the most expensive items on a menu to be the most desirable.

While it may be possible to somehow spin what this choice says to make it seem to affect the argument, the truth is that, even though people may perceive the most expensive items to be the most desirable, people are not ordering those items.So, what this choice says does not affect the support for the conclusion that removing the items will result in increased efficiency and, thus, increased profitability.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Most of the people who patronize L’Experience tend to shy away from ordering the most expensive items on any menu.

What this choice says may seem to add little to the argument, as we have already been told that the people who patronize L’Experience rarely order the most expensive items on L’Experience’s menu.However, what this choice says implies the following.If the most expensive items are removed from L’Experience’s menu, and the next most expensive items become the most expensive items, people will stop ordering those next most expensive items and move to ordering even less expensive items.

Since the difference between selling price and ingredient costs tends to be lower for less expensive items, the implication of what this choice says is that removing the most expensive items from the menu will result in people’s moving to ordering items with margins between selling prices and ingredient costs that are lower than the margins of the items that people are ordering now.While we don’t have enough information to be sure that profitability will decrease as a result of this shift to ordering lower-margin items, that this shift would occur does serve to call into question the idea that removing the most expensive items will result in increased profitability for L’Experience.
(D) Improved efficiency in the kitchen would result in faster service times without any increase in staff or equipment.

The fact that improved efficiency would result in faster service tends to strengthen rather than weaken the conclusion that removing the rarely ordered, more expensive items from the menu will result in increased profitability for L’Experience.
(E) L’Experience has both menus that include prices, meant for patrons planning to pay, and menus that do not include prices, meant for guests of patrons planning to pay.

What this choice says could be taken as indicating that not everyone who patronizes L’Experience is aware of the prices of items on the menu and that, therefore, contrary to what the argument says, people do order the most expensive items.However, this choice is not a weakener, because the argument has already established that, regardless of whether the prices of items appear on every menu, the most expensive items on the menu at L’Experience are rarely ordered.It’s important to remember that the premises provided to us in the passage are factual, and answer choices that somehow seem to contradict those premises do not weaken the argument.

question.text: A company is considering offering a wellness program to its 3,000 employees in order to generate multiple benefits, including a reduction in health care costs.Before offering the program company wide, the company has tested it by offering it to 500 randomly selected employees.Of those 500, 250 signed up for the program and 250 didn’t.Over the next year, the average per person health care cost of the 250 who signed up for the program was approximately $300, while that of the 250 who didn’t was approximately $500.Clearly, offering the program company wide would result in a reduction in health care costs.Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the force of the evidence presented?

answers.text: For the year during which the wellness program was tested, the health care costs associated with some of the 2,500 employees not offered the program were well under $300 per person.
Even after being made aware of the results of the test, many of the company’s employees indicated that they would not sign up for the program.
At another company of similar size with no wellness program, the annual average per person health care cost is approximately $250.
The average per person health care cost of the 2,500 employees not offered the program was approximately $400 for the year over which the wellness program was tested.
The 10 employees with the highest health care costs for the year over which the wellness program was tested were ones who had signed up for the program.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Management tested a wellness program by offering it to 500 randomly selected employees.Of those 500, 250 signed up for the program and 250 didn’t.
Premise: Over the next year, the average per person health care cost of the 250 who signed up for the program was approximately $300, while that of the 250 who didn’t was approximately $500.
Conclusion: Clearly, offering the program company wide would result in a reduction in health care costs.

Notice that management tested the program by offering it to 500 randomly selected employees, of whom half enrolled, and half did not.Because the average per person health care cost of the people who enrolled in the program was significantly lower than that of the people who did not, the speaker believes that offering the program company wide would generate the desired result of a reduction in health care costs.Is the situation that simple, or could there be more to the story? What might the speaker have failed to consider?
YOUR CHOICE(A) For the year during which the wellness program was tested, the health care costs associated with some of the 2,500 employees not offered the program were well under $300 per person.

The nature of averages is such that, generally, some of the data points in a set will be below the average for that set.So, the fact that health care costs of “some” employees not offered the program were lower than the average health care cost of employees who signed up for the program does not mean that offering the program does not result in health care costs that are, on average, lower than they would have been otherwise.For all we know from what this choice says, theaverageper person health care cost of the employees not offered the program was much higher than the average per person healthcare cost of those who signed up for the program.So, this information about “some” data points does not reliably weaken the support for the conclusion.
(B) Even after being made aware of the results of the test, many of the company’s employees indicated that they would not sign up for the program.

Even if a minority of the employees signed up for the program, offering the program could still result in a reduction in health care costs.After all, as long as some employees sign up, there could be a reduction in health care costs.Thus, the fact that many employees indicated that they would not sign up does not weaken the argument.
(C) At another company of similar size with no wellness program, the annual average per person health care cost is approximately $250.

The employees at the other company may be very different from the employees at the company described in this passage.After all, the other company may be a low-stress environment in which people who have healthy lifestyles work, and the company considering offering the wellness program could be high-stress environment in which employees regularly take clients out for drinks, heavy meals, and cigars.Therefore, what occurred at the other company does not serve as a convincing basis for determining whether offering the wellness program would be beneficial, and the force of the argument’s evidence is not undermined by this choice.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The average per person health care cost of the 2,500 employees not offered the program was approximately $400 for the year over which the wellness program was tested.

If the average per person health care cost of the 2,500 employees not offered the program was approximately $400 for the year over which the wellness program was tested, then the argument is weakened.Why? Consider the following.

The average per person health care cost of the 250 who signed up for the program was approximately $300, while that of the 250 who didn’t was approximately $500.So, the average per person cost of those offered the program was ($300 + $500)/2 = $400.So, the average per person cost of those who were offered the program was ($300 + $500)/2 = $400.

If the average per person health care cost of the 2,500 employees not offered the program was, as this choice says, approximately $400 for the year over which the wellness program was tested, and the average per person cost of those who were offered the program was also $400, then there is good reason to believe that offering the program would not result in a reduction in health care costs, because the average per person health care cost of those offered the program was no different from the average per person health care cost of those not offered the program.(How tricky is that?!)
(E) The 10 employees with the highest health care costs for the year over which the wellness program was tested were ones who had signed up for the program.

Remember, the nature of averages is such that, generally, some of the data points in a set will be above the average for that set.The fact that 10 of the data points in the set of per person health care costs of employees who signed up for the program were higher than any other employees’ health care costs does not change the fact that the average per person health care cost of the 250 who signed up for the program was, at $300, significantly lower than the average per person health care cost of those who were offered the program but did not sign up, $500, and does not affect the implications of that fact.

question.text: A study of online searches for “veggies in comfort foods” found that the number of such searches rose 14 percent over the course of the past year.It has also been determined that the number of searches related to traditional, often less healthy, comfort foods, such as macaroni and cheese and beef Stroganoff, declined over the same period.Therefore, it may be concluded that people are more likely to eat healthier types of comfort foods in the coming year than they were in the last.Which of the following, if true, outlines the greatest challenge to the above prediction?

answers.text: People do not always follow through with good intentions they have for their diets.
Many people include both vegetables and ingredients that are less healthy when preparing meals for comfort.
The awareness people have about what constitutes a healthy diet has risen dramatically since they gained the ability to research nutrition online.
It is often the case that older people who are seeking to be careful about what they eat do not use online searches when choosing foods.
Companies that sell comfort foods that are less healthy are increasing their marketing efforts considerably in response to the recent challenges to their businesses.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: The number of online searches for “veggies in comfort foods” rose 14 percent over the course of the past year.
Premise: The number of searches related to traditional, often less healthy, comfort foods declined over the same period.
Conclusion: People are more likely to eat healthier types of comfort foods in the coming year than they were in the last.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since people are increasingly using the search term “veggies in comfort food” and decreasingly using terms that describe less healthy comfort foods, people are more likely to eat healthier comfort foods in the coming year than they were in the previous year.A challenge to the prediction will provide reason to believe that, even though people may be searching for healthier comfort foods, they will not necessarily be likely to eat more of those types of foods during the next year.
YOUR CHOICE(A) People do not always follow through with good intentions they have for their diets.

The conclusion of the argument is about the direction of a change: people will be “more likely” to eat healthier comfort foods.What this choice says affects thedegreeof change, but not thedirection.In other words, while this choice indicates thatsomepeople may not follow through with plans to eat healthier foods, and so the change to eating healthier foods may not happen to the degree that the 14 percent increase in searches suggests, that fact does not really weaken the conclusion that, in general, people are at least “more likely” to eat healthier foods.Even if only a third of the people who intended to eat healthier foods actually do, the conclusion still stands.
(B) Many people include both vegetables and ingredients that are less healthy when preparing meals for comfort.

A meal that includes both healthy ingredients and unhealthy ingredients is still healthier than a meal that includes only unhealthy ingredients.Also, while many people may include both healthy and unhealthy ingredients in their meals, others may include only healthy ingredients.So, even with the information provided in this choice, the conclusion of the argument still holds.
(C) The awareness people have about what constitutes a healthy diet has risen dramatically since they gained the ability to research nutrition online.

There is no reason to believe that gaining an awareness of what constitutes a healthy diet makes people less likely to eat healthy foods.If anything, that awareness would make peoplemorelikely to eat healthy foods.So, this choice strengthens the support for prediction rather than challenging it.
(D) It is often the case that older people who are seeking to be careful about what they eat do not use online searches when choosing foods.

If anything, the fact that older people seeking to eat healthier foods do not use online searches indicates that, in addition to the people who were conducting online searches related to healthy ingredients, older people are seeking to eat healthier foods.So, the trend toward healthier eating could be even stronger than the online search data indicated.Thus, if the information in this answer choice affects the prediction at all, it provides further support for the prediction rather than challenges the prediction.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Companies that sell comfort foods that are less healthy are increasing their marketing efforts considerably in response to the recent challenges to their businesses.

This choice may not seem to describe a challenge to the prediction, because we can’t be sure that the marketing efforts will be effective in getting people to switch back from eating healthier comfort foods to eating comfort foods that are less healthy.All the same, an increase in such marketing efforts does present a challenge to the prediction that people will be more likely to eat healthier comfort foods next year, because the marketing efforts may be effective.

question.text: Whereas, in the relatively recent past, long-distance communication was often low quality and expensive, now, people in almost any area of the world can relatively inexpensively engage in high quality video communication with each other over long distances.Therefore, the amount of business travel for face-to-face meetings with customers and business partners is certain to decrease.Which of the following, if true, most serves to call into question the above prediction?

answers.text: In many cities, there are few vacant hotel rooms during times of the year known to be peak times for business travel.
Airlines are known to cater to business travelers because of the substantial revenue business travelers bring the airlines.
Transportation expenses incurred in the course of doing business can be deducted from income reported for income tax purposes in most countries.
The lower the expense and greater the quality of long-distance communication, the more long-distance business relationships people develop.
In the time that it takes to travel to one business meeting, a person could use the latest technology to inexpensively communicate with many people around the world.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In the relatively recent past, long-distance communication was often low quality and expensive.
Premise: Now, people in almost any area of the world can relatively inexpensively engage in high quality video communication with each other.
Conclusion: Business travel for face-to-face meetings with customers and business partners is certain to decrease.

The speaker takes the fact that a relatively recent change has occurred–inexpensive, high quality video communication has become widely available–to be a reason to believe that another change will occur–the amount of business travel for face-to-face meetings will decrease. While this logic makes sense, a change can have multiple effects. What has the speaker failed to consider?
YOUR CHOICE(A) In many cities, there are few vacant hotel rooms during times of the year known to be peak times for business travel.

What this choice says could be spun in multiple ways.For instance, the fact that there are few vacant hotel rooms could be perceived as strengthening the argument, as potential business travelers would rather use long-distance communication than deal with finding vacant hotel rooms, and therefore they would reduce their travel if they could inexpensively engage in high quality communication without traveling.Alternatively, one could make the case that low numbers of vacant hotel rooms exist because people continue to travel for business meetings even though long-distance communication is high quality and inexpensive.The truth is that the information in this choice does not clearly affect the argument one way or another, as it does not clearly change the case for what can be expected as a result of the recent changes in the quality and expense of long-distance communication.
(B) Airlines are known to cater to business travelers because of the substantial revenue business travelers bring the airlines.

What this choice says could seem to weaken the prediction that business travel will decrease, as people might find business travel enjoyable or convenient as a result of their being catered to by airlines.However, notice that this choice describes a factor that is constant, how airlines treat business travelers, while the prediction is based on changes in long-distance communication.While airlines may continue to cater to business travelers, as they have all along, another factor, the availability of video communication, could at the same time serve to reduce the value of engaging in business travel.Therefore, this choice does not affect the argument.
(C) Transportation expenses incurred in the course of doing business can be deducted from income reported for income tax purposes in most countries.

This choice could be tempting, in that it brings up a factor that mitigates the cost of business travel and thus provides reason to believe that people will choose to engage in business travel.However, even though people can deduct travel expenses from income when calculating income taxes, the fact that inexpensive video communication has become available could still influence people to travel less.So, this choice does not affect the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The lower the expense and greater the quality of long-distance communication, the more long-distance business relationships people develop.

Even if the fact that people can easily and inexpensively use video communication is a factor influencing people to travel less, what this choice mentions, that people develop more long-distance business relationships because long-distance communication is easy and inexpensive, could be a factor influencing people to travel more.Since, given what this choice says, it’s not clear whether they will travel more or travel less as a result of the change related to long-distance communication, this choice serves to call into question the prediction.
(E) In the time that it takes to travel to one business meeting, a person could use the latest technology to inexpensively communicate with many people around the world.

This choice is relevant to the discussion in the passage, but it is a strengthener, not a weakener.The fact that a person can use the latest technology to communicate with many people around the world in the amount of time that it takes to travel to one business meeting supports the author’s prediction that people will travel less as a result of improvements in long-distance communication.We need an answer choice that calls the prediction into question.

question.text: Once a professor at a university gains tenure, that professor is assured a job at the university unless the professor engages in severe misconduct or fails to perform basic job responsibilities.So, university professors who have gained tenure are very difficult to fire.Since, in lacking the power to easily fire tenured professors, the administrations of universities are limited in their ability to control those professors’ activities, the recent trend toward not granting tenure to professors bodes well for the education of university students.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the support for the above conclusion?

answers.text: In seeking to gain tenure, non-tenured professors who feel that they could gain tenure may engage in a frenzy of research activities.
At many universities, students are asked to anonymously provide their opinions of the teaching performance of both tenured and non-tenured professors.
Tenured professors sometimes engage in research activities that have little to do with the curricula they cover when teaching classes.
A non-tenured professor is more likely than is a tenured professor to move from working at one university to working at another.
The aims of the administrators of universities and the optimal methods for educating students often do not match.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: A professor who gains tenure at a university is assured a job at that university except in certain extreme circumstances.
Premise: University professors who have gained tenure are very difficult to fire.
Premise: Lacking the power to easily fire tenured professors, universities are limited in their abilities to control the professors’ activities.
Conclusion: The recent trend toward not granting tenure bodes well for the education of university students.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since granting tenure to university professors makes firing professors very difficult, the recent trend toward not granting tenure bodes well for the education of university students.Implicit in the argument is that, were universities less limited in their abilities to control professors’ activities, universities would take more control of those activities.

There is also a key assumption that ties the premises to the conclusion.The argument rests on the assumption that universities’ having more control over professors is good for the education of university students.Is that a sound assumption?
YOUR CHOICE(A) In seeking to gain tenure, non-tenured professors who feel that they could gain tenure may engage in a frenzy of research activities.
This answer choice may be tempting because what it says seems to indicate that the possibility of tenure serves as an incentive for non-tenured professors to engage in research.

However, whether professors’ engaging in research benefits or is detrimental to the education of university students is not clear, and we could spin a story via which this choice weakens the argument, a story via which this choice strengthens the argument, or a story via which this choice does not affect the argument at all.So, since the way in which this choice affects the argument is unclear, this is not our weakening answer.
(B) At many universities, students are asked to anonymously provide their opinions of the teaching performance of both tenured and non-tenured professors.
This choice could be tempting in that what is says seems to indicate that even tenured professors are subject to some degree of scrutiny.

However, the conclusion is not based on a premise that tenured professors are completely beyond anyone’s control or oversight.Notice also that, although the conclusion pertains to the quality of education students will receive, the conclusion is not based on students’ ability to influence professors’ activities.The conclusion is based on the idea that university administrators, specifically, can gain more control over professors’ activities by not granting tenure.The effects that students’ opinions may or may not have on the activities of professors does not change any aspect of the argument.Thus, what this answer choice says does not undermine the conclusion.
(C) Tenured professors sometimes engage in research activities that have little to do with the curricula they cover when teaching classes.

Since what this choice says could be taken as implying that tenured professors engage in research that may distract them from educating students, if this information affects the argument at all, it strengthens the conclusion that the trend away from granting tenure bodes well for the education of university students. Really, though, what this choice says does not present a clear reason for us to think that the research activities mentioned have a particular impact–negative, positive, or even significant -- on the education students receive.
(D) A non-tenured professor is more likely than is a tenured professor to move from working at one university to working at another.

Whether professors’ moving from university to university is beneficial or detrimental to students’ education is not clear; such turnover could be spun as either.Thus, this choice has no clear effect on the conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) The aims of the administrators of universities and the optimal methods for educating students often do not match.

The statement that university administrators’ aims often do not match what is optimal for students’ education indicates that university administrators’ having more control over professors actually could be detrimental to students’ education.This choice attacks the argument’s key assumption, that university administrators’ having more control over professors’ activities is good for students’ education, and thus this choice weakens the argument.

question.text: For a study, people experiencing depression were placed into two groups, the Exercise Group and the Control Group.The people in the Exercise Group were given support for creating and following an exercise program, with the result that 50 percent of those in the Exercise Group reported getting regular exercise, while those in Control Group were given no such support.After a year, there was little difference between the average level of depression seen in members of the Exercise Group and that seen in members of the Control Group.Clearly, exercise is not effective in mitigating depression.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the force of the evidence cited?

answers.text: The members of the Exercise Group did not all use the same forms of exercise.
A significant proportion of the members of the Exercise Group were considered no longer depressed after a year.
The physiological effects of vigorous exercise are in some aspects quite different from those of light exercise.
Endorphins, chemicals released by the body during exercise, can contribute to a person’s having a positive outlook on life.
Typically, about 45 percent of people choose to exercise regularly.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: People in the Exercise Group, but not those in the Control Group, were given support for creating and following an exercise program.
Premise: 50 percent of those in Exercise Group reported that they exercised.
Premise: After a year, there was little difference between the average level of depression seen in members of the Exercise Group and that seen in members of the Control Group.
Conclusion: Exercise is not effective in mitigating depression.

The evidence seems pretty convincing, and what’s described in the passage fairly closely matches a real-world study the results of which were published and considered newsworthy.However, one answer choice brings up something that the author of the passage, and the real-world researchers, missed, something that dramatically undermines the support that the evidence presented provides for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The members of the Exercise Group did not all use the same forms of exercise.
What this choice says may appear to be a reason to question the results of the study.However, this choice is a pseudo-weakener that doesn’t actually affect the support for the conclusion.

There is no clear reason to believe that, since the members of the Exercise group did not all use the same forms of exercise, the results of the study are not valid.Therefore, this choice does not affect the argument.
(B) A significant proportion of the members of the Exercise Group were considered no longer depressed after a year.
This answer choice is tempting, in that it could be taken as weakening the argument by suggesting that the study results do indicate that exercise mitigates depression.

However, the conclusion is based on the lack ofdifferencebetween the results experienced by the Exercise Group and the results experienced by the Control Group, and what this choice says does not change the fact that the two sets of results differed little from each other.
For all we know, a significant proportion of the members of the Control Group also were considered no longer depressed after a year.
(C) The physiological effects of vigorous exercise are in some aspects quite different from those of light exercise.

What this choice says, that vigorous and light exercise differ in their effects, could somehow be construed as indicating that there is a factor–this difference–that the study did not take into account. However, this choice is a pseudo-weakener that doesn’t actually affect the support for the conclusion.

Since there is no clear reason to believe that this difference between the effects of different kinds of exercise affected the results of the study, this choice does not effectively weaken the argument.
(D) Endorphins, chemicals released by the body during exercise, can contribute to a person’s having a positive outlook on life.

By indicating that chemicals released by the body during exercise can positively affect a person’s outlook on life, this choice may seem to weaken the conclusion of the argument by presenting a pathway via which exercise could be effective in mitigating depression.

However, information indicating that exercise could, or even should, be effective in mitigating depression does not negate the fact that the study results showed little difference between the average level of depression in members of the two groups.So, regardless of the fact that endorphins have certain effects, the evidence still seems to support the conclusion that exercise is not effective in mitigating depression.
In other words, the force of the evidence presented is not weakened in any way by this information about endorphins.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Typically, about 45 percent of people choose to exercise regularly.

The conclusion is based on the lack of difference between the levels of depression seen in the people in the Exercise Group, 50 percent of whom exercised, and levels of depression seen in those in the Control Group.However, in saying that typically 45 percent of people exercise, this choice indicates that, without the type of support the people in the Exercise Group received, typically 45 percent of people exercise anyway.

If, ordinarily, 45 percent of people exercise, then we have reason to believe that it is quite likely that 45 percent of the people in the Control Group exercised, and thus, that the percentage of people in the Control Group who exercised could very well be little different from the percentage of those in the Exercise Group who exercised.

If the respective percentages of the people in the two group who exercised are almost equal, the results of the study are virtually meaningless.So, what this choice says clearly undermines the support for the conclusion (and, in the real world, such information completely wrecked the support for the conclusion arrived at by real-world researchers who undertook the study that is the basis for this question).

question.text: Many New World monkeys, which live in American forests, have prehensile tails, whereas none of the Old World monkeys, which live in Asian and African forests, have prehensile tails.Maintaining an appendage like a prehensile tail requires the use of energy, and animals tend to evolve to have an appendage only when the benefits of having that appendage outweigh the costs.Therefore, it’s likely that the reason why the tails of Old World monkeys did not evolve to become prehensile is that prehensile tails would not benefit Old World monkeys as much as such tails benefit New World monkeys.Which of the following, if true, would provide the additional support for the above conclusion?

answers.text: As body hair became less useful to humanoids, they evolved to have much less body hair than they had previously.
New World monkeys tend to get the bulk of their energy from fruit, whereas Old World monkeys get much of their energy from leaves.
Great apes, with their relatively high levels of intelligence, use their opposable thumbs extensively, whereas other primates, with lower levels of intelligence, lack opposable thumbs.
Vines, which can be put to use in a variety of ways by animals with prehensile tails, are much more common in American forests than in Asian or African forests.
If animals were to use energy unnecessarily, they would have to consume food to generate that energy and would reduce the likelihood of their surviving periods of food scarcity.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many New World monkeys, which live in American forests, have prehensile tails, whereas none of the Old World monkeys, which live in Asian and African forests, have prehensile tails.

Premise: Maintaining an appendage like a prehensile tail requires the use of energy, and animals tend to evolve to have an appendage only when the benefits of having that appendage outweigh the costs.

Conclusion: Therefore, it’s likely that the reason why the tails of Old World monkeys did not evolve to become prehensile is that prehensile tails would not benefit Old World monkeys as much as such tails benefit New World monkeys.

The author cites two facts: that many of the New World monkeys have prehensile tails, but none of the Old World monkeys do; and that animals typically have appendages when their benefits outweigh their costs.From these facts, she concludes that the reason why Old Word monkeys don’t have prehensile tails is that such tails would not benefit Old World monkeys as much as such tails benefit New World monkeys.

It’s important to see that her conclusion is simply her own interpretation of the evidence.Is her conclusion correct? If we gave the same evidence to 10,000 other biologists, would they all arrive at the same conclusion, or would they draw different opinions from the evidence?
Anyone can look at some evidence and draw a conclusion.Our job in answering this question is to find an answer choice that would make us even just a little more confident in her conclusion.
(A) As body hair became less useful to humanoids, they evolved to have much less body hair than they had previously.

This choice strengthens the wrong thing.By providing the example of a group of animals, humanoids, that evolved away from having a physical attribute, body hair, when that attribute became less useful, this choice tends to confirm that animals evolve away from having physical attributes that are not useful to them.

However, since the author has stated as fact the premise that animals tend to evolve to have an appendage only when the benefits of having that appendage outweigh the costs, confirmation that animals evolve in that way does not strengthen the argument.A stated fact is considered true in GMAT Critical Reasoning, and what’s already considered true requires no further confirmation.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(B) New World monkeys tend to get the bulk of their energy from fruit, whereas Old World monkeys get much of their energy from leaves.

While this choice does provide information on a key difference between New World monkeys and Old World monkeys, and touches on a detail in the author’s argument, energy, the author’s argument the does not seek to compare different sources of energy of monkeys.
Rather, the conclusion of the argument is about the usefulness of a certain anatomical feature of monkeys.
Since we have no information that connects what this choice says about energy sources with monkeys’ tails, there’s no clear way to use this choice to support the argument’s conclusion about tails.
(C) Great apes, with their relatively high levels of intelligence, use their opposable thumbs extensively, whereas other primates, with lower levels of intelligence, lack opposable thumbs.

This choice could be tempting because, like the argument, it provides a comparison, in this case between great apes and other primates.It also seems to be connected to the thread of evolving appendages because of the benefit they provide, because it seems to connect intelligence and opposable thumbs.We might presume that a more intelligent animal would be more able to use an opposable thumb to perform complicated tasks (and thus more able to derive benefits from the thumb) than a less intelligent animal.

While all of this information seems to mirror the argument laid out in the passage, it does not actually provide any further support for the specific conclusion that prehensile tails benefit New World monkeys more than prehensile tails benefit Old World monkeys.This choice is focused on a separate matter, what seems to be a connection between intelligence and opposable thumbs.

To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to read into a statement more than what that statement actually says.Yes, great apes are similar to monkeys, and yes apes have evolved to have appendages of a certain type, opposable thumbs.However, we can’t let the similarity of what this choice says to what the passage says tempt us into believing that this choice confirms the argument’s conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Vines, which can be put to use in a variety of ways by animals with prehensile tails, are much more common in American forests than in Asian or African forests.

If vines, which can be put to use in a variety of ways by animals with prehensile tails, are much more common in American forests, where there are New World monkeys, than in Asian or African forests, where there are Old World monkeys, then we have some additional evidence to strengthen the author’s conclusion about why Old World monkeys’ tails are not prehensile.

Since there are fewer vines in Asian and African forests, prehensile tails would not benefit Old World monkeys in Asian and African forests as much as such tails benefit monkeys where there are more vines.

So, this choice helps to confirm the conclusion by providing clear evidence that prehensile tails would not benefit Old World monkeys as much as such tails benefit New World monkeys.Given this evidence, we have more reason to believe that the reason the Old World monkeys tails didn’t evolve to become prehensile is that having such tails would not benefit Old World monkeys.
(E) If animals were to use energy unnecessarily, they would have to consume food to generate that energy and would reduce the likelihood of their surviving periods of food scarcity.

This answer choice could be tempting because it seems similar to the premises that we saw in the passage: “maintaining an appendage such as a prehensile tail requires the use of energy, and animals tend to evolve to have an appendage only when the benefits … outweigh the costs.”

So, in reading what this choice says, that “if animals were to use energy unnecessarily, they … would reduce the likelihood of their surviving periods of food scarcity” we might decide that we have information that further supports the author’s conclusion about why Old World monkeys’ tails are not prehensile.
Notice, however, that this choice supports a conclusion different from the one that we are seeking to support.

This choice supports the conclusion that an animal would not have an appendage that isnot necessary, while the conclusion that we have to support is that Old World monkeys don’t have prehensile tails because such tails wouldnot benefitOld World monkeys.

Necessary and beneficial are not the same thing.Something could be beneficial without being necessary.For instance, it could be that New World monkeys don’tneedprehensile tails to function, but theybenefitfrom having the tails.
So, this choice supports a conclusion other than the one for which we are seeking support.

question.text: Many art school graduates fail to find work in the art field after graduation and accept employment in positions unrelated to their art skills.However, the Art School of Newtown places 78 percent of its graduates in publicly funded museums and private galleries.The Art School of Newtown claims that this high placement rate is due to the financial management classes that its students are required to take as part of the curriculum.Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the Art School of Newtown’s claim?

answers.text: Many art school graduates choose to pursue careers in finance.
Publicly funded museums and private galleries prefer to hire people with business degrees to be in charge of the finances of these institutions.
After graduation, some art school graduates realize that they are not interested in working in the art industry.
Newtown has more publicly funded museums and private galleries than most other cities where art schools are located have.
Publicly funded museums and private galleries have limited funding for staff and prefer to hire people with broad skill sets.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Many art school graduates fail to find work in the art field after graduation and accept employment in positions unrelated to their art skills.
Premise: However, the Art School of Newtown places 78 percent of its graduates in publicly funded museums and private galleries.
Conclusion: The Art School of Newtown claims that this high placement rate is due to the financial management classes that its students are required to take as part of the curriculum.

The argument provides us with a fact – that the Art School of Newtown places an especially high percentage of graduates in art-related jobs.The argument then concludes that this outcome is the result of its students taking financial management classes.

Notice that such reasoning is based on cause and effect.As we well know, we should be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim made by a speaker in a Critical Reasoning question.Could there be an alternative explanation?

Notice that the argument fails to explain why these classes make graduates more employable.Because our job is to strengthen the argument in this question, we’ll seek an answer choice that increases the likelihood that the required financial management classes are in fact the cause of the observed effect.
(A) Many art school graduates choose to pursue careers in finance.

This choice could be tempting, because it could be taken as indicating that knowledge gained via taking the financial management courses helps many graduates of the school to pursue their careers of choice, careers in finance.

However, the conclusion of the argument is not that graduates of the school are successful in pursuing their chosen careers because of their having taken financial management courses.The conclusion is that the reason for graduates’high rate of placement in art-related positionsis the school’s requiring students to take financial management courses.
So, the information in this answer choice has no impact on the conclusion.
(B) Publicly funded museums and private galleries prefer to hire people with business degrees to be in charge of the finances of these institutions.
This choice could be tempting because it can be perceived as confirming that having financial knowledge would help art school graduates to get jobs at museums and galleries.

Notice, however, that financial management knowledge would not helpart school graduatesto secure jobs in museums or galleries if, as this choice says,people with business degreesare preferred for those jobs.We must be sure not to confuse “people with business degrees” with art school graduates with some education in financial management.
What this answer choice says does not help to confirm that the art school’s high placement rate is the result of its requiring its students to take financial management classes.
(C) After graduation, some art school graduates realize that they are not interested in working in the art industry.
This answer choice has no effect on the argument.
The conclusion is about art school graduates who are placed in jobs in the art industry, while this choice is about art school graduates who are not interested in working in the art industry.

What this answer choice says might help explain why many art school graduates fail to find employment in their field, but it does not tell us anything about why the Art School of Newton has a high placement rate.
(D) Newtown has more publicly funded museums and private galleries than most other cities where art schools are located have.

This answer is tempting because it can be perceived as related to the argument.The argument is about the school’s high rate of placement of graduates in jobs in museums and galleries, and this choice is about high numbers of museums and galleries into which, presumably, graduates of the school could be placed.

However, if anything, this choice weakens the support for the Art School’s cause-and-effect claim, because it provides a possible alternative explanation for the school’s high placement rate: the school’s graduates find jobs at museums and galleries at a high rate, not because of the graduates having taken financial management classes, but because there are many local museums and galleries that hire the school’s graduates.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Publicly funded museums and private galleries have limited funding for staff and prefer to hire people with broad skill sets.

If museums and galleries want to hire people who have not just art knowledge but broad skill sets, financial management knowledge is an asset that could make graduates from the Art School of Newtown particularly desirable employees for museums and galleries.

So, by tending to indicate that a person who has taken financial management classes would be particularly valuable to a museum or gallery, this choice strengthens the case for believing that the school’s requiring its students to take financial management classes is the cause of the high placement rate.

question.text: Economist: In some cases, advertising-generated interest in a product can result in an increase in the price of that product.However, the overall effect of advertising in general is to provide information to consumers, and well-informed consumers tend to spur companies to compete with one another on price.My hypothesis is that advertising’s net effect on prices is to cause them to be lower than they would otherwise be.Which of the following, if true, provides further indication that the economist’s hypothesis will turn out to be correct?

answers.text: Advertising often enables companies to sell higher volumes of products than the companies would otherwise sell, allowing companies to be profitable while receiving lower revenue per unit sold.
When a manufacturer uses advertising to sell a product, some of the revenue received via sales of that product goes to pay for advertising rather than going to the manufacturer.
In addition to becoming informed about products through exposure to advertising, consumers can get extensive information about products by doing internet research.
Advertising can be used by large companies to dominate markets and thus make it difficult for smaller companies to effectively enter into and compete in those markets.
Advertising that uses creative methods to connect with potential consumers is generally more effective than advertising that simply provides information about products.

explanation_parts.text: The economist states the following hypothesis:
Conclusion: Advertising’s net effect on prices is to cause them to be lower than they would otherwise be.
The conclusion is supported by the following premises:
Premise: The overall effect of advertising in general is to provide information to consumers.
Premise: Well-informed consumers tend to spur companies to compete with one another on price.
In order to correctly answer the question, we have to find an additional premise that supports the conclusion that advertising causes prices to be lower than they otherwise would be.
In considering the choices, we have to be careful not to spin unsupported stories that could make incorrect choices that have no bearing on the argument seem to support the argument.

YOUR CHOICE(A) Advertising often enables companies to sell higher volumes of products than the companies would otherwise sell, allowing companies to be profitable while receiving lower revenue per unit sold.

.Since revenue per unit sold is essentially price, this choice is basically saying that, via advertising, companies can be profitable while selling products at prices lower than those at which profitability is possible without advertising.Will companies sell products at lower prices if they can still be profitable at those prices? Not necessarily, but common sense tells us that some likely will, and so, what this choice says provides further indication that advertising causes lower prices.
(B) When a manufacturer uses advertising to sell a product, some of the revenue received via sales of that product goes to pay for advertising rather than going to the manufacturer.
.This choice may seem relevant to the argument, but this choice indicates that advertising causes a decrease in the wrong thing.

We need to confirm that advertising causes lower prices.However, this choice indicates that advertising costs cause the amount of revenue from sales that goes to a manufacturer to be lower than it would be if those advertising costs did not exist.
Thus, this choice does not present information that is relevant to the economist’s hypothesis.
(C) In addition to becoming informed about products through exposure to advertising, consumers can get extensive information about products by doing internet research.

This choice could be tempting if we were to lose track of the conclusion and think that the conclusion is something along of the lines of that prices will be held down by well-informed consumers.After all, if the conclusion were that prices will be held down by well-informed consumers, then the fact that consumers can become informed not only through exposure to advertising but also by doing internet research could provide additional support for the conclusion by giving us more reason to believe that consumers will be well-informed and, therefore, hold down prices.

However, since the conclusion is that advertising causes prices to be lower than they otherwise would be, not that prices will be held down by well-informed consumers, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.After all, the fact that consumers can get information about products by doing internet research does not provide any confirmation that advertising causes prices to be lower than they otherwise would be.
(D) Advertising can be used by large companies to dominate markets and thus make it difficult for smaller companies to effectively enter into and compete in those markets.

.The effect of this choice is the opposite of the effect we need.If companies can use advertising to reduce competition, then they likely can use advertising to keep prices higher than prices otherwise would be.After all, if competition is reduced, then there isn’t much need for “companies to compete with one another on price,” right?
(E) Advertising that uses creative methods to connect with potential consumers is generally more effective than advertising that simply provides information about products.

.While the comparison presented by this choice does mention information presented in advertising, a topic addressed in the argument, comparing the effectiveness of providing information with the effectiveness of creativity does nothing the support the conclusion that advertising results in prices that are lower than they would otherwise be.

If anything, what this answer choice says could seem to weaken the support for economist’s conclusion, if one were to assume that the more effective, creative advertising creates the sort of interest in products that results in increased prices.

question.text: While it pays teachers lower salaries than are paid to teachers in any other town in the state, the town of Dog Creek is offering to pay off the balances on the student loans of any teacher who teaches there for at least five years.Therefore, the town can expect to see an influx of new teachers teaching at its schools next year.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the support for the conclusion?

answers.text: The amounts of money that many teachers in the state owe on their student loans represent significant percentages of the typical salary paid to a teacher in the state.
The cost of living in Dog Creek is considerably higher than that in any other town in the state.
Some teachers teaching in the state do not have student loan balances.
Students at Dog Creek schools tend to rate the teaching skills of their teachers more highly than students in other towns in the state rate the skills of their teachers.
In five years, the town of Dog Creek will have paid off bonds that it issued and, therefore, will no longer have to pay interest on those bonds.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: While it pays teachers lower salaries than are paid to teachers in any other town in the state, the town of Dog Creek is offering to pay off the balances on the student loans of any teacher who teaches there for at least five years.
Conclusion: The town can expect to see an influx of new teachers teaching at its schools next year.
The town of Dog Creek has a plan.In answering this question, we’ll be seeking an answer choice that gives us more faith that the plan will work as expected.
What information would give us more faith that the plan will work?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The amounts of money that many teachers in the state owe on their student loans represent significant percentages of the typical salary paid to a teacher in the state.

If the amounts of money that many teachers in the state owe on their student loans represent significant percentages of the typical salary paid to a teacher in the state, then we have more faith that the plan will work.
After all, the town of Dog Creek is offering to pay off the balances on the student loans of any teacher who teaches there for at least five years.
We can think of this payment as a “perk” of taking a teaching job in Dog Creek.Of course, the more valuable the perk, the more faith we have that the perk will actually attract teachers there.

What this choice says confirms that this perk is valuable to teachers who might consider working in Dog Creek by indicating that the amount of money they would be effectively receiving by having their loan balances paid off is significant to them.
Notice that what this answer choice says gives us another piece of information that makes us say, “Yes I think that the town’s plan will work.”
(B) The cost of living in Dog Creek is considerably higher than that in any other town in the state.

What this answer choice says weakens the argument.If the cost of living in Dog Creek is considerably higher than in any other town in the state, then we have less faith that the plan will work as expected.

After all, any teacher considering moving to Dog Creek to live and teach will need to consider not only all of the benefits of living and teaching there, such as having student loan balances paid off, but also any drawbacks of living and teaching there.
Certainly, if the cost of living in Dog Creek is considerably higher than that in any other town in the state, that high cost of living would be a drawback to living and teaching in Dog Creek.
(C) Some teachers teaching in the state do not have student loan balances.
What this answer choice says does not really affect the strength of the argument.
That some teachers teaching in the state do not have student loan balances is no surprise, but that fact does not increase or decrease our faith in the plan.

The plan is targeted at those teachers who do have student loans.As long as some teachers have student loans, the plan could work, and the fact that some don’t have student loans certainly doesn’t help the plan to work.
(D) Students at Dog Creek schools tend to rate the teaching skills of their teachers more highly than students in other towns in the state rate the skills of their teachers.

The fact that students at Dog Creek schools tend to rate the teaching skills of their teachers more highly than students in other towns in the state rate the skills of their teachers could indicate a variety of things, such as that Dog Creek hires skilled teachers.
However, there is no clear connection between what this choice says and the plan outlined.So, this choice does not strengthen the argument.

In order to avoid choosing an incorrect choice like this one, we have to be careful not to come up with convoluted, unsupported stories, such as that teachers will want to work in Dog Creek because they will get high ratings.We really don’t know that teachers would expect to get high ratings if they work in Dog Creek or that the prospect of getting high rating from Dog Creek students would motivate teachers to work there.
(E) In five years, the town of Dog Creek will have paid off bonds that it issued and, therefore, will no longer have to pay interest on those bonds.
This choice is an interesting trap because what it says is clearly pertinent to what's discussed in the passage.
However, this choice does not have the effect we need, which is to strengthen the support for conclusion of the argument.
This choice has two effects.

One effect is that it seems to explain why Dog Creek is offering to pay off student loans of teachers who teach there for at least five years.After all, if, in five years, the town of Dog Creek will have paid off bonds that it issued and, therefore, will no longer have to pay interest on those bonds, then, presumably, in five years, Dog Creek will suddenly be able to afford to pay off student loans of teachers who have taught there for five years, because it will be able to use the revenue that it has been using to pay interest to pay off teachers' student loans instead.So, the fact that Dog Creek will likely be in a position to pay off student loans in five years could explain why Dog Creek is offering to compensate teachers in this way.

The second effect is that this choice supports a conclusion related to the argument's conclusion, such as the conclusion that, in five years, Dog Creek will be able to pay off the student loans of teachers who have taught there for at least five years, because if Dog Creek no longer has to pay interest on bonds, it will likely have funds to pay off teachers' student loans.Of course, since the conclusion of the argument is not that Dog Creek will be able to pay off the student loans but that there will be an influx of teachers to Dog Creek, this choice supports the wrong conclusion.
So, since neither of the effects that this choice has are the effect we need, this choice is not the correct answer.

question.text: Zoologists have advanced multiple theories regarding how it is possible that, after spending years at sea swimming thousands of miles, a loggerhead sea turtle can, to lay her eggs, find her way back to the nesting site where she was born.It has been discovered that loggerhead turtles can sense magnetic fields.Therefore, it is likely that loggerheads use Earth’s magnetic fields to navigate back to their nesting sites.Which of the following, if true, provides the most additional support for the conclusion?

answers.text: It is believed that baby sea turtles imprint certain unique qualities of the beaches where they are born, such as smell, sound vibrations, and characteristics of nearby ocean currents.
Conservationists are concerned that metal cages used to protect loggerhead turtle nests may interfere with baby loggerheads’ sensing of magnetic fields.
Sharks, which are known to be able to sense magnetic fields, have been observed to swim in straight lines for long distances across the open ocean.
By laying eggs in the same location where she was born, a loggerhead maximizes the probability that the site where she lays her eggs will support their hatching.
When Earth’s magnetic fields shift slightly, loggerhead turtles slightly shift the locations where they lay their eggs.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: A loggerhead sea turtle can, to lay her eggs, find her way back to the nesting site where she was born.
Premise: It has been discovered that loggerhead turtles can sense magnetic fields.
Conclusion: It is likely that loggerheads use Earth’s magnetic fields to navigate back to their nesting sites
(A) It is believed that baby sea turtles imprint certain unique qualities of the beaches where they are born, such as smell, sound vibrations, and characteristics of nearby ocean currents.
The argument’s conclusion is that it is likely that loggerheads use Earth’s magnetic fields to navigate back to their nesting sites.What this choice says would weaken the support for that conclusion.

If baby sea turtles imprint qualities of the beaches where they are born, such as smell, sound vibrations, and characteristics of nearby ocean currents, then we have an alternative explanation for how the turtles are able to find their way back to the nesting sites.The turtle’s use what they have imprinted, rather than magnetic fields, to navigate back to their nesting sites.

The fact that there is another way in which turtles may navigate back to the sites is a reason to wonder whether the use magnetic fields to do so, not a reason to believe that they do use magnetic fields.
(B) Conservationists are concerned that metal cages used to protect loggerhead turtle nests may interfere with baby loggerheads’ sensing of magnetic fields.
The passage already states that loggerhead turtles can sense magnetic fields.Thus, this answer choice basically just confirms what we already know.

The new information that this answer choice does provide, that metal cages used to protect loggerhead turtle nests may interfere with baby loggerheads’ sensing of magnetic fields, certainly does not provide additional support for the conclusion, which is that loggerheads use Earth’smagnetic fields to navigate back to their nesting sites.

If the conclusion were simply that loggerheads sense magnetic fields, then perhaps this answer choice would provide some support for that conclusion, but that they sense magnetic fields in general is a stated premise, and we are not looking to reiterate a premise.
(C) Sharks, which are known to be able to sense magnetic fields, have been observed to swim in straight lines for long distances across the open ocean.
Whatever this information may tell us about sharks and magnetic fields, we have no reason to assume that what is true of sharks is also true of loggerhead turtles.

Furthermore, what this choice says doesn’t even confirm that sharks use magnetic fields to navigate.Sure, sharks sense magnetic fields, and yes, sharks swim in straight lines, but do they use the magnetic fields in maintaining those straight lines? What this choice says does not tell us that they do.

If the foregoing is hard to see, consider the following sentence: “Sharks, which can see sunlight, have been observed to swim on straight lines for long distances across the open ocean.” Does that sentence mean that sharks navigate via the sun? Of course not.
This choice doesn’t even confirm that sharks use magnetic fields to navigate, and it certainly doesn’t confirm that turtles do.
(D) By laying eggs in the same location where she was born, a loggerhead maximizes the probability that the site where she lays her eggs will support their hatching.

What this answer choice says may explain why a loggerhead turtle would want tofind her way back to the site where she was born.However, we are not looking for an explanation for the turtle’s behavior.We are looking for support for a conclusion regardinghowthe turtle accomplishes her goal.Knowing the loggerhead’s motivation for returning to her birth site does nothing to strengthen the conclusion that loggerheads likely use Earth’s magnetic fields to navigate to such sites.
YOUR CHOICE(E) When Earth’s magnetic fields shift slightly, loggerhead turtles slightly shift the locations where they lay their eggs.

The argument’s conclusion is that loggerheads likely use Earth’s magnetic fields to navigate back to their nesting sites.In other words, the conclusion is that these turtles are able to somehow follow Earth’s magnetic fields.Evidence that loggerheads shift nesting locations in congruence with shifts in Earth’s magnetic fields provides strong support for that conclusion.
Imagine if a shift in Earth’s magnetic fields had no effect on where loggerheads nested? We could consider that a pretty clear weakener of the argument’s conclusion, right?
On the other hand, the fact that loggerheads shift where they nest when the magnetic fields shift tends to indicate that the loggerheads use the magnetic fields in navigating to nesting sites.

question.text: Fluent Representative: Our Spanish course is an amazingly effective way to quickly master the Spanish language; it teaches everything one needs to gain fluency.To 100 graduates of Fluent’s Spanish course and 120 students who graduated from an array of other Spanish language courses, we recently administered a rigorous, 200-question exam, which tested students’ Spanish-speaking ability.On average, the 100 Fluent graduates outscored the 120 other students by 87 points.Clearly, using Fluent’s Spanish course is the best way to master Spanish.Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the Fluent representative’s argument that using Fluent’s Spanish course is the best way to master Spanish?

answers.text: Most of the 120 students who used the other Spanish courses studied for fewer hours than the students who used Fluent studied for.
Most teachers of Spanish feel that Fluent graduates tend to speak near-perfect Spanish and that graduates demonstrate a deep mastery of Spanish grammar, style, and rhetoric.
An intake survey administered by Fluent’s director of global sales showed that none of the 100 Fluent graduates had previously used any other course to learn Spanish.
Students who graduated from Fluent’s French course scored significantly higher on a similar exam than students of other French courses did.
Before enrolling in a Spanish course, a majority of the Fluent graduates had read some books on basic Spanish, but few of the other 120 students had.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Our Spanish course is an amazingly effective way to quickly master the Spanish language; it teaches everything one needs to gain fluency.
