Premise: To 100 graduates of Fluent’s Spanish course and 120 students who graduated from an array of other Spanish language courses, we recently administered a rigorous, 200-question exam, which tested students’ Spanish-speaking ability.
Premise:On average, the 100 Fluent graduates outscored the 120 other students by 87 points.
Conclusion: Clearly, using Fluent’s Spanish course is the best way to master Spanish.

We have a cause-and-effect argument.As we know well, we must be skeptical of such arguments; however, because this is a Strengthen the Argument question, our job is to bolster the Fluent representative’s argument that the Fluent course was what caused Fluent graduates to outscore the other students.It will be helpful to rule out any alternative explanations for why the Fluent students performed so well on the exam.
(A) Most of the 120 students who used the other Spanish courses studied for fewer hours than the students who used Fluent studied for.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument by providing an alternative explanation for the superior performance of Fluent graduates.

If most of the students who used other courses studied for fewer hours than students who used the Fluent course did, then we have less reason to believe that the Fluent course was the cause of Fluent students’ superior performance.Rather, the respective study times could be the reason for the gap in performance between the two groups of students.
Thus, what this answer choice says has an effect that is the opposite of what we’re seeking to do, since we’re seeking to strengthen the argument that the course was the cause.
(B) Most teachers of Spanish feel that Fluent graduates tend to speak near-perfect Spanish and that graduates demonstrate a deep mastery of Spanish grammar, style, and rhetoric.
This answer choice could be tempting because it seems to provide additional evidence to support the conclusion that the Fluent course is the best course for mastering Spanish.

However, even if every expert in the world could feel that Fluent students are skilled at speaking and writing Spanish, this fact would not strengthen the argument that the Fluent course, in particular, was the cause of those Spanish skills.It could be the case that the Fluent course is not very good at all, but people taking the course are highly motivated studiers who seek outside resources to supplement their learning with the Fluent course.
So, what this answer choice says does not give us any further reason to believe that the Fluent course, in particular, was the cause of students’ superior exam performance.
YOUR CHOICE(C) An intake survey administered by Fluent’s director of global sales showed that none of the 100 Fluent graduates had previously used any other course to learn Spanish.
What this answer choice says strengthens the Fluent representative’s argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for the Fluent students’ superior exam performance.

If it is true that none of the 100 Fluent graduates previously used any other course to learn Spanish, then we have stronger reason to believe that the Fluent course was actually what helped these students perform so well on the exam.After all, if all of the 100 Fluent graduates had used a number of other Spanish courses before they used the Fluent course, wouldn’t we be less convinced the Fluent course was the cause of their superior performance on the exam? We’d wonder whether their superior performance were instead the result of their having taken multiple Spanish courses, or whether one of the other courses had taught them the bulk of what they needed to know.
By ruling out a potential weakener, that Fluent students may have gained their skills from some other course, the choice strengthens the argument.
(D) Students who graduated from Fluent’s French course scored significantly higher on a similar exam than students of other French courses did.

Even if the Fluent French course were exactly the same as the Fluent Spanish course, the fact that graduates of Fluent’s French course scored significantly higher on a similar exam than students of other French courses scored does not tell us that the Fluent course was thecauseof the superior exam performance.Moreover, the Fluent French course could be very different from the Fluent Spanish course.Maybe the Fluent French course is amazing, but the Fluent Spanish course is mediocre.

Perhaps the information in this answer choice would strengthen an argument that graduates of Fluent courses, in general, perform better on language tests than graduates of other language courses do.But, this choice has no clear effect on the given argument.
(E) Before enrolling in a Spanish course, a majority of the Fluent graduates had read some books on basic Spanish, but few of the other 120 students had.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument by providing an alternative explanation for Fluent students’ superior exam performance.

If a majority of the Fluent graduates read some basic Spanish before enrolling in a Spanish course, but few of the non-Fluent students did, then we have a good reason to believe that Fluent students outperformed non-Fluent students not because of the Fluent course, but because they had more prior Spanish language training and experience than was had by the non-Fluent students.
By providing a plausible alternative explanation for the observed effect, this answer choice makes the representative’s cause-and-effect argument less convincing.

question.text: The Lakeland County Fair comes to town this weekend. At the fair will be vendors selling candy apples. Camila loves Candy apples. Clearly, Camila will attend the fair this weekend.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the support for the conclusion?

answers.text: There will be fewer candy apple vendors at the fair this year than there were last year.
Camila is aware that the fair is coming to town this weekend.
Some of Camila’s friends have invited her to a concert this weekend.
The price of admission to the fair is the same this year as it was last year.
Camila is considered an expert in judging candy apples.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: The Lakeland County Fair comes to town this weekend.
Premise: At the fair will be vendors selling candy apples.
Premise: Camila loves Candy apples.
Conclusion: Clearly, Camila will attend the fair this weekend.
As you know, we must always be on the lookout for assumptions made by the author.What does the author assume in making the argument?
A choice that states outright an assumption made by the author will strengthen the support for the conclusion.
(A) There will be fewer candy apple vendors at the fair this year than there were last year.
The fact that there will be fewer candy apple vendors at the fair this year than there were last year does not strengthen the argument because we don’t know how this fact impacts the argument.
What does it mean that there will be fewer candy apple vendors at the fair this year than there were last year? Does that fact increase or decrease our conviction that Camilla will attend?

Does she like fairs with relatively many candy apple vendors or fairs with relatively few candy apple vendors? We don’t know the answer to that question, and thus, we don’t know in what direction this answer choice takes us.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Camila is aware that the fair is coming to town this weekend.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by stating as fact an assumption made by the author.

Notice that, in making her argument, the author assumes that Camila is aware that the fair is coming to town this weekend.After all, if Camila is not aware that the fair is coming to town this weekend, the argument falls apart, because, regardless of the facts that there will be vendors selling candy apples and that Camilla loves candy apples, if Camilla is not aware that the fair is coming to town, then concluding that she will go to the fair does not make sense.
Whenever we can take an assumption made by an author and turn it into a statement of fact, the argument will be stronger.
(C) Some of Camila’s friends have invited her to a concert this weekend.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument.
After all, if some of Camila’s friends have invited her to a concert this weekend, then perhaps she will go with them.In that case, she will not be attending the Lakeland County Fair.
(D) The price of admission to the fair is the same this year as it was last year.
We don’t know what effect this choice has.

It’s tempting to believe that what this choice says means that the price will be low this year, but the truth is that last year the price of admission could have been high or low, and so, the fact that the price will be the same this year as it was last year could mean that the price will be very high.
Thus, whether this choice supports or calls into question the conclusion that Camila will attend is not clear.
(E) Camila is considered an expert in judging candy apples.

That fact that Camila is considered an expert in judging candy apples does not strengthen the argument.After all, a person could be an expert at many things and still not attend events involving those things.

question.text: Global warming is caused by increased levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide, much of which comes from the burning of fossil fuels.Ocean waters in a highly alkaline state are capable of absorbing huge amounts of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thus reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide levels, and the oceans can be made highly alkaline by adding to them powdered olivine rock, which can be mined in large quantities.Therefore, adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans is an effective approach to reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide and thereby combating global warming.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

answers.text: Emissions of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere cannot be adequately reduced by changing methods of producing food for human consumption.
Most country’s politicians would support legislative bills to allow powdered olivine rock to be added to nearby oceans.
The process of mining and powdering rock can be carried out using solar-powered heavy machinery, the use of which does not result in emission of carbon dioxide.
There are no minerals other than olivine rock that could be used for absorbing carbon dioxide without mining or powdering.
Technology that could be used for mining and powdering olivine rock has been available for many years.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Global warming is caused by increased levels of atmospheric carbon dioxide, much of which comes from the burning of fossil fuels.
Premise: Ocean waters in a highly alkaline state are capable of absorbing huge amounts of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thus reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide levels.
Premise: Oceans can be made highly alkaline by adding to them powdered olivine rock, which can be mined in large quantities.
Conclusion: Adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans is an effective approach to reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide and thereby combating global warming.

This argument lays out a plan.Has the author fully considered the details of the plan? Are there considerations that the author may have missed? Does the author provide us with sufficient evidence to justify the plan’s success? What assumptions has the author made? What additional evidence would provide greater certainty that the author’s plan will work?
(A) Emissions of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere cannot be adequately reduced by changing methods of producing food for human consumption.

That carbon dioxide emissions cannot be adequately reduced by changing food production methods does not affect this argument.We are not concerned with other plans for reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide; we are concerned with the effectiveness of the plan laid out in the argument.

In general, it’s important to be wary of answer choices that describe different or better plans.Our job is to strengthen the case for the given plan.Showing that there is not another plan that would work does not strengthen the argument.
(B) Most country’s politicians would support legislative bills to allow powdered olivine rock to be added to nearby oceans.
To avoid choosing a trap choice like this one, we must clearly identify and keep in mind the argument’s conclusion.

The conclusion is that “adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans is an effective approach to reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide and thereby combating global warming.” What this choice says seems to support a related conclusion but not the conclusion we need to support.This choice seems to support a conclusion along the lines of that the approach islikely to be used.The conclusion we need to support is that the approach iseffective.
In general, when an argument’s conclusion is concerned with the effectiveness of an action, a common incorrect choice will be concerned with the likelihood that the action will be taken.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The process of mining and powdering rock can be carried out using solar-powered heavy machinery, the use of which does not result in emission of carbon dioxide.

Let’s look back at the argument’s conclusion: “Adding powdered olivine rock to the oceans is an effective approach toreducing atmospheric carbon dioxide and thereby combating global warming.” The goal of the plan is to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide and combat global warming.

However, what if the described approach itself resulted in the emission of carbon dioxide? In that case, there would be a good chance that that plan’s goal to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide would not be achieved.

Thus, the fact that the process described can be carried out using machinery that does not emit carbon dioxide strengthens the argument (i.e., supports the conclusion that the plan will work) by ruling out what would be a very plausible objection to the argument, or unforeseen problem in the plan.We now have greater conviction that the author’s suggested method of reducing atmospheric carbon dioxide is a viable one.
(D) There are no minerals other than olivine rock that could be used for absorbing carbon dioxide without mining or powdering.

The author never makes the claim that olivine rock is the only mineral that would work.Furthermore, saying that olivine rock is the only mineral that would work does not make this argument any stronger.If the process works, it works regardless of whether olivine rock is the only mineral that will work or whether there are hundreds of others that will work.So, what this answer choice says has no impact on the argument.
Remember, we are not concerned with a better or different plan; we are concerned with the plan laid out in the argument.
(E) Technology that could be used for mining and powdering olivine rock has been available for many years.

What this answer choice says has no impact on the argument.The length of time that the technology has been available does not strengthen or weaken the argument.Either old or new technology could work well or not work well.The technology need not have been around for many years for the plan to be successful.

question.text: Intenzfit, a group exercise program, pushes participants through rigorous workouts that tax their cardiovascular systems.Although many people have achieved dramatic weight loss by following the Intenzfit program, more than a few others have suffered heart attacks during its classes.Some have argued that the intensity of the program’s classes caused these heart attacks and warn even the healthiest of people to avoid the program altogether.However, the program can be safe for almost all participants.Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the author’s final conclusion?

answers.text: Most of the people who suffered heart attacks during Intenzfit classes had undiagnosed, unusual heart problems.
Intenzfit participants tend to be in significantly better shape than the general population.
Intenzfit classes were designed by an award-winning cardiologist.
There are other programs with classes that are more intense than those at Intenzfit.
Many doctors recommend that their patients with heart problems avoid Intenzfit.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Intenzfit pushes participants through rigorous workouts that tax their cardiovascular systems.
Background: More than a few participants have suffered heart attacks during Intenzfit’s classes.
Background: Some people have argued that the intensity of the classes caused these heart attacks and warn even the healthiest of people to avoid the program altogether.
Conclusion: However, the program can be safe for almost all participants.

Notice that those opposed to Intenzfit are drawing a cause-and-effect link between Intenzfit and heart attacks.Anytime an argument claims a cause-and-effect link between variables, we should be skeptical as to whether the link actually exists.The fact that two variables occur together does not provide us with sufficient evidence to conclude that one of the variables causes the other.In Critical Reasoning questions, we must always be on the lookout for errors in cause-and-effect reasoning.

Since we’re looking for an answer that strengthens the conclusion, which is that, despite the fact that people have suffered heart attacks in its classes, the program can be safe for almost all participants, one way we could strengthen the argument is to weaken the cause-and-effect claim that the Intenzfit classes are so intense that they cause heart attacks.A way to weaken that claim would be by finding an alternative cause for the heart attacks.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Most of the people who suffered heart attacks during Intenzfit classes had undiagnosed, unusual heart problems.
What this answer says strengthens the argument by providing a plausible alternative explanation for why people in Intenzfit classes suffered heart attacks.

If many of the people who suffered heart attacks during the classes had undiagnosed, unusual heart problems, then we can’t be so sure that the classes were actually the cause of the heart attacks, right? So, the evidence for the conclusion that the program can be safe for almost all participants is stronger than it was before we had this information.
(B) Intenzfit participants tend to be in significantly better shape than the general population.

If anything, the fact that Intenzfit participants tend to be in significantly better shape than the general population weakens the argument by making the possibility that some other factor, such as obesity or sedentary lifestyle, caused the heart attacks seem even less likely.In other words, if even people who are “in significantly better shape than the general population” are suffering heart attacks during Intenzfit classes, then we have more reason to believe that the conclusion that “the program can be safe for almost all participants” is inaccurate.Of course, we are looking for evidence to strengthen, not weaken, the conclusion.
(C) Intenzfit classes were designed by an award-winning cardiologist.

The best cardiologist in the universe could have designed the classes, and that fact would not mean that the classes are safe.Remember, we are given the premise that more than a few participants have suffered heart attacks during Intenzfit’s classes, and we must take that premise as true.So, regardless of who designed the classes or what that person’s qualifications are, we have no further reason to believe that participants’ safety concerns are unwarranted.

Sadly, human history is filled with the “best of the best” creating products and services that have poor, and sometimes disastrous, results.The results of a program itself is a far more reliable measure than the credentials of the person who created it.
(D) There are other programs with classes that are more intense than those at Intenzfit.

The intensity of other exercise classes does not affect a conclusion about the safety of Intenzfit classes.Note that the author makes no comparisons between Intenzfit and other classes in the argument.What this answer choice says has no impact on this argument, which is centered solely on Intenzfit.
(E) Many doctors recommend that their patients with heart problems avoid Intenzfit.

That many doctors recommend that their patients with heart problems avoid Intenzfit seems to weaken the case for the conclusion rather than strengthen it.After all, why would many doctors advise these patients against participation in Intenzfit classes if the classes posed no health risks to people with heart problems? Of course, we are looking for evidence to strengthen, not weaken, the conclusion.

question.text: While only about 10 percent of the human population is left-handed, in certain professional sports, the proportion of players who are left-handed is much greater than 10 percent.It’s probable that the reason for the greater than normal proportion of left-handed people among those who play certain sports at a professional level is that, since most people who play sports are most accustomed to playing against right handed players, most are relatively unaccustomed to reacting to the moves of left-handed players, a factor giving left-handed players an advantage.Which of the following, if true, most helps to confirm the explanation above?

answers.text: The relative proportions of right-handed and left-handed people among those who play sports at an amateur level tend to match those in the general population.
Greater than normal ratios of left-handed players to right-handed players in professional sports exist both in some team sports and in some sports that are played one on one.
The professional sports in which the ratios of left-handers to right-handers are the greatest are those in which players generally have the least time to react to opposing players’ moves.
In some professional sports, the ratio of left-handed people to right-handed people is two or more times as great as that seen in the general population.
If the owners of professional sports teams were to refuse to hire left-handed players, the owners would have smaller pools of players from which to choose.

explanation_parts.text: Premise: While only about 10 percent of the human population is left-handed, in certain professional sports, the portion of players who are left handed is much greater than 10 percent.

Conclusion: It’s probable that the reason for the greater than normal proportion of left-handed people among those who play certain sports at a professional level is that, since most people who play sports are most accustomed to playing against right handed players, most are relatively unaccustomed to reacting to the moves of left-handed players, and, thus, left handed players have an advantage.

A question like this is a good opportunity to remind ourselves that a conclusion is not factual.Rather, a conclusion is an author’s interpretation of some data.Here, the author states the fact that “While only about 10 percent of the human population is left-handed, in certain professional sports, the portion of players who are left-handed is much greater than 10 percent.” Ok, that’s a fact.But his conclusion is his interpretation of that fact.For all we know, he may be completely correct in his reasoning, or he may be completely wrong.With what’s presented to us, we just don’t know.So, our job in answering this question is to find another fact that will give us more conviction in the author’s conclusion.
(A) The relative proportions of right-handed and left-handed people among those who play sports at an amateur level tend to match those in the general population.

This choice is a tempting pseudo-strengthener, because, by showing that the disproportionate presence of left-handed people that exists in professional sports does not exist in amateur sports, it could seem to confirm that there is something special about left-handed people that results in the disproportionate presence of left-handed people in professional sports.

However, the conclusion for which we are seeking support is not simply that there is something special about left-handed people; it’s specifically that left-handed people have an advantage in sports because people are relatively unaccustomed to reacting to the moves of left-handed players.Since this choice does not indicate that the existence of that specific factor is the reason why there is a disproportionate presence of left-handed people in professional sports, this choice is not the correct answer.
(B) Greater than normal ratios of left-handed players to right-handed players in professional sports exist both in some team sports and in some sports that are played one on one.

The fact that the disproportionate presence of left-handed players in professional sports exists in both team sports and one-on-one sports does not indicate anything about why the left-handed players are disproportionately present.

This choice basically just tells us what we already know, that left-handed players are disproportionately present in professional sports.It just adds the information that they are disproportionately present in two types of sports.
This choice could seem to matter, but it doesn’t really.So, it’s a pseudo-strengthener, rather than the correct answer.

YOUR CHOICE(C) The professional sports in which the ratios of left-handers to right-handers are the greatest are those in which players generally have the least time to react to opposing players’ moves.

This choice tends to confirm the author’s conclusion by showing that the ratio of left-handed players to right-handed players is greatest in the professional sports in which players’ being accustomed to reacting to each other’s moves is most important.
When would being accustomed to reacting to another player’s moves be most important? When you don’t have much time to react.
Here’s why.
If you don’t have much time to react, and an opposing player makes a move you aren’t accustomed to, you may have a hard time reacting.

On the other hand, if you have a lot of time to react, and an opposing player makes a move you aren’t accustomed to, you may be able to handle that move pretty well, because you have plenty of time to react.

So, what makes sense is that, if left-handed players have an advantage because their moves are different from other players’ moves, that advantage would be most pronounced in sports in which players have little time to react.It is in those sports that making a move that another player is not accustomed to makes the most difference.

Well, according to this choice, left-handed players are in fact most common in sports in which players generally have the least time to react to opposing players’ moves.So, this choice provides some confirmation for the author’s theory, by showing that left-handed players are most common in the sports in which they would be most common if the theory were correct.
This choice is our strengthener.
(D) In some professional sports, the ratio of left-handed people to right-handed people is two or more times as great as that seen in the general population.

This choice provides support for the wrong conclusion, the conclusion that the disproportionate presence of left-handed people in professional sports is not random.The fact stated by this choice, that, in some professional sports, the ratio of left-handed people to right-handed people is two or more times as great as that seen in the general population, indicates that it’s rather unlikely that the disproportionate presence of left-handed people in professional sports is the result of random events.

However, since that conclusion is not the conclusion for which we need support, and since the existence of relatively high ratios of left-handed to right-handed players in “some professional sports” does clearly indicate anything about why the higher than normal ratios exist, this choice is not our strengthener.
(E) If the owners of professional sports teams were to refuse to hire left-handed players, the owners would have smaller pools of players from which to choose.
This choice supports the wrong conclusion.It supports, among others, the conclusion that owners of professional sports teams are unlikely to refuse to hire left-handed players.

However, the fact that not hiring left-handed players at all would result in owners’ having smaller pools of players from which to choose does not support any conclusion about why left-handed players are hireddisproportionately.

question.text: Over the past ten years, the number of students who attended Woodbury High School, and then were accepted into top colleges, has been greater than the number of students who attended Thomas High School and were accepted into top colleges.Clearly, then, a student who attended Woodbury High School has a greater likelihood of being accepted into a top college than a student who attended Thomas High School has.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the above argument?

answers.text: The proportion of Woodbury High School students who go to college is greater than the proportion of Thomas High School students who do.
Over the past ten years, the number of students per grade at Woodbury High School has not been greater than the number per grade at Thomas High School.
Parents who graduated from top colleges are much more likely to send their children to Woodbury High School than to Thomas High School.
The alumni of Thomas High School donate far less money to top colleges, after they graduate from these colleges, than the alumni of Woodbury High School donate.
Over the past ten years, there has been a steady decrease in the number of students who attend Thomas High School and are accepted into top colleges.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Over the past ten years, the number of students who attended Woodbury High School, and then were accepted into top colleges, has been greater than the number of students who attended Thomas High School and were accepted into top colleges.
Conclusion: A student who attended Woodbury High School has a greater likelihood of being accepted into a top college than a student who attended Thomas High School has.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The proportion of Woodbury High School students who go to college is greater than the proportion of Thomas High School students who do.
This choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.

The likelihood that a student at a school will be accepted into a top college is, of course, essentially equal to the proportion of students from that school who have been accepted into top colleges.So, if we had the information on the proportion of Woodbury High School Students and the proportion of Thomas High School studentswho have been accepted into top colleges, then we could use that information to support a conclusion about the likelihoods of students from the schools being accepted into top colleges.

However, this choice provides information on the relative proportions of students from the respective schoolswho attend college in general, and that information is useless in determining how likely it is that a student from either high school will be accepted into a top college.

It could be that a student from Woodbury High School is much more likely to attend college than a student from Thomas High School is, but muchlesslikely to be accepted into a top collegespecificallythan a Thomas High School student is.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Over the past ten years, the number of students per grade at Woodbury High School has not been greater than the number per grade at Thomas High School.

Imagine if the opposite of what this choice says were true, and thus the number of students per grade at Woodbury High School WAS significantly greater than the number per grade at Thomas High School.

In that case, the fact that more Woodbury High School students than Thomas High School students had been accepted into top colleges would not indicate that a Woodbury High School student has a greater likelihood of being accepted into a top college than a Thomas High School student has.Rather, students from the two schools could very well be equally likely to be accepted into top colleges, right? If students from the two schools are equally likely to be accepted into top colleges, but there are more students per grade at Woodbury High, then of course more Woodbury students, in terms of raw numbers, would be accepted into top colleges.

However, this choice says that the number of students per grade at Woodbury High has NOT been greater than the number per grade at Thomas High.But, we know from the passage that “the number of students who attended Woodbury High School, and then were accepted into top colleges,hasbeen greater.” Thus, a greaterproportionof Woodbury students have been accepted into top colleges.
So, this information tends to confirm that Woodbury High School students are more likely to be accepted into top colleges than Thomas High School students are.
(C) Parents who graduated from top colleges are much more likely to send their children to Woodbury High School than to Thomas High School.
The fact that parents who graduated from top colleges are much more likely to send their children to Woodbury High School than to Thomas High School does not strengthen the argument.

Even if we had the additional information that all of the children of these parents are accepted into top colleges, we still wouldn’t know the proportions of students at the two high schools who are accepted into top colleges or the likelihood that any given student from either school will be accepted into a top college.
Thus, this choice does not provide new information that affects the support for the conclusion.
(D) The alumni of Thomas High School donate far less money to top colleges, after they graduate from these colleges, than the alumni of Woodbury High School donate.

If you choose this answer, you likely assumed that, if Thomas High School alumni donate far less money to top colleges than Woodbury High School alumni donate, then the students from Woodbury High School have some form of favoritism available to them, as a result of their larger donations, when they apply to top colleges.

However, even if such favoritism exists, that fact would not do what the correct answer has to do: support the conclusion of the argument.That fact would insteadexplainthe conclusion of the argument.It would explainwhystudents who attended Woodbury High School are more likely to be accepted: favoritism exists because of alumni donations.

However, the presumption that such favoritism exists is not really supported by the information that we have.Furthermore, it could be that, even with such favoritism, other factors cause Woodbury High School students to be less likely, rather than more likely, than Thomas High School students to be accepted into top colleges.
So, this choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(E) Over the past ten years, there has been a steady decrease in the number of students who attend Thomas High School and are accepted into top colleges.

Even if the number of Thomas High School students who are accepted into top colleges each year has been decreasing, the likelihood that a student from Thomas High School will be accepted into a top college still could be much higher than the likelihood that a student from Woodbury High School will.
The simplest way to eliminate this choice is to see that the fact that a numerical statistic has been decreasing doesn’t mean that it is lower than another numerical statistic.
This choice does not affect the support for the conclusion.

question.text: Female sea turtles are known to leave the beaches on which they were born, swim thousands of miles into the ocean, mate with males, and, almost miraculously, return to their birthplace beaches to lay their eggs, which will hatch into future generations of sea turtles.Because it is known that every beach has its own unique magnetic signature, scientists have hypothesized that, as babies, female sea turtles memorize the unique magnetic signatures of their natal beaches and later use those unique magnetic impressions to navigate back to those beaches to lay eggs.Each of the following, if true, strengthens the support for scientists’ hypothesis, EXCEPT:

answers.text: During times when Earth’s magnetic field has shifted so that the magnetic signals across beaches have moved closer together, turtle nests on those beaches are more densely packed.
The brains of female sea turtles are known to contain tiny charged particles, which have been shown to be responsive to magnetic fields.
When Earth’s magnetic field lines have moved farther apart, female sea turtles have still been able to navigate from far in the ocean back to their birthplaces.
Sea turtles swimming in the open ocean have been observed to consistently change direction when they arrive at locations marked by differences in magnetic fields.
The fossils of female sea turtles have been found in high concentrations along magnetic lines crossing beaches upon which female sea turtles are known to lay eggs.

explanation_parts.text: The argument tells us that scientists have hypothesized that female sea turtles use magnetic fields to get back to their natal beaches.Notice that, aside from the fact that every beach has its own unique magnetic signature, there is really no substantial evidence to support this hypothesis.Are female sea turtles really navigating via the magnetic signatures of their natal beaches?

Because this question is an EXCEPT question, we’ll see four answer choices that provide further evidence that the sea turtles are in fact using the magnetic signatures to navigate (these answers will be incorrect) and one answer that does not strengthen that hypothesis.That answer will be correct.
(A) During times when Earth’s magnetic field has shifted so that the magnetic signals across beaches have moved closer together, turtle nests on those beaches are more densely packed.

If during times when Earth’s magnetic field has shifted so that the magnetic signals across beaches have moved closer together, turtle nests on those beaches are more densely packed, then we have greater reason to believe that the scientists’ hypothesis is correct.After all, what this answer choice says provides evidence that the locations of turtle nests change in accordance with the movements of Earth’s magnetic field, and thus that female sea turtles are actually using magnetic fields to navigate.So, this information strengthens the argument.
Strengthens? Yes.
(B) The brains of female sea turtles are known to contain tiny charged particles, which have been shown to be responsive to magnetic fields.

If the brains of female sea turtles are known to contain tiny charged particles that are responsive to magnetic fields, then we have greater reason to believe that the scientists’ hypothesis is correct.After all, what this answer choice says provides evidence that there is a mechanism in female sea turtles that may allow them to navigate via magnetic fields.Thus, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
Strengthens? Yes.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) When Earth’s magnetic field lines have moved farther apart, female sea turtles have still been able to navigate from far in the ocean back to their birthplaces.

What this answer choice says tells us nothing that confirms that female sea turtles navigate via magnetic fields.If anything, given that the turtles are still able to navigate back to their birthplaces even when the magnetic fields change, this information seems to indicate that the turtles don't navigate via the magnetic fields.Thus, if what this answer choice says has any effect on the argument, it is a weakening effect, not a strengthening one.
Strengthens? No.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Sea turtles swimming in the open ocean have been observed to consistently change direction when they arrive at locations marked by differences in magnetic fields.

If the turtles consistently change direction when they arrive at locations marked by magnetic field differences, then we have greater reason to believe that the turtles are using the fields for navigation.This correlation does not prove for sure that turtles use magnetic fields for navigation, but it certainly constitutes additional evidence that they do, and thus it strengthens the argument.
Strengthens? Yes.
(E) The fossils of female sea turtles have been found in high concentrations along magnetic lines crossing beaches upon which female sea turtles are known to lay eggs.

If it is true that the fossils of female sea turtles have been found in high concentrations along magnetic lines crossing beaches upon which female sea turtles are known to lay eggs, then we have evidence of an association between the turtles and the magnetic lines, which provides some confirmation that the scientist’s hypothesis is correct.

If this is hard to see, think of the reverse situation, in which the fossils have been found in random locations relative to magnetic lines crossing beaches where female sea turtles lay eggs.Wouldn’t that fact give us less conviction in the hypothesis?

So, if the fossilsarefound along magnetic lines crossing beaches where female sea turtles lay eggs, we have greater reason to believe that the turtles used those magnetic lines for navigation.Thus, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.

question.text: Chefs know that one way to induce green tomatoes to ripen faster than they would on their own in open air is to place the green tomatoes in a sealed container with ripening bananas.In their ripening process, bananas release a gas called ethylene.Therefore, it must be that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument?

answers.text: When green tomatoes are placed in a sealed container with cherries, which do not ripen once picked, the tomatoes do not ripen significantly faster than they do in open air.
When a number of green bananas are left in open air, they will ripen significantly more slowly than they will when in a sealed container with a number of green tomatoes.
When left in open air, green tomatoes take considerably longer to ripen than they do when they are placed in a sealed container with certain other, non-banana, fruits.
Ethylene can be found in higher than normal concentrations around many different species of fruit trees, such as apple trees, orange trees, and pear trees.
Even ripe bananas continue to release large amounts of ethylene.

explanation_parts.text: The author is making a cause-and-effect claim, that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.Her evidence is that, if placed in a sealed container with ripening bananas, which release ethylene gas in their ripening process, green tomatoes ripen faster than they would on their own in open air.Our job is to find evidence that strengthens that cause-effect link.
YOUR CHOICE(A) When green tomatoes are placed in a sealed container with cherries, which do not ripen once picked, the tomatoes do not ripen significantly faster than they do in open air.

We know that one way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument is to show that when the cause is removed, so too is the effect.The argument’s conclusion is that it must be true that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.This answer choice essentially says that, when the ethylene gas (the cause) is removed, so too is the effect: green tomatoes no longer ripen faster than they would on their own.

Now, how do we know that the ethylene gas is not present when cherries instead of bananas are in the container with the tomatoes? Well, we don’t know for sure.However, what we do know from the passage is that ethylene gas is present when bananas are in the container because bananas release ethylene “in their ripening process.”

Notice that this choice says that cherries “do not ripen once picked.” In other words, the cherries in the container, which have been picked,would not be ripening.So, if bananas release ethylene when ripening, but cherries would not be ripening, it seems likely that the cherries would not be releasing ethylene.

So, it is likely that, when cherries instead of bananas are in the container, ethylene is not present, and so the tomatoes don’t ripen as quickly.The contrast between that situation and the situation in which bananas are in the container provides some confirmation that it is indeed ethylene that causes tomatoes to ripen.
(B) When a number of green bananas are left in open air, they will ripen significantly more slowly than they will when in a sealed container with a number of green tomatoes.
What this choice says strengthens the support for the wrong conclusion.

The argument’s conclusion is that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.Bananas are used as an example to support the conclusion because bananas release ethylene.In other words, the argument makes a claim about how bananas (via the release of ethylene) affect tomatoes, whereas this answer choice provides information that seems to support a claim about how tomatoes affect bananas.

When we seek to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument, it’s important to not mix up which variable represents the proposed cause and which variable represents the observed effect.Otherwise, we run the risk of strengthening the wrong conclusion.
(C) When left in open air, green tomatoes take considerably longer to ripen than they do when they are placed in a sealed container with certain other, non-banana, fruits.
The argument’s conclusion is that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.Bananas are used as an example because they release ethylene.

Do the non-banana fruits mentioned in this choice release ethylene? Perhaps.If they do release ethylene, by showing that tomatoes ripen faster than otherwise when these fruits are present, this choice would strengthen the support for the conclusion that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.
However, if these “certain other, non-banana, fruits” do not release ethylene, then maybe there is something else about fruits that makes green tomatoes ripen faster.
Because this answer choice doesn’t indicate whether the other fruits release ethylene, we don’t have information that we can use to make any determination about the effect of ethylene.
(D) Ethylene can be found in higher than normal concentrations around many different species of fruit trees, such as apple trees, orange trees, and pear trees.

The fact that ethylene can be found in higher concentrations around many different species of fruit trees, such as apple trees, orange trees, and pear trees, doesn’t tell us anything about ethylene’s effect on green tomatoes.We must remember that we are seeking to strengthen a cause-and-effect link.

The information in this answer choice seems to indicate that many different fruits are somehow associated with ethylene.Perhaps the fruit on these tress produces ethylene.Perhaps ethylene is somehow attracted to the trees.We don’t know enough to know for sure why the ethylene is found in high concentrations “around” these trees, or whether the ethylene is causing the fruit on these trees to ripen faster.Thus, this information does nothing to help us confirm the cause-and-effect link proposed by the author.
(E) Even ripe bananas continue to release large amounts of ethylene.
What this answer choice says has no effect on the argument.
The argument’s conclusion is that ethylene causes green tomatoes to ripen.In other words, we have a cause-and-effect claim.Ripening bananas are mentioned because ripening bananas release ethylene.

The fact that bananas release ethylene even at times when they are not ripening does not somehow change the fact that ripening bananas release ethylene and does not strengthen the link between the presence of ethylene and the ripening of tomatoes.

question.text: Long ago, before they slithered to move from place to place, snakes used legs similar to those used by lizards today.However, about 100 million years ago, snakes evolved to have no legs.Since legs would inhibit snakes from burrowing into the ground to hunt and to hide from predators, it is likely that snakes evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the support for the conclusion stated above?

answers.text: Fossil evidence indicates that, around 100 million years ago, dinosaur eggs that were sitting unprotected above ground were at risk of being eaten by snakes.
Some snakes alive today have hatched from their eggs with four legs similar to those used by lizards.
Snakes without legs are able to move more quickly through water than they would be able to if they had legs.
Fossils of snakes that lived almost 100 million years ago reveal that the skulls of snakes living at that time were particularly well adapted for burrowing into the ground.
Other species of animals also evolved to have no legs around 100 million years ago.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Long ago, before they slithered to move from place to place, snakes used legs similar to those used by lizards today.However, about 100 million years ago, snakes evolved to have no legs.
Premise: Legs would inhibit snakes from burrowing into the ground to hunt and to hide from predators.
Conclusion: It is likely that snakes evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.

Notice that the author has provided us with limited evidence to support his claim.Therefore, as we go to the answer choices, we’ll be seeking an additional piece of evidence that would give us more faith that snakes evolved to become legless to be better able to burrow into the ground.
As always, it will be important for us to keep the author’s conclusion in mind as we evaluate the answer choices.
(A) Fossil evidence indicates that, around 100 million years ago, dinosaur eggs that were sitting unprotected above ground were at risk of being eaten by snakes.

This choice could be tempting because it seems to indicate that it was dangerous to be above ground 100 million years ago.So, we might be tempted to believe that this choice somehow strengthens the support for the case that snakes evolved to have no legs in order to be able to burrow into the ground to hide from predators.

However, notice that the danger to dinosaur eggs that were sitting above ground came from snakes.So, really all this choice tells us is that, around 100 million years ago, snakes spent some time above ground.

Of course, the fact that the snakes spent time above ground would not strengthen the support for the conclusion that snakes evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.
(B) Some snakes alive today have hatched from their eggs with four legs similar to those used by lizards.
The fact that some snakes alive today have hatched from their eggs with four legs similar to those used by lizards indicates that sometimes anomalies occur in snake reproduction.
However, the fact that snakes are sometimes born with legs does not support the argument’s conclusion about why snakes evolved to have no legs.
(C) Snakes without legs are able to move more quickly through water than they would be able to if they had legs.
What this choice says weakens the argument.

The author’s conclusion is that it is likely that snakes evolved to become legless to be better able to burrow into the ground.The fact that snakes without legs are able to move more quickly through water than they would be able to if they had legs provides us with an alternative explanation for why snakes became legless.Maybe they became legless in order to move more quickly through water than they would be able to if they had legs.
So, given what this answer choice says, we are less sure that snakes evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Fossils of snakes that lived almost 100 million years ago reveal that the skulls of snakes living at that time were particularly well adapted for burrowing into the ground.

If fossils of snakes that lived almost 100 million years ago reveal that the skulls of snakes living at that time were particularly well adapted for burrowing into the ground, then we have more faith in the argument.

Notice that this answer choice provides us with a piece of “hard evidence.” After all, if skulls of these snakes were particularly well adapted for burrowing into the ground, then it is likely that these snakes spent a lot of time burrowing into the ground.If it’s likely that the snakes spent a lot of time burrowing into the ground, then it’s also likely that snakes evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.After, all if burrowing into the ground is what they were doing, then it stands to reason that they evolved the way they did to be better able to burrow.

If this reasoning hard to see, consider the reverse situation, in which the fossils of snakes that lived almost 100 million years ago reveal that the skulls of snakes living at that time WERE NOT well adapted for burrowing into the ground.This information would weaken the argument by indicating that the snakes probably did not burrow into the ground and, thus, indicating that likely they would not have evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.After all, why would they have evolved to become better at something they didn’t do?

So then, the fact that the skulls of snakes living at that time were particularly well adapted for burrowing into the ground gives us more conviction that it is likely that snakes evolved to become legless in order to be better able to burrow into the ground.
(E) Other species of animals also evolved to have no legs around 100 million years ago.
The fact that other species of animals also evolved to have no legs around 100 million years ago does not strengthen the argument.
The passage already tells us that about 100 million years ago, snakes evolved to have no legs.Now, we need evidence supporting the author’s conclusion aboutwhysnakes evolved to become legless.
The fact that other animals also evolved to have no legs does not support any specific conclusion regarding whysnakes or other animals evolved to become legless.

question.text: Regular smokers tend to regulate their daily intakes of nicotine to keep those intakes consistent.For example, when researchers had regular smokers smoke only cigarettes with nicotine levels below those of normal cigarettes, the smokers tended to increase the number of cigarettes that they smoked each day, thus making up for the reduction in nicotine per cigarette.Nevertheless, experts maintain that, if cigarette companies sufficiently reduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes, people will reduce the number of cigarettes that they smoke per day.Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the experts’ claim?

answers.text: It is the tar and other more toxic components of cigarettes, rather than nicotine, that do the most to cause cancer and other smoking-related health problems.
When the level of nicotine in cigarettes is below a certain threshold, the disadvantages of smoking tend to outweigh any satisfaction gained from it.
In many countries that are large markets for cigarettes, it is illegal to place on packs of cigarettes labels indicating how much nicotine the cigarettes contain.
When smoking cigarettes containing relatively low amounts of nicotine, smokers often puff heavily to boost the amount of nicotine that they get from each cigarette.
Cigarette companies’ marketing messages implying that smoking low-nicotine cigarettes is less damaging to health than smoking normal cigarettes are misleading.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Regular smokers tend to regulate their daily intakes of nicotine to keep those intakes consistent.

Background: For example, when researchers had regular smokers smoke only cigarettes with nicotine levels below those of normal cigarettes, the smokers tended to increase the number of cigarettes that they smoked each day, thus making up for the reduction in nicotine per cigarette.

Conclusion: Nevertheless, experts maintain that, if cigarette companies sufficiently reduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes, people will reduce the number of cigarettes that they smoke per day.
(A) It is the tar and other more toxic components of cigarettes, rather than nicotine, that do the most to cause cancer and other smoking-related health problems.

Remember, the conclusion is that “if cigarette companies sufficiently reduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes, people will reduce the number of cigarettes that they smoke per day.” The conclusion does not relate to the reduction of smoking-related health problems or how the effects of nicotine compare to the effects of other substances in cigarettes.Therefore, which substances cause the most damage when a person smokes is not relevant, and this information does nothing to support the experts’ claim.
YOUR CHOICE(B) When the level of nicotine in cigarettes is below a certain threshold, the disadvantages of smoking tend to outweigh any satisfaction gained from it.

The argument claims that “if cigarette companies sufficiently reduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes, people will reduce the number of cigarettes that they smoke per day.” Without the fact provided by this answer choice – that, when the nicotine level is below a certain threshold, the disadvantages of smoking tend to outweigh any satisfaction gained from it – we really do not have any factual evidence to support the conclusion that people will reduce the number of cigarettes they smoke.To the contrary, the evidence presented in the argument seems to suggest that reducing nicotine levels results inincreases, rather than decreases, in the number of cigarettes that people smoke.

However, even if smokersincreasetheir daily intake of cigarettes when they “smoke only cigarettes with nicotine levelsbelow those of normal cigarettes,” this answer choice tells us that there is in fact “a certain threshold” that represents a nicotine level low enough to result in adecreasein the number of cigarettes people smoke.
So, it follows that the “nicotine levels below those of normal cigarettes” must have been somewhat lower than normal, but not low enough for the disadvantages to outweigh the satisfaction gained.

As the conclusion states, cigarette companies must “sufficientlyreduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes” in order for people to reduce the number of cigarettes they smoke.This answer choice supports the experts’ claim by providing supportive evidence that there is a threshold the attainment of which will be sufficient to result in that reduction.
(C) In many countries that are large markets for cigarettes, it is illegal to place on packs of cigarettes labels indicating how much nicotine the cigarettes contain.

The conclusion of the argument is that, if cigarette companies sufficiently reduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes, people will reduce the number of cigarettes that they smoke per day.We’re also told that “regular smokers tend to regulate their daily intakes of nicotine to keep those intakes consistent.”

The fact is, however, that smokers do not need to know how much nicotine is in a cigarette to know how much nicotine they need to feel satisfied.Thus, this information about the illegality of indicating nicotine amounts on the labels of cigarette packs has no effect on the conclusion.

Perhaps there could be some psychological effect of smokers’ knowing how much nicotine is in their cigarettes; however, even in that case, we have no way to know whether that effect would result in increased or decreased smoking, so this answer choice still is not a strengthener.
(D) When smoking cigarettes containing relatively low amounts of nicotine, smokers often puff heavily to boost the amount of nicotine that they get from each cigarette.

We can’t strengthen an argument by reiterating a fact that has already been stated.Remember that statements in the passage are considered factual, and note the first sentence of the argument: “Regular smokers tend to regulate their daily intakes of nicotine to keep those intakes consistent.”

In other words, what this answer choice says, that “when smoking cigarettes containing relatively low amounts of nicotine, smokers often puff heavily to boost the amount of nicotine that they get from each cigarette,” essentially tells us something that we already know.However they do it, whether by puffing heavily or by some other method, smokers regulate their daily nicotine intakes.

The fact that smokers puff heavily when they smoke cigarettes low in nicotine does not help to support the conclusion that smokers will take a different action, reducing the number of cigarettes that they smoke, if nicotine levels in cigarettes are sufficiently low.
(E) Cigarette companies’ marketing messages implying that smoking low-nicotine cigarettes is less damaging to health than smoking normal cigarettes are misleading.

What this answer choice says could seem to imply that the cigarette companies’ marketing messages would mislead people to actually smokemore.In other words, if smokers believe that low-nicotine cigarettes are less harmful than normal cigarettes, then perhaps smokers would feel that they have license to smoke more cigarettes each day than they normally would, as long as those cigarettes are low-nicotine.In that case, what this answer choice says could actually weaken the argument’s conclusion is that “if cigarette companies sufficiently reduce the levels of nicotine in their cigarettes, people will reduce the number of cigarettes that they smoke per day.”

Of course, we do not really have any evidence of what the impact of the companies’ marketing messages is on smokers’ behavior, so the misleading nature of the companies’ marketing doesn’t have any clear relevance to the argument.One thing we can say is that this information certainly does nothing tostrengthenthe experts’ claim.

question.text: Since a single sparrow is capable of consuming more than four kilograms of crop seeds per year, a company has developed a robotic scarecrow to be used to keep sparrows away from fields in which crops are grown.However, ecologists maintain that widespread use of the scarecrow would not result in an increase in crop yields, even though the scarecrow has been shown to be nearly 100 percent effective in keeping sparrows out of fields in which the scarecrow is placed.Which of the following, if true, does the most to confirm the ecologists’ claim?

answers.text: Because of habitat destruction and toxins present in the environment, sparrow populations have declined significantly since the time when development of the robotic scarecrow was begun.
In addition to eating seeds, sparrows eat insects, such as locusts, that inhabit crop fields and consume plants growing there.
As currently produced, the robotic scarecrow costs more to own and operate than farmers would gain via using the scarecrow to increase crop yields.
Hawks, which often prey on sparrows, will over time reduce their hunting for prey in an area in which the presence of prey has been greatly reduced.
Whereas sparrows, even where common, typically consume well under 8 percent of crops, use of certain farming practices can result in crop yield increases of 40 percent or more.

explanation_parts.text: The author mentions, but does not provide support for, the following claim by ecologists:
Conclusion: Widespread use of the scarecrow would not result in an increase in crop yields.

The correct answer will provide a reason to believe that, even though the scarecrow has been shown to be nearly 100-percent effective in keeping sparrows out of fields in which the scarecrow is placed, the ecologists’ claim is valid.
(A) Because of habitat destruction and toxins present in the environment, sparrow populations have declined significantly since the time when development of the robotic scarecrow was begun.

To avoid choosing this pseudo-strengthening choice, we have to notice that, even if sparrow populations have declined, sparrows presumably still exist and could consume crop seeds.So, the fact that sparrow populations have declined does not really provide any support for the conclusion that widespread use of the robotic scarecrow would not result in any increase in crop yields, because as long assomesparrows are scared away from crops by the scarecrow, yields should increase.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In addition to eating seeds, sparrows eat insects, such as locusts, that inhabit crop fields and consume plants growing there.

Common sense tells us that, if sparrows eat insects that eat crops, then keeping sparrows out of fields will result in an increase in crop-eating insects in those fields, and that increase will in turn have a deleterious effect on crop yields.

So, this choice supports the conclusion that, even though the robotic scarecrow keeps virtually all sparrows out of crop fields, use of the scarecrow wouldn’t result in increased crop yields, because the gains in yields resulting from scaring away sparrows could very well be offset by decreases in yields resulting from increases in populations of insects that consume crops.
(C) As currently produced, the robotic scarecrow costs more to own and operate than farmers would gain via using the scarecrow to increase crop yields.
This choice provides support for the wrong conclusion, the conclusion that the scarecrow will not be used because the cost of using it is greater than what farmers would gain from using it.

Notice that the conclusion that we need to support is not about whether the scarecrow will be used.It is about the results of “widespread use of the scarecrow.” In other words, the conclusion is about what will occur in the event that the scarecrowisused.
(D) Hawks, which often prey on sparrows, will over time reduce their hunting for prey in an area in which the presence of prey has been greatly reduced.

We could be tempted into choosing this choice if we were to come up with a convoluted story that, if hawks prey on sparrows, and if use of the scarecrow reduces the presence of sparrows, then hawks will stop hunting in areas where the scarecrow is used, leaving sparrows free to consume crops.By that circular logic, we might determine that this choice supports the conclusion that use of the scarecrow wouldn’t result in increased crop yields.

Of course, that reasoning misses a key point: the reason why hawks would stop hunting in an area is that there are no sparrows to hunt there.The fact that hawks would stop hunting in that area would make little to no difference in the number of sparrows eating crop seeds in that area, because the scarecrows would still be there to scare the sparrows away.
So, this choice does not add any support for the conclusion that keeping virtually 100 percent of sparrows out of crop fields won’t result in increased crop yields.
(E) Whereas sparrows, even where common, typically consume well under 8 percent of crops, use of certain farming practices can result in crop yield increases of 40 percent or more.

To avoid choosing this pseudo-strengthener, we have to notice that, even if crop yields are boosted by 40 percent of more, by consuming crop seeds, sparrows could cause crop yields to be lower than they would be were the sparrows not present.

As high as crop yields may be as a result of use of farming practices, yields could increase even more if use of the scarecrow results in a decrease in the presence of sparrows.So, the fact that effective use of certain farming practices can result in crop yield increases of 40 percent or more does not provide any support for the conclusion that widespread use of the robotic scarecrow would not result inanyincrease in crop yields.

question.text: 90 percent of all quinoa is grown in Bolivia and Peru on small farms with limited production capabilities.Because of increased demand, quinoa prices have more than doubled, making quinoa too expensive for Bolivians and Peruvians to purchase, an issue raising practical and ethical concerns.To address this issue, agro-researchers are considering producing a new, quinoa-like grain that could be cultivated in Bolivia and Peru and sold globally to help satisfy demand for quinoa.Each of the following, if true, adds support to the prediction that the plan described will succeed, EXCEPT:

answers.text: The new, quinoa-like grain would be similar to quinoa in taste and consistency.
It would be no more expensive to cultivate the new, quinoa-like grain than to cultivate quinoa.
Farmers outside of Bolivia and Peru would not begin cultivating and selling the new, quinoa-like grain in mass quantities.
Global consumers would be willing to purchase the new, quinoa-like grain.
The new, quinoa-like grain could be produced through the use of agricultural land not currently used for the production of quinoa.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Because of increased demand, quinoa prices have more than doubled, making quinoa too expensive for Bolivians and Peruvians to purchase, an issue raising practical and ethical concerns.
Premise: 90 percent of all quinoa is grown in Bolivia and Peru on small farms with limited production capabilities.

Conclusion(the plan): To address this issue, agro-researchers are considering producing a new, quinoa-like grain that could be cultivated in Bolivia and Peru and sold globally to help satisfy demand for quinoa.

The argument presents a plan to help meet global demand for quinoa.Anytime a speaker presents a plan, the speaker is making the assumption that the plan will work as expected and that there will not be unforeseen variables or situations that render the plan ineffective.

Because this is a “strengthen EXCEPT” question, our job is to find four answer choices that strengthen the support for the conclusion that the plan will succeed.These answers will be incorrect.The correct answer will not strengthen the argument.
Remember that “not strengthen” does not mean “weaken.” Rather, “not strengthen” means “have no effect or weaken.”
(A) The new, quinoa-like grain would be similar to quinoa in taste and consistency.

If the new, quinoa-like grain would be similar to quinoa in taste and consistency, then we have more reason to believe that consumers will accept the new grain as a substitute for quinoa.Thus, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.

Imagine if the new grain had the same consistency as quinoa but a completely different taste? Then, consumers might be much less likely to accept the new grain as a substitute for quinoa, and the plan would be more likely to fail.So, with the information this answer choice presents, we have greater conviction that the plan will work as expected.
(B) It would be no more expensive to cultivate the new, quinoa-like grain than to cultivate quinoa.

If cultivating the new grain would be no more expensive than cultivating quinoa, then we have more reason to believe that farmers would actually grow the new grain and that the new grain would turn out to be a viable quinoa substitute for consumers.Thus, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.

Imagine if the new grain were far more expensive to grow? In that case, farmers would make normal profits only if they were to charge much more for the new grain than is charged for quinoa.As a result, either the price of the new grain would be much higher than that of quinoa or farmers would be unlikely to grow the new grain.So, if the new grain were far more expensive to grow, the plan would be more likely to fail.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Farmers outside of Bolivia and Peru would not begin cultivating and selling the new, quinoa-like grain in mass quantities.

It’s important to rememberwhyagro-researchers have formulated their plan; the purpose of the plan is to make quinoa more affordable for Bolivians and Peruvians to purchase.Agro-researchers believe that they can accomplish this via the production of a new, quinoa-like grain that would help satisfy global demand for quinoa.So, regardless of where farmers are located, any farmers cultivating and selling the new grain in mass quantities should help satisfy global demand, thus bringing down the cost of quinoa for Bolivians and Peruvians (and, presumably, for all people).

On the other hand, if the opposite is true, that “farmers outside of Bolivia and Peru wouldnotbegin cultivating and selling the new, quinoa-like grain in mass quantities,” as this answer choice says, although that fact may not weaken the argument, it certainly does nothing to strengthen the argument.Obviously, farmers’ not growing a crop would not help to satisfy demand.
Remember, looking for an answer that is not a strengthener does not mean we need a weakener.
(D) Global consumers would be willing to purchase the new, quinoa-like grain.

For the plan to work, global consumers must be willing to purchase the new, quinoa-like grain.Thus, by stating as fact that consumers would be willing to do so, this answer choice strengthens the argument.
(E) The new, quinoa-like grain could be produced through the use of agricultural land not currently used for the production of quinoa.

Imagine if the quinoa-like grain could be produced only through the use of agricultural land currently used for the production of quinoa.This fact would create a major problem for the success of the plan, right? Thus, the fact that the quinoa-like grain could be grown on land not currently used for quinoa production strengthens the argument.

question.text: The management of a home energy efficiency and generation company contemplated selling the company’s barely profitable replacement window manufacturing and installation division in order to focus on areas of the business that are significantly more profitable for the company than replacement window manufacturing and installation.However, management decided not to sell that division because, in interacting with customers interested in window replacement, the company has opportunities to pitch those customers on the purchase of other, more profitable, products and services sold by the company.Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the case for the company’s continuing to operate the replacement window division?

answers.text: In many cases, the energy costs savings a homeowner achieves via replacing windows with energy efficient windows relatively quickly cover the entire cost of the replacement windows.
Often, people who decide to replace a house’s windows are interested in having done other work that changes the house’s appearance, such as replacement of kitchen décor or repainting.
Most of the houses such that it would make sense to replace their windows are older houses with many energy related issues.
Replacing all of the windows in a house can be a rather expensive project.
Customers initially interested in purchasing products and services other than replacement windows from the company often end up purchasing replacement windows as well.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: A home energy efficiency and generation company’s replacement window manufacturing and installation division is barely profitable.

Premise: In interacting with customers interested in window replacement, the company has opportunities to pitch those customers on the purchase of other, more profitable, products and services sold by the company.
Conclusion: Management decided not to sell (to continue operating) that division.

The passage has already provided some support for management’s decision not to sell, i.e., to continue operating, the replacement window division: salespeople can pitch people interested in replacement windows on purchasing other products and services from the company.
To correctly answer the question, we have to add to the case for the company’s continuing to operate that division.
(A) In many cases, the energy costs savings a homeowner achieves via replacing windows with energy efficient windows relatively quickly cover the entire cost of the replacement windows.
This choice supports the wrong conclusion.It supports the conclusion that purchasing replacement windows is cost effective for homeowners.

The conclusion that we have to support is that it makes sense for the company to continue to operate the replacement window division, and the fact that replacing windows is cost effective for customers doesn’t change the fact that, for the company the replacement window division is unprofitable.
(B) Often, people who decide to replace a house’s windows are interested in having done other work that changes the house’s appearance, such as replacement of kitchen décor or repainting.
In order to avoid being tempted into choosing this incorrect answer, we have to pay attention to some key details.

In reading this choice, we may get the impression that operating the replacement window division could be profitable for the company because the company could end up doing for customers interested in replacing windows other work such as replacement of kitchen décor or repainting.

Notice, however, that the type of work that this choice mentions, work that changes the house’s appearance, is not the type of work that the company does.The company is a “home energy efficiency and generation company.” So, there’s no reason to believe that the company would, for instance, replace kitchen décor.

Therefore, what this choice says has no effect on the case forthis company’soperating a replacement window division, because there’s no reason to believe that this company would do that type of work and, thus, profit from doing so.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Most of the houses such that it would make sense to replace their windows are older houses with many energy related issues.
This choice adds to the case for the company’s continuing to operate the replacement window division by providing further confirmation that doing so would be profitable.

Notice the passage says that, “in interacting with customers interested in window replacement, the company has opportunities to pitch those customers on the purchase of other, more profitable, products and services sold by the company.” OK, great, but will the customers who are “pitched” purchase other products and services from that company?
The information provided by this choice indicates that the customers are likely to do so.

If most of the houses such that it would make sense to replace their windows are older houses with many energy related issues, then customers who are interested in replacement windows are likely to own houses that havemany energy related issues.It’s quite likely that these are just the right people to whom to pitch the company’s profitable home energy related products and services.
Thus, this choice adds to the case for the company’s continuing to operate the replacement window division.
(D) Replacing all of the windows in a house can be a rather expensive project.
This choice could be tempting because what it says about replacing windows being “an expensive project” could be taken as indicating that the company would make a lot of money replacing windows.
Notice, however, that the passage describes the replacement window division as “barely profitable.”
