The fact that replacing windows can be expensive does not change the fact that, for the company, replacing windows is barely profitable.So, this choice has no effect on the case for the company’s continuing to operate the replacement window division.
(E) Customers initially interested in purchasing products and services other than replacement windows from the company often end up purchasing replacement windows as well.

The passage indicates that replacing windows is “barely profitable” for the company, and if we think for a moment, we realize that the fact of the matter is that the replacement window division is “barely profitable”even though what this choice says is true.
Really, all this choice does is provide information on how that barely profitable division gets business.
So, choice does not affect the case for continuing to operate the replacement window division.

question.text: Among the participants in a study of people over the age of 70, those who spent ten hours doing computer-based speed of processing training were less likely to experience significant cognitive decline ten years after they did the training than were those participants who did not do the training.However, it seems unlikely that a mere ten hours of speed of processing training could result in a reduced likelihood of cognitive decline ten years later.Which of the following, if true, would provide the most support for the conclusion that a person’s doing ten hours of speed of processing training could result in that person’s not experiencing cognitive decline ten years later?

answers.text: In another study, participants who took reasoning and memory classes were just as likely to experience significant cognitive decline as were those who did not take the classes.
Virtually everyone who goes through speed of processing training experiences measurable improvement in certain cognitive skills.
Doing computer-based speed of processing training is similar in some ways to playing video games, an activity that is engaged in mostly by younger people.
Older people who have done speed of processing training are more likely than those who have not to be able to continue driving, an activity that stimulates healthy brain function.
Ten years after being coached to optimize diet to preserve cognition, people who had received such coaching were less likely than other people to experience significant cognitive decline.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Among the participants in a study of people over the age of 70, those who spent ten hours doing computer-based speed of processing training were less likely to experience significant cognitive decline ten years after they did the training than were those participants who did not do the training.

Conclusion (which does NOT follow from the premise): However, it seems unlikely that a mere 10 hours of speed of processing training could result in a reduced likelihood of cognitive decline 10 years later.
To correctly answer this question, we need to be careful to seek support for the correct conclusion.

Notice that the argument’s conclusion is that “it seems unlikely that a mere ten hours of speed of processing training could result in a reduced likelihood of cognitive decline ten years later.” However, the question asks, “Which of the following, if true, would provide the most support to the conclusionthat a person’s doing ten hours of speed of processing training could result in that person’s not experiencing cognitive decline ten years later?”
So, we see that we’re being asked to find support for a counterpoint to the argument’s conclusion.
(A) In another study, participants who took reasoning and memory classes were just as likely to experience significant cognitive decline as were those who did not take the classes.

As always, we must be cognizant of the small details provided in the passage, which talks about speed of processing training.Notice that this answer choice discusses reasoning and memory classes.Reasoning and memory classes are not the same thing as speed of processing training, and if they are the same, we have no way to know that, so we can safely assume that they are not the same.So, what this answer choice says does not have any effect on the conclusion that we are seeking to support.
(B) Virtually everyone who goes through speed of processing training experiences measurable improvement in certain cognitive skills.

Notice that the argument discusses decreased likelihood of experiencing “significant cognitive declineten years later.” There is a difference between experiencing “measurable improvement in certain cognitive skills” and being less likely to experience significant cognitive decline ten years later.

Since improving cognitive skills in the short term is a result much different from and not clearly connected to preventing long term cognitive decline, this answer choice says has no clear effect on the conclusion we’re seeking to support.
(C) Doing computer-based speed of processing training is similar in some ways to playing video games, an activity that is engaged in mostly by younger people.

The fact that doing computer-based speed of process training is similar in some ways to playing video games, an activity that is engaged in mostly by younger people, does not mean that doing the training will make the people who do the training more like younger people, and thus, perhaps less likely to experience significant cognitive decline ten years later.

Further, this choice does not indicate that playing video games helps prevent cognitive decline, and even if playing video games did have that effect, being “similar in some ways” could mean just about anything.Thus, this answer choice does not support the conclusion in question.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Older people who have done speed of processing training are more likely than those who have not to be able to continue driving, an activity that stimulates healthy brain function.

If older people who have done speed of processing training are more likely than those who have not to be able to continue driving, an activity thatstimulates healthy brain function, then we have a reason to believe that a person’s doing speed of processing training does in fact result in that person’s not experiencing cognitive decline ten years later.

It may be that the speed of processing training does not have direct effects ten years later.Instead, the speed of processing training enables people to continue driving, and then the driving has effects ten years later, by stimulating healthy brain function.In that case, by enabling people to continue driving, speed of processing training indirectly prevents cognitive decline.
Notice that this information gives us greater conviction that the conclusion that we have to support is valid.
(E) Ten years after being coached to optimize diet to preserve cognition, people who had received such coaching were less likely than other people to experience significant cognitive decline.
There is a big difference between speed of processing training and being coached to optimize diet to preserve cognition.

The fact that taking an action, optimizing diet, seems to prevent cognitive decline may indicate that cognitive decline is preventable.However, that fact does virtually nothing to confirm that taking a much different action, going through speed of processing training, is an effective method of preventing cognitive decline.
So, what this answer choice says has little to no effect on the conclusion we’re seeking to support.

question.text: The Town of Doveport is considering charging motorists a fee of seven dollars per automobile, in each direction, to cross the Doveport Bridge, which connects the city’s downtown, where almost all citizens of Doveport work, with its residential areas, where almost all citizens of Doveport live.In recent years, there has been heavy automobile congestion on the bridge during the morning and evening commutes.The town believes that the fee would help alleviate automobile congestion on the bridge by motivating drivers to seek alternative ways to commute.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the support for the town’s conclusion?

answers.text: The Doveport Bridge was constructed during a time when there were far fewer automobiles using the roadways in Doveport than there are now.
Making a round trip on the subway that seamlessly connects downtown Doveport with the town’s residential areas costs three dollars.
Much of the revenue generated via the collection of the fee would be used for maintaining and improving the Doveport Bridge and the roads directly connected to the bridge.
Creating carpool lanes on the bridge would not result in a reduction in traffic congestion significantly greater than any that could conceivably result from the imposition of the fee.
Once the bridge fee goes into effect, motorists commuting over the Doveport Bridge in the morning and evening will not begin to carpool and rideshare.

explanation_parts.text: The Town of Doveport has a plan to motivate drivers to seek alternative ways to commute by charging motorists a fee of seven dollars per automobile to cross the Doveport Bridge in either direction.Why is the town doing this? Well, because in recent years, there has been heavy automobile congestion on the bridge during the morning and evening commutes, congestion that the town hopes to alleviate via the fee.

Notice that, in making this argument, the town assumes that the seven-dollar fee will be sufficient to motivate drivers to seek alternative ways to commute.Is this a sound assumption? With only the information presented in the passage, we can’t say for sure.
(A) The Doveport Bridge was constructed during a time when there were far fewer automobiles using the roadways in Doveport than there are now.

What this answer choice says, that “the Doveport Bridge was constructed during a time when there were far fewer automobiles using the roadways in Doveport than there are now,” does not allow us to conclude that the bridge was not designed to hold the number of cars that are now on the bridge.For all we know, the bridge could have been created with the future in mind.

Furthermore, even if you made the unwarranted assumption that there are too many cars on the bridge now, it is important to see that the passage already states as fact that “in recent years, there has been heavy automobile congestion on the bridge during the morning and evening commutes.” In other words, we already know that the bridge is congested, so further evidence to support that fact does not strengthen the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Making a round trip on the subway that seamlessly connects downtown Doveport with the town’s residential areas costs three dollars.

The passage tells us that Doveport is considering charging motorists a fee of seven dollars per automobile, in each direction, to cross the Doveport Bridge.Why is the town considering the implementation of the fee? The town believes that the fee would help alleviate automobile congestion on the bridge by motivating drivers to seek alternative ways to commute.However, given only what is stated in the passage, it’s hard to know whether this fee would actually motivate drivers to seek alternative ways to commute, right? Is seven dollars each way sufficient motivation?

What this answer choice says, that making a round trip on the subway that seamlessly connects downtown Doveport with the town’s residential areas costs three dollars, indicates that there is an alternative form of transportation that people could use, and that this alternative form of transportation costs significantly less than the fee for using the bridge.

In other words, what this answer choice says confirms that the commuters will commute in another way as a result of the imposition of the fee.In doing so, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument.
(C) Much of the revenue generated via the collection of the fee would be used for maintaining and improving the Doveport Bridge and the roads directly connected to the bridge.

Remember that the town believes that the fee would help alleviate automobile congestion on the bridge by motivating drivers to seek alternative ways to commute.In other words, the conclusion is concerned with what drivers will do as a result of the fee, not what the town will do as a result of the fee.Thus, what this answer choice says has no clear impact on the argument.

If anything, we might wonder if the improvements to the bridge made possible by fee collection would eventually motivate drivers, who were initially deterred by the fee from using the bridge, to start using the bridge again.In other words, the benefit to drivers of using the bridge might start to outweigh the cost, in which case the town’s plan would backfire.With that in mind, what this answer choice says potentially weakens the argument, if it has any effect on the argument at all.
(D) Creating carpool lanes on the bridge would not result in a reduction in traffic congestion significantly greater than any that could conceivably result from the imposition of the fee.

Notice that this answer choice describes a “different / better plan.” In making this argument, the author does not claim that there is no better plan or alternative plan out there to relieve congestion.The argument is concerned with whether one plan in particular will work.Thus, this information about another plan’s effectiveness, or lack thereof, does not strengthen the case for the plan laid out in the argument.
(E) Once the bridge fee goes into effect, motorists commuting over the Doveport Bridge in the morning and evening will not begin to carpool and rideshare.

The argument’s conclusion is that “the town believes that the fee would help alleviate automobile congestion on the bridge by motivating drivers to seek alternative ways to commute.” In other words, the town is hoping that fewer drivers use the bridge as a result of the fee.If the opposite of what this answer choice says were true, and once the bridge fee goes into effect, motorists commuting over the bridge WILL begin to carpool and rideshare, then the town’s objective would be met, right?

Therefore, what this answer choice says, that once the bridge fee goes into effect, motorists commuting over bridge will NOT begin to carpool and rideshare does not strengthen the argument.This information may not weaken the argument, since it does not indicate that motorists won’t seek alternative ways to commute, but it certainly doesn’t add support for the belief that the town will accomplish its goal.

question.text: If a dancer’s feet are not well suited to dancing en pointe, it can be difficult for the dancer to perform ballet at the professional level.As a result, many aspiring ballet dancers have quit because their feet are not well suited to dancing en pointe.However, The Barre Company now produces innovative shoes that dancers with almost any type of feet can use to dance en pointe with relative ease.Clearly, therefore, the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the prediction above?

answers.text: Some dancers with feet not well suited to dancing en pointe have had successful careers in ballet without using the innovative shoes produced by The Barre Company.
There are skills other than skill in dancing en pointe that a dancer must have to perform ballet at the professional level.
An initiative underway to provide more funding for the arts is expected to result in an increase in the number of ballet companies operating.
Dancing en pointe at the professional level requires years of training.
There are types of dance that are done professionally that do not involve dancing en pointe.

explanation_parts.text: To answer this question, we first read the passage.Doing so, we see that the argument uses the fact that The Barre Company produces shoes that address a problem that has caused many aspiring ballet dancers to quit to support the following conclusion:
Conclusion: Clearly, therefore, the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.

Our next move is to read the question.The question asks which of the choices “most strongly supports the prediction above.” Thus, we must find a choice that supports a prediction stated by the passage.So, this question is a Strengthen the Argument question.

At this point, we go back to the passage to confirm that “the prediction above” is the conclusion of the argument.We see that the conclusion is a prediction about the number of people with successful careers in ballet.So, we have confirmed that the correct answer must support the conclusion of the argument.In other words, the correct answer must make it more likely that the prediction “the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase,” is correct.
(A) Some dancers with feet not well suited to dancing en pointe have had successful careers in ballet without using the innovative shoes produced by The Barre Company.

This choice has no effect on the argument.After all, the fact that some dancers with feet not well suited to dancing en pointe have had successful careers in ballet without using the innovative shoes doesn’t mean that, in the future, the number of people with successful careers in ballet will, or will not, increase.It’s just a fact about what some dancers have been doing up to this point in time without using the innovative shoes.
(B) There are skills other than skill in dancing en pointe that a dancer must have to perform ballet at the professional level.

The fact that, as this choice says, dancers must have skills other than skill in dancing en pointe to perform ballet at the professional level certainly doesn’t make it more likely that the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.Rather, this information is a reason why it could be challenging for more people to have successful careers in ballet even if they can dance en pointe using the innovative shoes.They’ll need additional skills as well.
So, if anything, this choice does the opposite of what we’re looking for, making it less likely that number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.
YOUR CHOICE(C) An initiative underway to provide more funding for the arts is expected to result in an increase in the number of ballet companies operating.
By considering the implications of this choice, we can see that it does help to support the conclusion.Here’s how.

If “an initiative underway to provide more funding for the arts is expected to result in an increase in the number of ballet companies operating,” then it’s reasonable to expect that there will be an increase in the number of dancers needed by ballet companies.After all, an increase in the number of companies operating would logically be associated with an increase in the number of dancers needed by ballet companies.

Of course, if there’s an increase in the number of dancers needed by ballet companies, then if more aspiring dancers are able to dance en pointe because of the innovative shoes, then those dancers are likely to be able to “have successful careers in ballet.”
So, we can see that this choice provides an additional reason to conclude that “the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.”
(D) Dancing en pointe at the professional level requires years of training.

This choice certainly doesn’t make it more likely that the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.Rather, the information that dancing en pointe at the professional level requires years of training is a reason why it could be challenging for more people to have successful careers in ballet even if they can dance en pointe using the innovative shoes.They’ll still need years of training.
So, if anything, this choice does the opposite of what we’re looking for, making it less likely that number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.
(E) There are types of dance that are done professionally that do not involve dancing en pointe.

This choice has no clear effect on the argument.After all, the fact that there are types of dance that are done professionally that do not involve dancing en pointe doesn’t clearly indicate anything about whether the number of people with successful careers in ballet will increase.

question.text: Researchers have noticed that, recently, people have begun developing bone spurs on the backs of their skulls.Since bone spurs tend to develop when musculoskeletal structures are stressed by repetitive motions, and since people repeatedly tilt their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices, the researchers believe that the bone spurs that they have observed on the backs of people’s skulls are developing as a result of people’s repetitively tilting their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices.Which of the following, if true, does the most to confirm the researchers’ belief?

answers.text: When looking at cell phones and other electronic devices, most people position the devices so that looking at them requires tilting one’s head down.
Athletes often develop bone spurs in areas of their bodies that are repeatedly stressed in the course of athletic activities.
The incidence of back-of-skull bone spurs in men, who look at their cell phones more often than women do, is higher than that in women.
Because the extensive use of cell phones is a recent phenomenon, it is reasonable to suspect that there are unknown effects of such use that will be discovered over time.
Many people who have cell phones look at their phones ten or more times per hour when awake.

explanation_parts.text: Background:Recently, people have begun developing bone spurs on the backs of their skulls.
Premise: Bone spurs tend to develop when musculoskeletal structures are stressed by repetitive motions.
Premise: People repeatedly tilt their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices.
Conclusion:The bone spurs observed on the backs of people’s skulls are developing as a result of people’s repetitively tilting their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices.

Notice that this argument is making a cause-and-effect claim.We must always keep in mind that the fact that two variables occur together does not provide us with sufficient evidence to conclude that one of the variables causes the other.In answering Critical Reasoning questions, we must always be on the lookout for errors in cause-and-effect reasoning.
Our job here is to strengthen the cause-and-effect link between the formation of the bone spurs and people’s repetitively tilting their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices.
(A) When looking at cell phones and other electronic devices, most people position the devices so that looking at them requires tilting one’s head down.
We must be extra careful with an answer like this because it may seem to strengthen the argument, when in fact all it’s doing is validating something that we already know to be factual.

Looking back at the passage, we see that it says, “people repeatedly tilt their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices.” Of course, any premise given in the passage is factually correct.Thus, by indicating that the way in which people use electronic devices requires people’s tilting their heads down, this answer choice basically just confirms what we already know to be true.
(B) Athletes often develop bone spurs in areas of their bodies that are repeatedly stressed in the course of athletic activities.
Like choice (A), this choice validates something that we already know to be factual.
Notice that the passage says, “such bone spurs tend to develop when musculoskeletal structures are stressed by repetitive motions.”

So, the information in this answer choice, that “athletes often develop bone spurs in areas of their bodies that are repeatedly stressed” simply confirms something already established as true, that stress on bones causes bone spurs to develop.
Don’t fall for traps that confirm premises.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The incidence of back-of-skull bone spurs in men, who look at their cell phones more often than women do, is higher than that in women.
Think of what this answer choice says as describing an experiment meant to demonstrate what happens when a variable, looking at electronic devices, is present or not present.
Men look at their cell phones more often than women do.
Men have a higher incidence of back-of-skull bone spurs than women have.
So, when the variable, looking at cell phones, is more present, the variable, bone spurs, is more present as well.

Imagine if men looked down at their phones more often than women did, but women’s incidence of back-of-skull bone spurs were the same as men’s.We would have much less reason to believe the conclusion that repeatedly looking down at one’s phone caused bone spurs, right? We would wonder why, if men look at cell phones more, men’s incidence of bone spurs isn’t higher than women’s.

However, that case is not the actual case.The actual case is that men, who use cell phones more, have bone spurs more, a fact that tends to confirm the conclusion that repeatedly looking down at electronic devices is the cause of the bone spurs.
(D) Because the extensive use of cell phones is a recent phenomenon, it is reasonable to suspect that there are unknown effects of such use that will be discovered over time.

This answer choice may be tempting because it makes a statement that seems likely to be true, and the information that “the extensive use of cell phones is a recent phenomenon” seems to line up nicely with the fact stated in the passage that “recently, people have begun developing bone spurs on the backs of their skulls.”

However, we must remember that we’re trying to strengthen a cause-and-effect link between two phenomena.The link is between people’s repeatedly tilting their heads to look at cell phones and the formation of bone spurs on the backs of people’s skulls.

The statement in this answer choice merely confirms for us that, yes, there are probably some effects, in general, that extensive cell phone use will cause, and we probably don’t yet know all of those effects.This general statement does nothing to strengthen the argument’s claim about the specific effect that researchers believe cell phone use is causing.
(E) Many people who have cell phones look at their phones ten or more times per hour when awake.

This choice merely qualifies something that we already know to be factual.Notice that the passage says, “people repeatedly tilt their heads down to look at cell phones and other electronic devices.” So, we already know that people look at their cell phones repeatedly.
All this choice adds is how often “repeatedly” is.
Information on how often people look at their cell phones does not connect cell phone use to bone spur formation.People do many things often, and most of those activities don’t result in bone spurs.
We have to watch out for pseudo-strengthening answer choices that seem important but don’t really affect the argument.

question.text: Unlike most other fish, sharks travel in a pattern that involves first swimming at an upward angle, and then coasting at a downward angle.Sharks, unlike most other fish, are always denser than the water in which they swim and, therefore, sink when they are not actively swimming.Marine biologists believe that this fact is the reason why sharks travel differently from other fish.Which of the following best supports the marine biologists’ conclusion?

answers.text: When sharks rest by pumping their gills rather than swimming, they settle onto the sea floor.
Seals, which become denser as they go deeper into the ocean, switch from swimming horizontally near the ocean’s surface to swimming upward and coasting downward when they are deep underwater.
Many types of fish have internal mechanisms that enable them to match the density of their bodies to the density of the surrounding water.
Certain types of birds travel in a pattern that involves flying at an upward angle, and then coasting at a downward angle.
When non-shark fish use internal mechanisms to adjust their density to make it greater than that of the surrounding water, they sink deeper into the water.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Unlike most other fish, sharks travel in a pattern that involves first swimming at an upward angle, and then coasting at a downward angle.
Premise: Sharks, unlike most other fish, are always denser than the water in which they swim and, therefore, sink when they are not actively swimming.
Conclusion: Marine biologists believe that this fact is the reason why sharks travel differently from other fish.

The marine biologists believe that the fact that sharks are always denser than the water in which they swim, and therefore must keep swimming to prevent sinking, explains why sharks move differently from other fish through the water.On what is this theory based? Notice that the author does not provide us with any real evidence to support this theory.

Remember, a person can make observations such as “unlike most other fish, sharks travel in a pattern that involves first swimming at an upward angle and then coasting at a downward angle” and “unlike most other fish, sharks are always denser than the water in which they swim and, therefore, sink when they are not actively swimming” and come up with cool explanations and cause-and-effect arguments related to those observations.However, without real evidence, the hypothesis remains just that.
So, in answering this Strengthen the Argument question, we have to find an answer choice that makes the marine biologists’ beliefs more plausible.
(A) When sharks rest by pumping their gills rather than swimming, they settle onto the sea floor.
This answer choice is seductive because it discusses something similar to what the passage discusses.

The passage tells us that sharks“sink when they are not actively swimming.” Similarly, this answer choice tells us that “when sharks rest by pumping their gills rather than swimming, they settle onto the sea floor.”
However, this choice is not our correct answer, because this answer choice essentially merely confirms the premise that sharks “sink when they are not actively swimming.”
Remember, we are not just trying to match concepts or confirm that a premise is true.We already know that the given premises are factual.

The proposed reason (or cause) for the observed effect (sharks traveling differently) is their density, which causes them to sink when they’re not swimming.Giving us another example of sharks’ sinking when they’re not swimming, as this answer choice does, does nothing to strengthen the cause-effect link.

YOUR CHOICE(B) Seals, which become denser as they go deeper into the ocean, switch from swimming horizontally near the ocean’s surface to swimming upward and coasting downward when they are deep underwater.

This choice could seem irrelevant, because this choice is about seals, which are not fish, and the argument is about fish.However, we have to be careful not to eliminate a choice merely because it’s about a topic not obviously connected to the argument.In fact, this choice is a very effective strengthener.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by providing us with what amounts to an experiment to investigate the relationship between density and swimming patterns.
Notice what this choice tells us:
When seals areless dense, near the surface of the ocean, theydon’t swim in the way sharks swim.
When seals aredenser, as they would be when deep underwater, according to what this choice says, theyswim in the way sharks do.
So, what we have here is like an experiment to see whether density is the cause of the pattern of travel, and here are the results of that experiment.

When the cause (density) is not present, the effect (seals’ swimming in the way sharks swim) is not present.When the cause (density caused by seals’ swimming deep underwater) is present, the effect (seals’ swimming in the way sharks swim) is present too.
Perfect.We have information connecting density and that up-and-down pattern of travel again.So, it’s looking more likely that sharks swim the way they do because of their density.
This choice clearly provides additional support for the conclusion that sharks’ density is the reason why they travel in the way they travel.
(C) Many types of fish have internal mechanisms that enable them to match the density of their bodies to the density of the surrounding water.

This choice may be tempting because it discusses the density of fish and how that relates to the density of the surrounding water, a topic that seems to relate to the author’s discussion of the sharks’ density in relation to that of the water in which they swim.

However, we must remember that we are not seeking merely to match concepts; we are seeking to strengthen a cause-and-effect claim.The author’s claim is that sharks’ density is whatcausesthem to travel in a certain pattern.The fact that some fish can adjust their densities does not clearly connect density with the way that sharks, or any fish for that matter, swim.Thus, this choice does not provide further evidence for the conclusion we’re seeking to support.
(D) Certain types of birds travel in a pattern that involves flying at an upward angle, and then coasting at a downward angle.

This trap choice is seductive because it discusses an animal’s traveling “in a pattern that involves flying at an upward angle and then coasting at a downward angle,” and that pattern resembles sharks’ swimming patterns.
However, remember that Critical Reasoning questions are not word-matching exercises, and we are seeking an answer choice that strengthens a cause-effect claim.

This answer choice mentions one half of the cause-effect relationship laid out in the argument, theeffecthalf (traveling in a certain upward and then downward pattern), but does nothing to address thecauseof that pattern.

What is the cause of certain birds’ traveling in that way? Do certain birds fly in that way because they are denser than the air in which they fly? Do they fly in that way for some other reason? We aren’t told.Yes, birds are denser than the air in which they fly, but not all birds travel in an up-and-down pattern.So, what is going on?
We can’t reliably tell more from what this choice says than that some animals other than sharks travel in a way that resembles the way sharks travel.

If this choice said, for instance, thatonly the densest birdstravel in an up-and-down pattern that matches sharks’ pattern of travel, then we’d have something.Density and that pattern of travel would be showing up together again, indicating that perhaps density is the reason for that pattern of travel.
As is, however, this choice does not strengthen the support for the argument’s cause-and-effect conclusion.
(E) When non-shark fish use internal mechanisms to adjust their density to make it greater than that of the surrounding water, they sink deeper into the water.

This trap answer is seductive because it discusses something that resembles what’s discussed in the passage.The passage says, “Sharks, unlike most other fish, are alwaysdenser than the water in which they swim and,therefore, sinkwhen they are not actively swimming.” Similarly, this answer choice says, “When non-shark fish … adjusttheir density to make it greater than that of the surrounding water, they sinkdeeper into the water.” So, both sharks and non-sharks sink when they are denser than the surrounding water.Now we must ask, is that relevant?
The truth is that anything sinks in water when it is denser than water.So, this choice merely presents an example of a physical law in action.

Remember, Critical Reasoning questions are not word-matching exercises.As fascinating as the physics lesson this answer choice provides may be, we’re seeking to strengthen a cause-and-effect claim.The cause-and-effect claim relates to whether density (the cause) affects sharks’ travel patterns (the effect), not whether the basic laws of physics apply to marine animals.

question.text: Real Estate Expert: Yes, over the past five years, the average selling price of a home in Frohesberg has been lower than the average selling price of a home in other similar towns in the same area.At the same time, it’s likely that this difference has resulted primarily from the fact that, in Frohesberg, micro houses, which are generally the houses that are the lowest priced, represent a greater percentage of homes than the percentage of homes such houses represent in any of the other similar towns in the area.Which of the following, if true, does the most to confirm the validity of the real estate expert’s theory?

answers.text: During the past five years, the percentage of homes sold in Frohesberg has been the same as the percentage of homes sold in other similar towns in the area.
The percentage of homes represented by micro houses is higher in Frohesberg than in other similar towns in the area because Frohesberg is especially popular with minimalist lifestyle aficionados.
In Frohesberg, micro houses represent a percentage of houses greater than the percentage of houses represented by micro houses in other similar towns in the same area.
The percentage of homes represented by rental apartments in Frohesberg is no greater than the percentage of homes represented by rental apartments in other similar towns in the area.
The average selling price of non-micro houses in Frohesberg has been at least as great as that of non-micro houses in other similar towns in the same area.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Over the past five years, the average selling price of a home in Frohesberg has been lower than the average selling price of a home in other similar towns in the same area.

Conclusion: It’s likely that this difference has resulted primarily from the fact that, in Frohesberg, micro houses, which are generally the houses that are the lowest priced, represent a greater percentage of homes than the percentage of homes such houses represent in any of the other similar towns in the area.

The real estate expert has theorized that the reason why the average selling price of a home in Frohesberg has been lower than average selling price of homes in other towns is that micro houses, which tend to be low priced, are more common in Frohesberg than in other towns.

Of course, the fact that micro houses are more common in Frohesberg does not have to be the reason for the lower average selling price of a home there.There are many other potential reasons for this outcome, such as that all houses in Frohesberg sell for relatively low prices.

To correctly answer this question, we have to find evidence to confirm the theory that the reason why the average selling price is lower in Frohesberg is in fact that micro houses are more common in Frohesberg than in other towns.
(A) During the past five years, the percentage of homes sold in Frohesberg has been the same as the percentage of homes sold in other similar towns in the area.

This choice is a tempting pseudo strengthener.This choice could seem to eliminate a possible alternate reason for the lower average selling price of a home in Frohesberg by confirming that there has been no difference between the percentage of homes sold in Frohesberg and the percentages of homes sold in other towns.
But what if the percentages of homes sold had been different.Would that difference really explain the difference in selling prices? No.

So, this choice doesn’t really serve to eliminate an alternate explanation of why the average sale price of a home in Frohesberg was lower, because even if there were a difference in percentages of homes sold, that difference wouldn’t explain why the average selling price was lower in Frohesberg.
Since there’s no clear connection between percentages of homes sold in the towns and selling prices of homes in the towns, this choice does not affect the support for the conclusion.
(B) The percentage of homes represented by micro houses is higher in Frohesberg than in other similar towns in the area because Frohesberg is especially popular with minimalist lifestyle aficionados.
This choice has the wrong effect.It may seem to strengthen the argument, but what it really does is explain a part of the argument.
This choice explains why micro houses are especially common in Frohesberg: Frohesberg is especially popular with minimalist lifestyle aficionados.
We have to be careful not to be confused into thinking that a choice that explains something about the argument strengthens the argument.
(C) In Frohesberg, micro houses represent a percentage of houses greater than the percentage of houses represented by micro houses in other similar towns in the same area.
This choice tells us absolutely nothing new.

Any home either is a micro house or is not a micro house.So, in Frohesberg, and in the other towns mentioned in the passage, there are two kinds of homes: homes that are micro houses and non-micro house homes.
Now, let’s think about what this choice says.It tells us that non-micro house homes are not as common in Frohesberg as they are in other towns.Is that new information? It is not new at all.

We already knew from the passage that, in Frohesberg, micro houses represent a greater percentage of homes than the percentage of homes such houses represent in any of the other similar towns in the area.

What that information also has to mean is just what this choice says, that non-micro house homes represent a smaller percentage of homes than the percentage of homes such homes represent in any of the other similar towns in the area.

In summary, if micro houses represent a greater percentage of homes in Frohesberg, then all the other types of homes have to represent a smaller percentage of homes in Frohesberg.Simple math tells us that things have to work that way.
So, as soon as we read the passage, we could have said for sure what this choice says, because what this choice says follows logically from what the passage says.
Since this choice provides no truly new information, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
(D) The percentage of homes represented by rental apartments in Frohesberg is no greater than the percentage of homes represented by rental apartments in other similar towns in the area.

This choice is a pseudo-strengthener.This choice could seem to eliminate a possible alternate reason for the lower average selling price of a home in Frohesberg by confirming that the percentage of homes represented by rental apartments in Frohesberg is no greater than the percentage of homes represented by rental apartments in other similar towns in the area.
But would the percentage of homes represented by rental apartments really make any difference in average selling prices, and if so, what difference?

The truth is that this choice has no effect on the argument because we don’t have any information on selling prices of homes in these towns that are rental apartments.Do apartments that people rent out sell for more than micro houses sell for? less than micro houses sell for? Are these apartments big? Small? Luxurious? Spartan?
The argument is about selling prices, and we don’t have a way to connect the information provided by this choice with selling prices.
We have to be sure not to make up an unsupported story about apartments that would convince us that this choice affects the argument.
This choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The average selling price of non-micro houses in Frohesberg has been at least as great as that of non-micro houses in other similar towns in the same area.

This choice strengthens the support for the conclusion by serving to rule out an alternative cause for the average selling price of a home in Frohesberg being lower the average selling prices of homes in other towns.

What if, in contrast to what this choice says, the average selling price of non-micro houses in Frohesberg has NOT been at least as great as that of non-micro houses in other similar towns in the same area? In that case, it could be that the reason for the lower average selling price seen in Frohesberg is simply that homes in general sell for relatively low prices in Frohesberg.Right? In that case, it might not be the selling prices of micro houses that are bringing down the average.The average could have been lower even if there were no micro houses in Frohesberg.

However, this choice tells us that the average selling price of non-micro houses in Frohesberg HAS been at least as great as that of non-micro houses in other similar towns in the same area.What that means is that, if it weren’t for the low selling prices of micro houses, the average selling price of a home in Frohesberg would not have been lower than the averages for the other towns.
Of course, then, this choice tends to confirm the theory that the only reason why the average selling price is lower in Frohesberg is that micro houses are more common in Frohesberg.

question.text: Shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico have been declining at an alarming rate over the past decade.Given that sharks have no natural predators, the primary cause of the declining shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico must be the use of nets for tuna fishing, since wildlife conservationists, along with federal fish and game officials, have documented during this time a ten-fold increase in the number of sharks caught and killed by nets meant to catch tuna.Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the above conclusion EXCEPT:

answers.text: Shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico have seen much larger declines than have shark populations in waters in which nets are not used to catch tuna.
Smaller fish upon which sharks in the Gulf of Mexico feed have not seen population declines in more than twenty years.
Over the last decade, organizations that regulate fishing in the Gulf of Mexico have seen a significant decline in the number of shark fishing licenses for which fishermen applied.
The nets fishermen use to catch tuna in the Gulf of Mexico have been observed to often catch and kill other animals, including dolphins, porpoises, and even whales.
At no time over the past 20 years have shark migration patterns changed in ways that resulted in a significant net exodus of sharks from the Gulf of Mexico.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico have been declining at an alarming rate over the past decade.

Premise: During this time, wildlife conservationists, along with federal fish and game officials, have documented a ten-fold increase in the number of sharks caught and killed by nets meant to catch tuna.
Conclusion: Given that sharks have no natural predators, the primary cause of the declining shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico must be the use of nets for tuna fishing.
Notice that we have a cause-and-effect argument.The author believes that the cause of the Gulf of Mexico’s declining shark populations is the use of nets for tuna fishing.

What is the evidence for this conclusion? The evidence is that federal officials have documented a ten-fold increase in the number of sharks caught and killed by nets meant to catch tuna.It’s important to note that this evidence is simply an observation, and an observation by itself is not strong evidence of a cause-and-effect relationship.

As we well know, we must always be skeptical of cause-and-effect arguments.The fact that two events happen at the same time is not solid evidence that one event is the cause of the other.If we consistently observe events A (declining shark populations) and B (sharks caught in nets) at the same time, it is not necessarily the case that B causes A.It is possible that both A and B are caused by C, which is something else entirely, or that A and B have separate causes, D and E.

Of course, this is a Strengthen EXCEPT question.So, we will see four answers that strengthen the author’s cause-and-effect claim.The remaining answer, the one that does NOT strengthen the argument, will be the correct answer.It’s important to remember that we are not necessarily seeking an answer choice that weakens the argument; an correct answer could simply not affect the strength of the argument.
(A) Shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico have seen much larger declines than have shark populations in waters in which nets are not used to catch tuna.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by showing that when the cause is more prevalent, so too is the effect.
In general, when we are dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, if we can show that, when the cause is absent, so too is the effect, then the argument is much stronger.

If shark populations in the Gulf of Mexico have seen much larger declines than have been seen by shark populations in waters in which nets are not used to catch tuna (in other words, waters in which the stated cause is not present), then we have more reason to believe that the cause of the declining shark populations is in fact the nets.
Strengthens? Yes.
(B) Smaller fish upon which sharks in the Gulf of Mexico feed have not seen population declines in more than twenty years.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for why the shark populations have been declining.After all, if smaller fish upon which sharks in the Gulf of Mexico feed had seen population declines in the past twenty years, then we would be less sure that the true cause of the declining shark populations was the use of tuna fishing nets.The true cause could be that the sharks had lost their food supply.
In dealing with an argument that rests upon cause-and-effect logic, we can strengthen the claim made by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed events.
Strengthens? Yes.
(C) Over the last decade, organizations that regulate fishing in the Gulf of Mexico have seen a significant decline in the number of shark fishing licenses for which fishermen applied.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for why the shark populations have been declining.After all, if fewer fisherman have applied for shark fishing licenses, then the possibility is greatly reduced that the shark populations declined because of an increased number of fishermen fishing for sharks.
In dealing with an argument that rests upon cause-and-effect logic, we can strengthen the claim made by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed events.
Strengthens? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The nets fishermen use to catch tuna in the Gulf of Mexico have been observed to often catch and kill other animals, including dolphins, porpoises, and even whales.
It’s important to remember that a premise provided in an argument is considered factual.

From the passage, we already know that conservationists and federal officials have documented a “ten-fold increase in the number of sharks caught and killed by nets meant to catch tuna.” Therefore, the fact that tuna fishing nets often catch and kill other animals is something that we already know.Remember, answers that simply restate facts that have already been provided to us in the passage are not strengtheners.Thus, since this is a Strengthen EXCEPT question, this answer choice is correct.
Strengthens? No.
(E) At no time over the past 20 years have shark migration patterns changed in ways that resulted in a significant net exodus of sharks from the Gulf of Mexico.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for why shark populations have been declining.

Imagine if shark migration patternshadchanged in ways that resulted in a significant net exodus of sharks from the Gulf of Mexico? In such a situation, we’d have much less faith the argument’s conclusion that fishing nets had caused the decline in the shark population.We’d have a plausible alternative explanation, and so the argument would be weaker.Since this answer choice eliminates a possible weakener, the argument is stronger.
In dealing with an argument that rests upon cause-and-effect logic, we can strengthen the claim made by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed events.
Strengthens? Yes.

question.text: In a psychology experiment, paid volunteers were cast as either prisoners or guards in a mock prison.Those acting as guards quickly began to behave cruelly toward those acting as prisoners, and the results of the experiment led the researchers involved to conclude that the average person can quickly become quite cruel in roles involving having power over other people.However, this conclusion has been challenged on the basis of the fact that it is likely that people who knowingly volunteered to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison would be more predisposed than the average person to behave cruelly.The case for challenging the researchers’ conclusion would be most strengthened if, in a follow-up experiment, those acting as guards were significantly less cruel than those acting as guards in the original experiment when

answers.text: participants recruited via an advertisement for volunteers to participate in a psychology experiment were placed into an experimental situation duplicating the original one.
the original experiment was duplicated except in that the participants from the original experiment were recast with their roles reversed.
the original experiment was duplicated with new participants, except in that the participants were told that they would be paid only if the experiment were a success.
people actually employed as prison guards were recruited to act as guards in an experiment that was otherwise the same as the original one.
the original experiment was duplicated with new participants, except in that those acting as prisoners were instructed to avoid antagonizing or scaring those acting as guards.

explanation_parts.text: To correctly answer this question, we have to find a choice that strengthens the case for challenging the researchers’ conclusion.So, to answer the question, we have to identify the researchers’ conclusion, the support for the researchers’ conclusion, and the case for challenging the researchers’ conclusion.
Researchers’ Conclusion: The average person can quickly become quite cruel in roles involving having power over other people.
Support for the Researchers’ Conclusion: In a psychology experiment involving a mock prison, those acting as guards quickly began to behave cruelly toward those acting as prisoners.

Case for Challenging the Researchers’ Conclusion: It is likely that people who knowingly volunteered to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison would be more predisposed than the average person to behave cruelly.
Notice that the case for challenging the researchers’ conclusion involves a cause-and-effect claim.

The claim is that the reason for the observed cruel behavior was an unusual predisposition to act cruelly had by people who would volunteer to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison.

So, to strengthen the case for challenging the researchers’ conclusion, we need information that indicates that such an unusual predisposition, rather than the tendencies of the average person, is indeed the reason for the cruel behavior.

The question stem tells us that the information in the correct answer will be in the form of the setup of a follow-up experiment.To correctly answer the question, we need to find the experimental setup such that the fact that the guards don’t act cruelly in the new experiment indicates that the cause of the cruel behavior in the original experiment was indeed an unusual predisposition had by “people who would volunteer to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison.”
YOUR CHOICE(A) participants recruited via an advertisement for volunteers to participate in a psychology experiment were placed into an experimental situation duplicating the original one.

The case for challenging the researchers’ conclusion is that it is likely thatpeople who knowingly volunteered to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prisonwould be more predisposed than the average person to behave cruelly.

One way to support that case is to see what happens if we do the same experiment with “average” people, rather than with “people who would volunteer to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison.” A way to ensure that we recruit “average” people, or at least people who are not unusually cruel, is do what this choice describes: recruit volunteers without describing the experiment.

Then, rather than getting people who would volunteer to participate in “an immersive experiment into interactions in prison,” we’d get people who would volunteer to participate in a nondescript “psychology experiment,” one that could have nothing to do with the type of power dynamics that would exist in a prison.

In setting up the experiment in that way, we will have, in theory, eliminated the supposed cause (the predisposition to act cruelly).We could then observe whether the observed effect (cruel behavior) disappears once the supposed cause is gone.

Now, remember, the question stem tells us that the result of the experiment will be the following: “those acting as guards were significantly less cruel than those acting as guards in the original experiment.” So, the results we will get will be that the effect (cruel behavior) goes away.

Since, in this new experiment, the effect (cruel behavior) goes away when the supposed cause (the predispositions of people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment) is gone, the results of this new experiment confirm the cause-and-effect relationship that serves as the basis for the challenge to the researchers’ claim.
Remember, by showing that, when the cause goes away, the effect goes away too, we can strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect claim.
This choice is our strengthener.
(B) the original experiment was duplicated except in that the participants from the original experiment were recast with their roles reversed.

Performing this version of the experiment and getting the result that the guards were less cruel than the guards in the original experiment would not provide the support we seek for the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion.

The challenge to the researchers’ conclusion is based on the cause-and-effect claim that the cruelty on the part of the guards resulted from a predisposition to behave cruelly of people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment.

Notice that the participants in this version of the experiment are the same people who volunteered to participate in the first version of the experiment.In other words they are all people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment.So, the supposed cause, a predisposition to behave cruelly of people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment, should still be there.
However, the result of the experiment is that the effect, cruel behavior, is less present than it was when the original experiment was done.
So, according to those who challenge the researchers’ conclusion, we would still have the supposed cause.However, we don’t have the effect.

Since these results show a case in which we should still have the supposed cause but we don’t have the effect, these results would weaken, rather than strengthen, the cause-effect link that is the basis of the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion.
(C) the original experiment was duplicated with new participants, except in that the participants were told that they would be paid only if the experiment were a success.

Performing this version of the experiment and getting the result that the guards were less cruel than the guards in the original experiment would not provide the support we seek for the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion.

Other than the part about getting paid only if the experiment is a success, this version of the experiment is a duplicate of the original experiment.So, the participants in this version of the experiment are presumably “people who would volunteer to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison” just as the participants in the first version were.
So, we should still have the supposed cause (the predispositions of people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment), but now we don’t have as much of the effect (cruel behavior).

The fact that the effect is less present when the supposed cause should still be present tends to weaken rather than strengthen the cause-and-effect claim that is the basis of the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion.

Further, in this version of the experiment, a new factor has been introduced: the participants were told that they would be paid only if the experiment were a success.The effect of that new factor is not clear, making the results of this version of the experiment difficult to interpret.
(D) people actually employed as prison guards were recruited to act as guards in an experiment that was otherwise the same as the original one.

Performing this version of the experiment and getting the result that the guards were less cruel than the guards in the original experiment would not provide the support we seek for the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion.

The participants in this version are “people actually employed as prison guards” rather than “people who would volunteer to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison.” At the same time, common sense tells us that people employed as prison guards may not be “average people.”

The results of this experiment are that we no longer have the supposed cause (the predispositions of people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment) and we have less of the effect (cruel behavior).However, we really don’t know why this change has occurred, because we have introduced a complicating factor, the fact that the participants are employed as prison guards.
(E) the original experiment was duplicated with new participants, except in that those acting as prisoners were instructed to avoid antagonizing or scaring those acting as guards.

Performing this version of the experiment and getting the result that the guards were less cruel than the guards in the original experiment would not provide the support we seek for the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion.

The challenge to the researchers’ conclusion is based on the cause-and-effect claim that the cruelty on the part of the guards resulted from a predisposition to behave cruelly had by people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment.

Notice that, except for one change, this version of the experiment is a duplicate of the original experiment.So, presumably this version uses “people who would volunteer to participate in an immersive experiment into interactions in prison.” So, the supposed cause, a predisposition to behave cruelly of people who would volunteer to participate in a prison experiment, should still be there.
However, the result of the experiment is that the effect, cruel behavior, is less present than it was when the original experiment was done.
So, according to those who challenge the researchers’ conclusion, we would still have the supposed cause, but we don’t have the effect.
Furthermore, we have removed another factor: “antagonizing or scaring those acting as guards.”
So, what have we demonstrated with this version of the experiment?
Well, we kept everything the same except for removing the factor “antagonizing or scaring those acting as guards,” and the effect “behaving cruelly” became less present.
So, what we seem to have demonstrated is thatantagonizing or scaring the guardsis the cause of the cruel behavior.

Since the challenge to the researchers’ conclusion is not based on the claim that antagonizing or scaring those acting as guards is the cause of the cruel behavior, this version of the experiment supports the wrong cause-and-effect claim.

question.text: Resveratrol, a substance found in red wine, has long been suspected to have longevity-enhancing properties.In fact, in a recent study, scientists compared the average lifespans of people in Mediterranean countries where red wine is consumed regularly to the lifespans of people in Scandinavian countries where red wine is consumed significantly less regularly.It was determined that people in the Mediterranean countries lived, on average, seven years longer than people living in the Scandinavian countries lived.Thus, the scientists concluded that the increased intake of resveratrol explains the differences in longevity.Which of the following, if true, most helps to confirm the explanation above?

answers.text: Because red wine is fermented with grape skins for longer than white wine is, red wine contains more resveratrol than white wine contains.
Another study found that people in the Mediterranean countries also lived longer, on average, than people in some countries in South America did.
People living in Mediterranean countries drink red wine about three times as often as people living in Scandinavian countries do.
People living in Scandinavian countries do not eat, on average, more meat, eggs, and dairy, which are known to cause health problems, than people in Mediterranean countries eat.
People living in the Mediterranean countries do not engage in walking, which is associated with an increase in longevity, less each day than do people living in Scandinavian countries.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Resveratrol, a substance found in red wine, has long been suspected to have longevity-enhancing properties.

Premise: In a recent study, scientists compared the average lifespans of people in Mediterranean countries where red wine is consumed regularly to the lifespans of people in Scandinavian countries where red wine is consumed significantly less regularly.
Premise: It was determined that people in the Mediterranean countries lived, on average, seven years longer than people living in the Scandinavian countries lived.
Conclusion: The increased intake of resveratrol explains the differences in longevity.

We are presented with a cause-and-effect argument.The author claims that increased intake of resveratrol explains the observed differences in longevity.This conclusion is based on the evidence that people in Mediterranean countries where red wine is consumed in greater quantities lived longer, on average, than people living in Scandinavian countries lived.

As we’ve said so many time before, and will say so many times again, the fact that two variables occur together – in this case, increase red wine consumption and longer lifespans – does not mean that we should conclude that one of the variables is the cause of the other.In this case, of all things that could cause a difference in longevity (smoking habits, diet, exercise, etc.), the author believes that the difference in longevity is explained by differences in resveratrol intake.
Because this is a Strengthen the Argument question, our job is to strengthen the author’s claim that the resveratrol is in fact causing the longevity differences.
(A) Because red wine is fermented with grape skins for longer than white wine is, red wine contains more resveratrol than white wine contains.

This choice could be tempting because it seems to support a conclusion about red wine, possibly the conclusion that red wine is more effective in promoting longevity than white wine is.However, this choice is not correct because it doesn't support the conclusion that we have to support, that resveratrol is the cause of longer life spans.
This comparison between the amount of resveratrol in red wine and the amount in white wine tells us nothing about whether resveratrol produces a specific effect, a longer life span.

Remember, the conclusion is that resveratrol causes people in Mediterranean countries to live longer than people in Scandinavian countries, so we’re looking for an answer choice that provides further evidence that resveratrol is indeed the cause of the observed difference in longevity.

Perhaps the information in this answer choice would provide support for a different conclusion, a conclusion about whether fermentation causes higher resveratrol content, or whether red wine has more resveratrol than other wines have.But this information does not affect the cause-and-effect argument we’re seeking to strengthen.
(B) Another study found that people in the Mediterranean countries also lived longer, on average, than people in some countries in South America did.

The fact that people in the Mediterranean countries also lived longer than people in some South American countries does not provide further evidence that resveratrol is thecauseof the differences in longevity.

Maybe people living in South America tend to live in cites with a high level of smog, or maybe their diets are heavy in red meat.There could be any number of reasons, aside from resveratrol intake, for the lifespans of people in South America to be shorter than those of people in Mediterranean countries.Thus, simply knowing that people in Mediterranean countries live longer does not give us any further evidence to support an argument concerned with resveratrol’s effect on longevity.
(C) People living in Mediterranean countries drink red wine about three times as often as people living in Scandinavian countries do.

Notice that this choice essentially restates a premise.What do we already know from the passage? We know that people living in Mediterranean countries regularly consume red wine, whereas people living in Scandinavian countries do not.Therefore, the argument has already established that there is a big difference between the red wine consumptions of these people.
Remember, restating a premise will not strengthen an argument.Be on the lookout for these trap choices that simply restate an argument’s premise.
YOUR CHOICE(D) People living in Scandinavian countries do not eat, on average, more meat, eggs, and dairy, which are known to cause health problems, than people in Mediterranean countries eat.
By ruling out an alternative explanation for the argument’s conclusion, this answer choice strengthens the argument.Here is why:

Let’s assume that people in Scandinavian countriesdoeat more meat, eggs, and dairy than people in Mediterranean countries eat.Wouldn’t this higher intake of meat, eggs, and dairy – things that are known to cause health problems – provide an alternative explanation for the observed longevity differences? If there are other dietary differences, aside from red wine consumption, that have an impact on health, and thus longevity, then we have a very good reason to question whether the resveratrol is actually responsible for the differences in longevity.Perhaps the longevity differences are really a matter not of the people in Mediterranean countries doing something that extends their life spans (drinking red wine) but of the people in Scandinavian countries doing something that shortens their life spans (eating the above mentioned products).
Remember, if we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim, we can strengthen that claim by ruling out an alternative explanation for the observed effect.
(E) People living in the Mediterranean countries do not engage in walking, which is associated with an increase in longevity, less each day than do people living in Scandinavian countries.

Let’s assume the opposite of this answer choice, that people living in Mediterranean countries DO walk less than people in Scandinavian countries.In that case, we’d have greater conviction that the resveratrol was responsible for the increased longevity.

The opposite of this answer choice would serve to rule out an alternative explanation, that walking was the cause of the greater longevity of people in Mediterranean countries.If it were the case that people in Mediterranean countries DO walk less than people in Scandinavian countries, then walking wouldn’t be the cause of the greater longevity of people in Mediterranean countries.

Of course, this answer choice says that people in Mediterranean countries DO NOT walk any less than people in Scandinavian countries do, a statement that leaves open the possibility that people in Mediterranean countries walkmore.Not any less could be the same or more, right?
So, this statement does not help us to rule out the alternative explanation that people in Mediterranean countries walk more and does not give us any information that strengthens the argument.

question.text: For some time, biologists were sure that white sharks were not migratory animals and that such sharks would spend their entire lives in the same area of ocean, ranging no more than a few hundred miles in any direction.This conclusion was based in part on the fact that open oceans lack sources of food that sharks would need to sustain themselves during long open-ocean swims, during which a shark could easily burn hundreds of thousands of calories.However, recently, biologists have concluded that white sharks are actually migratory, regularly traveling great distances, often thousands of miles, through the oceans.Which of the following, if true, best supports biologists’ more recent conclusion about great white sharks?

answers.text: The population density of seals and sea lions, both of which are primary prey for white sharks, rises and falls in different areas of the oceans across different seasons.

Using DNA analysis, biologists determined that white sharks living in the Mediterranean are more closely related to those living off Australia than to their geographically closer cousins in the Atlantic.
White sharks that inhabit areas in which food sources go through cycles of scarcity and abundance would benefit from being able to migrate long distances.
Skeletons of female white sharks that have been determined to have lived in the waters off South Africa, have been found almost 11,000 km away in the waters off Australia.
White sharks inhabit a wide range of Earth’s oceans and seas, from the east coast of the United States to the southern oceans off Australia.

explanation_parts.text: We’re told biologists thought that white sharks were not migratory animals, spending their entire lives in the same area of ocean.One reason for this belief was that oceans lack sources of food that sharks would need to sustain themselves during long open-ocean swims.However, recently, biologists have concluded the following:
Conclusion: White sharks are actually migratory, regularly traveling great distances, often thousands of miles, through the oceans.
Notice that the passage does not provide any evidence to support the recent conclusion by biologists.
In order to correctly answer this question, we have to find the choice that most clearly provides some support for that recent conclusion.
(A) The population density of seals and sea lions, both of which are primary prey for white sharks, rises and falls in different areas of the oceans across different seasons.

The fact that the population densities of prey in different areas of the oceans rise and fall across different seasons supports the wrong conclusion.It supports the conclusion that white sharks would benefit from migrating, because by migrating white sharks could go to wherever prey is most common during a particular season.

However, we are not seeking support for the conclusion that white sharks would benefit from migrating.We are seeking support for the conclusion that they do migrate, and the fact that population densities of prey in different areas of the oceans rise and fall does not indicate that white sharks migrate.

(B) Using DNA analysis, biologists determined that white sharks living in the Mediterranean are more closely related to those living off Australia than to their geographically closer cousins in the Atlantic.

This choice is very tempting, because the fact that white sharks in one area of the oceans are related to white sharks in another area of the oceans could seem to indicate that white sharks must migrate.

The truth is, however, that white sharks are naturally going to be related to other white sharks, and the fact that white sharks that inhabit one area of the oceans are related to white sharks that inhabit another area of the oceans does not mean that white sharks migrate.It indicates just that these sharks that live far from each other have common ancestors.They could have common ancestors and not migrate.

If this is hard for you to see, consider, for instance, a person who lives in Florida who is related to another person who lives in Siberia.Does this fact mean that the person who lives in Florida regularly migrates to Siberia?
YOUR CHOICE(C) White sharks that inhabit areas in which food sources go through cycles of scarcity and abundance would benefit from being able to migrate long distances.
This choice provides one reason why white sharks would migrate if they did migrate.White sharks would benefit from migrating to where food is most abundant.

However, we don’t need an explanation for why white sharks would migrate if they did.We need evidence that they do in fact migrate, and the fact that white sharks would benefit from migrating does not mean that they do migrate.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Skeletons of female white sharks that have been determined to have lived in the waters off South Africa, have been found almost 11,000 km away in the waters off Australia.

The fact that skeletons of white sharks determined to have lived in the waters off South Africa have been found almost 11,000 kilometers away in the waters off Australia does not prove for sure that white sharks migrate.At the same time, it provides some support for the conclusion that the sharks migrate.

If those sharks lived off South Africa at some point, and their skeletons were found near Australia, we have to wonder how the skeletons ended up where they were found, and the sharks’ migrating is one reasonable explanation.

Notice the difference between this choice and the other choices.(A) and (C) tell us that white sharks would benefit from migrating.(B) and (E) tell us that white sharks are all over the planet and that they are related to each other.Only this choice gives us any indication that the same white sharks that have been in one area have also been in an area thousands of kilometers from the first area.
This choice could make biologists think, “Hmm.Maybe white sharks do migrate.”
(E) White sharks inhabit a wide range of Earth’s oceans and seas, from the east coast of the United States to the southern oceans off Australia.

The fact that white sharks inhabit a wide range of Earth’s oceans and seas does not indicate that they migrate.It means just that, over time, sharks have distributed themselves around the planet, perhaps traveling a few hundred miles at a time, not necessarily thousands of miles regularly.
