question.text: In a recent college admissions scandal, wealthy parents, in addition to parents with average incomes, were accused of bribing universities in order to secure admission for their children.When the cases went to trial, the number of parents with average incomes who were convicted of charges was much greater than the number of wealthy parents convicted.Clearly, being wealthy confers an advantage to criminal defendants in court.Which of the following, if true, most helps to confirm the above conclusion?

answers.text: Many public defense attorneys who defend those accused of crimes at no cost to the accused rank among the top 5 percent of all law school graduates.
Wealthy parents accused of crimes are significantly more likely to be mentioned in news media than are parents with average incomes who are accused of crimes.
Of the wealthy parents accused of bribing universities, the fraction who actually had bribed universities was equal to the fraction of the accused parents with average incomes who actually had.
On average, the wealthy parents paid about $327,000 in defense attorney’s fees, whereas the parents with average incomes paid approximately $76,000.
The number of parents with average incomes accused of bribing universities was no greater than the number of wealthy parents accused of doing so.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: In a recent college admissions scandal, wealthy parents, in addition to parents with average incomes, were accused of bribing universities in order to secure admission for their children.
Premise: When the cases went to trial, the number of parents with average incomes who were convicted of charges was much greater than the number of wealthy parents convicted.
Conclusion: Clearly, being wealthy confers an advantage to criminal defendants in court.

As always, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim.Is wealth really the reason why more parents with average incomes than wealthy parents were convicted? Could there be an alternative explanation for the observed pattern?
Of course, because this is a Strengthen the Argument question, our job is to strengthen the support for claim that wealth in fact confers an advantage to criminal defendants in court.
(A) Many public defense attorneys who defend those accused of crimes at no cost to the accused rank among the top 5 percent of all law school graduates.

Notice that the argument’s conclusion is that being wealthy confers anadvantageto criminal defendants in court.What this answer choice says, that “many public defense attorneys who defend those accused of crimesat no cost to the accusedrank among the top 5 percent of all law school graduates” goes in the opposite direction.After all, the fact that people accused of crimes can secure the services of these bright attorneys at no cost certainly does not strengthen the support for the conclusion that being wealthy confers an advantage to criminal defendants in court.
(B) Wealthy parents accused of crimes are significantly more likely to be mentioned in news media than are parents with average incomes who are accused of crimes.

If you chose this answer, you likely did so because what this answer says is very likely true in reality.But we are not seeking an answer choice that says something that is very likely true.We are seeking an answer choice that strengthens the argument.

How does being significantly more likely to be mentioned in news strengthen the support for the claim that wealth confers an advantage to criminal defendants in court? Supposing that news coverage has any effect at all, we have no clear reason to believe that such coverage is more likely to be beneficial than it is to be detrimental to criminal defendants’ in court.

YOUR CHOICE(C) Of the wealthy parents accused of bribing universities, the fraction who actually had bribed universities was equal to the fraction of the accused parents with average incomes who actually had.

This choice is tempting because it rules out the possibility that the difference between the number of convicted wealthy parents and the number of convicted parents with average incomes resulted from a difference in the fractions of the parents in each group who actually had bribed the universities.Thus, this choice seems to strengthen the case for concluding that being wealthy confers an advantage in court.
Notice, however, that the argument does not rely on information about thefractionof parents in each category.The argument uses only information about theabsolute numbersof parents convicted.

Since neither the passage nor this choice provides information connecting what this choice says about the fraction of parents in each category with what the passage says about the absolute number of parents in each category, the relationship between what this choice says and the facts presented by the passage is not clear.Thus, this choice does not strengthen the argument.
(D) On average, the wealthy parents paid about $327,000 in defense attorney’s fees, whereas the parents with average incomes paid approximately $76,000.

Is the fact that wealthy defendants paid more in attorney fees additional evidence that being wealthy confers an advantage in court? Without information on the effect on trial outcomes of that difference in fees paid, we don’t have a clear reason to take this choice as additional evidence that being wealthy confers an advantage.
For all we know from what the passage says and what this choice says, that difference in attorney fees paid didn’t make any difference in the outcomes of the trials.

Furthermore, it could be that the wealthy parents paid more in attorney fees because their trials were, for some reason, longer or more complex than the other parents’ trials.It could even be that wealthy parents paid more in attorney fees because, for some reason, being wealthy isdisadvantageousin court.
Without information on why the wealthy parents paid more in attorney fees or what the effects of their paying more were, there is no clear reason to believe that this choice strengthens the argument.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) The number of parents with average incomes accused of bribing universities was no greater than the number of wealthy parents accused of doing so.

Imagine if we were given information that, in contrast to what this choice says, the number of parents with average incomes who were accused of bribing universities WAS much higher than the number of wealthy parents accused.Surely, that information would weaken the argument, since we’d have plausible reason to believe that fewer wealthy parents were convicted simply because fewer wealthy parents were accused, not because they were wealthy.
Therefore, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out that plausible alternative explanation for why fewer wealthy parents were found guilty.
Remember, if we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim, we can strengthen the case for that claim by ruling out an alternative explanation for the observed pattern of events.

question.text: Researchers found that the brain chemistry of young people addicted to smartphones is imbalanced.Test results showed that the level of the chemical GABA in the brains of those young people was higher than the level in the brains of other young people, and that the level of the chemical Glx was lower than normal.Researchers also found that the proportion of young people addicted to smartphones who were considered depressed was higher than the proportion of young people not addicted to smartphones who were considered depressed.Therefore, it’s clear that smartphone addiction causes imbalanced brain chemistry and depression in young people.All of the following, if true, strengthen the argument, EXCEPT:

answers.text: It was determined that a combination of excessive GABA and insufficient Glx in a young person’s brain does not cause both smartphone addiction and depression.
It was determined that lower than normal levels of Glx in young people do not cause higher than normal levels of GABA or depression.
It was determined that young people are less likely to be depressed and less likely to have relatively high GABA and relatively low Glx levels if smartphones are not available.
It was determined that a young person who is depressed is more likely to be addicted to smartphones than is a young person who is not depressed.
It was determined that, by reducing their use of smartphones, young people can cause the levels of GABA and Glx in their brains to become normal and can ease depression.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: In the brains of young people addicted to smartphones, the level of the chemical GABA was higher than the level in the brains other young people, and the level of the chemical Glx were lower.
Premise: The proportion of young people addicted to smartphones who were considered depressed was higher than the proportion of young people not addicted to smartphones who were considered depressed.
Conclusion: Smartphone addiction causes imbalanced brain chemistry and depression in young people.
Notice that we have a cause-and-effect argument: the author claims that smartphone addiction causes imbalanced brain chemistry and depression in young people.
As you know well at this point, the fact that two variables occur together does not allow us to conclude that one of the variables is the cause of the other.

In this “EXCEPT” question, four answers strengthen the cause-and-effect link between smartphone addiction and imbalanced brain chemistry and depression in young people; those answers will be incorrect.The remaining answer willnotstrengthen the support for the author’s cause-and-effect conclusion; that answer will be correct.

Keep in mind that “not strengthening” an argument does not necessarily mean weakening an argument.Rather, not strengthening simply means not adding support.An answer choice could “not strengthen” by having no effect on the argument or by weakening the argument.

Also, to make sure that we choose the non-strengthening choice, we have to be careful to remember what kind of choice we need and not switch to choosing a strengthener in the process of going through the choices.
(A) It was determined that a combination of excessive GABA and insufficient Glx in a young person’s brain does not cause both smartphone addiction and depression.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for the cause and effect.

The argument claims that smartphone addiction is the cause of excessive GABA, insufficient Glx, and depression, but what if the direction of the cause and effect were reversed? What if the author has the cause and effect wrong, and excessive GABA and insufficient Glx causes smartphone addiction and depression, instead of the other way around? In that situation, the author’s conclusion would be invalid.

Since what this answer choice says serves to RULE OUT the possibility that that alternative, reversed, explanation of the cause and effect is correct, this choice strengthens the support for the argument’s conclusion.
Strengthens? Yes.
(B) It was determined that lower than normal levels of Glx in young people do not cause higher than normal levels of GABA or depression.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out an alternative explanation for the cause and effect.

The argument claims that smartphone addiction is the cause of excessive GABA, insufficient Glx, and depression, but what if, instead, lower than normal levels of Glx cause higher than normal levels of GABA and cause depression? That fact would conflict with the author’s conclusion.
Since what this answer choice says serves to RULE OUT that alternate cause-and-effect scenario, this choice strengthens the case for the cause and effect that the argument lays out.
Strengthens? Yes.
(C) It was determined that young people are less likely to be depressed and less likely to have relatively high GABA and relatively low Glx levels if smartphones are not available.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by showing that, when the stated cause is eliminated, the stated effect is also eliminated, and thereby making the cause-effect link more plausible.

If young people are less likely to be depressed and to have chemical imbalances if smartphones are not available, then we have more conviction in the argument’s conclusion because this fact demonstrates a clear cause-and-effect relationship between smartphone use and depression and chemical imbalances.
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, if we can show that, when we take away the cause, we also take away the effect, the author’s claim is stronger.
Strengthens? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) It was determined that a young person who is depressed is more likely to be addicted to smartphones than is a young person who is not depressed.

What came first, the chicken or the egg? This answer choice basically describes a chicken-or-the-egg scenario – in other words, a scenario in which the stated cause is just as likely to be the effect as it is to actually be the cause.

In this case, did the depressed young people become addicted to their smartphones because they were depressed, or did they become depressed because they were addicted to their smartphones? In other words, did depression cause the addiction, or did the addiction cause depression? From the information given in this answer choice, we have no way to know, and thus we can’t say that this information strengthens the argument.
Strengthens? No.
(E) It was determined that, by reducing their use of smartphones, young people can cause the levels of GABA and Glx in their brains to become normal and can ease depression.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by showing that, when the presence of the cause is reduced, the effect ceases, and thereby making the cause-effect link more plausible.

If, by reducing their smartphone use, young people can correct chemical imbalances and ease depression, then we have a very clear cause-and-effect relationship between smartphone use and chemical imbalances and depression.
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, if we can show that, when we take away the cause, we also take away the effect, the author’s claim is stronger.
Strengthens? Yes.

question.text: In the 1930s, United States President Franklin Delano Roosevelt responded to the economic calamity of the Great Depression by instituting a set of programs and regulations known as the New Deal.While federal spending on New Deal programs was said to be focused where it was most needed, some historians believe that Roosevelt was as interested in gaining votes as he was in restoring the country’s economy.These historians cite the fact that a large amount of New Deal federal spending went to key states that Roosevelt needed to win in order to secure reelection.Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the historians’ hypothesis?

answers.text: A large amount of New Deal federal spending went to relatively affluent states where incomes remained higher than those in other states.
The high taxes necessary for funding New Deal programs took money from the hands of businesses and consumers, prolonging the period of high unemployment associated with the Great Depression.
The states whose representatives in the federal government had the greatest seniority received disproportionately large amounts of New Deal federal spending.
There was relatively little New Deal federal spending in states where overwhelming majorities of people had voted for Roosevelt in the last election.
A significant portion New Deal federal spending involved providing loans that aided the survival of large private businesses such as banks and railroad companies.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: United States President Franklin Delano Roosevelt responded to the economic calamity of the Great Depression by instituting a set of programs and regulations known as the New Deal.
Premise: A large amount of New Deal federal spending went to key states that Roosevelt needed to win in order to secure reelection.
Conclusion: Roosevelt was as interested in gaining votes as he was in restoring the country’s economy.

Some historians have concluded that New Deal spending was influenced by Roosevelt's desire to gain votes in states he needed to win for reelection.The correct answer to this Strengthen question will give us more reason to believe that Roosevelt was interested in gaining votes.
To arrive at that answer, we have to be careful not to select a trap choice that supports another conclusion about New Deal spending that is not the conclusion we need to support.
(A) A large amount of New Deal federal spending went to relatively affluent states where incomes remained higher than those in other states.

This choice is tempting because it seems to provide some support for the conclusion that New Deal spending was poorly directed or even misdirected.However, that conclusion is not the one we need to support.

Notice that we are not told whether the relatively affluent states that received a large amount of New Deal spending were states that Roosevelt needed to win for reelection.So, we don’t have a clear way to connect the states mentioned in this choice to the states mentioned in the argument.
Thus, this choice does not provide support for the conclusion that Roosevelt used New Deal spending to try to gain votes.
Be careful not to select a choice that seems to go in the same direction as an argument's conclusion but does not actually support what the conclusion says.
(B) The high taxes necessary for funding New Deal programs took money from the hands of businesses and consumers, prolonging the period of high unemployment associated with the Great Depression.

This choice could be tempting because it supports the general conclusion that there were issues with New Deal spending, by indicating that the taxing done to fund the spending resulted in the prolongation of unemployment.

However, the conclusion that we have to support is not the general conclusion that there were issues with New Deal spending.We need to support a specific conclusion that Roosevelt used New Deal spending to gain votes.
So, this choice supports the wrong conclusion.
(C) The states whose representatives in the federal government had the greatest seniority received disproportionately large amounts of New Deal federal spending.

This choice is tempting because it seems to provide some support for the conclusion that New Deal spending went to certain states for reasons other than doing what was best for restoring the economy, and that conclusion is similar to the conclusion that we have to support.

However, that conclusion is not actually the one that we have to support; we don’t have to support a general conclusion that the funds went to states for the wrong reasons.We have to support the more specific conclusion that the spending went to states for one wrong reason in particular: Roosevelt was seeking to use New Deal spending to gain votes.
So, this choice supports the wrong conclusion.

(D)Correct.The conclusion that Roosevelt directed spending to gain votes is based on information about which states the funding went to: the funding went to key states that Roosevelt needed to win to secure reelection.

Now, why wouldn’t Roosevelt have directed money to states where overwhelming majorities of people had voted for him in the last election? Well, given the way the people in those states voted in the last election, it was likely that the overwhelming majority of them werealreadyRoosevelt supporters.Thus, they would likely vote for him again, and for the purposes of gaining votes, New Deal spending would be more strategically allocated to states where people still needed to be persuaded to vote for him.

So, the fact that there was little New Deal federal spending in states where Roosevelt already had substantial voter support strengthens the conclusion that Roosevelt directed spending to gain votes because it shows that the states whose votes he likely didn’t need to gain didn’t get much money.
(E) A significant portion New Deal federal spending involved providing loans that aided the survival of large private businesses such as banks and railroad companies.

This choice could be tempting because it appears to support a conclusion that is seemingly related to the historians' conclusion: that Roosevelt attempted to gain the votes of business owners by providing them with loans that helped their businesses survive.

Notice, however, that this choice does not tell us which states those businesses were based in.Consequently, we have no way to connect the businesses mentioned in this choice to the states mentioned in the argument.

So, while we might assume that large private businesses represented an important voting bloc for Roosevelt, for all we know, Roosevelt already had their support in the last election.Thus, this choice does nothing to support the historians' conclusion that Roosevelt directed spending to gain votes.

question.text: To get people who have been reluctant to reduce or eliminate their consumption of meat to move to following plant-based diets, the Magical Meat company has developed meat-like products that are pea and rice protein based.The company’s founders are confident that, once Magical Meat’s products are widely available, many people who would not otherwise have done soin the short termwill make the switch to plant-based diets, because the difference between the appearance, taste, and feel of Magical Meat’s products and those of meat is minimal.Which of the following, if true, provides the most support for the expectation of the founders of Magical Meat?

answers.text: Magical Meat has been able to make its products appear, taste, and feel minimally different from meat because of certain properties of coconut oil and cocoa butter.
Much of the reason why people are reluctant to switch from diets including meat to plant-based diets is that they are convinced that all plant-based foods contain inadequate quantities of protein.
Digestive problems that many people experience after consuming whole peas are not often experienced by people who have consumed pea protein.
Many people who follow plant-based diets prefer Magical Meat’s products to other similar products.
Because pea protein and rice protein are readily available in great quantities, Magical Meat will be able to produce as much of its products as is necessary to make them widely available.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Background: To get people who have been reluctant to reduce or eliminate their consumption of meat to move to following plant-based diets, the Magical Meat company has developed meat-like products that are pea and rice protein based.
Premise: The difference between the appearance, taste, and feel of Magical Meat’s products those of and meat is minimal.

Conclusion: The company’s founders are confident that, once Magical Meat’s products are widely available, many people who would not otherwise have done soin the short termwill make the switch to plant-based diets.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Magical Meat has been able to make its products appear, taste, and feel minimally different from meat because of certain properties of coconut oil and cocoa butter.

This choice could be tempting because it is closely related to the premise used to support the conclusion: the difference between the appearance, taste, and feel of Magical Meat’s products and meat is minimal.
Notice, however, that this choice does not add information that strengthens the argument.

We already know from the passage that Magical Meat’s products are minimally different from meat.This choice merelyexplains whyMagical Meat’s products are minimally different from meat: Magical Meat has been able to make the products minimally different from meatbecause of certain properties of coconut oil and cocoa butter.
Remember, we have to take the premises of arguments as true, and explaining why something is true does not make it any truer.
So, this choice, which merely partly explains the premise used to support the conclusion, has no effect on the strength of the support for the conclusion.

(B) Much of the reason why people are reluctant to switch from diets including meat to plant-based diets is that they are convinced that all plant-based foods contain inadequate quantities of protein.

We could be tricked into choosing this choice by the fact that it mentions people's being convinced that all plant-based foods contain inadequate quantities of protein, and the passage says that Magic Meat’s products are pea protein and rice protein based.We might think something along the lines of, “The fact that the products are pea protein and rice protein based solves the problem of people’s being convinced that all plant-based foods lack protein.So, by indicating that that perception is the reason why people don’t follow plant-based diets, this choice strengthens the argument.”

The idea that the products would solve the problem of that perception is a story that sounds sort of right, but isn’t actually supported by the information that we have.Is the fact that these plant-based products are plant-protein based enough to change people’s perceptions of plant-based foods? Nothing in the passage or this answer choice provides evidence for that outcome.For all we know from the information given, people will continue to be convinced that all plant-based foods, including Magical Meat's products, contain inadequate quantities of protein.

Furthermore, what this answer choice says could actually weaken the argument, since the company’s founders conclude that people “will make the switch to plant-based diets, because the difference between theappearance, taste, and feelof Magical Meat’s products and meat is minimal.” So, if what this answer choice says is true, andmuch of the reason whypeople are reluctant to switch to plant-based diets is because they they are convinced that those diets includeinadequate protein, then meat-like “appearance, taste, and feel” may not be enough to convince these people to switch.
So, given all the facts we know, this choice adds no support for the conclusion and may even weaken the support for conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Digestive problems that many people experience after consuming whole peas are not often experienced by people who have consumed pea protein.

This choice strengthens the case for expecting that the availability of Magical Meat’s products will cause people to switch to plant-based diets by addressing a possible issue that would make the company’s expectation seem more unrealistic, the digestive issues that follow consumption of peas.

Given what this choice says, we now know that people won’tnotmake the switch because of digestive issues.So, with one less reason for them not to make the switch, we have at least some greater conviction that they will make the switch.Of course, there is no guarantee, but the argument’s conclusion is at least more likely to be correct.
(D) Many people who follow plant-based diets prefer Magical Meat’s products to other similar products.

This choice could be tempting because it supports a conclusion closely related to the argument’s conclusion.This choice supports the conclusion that Magical Meat’s products will be consumed in large quantities.

However, we have to keep track of the conclusion that we have to support, which is that the availability of Magical Meat’s products will result in people’s switching to plant-based diets, and this choice does not support that conclusion at all.This choice is not about people who would switch.This choice is about people who are already following plant-based diets.
(E) Because pea protein and rice protein are readily available in great quantities, Magical Meat will be able to produce as much of its products as is necessary to make them widely available.
This choice could be tempting because it supports a conclusion related to the argument’s conclusion.This choice supports the conclusion that Magical Meat’s products will be widely available.
However, we have to keep track of the conclusion that we’re looking to support, which is not that Magical Meat’s products will be widely available.

The conclusion we have to support is that, once Magical Meat’s products are widely available, people will switch to plant-based diets.In other words, the argument’s conclusion is not about WHETHER Magical Meat’s products will be available.The argument’s conclusion presumes that the productswillbe available and is about the RESULT of their being available: people will switch.
So, this choice supports the wrong conclusion and does not add support for the conclusion of the argument at hand.

question.text: In response to the passage of a certain new law by the City of Redville, StarMediaCom, a leading provider of phone and internet services, has decided to stop offering telecom services in Redville.Because phone and internet services are vitally important to not only the health of all businesses in Redville but also the wellbeing of its residents, Redville residents will soon begin pressuring the government to repeal or revise the law.Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument above?

answers.text: As an indirect result of StarMediaCom’s ceasing to offer services in Redville, many Redville residents not currently aware of the new law will become aware of it.
Without phone and internet services, the businesses where many of Redville's residents work would find it difficult to conduct daily business operations.
If a sufficiently large number of Redville's residents were to mobilize and pressure the city government, it would take the residents seriously and consider repealing or revising the new law.
Redville residents who prefer StarMediaCom to other service providers will become aware of why StarMediaCom stopped offering telecom services.
By providing telecom services in other cities, StarMediaCom earns enough revenue that its profitability will not be significantly affected by its ceasing to offer telecom services in Redville.

explanation_parts.text: YOUR CHOICE(A) As an indirect result of StarMediaCom’s ceasing to offer services in Redville, many Redville residents not currently aware of the new law will become aware of it.

Simply becoming aware of the new law does not mean that residents will connect the law to StarMediaCom’s decision to stop providing services.Moreover, even if residents did make that connection, what this answer choice says does not give us any reason to believe that they would pressure the government to repeal or revise the law.

It could be that more Redville residents will become aware of the new law, yet they will not know that StarMediaCom’s actions are related to the law or not care enough about the law to complain about it.It could even be that, as more people find out about the law, the law gains supporters rather than opponents.
A large gulf exists between awareness and action, and this answer choice gives us no reason to believe that the former would lead to the latter.
(B) Without phone and internet services, the businesses where many of Redville's residents work would find it difficult to conduct daily business operations.

Notice that the passage already says that “phone and internet services are vitally important to not only the health of all businesses in Redville but also the wellbeing of its residents.” Therefore, what this answer choice says, that “without phone and internet services, the businesses where many of Redville's residents work would find it difficult to conduct daily business operations,” is not new information.
Rather, what this answer choice says simply elaborates on a fact already stated in the passage.Watch out for trap answers that simply restate or confirm a given premise.
(C) If a sufficiently large number of Redville's residents were to mobilize and pressure the city government, it would take the residents seriously and consider repealing or revising the new law.

If you selected this answer choice, you may have done so because you misread the argument’s conclusion.Notice that the argument’s conclusion is that Redville residents “will soon begin pressuring the government to repeal or revise the law.” The author does not make any claim about whether residents will be effective in pressuring the government.
In other words, the conclusion is not that pressure from residents will result in the repeal or revision of the law.

Therefore, what this answer choice says strengthens a different conclusion.The conclusion that we must strengthen is focused on the actions that the residents will take, not the actions that the government will take.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Redville residents who prefer StarMediaCom to other service providers will become aware of why StarMediaCom stopped offering telecom services.

The passage says thatStarMediaCom has decided to stop offering telecom services in Redville in response to the law.This argument assumes that Redville residents will know why StarMediaCom has halted the phone and internet services, and therefore want the law repealed or revised.

What this answer choice says addresses that assumption.If Redville residents who prefer StarMediaCom become aware that the company stopped providing services because of the city government’s new law, then we have good reason to believe that those residents will pressure the government to repeal or revise the law, so that their preferred services can resume.

Notice that this choice goes a step further than choice (A), which does not directly address the assumption made in the argument, that the residents will connect the law to StarMediaCom’s decision and thus be opposed to the law.
This choice confirms the argument’s assumption as a fact, and thus strengthens the argument.
(E) By providing telecom services in other cities, StarMediaCom earns enough revenue that its profitability will not be significantly affected by its ceasing to offer telecom services in Redville.
The author makes no claim that revenue is a factor in StarMediaCom’s decision-making.

Furthermore, the conclusion is about how StarMediaCom’s decision will affect residents (it will prompt them to pressure the government), not how StarMediaCom’s decision will affect the company.Whether StarMediaCom’s profitability is or is not significantly affected by its decision to stop offering telecom services in Redville, the fact remains that it has decided to stop services.
So, this answer choice makes the author’s conclusion no more or less plausible.

question.text: Business Software Company Z is considering extending the length of its technical support contracts from two years to three years in order to differentiate itself from competitors and attract more customers.Since purchasers of the company’s products receive the contracted technical support at no additional cost, Company Z is concerned that the costs associated with providing an additional year of technical support will reduce its profits.Which of the following strategies would be most likely to maximize the profits of Company Z if it extends the length of its technical support contracts?

answers.text: Sell only to customers who plan to use the software for six years or more.
Offer training on how to troubleshoot its software to customers’ technical staff.
Remove advanced features from its software in order to reduce the likelihood of customers’ needing technical support.
Offer customers the option of purchasing the company’s software at a discounted price if they choose a two-year service contract rather than a three-year service contract.
Mention the extension of the service contracts in the company’s marketing materials.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Business Software Company Z is considering extending the length of its technical support contracts from two years to three years in order to differentiate itself from competitors and attract more customers.
Premise: Purchasers of the company’s products receive the contracted technical support at no additional cost.
Conclusion: Company Z is concerned that the costs associated with providing an additional year of technical support will reduce its profits.

Company Z is concerned that the cost of providing extended technical support will reduce its profits, and so, the strategy that will maximize Company Z’s profits will be the one that addresses or offsets that cost.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Sell only to customers who plan to use the software for six years or more.
How long a company uses the software after the three years of technical support has expired will not have any impact on the profit made on the original sale.

In fact, since the argument states that offering technical support for longer likely will serve to reduce profits, Company Z would presumably minimize support costs by selling to customers who will use the software (and, therefore, the technical support) forlessthan three years.Thus, selling only to customers who plan to use the software for six years or more would not be a viable strategy for maximizing profits.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Offer training on how to troubleshoot its software to customers’ technical staff.

If the customers’ technical staff can troubleshoot problems with the software, the customer will be less likely to call Company Z for help.Since the argument states that offering an additional year of technical support is likely to reduce profits, if customers don’t use the technical support, then profits can be maximized.

The cost of training customers’ technical staff would offset some of the savings realized by training them, but of the strategies described in the answer choices, this one is the one most likely to maximize profits.
(C) Remove advanced features from its software in order to reduce the likelihood of customers’ needing technical support.
It is possible that this strategy could increase profits, but it may not.

Removing advanced features from the software may reasonably be expected to result in a reduction in the amount of technical support required.At the same time, removing features would likely reduce the desirability of the software, and hence sales or the selling price of the software.In other words, removing advanced features from the software may result in significantly reduced sales or may mean that the software must be sold at a significantly lower price.These effects would have negative impacts on profitability, which impacts would offset, and perhaps even exceed, savings on technical support costs.

Since a design change affects multiple aspects of the “profit margin” calculation, we cannot reliably accept what this answer choice says as the strategy most likely to maximize the company’s profits; we simply don’t have enough information to assess this option.
(D) Offer customers the option of purchasing the company’s software at a discounted price if they choose a two-year service contract rather than a three-year service contract.

Because the effect of this strategy could be to influence some customers to choose the shorter support contract, this strategy could be perceived as mitigating the costs of the longer support contracts that will serve to reduce profit margins.

However, since this strategy involves selling at lower prices, it could be even more deleterious to profits than the extended technical support would be.In fact, the company could end up in the following situation.

It would be selling some of the software with the previous, two-year support contract, but at a discounted price, a setup that doesn’t seem good for profits.It would be selling the rest of the software with a three-year support contract that we already know may serve to reduce profits.We can see that that situation may be even worse than selling all of the software with a three-year support contract.

Perhaps if we knew how much Company Z would have to reduce prices in order to incentivize purchases with shorter contracts, we could determine the effectiveness of this strategy, but as it stands, the potential is great for this strategy to have the opposite of the intended effect.
(E) Mention the extension of the service contracts in the company’s marketing materials.

While mentioning the longer contracts as part of its marketing strategy might help the company to sell more software, such marketing does not clearly solve the problem at hand, which is that the costs of technical support associated with each unit of software sold may increase as a result of the company’s offering longer contracts.

Even if mentioning the longer contracts would result in the company’s selling more software, the costs of the service contracts associated with the software sold may have still a negative impact on profits that offsets or exceeds the positive effects of increased software sales.
So, it’s not clear that mentioning the longer contracts in marketing materials would solve the company’s problem.

question.text: In general, cars that are constantly accelerating and decelerating burn much more fuel than and produce much more air pollution than is produced by cars that maintain constant speeds.Therefore, in order to reduce air pollution, the city of Verkerdorf smoothed traffic flow by linking all of the traffic signal lights in the city via a central computer system.As a result, traffic in Verkerdorf moves much more smoothly than it did before the lights were linked.However, after the project was completed, air pollution in Verkerdorf increased rather than decreased.Which of the following, if true, best explains why the result was the opposite of what had been sought?

answers.text: Buses traveling on the streets of Verkerdorf use their own lanes and at some intersections are given priority over cars by traffic signal lights.
People who used to avoid driving in Verkerdorf because of street congestion are now happy to drive in Verkerdorf.
At peak travel times, some streets in Verkerdorf still become congested, with cars stopping and starting constantly.
Linking the traffic signal lights had essentially no effect on traffic flows on the highways leading to Verkerdorf.
Taxis in Verkerdorf often temporarily block a lane of traffic when they stop to pick up or let out people.

explanation_parts.text: Background:In general, cars that are constantly accelerating and decelerating burn much more fuel and produce much more air pollution than cars that maintain constant speeds do.

Fact 1: In order to reduce air pollution, the city of Verkerdorf smoothed traffic flow by linking all the traffic signal lights in the city via a central computer system, and,as a result, traffic in Verkerdorf moves much more smoothly than it did before the lights were linked.
Fact 2: After the project was completed, air pollution in Verkerdorf increased rather than decreased.
What has to be explained: Why, after a project to make traffic flow more smoothly was completed, did air pollution in Verkerdorf increase rather than decrease?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain anincrease.
(A) Buses traveling on the streets of Verkerdorf use their own lanes and at some intersections are given priority over cars by traffic signal lights.
This choice could be tempting because it mentions traffic signal lights, which were a key component of the city’s method of smoothing traffic flow.

However, regardless of what type of vehicle is given priority by traffic signals, the facts that we were given in the passage remain: traffic signals were linked via a central computer system, resulting in a smoother flow of traffic in Verkerdorf.

What this answer choice says about buses would be true with or without the smoother traffic flow.So, we’re still wondering why pollution increased, rather than decreased, despite the smoother traffic flow.
YOUR CHOICE(B) People who used to avoid driving in Verkerdorf because of street congestion are now happy to drive in Verkerdorf.

If people who used to avoid driving in Verkerdorf because of street congestion are now happy to drive in Verkerdorf, then we understand why air pollution in Verkerdorf increased rather than decreased.

Yes, the smoother traffic flow reduced pollution.But, because of the smoother traffic flow, people who used to avoid driving in Verkerdorf are now driving there.Of course, more cars would translate to increased pollution.This choice resolves the paradox.
(C) At peak travel times, some streets in Verkerdorf still become congested, with cars stopping and starting constantly.

The passage states that “traffic in Verkerdorf moves much more smoothly than it did before the lights were linked.” That fact is given to us as true, and so it must take into account any remaining instances of traffic congestion.In other words, traffic congestion at certain times does not override the fact that, in general, the traffic flow has improved since the lights were linked.Thus, what this answer choice says does not resolve the paradox.
(D) Linking the traffic signal lights had essentially no effect on traffic flows on the highways leading to Verkerdorf.

Regardless of whether linking the traffic signal lights improved traffic flows on the highways leading to Verkerdorf, the fact remains that “traffic in Verkerdorf moves much more smoothly than it did before the lights were linked.” So, even if the linkage had no effect on the highway traffic, the traffic in the city now flows better, and we should still expect to see at least some decline in pollution, given that improvement.Thus, what this answer choice says does not resolve the paradox.
(E) Taxis in Verkerdorf often temporarily block a lane of traffic when they stop to pick up or let out people.
This choice could be tempting because it describes a scenario in which the flow of traffic would be interrupted.

However, the passage states that “traffic in Verkerdorf moves much more smoothly than it did before the lights were linked.” That fact is given to us as true, and so it must take into account any remaining instances of reduced traffic flow.In other words, reduced traffic flow in certain scenarios does not override the fact that, in general, traffic flow has improved since the lights were linked.
So, we’re still left wondering why there is now more air pollution.This answer choice does not provide information to resolve that paradox.

question.text: Two years ago, citizens of Zanzan voted to remove its then leader, who, for decades, had used religious doctrine to craft oppressive laws, including ones making divorce difficult, and instate a president who quickly acted to eliminate many of the country’s archaic laws, including the laws that had made divorce difficult.Although it is known that 50 percent of all women in Zanzan were unhappy in their marriages, since the instatement of the new president, the country has seen its divorce rate fall significantly, to its lowest level since the country began tracking such statistics nearly 40 years ago.Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain this surprising turn of events?

answers.text: Historically, less than 50 percent of all married men in Zanzan have been unhappy in their marriages.
Traditional family customs in Zanzan make it difficult for women in unhappy marriages to seek assistance with filing for divorce.
The removal of archaic, hard line religious doctrine from Zanzan’s laws improved the personal and professional lives of its women, making them generally more content in their lives.
For many years, the leader of Zanzan had the support of the people in crafting oppressive laws.
Many women in Zanzan choose to follow religious doctrine even without laws in place to compel them to do so.

explanation_parts.text: Background: Two years ago, citizens of Zanzan voted to remove its then leader, who, for decades, had used religious doctrine to craft oppressive laws, including ones making divorce difficult, and instate a president who quickly acted to eliminate many of the country’s archaic laws, including the laws that had made divorce difficult.
Fact 1: 50 percent of all women in Zanzan were unhappy in their marriages.
Fact 2: Since the instatement of the new president, the country has seen its divorce rate fall significantly, to its lowest level since the country began tracking such statistics nearly 40 years ago.

Picture it: For a long time, women in Zanzan, 50 percent of whom were unhappy in their marriages, could not get a divorce.Now suddenly, during a time in which divorce is possible for these women, the country has seen its divorce rate fall significantly to its lowest level since the country began tracking such statistics nearly 40 years ago.Why?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain adecrease.
(A) Historically, less than 50 percent of all married men in Zanzan have been unhappy in their marriages.

Notice that this argument is centered on women, how 50 percent were unhappy in their marriages, and how, now that divorce is easier, the divorce rate has fallen to the lowest level in almost 40 years.Therefore, knowing that historically, less than 50 percent of all married men in Zanzan have been unhappy in their marriages does not help us to resolve the paradox.

Regardless of whether the men were unhappy in their marriages, the women were.Given that fact, what would have seemed most likely is that, with the elimination of the laws making divorce difficult, the divorce rate would have increased.

Now, it could have made sense that, since less than 50 percent of men were unhappy in their marriages, the divorce rate would haveremained about the sameafter the elimination of the laws making divorce difficult.Perhaps the men, less than 50 percent of whom were unhappy in their marriages, could have kept their marriages together even though most of the women weren’t happy.
However, the fact that less than 50 percent of men were unhappy in their marriages would not explain why the divorce ratedecreased.
(B) Traditional family customs in Zanzan make it difficult for women in unhappy marriages to seek assistance with filing for divorce.

This choice is tempting, because the fact that traditional family customs in Zanzan make it difficult for women to seek assistance with filing for divorce could seem to explain why the women are not getting divorces although they now can.

Notice, however, presumably this fact was true before the laws making divorce difficult were eliminated.So, this choice is about something that has not changed.The traditional family customs have existed all along.
It does not make sense that something that has existed all along would have suddenly caused a decrease in the divorce rate.

Now, it would make sense that the existence of these traditional family customs would have caused the divorce rate toremain about the sameafter the laws making divorce difficult were eliminated.We could see how it could be that, even though the laws no longer existed, the family customs could have kept women from getting divorced.
However, the continued existence of these customs does not explain what we need to explain, which is not that the divorce rate remained the same but that it decreased.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The removal of archaic, hard line religious doctrine from Zanzan’s laws improved the personal and professional lives of its women, making them generally more content in their lives.
This choice is the only one that describes something that would have resulted in a decrease in the divorce rate in Zanzan.

Given that more than 50 percent of women were unhappy in their marriages, what might reasonably have been expected after the laws making get divorced were eliminated was an increase in the divorce rate.So, why has the divorce rate decreased instead?
Well, this choice does not mention marriage or divorce, but it does mention something that could have caused a decrease in the divorce rate, women’s becoming more content in their lives.

Does someone’s becoming more content necessarily keep that person from getting divorced.No, but becoming more content in life could cause someone to become less likely to get divorced and might even affect how happy that person is in her marriage.

So, what this choice says about women becoming generally more content as a result of the elimination of the oppressive laws could explain why the divorce rate has decreased.Yes, it is easier to get divorced, and maybe some people have gotten divorced as a result of the change in laws.At the same time, it could be that many women remained in marriages in which they would not have remained had the laws not been eliminated, because, as a result of the elimination of the laws, they became more content overall in their lives.
We can see that, unlike any other choice in this question, this choice could help to explain the decrease in the divorce rate and, thus, resolve the apparent paradox.
(D) For many years, the leader of Zanzan had the support of the people in crafting oppressive laws.
That, for many years, the leader of Zanzan had the support of the people in crafting oppressive laws does not help us to resolve the paradox.
Yes, for many years, the people supported him and the oppressive laws, but now they don’t, and the laws have been eliminated.

In order to explain the decrease in the divorce rate we need new information on the situation existing when the divorce rate has decreased, not information on a different situation that existed before the occurrence of the events described in the passage.

This choice does not tell us anything new about the situation existing when the divorce rate decreased and, as a result, does not explain the decrease in the divorce rate or resolve the apparent paradox.
(E) Many women in Zanzan choose to follow religious doctrine even without laws in place to compel them to do so.
That many women in Zanzan choose to follow religious doctrine even without laws in place to compel them to do so does not help us to resolve the paradox.

We might reasonably presume, given what the passage says about religious doctrine, that the religious doctrine that the women mentioned in this choice follow somehow serves to prevent divorce.However, presumably these women were choosing to follow religious doctrineboth before and afterthe elimination of the laws making divorce difficult.So, presumably their following religious doctrine had been going on all along.
Thus, if their following religious doctrine would prevent them from getting divorced, it would have prevented it all along, not relatively suddenly caused the divorce rate to decrease.
Women’s following religious doctrine on a continuous basis would not explain why the divorce rate has decreased since the oppressive laws were eliminated.

question.text: The size and yields of grape vines can be increased through the application of synthetic chemical fertilizers, which provide nutrients necessary for the health of the vines.However, during the recent drought, vineyard owners observed that the vines not treated with such fertilizer handled the hot, dry conditions better than those treated with fertilizer did.Which of the following, if true, would best explain the surprising result?

answers.text: Some vineyard owners used fertilizer in concentrations much lower than those recommended by the fertilizer manufacturer.
The over-application of fertilizer can weaken plants, making them susceptible to disease and to insect attacks.
In the absence of fertilizer, vines grow deeper roots in order to find nutrients in the soil.
Water is as important as any nutrient for the health of grape vines.
Vineyard owners who use organic methods do not use synthetic chemical fertilizers to treat the soil in which vines grow.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: The size and yields of grape vines can be increased through the application of synthetic chemical fertilizers, which provide nutrients necessary for the health of the vines.
Fact 2: During the recent drought, vineyard owners observed that the vines not treated with such fertilizer handled the hot, dry conditions better than those treated with fertilizer did.

What has to be explained: Why is it that, even though synthetic chemical fertilizers provide grape vines with nutrients necessary for the health of the vines, and even though grape vines become larger and more productive if they are fertilized, vines not treated with such fertilizer handled hot, dry conditions better than vines treated with fertilizer did?
(A) Some vineyard owners used fertilizer in concentrations much lower than those recommended by the fertilizer manufacturer.

The passage says, “vineyard owners observed that the vinesnot treatedwith such fertilizer handled the hot, dry conditions better than thosetreatedwith fertilizer did.” In other words, some vines were treated and some were not.

Treating vines with a lower concentration of fertilizer is not the same as not treating vines with fertilizer.Thus, what this answer choice says, that some vineyard owners used fertilizer in concentrations much lower than those recommended by the manufacturer, does not really change the facts.There were vines thatwere treatedwith fertilizer, there were vines thatwerenot at all treatedwith the fertilizer, and the untreated vines handled the dry conditions better than the treated vines did.
Naturally, since this choice does not materially change what we already knew, it does not resolve the apparent paradox.
(B) The over-application of fertilizer can weaken plants, making them susceptible to disease and to insect attacks.
This answer choice could be tempting, because it could seem to explain why the vines treated with fertilizer did not do as well as the others.

Notice, however, that we are not given any reason to believe that the vineyard owners described in the passage actually over-applied fertilizer to the vines they treated with it.So, in order for us to use this choice to explain why the treated vines didn’t do as well, we’d have to make an unsupported leap, going from “treated with fertilizer” to “over-application of fertilizer” without any clear reason for doing so.

Furthermore, in order to use this choice to explain why the fertilized vines did not do as well as the others did in dry conditions, we’d have to assume that “being susceptible to disease and to insect” attacks is connected to vines’ not doing well in dry conditions.
Since there’s no way to connect what this choice says to the facts outlined in the passage without making unsupported leaps, this answer choice does not resolve the paradox.
YOUR CHOICE(C) In the absence of fertilizer, vines grow deeper roots in order to find nutrients in the soil.

If, in the absence of fertilizer, vines grow deeper roots in order to find nutrients in the soil, then we can see why unfertilized vines would do better in dry conditions than fertilized vines.The unfertilized vines would have deeper roots that would enable them to do better in drought-like conditions than vines with shallower roots would.Thus, what this answer choice says explains the “surprising result” and resolves the apparent paradox.

Now, some of you reading this explanation may feel that this answer’s being correct depends upon some outside information that some test-takers might not know – that the deeper a plant’s roots, the better those roots are at getting water from the soil.If you feel that way, this is a teachable moment for you.

First, realize that, if you think about it, this fact about the roots makes sense; it’s not a stretch to think that deeper roots would be better in drought-like conditions at getting water from the soil.Furthermore, and maybe more importantly, when taking the GMAT, test-takers need to make judgement calls on some questions.Not every GMAT verbal question will have a correct answer such that you are totally convinced it is correct.So, when four answers are clearly incorrect and one is conceivably correct, make the judgment call to go with the conceivably correct answer.
(D) Water is as important as any nutrient for the health of grape vines.

What this answer choice says indicates that water is as important as the nutrients provided by fertilizer.OK, but does that fact mean that adding fertilizer is a negative? No.It means only that water is important even if fertilizer is used.
Of course, water is important also if fertilizer isnotused.So, this choice does not mention any reason why adding fertilizer would make any difference.
Therefore, this choice does nothing to explain why the vines that are fertilized don’t do as well in dry conditions as the other vines do and does not address the paradox laid out in the passage.
(E) Vineyard owners who use organic methods do not use synthetic chemical fertilizers to treat the soil in which vines grow.

Notice that we do not have any reason to believe that organic methods, which do not use synthetic chemical fertilizers to treat the soil in which vines grow, are more favorable in drought conditions.So, while this choice may seem to say something significant, as in “Hmm.Maybe it’s the organic methods that make the difference,” we don’t have any firm basis for believing that this information on the use of organic methods explains why the vines to which synthetic fertilizer had been applied didn’t do as well in dry conditions as other vines.

Furthermore, even deciding that the vines that are not treated with synthetic fertilizer are grown via the use of organic methods does not make complete sense.The passage never says that any of the vines mentioned are grown via the use of organic methods.

Since this choice does not provide any information about how vines grown using organic methods fare in various climates, and since it’s not 100 percent clear that any of the vines mentioned in the passage are grown via the use of organic methods, this choice does not effectively address the “surprising result” we’re seeking to explain, that vines not treated with fertilizer did better in dry conditions, even though fertilizer provides key nutrients.

question.text: In Rusminia, there are only three companies that manufacture and sell laundry detergent.Recently, all three began investing significant amounts of money in advertising.Interestingly, after the companies began their advertising campaigns, the total profits from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia decreased rather than increased.Which of the following does the most to explain why the outcome was the reverse of what might be expected?

answers.text: The share of the laundry detergent market held by one of the companies increased, and the market shares of the other two both decreased as a result.
None of the three companies changed the appearance of the packaging of their laundry detergent after beginning their advertising campaigns.
Few people in Rusminia were influenced by the advertising to switch from using laundry detergent made by one of the three companies to using detergent made by another of them.
Since households tend to consistently use the same amount of laundry detergent, the total revenue from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia remained unchanged.
Much of the information mentioned in the companies’ advertisements was already available to people who were willing to do some research on their own.

explanation_parts.text: Background: In Rusminia, there are only three companies that manufacture and sell laundry detergent.
Fact 1: Recently, all three began investing significant amounts of money in advertising.
Fact 2: After the companies began their advertising campaigns, the total profits from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia decreased rather than increased.

We typically expect that significant money spent on advertising will increase, not decrease, total profits.So, what could explain why the three companies’ profits from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia decreased rather than increased after the companies began investing heavily in advertising?
(A) The share of the laundry detergent market held by one of the companies increased, and the market shares of the other two both decreased as a result.
The market is the market, regardless of which companies have which amount of market share.
The paradox we’re concerned with is whytotal profitsfrom selling laundry detergent in Rusminia decreased despite increased advertising.
This choice is about changes inthe proportionsof themarketthat the companies have.
It tells us nothing about increases or decreases in thetotal profitsof the three companies.
Therefore, this information provides no insight into why total profits decreased.
(B) None of the three companies changed the appearance of the packaging of their laundry detergent after beginning their advertising campaigns.

That none of the three companies changed the appearance of the packaging of their laundry detergent after beginning their advertising campaigns does not help us to explain why the total profits from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia decreased rather than increased.

After all, all three companies began investing significant amounts of money in advertising.So, even with the appearance of the packaging of their laundry detergent remaining constant, we’d still expect to see an increase in the total profits from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia.
(C) Few people in Rusminia were influenced by the advertising to switch from using laundry detergent made by one of the three companies to using detergent made by another of them.
The paradox we’re concerned with is why total profits from selling laundry detergent in Rusminiadecreaseddespite increased advertising.

Customer’s not switching might show that the advertisinghad little effectordid not cause change, but that advertising had little effect or did not cause change certainly does not explain why profits decreased.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Since households tend to consistently use the same amount of laundry detergent, the total revenue from selling laundry detergent in Rusminia remained unchanged.

This choice may seem to be clearly incorrect because it indicates that the advertising did not cause any change in revenue, and so, this choice may seem to have nothing to do with the fact that profits decreased.

However the truth is that, if, despite the new advertising campaigns, people in Rusminia continued to use the same amount of laundry detergent they used before the campaigns, then it makes sense that the total profits decreased, and here’s why.

Because customers continued to use the same amount of laundry detergent,the amount of revenuethat the companies receivedremained the same.However, the companies’expenses increasedbecause all three “began investing significant amounts of money in advertising.” So, profits, which equal revenues minus expenses, decreased.
In other words, if expenses go up, but revenue stays the same, profits will go down.
This choice clearly explains why total profits decreased and resolves the apparent paradox.
(E) Much of the information mentioned in the companies’ advertisements was already available to people who were willing to do some research on their own.
That much of the information mentioned in the companies’ advertisements was already available to people who were willing to do some research on their own does not explain a decrease in profits.

Do we have reason to believe that people were doing research on their own? Unless we have reason to believe that people were in fact researching and finding all the information that the advertising provided, the advertising could still have made a difference even if the information provided by the advertising was available somewhere to people willing to do research.

Now, if we were to take this choice to mean that the advertising provided people with little information that they didn’t already have, this choice could have explained why total profits remained the same, if profits had indeed remained the same.If the advertising provided people with little new information, then maybe people would have just continued to purchase laundry detergent as they had been before the advertising campaigns, because the advertising wouldn’t have given people any reason to change what they were doing.In that case, the advertising could have had no effect on profits.

However, we are not seeking to explain why profits remained the same.We have to explain why profits decreased, and the fact that, by doing some research, people could have found the information provided by the advertising does not explain why total profits decreased.

question.text: Physicians noticed that women in their care undergoing hormone replacement therapy, a treatment for addressing extreme changes in hormone levels, experienced heart disease significantly less often than women not undergoing the therapy.However, when formal studies were then undertaken to confirm that hormone replacement therapy prevents heart disease, the results were mixed and indicated that, in some cases, hormone replacement therapy may contribute to the development of heart disease.Which of the following, if true, would best explain why the results of the studies differed from what physicians had observed?

answers.text: The original reason for using hormone replacement therapy was not to prevent heart disease but rather to mitigate discomfort caused by changes in levels of hormones.
Many physicians who have worked with women using hormone replacement therapy have not specialized in the treatment of heart disease.
Most of the women who used hormone replacement therapy before its effectiveness in preventing heart disease was formally studied were women who were unusually careful about maintaining their health.
The women participating in the formal studies of hormone replacement therapy and heart disease did not pay for the study-related medical services that they received.
The relationship between hormone replacement therapy and heart disease has become better understood with years of study.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Physicians noticed that women in their care undergoing hormone replacement therapy tended to experience heart disease significantly less often than women not undergoing the therapy.

Fact 2: When formal studies were then undertaken to confirm that hormone replacement therapy prevents heart disease, the results were mixed and indicated that, in some cases, hormone replacement therapy may contribute to the development of heart disease.

What Has to Be Explained: Why was it that, in contrast to what might have been expected, when formal studies were undertaken to confirm that hormone replacement therapy prevents heart disease, the results were mixed and indicated that, in some cases, hormone replacement therapy may actually contribute to the development of heart disease?
(A) The original reason for using hormone replacement therapy was not to prevent heart disease but rather to mitigate discomfort caused by changes in levels of hormones.

The primary reason why this choice does not resolve the paradox is that it basically repeats what the passage says.Notice that the passage describes hormone replacement therapy as “a treatment for addressing extreme changes in hormone levels.” This choice says basically the same thing, with the small additions of saying that hormone replacement therapy is used to “mitigate discomfort” and that mitigating discomfort associated with changes in hormone levels was “the original reason” for using hormone replacement therapy.

Furthermore, information on the original reason for using hormone replacement therapy does not explain why hormone replacement therapy has or does not have certain effects.Hormone replacement therapy would have the effects that it has regardless of why people use it.
(B) Many physicians who have worked with women using hormone replacement therapy have not specialized in the treatment of heart disease.

If you choose this answer choice, you likely assumed something along the lines of that the fact that the physicians treating the women have not specialized in the treatment of heart disease was somehow the reason why the effectiveness of hormone replacement therapy in preventing heart disease seemed to be inconsistent.

However, the truth is that it is the hormone replacement therapy, not the physicians, that would or would not have prevented heart disease, and hormone replacement therapy would prevent heart disease, if it could, regardless of whether physicians administering the therapy specialize in the treatment of heart disease.
So, what this choice says about the physicians does not explain what we need to explain about the results of replacement therapy.

YOUR CHOICE(C) Most of the women who used hormone replacement therapy before its effectiveness in preventing heart disease was formally studied were women who were unusually careful about maintaining their health.

This choice resolves the paradox by showing that there is strong reason to believe that it was not hormone replacement therapy that was responsible for the relatively low rate of heart disease seen in the women who used hormone replacement therapy before its effectiveness in preventing heart disease was formally studied, and that there was another cause for that relatively low rate of heart disease.
Let’s think about what this choice says.

This choice tells us that the women who used hormone replacement therapy before its effectiveness in preventing heart disease was formally studied wereunusually careful about maintaining their health.Of course, when women are unusually careful about maintaining their health, we would expect them to experience fewer health problems such as heart disease.

So, by telling us that these women were unusually careful about maintaining their health, this choice indicates that,regardless of what the effects of hormone replacement therapy are, the women who used hormone replacement therapy before its effectiveness in preventing heart disease was formally studied would likely have had a relatively low rate of heart disease.

In other words, the cause of the relatively low rate of heart disease in those women could very well have been, not hormone replacement therapy, but rather their being usually careful about maintaining their health.

OK, so, what then happened was that hormone replacement therapy was formally studied, and the study results indicated that it doesn’t prevent heart disease, and now, we have an explanation for why those results were different from what might have been expected.

The explanation for why the results were different from what might have been expected is that hormone replacement therapy wasn’t preventing heart disease in the first place.Hormone replacement therapy onlyseemedto be preventing heart disease, and what was really going on was that the women’s being unusually careful about maintaining their health was preventing heart disease.Then, when hormone replacement therapy was formally studied, the factor “being unusually careful about maintaining their health” was not present, and heart disease was not prevented.
So, we can see that this choice explains why, when hormone replacement therapy was formally studied, the study results indicated that hormone replacement therapy does not prevent heart disease.
(D) The women participating in the formal studies of hormone replacement therapy and heart disease did not pay for the study-related medical services that they received.

Since there is no clearly supported reason to believe that paying for hormone replacement therapy would affect the effectiveness of that therapy, and since there’s no clearly supported reason to believe that the study participants’ not paying for hormone replacement therapy or other study-related medical services would have affected the results of the study, this choice does not explain why the study results were different from what might have been expected.
(E) The relationship between hormone replacement therapy and heart disease has become better understood with years of study.
What we are seeking to explain is why the results of formal studies of the effectiveness of hormone replacement therapy in preventing heart disease were different from what might have been expected.

The fact that, as this choice says, the relationship between hormone replacement therapy and heart disease has become better understood with years of study, would notexplainwhy the study results were different from what might have been expected.The results of the studies were what they were because of how the studies were set up, not because people have gained understanding over time.
People would gain understandingfromstudy results.The fact that people have gained understanding does not affect or explain study results.

question.text: Although the bodies of larger organisms contain more cells than are contained by those of smaller organisms, and although the incidence of cancer should almost certainly increase with the number of cells found in a body, biologists have observed that the incidence of cancer is much lower in blue whales, which have nearly 1,000 times as many cells as humans have, than it is in humans, and that the incidence of cancer in humans is lower than that in mice, which have about one one-thousandth as many cells as humans have.In general, scientists observe that animals with more cells have lower incidences of cancer than are had by animals with fewer cells.Each of the following, if true, helps to resolve the apparent paradox, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Animals with more cells have fewer proto oncogenes, which produce compounds that encourage malignant cancer cell growth.
Animals with more cells have a greater number of tumor suppressor genes, which, through a complex series of biochemical reactions, produce cancer fighting proteins.
Cancer incidences of many species of animals have not been well documented over the last fifty years, making it difficult to make comparisons of cancer incidences of these animals.
Animals with fewer cells have less immune surveillance, which is the immune system’s scanning for, and when necessary, fixing or eliminating, cancerous cells.
Research shows that the more cells in an animal, the better its ability to destroy endogenous retroviruses, which jump into DNA, often causing damage, which can initiate cancer cell growth.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Bodies of larger organisms contain more cells than are contained by those of smaller organisms

Fact 2: The incidence of cancer should almost certainly increase with the number of cells found in a body, but biologists have observed that the incidence of cancer is much lower in blue whales, which have nearly 1,000 times as many cells as humans have, than it is in humans, and the incidence of cancer in humans is lower than that in mice, which have about one one-thousandth as many cells as humans have.In general, scientists observe that animals with more cells have lower incidences of cancer than are had by animals with fewer cells.

Picture it: If the incidence of cancer should almost certainly increase with the number of cells found in a body, then why do scientists observe that animals with more cells have lower incidences of cancer than are had by animals with fewer cells? What would explain this pattern that is the reverse what might have been expected?

Because this question is an EXCEPT question, four answers say things that DO resolve the paradox.These will be incorrect answers.The remaining answer, the one that does not resolve the paradox, will be the correct answer to this question.
