(A) Animals with more cells have fewer proto oncogenes, which produce compounds that encourage malignant cancer cell growth.
What this answer choice says resolves the paradox
If animals with more cells have fewer proto oncogenes, which produce compounds that encourage malignant cancer cell growth, then we would expect lower incidences of cancer in these animals.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
(B) Animals with more cells have a greater number of tumor suppressor genes, which, through a complex series of biochemical reactions, produce cancer fighting proteins.
What this answer choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that animals with more cells have a greater number of tumor suppressor genes, which, through a complex series of biochemical reactions, produce cancer fighting proteins, then the paradox is resolved.After all, cancer fighting proteins would explain why the incidences of cancer are lower in animals with more cells.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Cancer incidences of many species of animals have not been well documented over the last fifty years, making it difficult to make comparisons of cancer incidences of these animals.
What this answer choice says does not resolve the paradox.

The author mentions that the incidence of cancer is much lower in Blue Whales, which have nearly 1,000 times as many cells as humans have, than it is in humans, and that the incidence of cancer in humans is lower than that in mice, which have about one one-thousandth as many cells as humans have.She then says that, in general, scientists observe that animals with more cells have lower incidences of cancer than are had by animals with fewer cells.
All this information indicates that something unexpected and seemingly paradoxical going on.

The fact that cancer incidences in many other species of animals have not been documented does not make the situation described less paradoxical.Regardless of what the incidences of cancer in those other animals may be, we are still faced with the fact that the incidences of cancer in animals in which the incidences of cancer would be expected to be relatively high are relatively low.
So, the information provided by this choice does not cause the situation described to seem less paradoxical.
Resolves the paradox? No.
(D) Animals with fewer cells have less immune surveillance, which is the immune system’s scanning for, and when necessary, fixing or eliminating, cancerous cells.
What this answer choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that animals with fewer cells have less immune surveillance, which is the immune system’s scanning for, and when necessary, fixing or eliminating, cancerous cells, then it makes sense that animals with fewer cells have relatively high incidences of cancer.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
(E) Research shows that the more cells in an animal, the better its ability to destroy endogenous retroviruses, which jump into DNA, often causing damage, which can initiate cancer cell growth.
What this answer choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that research shows that the more cells in an animal, the better its ability to destroy endogenous retroviruses, which jump into DNA, often causing damage, which can initiate cancer cell growth, then it makes sense that animals with more cells have relatively low incidences of cancer.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.

question.text: In Wedania, the government responded to a shortage of people choosing to enter the field of nursing by approving an increase in pay rates for nurses in public hospitals.However, two years after the pay increase went into effect, fewer people were choosing to go into nursing than had been doing so before the pay increase went into effect.Which of the following does the most to explain the surprising result of the policy change?

answers.text: Most hospitals responded to the higher pay rates by using fewer nurses, significantly increasing the workloads and stress levels of the members of their nursing staffs.
While, generally, physician assistants undergo only a few more years of training than nurses do, physician assistants are paid significantly more on average than nurses are.
Many nurses do not work in hospitals, but instead work in alternative settings such as insurance companies, pharmaceutical companies, and schools.
In order to remain competitive in hiring, private hospitals increased nurse pay rates to match the pay increase received by nurses in public hospitals.
Wedania’s aging population’s increasing demand for healthcare services has increased demand for nurses faster than nursing schools have increased capacity.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1:In Wedania, the government responded to a shortage of people choosing to enter the field of nursing by approving an increase in pay rates for nurses in public hospitals.
Fact 2:Two years after the pay increase went into effect, fewer people were choosing to go into nursing than had been doing so before the increase went into effect.

What has to be explained:Why is it the case that, after the government responded to a shortage of nurses by approving an increase in pay for nurses in public hospitals, the number of people choosing to go into nursing decreased, rather than increased, as might have been expected?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain adecrease.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Most hospitals responded to the higher pay rates by using fewer nurses, significantly increasing the workloads and stress levels of the members of their nursing staffs.

The fact that most hospitals responded to the higher pay rates by using fewer nurses, significantly increasing the workloads and stress levels of their nurses, explains why fewer people would go into nursing despite higher pay rates than before.

While, typically, higher pay would attract more people to the nursing field, significantly increased workloads and stress levels offset that higher pay and even resulted in a decrease in attractiveness of the field of nursing.
This choice clearly explains the decrease and resolves the apparent paradox.
(B) While, generally, physician assistants undergo only a few more years of training than nurses do, physician assistants are paid significantly more on average than nurses are.

This choice be tempting, because the fact that physician assistants generally undergo only a few more years of training than nurses do but are paid significantly more than nurses are could be perceived as a reason why people would choose not to enter the field of nursing.

Notice, however, that these facts about physician assistants would remain true with or without the pay increase for nurses.In other words, we have no reason to believe that this information about physician assistants was any different before the pay increase for nurses, and the information remains true after the increase.

So, before the pay increase these facts about physician assistants were true, and then, after the pay increase, these facts were still true, but nurses were paid more.So, we can see that the only change in these facts was that nurses were paid more.So, we would have expected an increase, not a decrease in the number of people entering the field of nursing.
So, this choice does not explain the decrease that we have to explain.
(C) Many nurses do not work in hospitals, but instead work in alternative settings such as insurance companies, pharmaceutical companies, and schools.

Regardless of the fact that many nurses do not work in hospitals, but instead work in alternative settings, the fact remains that fewer people were choosing to go into nursing, in any setting, despite the increased pay rates.

So, even though the increased pay rates applied to only public hospitals, what this answer choice says does not explain whyfewerpeople would choose to become nurses.After all, though many nurses do not work in hospitals, some still do, and so, we would expect that increased pay for public hospital work would entice at least a small number of people who wouldn’t have otherwise to choose nursing as a career.

The fact that many nurses do not work in hospitals does not explain why a pay increase for nurses who do work in hospitals was followed by a decrease in the number of people choosing to go into nursing.
(D) In order to remain competitive in hiring, private hospitals increased nurse pay rates to match the pay increase received by nurses in public hospitals.
What this answer choice says deepens the paradox rather than resolves it.

If, in order to remain competitive in hiring, private hospitals increased nurse pay rates to match the pay increase for nurses in public hospitals, then people would have even more reason to choose nursing as a career.Whether they preferred to work in a public hospital or a private one, people would have higher pay than was available before.
So, given what this choice says, we have even more reason to wonder why fewer people would choose to go into nursing than had been doing so before the increased pay rates went into effect.
(E) Wedania’s aging population’s increasing demand for healthcare services has increased demand for nurses faster than nursing schools have increased capacity.
This choice could be tempting because it does explain something related to what’s discussed in the passage.However, this answer choice explains the wrong thing.

The fact that Wedania’s aging population’s increasing demand for healthcare services has increased demand for nurses faster than nursing schools have increased capacity would explain why there wasa shortageof nurses, if there was a such a shortage: people want more nurses than nursing schools can produce.

Of course, we are not seeking to explain a shortage of nurses.We have to explain why the number of people entering the field of nursing decreased.An increase in demand for nurses would not explain why fewer people were entering the field of nursing.

question.text: At the beginning of 2012, a ground beef filler known as “pink slime” constituted a significant percentage of the volume of ground beef sold in the United States.Then, in early 2012, a major news organization reported extensively on pink slime and how it is created, generating significant consumer concern that resulted in a decrease in demand for ground beef.However, while normally a decrease in demand for a product results in a decrease in the price of that product, over the course of following several months, the price of ground beef increased significantly.Which of the following does the most to explain this surprising development?

answers.text: Government regulators stated publicly that the process via which pink slime is created makes it safe to eat.
Many schools, restaurants, and stores stopped serving or selling ground beef products that include pink slime among their ingredients.
In response to consumer concerns, some major manufacturers of ground beef stopped adding pink slime to ground beef, thus effectively reducing the supply of ground beef.
In some countries, no ground beef contained pink slime, as adding pink slime to ground beef was banned before 2012 in those countries.
Many people report that they can taste the difference between ground beef that includes pink slime and ground beef that does not.

explanation_parts.text: Background: At the beginning of 2012, a ground beef filler known as “pink slime” constituted a significant percentage of the volume of ground beef sold in the United States.

Fact 1: In early 2012, a major news organization reported extensively on pink slime and how it is created, generating significant consumer concern that resulted in a decrease in demand for ground beef.

Fact 2: While normally a decrease in demand for a product results in a decrease in the price of that product, over the course of following several months, the price of ground beef increased significantly.
What has to be explained: Why, after demand for ground beef fell after consumers found out from a news organization that ground beef contains pink slime, did the price of ground beef increase?
Notice that, in order to correctly answer this question, we have to explain a priceincreasethat occurred even though demand decreased.
(A) Government regulators stated publicly that the process via which pink slime is created makes it safe to eat.

This choice could be tempting because government regulators’ stating that the process via which pink slime is created makes it safe to eat could be perceived as something that would have prevented the decrease in demand for ground beef mentioned in the passage.If the decrease in demand had been prevented, the increase in the price of ground beef, while not explained, certainly would be less surprising.
However, the truth is that what the government regulators stated does not change what occurred.We have to stick with the fact stated by the passage: demand for ground beef decreased.
So, we’re still faced with the paradox that the price of ground beef increased even though demand decreased.
(B) Many schools, restaurants, and stores stopped serving or selling ground beef products that include pink slime among their ingredients.

That many places stopped serving or selling ground beef products that include pink slime to a degree echoes what the passage already says, which is that demand for ground beef went down.Since this answer choice basically adds a little more detail to a fact we’re already given, it does not resolve the paradox.
YOUR CHOICE(C) In response to consumer concerns, some major manufacturers of ground beef stopped adding pink slime to ground beef, thus effectively reducing the supply of ground beef.

If, in response to consumer concerns, some major manufacturers of ground beef stopped adding pink slime to ground beef, thus effectively reducing the supply of ground beef, then, even as the demand for ground beef decreased, so too did the supply.

Given that supply of ground beef decreased, we now understand why the price of ground beef increased significantly.Generally, a decrease in supply of a product has an upward effect on the price of that product.
This choice explains the increase in price and resolves the apparent paradox.
(D) In some countries, no ground beef contained pink slime, as adding pink slime to ground beef was banned before 2012 in those countries.
That, in some other countries, no ground beef contained pink slime, as, in those countries, adding pink slime to ground beef had been banned before 2012, does not resolve the paradox.

For one thing, the ground beef in those countries didn’t contain pink slime before the news organization reported on pink slime or after the news organization reported on pink slime.So, this choice is about something that didn’t change.Why would information about something that didn’t change explain the price increase?

Overall, there’s no clear reason why an ongoing absence of pink slime from beef in countries other than the United States would cause the price of beef in the United States to relatively suddenly increase in 2012.So, this choice does not explain what we have to explain.
(E) Many people report that they can taste the difference between ground beef that includes pink slime and ground beef that does not.
Regardless of whether people can taste the difference between beef with pink slime and beef without, the fact stated in the passage remains true: demand for ground beef products decreased.

Note that this answer choice doesn’t even tell us which taste people prefer, only that they can taste “the difference.” At the same time, even if we assume that people prefer the beef without pink slime, that fact does not really provide us with any new information to resolve the paradox.We already know that demand for ground beef decreased because people were concerned about the use of pink slime as an ingredient.So, if anything, their being able to taste the (presumably) undesirable ingredient would just provide further explanation for why demand for ground beef decreased.

Of course, we’re not looking for further explanation for why demand for ground beef decreased; we’re seeking to explain how demand for beef could decrease, yet the price of beef could still increase.Information that may help to explain why demand decreased does not resolve that paradox.

question.text: The Oral Health Company claims that its alcohol-based mouthwash kills 99.99 percent of all mouth bacteria within 30 seconds of a person’s rinsing with the product.Mouth bacteria are the primary cause of bad breath.However, many dentists advise their patients with bad breath not to use the Oral Health Company’s mouthwash.Which of the following statements, if true, would best resolve the apparent contradiction described above?

answers.text: Most dentists tell their patients with bad breath to brush their teeth, tongue, and gums with warm water and toothpaste for two minutes, four times a day, every day.
Many dentists feel that their patients will not consistently rinse their mouths with alcohol-based mouthwash for 30 seconds each day because of the intense mouth feeling produced by the alcohol.
Alcohol serves only as a stabilizing agent in the mouthwash but is not the ingredient in the mouthwash responsible for killing bacteria.
Alcohol can dry sensitive mouth tissues, the result of which drying can be a decrease in immune system functioning in the mouth that leads to increased bacteria growth.
Bad breath that results from bacteria in the mouth is not typically considered a health problem by most dentists.

explanation_parts.text: If the Oral Health Company’s alcohol-based mouthwash kills 99.99 percent of all mouth bacteria within 30 seconds of a person’s rinsing with the product, and if mouth bacteria are the primary cause of bad breath, why is it that many dentists advise their patients with bad breath not to use the Oral Health Company’s mouthwash? What could resolve this seeming paradox?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain why dentists actively advise their patients with bad breathnotto use the mouthwash.
(A) Most dentists tell their patients with bad breath to brush their teeth, tongue, and gums with warm water and toothpaste for two minutes, four times a day, every day.

What this answer choice says, that most dentists tell their patients with bad breath to brush their teeth, tongue, and gums with warm water and toothpaste for two minutes, four times a day, every day, does not change the facts stated by the passage that the company’s alcohol-based mouthwash kills 99.99 percent of all mouth bacteria within 30 seconds of a person’s rinsing with the product, and that mouth bacteria are the primary cause of bad breath.
In other words, what this answer choice says could coexist with the facts provided in the passage.
Also, someone who brushes his or her teeth four times a day could use mouthwash as well.
Thus, we’re still confused as to why many dentists advise their patients with bad breath not to use the mouthwash.
(B) Many dentists feel that their patients will not consistently rinse their mouths with alcohol-based mouthwash for 30 seconds each day because of the intense mouth feeling produced by the alcohol.

Notice that what we have to explain is not why dentists don’t bother advising patients to use the mouthwash.Rather, we have to explain why dentists advise their patients with bad breathnotto use the Oral Health Company’s mouthwash.In other words, the dentists actively advise patientsagainstusing the mouthwash.

This answer choice, “Many dentists feel that their patients will not consistently rise their mouths with alcohol-based mouthwash for 30 seconds each day because of the intense mouth feeling produced by the alcohol,” would not be a reason for them to tell their patients not to use mouthwash.

This choice might be a reason why the dentists would not bother telling patients to use mouthwash, because what this choice says indicates that, even if dentists did tell patients to use mouthwash, the patients would not use it.
However, we’re seeking a reason to explain why these dentists recommend that their patients do not use mouthwash.
(C) Alcohol serves only as a stabilizing agent in the mouthwash but is not the ingredient in the mouthwash responsible for killing bacteria.
What this answer choice says does not change the facts provided in the passage.

Regardless of whether alcohol serves only as a stabilizing agent in the mouthwash but is not the ingredient in the mouthwash responsible for killing bacteria, it would still be the case that, as the passage says, the mouthwash kills 99.99 percent of all mouth bacteria within 30 seconds of a person’s rinsing with the product.
Furthermore, the fact that alcohol is not the ingredient that kills bacteria would not be a reason not to use the mouthwash.
We need to explain why dentists would recommend that patients not use the mouthwash, and this information on the role of alcohol in the mouthwash is not a reason not to use it.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Alcohol can dry sensitive mouth tissues, the result of which drying can be a decrease in immune system functioning in the mouth that leads to increased bacteria growth.

If it is true that alcohol can dry sensitive mouth tissues, the result of which drying can be a decrease in immune system functioning in the mouth that leads to increased bacteria growth, then it seems likely that, even though an alcohol-based mouthwash would kill 99.99 percent of bacteria in a person’s mouth, a person who used such mouthwash could end up with even more mouth bacteria than were present before the person used the mouthwash.

We are told that bacteria are the primary cause of bad breath.Therefore, since what this choice says indicates that use of the company’s mouthwash could cause anincreasein the number of bacteria in a person’s mouth, this choice would certainly explain why dentists would recommend that their patients not use the mouthwash.
(E) Bad breath that results from bacteria in the mouth is not typically considered a health problem by most dentists.

The fact that bad breath that results from bacteria in the mouth is not typically considered a health problem by most dentists could be a reason why dentists would not bother to recommend that patients with bad breath use a mouthwash that kills mouth bacteria.

However, the fact that bad breath that results from bacteria in the mouth is not typically considered a health problem by most dentists would not be a reason for dentists to actively recommend that patientnotuse mouthwash that kills bacteria.

question.text: For years, once tickets to the major tennis tournament held in Sartania became available, the vast majority were quickly purchased by ticket brokers who resold them at greatly marked-up prices.Believing this situation to be unfair to the average tennis fan, the Sartanian Tennis Commission devised a plan to leave most of the tickets to the major tournament available at their original, reasonable, prices by making it difficult for brokers to purchase the tickets in great quantities.Unexpectedly, in the years following the implementation of the plan, tournament attendance was significantly lower than it had been before the plan was implemented.Which of the following, if true, best explains the surprising result of implementing the plan?

answers.text: The implementation of the plan did not include any public messaging to inform tennis fans that attending the tournament had become less expensive.
Average tennis fans in Sartania have financial means such that they can easily afford to purchase tickets to the major tournament at greatly marked-up prices.
In Sartania, many tickets to sporting events are purchased by companies seeking to impress clients by giving them expensive gifts.
Even at their lower, original prices, tickets to the major tennis tournament are considered unaffordable by many tennis fans.
The first year the plan was implemented, a tennis tournament that had preceded the major tennis tournament annually in Sartania was discontinued because of lack of interest.

explanation_parts.text: Background: For years, once tickets to the major tennis tournament held in Sartania became available, most were quickly purchased by ticket brokers who resold them at greatly marked-up prices.

Fact 1: The Sartanian Tennis Commission devised a plan to leave most of the tickets to the major tournament available at their original, reasonable, prices by making it difficult for brokers to purchase the tickets in great quantities.
Fact 2: In the years following the implementation of the plan, tournament attendance was significantly lower than it had been before the plan was implemented.

What has to be explained: Why would it have been that, after the Tennis Commission implemented a plan to leave most of the tickets to the major tournament available at reasonable prices by making it difficult for brokers to purchase the tickets, tournament attendance decreased?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain adecrease.
(A) The implementation of the plan did not include any public messaging to inform tennis fans that attending the tournament had become less expensive.

The fact that the implementation of the plan did not include any public messaging to inform tennis fans that attending the tournament had become less expensive might explain why tournament attendanceremained the same, had it been the case that attendance simply remained the same.It could have been that few people knew that it had become less expensive to attend.

However, lack of public messaging about, or public awareness of, the fact that attending the tournament had become less expensive certainly would not explain why tournament attendancewas significantly lowerthan it had been before the plan was implemented.
This choice would explain why attendance did not increase, if attendance had not increased, but it does not explain the decrease in attendance that occurred.
(B) Average tennis fans in Sartania have financial means such that they can easily afford to purchase tickets to the major tournament at greatly marked-up prices.

The fact that average tennis fans in Sartania have financial means such that they can easily afford to purchase tickets to the major tournament at greatly marked-up prices might have explained why tournament attendanceremained the same, had it been the case that tournament attendance simply remained the same.Maybe the fans could afford the tickets even when the tickets were expensive, in which case making more tickets available at reasonable prices might not make much difference.
However, the fact that fanscan affordto purchase tickets certainly would not explain why tournament attendance was significantlylowerthan it had been before the plan was implemented.
YOUR CHOICE(C) In Sartania, many tickets to sporting events are purchased by companies seeking to impress clients by giving them expensive gifts.

If it is true that the plan leaves most tournament tickets available at their original, reasonable, prices by making it difficult for brokers to purchase the tickets in great quantities, and if it is true that, in Sartania, many tickets to sporting events are purchased by companies seeking to impress clients by giving them expensive gifts, then we can see why, in the years following the implementation of the plan, tournament attendance was significantly lower than it had been before the plan was implemented.

At their “original, reasonable prices,” tickets to the tournament would no longer constitute “expensive gifts.” So, companies seeking to impress clients would no longer purchase the tickets.As a result, ticket purchases, and presumably, tournament attendance, would decrease.
This choice resolves the apparent paradox.
(D) Even at their lower, original prices, tickets to the major tennis tournament are considered unaffordable by many tennis fans.
Notice that what this answer choice says is, essentially, that something did not change.

In saying that, “even at their lower, original prices, tickets to the major tennis tournament are considered unaffordable by many tennis fans,” this choice indicates that the tickets were considered unaffordable by many fans before the implementation of the plan and were considered unaffordable by many fans after the implementation of the plan.
The fact that affordability of the tickets did not change cannot help us to explain the change presented in the passage.

What this choice says would explain why attendanceremained the same, if indeed attendance had remained the same.However, the fact that affordability of the tickets did not change does not explain why attendancedecreased.
(E) The first year the plan was implemented, a tennis tournament that had preceded the major tennis tournament annually in Sartania was discontinued because of lack of interest.
Notice that we don’t really know the effect of what this answer choice says.

It could have been that the discontinuation of the other tournament somehow had a negative effect on the popularity of tennis in Sartania, in which case that negative effect would be the reason for the decrease in attendance at the major tournament.

On the other hand, maybe the fans of the discontinued tennis tournament have flocked to the major tennis tournament now that they can’t attend the one that was discontinued.Of course that development would not have resulted in a decrease in attendance at the major tennis tournament.
Since, the effect of the discontinuation of the other tournament is not clear, this choice does not explain the decrease in attendance or resolve the apparent paradox.

question.text: Levels of testosterone, a human hormone partly responsible for production of muscle, can drop below the normal range, which is defined as 270-1070 ng/dL, as men age, a change resulting in unwanted symptoms, such as loss of muscle mass.Testosterone replacement therapy, “TRT,” involves giving a patient exogenous testosterone.After TRT, most men report increases in muscle mass.Interestingly, however, many who report significant increases in muscle mass resulting from TRT do not report significant increases in strength.Such an observation is puzzling since we know that, in general, more muscle means greater strength.Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain the apparent inconsistency?

answers.text: TRT, unlike use of anabolic steroids, which often brings testosterone levels significantly above 1070 ng/dL, will bring testosterone levels only to the high end of the normal range.
Most men who use TRT see increases in testosterone levels to about 500 ng/dL, lower than levels most of them had when they were younger and had more muscle mass.
Many of the men who use TRT do not engage in intensive strength training, which is well known to increase not only muscle mass but also strength.
The exogenous testosterone used in TRT is not as effective at increasing muscle mass as is a man’s natural testosterone produced by his own body.
A man’s ligaments and tendons, both of which play important roles in utilization of muscle strength, are largely unaffected by TRT.

explanation_parts.text: Background: Levels of testosterone, a human hormone partly responsible for production of muscle, can drop below the normal range, which is defined as 270-1070 ng/dL, as men age, a change resulting in unwanted symptoms, such as loss of muscle mass.Testosterone replacement therapy, “TRT,” involves giving a patient exogenous testosterone.
Fact 1: After TRT, most men report increases in muscle mass.
Fact 2: Interestingly, however, many who report significant increases in muscle mass resulting from TRT do not report significant increases in strength.

What Has to Be Explained: Why do men who report significant increases in muscle mass resulting from TRT not report significant increase in strength, when in general, more muscle means greater strength?

Picture it: After undergoing TRT, many men who report significant increases in muscle mass do not also report significant increases in strength.Why would these men see “significant” increases in muscle mass but not also see significant increases in strength? What could explain these surprising results?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain why somethingdoes not change.
(A) TRT, unlike use of anabolic steroids, which often brings testosterone levels significantly above 1070 ng/dL, will bring testosterone levels only to the high end of the normal range.

If you chose this answer as correct, you likely assumed that TRT can lead to significant increases in strength only if the therapy brought testosterone levels above the normal range.However, we have no evidence to believe such an assumption in valid.

In fact, what this answer choice says does not change the facts presented in the passage, which are (1) that after TRT, most men report increases in muscle mass and (2) that many who report significant increases in muscle mass do not report significant increases in strength.So, regardless of the degree to which TRT increases testosterone levels, the men mentioned did experience both increases in testosterone levels and increases in muscle mass.In other words, regardless ofhow muchtheir testosterone levels or muscle mass increased, they did increase.So, an increase in strength would have been expected as well.

Even though TRT does not increase testosterone levels as much as anabolic steroids do, TRT does increase testosterone levels and does result in increased muscle mass.So regardless of what this choice says, we would have expected TRT to result in increases in strength.
Thus, this choice does not explain why men undergoing TRT did not report increases in strength.
(B) Most men who use TRT see increases in testosterone levels to about 500 ng/dL, lower than levels most of them had when they were younger and had more muscle mass.

What this answer choice says does not change the facts presented in the passage, which are that (1) after TRT, most men report increases in muscle mass and (2) many reporting significant increases in muscle mass do not report significant increases in strength.So, even with what this answer choice says, we are still confused regarding why these men are not seeing significant increases in strength.

If you chose this answer as correct, you likely fell for faulty logic along the lines of that, because TRT increases men’s testosterone levels to “only” about 500 ng/dL, which is lower than levels most of them had when they were younger and had more muscle mass, we see why these men don’t report increases in strength.

It’s important to see that a man need not return his testosterone levels back to those during the time when he was younger and had more muscle mass to see a significant increase in strength after using TRT (and after seeing a significant increase in muscle).
If a man reports seeing a significant increase in muscle mass, the fact that he has not also seen a significant increase in strength is surprising regardless of what his testosterone level is.
(C) Many of the men who use TRT do not engage in intensive strength training, which is well known to increase not only muscle mass but also strength.
The fact that intensive strength training is well known to increase muscle mass and strength does not mean that engaging in intensive strength training is necessary for increasing strength.

Furthermore, what this choice says does not change the facts presented in the passage, which are that (1) after TRT, most men report increases in muscle mass and (2) many who report significant increases in muscle mass do not report significant increases in strength.

Regardless of whether men undergoing TRT engage in intensive strength training, they have reported increases in muscle mass.So, even taking into account what this answer choice says, we are still wondering why many who report significant increases in muscle mass do not report significant increases in strength.
(D) The exogenous testosterone used in TRT is not as effective at increasing muscle mass as is a man’s natural testosterone produced by his own body.

The fact that the exogenous testosterone used in TRT is not as effective at increasing muscle mass as is a man’s natural testosterone produced by his own body does not mean that exogenous testosterone is not effective at increasing a man’s muscle mass.After all, exogenous testosterone could be very effective at increasing muscle mass even if it is not as effective as is a man’s natural testosterone.

Furthermore, what this answer choice says does not change the facts presented in the passage, which are that (1) after TRT, most men report increases in muscle mass and (2) many who report significant increases in muscle mass do not report significant increases in strength.

So, regardless of how effective exogenous testosterone is in increasing muscle mass, men using it did report increases in muscle mass.Since we would expect any increase in muscle mass to be accompanied by an increase in strength, even taking into account what this answer choice says, we are still wondering why many reporting significant increases in muscle mass do not report significant increases in strength.
YOUR CHOICE(E) A man’s ligaments and tendons, both of which play important roles in utilization of muscle strength, are largely unaffected by TRT.

The fact that a man’s ligaments and tendons, both of which play important roles in utilization of muscle strength, are largely unaffected by TRT explains why men who report increases in muscle mass do not report increases in strength.
Let’s think about this scenario.
When a man undergoes TRT, his testosterone levels increase.As a result, he experiences significant increases in muscle mass but does not report significant increases in strength.Why?
Well, as this choice says, the man’s tendons and ligaments were not affected by the TRT, and as this choice tells us, tendons and ligaments play important roles in utilization of muscle strength.
Now, we can see what’s going on.

The muscles get bigger, but the tendons and ligaments, which play important roles in utilization of muscle strength, are largely unaffected by the TRT.Therefore, they do not become stronger along with the muscles.As a result, these men did not report significant increases in strength.Can you imagine trying to bench press with strong muscles but weak tendons and ligaments?
So, clearly, this choice explains why men who undergo TRT often report increases in muscle mass but not increases in strength.

question.text: Etruria Motors, the only manufacturer that sells motor vehicles in Etruria, complied with new, government fuel economy standards by increasing the average estimated fuel economy of all of the cars it sells from 27 miles per gallon of fuel to 32 miles per gallon of fuel and that of all of the light trucks it sells from 19 miles per gallon of fuel to 22 miles per gallon of fuel.However, the average estimated fuel economy of all of the new cars and light trucks sold in Etruria during the year following the increases was lower than that of those sold the year before.Which of the following, if true, provides the best explanation for this surprising result?

answers.text: Because making vehicles more fuel efficient resulted in their being relatively expensive, many people in Etruria decided to continue to use the less fuel-efficient vehicles that they already owned.
Because of the relatively small sizes of the new, more fuel-efficient cars, many people who would otherwise have bought cars bought light trucks instead.
The fuel economy achieved when someone actually uses a vehicle can differ significantly from the estimated fuel economy of that vehicle.

In the nation of Gertraria, the management of Gertraria Motors was caught falsifying results, making it appear that cars manufactured by Gertraria Motors were more fuel efficient than they actually were.
Many buyers of vehicles in Etruria are not very concerned with fuel economy and base purchasing decisions primarily on other vehicle characteristics.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Etruria Motors, the only manufacturer that sells vehicles in Etruria, complied with new government fuel economy standards by increasing the average estimated fuel economy of its cars from 27 to 32 miles per gallon and that of its light trucks from 19 to 22 miles per gallon.

Fact 2: The average estimated fuel economy of all of the new cars and light trucks sold in Etruria during the year following the increases in fuel economy was lower than that of those sold the year before.

What Has to Be Explained:How could it be that, after the only manufacturer that sells vehicles in Etruria complied with new government fuel economy standards by increasing the average estimated fuel economy of its cars and that of its light trucks, the average estimated fuel economy of all of the new cars and light trucks sold in Etruria during the year following the increases was lower than that of those sold the year before?

Picture it: Etruria Motors, the only manufacturer that sells vehicles in Etruria, improves the fuel efficiency of its cars and light trucks.But, the average fuel economy of all cars and light trucks sold in Etruria goes down after this change.Why?
Notice, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain a decrease.

(A) Because making vehicles more fuel efficient resulted in their being relatively expensive, many people in Etruria decided to continue to use the less fuel-efficient vehicles that they already owned.

Remember, the paradox is that the average estimated fuel economy of all new cars and light truckssoldin Etruria decreased even though the manufacturer increased the fuel economy of new cars and trucks.Thus, the continued use of less fuel-efficient vehicles that people in Etruriaalready owndoes not relate to the paradox.

The people who decide to keep their current vehicles rather than purchasing new ones, because new vehicles have become more expensive, do not have any impact on what “the average estimated fuel economy of all of the new cars and light truckssoldin Etruria” is, since those people are not buying new vehicles.

Those people’s vehicles would of course be factored into the average fuel economy ofall vehicles being driven in Etruria, but the paradox we need to resolve relates only tonew vehicles soldin the year after the change in fuel standards.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Because of the relatively small sizes of the new, more fuel-efficient cars, many people who would otherwise have bought cars bought light trucks instead.
This choice resolves the apparent paradox by providing a reason why the average estimated fuel economy decreased.
The average estimated fuel economy with which we are concerned is that of “all of the new cars and light trucks sold in Etruria.”

Looking at the numbers in the passage, we can see that, both before and after Etrutria Motors increased the fuel economy of the cars and light trucks it sells, the light trucks had a significantly lower average estimated fuel economy than was had by the cars.
So, let’s consider what likely would have happened when, as this choice says, “many people who would otherwise have bought cars bought light trucks instead.”
Since people were buying light trucks, rather than cars, the number of light trucks sold was pushed higher, and the number of cars sold was pushed lower.

We can see that, since light trucks have lower fuel economy than is had by cars, if the number of trucks sold is pushed up, and the number of cars sold is pushed down, the average fuel economy of all vehicles sold is going to decrease.The lower fuel economy of the trucks sold is going to drag the average down, and fewer cars, which have the higher fuel economy, will be sold to hold the average up.

So, it's clear that what this choice says, “many people who would otherwise have bought cars bought light trucks instead,” explains the decrease in average fuel economy and resolves the apparent paradox.
(C) The fuel economy achieved when someone actually uses a vehicle can differ significantly from the estimated fuel economy of that vehicle.

That thefuel economy when someone actually uses a vehiclecan differ significantly from theestimated fuel economy of that vehiclecannot resolve the paradox because the paradox is concerned only with “averageestimatedfuel economy.”

The comparison made in the passage is between the averageestimatedfuel economy of cars sold in the year before the change in fuel standards and the averageestimatedfuel economy of cars sold in the year after the change.In other words, we’re comparing apples to apples.

Since actual fuel economy and estimated fuel economy are not the same thing, information about theactualfuel economy achieved by a vehicle, whether better or worse than theestimatedfuel economy, does not factor into the comparison made in the passage.
So, there’s no way for information about actual fuel economy to explain why estimated fuel economy decreased.

(D) In the nation of Gertraria, the management of Gertraria Motors was caught falsifying results, making it appear that cars manufactured by Gertraria Motors were more fuel efficient than they actually were.

This choice could be tempting because it could be perceived as providing a plausible explanation for how average estimated fuel economy could have decreased after a vehicle manufacturer increased fuel economy.That explanation would be along the lines of, “Vehicle manufacturers dishonestly inflate estimated fuel economy of the vehicles they sell, and so, the actual fuel economy of the vehicles is lower than the estimates provided by the manufacturers, and somehow, the existence of this situation resulted in a decrease in average estimated fuel economy when an increase would have been expected.”

However, we have no way to know whether what happened in Gertraria also happened in Etruria.Maybe it did, and maybe it did not.Also, nothing in this answer choice or the passage tells us that Gertraria Motors’ unethical practices are commonplace in the vehicle industry.

Furthermore, we don’t even have a clear way to connect vehicle manufacturer dishonesty with the surprising decrease in average estimated fuel economy of cars sold.Was that average based on the inflated numbers provided by the manufacturers? If not, where did it come from? Why would it have decreased? We don’t have any answers to these questions.
Thus, this answer choice does not provide any information to resolve the paradox.
(E) Many buyers of vehicles in Etruria are not very concerned with fuel economy and base purchasing decisions primarily on other vehicle characteristics.

The passage tells us that Etruria Motors is the only manufacturer that sells vehicles in Etruria.Therefore, regardless of whether people in Etruria base vehicle purchasing decisions on fuel efficiency, people who purchased new vehicles in Etruria chose among the vehicles offered by Etruria Motors.

All new vehicles sold by Etruria Motors had higher estimated fuel economy than similar vehicles manufactured by Etruria Motors had previously.So, even if many people didn’t pay much attention to fuel efficiency when they were buying new vehicles, we would still have expected average fuel economy of new vehicles sold to increase, not decrease.
Thus, the information in this answer choice does nothing to resolve the paradox.

question.text: For years, the Quantum Corporation has been seeking to achieve an elusive goal – the development of a quantum processor, a computer processor able to perform calculations billions of times faster than can even the fastest processor today.Recently, information related to studies done by the Quantum Corporation has indicated that the company may have recently succeeded in producing a quantum processor.Although all of humanity could benefit from having access to data from the studies, in a radical step, the corporation is not only refraining from sharing any of this data but also ending all funding to its quantum processor division.Which of the following, if true, would best explain the Quantum Corporation’s surprising decision?

answers.text: Most top computer scientists believe that the concept of a quantum processor is merely a fairy tale and that corporations should work on projects that will actually benefit humanity.
Executives at the Quantum Corporation determined that the release of data or further generation of data on quantum processors could endanger the revenues of the entire corporation.
Over the years, the Quantum Corporation has spent billions of dollars in pursuit of producing a viable quantum processor, and, meanwhile, the quantum processor division has produced no revenue.
This year, the Quantum Corporation lost a major government grant that had been helping to fund its research into the development of a quantum processor.
Even if the Quantum Corporation were to release the data from the studies, other corporations seeking to develop quantum processors would take many years to understand and apply the data.

explanation_parts.text: What has to be explained: If the Quantum Corporation may have recently succeeded in producing a quantum processor, and if all of humanity could benefit from having access to data from the studies, then why is the corporation not only refraining from sharing any of this data but also ending all funding to its quantum processor division? What would help us to explain these surprising decisions?
(A) Most top computer scientists believe that the concept of a quantum processor is merely a fairy tale and that corporations should work on projects that will actually benefit humanity.

What “most” top computer scientists believe is all well and good, but the fact of the matter is that the Quantum Corporation may have successfully produced a quantum processor and that all of humanity could benefit from having access to data from the studies.What this answer choice says does not change that fact and does not explain the surprising decisions on the part of Quantum Corporation.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Executives at the Quantum Corporation determined that the release of data or further generation of data on quantum processors could endanger the revenues of the entire corporation.

If it is true that executives at the Quantum Corporation determined that the release of data or further generation of data on quantum processors could endanger the revenues of the entire corporation, then this fact would help to explain why the corporation is not only refraining from sharing any of this data but also ending all funding to its quantum processor division.
This choice adds key new information to what we already know and helps us to understand why the Quantum Corporation made the decisions it made.
(C) Over the years, the Quantum Corporation has spent billions of dollars in pursuit of producing a viable quantum processor, and, meanwhile, the quantum processor division has produced no revenue.

What this answer choice says, that “Over the years, the Quantum Corporation has spent billions of dollars in pursuit of producing a viable quantum processor, and, meanwhile, the quantum processor division has produced no revenue, would not explain why the corporation is not only refraining from sharing any of this data but also ending all funding to its quantum processor division.

After all, what this answer choice says essentially is that the company has had to make an investment in the pursuit of the quantum processor.But isn’t that business? And now, after a lot of time and a lot of money spent, now that the company may have succeeded in producing a quantum processor, it is going to close up shop? That idea is not a reasonable one.

Furthermore, even if the Quantum Corporation were concerned about how much money it has been investing in the quantum processor, that fact would not explain why the company is refraining from sharing data.Sharing data would not require the company to continue making significant investments.
Clearly, this choice does not explain why the Quantum Corporation has made the decisions it has made.
(D) This year, the Quantum Corporation lost a major government grant that had been helping to fund its research into the development of a quantum processor.

Notice that, given what the passage says, it appears that the research phase for this quantum processor is over or close to over.After all, we’re told the company may have successfully produced a quantum processor.So why would it matter that this year, the Quantum Corporation lost a major government grant that had been helping to fund its research into the development of a quantum processor? And, furthermore, why would the loss of that grant cause the corporation to refrain from sharing any data?
(E) Even if the Quantum Corporation were to release the data from the studies, other corporations seeking to develop quantum processors would take many years to understand and apply the data.

Even if corporations would take many years to understand and apply the data, by releasing the data, Quantum Corporation could provide something valuable that would benefit humanity.So why would the corporation be not only ending all funding to its quantum processor division but also refraining from sharing any data? Clearly, what this answer choice says does not explain the corporation’s decisions.

question.text: The results generated by polygraphs, also known as “lie detectors,” are not actually accurate indicators of whether people are lying.However, many employers who are aware of the shortcomings of polygraphs continue to use them regularly to test employees in situations in which lying employees could do significant damage to company interests.Which of the following best explains these employers’ surprising behavior?

answers.text: It can be costly and difficult to detect fraudulent behavior engaged in by employees who are responsible for handling important matters for a company.
There are employers who are not aware that the results generated by polygraphs are not accurate indicators of whether people are lying.
It is often the case that police detectives employing polygraphs in the process of questioning suspects are aware that polygraph results are not accurate indicators of veracity.
Many people who regularly undergo polygraph testing in connection with their employment are not aware that polygraphs are poor detectors of lying.
While polygraph testing of employees in certain types of situations is prohibited by some governments, they allow polygraph testing in other types of situations.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: The results generated by polygraphs, also known as “lie detectors,” are not actually accurate indicators of whether people are lying.

Fact 2: Many employers who are aware of the shortcomings of polygraphs continue to use them regularly to test employees in situations in which lying employees could do significant damage to company interests.
Picture it: Polygraphs are not actually accurate indicators of whether people are lying.However, employers – although they are aware of these shortcomings – use polygraphs anyway.Why?
(A) It can be costly and difficult to detect fraudulent behavior engaged in by employees who are responsible for handling important matters for a company.

The fact that it can be costly and difficult to detect fraudulent behavior engaged in by employees does not explain why employers use polygraphs to test employees even though the results not accurate.Neither this choice nor the passage provides information indicating that inaccurate polygraph results would somehow solve the problem that it can be costly and difficult to detect fraudulent behavior.
(B) There are employers who are not aware that the results generated by polygraphs are not accurate indicators of whether people are lying.

This choice could be tempting, because employers’ lack of awareness of the fact that polygraph results are not accurate indicators of whether people are lying would certainly explain why employers would use polygraphs even though the results aren’t accurate.

Notice, however, what the passage says.The passage says that many employers whoare aware of the shortcomings of polygraphscontinue to use them regularly to test employees.So, the seemingly paradoxical situation involves employers whoare awarethat the results of polygraph tests are not accurate indicators of whether people are lying.

The fact that some employers are not aware that the results of polygraph tests are not accurate indicators of whether employees are lying clearly does not explain why employers who are aware of the shortcomings of polygraphs use them anyway.
(C) It is often the case that police detectives employing polygraphs in the process of questioning suspects are aware that polygraph results are not accurate indicators of veracity.

The fact that police detectives also use polygraphs even though they are aware that the results are not accurate indicators of veracity shows only that employers who do so are not the only ones who do so.
Given the information provided by this choice, we now have no explanation for why both employers and police detectives use polygraphs despite being aware of the shortcomings of the results.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Many people who regularly undergo polygraph testing in connection with their employment are not aware that polygraphs are poor detectors of lying.

If many employees are not aware that polygraphs are poor detectors of lying, then polygraphs could actually serve a valid purpose: a company hooks an employee up to a polygraph, and because the employee does not know that a polygraph is an imperfect tool, the employee tends to tell the truth, for fear that the polygraph will “catch him lying” if he lies.
Therefore, what this answer says resolves the paradox by showing that the polygraph serves a valuable purpose for employers, even if that purpose is not quite the same as the presumed purpose.
(E) While polygraph testing of employees in certain types of situations is prohibited by some governments, they allow polygraph testing in other types of situations.

If you choose this answer as correct, you likely assumed that, the fact that polygraph testing is allowed in some types of situations indicates that the results of such testing may somehow or sometimes be valid.
However, as we know, we can’t argue with the statements in the passage, and we’re told that “the results generated by polygraphs … are not actually accurate indicators of whether people are lying.”

So, all this choice really tells us is that using polygraphs is sometimes legal, and the fact that using polygraphs is legal does not explain why employers use them even though the results generated are not accurate indicators of whether employees are lying.

question.text: Physiologists in Country P have determined that people require at least 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day to remain in optimal health.However public health officials in Country P have recommended that all people walk at a brisk pace for at least 20 minutes a day, five days a week.Which of the following statements, if true, would best explain the apparent contradiction described above?

answers.text: Most people in Country P do not have easy access to gyms, which are primarily located in the country’s capital city.
Many people in Country P do not agree on the precise definition of optimal health.
Walking at a brisk pace for 20 minutes is enough exercise to elevate one’s heart rate.
Public health officials in Country P are trained to recommend what they think people will do, not what they should do.
Many of the people in Country P are skeptical of recommendations made by public health officials.

explanation_parts.text: The passage presents us with a paradox.If physiologists have determined that people require at least 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day to remain in optimal health, then why have public health officials in Country P recommended that all people walk at a brisk pace for at least 20 minutes a day, five days a week? What could explain this discrepancy?
(A) Most people in Country P do not have easy access to gyms, which are primarily located in the country’s capital city.

What this answer choice says would not help us to resolve the paradox.After all, the paradox centers on why officials are recommending less exercise than is optimal.The fact that most people in Country P do not have easy access to gyms would not resolve that paradox.Gyms are not the only way for people to exercise.
So, what this choice says does not explain why officials are recommending less exercise than physiologists have determined to be required.
(B) Many people in Country P do not agree on the precise definition of optimal health.

The passage tells us that people require at least 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day to remain in optimal health.That fact’s being true does not require that all people agree on the exact definition of optimal health.
So, what this choice says leaves the scenario basically the same and does not explain the difference between what physiologists have determined to be required and what officials recommend.
(C) Walking at a brisk pace for 20 minutes is enough exercise to elevate one’s heart rate.

What this answer choice says does not change the fact that people require at least 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day to remain in optimal health.In other words, it could very well be true that walking at a brisk pace for 20 minutes is enough exercise to elevate one’s heart rate, and at the same time, people still require at least 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day to remain in optimal health.
So, what this choice says leaves the scenario basically the same and does not explain the difference between what physiologists have determined to be required and what officials recommend.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Public health officials in Country P are trained to recommend what they think people will do, not what they should do.

If it is true that public health officials in Country P are trained to recommend what they think people will do, not what they should do, then, we can see why, although people require at least 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day to remain in optimal health, public health officials in Country P have recommended that all people walk at a brisk pace for at least 20 minutes a day, five days a week.

In other words, yes, it is true that people require 60 minutes of vigorous daily exercise, but the public health officials are not recommending what is optimal; they are making a recommendation based upon what they believe people will actually do.Perhaps these officials believe that, if they recommend 60 minutes of vigorous exercise each day, people, unwilling to exercise that much, may simply end of doing no exercise each day.
So, this choice explains why there is a difference between what physiologists have determined to be required and what health officials recommend.
(E) Many of the people in Country P are skeptical of recommendations made by public health officials.

The fact that many of the people in Country P are skeptical of recommendations made by public health officials would not explain the discrepancy between the amount of exercise the officials recommend and the amount that physiologists have determined to be required.After all, if what this answer choice says is true, then whatever recommendation the officials make will be viewed skeptically by people.Right? So, then, the officials should just recommend whatever they feel to be the correct amount of exercise.

question.text: Although the country of Farland had had a monarchy from the inception of the country's existence, the people of Farland changed their views on the monarchy, citing the very large tax burden imposed on them in order to support the activities of the monarchy.Therefore, in order to reduce the taxpayer burden, Farland dissolved the monarchy and began requiring the former monarchs to pay taxes along with the rest of the people of Farland.Contrary to expectation, the taxpayer burden began to increase substantially in the following years.Which of the following, if true, best explains the unexpected outcome?

answers.text: The monarchy had always employed a substantial number of people for each member of the monarchy.
Many former monarchs were able to find employment easily and contributed their tax shares.
The government had been receiving an enormous amount of money from tourists interested in the lives of members of the monarchy.
Many citizens of Farland had looked up to the members of the monarchy and had sought to emulate their behavior.
A significant percentage of Farland's citizens had not minded paying taxes to support the activities of the monarchy.

explanation_parts.text: Background: Although the country of Farland had had a monarchy from the inception of the country's existence, the people of Farland changed their views on the monarchy, citing the very large tax burden imposed on them in order to support the activities of the monarchy.
Fact 1: In order to reduce the taxpayer burden, Farland dissolved the monarchy and began requiring the former monarchs to pay taxes along with the rest of the people of Farland.
Fact 2: Contrary to expectation, the taxpayer burden began to increase substantially in the following years.

What has to be explained: Why is it that, in Farland, even though supporting the activities of the monarchy resulted in the imposition of a large tax burden on the citizens, when the monarchy was dissolved, and the former monarchs were required to pay taxes, the taxpayer burden began to increase?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain anincrease.
(A) The monarchy had always employed a substantial number of people for each member of the monarchy.

It might be tempting to create a story along the lines of that, once the monarchy was dissolved, the people who had been employed by the monarchy did not have jobs and hence were not paying taxes, with the result that the taxpayer burden increased.
However, we can’t make unsubstantiated assumptions, and the truth is that we don’t know the direction in which what this answer choice says takes us.

For instance, it could have been that, rather than become unemployed, all of the people who had been employed by the monarchy got new jobs and paid just as much in taxes as they had paid when employed by the monarchy.

Furthermore, given what the passage says, it seems likely that tax revenue was being used by the monarchy to employ all these people.So, even if this substantial number of people did not find new jobs and, therefore, stopped paying taxes, the loss of tax revenue resulting from their stopping paying taxes would be offset by the fact that tax revenue was no longer being used to pay them.
We can see that this choice does not explain why the taxpayer burden increased.
(B) Many former monarchs were able to find employment easily and contributed their tax shares.
If anything, what this answer choice says deepens the paradox.

If it is true that many former monarchs were able to find employment easily and contributed their tax shares, then why did the taxpayer burden begin to increase substantially in the following years? After all, with these former monarchs working and paying their share of the taxes, we certainly would not expect other taxpayers all of a sudden to need to pay more taxes.Right?
YOUR CHOICE(C) The government had been receiving an enormous amount of money from tourists interested in the lives of members of the monarchy.

If it is true that the government had been receiving an enormous amount of money from tourists interested in the lives of members of the monarchy, we would certainly expect that, once the monarchy was dissolved, the government would no longer have received this enormous amount money, since with the dissolving of the monarchy, the tourists would have stopped coming and paying money to the government.

This information would certainly explain why the taxpayer burden began to increase substantially in the following years.The government would likely have had to increase tax revenue to make up for the loss of the revenue that had been coming from tourists.
This choice explains the increase in the tax burden and resolves the apparent paradox.
(D) Many citizens of Farland had looked up to the members of the monarchy and had sought to emulate their behavior.

Notice that, although what this answer choice says sounds nice, we really don’t have any idea what its implications are.In which ways did many citizens of Farland seek to emulate the behavior of members of the monarchy, and how would citizens’ acting like monarchs affect taxes?
Since what this choice says is not clearly connected to taxes or to the increase in the taxpayer burden, it does not explain the increase in the taxpayer burden.
(E) A significant percentage of Farland's citizens had not minded paying taxes to support the activities of the monarchy.

How does the fact that a significant percentage of Farland's citizens had not minded paying taxes to support the activities of the monarchy explain why the tax burden increased after the monarchy was dissolved? If you come up with an answer, please email me atscott@targettestprep. com. 😊

question.text: Although antibiotics are useful as treatment for bacterial infections only and have no effect on viruses, doctors often prescribe antibiotics to patients in whom only a viral infection is present.Many, if not most, of these patients are unaware that antibiotics do not work on viruses.Interestingly, of the people who have only viral infections and see the doctor, those who take antibiotics tend to return to full health faster than do those who are not prescribed antibiotics.Which of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent paradox?

answers.text: Most antibiotics come with side effects, such as the killing of a patient’s beneficial gut bacteria, which are known to compose a major component of a healthy immune system.
Most viral infections will clear without medical intervention within a relatively short period of time.
Patients who feel that their doctors are caring for them properly are more likely than those who don’t to follow their doctors’ advice to rest, hydrate, and eat healthy foods.
When people experiencing illness believe that they are actively taking steps to become healthy, they experience reductions in anxiety, which is known to interfere with immune system functioning.
Generally, those who can afford to see a doctor can also afford beneficial treatments, such as massage, supplementation, and energy work, that are known to speed healing.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Although antibiotics are useful as treatment for bacterial infections only and have no effect on viruses, doctors often prescribe antibiotics to patients in whom only a viral infection is present.
Fact 2: Many, if not most, of these patients are unaware that antibiotics do not work on viruses.

Fact 3: Interestingly, of the people who have only viral infections and see the doctor, those who take antibiotics tend to return to full health faster than do those who are not prescribed antibiotics.

Picture it: Antibiotics can affect a bacterial infection, but they have no effect on viral infections.Be that as it may, doctors often give antibiotics to patients with viral infections, and curiously, these patients tend to return to full health faster than do those who have a viral infection and who are not prescribed antibiotics.Why would that be? What could be happening here?
(A) Most antibiotics come with side effects, such as the killing of a patient’s beneficial gut bacteria, which are known to compose a major component of a healthy immune system.
What this answer choice says actually deepens the paradox.

This choice indicates that antibiotics can kill a patient’s beneficial gut bacteria, which are known to compose a major component of a healthy immune system.So, taking antibiotics could result in a weakening of a person’s immune system.

So, since people use their immune systems in combating viral infections, we would expect people who take antibiotics to tend to recover from viral infections, not more quickly, but more slowly than do people who do not take antibiotics.After all, if a person’s immune system is damaged by antibiotics, that person is likely less able to fight a viral infection than is a person who has an undamaged immune system.

Since this choice provides a reason to believe that the result of taking antibiotics would be slower, not faster, return to health by people experiencing viral infections, what this answer choice says leaves us even more confused than we were.
We must be careful not to choose as correct a choice that deepens a paradox.
(B) Most viral infections will clear without medical intervention within a relatively short period of time.
That most viral infections will clear without medical intervention within a relatively short period of time does not resolve the paradox.
What we are seeking to explain is why people in one group, people who take antibiotics, tend to recover faster than people in another group, those who do not take antibiotics.
The fact that they all would recover regardless of whether any take antibiotics does not explain why there is adifferencebetween the rates of recovery of the two groups.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Patients who feel that their doctors are caring for them properly are more likely than those who don’t to follow their doctors’ advice to rest, hydrate, and eat healthy foods.

This choice could be tempting because we could decide that patients whose doctors prescribe antibiotics are more likely to believe that their doctors are caring for them properly than are patients whose doctors do not prescribe antibiotics.In that case, given what this choice says, we’d expect the people prescribed antibiotics to return to health faster than the others.
However, the truth is that we don’t have any information indicating that the people in the two groups have different opinions on their doctors’ care.

People who are not prescribed antibiotics could perceive their doctors to be caring for them properly.Maybe their doctors tell them that antibiotics do not affect viral infections, in which case, if their doctors were to prescribe antibiotics, they would feel that their doctors werenotcaring for them properly.
We have to be careful not to make an unsupported assumption about the facts presented in a choice.

Because we do not have information that clearly connects what this choice says about “patients who feel that their doctors are caring for them properly” to either of the two groups of people involved in the situation described, this choice does not explain why the people prescribed antibiotics tend to recover faster than the others.

CORRECT ANSWER(D) When people experiencing illness believe that they are actively taking steps to become healthy, they experience reductions in anxiety, which is known to interfere with immune system functioning.

Notice that the passage says that “Many, if not most, of these patients are unaware that antibiotics do not work on viruses.” Therefore, patients who are taking antibiotics, in general, are not aware that their taking antibiotics for viral infections is not a productive move.
In other words, people taking antibiotics could be feeling that they are actively taking steps to become healthy.

In this case, what this answer choice says, that “When people experiencing illness believe that they are actively taking steps to become healthy, they experience reductions in anxiety, which is known to interfere with immune system functioning,” would certainly explain why people taking antibiotics recover more quickly from viral infections than other people.The people taking antibiotics reduce anxiety, and thereby, improve the functioning of their immune systems, which in turn clear the viral infections faster than they otherwise would have.
(E) Generally, those who can afford to see a doctor can also afford beneficial treatments, such as massage, supplementation, and energy work, that are known to speed healing.

That generally, those who can afford to see a doctor can also afford beneficial treatments, such as massage, supplements, and energy work, that are known to speed healing does not help us resolve the paradox.After all, the passage tells us that both groups of people, those who take antibiotics and those who do not take antibiotics, see doctors.Because both groups of people see doctors, what this answer choice says applies to both groups of people.
Therefore this choice does not tell us about a difference between people in the two groups that would explain why those who take antibiotics return to health faster than the others do.

question.text: The profit margins on sales of new cars are typically very low.A new car purchased by a car dealer from a manufacturer for $25,000 might have a sticker price of $28,000 and be sold for $26,500 after a customer negotiates a lower price.The $1,500 made on the sale of the car must then help cover numerous dealership expenses, such as electricity, marketing, and health insurance for the dealership’s personnel, covering which might effectively reduce the $1,500 to $100, an amount too small to keep a dealership in business, even if the dealership sells thousands of cars.Yet, in many cities, there are dozens of new car dealers selling new cars.Which of the following, if true, would best resolve the apparent contradiction described above?

answers.text: Many new car dealerships use deceptive advertising to entice many customers to come into their dealerships and shop for new cars.
Most new car dealerships belong to larger conglomerates that own and operate multiple new car dealerships in many cities.
The salespeople in new car dealerships are skilled at negotiation, often getting customers to pay for new cars significantly more money than they intended to pay.
Most buyers of new cars cannot afford to pay in full at the time of purchase and must borrow money from dealerships at high interest rates.
Many new car dealerships are exceptionally effective at keeping operating expenses, such as costs of electricity, marketing, and health insurance, as low as possible.

explanation_parts.text: (A) Many new car dealerships use deceptive advertising to entice many customers to come into their dealerships and shop for new cars.

Notice that, even if what this answer choice says, that many new car dealerships use deceptive advertising to entice many customers to come into their dealerships and shop for new cars, is true, the facts of the passage do not change.In other words, once the customers get to the dealership the economics described in the passage still stand.

Regardless of how many customers dealerships entice to come and shop for new cars, it is still the case that each time a customer purchases a car, a dealership seems to make only the tiny profit that the passage describes as “an amount too small to keep a dealership in business.”
If the economics described in the passage still stand, then why in many cities are there dozens of new car dealers selling new cars?
(B) Most new car dealerships belong to larger conglomerates that own and operate multiple new car dealerships in many cities.

Notice that, even if what this answer choice says, that most new car dealerships belong to larger conglomerates that own and operate multiple new car dealerships, is true, the key aspects of the situation remain the same.
