If the economics of selling new cars are poor, then they will be poor for a large conglomerate that owns dealerships just as they are for individual dealerships.An “amount too small to keep a dealership in business” would also be an amount too small to keep a conglomerate of dealerships in business.
So, why in many cities are there are dozens of new car dealers selling new cars?
(C) The salespeople in new car dealerships are skilled at negotiation, often getting customers to pay for new cars significantly more money than they intended to pay.

Notice that what this answer choice says, that the salespeople in new car dealerships are skilled at negotiation, often getting customers to pay for new cars significantly more money than they intended to pay, does not change the key aspects of the situation described by the passage.

Let’s think about it.These skilled salespeople get customers to pay significantly more than they intended to pay.Great.Even so, the poor economics highlighted in the passage still exist.Even though the salespeople in new car dealerships are skilled at negotiation, the difference between what a customer pays for a new car and a dealership’s costs is “an amount too small to keep a dealership in business.”
So, why in many cities are there dozens of new car dealers selling new cars?
YOUR CHOICE(D) Most buyers of new cars cannot afford to pay in full at the time of purchase and must borrow money from dealerships at high interest rates.

If it is true that most buyers of new cars cannot afford to pay in full at the time of purchase and must borrow money from dealerships at high interest rates, then that borrowing at high interest rates would represent another profit stream that was not accounted for in the passage.

In other words, yes, the profit margins are low on the sale of new cars, but most people finance their purchases of cars, paying interest that constitutes an additional revenue stream for the dealers.This additional revenue stream could be sufficient to make dealerships profitable.
So, given what this choice says about buyers borrowing money from dealerships, we can see why, in many cities, there are dozens of new car dealers selling new cars.
What this choice says resolves the paradox.
(E) Many new car dealerships are exceptionally effective at keeping operating expenses, such as costs of electricity, marketing, and health insurance, as low as possible.

Even if it is true that many new car dealerships are exceptionally effective at keeping operating expenses, such as costs of electricity, marketing, and health insurance, as low as possible, the facts presented in the passage stand.

In other words, ok, many new car dealerships are exceptionally effective at keeping operating expenses low, andstill, the difference between a what a customer pays for a new car and a dealership’s costs is “an amount too small to keep a dealership in business.”

What this choice says does not really change what we know about the economics of operating a new car dealership and leaves us still wondering why, in many cities, there are dozens of new car dealers selling new cars.

question.text: Many new cars sold today include features that enable the cars to help prevent collisions by taking preemptive actions such as hitting the brakes before a collision or alerting drivers to hazards in their blind spots.The presence of these features results in reduced risk of collisions.However, the cost of collision insurance for cars with such features is generally higher than the cost of collision insurance for cars without such features.Which of the following best explains this seemingly paradoxical pattern?

answers.text: When cars that have sophisticated features become damaged, the cost to repair such cars is generally higher than the cost to repair similarly damaged cars without such features.
Some drivers of cars with features that automatically prevent collisions choose to turn off those features when driving such cars.
Cars without features that automatically prevent collisions experience reduced risk of being involved in collisions as a result of the presence of such features in other cars on the roads.
A driver operating a car with features that automatically prevent collisions may become complacent in driving, thus offsetting much of the benefit of having such features.
Currently available car features that automatically prevent collisions have little to no ability to prevent many of the most catastrophic types of high-speed car crashes.

explanation_parts.text: Background: Many new cars sold today include features that enable the cars to help prevent collisions by taking preemptive actions such as hitting the brakes before a collision or alerting drivers to hazards in their blind spots.
Fact 1: The presence of these features results in reduced risk of collisions.
Fact 2: The cost of collision insurance for cars with such features is generally higher than the cost of collision insurance for cars without such features.

Picture it: These new cars have these cool safety features that help drivers prevent collisions.Be that as it may, insurance for these cars cost more than insurance for cars without these cool safety features.Why would that be?

Our sensibilities tell us that we might, upon first analysis, feel that collision insurance for cars with these features should be cheaper because of the reduced risk of these cars’ being involved in collisions.To answer the question, we’ll be seeking an answer choice that resolves this seemingly paradoxical situation.

Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to find a choice that explains why the cost of insurance for cars with collision prevention features ishigherthan the cost of insurance for other cars.
YOUR CHOICE(A) When cars that have sophisticated features become damaged, the cost to repair such cars is generally higher than the cost to repair similarly damaged cars without such features.

This choice mentions “cars that have sophisticated features.” Of course, features that enable cars to prevent collisions are sophisticated features.So, it stands to reason that what this choice says applies to cars with features that prevent collisions.

Therefore, what this choice indicates is that, when cars that have collision prevention features become damaged, the cost to repair such cars is generally higher than the cost to repair similarly damaged cars without such features.

What follows then is that, even though cars with collision prevention features may be less likely than other cars to be involved in collisions, when cars with the features are involved in collisions, the cost of repairing them is generally relatively high.

Of course, in pricing collision insurance for cars with collision prevention features, the insurance companies would take into account not only the likelihood that the cars would be involved in collisions but also the cost to repair the cars if they are involved in collisions.We can, therefore, see why insurance for cars with collision prevention features would cost more than insurance for other cars: the insurance companies charge more to insure such cars because they expect to have to pay more to have such cars repaired.

So, this choice explains why the cost of collision insurance for cars with collision prevent features is generallyhigherthan the cost of collision insurance for other cars and resolves the apparent paradox.
(B) Some drivers of cars with features that automatically prevent collisions choose to turn off those features when driving such cars.
There are two important things to notice here.

Firstly, the word “some” is a vague term.Does “some” mean two people or two hundred million people? Of course, the answer to that question could make a significance difference in the result of “some” people turning off the features.

Secondly, and much more importantly, even if, for example, two hundred million people choose to turn off the collision features, all they would be doing, essentially, is making their carsjust as likely to be involved in collisions as other cars.Right? In other words, by turning off the features, they would be making their carsmore similarto other cars.

Something that indicates that cars with collision prevention features can be made similar to cars without such features certainly would not explain why the cost of insurance for cars with the features is higher than the cost of insurance for cars without the features.To explain why the insurance costs are different, we need a way in which the two kinds of cars aredifferentfrom each other.
(C) Cars without features that automatically prevent collisions experience reduced risk of being involved in collisions as a result of the presence of such features in other cars on the roads.

What this choice says indicates that collision prevention features had by some cars cause all cars, including the cars that don’t have the features, to have a reduced risk of being involved in collisions.

Given that information, what we would expect is that the presence of the features would cause the cost of collision insurance of all cars to be reduced, because the insurance companies would price the insurance in accordance with reduced likelihood of their having to pay for collision related repairs.
Of course, the fact that all cars have a reduced risk of being involved in collisions does not explain why the cost of insurance for some cars, the cars with the features, is higher.
(D) A driver operating a car with features that automatically prevent collisions may become complacent in driving, thus offsetting much of the benefit of having such features.

That a driver operating a car with features that automatically prevent collisions may become complacent in driving, thus offsetting much of the benefit of having such features does not help us to resolve the paradox.
Notice that this choice uses the word “much,” which conveys “some, but not all.”
As a result of the use of the word “much,” this choice conveys that some, but not all, of the benefit “may” be offset in certain cases.
That statement is not a very strong one, is it?
Also, that statement indicates that some of the benefitwill not beoffset.

Of course, as long as some of the benefit of collision prevention features is not offset by driver complacency, then a car with such features should be less likely to be involved in a collision than a car without such features.

So, even if a driver of a car with collision prevention features may become somewhat complacent and thus offset some of the benefit of the features, we’d still expect the cost of collision insurance for such a car to be relatively low.
Thus, this choice does not explain why the cost of collision insurance for cars with collision prevention features is higher than the cost of collision insurance for other cars.
(E) Currently available car features that automatically prevent collisions have little to no ability to prevent many of the most catastrophic types of high-speed car crashes.

That currently available car features that automatically prevent collisions have little to no ability to prevent many of the most catastrophic types of high-speed car crashes does not help us to resolve the paradox.

For one thing, “the most catastrophic types of high-speed car crashes” are not all crashes.They represent only a fraction of crashes.So, the features could fail to preventhigh-speedcrashes and still preventmostcrashes.

Furthermore, and more importantly, even if the featuresdid not prevent any crashes at all, the fact that they didn’t prevent crashes would make the cars that have the features basicallyjust like other cars.

So, we can see that the fact that the features don’t prevent all crashes means that the cars with the features are in a way similar to other cars.Neither cars with the features nor cars without the features prevent the most catastrophic types of high-speed car crashes.
The fact that cars with the features and cars without the features are similar in this way certainly would not explain why the cost of insurance for the cars with the features is higher.

question.text: Since automated teller machines (ATMs) can handle many of the tasks that are handled by tellers in banks, banks that use ATMs generally need fewer tellers per bank branch than would be necessary without the use of ATMs.In the United States, the number of ATMs increased by approximately 300 percent from 1995 to 2010.Yet, during the same time period, the number of tellers working for banks also increased.Which of the following, if true, best explains the surprising outcome?

answers.text: Since the use of ATMs reduces the costs of operating a branch, banks opened branches in locations where, without ATMs, branches would not have been profitable.
There are many tasks that bank tellers handle that are too sophisticated for ATMs to handle.
Some bank customers prefer to use tellers rather than ATMs, even for transactions that could be handled by ATMs.
Because many bank customers used ATMs, as ATM use increased, the lines associated with using bank tellers became shorter, on average, than such lines were in the past.
As ATMs took over handling many of the simpler tasks that tellers had handled, tellers took on more complex tasks and got paid more.

explanation_parts.text: Background:Since automated teller machines (ATMs) can handle many of the tasks that are handled by tellers in banks, banks that use ATMs generally need fewer tellers per bank branch than would be necessary without the use of ATMs.
Fact 1: In the United States, the number of ATMs increased by approximately 300 percent from 1995 to 2010.
Fact 2: During the same time period, the number of tellers working for banks also increased.
What Has to Be Explained: How could it have been that, even as the number of ATMs increased by approximately 300 percent from 1995 to 2010, the number of tellers working for banks also increased?
Picture it: Although ATMs can handle many of the tasks that bank tellers handle, the number of tellers working for banks increased even as the presence of ATMs increased.Why?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain anincrease.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Since the use of ATMs reduces the costs of operating a branch, banks opened branches in locations where, without ATMs, branches would not have been profitable.

We know from the passage that “banks that use ATMs generally needfewertellers per bank branch than would be necessary without the use of ATMs.” So, it stands to reason that bank branches will still needsometellers, even at branches with ATMs.Thus, if banks opened branches in locations where, without ATMs, they otherwise could not have, more bank tellers would have been hired to staff those new branches.
Thus, the fact that, even as the use of ATMs increased, the number of bank branches also increased could explain why the number of bank tellers increased.
What this answer choice says explains the increase in the number of tellers and resolves the apparent paradox.
(B) There are many tasks that bank tellers handle that are too sophisticated for ATMs to handle.

This choice is tempting because the fact that there are many tasks that bank tellers handle that are too sophisticated for ATMs to handle could seem to be a reason why banks would increase the numbers of tellers they employed.

However, tellers would have handled those tasksbeforethe increase in the number of ATMs, not justwhilethe increase in the number of ATMs was occurring.In other words, what this choice says did not become true when ATM use was increasing.It was true all along.
Something that was true all along would not explain why the number of tellers increased while the number of ATMs was increasing.

Furthermore, the passage has stated that “banks that use ATMs generally need fewer tellers per bank branch than would be necessary without the use of ATMs.” What this choice says does not negate that fact.In other words,even thoughtellers can handle tasks too sophisticated for ATMs to handle, banks that use ATMs need fewer tellers per branch than they would need if they weren’t using ATMs.
So, despite what this choice says, we would have expected the number of tellers to decrease as the number of ATMs used increased.
We can see that this choice does not explain the increase in the number of tellers.
(C) Some bank customers prefer to use tellers rather than ATMs, even for transactions that could be handled by ATMs.

What this answer says goes in the wrong direction to explain what we need to explain: As the number of ATMs increased by approximately 300 percent from 1995 to 2010, why did the number of tellers working for banks also increase?

What this answer says could explain why the number of tellers remained constant (if that were the case).It could have been that the number of tellers remained constant because, even though ATMs were available, some customers preferred to and, therefore, continued to use tellers.

To use what this answer choice says to resolve the paradox, we would need some other information in conjunction with it, such as “The number of customers increased by 5000 percent,” in which case we would have, “Some customers prefer to use tellers, and the number of customers increased by 5000 percent.” However, of course, such information is not provided to us, and we surely can’t make any unwarranted assumptions.
On its own without additional information or unwarranted assumptions, this choice does not explain the increase in the number of tellers.
(D) Because many bank customers used ATMs, as ATM use increased, the lines associated with using bank tellers became shorter, on average, than such lines were in the past.

Don’t create stories such as “If the lines for tellers became shorter, it must be true that there were more tellers, because more tellers means shorter wait times.” Even if that story were true, it does not explain WHY the number of tellers increased.
Meanwhile, the truth is that what this choice says has an effect opposite the one we need.
The facts that fewer customers were using tellers and that lines for tellers became shorter tend to indicate that banks neededfewertellers, not more.

So, this choice explains the wrong thing.It seems to explain why, as the passage said, banks that use ATMs generally need fewer tellers per bank branch than would be necessary without the use of ATMs: fewer customers use tellers, and therefore, banks need fewer tellers.
This choice certainly does not explain the increase in the number of tellers.
(E) As ATMs took over handling many of the simpler tasks that tellers had handled, tellers took on more complex tasks and got paid more.
To make this choice work, we’d need a story is not supported by the passage.

For example, “Because bank tellers did more complicated tasks and were, therefore, paid more, more and more people wanted to become bank tellers, and therefore the number of bank tellers increased.” It’s important to see that that story has more assumptions than a mystery novel; that story is not supported by what the passage says.

Furthermore, what the passage does say is that “banks that use ATMs generally need fewer tellers per bank branch than would be necessary without the use of ATMs.” What this choice says does not negate that fact.In other words,even thoughtellers took on more complex tasks and got paid more, banks that use ATMs need fewer tellers per branch than they would need if they weren’t using ATMs.So, even if more people wanted to become tellers because of the increase in how much tellers get paid, banks would not have hired those people.
Thus, despite what this choice says, we would have expected the number of tellers to decrease as the number of ATMs used increased.
We can see that this choice does not explain the increase in the number of tellers.

question.text: An experimental program in France involved having hiring companies that received resumes from the state employment agency volunteer to receive the resumes they got from the agency in anonymous form, i.e., without the applicants’ names or addresses.The purpose of the program was to determine whether using such resumes would reduce hiring discrimination against recent immigrants, who often could be identified as such as a result of their names or places of residence.Surprisingly, while participating in the program, the companies reduced rather than increased their hiring of recent immigrants.Which of the following, if true, best explains why the results of the program were different from those that might have been reasonably expected?

answers.text: In France, the resumes of recent immigrants are more likely to include significant gaps in work history than are those of other job applicants.
No companies known to have track records of hiring discrimination against recent immigrants participated in the program.
In addition to receiving anonymous resumes from the agency, the companies involved in the program continued to receive resumes with names and addresses directly from private individuals.
In many cases, it is possible to determine via considering aspects of a resume other than name and address that it is likely that an applicant is a recent immigrant.
The hiring practices of most of the companies that volunteered to receive anonymous resumes included making allowances for minor deficiencies in resumes of recent immigrants.

explanation_parts.text: Our job in answering this question is to find an answer choice that resolves the paradox presented to us.

Why is it that, after the names and addresses, which could be used to identify recent immigrants as recent immigrants, were removed from the resumes did the companies reduce rather than increase their hiring of recent immigrants?
What could have been responsible for this puzzling outcome?
(A) In France, the resumes of recent immigrants are more likely to include significant gaps in work history than are those of other job applicants.

This choice is a tempting trap, because the fact that resumes of recent immigrants are more likely to include significant gaps in work history than are those of other job applicants might seem to explain why the companies reduced their hiring of recent immigrants.Even with the names and addresses gone, the resumes would still have included those gaps.
Notice the following, however.

The fact that resumes of recent immigrants are more likely to include significant gaps in work history than those of other job applicants hasnot changedwith the anonymizing of the resumes.In other words, these significant gaps in work history would have been present on the resumes of immigrants regardless of whether an applicant’s name and address were present.

The resumes that the companies were getting from recent immigrants before participating in the program would have included gaps in work history, and the anonymous resumes associated with the program would also have included gaps in work history.
So, this aspect of the resumes, the gaps, has not changed.
Something that has not changed would not have caused the change, i.e., the reduction, that occurred.
(B) No companies known to have track records of hiring discrimination against recent immigrants participated in the program.

The fact that no companies known to have track records of hiring discrimination against recent immigrants participated in the program does not explain the paradox.We don’t care about companies that did not participate; we care about the ones that did participate and about why these participating companies reduced rather than increased their hiring of recent immigrants.

Now, if there had been no change in hiring of recent immigrants, this choice could explain why there was no change.If no companies known to discriminate participated in the program, then maybe only companies thatweren’t discriminating anywayparticipated in the program.In that case, we could see why anonymizing the resumes wouldn’t have made any difference, because the companies wouldn’t have been discriminating even when the resumes were not anonymized.
However, we don’t have to explain why there was no change.We have to explain a reduction in hiring of immigrants, and this choice does not explain that.
(C) In addition to receiving anonymous resumes from the agency, the companies involved in the program continued to receive resumes with names and addresses directly from private individuals.

That fact that, in addition to receiving anonymous resumes from the agency, the companies involved in the program continued to receive resumes with names and addresses directly from private individuals does not resolve the paradox.The companies were getting resumes from private individuals before the program, and they were getting them from private individuals while participating in the program.

In other words, what this answer choice says does not highlight a change that occurred when these companies entered into the program and received resumes without names and addresses.This choice highlights something that remained the same.

In addition, even if companies involved in the program continued to receive resumes with names and addresses directly from private individuals, the fact remains that these companies now also received the resumes without names and addresses.So, why then did the companies reduce rather than increase their hiring of recent immigrants?
The fact that something, receiving resumes from private individuals, remained the same, might have explained why things didn’t change,ifthey hadn’t changed.
However, it would not explain the reduction in hiring of immigrants that occurred.
(D) In many cases, it is possible to determine via considering aspects of a resume other than name and address that it is likely that an applicant is a recent immigrant.
What this answer choice says goes in the wrong direction.

In conveying that, in many cases, the companies did not actually need an applicant’s name and address to determine whether that person was an immigrant, this choice indicates that removing the names and addresses didn’t change the situation as much as it may have seemed to.Sure, the resumes were anonymized, but, in many cases, the companies could still tell whether applicants were recent immigrants just by looking at the resumes (without needing names or addresses).So, in many cases, anonymizing the resumes didn’t make the difference that it was intended to make; the resumes still indicated that the applicants were recent immigrants.
Since this choice shows that things didn’t change as much as they seemed to, this choice could possibly explain why hiring of immigrants didn’t change much,ifindeed it hadn’t changed much.

However, hiring of immigrants did change.It was reduced, and the fact that changing the resumes didn’t change things as much as it may have seemed to change them does not explain why that reduction occurred.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The hiring practices of most of the companies that volunteered to receive anonymous resumes included making allowances for minor deficiencies in resumes of recent immigrants.

If it is true that the hiring practices of most of the companies that volunteered to receive anonymous resumes included “making allowances for minor deficiencies in resumes of recent immigrants,” then these companies were making a point of allowing immigrants leeway in deciding whether to hire them.Right?

So, we can see that, if most of the companies that volunteered to receive anonymous resumes normally made “allowances for minor deficiencies in resumes of recent immigrants,” then these companies were clearly willing to accept applicants whom they maynothave accepted had those applicants not been immigrants.

Of course, then, once the names and addresses of the applicants were removed from the resumes, these companies no longer had the ability to “make allowances for minor deficiencies in resumes of recent immigrants.” As a result of their losing that ability, they were no longer able to decide to consider or hire the immigrants even though the immigrants’ resumes had minor deficiencies.
In such a situation, we can see why, while participating in the program, the companies reduced rather than increased their hiring of recent immigrants.

question.text: Because many have argued that use of the College Aptitude Test (CAT) in the college admissions process puts students from lower-income families, who tend to score lower on the CAT than students from higher income families, at a disadvantage, colleges in Urbana have made the CAT optional, with the result that many applicants from lower-income families have stopped submitting CAT scores with their applications.However, since making the change, instead of enrolling greater numbers of students from lower-income families, colleges in Urbana have enrolled the lowest numbers of such students enrolled in decades.Each of the following, if true, helps to explain the puzzling outcome described above, EXCEPT:

answers.text: After making the CAT optional, colleges were forced to place significantly more emphasis on applicants’ high school grades, which are often significantly inflated at schools in more affluent communities.
Funding committees have continued to award scholarships and grants, upon which many students rely heavily to make college affordable, to those applicants with the highest CAT scores.

The CAT score report contains information indicating which students are from lower-income families, with the result that admissions officers can readily assign extra points to such students’ applications.
With the CAT optional, admissions officers began to perceive applicants who invested time and energy into taking the CAT as those most motivated to excel in college.
Research done by colleges has demonstrated that applicants’ CAT scores are accurate predictors of how those applicants will go on to perform academically in college.

explanation_parts.text: Background: Many have argued that use of the College Aptitude Test (CAT) in the college admissions process puts students from lower-income families, who tend to score lower on the CAT than students from higher income families, at a disadvantage.
Fact 1: Colleges in Urbana have made the CAT optional, with the result that many applicants from lower-income families have stopped submitting CAT scores with their applications.
Fact 2: Since making the change, instead of enrolling greater numbers of students from lower-income families, colleges in Urbana have enrolled the lowest numbers of such students enrolled in decades.

What has to be explained: Why is it that, after colleges made the CAT optional and many applicants from lower-income families stopped submitting CAT scores with their applications, colleges in Urbana enrolled fewer students from lower-income families than the colleges had enrolled before?
Notice that, in order to answer this question correctly, we have to explain adecrease.

Remember that, since this is an EXCEPT question, four choices will explain why the colleges enrolled fewer students from lower-income families.Those choices will be incorrect.The remaining choice willnotexplain why the colleges enrolled fewer such students, and will be the correct answer.

(A) After making the CAT optional, colleges were forced to place significantly more emphasis on applicants’ high school grades, which are often significantly inflated at schools in more affluent communities.
What this answer choice says certainly helps to resolve the paradox.

After all, if, after making the CAT optional, colleges were forced to place significantly more emphasis on applicants’ high school grades, which are often significantly inflated at schools found in more affluent communities, then, without the CAT, students enrolled in schools in more affluent communities would have had an unfair advantage over their less affluent counterparts.Colleges would have been comparing inflated grades of applicants from more affluent communities with the, presumably, not inflated grades of lower-income applicants.

In other words, we see that the CAT was actually serving as a tool that leveled, not skewed, the playing field, by providing a way for colleges to evaluate applicants on the basis of something other than high school grades, which are often inflated at schools in more affluent communities.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
(B) Funding committees have continued to award scholarships and grants, upon which many students rely heavily to make college affordable, to those applicants with the highest CAT scores.
What this answer choice says certainly helps to resolve the paradox.

We would certainly expect that students from lower-income families would be more dependent on scholarships and grants to make college affordable than students from more affluent communities would be.Therefore, if it is true that funding committees have continued to award scholarships and grants, upon which many students rely heavily to make college affordable, to those applicants with the highest CAT scores, then we can clearly see why colleges in Urbana have enrolled the lowest numbers of lower-income students enrolled in decades.

Because lower-income students are no longer submitting CAT scores with their applications, these students are getting fewer scholarships and grants, and thus, these students have lost an important source of funding for college expenses.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.

(C) The CAT score report contains information indicating which students are from lower-income families, with the result that admissions officers can readily assign extra points to such students’ applications.
What this answer choice says certainly helps to resolve the paradox.

If it is true that the CAT score report contains information indicating which students are from lower-income families, with the result that admissions officers can readily assign extra points to such students’ applications, then we can certainly see why colleges in Urbana have enrolled the lowest numbers of students from lower-income families in decades.

Since colleges in Urbana have made the CAT optional, with the result that many applicants from lower-income families have stopped submitting CAT scores with their applications, the colleges have lost their ability to assign a few extra points to lower-income students’ applications, because they can no longer tell which applications belong to such students.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) With the CAT optional, admissions officers began to perceive applicants who invested time and energy into taking the CAT as those most motivated to excel in college.
What this answer choice says certainly helps to resolve the paradox.

If it is true that, with the CAT optional, admissions officers began to perceive applicants who invested time and energy into preparing for and taking the CAT as those most motivated to excel in college, and if it is true that, since colleges in Urbana have made the CAT optional, many applicants from lower-income families have stopped submitting CAT scores with their applications, then we can certainly see why colleges in Urbana have enrolled the lowest numbers of students from lower-income families in decades.

Since lower-income students are no longer submitting CAT scores, admissions officers are perceiving the lower-income students as less motivated to excel in college than other applicants are and, therefore, are not admitting as many lower-income students as they admitted in the past.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Research done by colleges has demonstrated that applicants’ CAT scores are accurate predictors of how those applicants will go on to perform academically in college.
What this answer choice says does not help to resolve the paradox.
What this answer choice says is essentially that CAT scores have predictive value.

The fact that CAT scores have predictive value does not really affect the strength of applications of people who do or do not take the CAT.That fact doesn’t make the application of someone who took the CAT look stronger, and it doesn’t make the application of someone who didn’t take the CAT look weaker.

All that the fact that CAT scores have predictive value means is that a CAT score included in an application provides an additional way for an admissions officer to predict how the applicant will do in college.

Therefore, there’s no clear reason to believe that the fact that CAT scores have predictive value would cause colleges not to accept applicants who did not take the CAT.Those applicants’ applications would still be full of information upon which the colleges could base decisions.

So, this choice does not explain why, since making the change, instead of enrolling greater numbers of students from low-income families, colleges in Urbana have enrolled the lowest numbers of such students enrolled in decades.
Resolves the paradox? No.

question.text: One hundred years ago, words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing, such as “whereby” or “such that,” represented a percentage of printed text much greater than the percentage of printed text represented by such words today.This change has occurred even though the proportion of the world population considered well educated has increased many fold over the same period of time.Which of the following, if true, best explains the apparent discrepancy between the two trends described above?

answers.text: The number of living people considered poorly educated or illiterate has increased significantly over the past hundred years.
Currently, people often employ words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing for the purpose of making writing that is not particularly sophisticated seem sophisticated.
Over time, the popularity of words or expressions can increase or decrease significantly.
Over the past hundred years, equipment used for generating printed text has become increasingly available to people with little education.
It is often possible to convey sophisticated ideas without the use of words or expressions normally associated with sophisticated writing.

explanation_parts.text: We know that, one hundred years ago, words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing, such as “whereby” or “such that,” represented a percentage of printed text much greater than the percentage of printed text represented by such words today.We see that that phenomenon is paradoxical because this change has occurred even though the proportion of the world population considered well educated has increased many fold over the same period of time.

If the proportion of the world population considered well educated has increased many fold, then why has the percentage of printed text represented by words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing decreased?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explaina decrease in a percentage.
(A) The number of living people considered poorly educated or illiterate has increased significantly over the past hundred years.
Notice that what this answer choice says does not change the facts provided to us.

The passage says that “the proportion of the world population considered well educated has increased many fold over the same period of time.” Therefore, even though what this choice says, that the number of living people considered poorly educated or illiterate has increased, is true, the percentage of people represented by people considered well educated has increased.

Remember, absolute numbers and percentages are different things.So, if the total world population has increased, it can be that thenumberof living people considered poorly educated or illiterate has increased even though thepercentageof all people represented by well-educated people has increased.

So, regardless of what this choice says, it is still the case that the percentage of people represented by well-educated people has increased.Thus, we are still wondering why words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing have become less common in printed text.
(B) Currently, people often employ words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing for the purpose of making writing that is not particularly sophisticated seem sophisticated.

Notice that what this answer choice says would deepen the paradox.After all, if it is true that currently, people often employ words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing for the purpose of making writing that is not particularly sophisticated seem sophisticated, then we would expect to see an increase in the usage of these sophisticated words, not a decrease.
(C) Over time, the popularity of words or expressions can increase or decrease significantly.

Notice that what this answer choice says provides us with the opportunity to go in different directions.Does the fact that, over time, the popularity of words or expressions can increase or decrease significantly deepen or resolve the paradox?

We have to be careful not to take what this choice says as indicating that the popularity of sophisticated words and expressions did in fact either increase or decrease significantly.This choice does not say that the popularity of sophisticated words and expressionsdid in factdecrease, or increase.All this choice indicates is that the popularity of words or expressionscan change.

So, did the popularity of sophisticated words and expressions increase? Decrease? Remain the same? Given what this choice says, the popularity of sophisticated words and expressions could have done any of the three.

Since this choice conveys only that a variety of things could have occurred and does not tell us which of them actually occurred, it does not explain why sophisticated words and expressions have become less common in printed text.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Over the past hundred years, equipment used for generating printed text has become increasingly available to people with little education.

If it is true that, over the past hundred years, equipment used for generating printed text has become increasingly available to people with little education, then we can see why the use of words or expressions associated with sophisticated writing has become less common in printed text, even though the proportion of the world population considered well educated has increased over the same period of time.

What has likely occurred is that, since printing equipment has become increasingly available to people with little education, the proportion of printed text created by people with little education has increased.Generally, people with little education would not use sophisticated words or expressions in the printed text they create.So, since people with little education are now creating a greater proportion of the printed text created, the proportion of printed text created by people whodo usesophisticated words or expressions hasdecreased.

So, this choice explains why the use of sophisticated words or expressions has become less common in printed text, by providing a reason to believe that an increasing proportion of printed text is created by people who would not use such words or expressions.
(E) It is often possible to convey sophisticated ideas without the use of words or expressions normally associated with sophisticated writing.

What this answer choice says was true one hundred years ago and what it says is also true today.People could have chosen to convey sophisticated ideas without the use of words or expressions normally associated with sophisticated writing one hundred years ago, and they can choose to do so today.
In other words, this choice describes something that is constant.Something that is constant would not help us to explain the change described by the passage.

question.text: Every test-taker’s results on a widely used college entrance exam include a quant score, a verbal score, and a total score that is based on the test-taker’s quant and verbal scores.Generally, there is a positive correlation between the levels of test-takers’ quant and verbal scores; test-takers who score relatively high on quant tend to score relatively high on verbal.However, the test results of Brewart University students who have not been awarded scholarships by the university show a negative correlation between quant and verbal scores; the students who have quant scores higher than those of the others not awarded scholarships tend to have lower verbal scores, and vice versa.Of the following, which best explains why the pattern of these students’ results differs from that of results on the exam in general?

answers.text: By preparing for the college entrance exam, a prospective test-taker can achieve a quant score or a verbal score significantly higher than that test-taker would achieve without preparation.
Unlike the programs at many universities, the programs at Brewart University are known to be particularly strong in both sciences and engineering and humanities and social sciences.
Brewart University awards scholarships to all students who have achieved total scores higher than a particular level on the college entrance exam.
On the college entrance exam, quant and verbal questions appear in two separate sections, the order of appearance on the test of which is chosen by the test-taker.
The average total score on the college entrance exam of Brewart University students who have not been awarded scholarships is higher than the average total score of all test-takers.

explanation_parts.text: (A) By preparing for the college entrance exam, a prospective test-taker can achieve a quant score or a verbal score significantly higher than that test-taker would achieve without preparation.

What this answer choice says would indicate that test preparation would drive all scores higher.After all, if by preparing for the college entrance exam, a prospective test-taker can achieve a quant score or a verbal score significantly higher than that test-taker would achieve without preparation, then we would expect to see that those who prepared scored relatively high on both sections of the exam.However, what this answer choice says doesn’t explain the phenomenon mentioned in the passage.

The fact that test-takers can score higher by preparing does not explain why, when one of a student’s two scores is higher than that of other students the other score is lower than that of other students.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Unlike the programs at many universities, the programs at Brewart University are known to be particularly strong in both sciences and engineering and humanities and social sciences.
Notice that we really don’t know what this choice really means.

For example, what this answer choice says could seem to deepen the paradox if you took the sentence to mean that the students at Brewart University are, generally, very skilled in in both sciences and engineering and humanities and social sciences.Right? In that case, why would it be true that the students who have quant scores higher than those of the others tend to have lower verbal scores, and vice versa? In that case, we would expect the students to have both strong quant skills and strong verbal skills.

On the other hand, if you took what this sentence says to mean that some students at Brewart University take only sciences and engineering classes (and are strong in math but weak in verbal), and that some other students take only classes in humanities and social sciences (and are strong in verbal but weak in math), then you might have felt that what this answer choice says does resolve the paradox.
Because we could interpret what this answer choice says in multiple ways, it cannot help us to explain the paradoxical situation.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Brewart University awards scholarships to all students who have achieved total scores higher than a particular level on the college entrance exam.
Notice what this choice tells us.It tells us that Brewart University students who have total scores on the entrance exam higher than a particular level get scholarships.

How would students achieve total scores higher than that particular level? Since their total scores are based on their quant scores and their verbal scores, they would achieve total scores above that particular level by scoring high enough on the quant section and high enough on the verbal section for their total scores to be higher than that particular level.

Of course, the pattern of scores that we have to explain involves students who have not been awarded scholarships.To understand why this choice explains that pattern, we have to consider why these students have not been awarded scholarships.
The reason they have not been awarded scholarships is that they didnotscore higher than that particular level on the entrance exam.So, these students’ total scores were allbelowthat particular level.
Now, think about the following.What would happen if a student were to score very high on BOTH quant and verbal? The student would achieve a high total score and, presumably, get a scholarship.
Therefore, in order not to get a scholarship, a student would have to achieve a total score below that particular level by doing, essentially, one of the following:
- Score low on both quant and verbal.
- Score high on quant and low on verbal.
- Score low on quant and high on verbal.
We can see that the above are the only three ways not to get a scholarship.The fourth option – score high on both quant and verbal – gets a student a scholarship.

Now, we can see why the quant and verbal scores of students who don’t get scholarships tend to be negatively correlated.Such a student’s quant and verbal scores could both be low.However, it can’t be the case that both are high, because if both were high, the student would have gotten a scholarship.So, in order not to get a scholarship, a student who scores relatively high on quant has to score relatively low on verbal, and a student who scores relatively high on verbal has to score relatively low on quant.

Thus, we can see that what this choice says, that Brewart University awards scholarships to all students who have achieved total scores higher than a particular level on the college entrance exam, explains exactly why the quant scores and verbal scores of students not awarded scholarships tend to be negatively correlated.
(D) On the college entrance exam, quant and verbal questions appear in two separate sections, the order of appearance on the test of which is chosen by the test-taker.

What this choice says, that on the college entrance exam, quant and verbal questions appear in two separate sections, the order of appearance on the test of which is chosen by the test-taker, could lead us in multiple directions.
Maybe this information indicates that, by choosing the section order, students can cause their quant and verbal scores to be similar.

Maybe this information indicates that, because one section follows the other, students tend to score relatively high on whichever section they choose to take first and relatively low on whichever section they take second.
So, we just can’t say whether this choice deepens or resolves the paradox.Right?
It’s important to not make up unsupported stories.
(E) The average total score on the college entrance exam of Brewart University students who have not been awarded scholarships is higher than the average total score of all test-takers.

Notice that this answer choice is about Brewart University students who have not been awarded scholarships’ total scores only and does not provide us with any information on their quant scores or verbal scores.
Furthermore, this choice does not provide any information on why Brewart University students who have not been awarded scholarships score how they score on the exam.
What this choice tells us is theresultof how these students perform on the quant and verbal portions of the exam: their average total score is higher than the average total score of all test-takers.

This information on the result of how the students perform on the quant and verbal portions of the exam does not indicate why they perform how they perform or why their quant and verbal scores are negatively correlated.

question.text: In the past, most people in Minlehoma first got driver’s licenses at age 16, and drivers aged 16 to 17 have been those most likely to get into automobile crashes.However, in recent years, many people have been putting off getting licenses until after reaching age 18.Since this change has resulted in a decrease in the number of drivers aged 16 to 17 on the roads, the number of automobile crashes per mile driven in Minlehoma was expected to decrease.However, the number of automobile crashes per mile driven in Minlehoma has remained approximately the same.Which of the following does the most to explain why the expected change did not occur?

answers.text: The average age at which people in Minlehoma first get driver’s licenses has been increasing slowly but steadily.
In Minlehoma, only people who wish to get a driver’s license before their eighteenth birthday are required to undergo extensive driver safety training.
Regardless of a person’s age, in order to receive a first driver’s license in Minlehoma, the person must pass a written test and a rigorous driving test.
The speed limits on the highways in Minlehoma today are, in most locations, the same as they were in the past.
Unlike other drivers in Minlehoma, drivers under age 18 are not allowed to use hands-free cell phones to communicate while driving.

explanation_parts.text: Background:In the past, most people in Minlehoma first got driver’s licenses at age 16, and drivers aged 16 to 17 have been those most likely to get into automobile crashes.In recent years, many people have been putting off getting licenses until after reaching age 18.
Fact 1: This change has resulted in a decrease in the number of drivers aged 16 to 17 on the roads.
Fact 2:The number of automobile crashes per mile driven in Minlehoma has remained approximately the same.

Picture it: In Minlehoma, most people got driver’s licenses at age 16, and drivers aged 16 to 17 have been those most likely to get into crashes, but now many people are waiting until they reach age 18 to get a license.Therefore, the number of automobile crashes per mile driven in Minlehoma was expected to decrease.However, the number has remained the same.What could explain this unexpected result?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain why the number of crashes per mile drivenremained the same.
(A) The average age at which people in Minlehoma first get driver’s licenses has been increasing slowly but steadily.

It’s important to remember that the statements in the passage are factual.The passage tells us that “in the past, most people in Minlehoma first got driver’s licenses at age 16” and that “in recent years, many people have been putting off getting licenses until after reaching age 18.”

The fact that the average age at which people in Minlehoma first get driver’s licenses has been increasing slowly but steadily does not really change what we know about the situation.Regardless of how quickly or slowly and steadily or unsteadily the average age at which people in Minlehoma first get driver’s licenses has increased, the fact stated in the passage about the age at which people first get driver’s licenses is still true.
So, with the addition of what this choice says, what we know about the situation remains essentially the same, and the paradox remains unresolved.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In Minlehoma, only people who wish to get a driver’s license before their eighteenth birthday are required to undergo extensive driver safety training.

If, as this choice says, the only people in Minlehoma who are required to undergo extensive driver safety training are those who wish to get a driver’s license before they reach age 18, but, as the passage says, more people are waiting until after they reach age 18 to get a license, then it’s likely that fewer people are getting safety training.So, we might have expected the number of automobile crashes to increase.

However, we must remember that the passage tells us that “drivers aged 16 to 17 have been those most likely to get into automobile crashes” and that now, a decrease in the number of drivers aged 16 to 17 on the roads has occurred.So, we know that the number of drivers in the group that get into the most automobile crashes has decreased.

So, we have two countervailing forces.Fewer people are getting driver safety training, a development that would result in an increase in the number of crashes.At the same time there are fewer drivers aged 16 to 17 on the roads, a development that would result in a decrease in the number of crashes.

So, we can see why the number of automobile crashes per mile driven has remained approximately the same.Even though there are fewer drivers in the “risky” 16 to 17 year old age group on the roads, any reduction in the number of crashes that has resulted from that development has been offset by an increase in the number of crashes that has resulted from the fact that fewer drivers are getting driver safety training.
This choice explains why the number of crashes per mile driven has remained approximately the same and resolves the apparent paradox.
(C) Regardless of a person’s age, in order to receive a first driver’s license in Minlehoma, the person must pass a written test and a rigorous driving test.
What this answer choice says does not materially change what we know about the situation.

Regardless of that fact that, in order to receive a first driver’s license in Minlehoma, the person must pass a written test and a rigorous driving test, drivers aged 16 to 17 have still been getting into automobile crashes and have been those drivers most likely to get into crashes.
So, even given what this choice says, we would have expected a decrease in the number of drivers aged 16 to 17 to have resulted in a decrease in the number of crashes per mile driven in Minlehoma.
So, this choice does not change what would have been expected to occur and, obviously, does not change what did in fact occur, leaving the paradox unresolved.
(D) The speed limits on the highways in Minlehoma today are, in most locations, the same as they were in the past.

This answer choice could be tempting because it says that speed limits remained the same, and what has to be explained is why the number of automobile crashes per mile driven remained the same, and those two facts seem to line up.

However, we must remember that the passage gives us two key pieces of information: that drivers aged 16 to 18 have been those most likely to get into crashes and that, recently, many people have been waiting until they reach age 18 to get a license.

So, one variable has changed recently: the age at which many people first get a license.Given that change, even though speeds limits have remained the same, we would have expected to see a decrease in crashes, but such a decrease did not occur.
Thus, something other than speed limits must be offsetting the effect of the change in the age at which people get licenses, and this choice does not tell us what that thing is.
(E) Unlike other drivers in Minlehoma, drivers under age 18 are not allowed to use hands-free cell phones to communicate while driving.

This choice could be tempting, because the fact that drivers under 18 are not allowed to use hands-free cell phones while driving could be perceived as offsetting the fact that drivers under 18 are prone to getting into crashes.As a result of that perception, we could mistakenly decide something along the lines of that the reason why the number of crashes per mile driven did not decrease when the number of drivers aged 16 to 17 decreased was that the number of drivers prohibited from using hands-free cell phones also decreased.

Remember, however, the passage states as fact that drivers aged 16 to 17 have been those most likely to get into automobile crashes.What that statement means is that, even though those drivers are not allowed to use hands-free cell phones while driving, those drivers werestillthose most likely to get into crashes.

So, the fact that drivers under 18 are not allowed to use hands-free cell phones does not actually offset the fact that drivers under 18 are prone to getting into crashes, and this choice changes nothing about the scenario and does not explain why the number of crashes remained the same.

question.text: Five years ago, Cooper Hills began generating some of its power by using nonpolluting methods.The town’s engineers felt that each unit of power generated via these nonpolluting methods would represent the avoidance of a certain amount of air pollution, because any unit of power generated via the use of a nonpolluting method is a unit of power that is not generated via burning of fossil fuels, which does create pollution.However, the amount of pollution avoided by Cooper Hills by generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods has been steadily declining.Which of the following, if true, would best explain the trend described above?

answers.text: It has become less expensive for consumers to utilize technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels.
Technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels have improved significantly in recent years, allowing for a unit of power to be generated with less pollution created.
The technology that Cooper Hills employs for generating power via using nonpolluting methods has become outdated as better nonpolluting power generation technology has been developed.
Many other towns near to Cooper Hills have also begun generating significant amounts of their power using nonpolluting methods.
Cooper Hills generates less than 50 percent of its total power via using nonpolluting methods.

explanation_parts.text: Background: Five years ago, Cooper Hills began generating some of its power by using nonpolluting methods.

Fact 1: The town’s engineers felt that each unit of power generated via these nonpolluting methods would represent the avoidance of a certain amount of air pollution, because any unit of power generated via the use of a nonpolluting method is a unit of power that is not generated via burning of fossil fuels, which does create pollution.
Fact 2:The amount of pollution avoided by Cooper Hills by generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods has been steadily declining.
Picture it: Theamount of pollution avoided(by generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods) has been steadily declining.
For example, at some point in the past, the following could have been true.
In generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods, 0 units of pollution were created.
In generating a unit of power via burning of fossil fuels, 20 units of pollution were created.

In that case, generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods rather than by burning fossil fuels would have resulted in the avoidance of 20 units of pollution.In other words, 0 units rather than 20 units of pollution would have been created.
However, that gap has been shrinking.Perhaps now, generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods results in the avoidance of only 10 units of pollution.
Why is the amount of pollution avoided for each unit of power generated steadily declining?
YOUR CHOICE(A) It has become less expensive for consumers to utilize technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels.
This choice explains the wrong thing.

If, as this choice says, it has become less expensive for consumers to utilize technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels, we would expect consumers to increase their use of technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels.Since burning fossil fuels results in pollution, this choice would explainan increase in total pollution.

However, an increase in total pollution is not what we have to explain.We have to explain why the amount of pollution Cooper Hills avoids by using nonpolluting methods has been declining, and the fact that consumers are polluting more would not explain why Cooper Hills is avoiding less pollution in using nonpolluting methods.
Regardless of what consumers are doing, we’d expect what Cooper Hills has been doing to continue to have the same results, but the results have changed.Why? This choice does not tell us.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels have improved significantly in recent years, allowing for a unit of power to be generated with less pollution created.
What this answer choice says explains the decline in pollution avoided.

If it is true that technologies that generate power via burning fossil fuels have improved significantly in recent years, allowing for a unit of power to be generated with less pollution created, then we would expect to see the pollution gap described in the introduction to this explanation shrink, and here’s why.

We said in the introduction to this explanation that, if, for example, at some point in the past, in generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods, 0 units of pollution were created, and if, for example, at the same past point, in generating a unit of power via burning of fossil fuels 20 units of pollution were created, then via the generation of a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods, 20 units of pollution were avoided.
Now consider the following.

If it is true that technologies that create power via burning fossil fuels have improved significantly in recent years, allowing for a unit of power to be generated with less pollution created, then perhaps at this point, in generating a unit of power via burning of fossil fuels 10 units of pollution are created.We can see that now, by using nonpolluting methods instead of burning fossil fuels to generate a unit of power, Cooper Hills would avoid only 10 – 0 = 10 units of pollution.

So, what has happened is that, while the nonpolluting methods still don’t pollute, the fossil fuel burning methods pollute less than they did in the past.So, using nonpolluting methods rather than burning fossil fuel doesn’t make as much difference as it did before.
This choice explains why the pollution gap has shrunk.
(C) The technology that Cooper Hills employs for generating power via using nonpolluting methods has become outdated as better nonpolluting power generation technology has been developed.

This choice could seem to explain why Cooper Hills isn’t avoiding as much pollution as it used to avoid by using the nonpolluting methods it has been using.“Of course,” we might think, “The technology has become outdated.That explains the decline!”
But does that fact really explain the decline? No.

When technology becomes “outdated,” that technology hasn’t changed.All that has happened is that more advanced technology has become available.In this case, the technology that Cooper Hills has been using hasn’t changed.All that has happened is that “better nonpolluting power generation technology has been developed.”

The fact that better technology has been developed does not explain why the amount of pollution avoided viaCooper Hills’ continuing to use the same technology it has been using all alonghas been declining.
(D) Many other towns near to Cooper Hills have also begun generating significant amounts of their power using nonpolluting methods.
That many other towns near to Cooper Hills have also begun generating significant amounts of their power using nonpolluting methods does not resolve the paradox.

If you chose this answer as correct, you may have reasoned that, since many other towns are generating significant amounts of power using nonpolluting methods, there is less pollution to be avoided, and that, therefore, Cooper Hills’ using nonpolluting methods is not making as great a difference as it used to make.

Think about it, though.Even if nobody else were polluting, by using nonpolluting methods Cooper Hills would be avoiding polluting, because, if Cooper Hills were using other, polluting methods, thenCooper Hillswould be polluting.
Thus, regardless of whether some, all, or no other towns are using nonpolluting methods, we’d expect that by using nonpolluting methods, Cooper Hills would be avoiding a certain amount of pollution.

So, the fact that other towns are polluting less does not affect how much pollution Cooper Hills avoids and does not explain why the amount of pollution Cooper Hills has been avoiding has been declining.
(E) Cooper Hills generates less than 50 percent of its total power via using nonpolluting methods.
This choice is about the wrong variable.
The variable that the apparent paradox involves isthe amount of pollution avoided by Cooper Hills by generating one unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods.
This choice, on the other hand, is aboutthe percentage its power Cooper Hills generates via the use of nonpolluting methods.

The amount of pollution avoided in generating one unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods would not at all be affected by how much of its power Cooper Hills generates via the use of nonpolluting methods.
If Cooper Hills were to generate all of its power via the use of nonpolluting methods, it would avoid a certain amount of pollution for each unit of power generated.
If Cooper Hills were to generate half of its power via the use of nonpolluting methods, it would avoid the same certain amount of pollution per unit of power generated.

Of course, as this choice says, Cooper Hills generates less than 50 percent of its total power via using nonpolluting methods, and in this case, Cooper Hills avoids the same certain amount of pollution for each unit of power generated that it avoids in the above two scenarios.

So, we can see that this choice clearly does not explain why the quantity of pollution avoided by Cooper Hills by generating a unit of power via the use of nonpolluting methods has been steadily declining.

question.text: Over the past three years, fees for parking automobiles in Shemerton’s central business district have increased, and the Shemerton transportation authority has improved Shemerton’s public transportation system by adding bus routes and by increasing the number of trains that run per hour.Surprisingly, however, the number of people using public transportation to travel to and around Shemerton has decreased over the same period of time.Which of the following does the most to explain the surprising trend in public transportation use in the Shemerton area?

answers.text: The bus routes added over the past three years were added in areas in and around Shemerton where the population densities are relatively low.
Many people who live in the Shemerton area do not consider the cost savings they can achieve using public transportation worth the hassles of using it.
In the Shemerton area, people’s ability to afford to purchase and maintain automobiles was hampered by an economic recession that ended three years ago.
In the Shemerton area, the price of fuel for automobiles has not increased significantly over the past three years.
While the number of trains per hour in Shemerton increased, the routes traveled and the stops made by trains did not change.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Over the past three years, fees for parking automobiles in Shemerton’s central business district have increased, and the Shemerton transportation authority has improved Shemerton’s public transportation system by adding bus routes and by increasing the number of trains that run per hour.
Fact 2: The number of people using public transportation to travel to and around Shemerton has decreased over the same period of time.

Picture it: It became more expensive to park automobiles in Shemerton’s central business district, and the public transportation system improved.Yet, the number of people using the public transportation system decreased.

The key to answering this question is noticing that what has to be explained is not thatfew people are usingpublic transportation or that public transportation usehas not increased.It’s that public transportation usehas decreased.If we fail to pay attention to this key directional detail, we could easily end up choosing an incorrect answer, one that explains the wrong phenomenon.
To correctly answer this question, we have to explain a decrease.
(A) The bus routes added over the past three years were added in areas in and around Shemerton where the population densities are relatively low.

The fact that the bus routes were added in areas with relatively low population densities might explain a different outcome, such as the number of people using public transportation remaining about the same.However, theadditionof bus routes would not be the reason for adecreasein ridership.

The fact of the matter is that there are still more bus routes now than there were before, so even if routes were added only in areas with low population densities, we would still expect to see a slight increase or no change in ridership, not a decrease.
Thus, what this answer choice says does not resolve the paradox.
(B) Many people who live in the Shemerton area do not consider the cost savings they can achieve using public transportation worth the hassles of using it.
What this choice says might explain why the number of people using public transportationdid not increase.However, it does not explain a decrease.

We have no reason to believe that the Shemertonians who don’t consider the cost savings from using public transportation worth the hassles didn’t already have that opinionbeforethe improvements to the system were made.So, they likely weren’t using public transportation before, and they’re still not using it now.

The fact that people feel this way about public transportation could explain there being no change in ridership.However, this information about something that has likely been true on an ongoing basis does not explain the decrease in ridership we’re seeking to explain.
YOUR CHOICE(C) In the Shemerton area, people’s ability to afford to purchase and maintain automobiles was hampered by an economic recession that ended three years ago.

If, as this choice tells us, the recession ended three years ago, then, presumably, people in the Shemerton area have become more able to purchase and maintain automobiles than they were during the recession.Therefore, what makes sense is that the number of people driving cars would have increased over the three years since the recessions ended.

So, this change in people’s economic circumstances certainly explains why, despite the increases in parking fees and the improvement of the public transportation system, the number of people using public transportation has decreased.People have switched to using cars.
So, this choice explains the decrease and resolves the apparent paradox.
Now, here’s a tip on execution.

Notice that this choice could seem to be about a recession and, therefore, could seem to have nothing to do with public transportation.Because of that characteristic of this choice, many people decide that this choice is irrelevant before they read the entire choice.In doing so they miss the last five words of the choice, which five words they need to read in order to see that this choice explains the decrease.As a result of not reading those five words, they miss the question.The takeaway here is that it’s important to read Critical Reasoning answer choices in their entirety to ensure that we don’t miss something key.
(D) In the Shemerton area, the price of fuel for automobiles has not increased significantly over the past three years.

The fact that the price of fuel for automobiles has not increased significantly means that the price of fuel has either remained about the same or decreased; we don’t know which.So, all we can tell from what this choice says is that a particular type of change, a significant increase, has not occurred.

Perhaps if the price of fuel for automobiles had decreased significantly, that decrease would explain the decrease in the use of public transportation, but since we have no reason to believe that a drop in price is any more likely than no change in price, this answer choice does not resolve the paradox.
As we consider this choice, we have to be careful not to turn “has not increased significantly” into “has decreased” in our minds.
(E) While the number of trains per hour in Shemerton increased, the routes traveled and the stops made by trains did not change.
Even without any addition of train routes or stops, an increase in the number of trains per hour should result in either an increase or no change in ridership, and certainly not a decrease.

What this choice says does not change the fact stated in the passage: the number of trains per hour increased.An increase in the number of trains would not have caused a decrease in public transportation ridership.Thus, this answer choice does not resolve the paradox.

question.text: Because cyclists involved in crashes are less likely to be severely injured if they are wearing helmets, five years ago, the town of Danterton passed a law requiring cyclists to wear helmets when riding in Danterton.Since the law was enacted, almost all cyclists riding in Danterton have worn helmets.Further, since some people find cycling less enjoyable or less convenient if they have to wear helmets, the number of cyclists on the roads in Danterton has decreased since the law was enacted.Nevertheless, the number of serious cycling related injuries per year in Danterton has increased since the law was enacted.Which of the following, if true, best explains the surprising outcome described above?

answers.text: Most cyclists who, as a result of Danterton’s helmet law, have reduced the amount of riding they do are occasional cyclists who would not ride much even without the law.
Although the percentage of cyclists who wear helmets in Danterton increased immediately after the helmet law was enacted, it has remained approximately the same ever since.
The more cyclists drivers of automobiles encounter while driving, the more aware those drivers become of how to safely share the roads with cyclists.
Only certain types of injuries are prevented by helmets; the likelihood of a crashing cyclist’s experiencing injuries of other types is not affected by that cyclist’s wearing a helmet.
Towns around Danterton that have not enacted cycling helmet laws have not experienced increases in the numbers of serious cycling related injuries per year in those towns.

explanation_parts.text: We are presented with a paradox.The passage says that cyclists involved in crashes are less likely to be severely injured if they are wearing helmets.It also says that the town of Danterton passed a law requiring cyclists to wear helmets when riding in Danterton, with the result that since its passage, almost all cyclists riding in Danterton have worn helmets.Also, number of cyclists on the roads in Danterton has decreased since the law was enacted.

Given those facts, we certainly would have expected the number of serious cycling related injuries per year in Danterton to decrease.However, rather than decrease, the number of serious cycling related injuries per year in Danterton has increased since the law was enacted.
Why?
Notice that, to correctly answer this question, we have to explain an increase.
(A) Most cyclists who, as a result of Danterton’s helmet law, have reduced the amount of riding they do are occasional cyclists who would not ride much even without the law.
