Notice that what this answer choice effectively says is that some cyclists in Danterton (those who are occasional cyclists) decreased the amount of riding they do.If some percentage of cyclists decreased the amount of riding they do, then why did the number of serious cycling related injuries per year in Danterton increase since the law was enacted? After all, a reduction the amount of riding done by some people – whoever those people are – should result in a reduction of cycling related injuries.
A decrease in the amount of riding done does not explain the increase in injuries and does not resolve the apparent paradox.
(B) Although the percentage of cyclists who wear helmets in Danterton increased immediately after the helmet law was enacted, it has remained approximately the same ever since.

Notice that this answer choice conveys that the percentage of cyclists in Danterton wearing helmets increased after the passage of the law.The passage says, “since the law was enacted, almost all cyclists riding in Danterton have worn helmets.” Notice, however, that the passage never says directly that the percentage of cyclists in Danterton wearing helmets increased after the passage of the law.So, in confirming what seemed likely, that the percentage of cyclists wearing helmets increased, this choice provides new information.

Therefore, if anything, what this answer choice says slightly deepens the paradox.Now we know for sure that the percentage of cyclists in Danterton wearing helmets increased after the passage of the law.So, why did the number of serious cycling related injuries per year in Danterton increase since the law was enacted?
The information provided by this choice provides confirmation that the situation is paradoxical and does not explain the increase that we have to explain.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The more cyclists drivers of automobiles encounter while driving, the more aware those drivers become of how to safely share the roads with cyclists.
Notice that the passage tells us that the number of cyclists on the roads in Danterton has decreased since the law was enacted.

If it is true that, as this choice says, themore cyclistsdrivers of automobiles encounter while driving, themore awarethose drivers become of how to safely share the roads with cyclists, then it must be true also that thefewer cyclistsdrivers of automobiles encounter while driving, theless awarethose drivers will be of how to safely share the roads with cyclists.

It follows then, that, because there has been a decrease in the number of cyclists on the roads of Danterton, drivers of automobiles there now are less aware than before of how to safely share the roads with cyclists.This decrease in drivers’ awareness of how to safely share the roads with cyclists could explain the increase in cycling related injuries.
We see that what this answer choice says explains the increase and resolves the apparent paradox.
(D) Only certain types of injuries are prevented by helmets; the likelihood of a crashing cyclist’s experiencing injuries of other types is not affected by that cyclist’s wearing a helmet.

The passage states as fact that “cyclists involved in crashes are less likely to be severely injured if they are wearing helmets.” Notice that what this answer choice says is essentially that some types of cycling injuries are prevented by wearing helmets.If wearing helmets while cycling does prevent some cycling injuries, and if wearing helmets has become mandatory, then we would expect to see a decline in the number of cycling injuries, even if only certain types of injuries are prevented by helmets.So, why has there been an increase? This choice does not answer that question.
(E) Towns around Danterton that have not enacted cycling helmet laws have not experienced increases in the numbers of serious cycling related injuries per year in those towns.

Notice that what this answer choice says provides further confirmation that the situation described is paradoxical.After all, with the passage of the law, the number of serious cycling related injuries per year in Danterton has increased.Meanwhile, towns around Danterton that have not enacted cycling helmet laws have not experienced increases in the numbers of serious cycling related injuries per year in those towns.

This information on the contrasting experiences of the towns tends to confirm that something paradoxical is occurring in Danterton, by showing that the increase in injuries seen in Danterton is unique to Danterton, the only town that has made wearing helmets mandatory.However, we are not seeing a choice that provides such confirmation.We are seeking a choice that resolves the paradox.
The fact that other towns did not experience increases in serious cycling related injuries does not explain why Danterton did.

question.text: For more than a decade, doctors have recommended that women over age 40 get yearly mammograms, which use x-rays to enable doctors to better check for cancerous breast tumors.The rationale is that by finding a cancerous tumor early, doctors have the best chance of successfully treating the tumor.In fact, almost all research indicates that mammograms do find tumors in all stages of growth, from early to advanced.However, in recent years some doctors have stopped recommending yearly mammograms to their female patients.Each of the following, if true, helps to resolve the apparent paradox, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Estimates suggest that about two-thirds of the reduction in breast cancer deaths in recent years is due to better treatments, not better detection.
The X-rays used in mammograms in creating an image of a woman’s breast can cause cancerous tumors to grow in the tissue the X-rays penetrated.
The detection of larger, more aggressive tumors, the ones that will cause health problems, was unchanged in frequency from the prior decade, when mammograms were not routinely used.
A mammogram can often indicate suspicious breast tissue when no suspicious tissue actually exists, resulting in a “false positive,” which can lead to unnecessary medical procedures with health risks.
Recent studies show that mammograms have not increased the survival rates of women over 40 with breast cancer.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the passage:
Background: For more than a decade, doctors have recommended that women over age 40 get yearly mammograms, which use x-rays to enable doctors to better check for cancerous breast tumors.

Fact 1: Almost all research indicates that mammograms find tumors in all stages of growth, from early to advanced, and by finding a cancerous tumor early, doctors have the best chance of successfully treating the tumor.
Fact 2: In recent years, some doctors have stopped recommending yearly mammograms to their female patients.

Picture it: We are presented with an apparent paradox.If mammograms detect tumors at all stages of their growth and if the earlier a tumor is detected, the better chance there is of successfully treating the tumor, then why have doctors stopped recommending yearly mammograms to their female patients? Our job in answering this question is to solve that mystery.

Because this question is an EXCEPT question, four answers say things that DO resolve the paradox.These will be incorrect answers.The remaining answer, the one that does not resolve the paradox, will be the correct answer to this question.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Estimates suggest that about two-thirds of the reduction in breast cancer deaths in recent years is due to better treatments, not better detection.
What this choice says does not resolve the paradox.

That two thirds of the reduction resulted from treatment means that there is still one-third of the reduction that may have resulted from detection.The fact that detection caused at maximum only a third of the reduction in deaths is certainly no reason to stop using mammography.After all, one third of the number of people who survive breast cancer because of treatment may equate to tens of thousands of people.
This choice is a trap that could be perceived as indicating that detection is not beneficial, when actually, what this choice says indicates that detection may still be quite important.
Resolves the paradox? No.
(B) The X-rays used in mammograms in creating an image of a woman’s breast can cause cancerous tumors to grow in the tissue the X-rays penetrated.
What this choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that the x-rays used in mammograms in creating an image of a woman’s breast can cause cancerous tumors to grow in the tissue the x-rays penetrated, then there is a very good reason for doctors to stop recommending mammograms to their female patients.After all, a doctor who is looking out for the best interests of her patients would cease recommending a treatment if she knew that that treatment could cause harm.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
(C) The detection of larger, more aggressive tumors, the ones that will cause health problems, was unchanged in frequency from the prior decade, when mammograms were not routinely used.
What this choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that the detection of larger, more aggressive tumors, the ones that will cause health problems, was unchanged in frequency from the prior decade, when mammograms were not routinely used, then although mammograms do find tumors, they are not any better at finding the tumors that will cause health issues than the diagnostic tools used prior to mammograms.
This fact could explain why doctors have stopped recommending mammograms to their female patients.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.

(D) A mammogram can often indicate suspicious breast tissue when no suspicious tissue actually exists, resulting in a “false positive,” which can lead to unnecessary medical procedures with health risks.
What this choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that a mammogram can often indicate suspicious breast tissue when no suspicious tissue actually exists, resulting in a “false positive,” which can lead to unnecessary medical procedures with health risks, then there is a good reason for doctors to stop recommending mammograms to their female patients.After all, a doctor who is looking out for the best interests of her patients would cease recommending a procedure if she knew that that procedure could cause harm.
Resolves the paradox? Yes.
(E) Recent studies show that mammograms have not increased the survival rates of women over 40 with breast cancer.
What this choice says resolves the paradox.

If it is true that recent studies show that mammograms have not increased the survival rates of women over 40 with breast cancer, then there is a good reason for doctors to stop recommending mammograms to their female patients.After all, what would be the utility (at least according to what we know from this passage), if mammography dd not increase survival rates?
Resolves the paradox? Yes.

question.text: Immigrants to developed countries often come from less developed countries where residents are, on average, less healthy than native-born residents of the developed countries to which the immigrants migrate.Interestingly, however, immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries tend to be healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated.Which of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent paradox?

answers.text: Successfully migrating from a less developed country to a more developed country tends to require better than average skills in planning and problem solving.

Native-born residents of developed countries could gain insights into how to improve health by determining why immigrants from less developed countries are healthier than native-born residents of developed countries.
Because of language barriers, immigrants to developed countries are often reluctant to access health supporting services unless the immigrants are experiencing particularly serious health issues.
In some aspects, the lives of immigrants who move to developed countries resemble the lives that they led in their countries of origin.
One of the reasons why immigrants leave their countries of origin is to live in countries with better sanitation and other characteristics that contribute to better support of health.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Immigrants to developed countries often come from less developed countries where residents are, on average, less healthy than native-born residents of the developed countries to which the immigrants migrate.

Fact 2: Immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries tend to be healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated.

Picture it: In these less developed nations, residents are, on average, less healthy than native-born residents of the developed countries to which the immigrants migrate.However, curiously, when we find immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries, these immigrants are healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated.Why might that be?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Successfully migrating from a less developed country to a more developed country tends to require better than average skills in planning and problem solving.

If it is true that successfully migrating from a less developed country to a more developed country tends to require better than average skills in planning and problem solving, then the immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries likely have better than average skills in planning and problem solving.

When people have better than average skills in planning and problem solving, we expect to see, on average, better health in those people than we see in people with less than better than average skills in planning and problem solving.After all, people with better than average skills in planning and problem solving will, for example, take time each day to exercise, eat organic fruits and vegetables, seek quality medical care, and avoid activities, such as smoking tobacco and drinking alcohol, that are known to be harmful to health.

So, this choice provides a reason why immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries tend to be healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated.The immigrants’ having better than average skills in planning and problem solving results in their having better than average health.

(B) Native-born residents of developed countries could gain insights into how to improve health by determining why immigrants from less developed countries are healthier than native-born residents of developed countries.

What this answer choice says is merely a statement that follows from what the passage says, which is that “Immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries tend to be healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated.”

We have to be careful to remember what the answer choice we are seeking has to do, which in this case is not to state something that follows from what the passage says but to explain an aspect of what the passage describes.
The fact that native-born residents of developed countries could get insights about health from immigrants does not explain why immigrants are healthier than native-born residents.
(C) Because of language barriers, immigrants to developed countries are often reluctant to access health supporting services unless the immigrants are experiencing particularly serious health issues.
What this answer choice says can only deepen the paradox.

After all, the fact that, because of language barriers, immigrants to developed countries are often reluctant to access health supporting services unless the immigrants are experiencing particularly serious health issues, seems to be an issue that would negatively impact the health of the immigrants.

So, why do these immigrants from less developed countries living in more developed countries tend to be healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated?
What this answer choice says leaves us more confused.
(D) In some aspects, the lives of immigrants who move to developed countries resemble the lives that they led in their countries of origin.
The effect of what this choice says is unclear.

This choice does not tell us in what aspects the lives of immigrants who move to developed countries resemble the lives that they led in their countries of origin.Without information on the aspects this choice is about, we have no way to determine whether those aspects would be beneficial or detrimental to health.In other words, those aspects could be anything from high levels of stress, which would likely be detrimental to health, to regularly eating fresh fruit, which would likely be beneficial to health.
So, we really have no clear reason to believe that what this choice says either resolves or deepens the paradox.

In order not to select as correct a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to somehow use the choice, which doesn’t have any clear effect, to spin an unsupported story that seems to resolve the paradox.We have to recognize that the choice has no clear effect one way or the other.
YOUR CHOICE(E) One of the reasons why immigrants leave their countries of origin is to live in countries with better sanitation and other characteristics that contribute to better support of health.
What this answer choice says simply provides us with some insight into why immigrants move to more developed countries.

Yes, what this choice says is health related.However, the fact that health-related factors motivate immigrants to move does not help us to understand why immigrants who have moved from less developed countries to more developed countries tend to be healthier than the native-born residents of the more developed countries to which the immigrants have migrated.
We need a choice that shows how the immigrants aredifferentfrom the native-born residents.The correct answer does so.This choice does not.

While this choice indicates that the immigrants have health-related goals, it's likely that, in general, the native-born residents of the countries to which the immigrants immigrate have those goals too.After all, the native-born residents are residents of countries with "characteristics that contribute to better support of health."

question.text: Since Morganville implemented a free after-school tuition program for students in grades 5 to 12, at least a quarter of the students in that age group have begun staying after school to receive the free tuition, and daytime vandalism in Morganville has decreased.Clearly, the free after-school tuition program has kept potential vandals off the streets.Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?

answers.text: The free after-school tuition program was created for the purpose of helping students to learn, not for the purpose of keeping potential vandals off the streets.
A civic initiative has increased the number of police patrolling Morganville during daylight hours by 50 percent.
A number of children who had previously been cited for vandalism have taken advantage of the after-school tuition program.
Expensive private tutors have been available in Morganville since long before the free after-school tuition program for students in grades 5 to 12 was implemented.
A survey of Morganville’s students indicates that a significant number do not perceive their academic performance to have been improved by the new tuition program.

explanation_parts.text: The conclusion of the argument is the following:
Conclusion: Clearly, the free after-school tuition program has kept potential vandals off the streets.
The conclusion is based on the fact that two events, the implementation of a free after-school tuition program and a decrease in daytime vandalism, have occurred at the same time.

In using that fact to support the conclusion, the argument depends on the assumption that the decrease in daytime vandalism has been caused by the implementation of the free after-school tuition program.

However, it is not clear that the after-school program actually caused the decrease in vandalism.Remember that the fact that two events have occurred at the same time does not mean that a cause-and-effect relationship is at play.
One way to weaken this argument is to present a reasonable alternative reason for the decline in the rate of daytime vandalism.
(A) The free after-school tuition program was created for the purpose of helping students to learn, not for the purpose of keeping potential vandals off the streets.
Regardless of the purpose for which the free after-school tuition program was created, it could have the effect of keeping potential vandals off the streets.
So, this choice has no effect on the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A civic initiative has increased the number of police patrolling Morganville during daylight hours by 50 percent.
This choice provides a reasonable alternative reason that daylight vandalism has decreased: the presence of more police on the streets is deterring vandalism.

Thus, after being presented with this fact, we are not sure whether the after-school tuition program has helped in decreasing daytime vandalism.Is it the program or the additional police that has caused the decrease?
So, this choice weakens the argument.
(C) A number of children who had previously been cited for vandalism have taken advantage of the after-school tuition program.

By suggesting that some of the people who were vandalizing are now occupied by the after-school program and, thus, are not out vandalizing, this choice tends to confirm that the program has caused the decrease in vandalism.
So, this choice strengthens, rather than weakens, the case for the argument’s conclusion.
(D) Expensive private tutors have been available in Morganville since long before the free after-school tuition program for students in grades 5 to 12 was implemented.

It might seem that this choice weakens the argument, because it could seem to be the case that, if tutors have been available for a while, then there isn’t necessarily any reason to believe that a new tuition program had a marked effect upon the vandalism rates.

However, there is a clear difference between “expensive private tutors” being available and “a quarter of the students” in the grades 5 to 12 age group staying after school to take part in “a free after-school tuition program.” Thus, it is clear that, by offering the new program, Morganville has introduced a new factor that could be responsible for the decrease in vandalism.
So, this choice does not really weaken the case for the argument’s conclusion.
(E) A survey of Morganville’s students indicates that a significant number do not perceive their academic performance to have been improved by the new tuition program.
This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as indicating that the free after-school program has not been effective.

However, the conclusion is not that the after-school program has been effective in improving students’ academic performance.The conclusion is that the after-school program has kept potential vandals off the streets.
So, this choice weakens the support for the wrong conclusion and, thus, has no effect on the strength of this argument.

question.text: In a study of over 1,000 people experiencing depression in Seattle, one of the rainiest cities in the U.S., it was determined that nearly 90 percent of the participants smoked marijuana on a regular basis.Therefore, it can be concluded that smoking marijuana is very likely a cause of depression.Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the above conclusion EXCEPT:

answers.text: Studies show that it is very unlikely for depressed people to self-medicate using marijuana.
Of the participants, none were taking prescription medications known to cause depression.
Numerous studies show that rainy weather is not a common cause of depression.
When 500 of the participants ceased marijuana use for 90 days, most saw significant declines in depression symptoms.
Research shows that there is a high incidence of depression in people who regularly consume alcoholic beverages.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Seattle is one of the rainiest cities in the U.S.
Premise: Of 1,000 depressed people in Seattle, 90 percent smoked marijuana on a regular basis
Conclusion: Smoking marijuana is very likely a cause of depression.

Notice that the author of this argument makes the cause-and-effect claim that smoking marijuana is very likely a cause of depression.What is her evidence? The evidence is the observation that of 1,000 depressed people in Seattle, 90 percent smoked marijuana on a regular basis.

Of course, the fact that 90 percent of the depressed people in the study smoked marijuana does not necessarily mean that smoking marijuana is the cause of their depression.There certainly could be other factors, such as the rainy Seattle weather, that are causing the people’s depression.
This is a Strengthen the Argument EXCEPT question..

So, in evaluating the answer choices, we will see and eliminate four choices that support the argument (strengthen the author’s cause and effect claim).Then, the final answer remaining, the one that does NOT strengthen the argument, will be the correct answer.Remember, “does not strengthen the argument” is not the same as “weaken the argument.” The correct answer could be a choice that weakens or a choice that has no effect at all.
(A) Studies show that it is very unlikely for depressed people to self-medicate using marijuana.
What this choice says strengthens the argument.

The fact that studies show that it is very unlikely for depressed people to self-medicate using marijuana rules out a plausible alternative reason for the apparent link between smoking marijuana and being depressed, and here’s why.

Although the argument claims that it’s the marijuana that is the cause of the people’s depression, it could be that, in fact, marijuana does not cause depression at all, and that the reason these people are smoking marijuana is that they are already depressed and, therefore, using the marijuana to self-medicate.
This choice serves to rule out that alternative explanation for the link between smoking marijuana and depression, and thus, strengthens the argument.
In other words, what this choice says causes us to have more conviction that the marijuana is causing the people’s depression.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(B) Of the participants, none were taking prescription medications known to cause depression.
What this choice says strengthens the argument.
The fact that none of the participants were taking prescription medications known to cause depression serves to rule out an alternative cause for the participant’s depression.

Although the argument claims that it’s the marijuana that is the cause of the people’s depression, it could be that, in fact, marijuana does not cause depression at all, and that the reason these people are depressed is that they are taking prescription medications known to cause depression.
Thus, by removing this potential alternative explanation (that it’s the prescription medicine that might be causing the depression), this choice makes the argument stronger.
In other words, this choice causes us to have more conviction that the marijuana is causing the people’s depression.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(C) Numerous studies show that rainy weather is not a common cause of depression.
What this choice says strengthens the argument.
The fact that numerous studies show that rainy weather is not a common cause of depression rules out a plausible alternative explanation for these people’s depression.

Although the argument claims that it’s the marijuana that is the cause of the people’s depression, it could be that, in fact, marijuana does not cause depression at all, and that the reason these people are depressed is that they live in Seattle, which is a rainy city.
Thus, by removing this potential alternative clause (that it’s the rainy weather that might be causing the depression), this choice makes the argument stronger.
In other words, this choice causes us to have more conviction that the marijuana is causing the people’s depression.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(D) When 500 of the participants ceased marijuana use for 90 days, most saw significant declines in depression symptoms.
What this choice says strengthens the argument.

The fact that, when 500 of the participants ceased marijuana use for 90 days, most saw significant declines in depression symptoms supports the conclusion that it is in fact the marijuana that is causing the people’s depression.

In dealing with a cause-and-effect claim, if, when we take away the cause (in this case the smoking of marijuana), the effect (in this case the people’s depression) goes away or becomes significantly reduced as well, we have some confirmation that that cause-and-effect claim is correct.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Research shows that there is a high incidence of depression in people who regularly consume alcoholic beverages.
This choice does affect the strength of the arguments.
That research shows that there is a high incidence of depression in people who regularly consume alcoholic beverages is interesting and seems to indicate that consuming alcohol can cause depression.

However, we don’t need support for the conclusion that consuming alcohol can cause depression.We need support for the conclusion that smoking marijuana causes depression, and the fact that drinking alcohol may cause depression provides virtually no confirmation that smoking marijuana causes depression.
Strengthens the argument? No.

question.text: The results of a long-term study of people’s exercise habits and health outcomes indicate that men who exercise for more than 7.5 hours per week experience heart disease at a rate higher than the rate of heart disease in men who exercise for around 2.5 hours per week.It is clear from these results that exercising for more than a certain amount of time per week can actually cause, rather than prevent, heart disease.Which of the following is an assumption necessary for arriving at the above conclusion?

answers.text: Some people who exercise for more than 7.5 hours a week do so in order to prevent heart disease.
Only people who regularly engage in strenuous exercise experience heart disease.
Subjecting the arteries of the heart to high levels of stress may contribute to the development of heart disease.
Preventing heart disease requires using a holistic approach that includes aspects other than exercising regularly.
People who exercise more than 7.5 hours per week are not more likely than others are to choose the types of stressful lifestyles that lead to heart disease.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Results of a study indicate that men who exercise for more than 7.5 hours per week experience heart disease at a rate higher than the rate of heart disease in men who exercise for around 2.5 hours per week.
Conclusion: Exercising for more than a certain amount of time per week can actually cause, rather than prevent, heart disease.
As we know so well, we must always be skeptical of conclusions involving cause-and-effect relationships.
The conclusion of this argument is supported by a correlation between exercising for more than 7.5 hours per week and relatively high rates of heart disease.

In reading this argument, we should be asking ourselves, “Is the exercise really the cause of the relatively high rates of heart disease, or could there be more to the story?” After all, it may be the case that exercising for more than 7.5 hours per week is not the cause of the higher incidence of heart disease, and that some other factor connects the greater amount of exercising with the higher incidence of heart disease.Using this line of thinking will help us to find the assumption upon which the conclusion rests.

Here’s why.When an author makes a cause and effect claim, she must assume that there is not an alternative explanation for the observation she describes.In other words, in making this argument, the author assumes that there is not another explanation for the relatively high rates of heart disease.So, this argument’s conclusion depends upon the assumption that the greater amount of exercise is the cause of the heart disease and that some other factor is not the source of the correlation between the two.
(A) Some people who exercise for more than 7.5 hours a week do so in order to prevent heart disease.

The point that the argument makes is not based on people’s motivations for exercising.The conclusion is simply about the effects of certain amounts of exercise.Since this cause-and-effect relationship would hold regardless of people’s motivations for exercising, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
(B) Only people who regularly engage in strenuous exercise experience heart disease.

While the conclusion is based on a correlation between exercising a lot and heart disease, the support for the conclusion does not depend on the assumption thatonlypeople who regularly engage in strenuous exercise experience heart disease.
Even if there are many causes of heart disease, and even if people who get little to no exercise experience heart disease, it could still be the case that exercising a lot causes heart disease.

The conclusion is that exercising more than a certain amount can cause heart disease; it’s not that exercising more than a certain amount is the only cause of heart disease, and the evidence provided supports the conclusion even if it is NOT the case that only people who regularly engage in strenuous exercise experience heart disease.
(C) Subjecting the arteries of the heart to high levels of stress may contribute to the development of heart disease.

This choice could be tempting, as it would make sense that the reason that exercising for more than 7.5 hours per week causes heart disease is that the arteries of the heart of a person exercising that much are subjected to high levels of stress.

However, coming to a cause-and-effect conclusion connecting exercising a lot with experiencing heart disease does not require assuming that any particular mechanism, such as arterial stress, serves as the connection between exercising a lot and heart disease.

In other words, the author’s evidence would still support the conclusion that exercising for more than a certain amount of time per week causes heart disease, even if what this choice says were not true.
(D) Preventing heart disease requires using a holistic approach that includes aspects other than exercising regularly.

While, given the speaker’s conclusion about lots of exercise causing heart disease, it could very well be that the speaker feels that, as this choice says, preventing heart disease requires a holistic approach, we are looking not for an answer that expresses what the speaker likely believes but, rather, for an assumption integral to the argument.
We don’t have to assume that preventing heart disease requires using a holistic approach in order to conclude that exercising a lot can cause heart disease.
In other words, even if it were true that preventing heart disease DOES NOT require the use of a holistic approach, this argument could still work.
YOUR CHOICE(E) People who exercise more than 7.5 hours per week are not more likely than others are to choose the types of stressful lifestyles that lead to heart disease.

In order to go from thecorrelationbetween exercising for more than 7.5 hours per week and a relatively high incidence of heart disease to the conclusion that exercising a lotcausesheart disease, the author must assume that there is not something other than a direct cause-and-effect relationship connecting exercising a lot with heart disease.

For instance, the conclusion depends on the assumption that those who exercise more than 7.5 hours per week are not more likely than others are to choose the types of stressful lifestyles that lead to heart disease.

If that statement were not true, and people who exercise for more than 7.5 hours a week DID choose stressful lifestyles, then it could be that the reason why those people experience heart disease more than do people who exercise less is not the exercise but, rather, the stressful lifestyles chosen by those who exercise that much.
Notice that the negation of what this answer choice says causes the argument to fall apart.

question.text: In the town of La Menta Estates, the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.Five years ago, the town began major construction on its highways.Therefore, it must be that this construction is to blame for the rapid decline in the honeybee population there.Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the above conclusion EXCEPT:

answers.text: Over the past five years, there has not been in La Menta Estates a significant decline in the populations of flowers from which honeybees derive their food.
Farmers in La Menta Estates did not begin five years ago spraying their crops with pesticides that are known to harm honeybees.
During the past five years, honeybees living in La Menta Estates have not been infected by a virus that weakens their immune systems.
Since the construction on the highways of La Menta Estate began five years ago, there have not been any weeks during which no work was done on the highways.
Since the initiation of the highway construction in La Menta Estates, the population of northern mockingbirds, which eat honeybees, has not increased appreciably.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In the town of La Menta Estates, the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.
Premise: Five years ago, the town began major construction on its highways.
Conclusion: It must be that this construction is to blame for the rapid decline in the honeybee population there.

Notice that the author uses cause-and-effect reasoning in her argument.Any time an author makes an argument involving cause-and-effect reasoning, we must be skeptical as to the validity of the cause-and-effect claim as well as the soundness of the argument.

Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the observation she seeks to explain.Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.

One way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument is to rule out alternative explanations for the events mentioned in the passage.Each time we rule out an alternative explanation, we have more conviction in the author’s cause-and-effect claim.
However, because this is a Strengthen EXCEPT question, our job is to eliminate four answer choices that strengthen the argument; these will be incorrect choices.
The one that remains, the one that doesnotstrengthen the argument, will be correct.
(A) Over the past five years, there has not been in La Menta Estates a significant decline in the populations of flowers from which honeybees derive their food.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out what would be an alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.

After all, if there HAD been a significant decline in the populations of flowers, we would have an alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.The existence of that alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the population of honeybees rapidly declining over the past five years was the major construction.The decline could have been caused by the drop in the number of Flowers.
So, by eliminating this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.Once all alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(B) Farmers in La Menta Estates did not begin five years ago spraying their crops with pesticides that are known to harm honeybees.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out what would be an alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.

After all, if farmers in La Menta Estates DID begin five years ago spraying their crops with pesticides that are known to harm honeybees, we would have an alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.The existence of that alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the population of honeybees rapidly declining over the past five years was the major construction.The decline could have been caused by the farmers spraying their crops with pesticides that are known to harm honeybees.
So, by removing this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.Once all alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(C) During the past five years, honeybees living in La Menta Estates have not been infected by a virus that weakens their immune systems.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out what would be an alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.

After all, if during the past five years, honeybees living in La Menta Estates HAVE been infected by a virus that weakens their immune systems, we would have a strong alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.The existence of that alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the population of honeybees rapidly declining over the past five years was the major construction.The decline could have been caused by the virus that weakens the bee’s immune systems.
So, by eliminating this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.Once all alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Since the construction on the highways of La Menta Estate began five years ago, there have not been any weeks during which no work was done on the highways.

What this answer choice says does not strengthen the argument.The fact that, since the construction on the highways of La Menta Estate began five years ago, there have not been any weeks during which no work was done on the highways simply indicates that the work was done at a steady pace.
That fact does not help to connect the construction to the decline in the honeybee population.
Strengthens the argument? No.
(E) Since the initiation of the highway construction in La Menta Estates, the population of northern mockingbirds, which eat honeybees, has not increased appreciably.

What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out what would be an alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.

After all, if since the initiation of the highway construction in La Menta Estates, the population of northern mockingbirds, which eat honeybees, HAS increased appreciably, we would have a strong alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.The existence of that alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the population of honeybees rapidly declining over the past five years was the major construction.The decline could have been caused by the appreciable increase in the population of northern mockingbirds, which eat honeybees.
So, by eliminating that alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why the population of honeybees has been rapidly declining over the past five years.Once all other alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.

question.text: Although the great white shark is one of the most robust animals ever to occupy the oceans, attempts to keep great whites in captivity have failed epically.Most great whites have died within 16 days of being taken from their ocean homes and placed into aquarium pools.Sharks are highly sensitive to electrical fields.In fact, strong electrical fields can disorient and confuse great whites, thus causing their injury and death.Some materials of which aquarium pools are composed produce electrical fields.It’s very clear that these electrical fields caused the deaths of captive great whites.Each of the following, if true, undermines the support for the above conclusion EXCEPT:

answers.text: In oceans, great whites eat only live prey, particularly marine mammals, but aquariums are unwilling to feed great whites live prey because of the public outcry that would result.
Great whites must swim continuously to oxygenate their blood, but because of the length of great whites, it’s difficult to find a pool large enough to fully accommodate this need.
Great white sharks are social animals, and the experience of being alone in an aquarium tank leads to depression, anxiety, and other distressing symptoms that cause physical and mental deterioration.
Great white shark physiology demands ocean water salinity for proper functioning, and aquariums have difficulty replicating precisely such salinity.
As they swim, great white sharks always produce electromagnetic fields in the surrounding water as a result of the activity of their nerves and muscles.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Most great whites have died within 16 days of being taken from their ocean homes and placed into aquarium pools.
Premise: Sharks are highly sensitive to electrical fields; strong electrical fields can disorient and confuse great whites, thus causing their injury and death.
Premise: Some materials of which aquarium pools are composed produce electrical fields.
Conclusion: These electrical fields in the aquariums caused the deaths of captive great whites.

Anytime a speaker posits a cause-and-effect link between variables, we should be skeptical as to whether that cause-and-effect link actually exists.The fact that two variables occur together does not provide us with sufficient evidence to conclude that one of the variables caused the other.

We’re told that some materials of which aquarium pools are composed produce electrical fields.We also know that sharks are very sensitive to electrical fields.Are we able to conclude, for sure, that it is the electrical fields that caused the sharks to die? Maybe there is another reason? Right? What else could explain the observation we see?

Since this is a weaken “EXCEPT” question, four choices say things that weaken (undermine) the support for the conclusion; these four choices are incorrect choices.The fifth answer choice will not weaken the support for conclusion; it will either do nothing to that support or it will strengthen it; this choice is the correct answer.
(A) In oceans, great whites eat only live prey, particularly marine mammals, but aquariums are unwilling to feed great whites live prey because of the public outcry that would result.
This choice weakens the argument.

If it’s true that, in oceans, great whites eat only live prey, particularly marine mammals, but aquariums are unwilling to feed great whites live prey because of the public outcry that would result, then the great whites in aquariums are not eating their usual diet.This significant dietary change could certainly be to blame for their deaths.

In other words, this choice brings up an alternative cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums.Of course, the fact that there is another possible cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums makes us less sure that it’s electrical fields in aquarium pools that cause the sharks’ deaths.
By showing that there may be another cause for the sharks’ deaths, this choice provides a reason to question the conclusion that electrical fields are the cause.
(B) Great whites must swim continuously to oxygenate their blood, but because of the length of great whites, it’s difficult to find a pool large enough to fully accommodate this need.
This choice weakens the argument.

If it’s true that great whites must swim continuously to oxygenate their blood, but because of the length of great whites, it’s difficult to find a pool large enough to fully accommodate this need, then we have a plausible alternative cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums.
Of course, the fact that there is another possible cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums makes us less sure that it’s electrical fields in aquarium pools that cause the sharks’ deaths.
By showing that there may be another cause for sharks’ deaths, this choice provides a reason to question the conclusion that electrical fields are the cause.

(C) Great white sharks are social animals, and the experience of being alone in an aquarium tank leads to depression, anxiety, and other distressing symptoms that cause physical and mental deterioration.
This choice weakens the argument.

If it is true that great white sharks are social animals, and that the experience of being alone in an aquarium tank leads to depression, anxiety, and other distressing symptoms that cause physical and mental deterioration, then we have a plausible alternative cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums.
Of course, the fact that there is another possible cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums makes us less sure that it’s electrical fields in aquarium pools that cause the sharks’ deaths.
By showing that there may be another cause for the sharks’ deaths, this choice provides a reason to question the conclusion that electrical fields are the cause.
(D) Great white shark physiology demands ocean water salinity for proper functioning, and aquariums have difficulty replicating precisely such salinity.
This choice weakens the argument.

If it is true that great white shark physiology demands ocean water salinity for proper functioning, and aquariums have difficulty replicating precisely such salinity, then it may be that swimming in water that is not quite the same as ocean water is the cause of the deaths of great whites in aquariums.

In other words, this choice says up an alternative cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums.Of course, the fact that there is another possible cause for the deaths of great whites in aquariums makes us less sure that it’s electrical fields in aquarium pools that cause sharks’ deaths.
By showing that there may be another cause for the sharks’ deaths, this choice provides a reason to question the conclusion that electrical fields are the cause.
YOUR CHOICE(E) As they swim, great white sharks always produce electromagnetic fields in the surrounding water as a result of the activity of their nerves and muscles.

This choice is written to seem to undermine the support for the conclusion, as what it says could lead a test-taker to engage in flawed thinking along the lines of "If sharks generate magnetic fields around them, then they must not be harmed by magnetic fields."

The issue with that thinking is that it contradicts a fact stated in the passage, that strong magnetic fields can disorient and confuse great whites.In Critical Reasoning, such a fact stated in the passage is taken to be true, and the truth is that that fact is unchanged by the fact that sharks themselves generate electromagnetic fields.Sharks could be affected by strong electromagnetic fields even if they themselves generate, perhaps weak, electromagnetic fields.So, this choice may seem to but does not in fact undermine the validity of that premise.

Since the fact that sharks generate electromagnetic fields cannot contradict a premise and does not provide any reason to doubt that there is a connection between the electromagnetic fields and the deaths of the sharks, this choice does not affect the argument.

question.text: Super Fitness Drink Salesperson: In a world in which many people who are seeking to attain their optimal weights are seeing little success, Super Fitness Drink is a standout product.One can easily see that, for the vast majority of people, using Super Fitness Drink is a clear path to attaining their optimal weights, since over 70 percent of the people who use Super Fitness Drink make significant progress toward their optimal weights within the first six months of their starting to consume it regularly.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the force of the evidence cited?

answers.text: Other companies that make fitness drinks cite similar statistics, but their drinks cost much less than Super Fitness Drink costs.
People who regularly consume Super Fitness Drink still have to drink water in order to maintain proper hydration.
The company that makes Super Fitness Drink has made a deal with a chain of fitness centers such that the drink is sold only in those centers.
Some people have reported that when they have stopped using Super Fitness Drink, they have seen their weights move away from their optimal weights.
The scientific community has yet to explain exactly how the ingredients in Super Fitness Drink could facilitate the attainment of optimal weight.

explanation_parts.text: Premise: Over 70 percent of the people who use Super Fitness Drink make significant progress toward their optimal weights within the first six months of their starting to consume it regularly.
Conclusion: One can easily see that, for the vast majority of people, using Super Fitness Drink is a clear path to attaining their optimal weights.

Notice that the salesperson claims that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the use of Super Fitness Drink and the attainment of a person’s optimum body weight.As we well know at this point, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claims.Is Super Fitness Drink really responsible for people’s attaining their optimum body weights? Could there be more to the story? Might the author have missed something? Might there be an alternative explanation?
(A) Other companies that make fitness drinks cite similar statistics, but their drinks cost much less than Super Fitness Drink costs.
That other companies that make fitness drinks cite similar statistics, but their drinks cost much less than does Super Fitness Drink does not affect the argument at all.

To avoid choosing this incorrect answer, we must clearly identify the argument’s conclusion, which is “that for the vast majority of people, using Super Fitness Drink is a clear path to attaining their optimal weights.” Notice that the conclusion has nothing to do with the cost of Super Fitness Drink relative to cost of other similar fitness drinks.

While cost may be pertinent to a general discussion of the merits of a product, the argument’s conclusion is not a general one about the merits of Super Fitness Drink.The conclusion is specifically that Super Fitness Drink works, helping people to attain their optimal weights, and Super Fitness Drink either works or it doesn’t work regardless of what it costs.
(B) People who regularly consume Super Fitness Drink still have to drink water in order to maintain proper hydration.
This choice has no effect on the argument.

To avoid choosing this incorrect answer, we must clearly identify the argument’s conclusion, which is that “for the vast majority of people, using Super Fitness Drink is a clear path to attaining their optimal weights.”
Notice that the conclusion has nothing to do with whether people, while using Super Fitness Drink, must drink water – the argument never makes such a claim.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The company that makes Super Fitness Drink has made a deal with a chain of fitness centers such that the drink is sold only in those centers.

It is reasonable to presume that the people found at fitness centers will make significant progress toward their optimal weights.After all, people who go to fitness centers are motivated to become fit and are going to places, fitness centers, that provide them with ways to become fit, i.e, at their optimal weights.

Now, if the company that makes Super Fitness Drink has made a deal with a chain of fitness centers such that the drink is sold only in those centers, then the people most apt to drink the drink will be the people in the fitness centers.

What we can see, then, is that, since Super Fitness Drink is sold only at fitness centers, it is probably sold almost exclusively to people who are likely to make significant progress toward their optimal weightsregardless of whether Super Fitness Drink helps people to attain their optimal weights.

If Super Fitness Drink helps, great.If it doesn’t work, these people are still motivated to attain their optimal weights and are still going to fitness centers.So, even if Super Fitness Drink doesn’t work, it would be no surprise that 70 percent of the people who use Super Fitness Drink make significant progress toward their optimal weights within the first six months of their starting to consume it regularly, becausethe people who use Super Fitness Drink all go to fitness centers.

So, what this choice says destroys the force of the evidence provided, because the fact that 70 percent of people who used Super Fitness Drink made progress toward their optimal weights doesn’t prove much about Super Fitness Drink if all those people were going to fitness centers.
(D) Some people have reported that when they have stopped using Super Fitness Drink, they have seen their weights move away from their optimal weights.
That some people have reported that, when they have stopped using Super Fitness Drink, they have seen their weights move away from their optimal weights does not weaken the argument.
Let’s focus on the argument’s conclusion: “for the vast majority of people, using Super Fitness Drink is a clear path to attaining their optimal weights.”
Notice that the conclusion is not that, once a person uses Super Fitness Drink, he or she will forever be at his or her optimal weight, even after the person stops consuming the drink.
(E) The scientific community has yet to explain exactly how the ingredients in Super Fitness Drink could facilitate the attainment of optimal weight.
That the scientific community has yet to explain exactly how the ingredients in Super Fitness Drink could facilitate the attainment of optimal weight does not weaken the argument.

Products can produce their desired effects without the scientific community being able to exactly explain how they work.In other words, the fact that we do not know why something works does not mean that it does not work.
Remember that failure to find evidence does not mean that that evidence does not exist.

question.text: Aleksi: A study showed that men in Finland who frequently used saunas were significantly less likely to experience death from heart disease than were those who did not use saunas.Clearly, using a sauna is beneficial for heart health.Lars: Adding a sauna adds to the cost of a house, and thus only those with sufficiently high incomes can afford to own saunas.Therefore, it is likely that it is income level rather than sauna use that somehow accounts for the differences in heart health.Which of the following, if true, would best support Aleksi’s conclusion in light of what Lars said?

answers.text: Sauna use is associated with reduced probability of experiencing death from heart disease whether the sauna used is located in a house, place of work, or public bathhouse.
In Finland, people are more likely to use saunas weekly than they are to exercise weekly for the amount of time considered optimal for health.
In Finland, not only men who use saunas but also women who use saunas are significantly less likely to experience heart disease than are people who do not use saunas.
The reduction in probability of dying of heart disease associated with sauna use is similar in degree to the reduction in probability of dying of heart disease associated with light exercising.
In Finland, the practice of using saunas for relaxation was first engaged in many centuries ago.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Aleksi claims that using a sauna is beneficial for heart health, because a study showed that men in Finland who frequently used saunas were significantly less likely to experience death from heart disease than were those who did not use saunas.Notice that Aleksi is seeking to establish a cause-and-effect connection between sauna use and heart health.

Lars counters with the idea that it is likely that it is income level rather than sauna use that somehow accounts for the differences in heart health, because a sauna adds to the cost of a house, and thus, only those with sufficiently high incomes can afford to own saunas.Notice that Lars is seeking to show that Aleksi has the cause and effect incorrect, by showing that perhaps there is an alternative explanation for the phenomenon Aleksi describes.
We’re seeking an answer choice that would best support Aleksi’s conclusion in light of what Lars said.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Sauna use is associated with reduced probability of experiencing death from heart disease whether the sauna used is located in a house, place of work, or public bathhouse.

If it is true that sauna use is associated with reduced probability of experiencing death from heart disease whether the sauna used is located in a house, place of work, or public bathhouse, then Aleski’s argument is strengthened.
After all, Lars makes the claim that it is not the sauna use that explains the differences in heart health; he claims that it is the income levels of people who can afford houses that have saunas.
Of course, then, what this answer choice says strengthens Aleski’s argument by showing that even people who use saunas not in houses benefit from increased heart health.

Since, presumably, using a sauna in a public bathhouse does not require having relatively high income, this choice weakens Lars’ point about sauna use and higher income levels.Yes, a having a sauna is a house is costly, but sauna use itself may not be costly.Thus, it may be that people of all income levels use saunas, not just people with relatively high incomes.
By serving to call into question Lars’ alternative theory about the correlation between sauna use and heart health, this choice strengthens the support for Aleksi’s conclusion.
(B) In Finland, people are more likely to use saunas weekly than they are to exercise weekly for the amount of time considered optimal for health.
This choice is a trap that seems to magnify a premise.
Aleksi’s claim is based upon the evidence that men in Finland who frequently used saunas were significantly less likely to experience death from heart disease than those who did not use saunas.
That evidence could seem to be somehow made more important or more powerful by the fact that, as this choice says, men are more likely to use saunas than to exercise.

The truth, however, is that this choice has no effect on the strength of the support for Aleksi’s conclusion.Saunas either have the effect that he claims them to have or they don’t, regardless of whether people are more likely to use saunas than to exercise.
(C) In Finland, not only men who use saunas but also women who use saunas are significantly less likely to experience heart disease than are people who do not use saunas.

By showing that members of another group, women who use saunas, are significantly less likely to experience heart disease than people who do not use saunas, this choice may seem to confirm the conclusion that using a sauna is beneficial for heart health.

However, what Lars says about income, sauna ownership, and heart health would logically apply to women just as well as it does to men.So, this choice does not help to support Aleksi's conclusion in light of what Lars said.

Another way to think about why this answer choice is incorrect is that we must take any premises given to be factual, and we already know that men in Finland who frequently used saunas were significantly less likely to experience death from heart disease than were those who did not use saunas.Therefore, what this answer choice says simply magnifies what this premise already says.In other words, this choice tells us something we already know, really, and just extends what we know to women.
(D) The reduction in probability of dying of heart disease associated with sauna use is similar in degree to the reduction in probability of dying of heart disease associated with light exercising.
This choice is tempting, because it seems to imply that the effects of sauna use are similar to the effects of light exercise, which is commonly known to be good for heart health.

Notice, however, that we ALREADY KNOW that men who use saunas are less likely to experience death from heart disease than those who do not use saunas.All this choice does is compare what we already know with a similar statistic related to men who engage in light exercise.
OK, so the two groups, men who use saunas and men who engage in light exercise, have similarly reduced probabilities of dying of heart disease.Great.

All the same, we are left asking the same question.Is sauna use the cause of the reduced probability of dying of heart disease of the men who use saunas? This choice does not give us any more reason than we already had to believe that sauna use is the cause.
This choice has no effect at all on the support for Aleksi’s conclusion.
(E) In Finland, the practice of using saunas for relaxation was first engaged in many centuries ago.

Of course, the amount of time that people have been using saunas does not strengthen Aleksi’s argument.That fact that sauna use in Finland is old, does not mean that the use of saunas provides cardiovascular benefits.

question.text: While the behaviors of nail biting and thumb sucking are associated with anxiety in children, a recent study found that children who frequently exhibit these behaviors are less likely to experience allergies than children who do not exhibit these behaviors.This finding suggests that thumb sucking and nail biting help in the development of functioning immune systems.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the support for the hypothesis presented above?

answers.text: Children who engage in thumb sucking are less likely than children who engage in nail biting to experience allergies.
Adults who bite their nails tend to contract, on average, more colds than adults who refrain from nail-biting contract.
Children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking are not more likely to receive preventive treatment for allergies than children who do not engage in those behaviors.
Children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking can learn to refrain from doing so by finding other ways to calm themselves.
Adults who as children engaged in nail biting and thumb sucking are not more likely to experience allergies than children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking.

explanation_parts.text: The argument concludes that (1), thumb sucking and nail biting, cause (2), functioning immune systems.
The conclusion is based on the correlation between thumb sucking and nail biting, and a lower incidence of allergies.
One way to strengthen this argument is to provide additional support for the claim that (1) causes (2).
(A) Children who engage in thumb sucking are less likely than children who engage in nail biting to experience allergies.

This choice compares the likelihoods that children in two different sets will experience allergies.Are we concerned with the comparative effects of nail biting and thumb sucking? No.The argument talks about thumb sucking and nail biting together and does not talk about children who engage in one activity and not the other.
So, the fact that there is a difference between the incidences of allergies in the two sets of children has no effect the argument.
(B) Adults who bite their nails tend to contract, on average, more colds than adults who refrain from nail-biting contract.

Since a functioning immune system should help with the prevention of colds, the fact that the incidence of colds in adults who bite their nails is higher than that in adults who do not bite their nails seems contrary to the conclusion that nail biting helps with the development of a functioning immune system.
So, to the degree that this choice has any effect on the argument, it weakens rather than strengthens the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking are not more likely to receive preventive treatment for allergies than children who do not engage in those behaviors.
This choice strengthens the argument by eliminating a potential weakener.

Imagine a case in which children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking are more likely to receive preventative treatment for allergies than children who do not engage in those behaviors.Wouldn’t the fact that they receive such treatment weaken the argument? Yes, it certainly would.After all, that fact would present a very plausible alternative reason why children who engage in thumb sucking and nail biting are less likely to experience allergies than other children.
So, by serving to eliminate this potential alternative reason for the lower incidence of allergies among children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking, this choice strengthens the argument.

If this choice doesn’t sound like a strengthener to you, try reading this choice without “not”.This choice without “not” provides an alternative explanation for the observed pattern and, thus, weakens the argument.
Thus, with “not,” this choice eliminates that alternate explanation and, thus, strengthens the argument.
(D) Children who engage in nail biting and thumb sucking can learn to refrain from doing so by finding other ways to calm themselves.

The information provided in this choice does not have the effect that we need.The fact that children can learn to refrain from nail biting and thumb sucking does not support the conclusion that children develop functioning immune systems by engaging in these activities.

(E)Incorrect.This choice could be tempting, because it could be taken as indicating that people do indeed develop their immune systems via nail biting and thumb sucking, as evidenced by the fact that adults who engaged in these activities while children are no more likely to experience allergies than are children who engage in these activities.So, the thinking would go, the adults must have developed their immune systems as children.

But, wait a minute; all this information really shows is a correlation between what adults experience and what children experience.It does not add information connecting the adults’ or the children’s incidence of allergies with nail biting and thumb sucking.
Furthermore, for all we know, the incidence of allergies is generally lower in all adults than it is in children, and this choice does not provide any comparison of adults with other adults.
So, really, this choice does not add support for the conclusion.

question.text: A recent study found that children living in households in which conventional disinfectant cleaning products were used regularly were more likely to be overweight than children living in households in which eco-friendly cleaning products were used.Clearly then, the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products can affect children in ways that result in children’s becoming overweight.In arriving at the above conclusion, the author assumes which of the following?

answers.text: People who use eco-friendly cleaning products tend to use cleaning products in quantities smaller than those used by people who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products.

The lifestyles of people who use eco-friendly cleaning products are not generally more conducive to children’s having healthy weights than the lifestyles of people who use conventional disinfectant ones.
Eco-friendly cleaning products are just as powerful in terms of their cleaning and disinfecting affects as conventional disinfectant cleaning products.
Parents who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products tend to feed their children foods the consumption of which tends to result in unhealthy weight gain.
The use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products causes children to harbor more gut bacteria of types that cause weight gain and fewer of types that help with maintaining healthy weight.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Children living in households in which conventional disinfectant cleaning products were used regularly were more likely to be overweight than children living in households in which eco-friendly cleaning products were used.
Conclusion: Clearly then, the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products can affect children in ways that result in children’s becoming overweight.

Notice that the speaker claims that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products and children becoming overweight.In making that cause-and-effect claim, the speaker is assuming that there is no other explanation for the observed pattern of events.
(A) People who use eco-friendly cleaning products tend to use cleaning products in quantities smaller than those used by people who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products.

Notice that the conclusion of the argument is that “the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products can affect children in ways that result in children’s becoming overweight.” So, the conclusion is specifically about the “use of” the products.
This choice brings up something else, the “quantities” of eco-friendly cleaning products used and the “quantities” of conventional disinfectant cleaning products used.
In order to arrive at a conclusion about the effects of using the products, the author does not have to assume that the products are used in different quantities.

In fact, by bringing up a difference in the quantities used of the products, this choice adds another variable to the scenario, making the case for the original conclusion less clear.After all, if people who use eco-friendly cleaning products tend to use cleaning products in quantities smaller than those used by people who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products, then it could be this difference in quantities, rather than a difference in the products themselves, that results in the difference in the likelihoods of children being overweight.
Rather than state a necessary assumption, this choice provides a reason to question the author’s conclusion.

YOUR CHOICE(B) The lifestyles of people who use eco-friendly cleaning products are not generally more conducive to children’s having healthy weights than the lifestyles of people who use conventional disinfectant ones.

The speaker claims that the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products is causing children to become overweight.His evidence is the fact that “Children living in households in which conventional disinfectant cleaning products were used regularly were more likely to be overweight than children living in households in which eco-friendly cleaning products were used.”

What if conventional disinfectant cleaning products don’t cause children to become overweight at all? What if the difference observed between the weights of children living in households in which conventional disinfectant cleaning products were used regularly and those of children living in households in which eco-friendly cleaning products were used is a result of the lifestyles of the different people in those different houses? In other words, what if the overall lifestyles of people who use eco-friendly cleaning products “are” generally more conducive to children’s having healthy weights than the lifestyles of people who use conventional disinfectant ones.
That fact would significantly weaken the argument, by showing that a third factor, lifestyle, underlies the association between conventional disinfectant cleaning products and overweight children.

Therefore, the speaker must be assuming that, as this choice says, the overall lifestyles of people who use eco-friendly cleaning products are NOT generally more conducive to children’s having healthy weights than the lifestyles of people who use conventional disinfectant ones.
(C) Eco-friendly cleaning products are just as powerful in terms of their cleaning and disinfecting affects as conventional disinfectant cleaning products.
Notice that this argument has nothing to do with the cleaning and disinfecting effects of cleaning products.

If you choose this answer choice as correct, you likely did so because it does sound relevant, but in seeking the correct answer to an assumption question, we are not merely seeking an answer that is relevant to the argument; we’re seeking an assumption upon which the argument depends.

Arriving at the argument’s conclusion about the effect of the use of the products on the weight of children does not require assuming that the eco-friendly cleaning products are just as effective at cleaning as conventional cleaning products.
(D) Parents who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products tend to feed their children foods the consumption of which tends to result in unhealthy weight gain.
This choice weakens the argument.
