After all, if parents who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products tend to feed their children foods the consumption of which tends to result in unhealthy weight gain, then we have strong reason to question the conclusion that the cause of these children’s becoming overweight is the conventional disinfectant cleaning products used in their homes.Now we’re wondering whether the conventional disinfectant cleaning products don’t cause children to become overweight, and whether the real cause of these kids’ being overweight is the foods they consume.
We’re not seeking a choice that weakens the argument; we’re seeking one that is an assumption upon which the argument depends.
(E) The use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products causes children to harbor more gut bacteria of types that cause weight gain and fewer of types that help with maintaining healthy weight.
This choice is very tempting, because it seems to connect the argument’s evidence to the argument’s conclusion.

However, this choice has the wrong effect.It’s not an assumption upon which the argument depends.This choice explains the how the use of conventional cleaning products could cause children to become overweight.

If the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products causes children to harbor more gut bacteria of types that cause weight gain and fewer of types that help with maintaining healthy weight, then that effect on gut bacteria could be the mechanism via which the use of conventional cleaning products causes children to become overweight.Of course, that information on a mechanism via which the use of conventional cleaning products could cause children to become overweight could explain exactly why the children of parents who use conventional disinfectant cleaning products are overweight.
Notice, however, that, even if that gut bacteria-related mechanism did not exist, there could still be a way in which the use of conventional cleaning products would cause children to be overweight.

So, in arriving at the conclusion that the use of conventional disinfectant cleaning products can affect children in ways that result in children’s becoming overweight, the author does not have to assume that conventional cleaning products affect gut bacteria.

question.text: To a survey administered to 500 people experiencing mouth sensitivity, 400 people responded that they regularly eat frozen yogurt.Therefore, frozen yogurt must be a cause of mouth sensitivity.Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the above conclusion EXCEPT:

answers.text: The people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt did not regularly brush their teeth incorrectly, which action often leads to mouth sensitivity.
The people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt often engaged in contact sports, such as boxing, that are known to cause face and mouth sensitivity.
Of the people with mouth sensitivity who ate frozen yogurt regularly, none had a long-lasting tooth infection, a common cause of mouth sensitivity.
None of the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt were suffering from recurring sinus infections, which more often than not cause mouth sensitivity.
Of the people with mouth sensitivity who ate frozen yogurt regularly, none were going through pregnancy, which often involves swelling of gums that results in mouth sensitivity.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: To a survey administered to 500 people experiencing mouth sensitivity, 400 people responded that they regularly eat frozen yogurt.
Conclusion: Therefore, frozen yogurt must be a cause of mouth sensitivity.

Notice that the author uses cause-and-effect reasoning in her argument.Any time an author makes an argument involving cause-and-effect reasoning, we must be skeptical regarding the validity of the cause-and-effect claim as well as the soundness of the argument.

Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the observation she seeks to explain.Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.

One powerful way to strengthen a cause and effect argument is to rule out alternative explanations for the cause-and-effect relationship mentioned in the passage.Each time we rule out an alternative explanation, we have more conviction in the author’s cause-and-effect claim.
However, because this is a Strengthen EXCEPT question, our job is to eliminate four choices that strengthen the argument; these will be incorrect choices.
The one that remains, the one that doesnotstrengthen the argument, will be correct.
(A) The people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt did not regularly brush their teeth incorrectly, which action often leads to mouth sensitivity.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out what would be an alternative explanation for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.

After all, if the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt DID regularly brush their teeth incorrectly, which action often leads to mouth sensitivity, we would have a strong alternative explanation for why people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.The existence of this alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the people’s experiencing mouth sensitivity was their regular eating of frozen yogurt.The mouth sensitivity could have been caused by the fact that they regularly brush their teeth incorrectly.
So, by serving to eliminate this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.Once all other alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt often engaged in contact sports, such as boxing, that are known to cause face and mouth sensitivity.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument by providing an alternative explanation for why the people had mouth sensitivity.

If it is true that the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt often engaged in contact sports, such as boxing, that are known to cause face and mouth sensitivity, then we have an alternative explanation for why these people have mouth sensitivity.In this case, logic would dictate that perhaps eating frozen yogurt is not at all the cause of the mouth sensitivity; perhaps the real cause of the mouth pain is the people’s engagement in contact sports, such as boxing.
Remember, when we are dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, an explanation alternative to the one provided by the author will weaken the argument.
Strengthens the argument? No.
(C) Of the people with mouth sensitivity who ate frozen yogurt regularly, none had a long-lasting tooth infection, a common cause of mouth sensitivity.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out what would be an alternative explanation for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.

After all, if some of the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt DID have long-lasting tooth infections, a common cause of mouth sensitivity, we would have an alternative explanation for why people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.The existence of this alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the people’s experiencing mouth sensitivity was their regular eating of frozen yogurt.The mouth sensitivity could have been caused by the fact that some had long-lasting tooth infections.
So, by serving to eliminate this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.Once all alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(D) None of the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt were suffering from recurring sinus infections, which more often than not cause mouth sensitivity.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out what would be an alternative explanation for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.

After all, if some of the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt WERE suffering from recurring sinus infections, which more often than not cause mouth sensitivity, we would have a strong alternative explanation for why people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.The existence of this alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the people’s experiencing mouth sensitivity was their regular eating of frozen yogurt.Their mouth sensitivity could be from recurring sinus infections, which more often than not cause mouth sensitivity.
So, by serving to eliminate this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.Once all other alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(E) Of the people with mouth sensitivity who ate frozen yogurt regularly, none were going through pregnancy, which often involves swelling of gums that results in mouth sensitivity.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out what would be an alternative explanation for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.

After all, if some of the people with mouth sensitivity who regularly ate frozen yogurt WERE going through pregnancy, which often involves swelling of gums that results in mouth sensitivity, we would have an alternative explanation for why people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.The existence of this alternative explanation would weaken the argument, because we would be less sure that the reason for the people’s experiencing mouth sensitivity was their regular eating of frozen yogurt.Their mouth sensitivity could be a result of their being pregnant.
So, by serving to eliminate this alternative explanation as a possibility, this choice makes the argument stronger.

If you have a hard time seeing why, imagine if we could remove every possible alternative explanation for why for why the people were experiencing mouth sensitivity.Once all other alternative explanations had been removed, the author’s argument would have been proven correct, because the author’s explanation would be the only possible explanation.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.

question.text: Clearly, Dr.Marks is an incompetent heart surgeon.Patients on whom he operates have a chance of dying during surgery nearly four times as high as that had by patients who undergo similar procedures but are operated on by the other surgeons at the hospital for which he works.Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

answers.text: When Dr.Marks worked at a different hospital, his patients were approximately twice as likely to die during surgery as patients who were operated on by other heart surgeons there.
At the hospital for which he works, Dr.Marks’ patients and colleagues consistently praise both his bedside manner and his surgical skill.
The hospital for which Dr.Marks works provides its surgeons with advanced training that studies have shown to reduce not only deaths during surgery but also post-surgical infections.
Hospital records show that, of the patients on whom Dr.Marks performs heart surgery, fewer than 8 of out of 100 experience post-surgical infections.
Because Dr.Marks has more experience than is had by the hospital’s other heart surgeons, the most difficult heart surgeries are assigned to him.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise:Patients on whom Dr.Marks operates have a chance of dying during surgery nearly four times as high as that had by patients who undergo similar procedures but are operated on by the other surgeons at the hospital for which he works.
Conclusion: Dr.Marks is an incompetent heart surgeon.

Notice that the author makes a cause-and-effect connection between the condition of Dr.Marks performing surgery on a set of patients (heart patients) and the condition of those patients experiencing an increased rate of death as compared to other patients (those of other surgeons).In other words, she claims that it is Dr.Marks’ incompetence as a heart surgeon that is the cause of his patients dying.

What is her evidence? Her evidence is the observation that Dr.Marks’ patients die more frequently than other surgeons’ patients.Remember that an observation that two variables occur together does not necessarily indicate that one variable (Dr.Marks operating on patients) has caused the other variable (patients die more frequently).

While it is possible that Dr.Marks is incompetent, are there other possible explanations for the difference in patient survival rates? Might the author have arrived at an incorrect conclusion? Might she have missed part of the story?
(A) When Dr. Marks worked at a different hospital, his patients were approximately twice as likely to die during surgery as patients who were operated on by other heart surgeons there.

What this answer choices says strengthens the argument because it provides further evidence that Dr.Marks’ patients die at rates much greater than those of patients who were attended to by other surgeons.

After all, it might be one thing for this phenomenon to occur at just one hospital.In that case, it could be that the support staff at the one hospital are not effective or that the equipment at that one hospital is subpar.In other words, if the phenomenon were occurring at just one hospital, we might be able to make the argument that the high death rate is as a result of hospital related factors rather than Dr.Marks’ incompetence.Right?
However, for this phenomenon to occur at two hospitals where Dr.Marks performs surgeries would certainly strengthen the argument that the problem lies squarely with Dr.Marks.
In answering a Weaken the Argument question, we must be careful to not choose as correct a choice that strengthens the argument.
(B) At the hospital for which he works, Dr. Marks’ patients and colleagues consistently praise both his bedside manner and his surgical skill.

His patients’ and colleagues’ consistently praising both his bedside manner and his surgical skill has little to no effect on the argument.Perhaps his colleagues are unethical people who know that Dr.Marks is a terrible surgeon but don’t like to talk badly about other surgeons.Or perhaps they and the patients are mostly ethical people, but all of them have been badly fooled for years by an incompetent but charming Dr.Marks.In general, the opinions and feelings of people do not take precedence over statistical data.
(C) The hospital for which Dr. Marks works provides its surgeons with advanced training that studies have shown to reduce not only deaths during surgery but also post-surgical infections.
What this answer choice says has no effect on the argument.

Regardless of any training the hospital may have provided to Dr.Marks, it is still the case that patients on whom Dr.Marks operates have a chance of dying during surgery nearly four times as high as that had by patients operated on by the other surgeons, and that evidence still supports the conclusion that Dr.Marks is incompetent.
(D) Hospital records show that, of the patients on whom Dr. Marks performs heart surgery, fewer than 8 of out of 100 experience post-surgical infections.
The argument is based on a comparison of Dr.Marks performance with that of his colleagues.Notice that this choice, on the other hand, provides information only about Dr.Marks.

Even though, 8 out of 100 may seem to be a pretty low infection rate, for all we know, only if a doctor were incompetent would 8 of out of 100 of that doctor’s patients experience post-surgical infections.
The truth is that we have nothing with which to compare the figures provided by this choice and no clear way to evaluate what they mean.
Also, the original argument is based on surgery-deaths rather than post-surgical infections, and the two may not be closely related.
So, the information provided by this choice has no clear effect on the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Because Dr. Marks has more experience than is had by the hospital’s other heart surgeons, the most difficult heart surgeries are assigned to him.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument by providing an alternative explanation for the observation that Dr.Marks’ patients die more frequently than other surgeons’ patients.

After all,if Dr.Marks is very experienced, and, as a result, the most difficult heart surgeries are assigned to him, the fact that his surgical-death rate is higher than the rate of the other surgeons is likely not the result of incompetence on the part of Dr.Marks.

What is likely going on is instead that Dr.Marks is performing surgery on patients who have a relatively low chance of survival, because their heart situations are particularly problematic.In this case, the higher chance of dying when operated on by Dr.Marks would not be any indication of incompetence.In other words, maybe he is so super-competent that he is sent only the most difficult of cases, in which patients naturally have a higher risk of death.

As always, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, we can weaken the support for that claim by showing that there is an alternative explanation for the observation made.In other words, when an author makes the claim that A is the cause of B, we can weaken that claim by showing factually that, in actuality, some other variable, call it C, might be the cause of B.

question.text: Five years ago, the city of Newvert imposed a new bicycle helmet regulation, requiring that anyone riding a bicycle in Newvert use a Newvert Transport Authority-approved helmet.Since that time, the number of head injuries treated by Newvert hospitals each year has steadily declined.Clearly, the new regulation has made it safer to ride a bicycle in Newvert.All of the following, if true, would strengthen the support for the conclusion, EXCEPT:

answers.text: The number of people riding bicycles in Newvert has not steadily declined over the last five years.
The proportion of people in Newvert treated by emergency rooms for head injuries not related to bicycles has steadily increased over the past five years.
Over the past five years, the city of Newvert has steadily added more miles of bicycle lanes on which bicyclists ride with unusually low risk of being involved in collisions.
Over the last five years, Newvert has not steadily added to its streets advanced ambulances that can treat less serious accident-related injuries, such as cuts and minor head injuries.
Over the past five years, it has not steadily become more convenient than it was previously for bicyclists injured in Newvert to be seen by hospitals in nearby Westvert.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Five years ago, the city of Newvert imposed a new bicycle helmet regulation, requiring that anyone riding a bicycle in Newvert use a Newvert Transport Authority-approved helmet.
Premise: Since that time, the number of head injuries treated by Newvert hospitals each year has steadily declined.
Conclusion: The new regulation has made it safer to ride a bicycle in Newvert.

Notice that we have an argument that involves cause-and-effect reasoning.Anytime an argument claims that there is a cause-and-effect link between events, we should be skeptical as to whether a cause-and-effect link actually exists.The fact that an event preceded a second event does not necessarily mean that the first event caused the second.

Now, this is a Strengthen the Argument EXCEPT question.So, in evaluating the answer choices, we will see four choices that support the author’s cause and effect claim.Then, the final choice remaining, the one that does NOT strengthen the argument, will be the correct answer.

In evaluating the answer choices, it’s important to remember that, in seeking an answer that does not strengthen the argument; we are not necessarily seeking an answer that weakens the argument.Like answers that weaken the argument, any answer that does not strengthen the argument is, well, an answer that does not strengthen the argument.So, the correct answer may be one that does not affect the argument at all.
(A) The number of people riding bicycles in Newvert has not steadily declined over the last five years.
This choice strengthens the argument.

Imagine if the number of people riding bicycles in Newvert HAS steadily declined over the last five years.Wouldn’t this fact provide a solid alternative explanation for why the number of head injuries treated by Newvert hospitals has steadily declined? Yes, it would, because fewer people riding bicycles in Newvert would likely result in fewer bicycle-related injuries of all types, including head injuries.In that case, the argument would be weakened.
Thus, what this choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out that possibility (an alternative explanation for why the number of head injuries has declined).
Remember, when presented with a cause and effect argument, we can strengthen the argument by ruling out alternative explanations for the cause and effect claim.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(B) The proportion of people in Newvert treated by emergency rooms for head injuries not related to bicycles has steadily increased over the past five years.
This choice strengthens the argument.

The fact that the proportion of people in Newvert treated by emergency rooms for head injuries not related to bicycles has steadily increased over the past five years confirms that the number of cycling-related head injuries has decreased.

After all, given what this choice says, we now know that not only has the total number of head injuries decreased but also the proportion of all head injuries represented by cycling-related head injuries has decreased.

The proportion of all head injuries represented by cycling-related head injuries must have decreased, because, if the proportion of head injuries not related to bicycles has steadily increased, the proportion of head injuries that are related to bicycles must have decreased.

Without the information provided by this choice, we did have from the passage the information that thetotalnumber of head injuries treated by Newvert hospitals has steadily declined, but notice, that decline could have come from a decline in non-cycling-related head injuries.In other words, for all we know from what the passage says, the number of head injuries declined, but the number of cycling-related head injuries did not.
However, when we combine what the passage says with what this choice says, we have information that makes clear that the number of cycling-related head injuries has in fact declined.
So, by confirming that fact, this choice strengthens the support for the conclusion.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Over the past five years, the city of Newvert has steadily added more miles of bicycle lanes on which bicyclists ride with unusually low risk of being involved in collisions.
This choice weakens the argument.

The argument is based on the premise that, after the city of Newvert imposed the helmet regulation, the number of head injuries treated in Newvert hospitals each year declined.Having observed that pattern of events, the author has concluded the first event (imposing the regulation) caused the second (decline in injuries).

This choice weakens the case for that cause-and-effect conclusion by providing an alternative explanation for the decline in head injuries: increasingly, people cycling in Newvert can use bicycle lanes on which bicyclists ride with unusually low risk of being involved in collisions, and, as a result of bicyclists’ using these lanes, the number of head injuries has decreased.

By indicating that that alternative explanation exists, this choice provides a reason to question the author’s conclusion that the imposition of the new regulation is the cause of the decline in head injuries, and thus weakens the argument.
Since this choice weakens, and therefore does not strengthen, the argument, this is our correct answer.
Strengthens the argument? No.
(D) Over the last five years, Newvert has not steadily added to its streets advanced ambulances that can treat less serious accident-related injuries, such as cuts and minor head injuries.
This choice strengthens the argument.

Imagine if, over the last five years, Newvert “HAS” steadily added to its streets advanced ambulances that can treat less serious accident-related injuries, such as cuts and minor head injuries.Wouldn’t that fact provide a solid alternative explanation for why the number of head injuries treated by Newvert hospitals has steadily declined? In that case, the argument would be weakened.
Thus, what this choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out that possibility.
Remember, when presented with a cause-and-effect argument, we can strengthen the argument by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed pattern of events.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(E) Over the past five years, it has not steadily become more convenient than it was previously for bicyclists injured in Newvert to be seen by hospitals in nearby Westvert.
This choice strengthens the argument.

Imagine if, over the past five years, it HAS steadily become more convenient than it was previously for bicyclists injured in Newvert to be seen by hospitals in nearby Westvert.Wouldn’t that fact provide a solid alternative explanation for why the number of head injuries treated by Newvert hospitals has steadily declined? After all, such a situation would likely result in more people being seen in Westvert hospitals, rather than Newvert hospitals, than were in the past.In that case, the argument would be weakened.
Thus, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument by serving to rule out that possibility.
Remember, when presented with a cause-and-effect argument, we can strengthen the argument by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed pattern of events.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.

question.text: One theory of the cause of drug addiction is that it is primarily the result of drugs’ interacting chemically with a person’s system in a way such that, once a person has used a drug for a certain period of time, the drug becomes so integrated into the person’s system that ending the use of the drug is extremely difficult.An alternative theory is that drug addiction is primarily a manifestation of continuing attempts to cope in stressful environments.Which of the following, if true, supports the conclusion that the second theory is the correct one?

answers.text: In communities in which availability of drugs is severely limited, it can be virtually impossible to become addicted to drugs.
When people seek to quit smoking, they often find refraining from smoking to be particularly difficult when they are in situations that they associate with smoking.
More than 75 percent of people who seek treatment for drug addiction report that they have engaged in violent behavior.
The majority of users of illicit addictive drugs live in rural and suburban areas rather than in urban areas.
When members of the military who have become addicted to drugs during wartime return home, most of them readily stop using drugs without professional assistance.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Theory 1: One theory of the cause of drug addiction is that it is primarily the result of drugs’ interacting chemically with a person’s system in a way such that, once a person has used a drug for a certain period of time, the drug becomes so integrated into the person’s system that ending the use of the drug is extremely difficult.
Theory 2: An alternative theory is that drug addiction is primarily a manifestation of continuing attempts to cope in stressful environments.
We are presented with two competing theories of the cause of drug addiction.Notice that for neither theory are we presented with any evidence to support the theory.
The question asks us to find support for the second theory.

The second theory is a theory of cause and effect.In other words, the second theory makes the claim that, when the cause (stressful environments) is present, so too is the effect (drug addiction).When evaluating the answer choices, we’ll be seeking one that provides support for that cause-and-effect claim.
(A) In communities in which availability of drugs is severely limited, it can be virtually impossible to become addicted to drugs.

If anything, the fact that, in communities in which availability of drugs is severely limited, it can be virtually impossible to become addicted to drugs strengthens the case for the first theory, because it shows that when drugs are not available to become “so integrated into the person’s system that ending the use of the drug is extremely difficult,” people don’t become addicted to drugs.
On the other hand, this choice does not strengthen the case for the second theory, since it does not provide a connection between stressful environments and drug use.
(B) When people seek to quit smoking, they often find refraining from smoking to be particularly difficult when they are in situations that they associate with smoking.

This choice could seem correct, because the theory for which we are seeking support is that addiction is a manifestation of people’s attempts to cope in “stressful environments,” and this choice says that refraining from smoking is difficult in certain “situations.” So, this choice could be perceived as confirming that addiction, such as addiction to smoking, is a manifestation of continuing attempts to cope in stressful environments.

However, we have to be careful not to mistakenly decide that the “situations” in which refraining from smoking is difficult are “stressful environments.” This answer choice does not say that the certain “situations” are “stressful environments.” It describes them only as “situations” that people “associate with smoking.” For all we know, those situations are not particularly stressful.A person could, for instance, associate going to the beach in summertime with smoking.

Since, for all we know from what this choice says, the situations mentioned in the choice are not particularly stressful, there’s no reason to believe that what this choice says confirms the theory that addiction is a manifestation of efforts to cope in stressful environments.
(C) More than 75 percent of people who seek treatment for drug addiction report that they have engaged in violent behavior.
If we were not reading carefully, we could choose this trap as the correct answer.

However, was the violent behavior a result of these people being in stressful environments, or was the violent behavior a result of these people using drugs regularly (and the environments in which they were in were not particularly stressfull)?

In other words, the fact that more than 75 percent of people who seek treatment for drug addiction report that they have engaged in violent behavior DOES NOT indicate that these people were also in stressful environments.
(D) The majority of users of illicit addictive drugs live in rural and suburban areas rather than in urban areas.

We might be tempted to decide that rural and suburban areas are environments more "stressful" than urban areas, possibly because of awareness on our part that some people living in rural areas live in poverty.

Once we would make that decision, we could then decide that this choice supports the conclusion, by showing that people living in the environments that we had decided are more "stressful" are more likely to use drugs, which information would demonstrate that the effect (drug use) is more likely to be present when the cause, (stressful environment) is present.

However, the truth is that we don't have a clear reason to decide that rural and suburban areas are environments more “stressful” than urban areas.Either type of area is stressful sometimes for some people and not particularly stressful at other times for other people.
So, what this choice says does not support the conclusion about coping in stressful environments and drug addiction.
YOUR CHOICE(E) When members of the military who have become addicted to drugs during wartime return home, most of them readily stop using drugs without professional assistance.
The second theory is that drug addiction is primarily a manifestation of continuing attempts to cope in stressful environments.

Notice that what this answer choice says, that when members of the military who have become addicted to drugs during wartime return home, most of them readily stop using drugs without professional assistance, gives us evidence to support this second theory.

This choice provides an interesting example.When the stressful environment (war) is present, the members of the military were addicted to drugs.However, when the cause went away (they came home), they stopped using drugs on their own.
So, this answer choice demonstrates that when the cause (stressful environment) is present, so too is the effect (the drug addiction).However, once the cause is taken away, so too is the effect.
Remember, in dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, by showing that, when the cause is removed, so too is the effect, we can strengthen the support for the cause-and-effect conclusion.

question.text: In Country X, mango retail sales volume has increased each year for the past decade.Over the same period of time, the retail price of mangos has steadily declined.We know from our economists that, as the price of an item in Country X drops, people tend to purchase more of that item.Therefore, it must be true that the declining price of mangos has driven the increase in mango sales volume.Which of the following, if true, most clearly strengthens the support for the conclusion?

answers.text: The population of Country X has steadily increased over the past ten years.
As a result of the price decline, the retail price of mangos in Country X is at a historic low.
During the past decade, the citizens of Country X have not become significantly more aware of the health benefits of eating fruit.
Over the past decade, farmers in Country X have lowered prices at which they sell mangos, enabling retailers to profit from the sale of mangos even at relatively low prices.
Over the last ten years, a significant proportion of the people of Country X have adopted a plant-based diet consisting mostly of fresh fruits and vegetables.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: In Country X, mango retail sales volume has increased each year for the past decade.
Premise: Over the same period of time, the retail price of mangos has steadily declined.
Premise: As the price of an item in Country X drops, people tend to purchase more of that item.
Conclusion: The declining price of mangos has driven the increase in mango sales volume.

In answering Critical Reasoning questions, we must always be on the lookout for errors in reasoning involving cause and effect.The fact that, in Country X, mango retail sales volume has increased each year for the past decade and the fact that, as price of an item in Country X drops, people tend to purchase more of that item combined do not necessarily indicate that the declining retail price of mangos has driven the increase in mango sales.

There could be something else that is actually responsible for the increase in mango sales volume, such as a greater number of people becoming aware of the health benefits of mangos or mango retailers engaging in better forms of marketing and advertising.

Now, of course, since this question is a Strengthen the Argument question, our job is to find an answer that increases the likelihood that it is in fact the declining retail price of mangos that has driven the increase in mango sales volume.
(A) The population of Country X has steadily increased over the past ten years.
The fact that the population of Country X has steadily increased over the past ten years clearly weakens this argument.

As the population of a country increases, the increase in the number of people purchasing goods and services naturally results in increased sales of all goods and services in that country.Therefore, this population increase would be a compelling alternative reason why sales of mangos have increased each year for the past decade in Country X.

By providing an alternative explanation for the increase in mango sales, what this choice says gives us less conviction that it has been the steady decline in the price of mangos that has been driving up retail mango sales.
(B) As a result of the price decline, the retail price of mangos in Country X is at a historic low.

The fact that, as a result of the price decline, the price of mangos in Country X is at a historic low just magnifies one of the argument’s premises, that the price of mangos has steadily declined.The fact that the price of mangos is at a historic low does not make the fact that the price of mangos had declined somehow truer and does not prove that there is a connection between the price decline and the increase in mango sales volume.

Be careful not to choose statements that repeat or magnify an argument’s premises.After all, we must take these premises to be factually true.Once a premise is factually true, restating or magnifying it will not add strength to an argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) During the past decade, the citizens of Country X have not become significantly more aware of the health benefits of eating fruit.

Imagine if, during the past decade, the citizens of Country X HAVE become significantly more aware of the health benefits of eating fruit.In this situation, we would certainly expect to have seen an increase in mango sales, and we would be less sure that it has been the steady decline in the price of mangos that has been driving up mango sales volume.

Thus what this choice says, that the citizens HAVE NOT become significantly more aware of the health benefits of eating fruit, strengthens the argument by serving to rule out an alternative explanation for the increase in sales volume.
Remember, when presented with a cause-and-effect argument, we can strengthen the argument by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed events.
(D) Over the past decade, farmers in Country X have lowered prices at which they sell mangos, enabling retailers to profit from the sale of mangos even at relatively low prices.
This choice has the wrong effect on the argument.

The passage tells us that retail sales of mangos have increased each year for the past decade, and that, over the same period of time, the retail price of mangos has steadily declined.What this answer choice says, that over the past decade, farmers in Country X have lowered prices at which they sell mangos, enabling retailers to profit from the sale of mangos even at relatively low prices, may explainwhythe retail price of mangos has steadily declined.

At the same, we already know that the price of mangos has steady declined, and the argument’s conclusion is based upon this fact.An explanation for why the decline has occurred does not make the fact that the decline has occurred truer, and an explanation for why the decline has occurred does not more clearly connect the decline in the retail price to the increase in mango retail sales volume.

In other words, a choice that explains why the supposed cause, the price decline, has occurred does not strengthen the case for believing that the price decline is the cause of the increase in sales volume.
(E) Over the last ten years, a significant proportion of the people of Country X have adopted a plant-based diet consisting mostly of fresh fruits and vegetables.
What this choice says weakens the argument.

If it is true that, over the last ten years, a significant proportion of the people of Country X have adopted a plant-based diet consisting mostly of fresh fruits and vegetables, then we have an alternative explanation for why mango retail sales volume has increased each year for the past decade: people are eating more fruit, such as mangos, in general.

Thus, by saying that a significant proportion of the people of Country X have adopted a plant-based diet consisting mostly of fresh fruits and vegetables, this choice provides an alternative explanation for the increase in mango sales volume, making us less convinced that the price decline was the cause.
Of course, we need a choice that strengthens, not one that weakens, the argument.

question.text: In talking with 500 people for a research study, a psychologist discovered that almost all of the participants who suffered from alcohol addiction also suffered from clinical depression.As a result, she hypothesized that their suffering from alcohol addiction was the cause of their clinical depression.Which of the following, if true, would cast the most doubt on the psychologist’s hypothesis?

answers.text: The experience of extremely dysfunctional family dynamics is known to influence people to become clinically depressed and to influence people to become addicted to alcohol.
One effect of a person’s drinking alcohol is increases in that person’s levels of norepinephrine, a hormone with stimulant effects rather than depressive effects.
Only 220 of the 500 participants in the study were addicted to alcohol.
Alcohol depresses a region of the brain that is known to be underactive in clinically depressed patients but not underactive in patients who are not clinically depressed.
Of the 500 people with whom the psychologist spoke, 450 indicated that they never experienced symptoms of clinical depression prior to drinking excessive amounts of alcohol.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In talking with 500 people for a research study, a psychologist discovered that almost all of the participants who suffered from alcohol addiction also suffered from clinical depression.
Conclusion: As a result, she hypothesized that their suffering from alcohol addiction was the cause of their clinical depression.

Notice that we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim.The psychologist sees a correlation between people’s experiencing alcohol addiction and clinical depression and arrives at the cause-and-effect conclusion that the people’s suffering from alcohol addiction was the cause of their clinical depression.

Remember that the fact that two variables occur together does not necessarily indicate that there exists a cause-and-effect link between those two variables.In other words, the fact that a psychologist discovered that almost all of the participants who suffered from alcohol addiction also suffered from clinical depression does not necessarily indicate that the alcohol addiction is the cause of the clinical depression.Right? Might there be an alternative explanation for this observation? Might there be another reason why these two variables are seen together?
YOUR CHOICE(A) The experience of extremely dysfunctional family dynamics is known to influence people to become clinically depressed and to influence people to become addicted to alcohol.
What this answer choice says weakens the argument.

The psychologist makes the claim that people’s experiencing alcohol addiction is the cause of clinical depression.This choice introduces a new variable, the experience of extremely dysfunctional family dynamics, which is known to cause both alcohol addiction and clinical depression.
This new information does not prove that the psychologist is definitely mistaken, but this new information casts doubt on the psychologist’s hypothesis in the following ways.
It provides a credible alternative cause for the clinical depression: the experience of extremely dysfunctional family dynamics.

It provides a credible alternative explanation for why the two phenomena (alcohol addiction and clinical depression) appear simultaneously: they are both caused by the experience of extremely dysfunctional family dynamics.

It’s important to keep in mind that, if we consistently observe two events, A and B, at the same time, it is possible that A does not cause B, and that, as we see in this situation, both events are caused by a third event, C.

(B)Incorrect.While it may seem that the fact that drinking alcohol causes increases in a person’s levels of a hormone with stimulant effects rather than depressive effects indicates that addiction to alcohol would not cause depression, this choice does not weaken the argument because the argument is about the effects ofsuffering from alcohol addiction, while this choice is aboutthe direct effects of drinking alcohol.
It could very well be that, even though drinking alcohol itself has stimulant effects, the overall experience of addiction to alcohol causes clinical depression.
Think of another extreme situation.Cocaine use produces an array of stimulatory effects in the body.However, people who are addicted to cocaine are often depressed.
(C) Only 220 of the 500 participants in the study were addicted to alcohol.
The fact that only 220 of the 500 participants in the study were addicted to alcohol has absolutely no effect on the argument.
The argument is not about all of the participants.It’s about the ones who suffered with alcohol addiction.So, all this choice tells us is how many of the participants the argument is about, 220.
(D) Alcohol depresses a region of the brain that is known to be underactive in clinically depressed patients but not underactive in patients who are not clinically depressed.

That alcohol depresses a region of the brain that is known to be underactive in clinically depressed patients but not underactive in patients who are not clinically depressed supports the psychologist’s hypothesis.

After all, the fact that alcohol depresses a region of the brain that is known to be underactive in clinically depressed patients, but not underactive in patients who are not clinically depressed, tends to indicate that consumption of alcohol causes depression.
Of course, we are not looking for support for the psychologist’s hypothesis.We are looking for information that serves to call it into question.
(E) Of the 500 people with whom the psychologist spoke, 450 indicated that they never experienced symptoms of clinical depression prior to drinking excessive amounts of alcohol.

The fact that people indicated that they never experienced symptoms of clinical depression prior to drinking excessive amounts of alcohol is certainly in line with the psychologist’s hypothesis that suffering with alcohol addiction was the cause of the clinical depression seen in the participants.
We are looking for a fact that serves to call the psychologist’s hypothesis into question, not a fact that’s in line with the psychologist’s hypothesis.

question.text: Researchers tested the blood of people who had reported consistently experiencing poor quality sleep.The researchers noticed that a high percentage of the study participants had low levels of vitamin-D, which is produced naturally by the body when the body is exposed to sunlight, and higher than normal sleep-time levels of the hormone cortisol, which is released during times of anxiety.The researchers concluded that low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol were sufficient to cause poor quality sleep.Each of the following, if true, would strengthen the support for the researchers’ conclusion, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Poor quality sleep does not cause low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol.
Daytime stress does not cause low levels of vitamin-D, higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol, and poor-quality sleep.
The combination of low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol has been associated with a variety of health issues.
Participating in a sleep research study does not cause a person to become tired and anxious during the day, leading to the person’s remaining indoors away from sunlight.
When participants were put on diets that caused their vitamin-D levels to increase and their sleep-time levels of cortisol to decrease, their sleep quality improved.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Researchers tested the blood of people who had reported consistently experiencing poor quality sleep.

Premise: The researchers noticed that a high percentage of the study participants had low levels of vitamin-D, which is produced naturally by the body when the body is exposed to sunlight, and higher than normal sleep-time levels of the hormone cortisol, which is released during times of anxiety.
Conclusion: The researchers concluded that low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol were sufficient to cause poor quality sleep.

Notice that we have a cause and effect argument.The author believes that low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol were sufficient to cause poor quality sleep.Why does he think that? His evidence is that a high percentage of the study participants had low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of the hormone cortisol.
It’s important to see that the fact that the occurrences described in the passage are found to happen at the same time is not solid evidence that some cause the other.

If we consistently observe A and B at the same time, it is equally possible that A causes B or that B causes A.It is also possible that both are caused by C, which is something else entirely, or that A and B have two separate causes, D and E.

In this situation, our job is to ask, “are low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol really sufficient to cause poor quality sleep, or might there be some other explanation at play?”

Of course, this is a Strengthen the Argument EXCEPT question.So, in evaluating the answer choices, we will see and eliminate four choices that support the argument.These four will be incorrect choices.The fifth choice, the one that does NOT strengthen the argument, will be the correct answer.

In evaluating the answer choices, it’s important to remember that, in seeking an answer that does not strengthen the argument, we are not necessarily seeking an answer that weakens the argument.Along with any choice that weakens the argument, any answer that does not strengthen the argument is, well, an answer that does not strengthen the argument.For example, an answer choice could say something that does not affect the strength or the argument.In a question like this, that answer choice would be correct.
(A) Poor quality sleep does not cause low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol.
What this choice says strengthens the argument.

Imagine if it were true that poor quality sleep DOES cause low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol.That fact would weaken the argument by indicating that the author’s conclusion that low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol were sufficient to cause poor quality sleep is likely incorrect.
In that case it would be likely that the author has the cause and effect reversed.

In other words, it would be likely that poor-quality sleep caused the low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol seen in the study participants.That is, the cause and effect would likely be the reverse of what the author claims.

So, what this answer choice says, that “Poor quality sleepdoes notcause low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol” strengthens the argument by ruling out that alternative explanation.
We can always strengthen a cause-and-effect argument by ruling out alternative explanations for the observed pattern of events.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(B) Daytime stress does not cause low levels of vitamin-D, higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol, and poor-quality sleep.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for the observation made.
After all, imagine if it were true that daytime stress DOES cause low levels of vitamin-D, higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol, and poor-quality sleep.

In that case, the author would likely have the cause and effect incorrect, because the truth of the matter would likely be that another factor – daytime stress – caused all three of the observed occurrences: low levels of vitamin-D, higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol, and poor-quality sleep.

So, by saying that daytime stress DOES NOT cause low levels of vitamin-D, higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol, and poor-quality sleep, this choice serves to rule out that alternative explanation and, thus, strengthens the argument.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The combination of low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol has been associated with a variety of health issues.

The statement made by this choice is a vague one.To which health issues does this statement refer? Notice that the conclusion of the argument is that low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol were sufficient to cause a specific health issue, “poor quality sleep.”

Of course, not everything that causes “a variety of health issues” causes “poor quality of sleep.” Therefore, the fact that that, as this choice says, the combination of low levels of vitamin-D and higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol has been associated with a variety of health issues does not confirm that that combination causes poor quality sleep.

If you thought this choice to be a strengthener, you likely somehow related poor-quality sleep to health issues.However, these two concepts are not the same.We must be careful to not make unwarranted assumptions or inferences, especially when presented with vague terms or concepts that are poorly defined.We really have no idea what “a variety of heath issues” means or how those health issues are related to poor quality sleep, if they even are at all.
Strengthens the argument? No.
(D) Participating in a sleep research study does not cause a person to become tired and anxious during the day, leading to the person’s remaining indoors away from sunlight.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by ruling out an alternative explanation for the observation made.
After all, imagine if participating in a sleep research study DOES cause a person to become tired and anxious during the day, leading to the person’s remaining indoors away from sunlight.

In this case, the author would likely have the cause and effect incorrect, because the truth of the matter would likely be that another variable – becoming tired and anxious during the day, leading to the person’s remaining indoors away from sunlight – caused all three observed occurrences: low levels of vitamin-D, higher than normal sleep-time levels of cortisol, and poor-quality sleep.

So, by saying that participating in a sleep research study DOES NOT cause a person to become tired and anxious during the day, leading to the person’s remaining indoors away from sunlight, this choice serves to rule out that alternate explanation for the observed pattern of occurrences, thus strengthening the argument.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.
(E) When participants were put on diets that caused their vitamin-D levels to increase and their sleep-time levels of cortisol to decrease, their sleep quality improved.
What this answer choice says strengthens the argument by showing that, once the cause is removed, the effect is also removed.

Given what this choice says, we see that, when participants were put on diets that caused their vitamin-D levels to increase and their sleep-time levels of cortisol to decrease, their sleep quality improved.
In other words, when the causes, relatively low vitamin-D levels and relatively high levels of cortisol were taken away, the effect, poor sleep, disappeared as well.
Therefore, given what this choice says, we have much more conviction in the accuracy of the author’s cause-and-effect claim.
Strengthens the argument? Yes.

question.text: To help its citizens avoid experiencing heart disease, nine years ago, the government of Zadan imposed a tax on the sale of any product containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving.Since then, purchases of products containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving have declined by 13 percent, and the incidence of heart disease in Zadan has decreased by 10 percent.The health minister of Zadan has declared the tax a resounding success, claiming that it must be responsible for the decrease in the incidence of heart disease.Which of the following, if true, most undermines the support for the health minister’s conclusion?

answers.text: Some people in Zadan currently consume more products that are high in refined sugar than they were consuming before the new tax was enacted.
Zadans who stopped purchasing the newly taxed products developed heart disease at the same rate as the general population.
Since the tax was enacted, retail sales of refined sugar itself have not declined significantly in Zadan.
Ten years ago, the government of Zadan imposed a tax on foods containing trans fats, but since then, the incidence of trans-fat-related health problems has not declined.
Since the time when the tax on products containing more than 10 grams of sugar per serving was enacted, the inflation adjusted effect of the tax has declined.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: Nine years ago, the government of Zadan imposed a tax on the sale of any product containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving.

Premise: Since then, purchases of products containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving have declined by 13 percent, and the incidence of heart disease in Zadan has decreased by 10 percent.
Conclusion: The health minister of Zadan has declared that the tax must be responsible for the decrease in the incidence of heart disease.

Notice that the health minister’s conclusion is that there is a cause-and-effect link between the tax on the sale of any product containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving and the 10 percent decline in the incidence of heart disease.

In essence, the minister concludes that the decline in purchases of the taxed, high-sugar products caused the decrease in the incidence of heart disease (presumably because people were consuming less sugar).
Of course, the fact that circumstances occur in succession does not provide us with evidence sufficient for concluding that any one circumstance caused the other.
Something other than the decline in purchases of the high-sugar products could have caused the decrease in heart disease.What might the author have failed to consider?
(A) Some people in Zadan currently consume more products that are high in refined sugar than they were consuming before the new tax was enacted.
It’s important to remember that the premises in Critical Reasoning questions are factual.
The passage says that “purchases of products containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving have declined by 13 percent.” This is a statement of fact.
That some people in Zadan currently consume more high-sugar products than they were consuming before the tax does not change the facts upon which the argument’s conclusion is based.

Yes, some people increased their consumption of products containing sugar, but overall, what the passage says remains true: purchases of products containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving have declined.
Thus, this choice has no effect on the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Zadans who stopped purchasing the newly taxed products developed heart disease at the same rate as the general population.

If it is true that the citizens who stopped purchasing the newly taxed products developed heart disease at the same rate as the general population, then the minister’s conclusion is weakened.After all, the minister concludes that the decline in the purchases of the taxed products caused the decrease in heart disease.
For that conclusion to be supported, we would need to see a decrease in the rate of heart disease among people who used to purchase the products but stopped doing so after the tax was enacted.
With no decrease in heart disease among that particular subset of the population, we have reason to believe that the decline in product purchases had no effect on the incidence of heart disease.
Thus, this choice weakens the argument.
(C) Since the tax was enacted, retail sales of refined sugar itself have not declined significantly in Zadan.
It’s important to remember that the premises provided to us in Critical Reasoning questions are factual.
The passage tells us that “purchases of products containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving have declined by 13 percent.” This statement is a fact.

So, even if it is the case that retail sales of refined sugar itself have not declined significantly, it is still true that purchases of products containing more than 10 grams of refined sugar per serving have declined.
After all, purchases of “refined sugar itself” are not the same thing as purchases of products containing certain amounts of refined sugar.
So, this choice does not affect the strength of the argument.
(D) Ten years ago, the government of Zadan imposed a tax on foods containing trans fats, but since then, the incidence of trans-fat-related health problems has not declined.

We may be tempted by this choice because it presents what seems to be a similar scenario to the one in the passage but states that the desired effect was not achieved.Thus, we may view this choice as evidence that the tax on sugary products would not work.
However, the ineffectiveness of a past tax on foods containing some other substance does not mean that the current tax on products containing refined sugars did not work.

Perhaps some taxes on unhealthy products don’t work to improve the population’s health and other taxes on other products do.Or perhaps there is something different about what was going on in Zadan ten years ago than there is today.
This choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
(E) Since the time when the tax on products containing more than 10 grams of sugar per serving was enacted, the inflation adjusted effect of the tax has declined.
This choice is incorrect because it doesn’t really change the facts.
We know that a tax on products containing refined sugar was imposed and that purchases of the taxed products have declined.

So, even if the tax has become less significant because of inflation, the tax could still be having some effect—even a sizable effect.After all, this choice does not say how much the effect of the tax has declined.
So, the tax could still be the cause of the decline in product purchases, and thus the decrease in the incidence of heart disease.
This choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
Answer: B

question.text: It must be true that the physicians who work in Vickensburg are among some of the least competent physicians in any high-income town in the state.Citizens of Vickensburg spend more per patient on healthcare than is spent per patient on healthcare in any other high-income town in the state.However, the citizens of Vickensburg have some of the worst patient health outcomes of citizens of any such town.Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the argument above?

answers.text: If physicians from other high-income towns were to replace Vickensburg’s current physicians, the amount spent per patient on healthcare in Vickensburg would decrease, and health outcomes there would improve.
Were Vickensburg to upgrade its hospitals and the medical devices used within them, the amount spent per patient on healthcare and health outcomes in Vickensburg would remain about the same.
Were Vickensburg citizens to seek medical care in other high-income towns, those towns would see increases in amounts spent per patient on healthcare and declines in average patient health outcomes.

If the number of physicians working in Vickensburg were to double, in Vickensburg, the amount spent per patient on healthcare would increase slightly, and patient health outcomes would remain constant.

If, in Vickensburg, more healthcare spending were directed to preventative healthcare, the number of citizens seeking treatment in hospitals there would decrease, and patient health outcomes would remain about the same.

explanation_parts.text: From the argument, we can see that the author believes that the physicians who work in Vickensburg are responsible for (are the cause of) the citizens of Vickensburg having some of the worst patient health outcomes of citizens of any such town.As always, we must be skeptical of cause-and-effect claims.Are the physicians who work in Vickensburg really to blame for the health outcomes? Could there be an alternative explanation? What might the author have missed? What else might explain the phenomenon presented in the passage?

(A) If physicians from other high-income towns were to replace Vickensburg’s current physicians, the amount spent per patient on healthcare in Vickensburg would decrease, and health outcomes there would improve.

Notice that what this answer choice says strengthens the author’s argument because it further substantiates the author’s point that it is the physicians who are responsible for the poor patient health outcomes.

After all, if we replace the physicians in question with a different group of physicians and all of a sudden the amount spent per patient on healthcare in Vickensburg decreases, and health outcomes improve, then there is a good chance that the physicians in Vickensburg were to blame.
Of course, we are not seeking an answer choice that strengthens the argument.Rather, we’re seeking one that weakens it.
(B) Were Vickensburg to upgrade its hospitals and the medical devices used within them, the amount spent per patient on healthcare and health outcomes in Vickensburg would remain about the same.

To not choose this answer as correct, we need to pay close attention to the author’s conclusion, which is “It must be true that the physicians who work in Vickensburg are among some of the least competent physicians in any high-income town in the state.”
To weaken this argument, we need something that indicates that, in fact, it is not the physicians who work in Vickensburg who are responsible for the poor patient outcomes.

However, notice that what this answer choice says, that were Vickensburg to upgrade its hospitals and the medical devices used within them, the amount spent per patient on healthcare and health outcomes in Vickensburg would remain about the same, essentially says that with better facilities and devices used within them, not too much would change in regards to amount spent per patient on healthcare and health outcomes.

Ok – great.But what does that tell us about whether or not the physicians who work in Vickensburg are responsible for (are the cause of) the citizens of Vickensburg having some of the worst patient health outcomes of citizens of any such town? What it says does not give us any less conviction regarding these physicians, right? So, what this answer choice says does not weaken the argument.

YOUR CHOICE(C) Were Vickensburg citizens to seek medical care in other high-income towns, those towns would see increases in amounts spent per patient on healthcare and declines in average patient health outcomes.

Notice that what this answer choice says points to an alternative explanation for the observation that, despite the fact that citizens of Vickensburg spend more per patient on healthcare than is spent per patient on healthcare in any other high-income town in the state, the citizens of Vickensburg have some of the worst patient health outcomes of citizens of any such town.

Of course, the author believes that the physicians who work in Vickensburg are the cause of this phenomenon.However, what this answer choice says weakens the argument by showing that the author’s cause-and-effect claim might very well be incorrect.Perhaps it’s not the physicians after all; perhaps it’s the patients.Right?

After all, if, were Vickensburg citizens to seek medical care in other high-income towns, those towns would see increases in amounts spent per patient on healthcare and declines in average patient health outcomes, then it would logical to believe that there is something about these patients that is causing them to have some of the worst patient health outcomes of citizens of any such town.
By providing an alternative explanation for the phenomenon provided in the passage, what this answer choice says weakens the argument.

(D) If the number of physicians working in Vickensburg were to double, in Vickensburg, the amount spent per patient on healthcare would increase slightly, and patient health outcomes would remain constant.

To not choose this answer as correct, we need to pay close attention to the author’s conclusion, which is “It must be true that the physicians who work in Vickensburg are among some of the least competent physicians in any high-income town in the state.”

To weaken this argument, we need something that indicates that, in fact, it is not the physicians who work in Vickensburg who are responsible for the poor patient outcomes.However, notice that what this answer choice says, that if the number of physicians working in Vickensburg were to double, in Vickensburg, the amount spent per patient on healthcare would increase slightly, and patient health outcomes would remain constant, essentially says that with double the number of physicians, not too much would change in regards to amount spent per patient on healthcare and health outcomes.

Ok – great.But what does that tell us about whether or not the physicians who work in Vickensburg are responsible for (are the cause of) the citizens of Vickensburg having some of the worst patient health outcomes of citizens of any such town? What it says does not give us any less (or more) conviction regarding these physicians, right? So, what this answer choice says does not weaken the argument.

(E) If, in Vickensburg, more healthcare spending were directed to preventative healthcare, the number of citizens seeking treatment in hospitals there would decrease, and patient health outcomes would remain about the same.

What this answer choice says, that if, in Vickensburg, more healthcare spending were directed to preventative healthcare, the number of citizens seeking treatment in hospitals there would decrease, and patient health outcomes would remain about the same, is what we would expect regardless of whether the argument’s conclusion is correct.

In other words, in what way would what this answer choice says weaken the argument? What it says does not give us any less (or more) conviction regarding these physicians, right? So, what this answer choice says does not weaken the argument.

question.text: Supplements Company Press Release: The results are in.Data collected from people using our herbal supplement to prevent joint inflammation show the incidence of joint inflammation in those people to be around the same as the incidence of joint inflammation in people who used the herb Devil’s Claw when they participated in research that showed Devil’s Claw to be effective in preventing joint inflammation.Therefore, anyone seeking to prevent joint inflammation can be confident that our herbal supplement will help them to do so.Which of the following, if true, most undermines the force of the evidence cited?

answers.text: Those who have used Devil’s Claw to prevent joint inflammation have reported no negative effects, even in cases in which it has been used in high doses for extended periods of time.
In preventing joint inflammation, more important than using supplements are reducing intake of sugar and getting sufficient sleep.
The mechanism via which the company’s herbal supplement prevents joint inflammation has not been identified.
Almost all people using herbal supplements to prevent joint inflammation include Devil’s Claw among the supplements that they use.
The studies demonstrating the effectiveness of Devil’s Claw were conducted over a period of years and involved many participants.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Premise: Data collected on people using our herbal supplement to prevent joint inflammation show the incidence of joint inflammation in those people to be around the same as the incidence of joint inflammation in people who used the herb Devil’s Claw when they participated in research that showed Devil’s Claw to be effective in preventing joint inflammation.
Conclusion: People can be confident that our herbal supplement will help them to prevent joint inflammation.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since the incidence of inflammation in people using the company’s supplement is the same at that in people using Devil’s Claw, which has been shown to be effective in preventing inflammation, the company’s supplement must also be effective in preventing inflammation.

A key thing to note here is the nature of the evidence that the argument uses.The only evidence provided to support the conclusion is the incidence of joint inflammation in people using the company’s supplement.There is plenty of room for the possibility that something other than use of the company’s supplement could be preventing joint inflammation in those people.

As always, we must be skeptical of cause-and-effect claims.Is the company’s herbal supplement really the cause of the reduced inflammation? Could there be an alternative explanation? What might the author have missed?
(A) Those who have used Devil’s Claw to prevent joint inflammation have reported no negative effects, even in cases in which it has been used in high doses for extended periods of time.

The fact that people using Devil’s Claw report no negative effects from its use does not have any effect on the support for the conclusion that the company’s supplement helps to prevent joint inflammation.
The point of the argument is not to compare the company’s supplement with Devil’s Claw.The conclusion is simple: the company’s supplement helps to prevent joint inflammation.

Since information about the side effects of using Devil’s Claw does not indicate anything about whether the company’s supplement helps with prevention of joint inflammation, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.

It’s important to not choose as correct answer choices that say things that are helpful to know, or that are true in reality, yet don’t weaken the argument.In other words, if you picked this choice as correct, you likely did so because what it says resembles what you might have heard regarding certain herbal supplements.However, we are not seeking something that is just true in reality; we are seeking something that weakens the argument.
(B) In preventing joint inflammation, more important than using supplements are reducing intake of sugar and getting sufficient sleep.

This is a typical GMAT Critical Reasoning “better method” trap answer, one that could be perceived as weakening the conclusion that one method is useful because it presents another method that is more useful, or in this case, more “important.”

The fact that sleeping and reducing sugar intake are more important than using supplements does not affect the support the evidence provides for the conclusion that the company’s supplement can be helpful.

We must always be watchful for the “better plan” / “different plan” trap choice.The fact that there is a plan better than the one an author proposes does not indicate that the author’s plan is not a sound one.So, when a plan is proposed, we can’t weaken that conclusion that that plan will work by claiming that there is a better plan available.
(C) The mechanism via which the company’s herbal supplement prevents joint inflammation has not been identified.

The conclusion is supported by statistical evidence that indicates that the company’s supplement is helpful.The fact that the mechanism via which the supplement works has not been identified does not affect the support the statistical evidence provides for the conclusion.

It’s important to realize that, although it would be great to understand the mechanism behind a process we use, the truth is that the fact that we don’t understand the mechanisms of a process does not mean that that process does not work.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Almost all people using herbal supplements to prevent joint inflammation include Devil’s Claw among the supplements that they use.
The conclusion that the company’s supplement is helpful is supported by the fact that people using the supplement see results similar to those achieved by people who used Devil’s Claw.

What this choice says significantly undermines the force of that evidence, because what it says indicates that it is likely that the people using the company’s supplements were using Devil’s Claw as well.Thus, what this choice says renders virtually meaningless what appeared to be supporting evidence, as what this choice says indicates that the results achieved by the people using the company’s supplement could be attributable to use of Devil’s Claw rather than to use of the company’s supplement.

Notice that what this answer choice says indicates that there is an alternative explanation for the pattern of events described in the passage.We can weaken a cause and effect argument by showing that perhaps there is an alternative explanation for the observed pattern.

Another way of looking at what this answer choice says is that the author must assume that the people whom he cites as evidence are not also taking Devil’s Claw.After all, if they were taking Devil’s Claw then the conclusion would not be sound because there would be no way to know whether it was the Devil’s Claw or the company’s supplement that was causing the positive effect.What this answer choices says is that that assumption is not accurate.If that assumption is not accurate, then the argument falls apart.
(E) The studies demonstrating the effectiveness of Devil’s Claw were conducted over a period of years and involved many participants.

The argument rests on the simple point that the people using the company’s supplement achieved results similar to those achieved by people who used Devil’s Claw when they participated in certain studies.
