The fact that there were many participants in those studies and that the studies took place over a period of years may make the results of the Devil’s Claw studies seem to provide more support for the conclusion that Devil’s Claw is helpful than the argument provides for the conclusion that the company’s supplement is helpful.

However, we are not seeking to compare the force of the argument’s evidence with the force of the evidence in another argument.We are looking for information that undermines the force of the argument’s evidence, and information on these details about the Devil’s Claw studies does not undermine the force of the argument’s evidence.

question.text: To improve the safety of people who ride motorcycles, we should encourage them to ride motorcycles with larger, more powerful engines.While motorcycles with larger, more powerful engines generally accelerate faster than and achieve higher speeds than are achieved by motorcycles that have smaller engines, the majority of motorcycle crashes involve motorcycles with smaller engines.Which of the following if true most seriously weakens the argument above?

answers.text: More experienced and knowledgeable riders are more likely to ride motorcycles that have larger engines than motorcycles that have smaller engines.
Motorcycles that have smaller engines tend to be lighter and therefore easier to maneuver at low speeds than motorcycles with larger engines.
The average speed at which motorcycles were traveling just prior to crashes is relatively low, approximately 30 miles per hour.
Approximately 75 percent of crashes involving motorcycles also involve another vehicle, most often a passenger automobile.
Passing on two lane highways can generally be done more quickly, and thus more safely, on a motorcycle with a larger engine than on one with a smaller engine.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Motorcycles with larger, more powerful engines accelerate faster and achieve higher speeds than do motorcycles that have smaller engines.
Premise: Majority of motorcycle crashes involve motorcycles with smaller engines.
Conclusion: To improve the safety of people who ride motorcycles, we should encourage them to ride motorcycles with larger, more powerful engines.

The reasoning of the argument is based on the assumption that the correlative relationship between motorcycles with smaller engines and crashes is also a cause-and-effect relationship.In other words, the author believes that riding motorcycles with smaller engines somehow results in crashes, and that riding motorcycles with larger engines causes motorcycle safety.

As always, we must be skeptical of cause and effect claims.Is riding motorcycles with larger engines really the cause of the improved safety? Could there be an alternative explanation? What might the author be missing? Might there be another cause for the observed correlation?
YOUR CHOICE(A) More experienced and knowledgeable riders are more likely to ride motorcycles that have larger engines than motorcycles that have smaller engines.

Since common sense tells us that more experienced riders would likely ride more safely, what this choice indicates, essentially, is that riders who ride more safely tend to ride motorcycles with larger more powerful engines.Thus, it seems likely that the correlation between larger engines and greater safety results not from the larger engines themselves but rather from safer riding that is attributable to experience.

So, what this answer choice says weakens the argument by providing reason to believe that the relationship between larger engines and safety is not a cause-and-effect relationship, and that instead, the same factor, rider experience, is connected both to riders’ riding motorcycles with larger engines and to greater safety.In other words, here we see that it may be a third variable, rider experience, that underlies the correlation upon which the argument is based.

Notice that this choice attacks the author’s assumption that the observed correlation between motorcycle engine size and safety is underlied by a cause-and-effect relationship between those two variables.
(B) Motorcycles that have smaller engines tend to be lighter and therefore easier to maneuver at low speeds than motorcycles with larger engines.
What this choice says could be construed as weakening the argument, because it presents what appears to be a safety enhancing advantage of motorcycles with smaller engines.

However, the truth is thatregardlessof the fact that, as this choice says motorcycles that have smaller engines tend to be easier to maneuver at low speeds than motorcycles with larger, the majority of motorcycle crashes involve motorcycles with smaller engines.
So, this choice does not affect the relationship between smaller engines and crashes and, thus, does not affect the argument.

Remember, it’s important not to try to weaken an argument by undermining premises provided in the argument, because any premise is considered factual.For example, in this argument, we are told – and must take it as fact that - the “majority of motorcycle crashes involve motorcycles with smaller engines.” That statement is one of fact that a choice cannot negate.
(C) The average speed at which motorcycles were traveling just prior to crashes is relatively low, approximately 30 miles per hour.

Since lower speed is associated with smaller motorcycles in the passage, one might perceive what this choice says as indicating something about the relative safety of different types of motorcycles.So, we might be tempted to take what this choice says and make up a story about motorcycle speeds and crashes that would somehow weaken the argument.

However, like what choice (B) says, what this choice says does not affect the basis of the argument, which is the fact that the majority of motorcycle crashes involve motorcycles with smaller engines.
Regardless of the speed at which motorcycles were traveling just prior to crashes, the premise that is used to support the conclusion is still true and still seems to support the conclusion.
(D) Approximately 75 percent of crashes involving motorcycles also involve another vehicle, most often a passenger automobile.

What this choice says could be perceived as indicating that most crashes involving motorcycles are actually caused by the actions of drivers of other vehicles, and by extension one could take what this choice says as indicating that, since the crashes are not caused by the riders of motorcycles, attributes of motorcycles, such as engine size, do not affect motorcycle rider safety.

However, if we really consider the logic of what this choice says, we can see that the fact that motorcycle crashes involve other vehicles does not really mean that the likelihood of motorcycle riders’ being involved in crashes has nothing to do with the type of motorcycles they ride.
So, what this choice says does not affect the argument.
(E) Passing on two lane highways can generally be done more quickly, and thus more safely, on a motorcycle with a larger engine than on one with a smaller engine.

Since it presents what appears to be a safety enhancing advantage of motorcycles with larger engines, what this choice says is in line with the conclusion of the argument.Therefore, this choice does not weaken the argument.
We must be careful not to choose as correct answer choices that say things that are in line with or strengthen the argument when we’re seeking a weakener.

question.text: According to health records of patients in Iran, people living in Iran have one of the highest rates of throat cancer in the world.A recent study sheds some light on this finding; it determined that Iranians typically drink considerably more Persian black tea per year than is drunk by people in other countries.Therefore, people would be well advised to eliminate Persian black tea from their diets to reduce their risk of developing throat cancer.All of the following, if true, would weaken the case for the recommendation made, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Iranians drink their Persian black tea at temperatures much higher than those at which other people drink tea, and unusually hot beverages are known to cause throat cancer.
Iranians almost always inhale tobacco smoke from cigarettes or pipes while drinking their Persian black tea.
Iranians consume a diet particularly high in meat, fish, eggs, and dairy, each of which is known to be a primary cause of many types of cancer, including throat cancer.
Most Iranians who consume Persian black tea do so after meals, during which they also consume alcohol, which has been linked to throat cancer.
The Iranians with the healthiest body weights tend to have the highest yearly consumptions of Persian Black tea had by any Iranians.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: People living in Iran have one of the highest rates of throat cancer in the world.
Premise: Iranians typically drink considerably more Persian black tea per year than is drunk by people in other countries.
Conclusion: People would be well advised to eliminate Persian black tea from their diets to reduce their risk of developing throat cancer.

The recommendation that people eliminate Persian black tea from their diets is based on a perceived cause-and-effect link between drinking Persian black tea and the development of throat cancer.Any time a conclusion involves a cause-and-effect link between variables, we should be skeptical as to whether that link actually exists.

The fact that people living in Iran have one of the highest rates of throat cancer in the world and the fact that they also drink more Persian black tea than anyone else do not necessarily mean that the tea is causing the throat cancer.There certainly could be other factors, such as diet, stress, or environmental pollution that could be the real cause of the throat cancer.

Because this is a weaken the argument “EXCEPT” question, our job is to eliminate four choices that do weaken the argument.These four choices will be incorrect choices.Then, the one that remains, the one that DOES NOT weaken the argument, will be the correct answer.
(A) Iranians drink their Persian black tea at temperatures much higher than those at which other people drink tea, and unusually hot beverages are known to cause throat cancer.
What this choice says weakens the argument.

If it’s true that Iranians drink their Persian black tea at temperatures much higher than those at which other people drink tea, and unusually hot beverages are known to cause throat cancer, then we have a plausible alternative cause of the throat cancer.

In other words, we are now less sure that Persian black tea is the actual cause of the throat cancer, because the cause could instead be the unusually high temperatures at which Iranian’s consume their tea.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
(B) Iranians almost always inhale tobacco smoke from cigarettes or pipes while drinking their Persian black tea.
What this choice says weakens the argument.
If it’s true that Iranians almost always inhale tobacco smoke from cigarettes or pipes while drinking their Persian black tea, then we have a plausible alternative cause of the throat cancer.
In other words, we are now less sure that Persian black tea is the actual cause of the throat cancer, because the cause could instead be the tobacco smoke, which is a known carcinogen.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
(C) Iranians consume a diet particularly high in meat, fish, eggs, and dairy, each of which is known to be a primary cause of many types of cancer, including throat cancer.
What this choice says weakens the argument.

If it’s true that Iranians consume a diet particularly high in meat, fish, eggs, and dairy, each of which is known to be a primary cause of many types of cancer, including throat cancer, then we have a plausible alternative cause of the throat cancer.

In other words, we are now less sure that Persian black tea is the actual cause of the throat cancer, because it could instead be caused by the Iranian’s consumption of a diet particularly high in meat, fish, eggs, and dairy, each of which is known to be a primary cause of many types of cancer, including throat cancer.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
(D) Most Iranians who consume Persian black tea do so after meals, during which they also consume alcohol, which has been linked to throat cancer.
What this choice says weakens the argument.

If it’s true that most Iranians who consume Persian black tea do so after meals, during which they also consume alcohol, which has been linked to throat cancer, then we have a plausible alternative cause of the throat cancer.
In other words, we are now less sure that Persian black tea is the actual cause of the throat cancer, which could be instead caused by the alcohol that most Iranians consume.
Weakens the argument? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The Iranians with the healthiest body weights tend to have the highest yearly consumptions of Persian Black tea had by any Iranians.
This choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.

This choice tells us about a correlation between Iranian’s having healthy body weights and their having relatively high yearly consumptions of Persian black tea.Notice, however, that what this choice says is not clearly connected to the argument about Persian black tea and throat cancer.

The argument is based on the facts that Iraniansin generaldrink more Persian black tea than other people, and that Iraniansin generalhave a higher rate of throat cancer than other people have.However, the argument does not involve any segment related statistics or information, such as information on the rates of cancer in Iranians with healthy body weights.
So, the information in this choice is basically useless to us.

Now, if we had the information that Iranian people with healthy body weights have the lowest rate of throat cancer, then the information provided by this choice, that people with healthy body weights have the highest yearly consumptions of Persian black tea would be pretty interesting.In that case, the people who drink the most Persian black tea would have the lowest rate of throat cancer, a fact that would certainly serve to call into question the conclusion that drinking Persian black tea causes throat cancer.
However, we don’t have that other information on Iranian people with healthy body weights.
Since the information provided by this choice has no clear connection to the information used to support the recommendation made in the passage, this choice has no effect on the argument.
Weakens the argument? No.

question.text: Of the participants in a study, the survival rate over a certain period of time of those who typically consumed spicy foods on six or seven days of every week was significantly greater than the survival rate over the same period of time of those who typically consumed spicy food on less than one day of every week.These results clearly indicate that, in general, by adding spices to their food more consistently, people who currently do not often add spices to their food can extend their lives.In arriving at the conclusion above, the author is relying on which of the following as an assumption?

answers.text: Consuming spicy foods is less likely to cause negative side effects than is taking prescription medications.
The ingredients that, when added to foods, make food taste spicy do not exacerbate any ongoing health issues when consumed six or seven days a week.
It is not the case that, when not prepared with spices, foods usually prepared with spices are healthier to eat than foods usually prepared without spices.
There is no spice free diet such that people following it would live longer on average than currently do people who consume spicy food six or seven days a week.
Reports of people’s having seriously hurt themselves via consuming extremely spicy foods are untrue or at least significantly exaggerated.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Of the participants in a study, the survival rate over a certain period of time of those who typically consumed spicy foods on six or seven days of every week was significantly greater than the survival rate over the same period of time of those who typically consumed spicy food on less than one day of every week.
Conclusion: These results clearly indicate that, in general, by adding spices to their food more consistently, people who currently do not often add spices to their food can extend their lives.

As you know well, we must always remember that the fact that two variables are seen together (for example, eating spicy foods on six or seven days of every week and significantly greater survival rates) does not mean that one of the variables (eating spicy foods on six or seven days of every week) causes the other variable (significantly greater survival rates). After all, there could be many other plausible explanations for the observation described in the passage.
However, because the author of this argument is making a cause-and-effect claim, the author is assuming that there is no other explanation for the phenomenon observed.
(A) Consuming spicy foods is less likely to cause negative side effects than is taking prescription medications.

This is not an argument about side effects.This is an argument about survival rates.Therefore, what the author proposes could be true regardless of the relative rates of side effects of consuming spicy foods and taking prescription medicines.
If you have a hard time seeing this, we can negate this statement:
Consuming spicy foods is NOT less likely to cause negative side effects than is taking prescription medications.

Even if that negated version of this choice is true, argument remains perfectly valid.After all, the conclusion is not that eating spicy foods will have fewer side effects than taking prescription medicines.

Also, regardless of what side effects eating spicy food may have, the fact remains that people who ate spicy foods on six or seven days of every week had a survival rate higher than that had by people who didn’t eat spicy foods as often.
So, the author need not assume what this choice says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The ingredients that, when added to foods, make food taste spicy do not exacerbate any ongoing health issues when consumed six or seven days a week.
What this answer choice says is not an assumption upon which the argument relies.

After all, the author never makes the claim that eating spicy foods six or seven days a week will not exacerbate any ongoing health issues.Rather, her conclusion is that by adding spices to their food more consistently, people who currently do not often add spices to their food can extend their lives.

It could very well be true that the ingredients that when added to foods make food taste spicy taste do exacerbate some ongoing health issues when consumed six or seven days a week, but be that as it may, by adding spices to their food more consistently, people who currently do not often add spices to their food can extend their lives.For example, maybe spicy foods sometimes exacerbate acid reflux, but at the same time, maybe they would cut a person’s risk of developing heart disease by 90 percent.

Meanwhile, regardless of whether spicy ingredients exacerbate any health issues, people who ate spicy foods on six or seven days of everydidhave a survival rate higher than that had by people who ate spicy foods less often.
Regardless of whether what this choice says is assumed, the evidence provided supports the author’s conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) It is not the case that, when not prepared with spices, foods usually prepared with spices are healthier to eat than foods usually prepared without spices.
Imagine if it were true that it IS the case that, when not prepared with spices, foods usually prepared with spices are healthier to eat than foods usually prepared without spices.

In that case, the argument’s conclusion would not be well supported by the evidence provided, because there would be a clear reason to believe that there is an alternative explanation for why the survival rate over a certain period of time of those who typically consumed spicy foods on six or seven days of every week was significantly greater than the survival rate over the same period of time of those who typically consumed spicy food on less than one day of every week.

If what this choice says were NOT true, it would be quite likely that the foods themselves, not the spices, are responsible for the higher survival rate, in which case, drawing the author’s conclusion from the evidence provided would not make sense.

Remember, in making a cause-and-effect claim, the author assumes that there is no other explanation for the observed effect.Therefore, for this argument to work, the author must assume that it is not the case that, when not prepared with spices, foods usually prepared with spices are healthier to eat than foods usually prepared without spices.

To illustrate this point, let’s consider a spicy dish that includes many nutritious vegetables that have health supporting properties.If someone who eats this dish often were especially healthy, would it make sense to conclude that the spices were causing that person to be especially healthy.No, because it could very well be that it is the vegetables, not the spices, that are causing the person to be especially healthy.
(D) There is no spice free diet such that people following it would live longer on average than currently do people who consume spicy food six or seven days a week.
We must always be on the lookout for the “better plan/different plan” trap answer.

Let’s negate what this answer choice says to arrive at: There IS a spice free diet such that people following it would live longer on average than currently do people who consume spicy food six or seven days a week.Notice that this negated version of this choice tells us that there is a better plan than consuming spicy food six or seven days a week.

However, this fact would not affect the strength of the argument at all, because the author is not claiming that there is not a better plan out there.Rather, she’s claiming that, by adding spices to their food more consistently, people who currently do not often add spices to their food can extend their lives, and the author’s plan could work just fine even if a better plan exists.
Remember that when an author proposes a plan, she need not assume that there is not another better plan out there.
(E) Reports of people’s having seriously hurt themselves via consuming extremely spicy foods are untrue or at least significantly exaggerated.
We need not assume that reports of people’s having seriously hurt themselves via consuming extremely spicy foods are untrue or at least significantly exaggerated.
Consuming extremely spicy foods is not the same as adding some spices to food.

Furthermore, regardless of whether some people have hurt themselves via consuming extremely spicy foods, the fact remains that people who ate spicy foods on six or seven days of every week had a survival rate higher than that had by people who ate spicy foods less often.

So, even if it WERE TRUE that people some people who have consumed extremely spicy foods have injured themselves via doing so, it would be reasonable to conclude from the evidence provided that, by adding spices to their food more consistently, people who currently do not often add spices to their food can extend their lives.
Therefore, what this choice says is not an assumption necessary for arriving at the argument’s conclusion.

question.text: Last year, in Country X, smaller private jets were involved in a greater number of accidents than larger commercial jets were involved in.It is well known that larger commercial jets are flown by flight crews more experienced on average than the flight crews on smaller private jets.Clearly, it is the greater on average experience of the flight crews in larger commercial jets that is responsible for this difference in accident statistics.In evaluating the argument, it would be useful to answer each of the following EXCEPT:

answers.text: Of all the flights made in Country X last year, what proportion of those flights were made by smaller private jets?
Do larger commercial jets have flight safety features more advanced than those often found on smaller private jets?
Do flight crews on smaller private jets receive the same amount of formal classroom training as is received by the flight crews on larger commercial jets?
What proportion of flights made by smaller private jets in Country X last year were piloted by pilots who have experience flying larger commercial jets?
Do flight crews flying smaller private jets typically have more tiring flight schedules than are had by flight crews flying larger commercial jets?

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Last year in Country X, smaller private jets were involved in a greater number of accidents than larger commercial jets were involved in.
Premise: It is well known that larger commercial jets are flown by flight crews more experienced on average than the flight crews on smaller private jets.
Conclusion: Clearly, it is the greater on average experience of the flight crews in larger commercial jets that is responsible for this difference in accident statistics.

Notice that the author is making a cause-and-effect claim about flight crew experience and numbers of accidents.Whenever an author claims that there is a cause-and-effect link between variables, we should be skeptical regarding whether a cause-and-effect link actually exists.

Are the more experienced flight crews really the cause of the lower number of accidents involving larger commercial jets? Might the author be missing something? What else might we want to know to better evaluate the efficacy of this argument?

Because this is an Evaluate EXCEPT question, we will see and eliminate four choices that pose questions the answers to which would be useful in the evaluation of the argument; these will be incorrect answer choices.
Then, the choice that remains, the one that does not that pose a question the answer to which would be useful in the evaluation of the argument, will be the correct answer.
(A) Of all the flights made in Country X last year, what proportion of those flights were made by smaller private jets?
For evaluating the argument, it would be useful to know what proportion of all flights made in Country X last year were made by smaller private jets.

After all, if it were true that smaller private jets were responsible for 9/10 of the flights, then we would expect to see a greater number of accidents in these smaller private jets than in larger commercial jets simply because, as the number of flights made goes up, so too does the number of accidents.

In that case, the argument would be weakened, because we’d have reason to believe that the greater experience of the flight crews flying large commercial jets is not the reason that these large commercial jets have experienced fewer accidents.In that case, the real reason is likely that the smaller private jets are making more flights, and so, of course, the smaller private jets will have more accidents than the larger commercial jets.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is, for example, 1/10, then the argument is strengthened because, in this case, smaller private jets make up just 1/10 of all flights yet are responsible for a greater number of accidents than the large commercial jets are responsible for.
Useful? Yes.
(B) Do larger commercial jets have flight safety features more advanced than those often found on smaller private jets?
The answer to whether larger commercial jets have flight safety features more advanced than those often found on smaller private jets would be helpful in evaluating the argument.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the argument is weakened, because in this situation, we have reason to believe that the better safety record of the larger commercial jets is not caused by the greater experience of the flights crews but rather is the result of larger commercial jets’ having more advanced safety features than are had by smaller private jets.

Now, on the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is stronger because we’re able to rule out an alternative explanation for why the larger commercial have fewer accidents than the smaller private jets.
Useful? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Do flight crews on smaller private jets receive the same amount of formal classroom training as is received by the flight crews on larger commercial jets?

The answer to this question would be useful in the evaluation of the argument, because it could provide an alternative explanation or serve to rule out an alternative explanation for the observed pattern of events.

If the answer is “No, flight crews on smaller private jets DO NOT receive the same amount of formal classroom training as is received the flight crews on larger commercial jets,” then the answer would provide an alternative explanation for why private jets were involved in more accidents than commercial jets were involved in.In this case, rather than a difference in experience, a difference in amounts of classroom training might explain the difference in numbers of accidents.

If the answer is “Yes, flight crews on smaller private jets DO receive the same amount of formal classroom training as is received the flight crews on larger commercial jets,” then the answer would serve to rule out an alternative explanation.In that case we could rule out the alternative explanation that small private jets were involved in more accidents than commercial jets were involved in because the flight crews of small private jets get less classroom training than commercial flight crews get.
Useful? Yes.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) What proportion of flights made by smaller private jets in Country X last year were piloted by pilots who have experience flying larger commercial jets?

This answer to the question posed by this choice could seem to be very helpful in the evaluation of the argument, because we could mistakenly decide that the answer indicates something new about the flight experience of the pilots of small private jets.

The passage says that larger commercial jets are flown by flight crews more experienced on average than the flight crews on smaller private jets.So, we could get the impression that, if the answer to the question posed by this choice is a high proportion, then most private jets are being flown by pilots with as much experience as pilots of commercial jets have, a fact that could seem to weaken the argument by indicating that there isn’t actually much difference in the amounts of experience had by the flight crews of the two different kinds of jets.
Here's the key thing to notice, though.

The passage states AS FACT that commercial jets are flown by flight crews more experienced on average than the flight crews on smaller private jets.So, regardless of the proportion of private jet pilots who have flown commercial jets, the crews of small private jets for sure have, on average, less experience than the flight crews of commercial jets pilots have.

So, the truth is that, no matter what the answer to the question posed by this choice is, it will remain the case that commercial jets are flown by flight crews more experienced on average than the flight crews on smaller private jets.
So, this choice is the correct answer to this Evaluate EXCEPT question, because the answer to the question posed by this choice will have no effect on the argument no matter what it is.
Useful? No.
(E) Do flight crews flying smaller private jets typically have more tiring flight schedules than are had by flight crews flying larger commercial jets?

The answer to the question of whether flight crews flying smaller private jets typically have more tiring flight schedules than are had by flight crews flying larger commercial jets would be important in evaluating the argument.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the argument is weakened because we now have an alternative explanation for why the smaller private jets are involved in a greater number of accidents.After all, tired pilots are less safe pilots.

However, if the answer to the question is “No,” then the argument is stronger because we’ve ruled out the alternative explanation that the difference in the numbers of accidents is the result of a difference in how tiring the flight schedules are.
Useful? Yes.

question.text: As Ludwig van Beethoven began to go deaf, his hearing became such that he could hear lower notes better than he could hear higher notes.Interestingly, at that same time, in composing music, he chose to use notes that were lower on average than the notes he had used before his hearing began to deteriorate.It seems likely that the reason for the change in his choices of notes was that he was gravitating toward using the notes that he could hear better given the state of his hearing.Which of the following, if true, adds the most support to the hypothesis articulated above?

answers.text: By holding a pencil in his mouth and touching it to the piano, Beethoven could feel the vibrations of low notes that he was playing as he composed.
Because he was concerned that his musical career could be damaged if people were aware that he was losing his hearing, Beethoven did his best to keep the deterioration secret.

When, after becoming almost completely deaf, Beethoven was forced to compose without hearing his compositions, he used notes that were, on average, as high as those he had used when his hearing was normal.
One of the first signs that Beethoven’s hearing was deteriorating was buzzing in his ears that interfered with his hearing relatively high notes clearly.
Beethoven was able to compose without hearing what he was composing because he had played and composed music for many years before his hearing began to deteriorate.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise (the cause): As Ludwig van Beethoven began to go deaf, his hearing became such that he could hear lower notes better than he could hear higher notes.
Premise (the effect): Interestingly, at that same time, in composing music, he chose to use notes that were lower on average than the notes he had used before his hearing began to deteriorate.
Conclusion: It seems likely that the reason for the change in his choices of notes was that he was gravitating toward using the notes that he could hear better given the state of his hearing.
In this case, the author simply mentions two developments that occurred at the same time and hypothesizes that one caused the other.

Of course, we know that the fact that two developments went on at the same time does not necessarily mean that one caused the other.There may not have been a cause-and-effect relationship between the two developments.

A key aspect of correctly answering this question is noticing what the supposed cause is exactly.In fact, this question is quite challenging because the supposed cause is a bit different from other causes that we have seen in most cause-and-effect scenarios.So, let’s identify the supposed cause.

The supposed cause is thedifferencebetween Beethoven’s ability to hear low notes and his ability to hear high notes.In other words, the author has hypothesized that the reason why Beethoven changed his choices of notes was that a difference between his ability to hear high notes and his ability to hear low notes had developed.
So, to correctly answer this question, we have to find a choice that provides some confirmation that that difference was indeed the cause of the change in Beethoven’s choices of notes.
How could a choice confirm that a difference was the cause of something?
(A) By holding a pencil in his mouth and touching it to the piano, Beethoven could feel the vibrations of low notes that he was playing as he composed.

This choice is a tempting trap, because this choice could seem to confirm what the passage says about Beethoven’s ability to hear low notes.We might think something along the lines of, “Ahah.Not only could he hear low notes better with his ears but also, he could feel the low notes by using a pencil.Of course, these facts would explain why he gravitated to using lower notes.”

Notice, however, that we don’t have to confirm that Beethoven could hear, or feel, low notes better than he could hear high notes.The passage has already stated as fact that, as Ludwig van Beethoven began to go deaf, his hearing became such that he could hear lower notes better than he could hear higher notes.What this choice says does not make that fact any truer, and therefore does not strengthen the support for the hypothesis.
Remember, the correct answer to a Strengthen question won’t be one that seems to confirm a premise stated in the passage, because the premises are already taken to be true.
(B) Because he was concerned that his musical career could be damaged if people were aware that he was losing his hearing, Beethoven did his best to keep the deterioration secret.

We have to be careful not to be tempted into making this choice into a strengthener by making up an unsupported story involving what this choice says, because the truth is that the effect that this choice has on the argument is not clear.
Would the fact that Beethoven did his best to keep the deterioration secret strengthen the support for the hypothesis? Perhaps it would seem to, if were to make up a convoluted story.

However, we could also make up a story that makes this choice into a weakener.For instance, we could make up the story that the there must have been some reason other than the hearing issues for Beethoven’s choosing lower notes, because his choosing lower notes could have clued people in to the fact that his hearing was deteriorating.So, he must have had some other, very important, reason for choosing lower notes.
Since only by making up unsupported stories can we connect this choice to the argument, this choice is not a clear strengthener.

YOUR CHOICE(C) When, after becoming almost completely deaf, Beethoven was forced to compose without hearing his compositions, he used notes that were, on average, as high as those he had used when his hearing was normal.
We know that one way to strengthen the case for a cause-and-effect claim is to show that, when the supposed cause goes away, the effect goes away, and that’s just what this choice does.
From the passage we know that, before he began to go deaf, Beethoven chose notes of a certain average pitch.
Next, according to the passage, Beethoven could hear lower notes better than he could hear higher notes.So, a difference had developed.

The author hypothesizes that this difference is why Beethoven chose to use “notes that were lower on average than the notes he had used before his hearing began to deteriorate.” In other words, the proposed cause for Beethoven’s gravitating toward using lower notes was a difference between his ability to hear high notes and his ability to hear low notes.
Now what does this choice tell us?
It tells us that, when Beethoven became almost completely deaf, he went back to choosing used notes that were, on average, as high as those he had used when his hearing was normal.
Now, here’s the key thing that we have to notice to understand why this choice strengthens the support for the conclusion.

We have to notice that, when Beethoven was almost completely deaf, he would not have been able to hear higher notes better than he heard lower notes.He wouldn’t have heard much of anything.Thus, when he was almost completely deaf, the difference between his ability to hear high notes and his ability to hear low notes would have ceased to exist.
So, when Beethoven was almost completely deaf, the cause (the difference) was gone.

Now, what else was gone at that point? The effect (gravitating toward using lower notes) was gone.At that point, Beethoven was back to using “notes that were, on average, as high as those he had used when his hearing was normal.”
So, this choice tell us that, when the cause was gone, the effect was too.Thus, this choice strengthens the support for the author’s cause-and-effect hypothesis.
(D) One of the first signs that Beethoven’s hearing was deteriorating was buzzing in his ears that interfered with his hearing relatively high notes clearly.

This choice is a very tempting trap, because this choice could seem to confirm what the passage says about Beethoven’s ability to hear low notes better than high notes.We might think something along the lines of, “OK.This choice is perfect.If buzzing in Beethoven’s ears interfered with his hearing relatively high notes clearly, then we have additional evidence supporting what the passage says about Beethoven hearing low notes better than he could hear high notes.”

Notice, however, that we don’t have to confirm that Beethoven could hear low notes better than he could hear high notes.The passage has already stated as fact that, as Ludwig van Beethoven began to go deaf, his hearing became such that he could hear lower notes better than he could hear higher notes.What this choice says about why he could not hear high notes does not make that fact any truer.
Remember, the correct answer to a Strengthen question won’t be one that seems to confirm a premise stated in the passage, because the premises are already taken to be true.
(E) Beethoven was able to compose without hearing what he was composing because he had played and composed music for many years before his hearing began to deteriorate.

If anything, the fact that, as this choice says, Beethoven could compose without hearing what he was composing would weaken, not strengthen the case for the author’s hypothesis that the reason why Beethoven changed his choices of notes was that he was gravitating toward using the notes that he could hear better given the state of his hearing.

The fact that Beethoven could compose without hearing what he was composing is a reason to question the validity of the author’s hypothesis because, if he could compose without hearing what he was composing, then he would not have needed to hear the notes he was using in order to compose.So, he would not have had to gravitate “toward using the notes that he could hear better” in order to compose.He could have composed just fine even if he had continued to use notes that he couldn’t hear very well.
Of course, then, if anything, the information provided by this choice is a reason to wonder whether Beethoven changed his choices of notes for a reason different from the one the author has proposed.
So, this choice tends to weaken, rather than strengthen, the case for the author’s hypothesis.

question.text: A company that manufactures and sells an expensive additive for fuel used in cars launched a massive ad campaign last week.The ads claimed that tests conducted by an independent agency found that, on average, cars whose owners use the company’s additive in their cars’ fuel accelerated faster and burned fuel more completely than did cars whose owners do not use the company’s additive.Which of the following statements, if true, would most weaken the causality that the company is seeking to build in the minds of potential customers?

answers.text: A person who cares for his or her car more and spends more on the car’s maintenance is more likely to purchase an expensive fuel additive.
The independent agency that conducted the tests tested more cars whose owners use the company’s additive than cars whose owners do not use the company’s additive.
No other company that sells additives for gasoline is currently making similar claims.
Few individuals who own cars would test their cars in the way in which the independent agency mentioned in the ads tests cars.
Car owners sometimes make judgments about the quality of a product based mostly on its price.

explanation_parts.text: Notice that, while the passage does not directly state a conclusion, we can tell that the company is seeking to build in the minds of potential customers a cause-and-effect link between the use of its expensive additive and the faster acceleration and better fuel burn of the cars in which that additive is used.

As always, we must be skeptical of-cause and-effect claims.Is the expensive additive really to blame for the faster acceleration and better fuel burn of the cars in which that additive was used? Could there be an alternative explanation?
YOUR CHOICE(A) A person who cares for his or her car more and spends more on the car’s maintenance is more likely to purchase an expensive fuel additive.

What this answer choice says casts doubt on the cause-and effect relationship that the company’s ads suggest exists by presenting an alternative explanation for the fact that cars whose owners use the company’s additive in their cars’ fuel accelerated faster and burned fuel more completely than did cars whose owners do not use the company’s additive.

That alternative explanation, is, of course, that, if it is true that a person who cares for his or her car more and spends more on the car’s maintenance is more likely to purchase an expensive fuel additive, then it just might be that the real cause of the faster acceleration and more complete fuel burn of the cars in which that additive is used is that they are simply better cared for.

In other words, imagine the people who would be willing to purchase expensive fuel additives for their cars.According to what this answer choice says, they are the ones washing and waxing their cars all weekend long and ensuring that even the smallest items of maintenance are conducted on the cars.These cars are like babies to these owners.Of course, all that love and care would almost certainly result in these cars’ accelerating faster and burning fuel more completely than other, less-well-cared-for, cars.Right?

This choice provides a reason to question the suggestion that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the use of the additive and the better performance of the cars whose owners use the additive.
(B) The independent agency that conducted the tests tested more cars whose owners use the company’s additive than cars whose owners do not use the company’s additive.

This choice could be tempting, because the fact that the agency tested more cars whose owners used the additive than cars whose owners did not use the additive could seem to mean that the testing process was biased somehow, and that, therefore, the test results are suspect.
However, the truth is that this choice does not weaken the argument at all.

To see why, imagine if the independent agency tested 1,000 cars whose owners use the company’s additive and 950 cars whose owners do not use the company’s additive.Certainly, those would be large samples of both cars whose owners use the company’s additive and cars whose owners do not use the company’s additive.Therefore, there would be no reason to question the results generated via the use of those samples even though the size of one sample is somewhat larger than the size of the other.
Be extra careful to make sure that a statement that seems to weaken an argument is not just a pseudo-weakener that doesn’t make any difference all in the strength of the argument.
(C) No other company that sells additives for gasoline is currently making similar claims.

What this answer choice says, that, no other company that sells additives for gasoline is currently making similar claims, does not affect the strength of this argument.After all, we’re concerned with the company that is making the claim, not with companies that are not making the claim.The fact that no other company is making the claim does not make the claim any less valid.

It could be that the company making the claim is far ahead of other companies in terms of research and development and technology.After all, Henry Ford did create an automobile during a time in which his competitors were outfitting horses …
(D) Few individuals who own cars would test their cars in the way in which the independent agency mentioned in the ads tests cars.

The fact that few individuals who own cars would test their cars in the way in which the independent agency mentioned in the ad tests cars does not indicate that the company’s claim is incorrect.Regardless of what this answer choice says, the company’s argument could be valid because the conclusion rests upon the test results, not on consumers’ doing the same type of testing and getting similar results.In other words, the argument rests on actual data.It does not rest on what consumers may or may not do.
(E) Car owners sometimes make judgments about the quality of a product based mostly on its price.

This choice weakens support for the wrong conclusion.This choice provides reason to question the conclusion that consumers will be influenced by the company’s ads, by providing reason to believe that consumers making judgments about the product might be influenced by product pricing rather than by the ads.

However, what this answer choice says does not change the facts provided and does not provide a reason to question the conclusion that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between use of the additive and better car performance.
The ads suggest that the additive has certain effects.The additive’s actually having those affects does not depend on consumers’ perceptions of the additive.

question.text: Over the past several decades, corporate downsizing achieved through reductions in numbers of employees has become increasingly popular.However, while the ostensible goal of such reductions is increased profitability, companies that employ such reductions are, over the five years following the reductions, less profitable on average than companies that have not chosen to reduce their numbers of workers.Clearly, corporate downsizing has a negative effect rather than a positive effect on profitability.Which of the following, if true, is most damaging to the above conclusion?

answers.text: Companies in expanding industries are less likely than are other companies to seek to use employee headcount reductions to achieve increased profitability.
After many employees are laid off from a company, the company is likely to experience serious issues in terms of both morale and effectiveness of the remaining employees.
Often, when companies lay off workers, they do so on the advice of management consultants who were hired to help the companies become more profitable.
In the process of downsizing, some companies lay off large percentages of employees and others small percentages.
Whether a company is profitable does not depend solely on whether it has the optimal number of employees.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Over the past several decades, corporate downsizing achieved through reductions in numbers of employees has become increasing popular.
Background: The ostensible goal of such reductions is increased profitability.
Premise: Companies that employ such reductions are, over the five years following the reductions, less profitable on average than companies that have not chosen to reduce their numbers of workers.
Conclusion: Corporate downsizing has a negative effect rather than a positive effect on profitability.

The reasoning of the argument involves using the fact that there is a repeating pattern – downsizing is followed by relatively low profitability – as evidence of a cause-and-effect relationship – downsizing negatively affects profitability.
Of course, the reason for the correlation between downsizing and relatively low profitability is not necessarily that the first causes the second.
This argument can be weakened by a choice that indicates that there is another reason why downsizing tends to be followed by relatively low profitability.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Companies in expanding industries are less likely than are other companies to seek to use employee headcount reductions to achieve increased profitability.

Notice that what this answer choice says, that companies in expanding industries are less likely than are other companies to seek to use employee headcount reductions to achieve increased profitability, indicates that maybe corporate downsizing DOES NOT have a negative effect rather than a positive effect on profitability after all.

This choice indicates that it could be that the real reason why companies that employ headcount reductions are less profitable on average than companies that don’t is that companies in expanding industries, in other words, companies expected to be relatively highly profitable, don’t employ headcount reductions, while companies in stagnant or declining industries do.

In other words, what this answer choice indicates is that perhaps the cause-and-effect nature of the relationship is actually the reverse of what the author says it is.Maybe what’s really going on is that the companies that have already have relatively low expectations in terms of future profitability are the ones that employ headcount reductions.

So, of course the companies that employ the headcount reductions are less profitable than the ones that don’t, but not because of the headcount reductions but, rather, because the ones that do are not in expanding industries.
We can weaken a cause-and-effect argument by showing that perhaps the author has reversed the cause and effect.
YOUR CHOICE(B) After many employees are laid off from a company, the company is likely to experience serious issues in terms of both morale and effectiveness of the remaining employees.
This choice is tempting because is clearly pertinent to what’s discussed in the passage.

However, this choice has entirely the wrong effect.It does not weaken the argument.Rather it explains how headcount reductions could result in a decrease in profitability: they are detrimental to morale and effectiveness of the remaining employees.
Of course explain how headcount reductions would cause decreases in profitability would not weaken the support for the conclusion that they cause decreases in profitability.
(C) Often, when companies lay off workers, they do so on the advice of management consultants who were hired to help the companies become more profitable.

This choice could be tempting, because we could get the impression that the fact that consultants hired to help companies become more profitable recommend headcount reductions indicates that headcount reductions must cause profitability, not cause reductions in profitability.
However, the truth is that, people, even professionals such as management consultants, often recommend actions that don’t work.

Furthermore, we don’t know what “often” means.Maybe “often” means 20 percent of the time that reductions are employed.So, it could be that consultants recommend headcount reductions on 20 percent of the occasions on which reductions are employed, and that, on the other 80 percent of the occasions on which headcounts are employed, consultants have not recommended headcount reductions but headcount reductions are used anyway, and end up causing decreases in profitability.
So, this choice is not a convincing weakener.
(D) In the process of downsizing, some companies lay off large percentages of employees and others small percentages.

This choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.The fact that companies don’t all lay off the same percentages of workers does not mean that laying off workers is not detrimental to profitability.
(E) Whether a company is profitable does not depend solely on whether it has the optimal number of employees.

This choice could be tempting because it could make downsizing seem unimportant.We could think something along the lines of, “If, as this choice says, whether a company is profitable does not depend solely on whether it has the optimal number of employees, then maybe changing the number of employees by downsizing doesn’t matter much.So, this choice indicates that downsizing doesn’t really affect profitability and, thus, weakens the argument.”
However, that thinking is flawed.

The simplest way to eliminate this choice is to see that this argument is not abouthaving the optimal number of employees.This argument is about the effects of headcount reductions, i.e.,reducing the number of employees.So, this choice and the argument are about two different things, and, as a result, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.

question.text: In a study undertaken in a certain country, office workers who sat at a desk for six hours or more each day had lifespans shorter than that of the average person in that country.Given these results, the researchers who had conducted the study concluded that sitting at a desk for six hours or more each day is sufficient to cause premature death.Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the support for the researchers’ conclusion?

answers.text: The office workers who participated in the study were not provided access to sit-stand work desks so that they could alternate their time between sitting and standing.
The average workday of the office workers who participated in the study was longer than the average workday of people in the country where the study was undertaken.
The office workers who participated in the study had better access to quality healthcare than was had by most people in the country where the study was undertaken.
In the country where the study was undertaken, office workers who have jobs at which they must stand for most of the day live to an average age of 82 years old.
At the inception of the study, the average age of the office workers who participated was close to the average age of all people living in the country where the study was undertaken.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Premise: In a study undertaken in a certain country, office workers who sat at a desk for six hours or more each day had lifespans shorter than that of the average person in that country.
Conclusion: The researchers who had conducted the study concluded that sitting at a desk for six hours or more each day is sufficient to cause premature death.
Notice that the researchers have made a cause-and-effect claim.In their opinion, sitting at a desk for six hours or more each day is sufficient to cause premature death.
In answering this question, our job is to strengthen the support for that cause-and-effect claim.
(A) The office workers who participated in the study were not provided access to sit-stand work desks so that they could alternate their time between sitting and standing.
We know that, in the study, office workers who sat at a desk for six hours or more each day had lifespans shorter than that of the average person.
It’s important to see that the study was about “office workers who sat at a desk for six or hours or more each day.”

What this choice says, that “the office workers who participated in the study were not provided access to sit-stand work desks so that they could alternate their time between sitting and standing” tends to confirm that the participants were sitting, and may explain why they were sitting.

So what though? We already were clear about the fact that they were sitting.                                                So, confirmation that they were sitting or an explanation for why they were sitting makes no difference.
This choice has no effect on the strength of the argument.
(B) The average workday of the office workers who participated in the study was longer than the average workday of people in the country where the study was undertaken.

This choice presents a characteristic other than sitting at a desk for six hours or more each day that made the office workers who participated in the study different from the average person.The office workers had longer-than-average workdays.

Since it’s possible that having longer-than-average workdays could cause a person’s lifespan to be shorter than average, the fact that the workers who participated in the study had longer-than-average workdays indicates that there may be an alternative explanation for their shorter lifespans.

In other words, this choice indicates that it could have been longer-than-average workdays, rather than sitting at a desk for six or more hours per day, that resulted in the shorter lifespans of the workers who participated in the study.

By providing reason to believe that there may have been a cause other than sitting at a desk for the shorter lifespans, this choice weakens, rather than strengthens, the case for the researchers’ cause-and-effect conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The office workers who participated in the study had better access to quality healthcare than was had by most people in the country where the study was undertaken.

The fact that the office workers who participated in the study had better access to quality healthcare than was had by most people in the country where the study was undertaken strengthens the argument, because it serves to rule out the alternative explanation that the reason these people had relatively short lifespans is that they did not have access to quality healthcare.

In other words, if these people had better access to quality healthcare, and yet they still had relatively short lifespans, then we have even more conviction that it was in fact sitting at a desk for six hours or more each day that caused their premature deaths.
Remember that, when we are dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, any time we can rule out an alternative explanation, we have strengthened the argument.
(D) In the country where the study was undertaken, office workers who have jobs at which they must stand for most of the day live to an average age of 82 years old.

The passage tells us that, in a study undertaken in a certain country, office workers who sat at a desk for six hours or more each day had lifespans shorter than that of the average person in that country.
Notice that the above premise involves relative longevity; it doesn’t provide an actual numerical lifespan, such as 90 years, or 78 years.

Because we don’t have any information on the exact average lifespan of people who sat at desks for six or more hours per day, the fact that, in the country where the study was undertaken, office workers who have jobs at which they must stand for most of the day live to an average age of 82 years old is useless to us.After all, we don’t have anything to compare with 82 years.
What was the average lifespan of the workers who sat at a desk for six hours or more each day? We don’t know, right? It could have been 81 years, and it could have been 100 years.

82 years may seem to be a pretty long lifespan, but since we don’t know what the average lifespan of a person who sits at a desk for six or more hours was, we have no reason to believe that this choice strengthens the support for the researchers’ conclusion.
(E) At the inception of the study, the average age of the office workers who participated was close to the average age of all people living in the country where the study was undertaken.

This choice is tempting because it mentions the average age of the office workers at the inception of the study, and that average age could seem to be related to their lifespans, which are the pieces of evidence the researchers used to support their conclusion.

However, the truth is that what this choice says makes no difference at all to the strength of the argument, because the age of the office workers at the inception of the study does not affect their lifespans.Their lifespans were how long they lived, not how long they lived after the study began.

So, regardless of whether the participants were relatively young, of average age, or relatively old at the inception of the study, their lifespans were what their lifespans were, and it was found that those who sat at desks for six or more hours per day had shorter-than-average lifespans.
Since the evidence would support the conclusion regardless of how old the participants were at the inception of the study, this choice does not affect the strength of the argument at all.

question.text: In the country of Westland, the number of cases of measles documented by the health department each year has steadily increased over the past ten years.Westland health officials have pointed out that, over the same time period, the number of people receiving vaccinations for measles has steadily declined.This decline in the number of people receiving measles vaccinations is clearly causing an increase in transmission of measles.In order to evaluate the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether some people who have been vaccinated for measles nevertheless experience measles.
Whether, over the past ten years, the reporting of cases of infectious diseases to the Westland health department has become more common.
Whether, over the past ten years, the number of citizens of Westland seeking exemptions from vaccination requirements has increased steadily.
Whether, over the past ten years, the people who have emigrated from Westland to another country have included many of those vaccinated for measles.
Whether there has been a growing shortage of measles vaccines in Westland over the past ten years.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: In the country of Westland, the number of cases of measles documented by the health department each year has steadily increased over the past ten years.
Premise: Westland health officials point out that, over the same time period, the number of people receiving vaccinations for measles has steadily declined.
Conclusion: This decline in the number of people receiving measles vaccinations is clearly causing an increase in transmission of measles.

Notice that the argument involves cause-and-effect reasoning.When we see a claim that there is a cause-and-effect link between variables, we should be skeptical regarding whether a cause-and-effect link actually exists.The fact that two variables have moved in the same direction at the same time is not clear evidence that the change in one variable caused the change in the second variable.
In this case, is the author’s cause-and-effect claim sound? Might he be missing something important? What else might we want to know in order to properly evaluate his claim?
(A) Whether some people who have been vaccinated for measles nevertheless experience measles.
It would not be helpful to determine whether some people who have been vaccinated for measles nevertheless experience measles.

Notice that the argument’s conclusion is essentially that the effect (the increase in documented cases of measles) is the result of the cause (the decline in the number of people receiving measles vaccinations).
That conclusion would be valid if it were the case that, yes, some people who have been vaccinated for measles nevertheless experience measles, or, no, such people don’t experience measles.
Why?

Because, if it is the case that some people who have been vaccinated for measles nevertheless experience measles, that fact would have been true ALL ALONG.So, that fact would not explain the increase in documented cases of measles, because that fact would have been just as true when the number of documented cases was lower, ten years ago, as it is now.

So, if people’s experiencing measles even though they have been vaccinated would affect how many cases of measles there are in Westland, it would have had that effect ten years ago, not just later, when the number of cases increased.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Whether, over the past ten years, the reporting of cases of infectious diseases to the Westland health department has become more common.
The answer to this question would certainly be helpful in evaluating the validity of the author’s conclusion.Since this question can be answered “Yes” or “No,” let’s apply the Yes/No test.

If we answer the question, “Yes,” then the reporting of cases of infectious diseases to the Westland health department has become more common.In that case, we have a possible alternative explanation for the increase in cases of measles documented by the health department: the actual number of cases has not been increasing; what’s actually going on is that people have been reporting a greater and greater percentage of the cases, with the result that the number of cases documented has increased.

Of course, then, since this “Yes” answer supports an alternative explanation for why there has been an increase in the number of cases of measles documented by the health department, this “Yes” answer weakens the support for the author’s conclusion that the increase in the number of cases documented is evidence that the decline in the number of people receiving measles vaccinations is causing an increase in transmission of measles.

If, on the other hand, we answer the question, “No,” then we rule out the alternative explanation outlined above, strengthening the case for concluding that the decline in the number of people receiving measles vaccinations is causing an increase in transmission of measles.

Since the “Yes” and “No” answers weaken and strengthen the argument, having the answer to the question posed by this choice would help us to evaluate the validity of the author’s cause-and-effect conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether, over the past ten years, the number of citizens of Westland seeking exemptions from vaccination requirements has increased steadily.
This choice is tempting, because an increase in the number of citizens’ seeking exemptions from vaccination requirements seems closely related to the decline in the number of measles vaccinations.
However, the answer to the question posed by this choice would have the wrong effect.It would not weaken or strengthen the case for the author’s conclusion.
A “Yes” answer would providea possible explanationfor decline in the number of people receiving vaccinations: people are seeking exemptions and are not getting vaccinated.
The information that the decline has been occurring is not made somehow more convincing by information onwhythat decline has been occurring.
So, an explanation for why the decline has been occurring does not affect the case for the author’s conclusion.
(D) Whether, over the past ten years, the people who have emigrated from Westland to another country have included many of those vaccinated for measles.

Since “many” could be 1,000, 10,000 or one million, all a “Yes” or “No” answer to this question would tell us is whether some people who have been vaccinated have left Westland along with other people who have left Westland.

The fact that people leave Westland and that some who leave have been vaccinated would not change the scenario materially and, thus, neither a “Yes” answer nor a “No” answer to this question helps us to evaluate the validity of the author’s conclusion.
(E) Whether there has been a growing shortage of measles vaccines in Westland over the past ten years.
This choice is tempting, because “a growing shortage of measles vaccines” in Westland seems closely related to the decline in the number of measles vaccinations.
However, the answer to the question posed by this choice would have the wrong effect.It would not weaken or strengthen the case for the author’s conclusion.

A “Yes” answer would providea possible explanationfor decline in the number of people receiving vaccinations: the number of people receiving vaccinations has declined because of a shortage of vaccines.
The information that the decline has been occurring is not made somehow more convincing by information onwhythat decline has been occurring.
So, an explanation for why the decline has been occurring does not affect the case for the author’s conclusion.

question.text: Young children of parents diagnosed with issues related to attention are more likely to be themselves diagnosed with attention issues than are young children whose parents have not been diagnosed with such issues.While many have proposed that attention issues are caused by household environment and diet related factors, this parent-child pattern clearly indicates that attention issues are traits that are passed on from parents to children via genetics.Which of the following, if true, most undermines the above conclusion?

answers.text: Most young children live with one or both of their parents and share their parents’ eating habits.
Research has not identified a clear causal connection between indoor pollution and diagnosis with attention issues.
Many parents of children diagnosed with attention issues were diagnosed with attention issues as adults rather than as children.
The percentage of children diagnosed with attention issues has been increasing even as the presence of some household environmental hazards, such as lead, has been decreasing.
There is among experts much disagreement regarding which methods for solving attention issues are most effective.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many have proposed that attention issues are caused by household environment and diet related factors.
Premise: Children of parents diagnosed with attention issues are more likely to be diagnosed with attention issues than are children whose parents have not been diagnosed with such issues.
Conclusion: Attention issues are passed on from parents to children via genetics.

The argument takes the fact that children of parents diagnosed with attention issues are more likely to themselves be diagnosed with attention issues than are children whose parents have not been diagnosed with such issues as indicating that attention issues are passed from parents to children via genetics.As always, we must be skeptical of cause-and-effect claims. Is genetics really to blame for the observation made? Could there be an alternative explanation? What might the author have missed?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Most young children live with one or both of their parents and share their parents’ eating habits.
What this choice says indicates that parents and their children could experience similar attention issues for reasons other than genetics.

If the parents and children diagnosed with attention issues live in the same household and share similar eating habits, then it could be that the household environment and eating habits underlie both the parents’ and the children’s attention issues.By offering a compelling explanation for the observed parent-child pattern that is different from the explanation favored by the argument, this choice weakens the argument.

If you did not choose this answer choice as the correct answer, you might have felt that this answer was “out of scope.” Remember, the phrase “out of scope” is significantly overused, and often correct answers don’t initially appear to be relevant to the passage presented.However, we need to learn to look deeper, to look beneath the surface, and to rigorously evaluate each answer choice.Do not call something “out of scope” just because you can’t see how it impacts the argument.

Also, remember that we are not seeking to disprove the argument.Rather, we’re simply seeking to cast some doubt, even a shadow of doubt, on the validity of the argument, and what this answer choice says certainly does just that.
(B) Research has not identified a clear causal connection between indoor pollution and diagnosis with attention issues.

What this answer choice says is in line with the conclusion of the argument, in that the conclusion is that genetics, rather than factors like indoor pollution, underlie attention issues, and this choice indicates that there is no known causal connection between indoor pollution and attention issues.
Therefore, what this choice says does not weaken the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Many parents of children diagnosed with attention issues were diagnosed with attention issues as adults rather than as children.
What this answer choice says could be taken as indicating that there is a difference between the parents’ attention issues and the children’s attention issues.

Notice, however, that this choice says merely that the parents’ issues are often diagnosed when the parents are adults.That the issues are diagnosed when the parents are adults does not indicate that the parents were not experiencing the issues as children.
So, what this choice says does not truly indicate any difference between the parents’ attention issues and the children’s attention issues, and therefore it does not affect the argument.
(D) The percentage of children diagnosed with attention issues has been increasing even as the presence of some household environmental hazards, such as lead, has been decreasing.

Because this choice indicates that there is a lack of correlation between levels of household environmental hazards and children’s being diagnosed with attention issues, it is in line with the assertion made by the argument that genetics rather than environmental factors underlie attention issues.Therefore, this choice does not weaken the argument.

If anything, what this answer choice says strengthens the argument somewhat, because it provides some evidence that supports ruling out an alternative cause, household environmental hazards, for the attention issues.
(E) There is among experts much disagreement regarding which methods for solving attention issues are most effective.

The fact that there is disagreement among experts regarding how to solve attention issues does not clearly indicate anything about the source of the issues, which is what the argument is about.Therefore, what this choice says does not affect the argument.

question.text: In comparison with a person who goes to college, a person who does not go to college is more likely to be unemployed for long periods of time and thus place on society financial strain that exceeds the cost of a college education.Therefore, the government could alleviate this strain and benefit taxpayers by encouraging all citizens to attend college and paying the college expenses of those who cannot afford to attend college.Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the above conclusion?

answers.text: It is currently the case that people with well-developed work related and social skills are more likely to attend college than those whose skills in those areas are less developed.
Rates of unemployment could be significantly reduced through improving government economic policy.
Often, people who have attended college are underemployed, filling jobs that otherwise may have been filled by people who did not attend college.
People who have recently immigrated to a country are often willing to take jobs that others are not interested in.
In times of economic recession, a significant proportion of college educated people may suddenly find themselves unemployed.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: In comparison with a person who goes to college, a person who does not go to college is more likely to be unemployed for long periods of time and thus place on society financial strain that exceeds the cost of a college education.

Conclusion: Therefore, the government could alleviate this strain and benefit taxpayers by encouraging all citizens to attend college and paying the college expenses of those who cannot afford to attend college.
The argument makes a cause-and-effect connection between not going to college and being unemployed for long periods and placing a financial strain on society.
