Can a college degree really explain why college graduates don’t, as often, experience these unwanted events? Might the author be missing something? Is there more to the story?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) It is currently the case that people with well-developed work related and social skills are more likely to attend college than those whose skills in those areas are less developed.

If it is true that, currently, people with well-developed work related and social skills are more likely to attend college than those whose skills in those areas are less developed, then we have an alternative explanation for why it is that, in comparison with a person who goes to college, a person who does not go to college is more likely to be unemployed for long periods of time and thus place on society financial strain that exceeds the cost of a college education.

After all, maybe it’s not going to college that makes the difference.Instead, perhaps, the true cause of the phenomenon is that people with well-developed work related and social skills are less likely to be unemployed for long periods of time, AND that people with well-developed work related and social skills are more likely to attend college.

Notice that what this answer choices says highlights that perhaps the author has the cause and effect wrong.The author claims that A is the cause of B.But what this answer choice says underscores that perhaps there is a third variable, C (in this case, well-developed work related and social skills), that is the cause of both A and B, and that here is actually no cause-and-effect link between A and B.
(B) Rates of unemployment could be significantly reduced through improving government economic policy.
That rates of unemployment could be significantly reduced through improving government economic policy does not affect the strength of this argument.

The fact that there is another way (improving government economic policy) to reduce rates of unemployment does not mean that the Government could not alleviate strain and benefit taxpayers by encouraging all citizens to attend college and paying the college expenses of those who cannot afford to attend college.

Notice that what this choice says is that there is “another plan.” As we know, we must be careful anytime a choice offers us another plan, because the fact that another plan exists does not weaken the case for a plan presented by an author.
Notice that, in the passage, the author never claims that her plan is the only plan possible.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Often, people who have attended college are underemployed, filling jobs that otherwise may have been filled by people who did not attend college.
That, often, people who have attended college are underemployed, filling jobs that otherwise may have been filled by people who did not attend college does not affect the strength of the argument.
First, being “underemployed” is still being employed.

Second, even if college graduates are underemployed, it would still be the case that in comparison with a person who goes to college, a person who does not go to college is more likely to be unemployed for long periods of time, and it would still be the case that that evidence supports the author’s conclusion.
(D) People who have recently immigrated to a country are often willing to take jobs that others are not interested in.
That people who have recently immigrated to a country are often willing to take jobs that others are not interested in does not weaken the argument.
This fact can completely coexist with the facts and the conclusion presented in the argument.

It is important that we not create unsubstantiated stories.For example, if you chose this answer as correct, you may have come up with some kind of story about recent immigrants with college degrees taking certain jobs and remaining employed only because of their circumstances.Of course, that story is neither clearly relevant to the argument nor supported by what the passage or this choice says.
(E) In times of economic recession, a significant proportion of college educated people may suddenly find themselves unemployed.
That in times of economic recession, a significant proportion of college educated people may suddenly find themselves unemployed does not weaken the argument.

What this choice says does not affect the validity of the supporting premise, which is, essentially, that people who go to college are less likely to be unemployed for long periods of time than people who don’t go to college.

So, it could very well be the case that, in times of economic recession, a significant proportion of college educated people may suddenly find themselves unemployed, and at the same time, it could also very well be the case that the government could alleviate strain and benefit taxpayers by encouraging all citizens to attend college and paying the college expenses of those who cannot afford to attend college.

If you chose this answer as correct, you likely matched words between this answer choice and the passage.What this answer choice says sounds like and certainly does seem relevant to what the passage says, but we must avoid choosing answers that simply use the same terms that the passage uses.

question.text: In seeking to determine the cause of extreme social anxiety, a condition characterized by extreme fear of being in certain types of social situations, researchers tested people experiencing such anxiety for biological markers.The researchers concluded that the cause of extreme social anxiety is an above average level of the chemical serotonin in the brain, because they found that one biological marker that consistently appeared in people who experience extreme social anxiety was brain levels of serotonin higher than the average brain level of serotonin of people who do not experience such anxiety.Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the support for the researchers’ conclusion?

answers.text: When people who have experienced extreme social anxiety are coached to less fearfully handle types of social situations that they have feared, their brain serotonin levels fall.
When people who experience extreme social anxiety take medications normally used by people experiencing heart disease, they experience reductions in severity of symptoms.
A person’s level of serotonin can be affected by various factors including diet and the amount of physical activity in which the person engages.
Some people who experience extreme social anxiety at one point in their lives do not experience it later in their lives.
The elevated levels of serotonin in people who experience extreme social anxiety are limited to one particular area of the brain.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Researchers tested people experiencing extreme social anxiety for various biological markers.

Premise: One biological marker that consistently appeared in people who experience extreme social anxiety was brain levels of serotonin higher than the average brain level of serotonin of people who do not experience such anxiety.
Conclusion: The cause of extreme social anxiety is an above average level of the chemical serotonin in the brain.

The reasoning of the argument is that, since the levels of the chemical serotonin are higher than average in the brains of people who experience extreme social anxiety, one can conclude that high serotonin levels are the cause of extreme social anxiety.Notice that the author makes a cause-and-effect claim.We know that we must be skeptical of such claims.
Are the higher levels of serotonin really to blame for the extreme social anxiety? Could there be another cause? Might the cause and effect be reversed?
YOUR CHOICE(A) When people who have experienced extreme social anxiety are coached to less fearfully handle types of social situations that they have feared, their brain serotonin levels fall.

By indicating that, when people learn to behave less fearfully their brain serotonin levels decrease, this choice provides reason to believe that the cause-and-effect relationship between extreme social anxiety and higher than average brain serotonin levels is the reverse of what the researchers concluded it to be.Rather than above average serotonin causing fear, fear is causing above average serotonin.
Remember, one way to weaken a cause and effect argument is to show that perhaps the cause and effect are actually reversed.
Thus, this answer choice seriously weakens the argument.
(B) When people who experience extreme social anxiety take medications normally used by people experiencing heart disease, they experience reductions in severity of symptoms.

This choice is tempting because we could perceive what this choice says as weakening the support for the conclusion by indicating that something other than an above average serotonin level – perhaps something related to heart disease – is the cause of extreme social anxiety.
However, the truth is that this choice does not have any clear effect at all, because this choice does not indicate what the medications it mentions do or how they work.
For instance, it could be that an above average level of serotonin is in fact the cause of extreme social anxiety, and that, by taking these medications, people simply repress their anxiety symptoms.

Alternatively, it could be that these medications somehow cause reductions in serotonin levels, in which case, the fact that the medications cause reductions in anxiety symptoms would be further indication that an above average serotonin level is the cause of extreme social anxiety.
Whatever the case may be, we are not provided information sufficient for determining that this choice weakens, or that it strengthens, the support for the conclusion.
To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to make up a story that isn’t supported by the limited information provided by the passage and the choice.
(C) A person’s level of serotonin can be affected by various factors including diet and the amount of physical activity in which the person engages.

The conclusion of the argument is simply that higher than average levels of serotonin cause extreme social anxiety.Coming to this conclusion does not require that a person’s serotonin levels do not change and are not affected by things the person does.

Maybe brain levels of serotonin are affected by things a person does, and when a person does certain things, that person brain’s levels of serotonin increase, and that person is fearful.In that case, the high levels could be the cause of the fearfulness.
In other words, what this answer choice says could be completely true, and at the same time, the strength of the argument would be unchanged.
(D) Some people who experience extreme social anxiety at one point in their lives do not experience it later in their lives.
The conclusion is that an above average level of brain serotonin is the cause of extreme social anxiety.

This choice could be tempting if we make the unwarranted assumption that a person who has an above average level of serotonin at one point in that person's life will have an above average level of serotonin throughout life.

If that assumption were correct, then certainly, the fact that some people who experience extreme social anxiety at one point in their lives do not experience it later in their lives, would weaken the argument, because, in that case, we would think that a person would have a high level of brain serotonin throughout life, but extreme social anxiety only at one point in life.In that case, the connection between high brain serotonin and extreme social anxiety would be weakened, because we would believe that high serotonin is present throughout life, but extreme social anxiety comes and goes.In other words, the cause would sometimes be present without the effect.

However, that assumption is unwarranted.For all we know, a person's serotonin levels change over the course of that person's life.So, it could be the case that, at one point in life the person has high serotonin levels and extreme social anxiety and, at another point, low serotonin levels and no extreme social anxiety.Thus, what this choice says does not conflict with conclusion of the argument.
(E) The elevated levels of serotonin in people who experience extreme social anxiety are limited to one particular area of the brain.

This answer could be tempting, in that, because it says that above average serotonin levels are found in only one part of the brain, it could be perceived as diminishing the significance of above average serotonin levels.
However, since it could be that above average serotonin levels in one part of the brain cause extreme social anxiety, this choice does not really affect the argument.

Remember, we are not expected to have PhD level knowledge of any field to take the GMAT.Don’t create stories about the implications of “isolated to one area of the brain.” We just don’t know what this fact means.

question.text: Ingredient X, an inactive ingredient used only for shelf-stability in some antibacterial hand soaps, has recently come under fire from public health groups, which claim that ingredient X alters the chemical composition of certain otherwise harmless bacteria commonly found on the outside of people’s bodies, causing these altered bacteria to cause atypical skin infections.They present as evidence that numerous studies have shown that people who frequently use antibacterial hand soaps with ingredient X are significantly more likely to experience these rare skin infections than are people who do not use such hand soaps.Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument of those against the use of ingredient X?

answers.text: Almost all doctors agree that the use of any form of antibacterial agent is likely to result in atypical skin infections via altering otherwise harmless bacteria.
Most of the people who participated in the studies cited did not report needing to shake hands with many different people each day as a function of their professions.
The atypical skin infections associated with use of antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X tend to be resistant to many antibiotics.
Most of the people who participated in the studies cited did not wash their hands more frequently than does the average user of soaps containing ingredient X.
The study participants using antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X were not doing so because of their having poorly functioning immune systems.

explanation_parts.text: To correctly answer this question, we have to find the choice that provides support for the following conclusion:

Conclusion: Ingredient X, which is found in some antibacterial hand soaps, alters the chemical composition of certain otherwise harmless bacteria commonly found on the outside of people’s bodies, causing these altered bacteria to cause atypical skin infections.
Notice that we’re presented with a cause-and-effect argument.

What evidence is provided? That numerous studies have shown that people who frequently use antibacterial hand soaps with ingredient X are significantly more likely to experience these rare skin infections than are people who do not use such hand soaps.

Notice that the evidence provided is weak, because the fact that two variables occur together (frequent use of antibacterial hand soaps with ingredient X and increased incidence of rare skin infections) does not on its own demonstrate a cause-and-effect relationship.
In answering this question, we’ll be seeking some stronger evidence to support the cause-and-effect claim made.
(A) Almost all doctors agree that the use of any form of antibacterial agent is likely to result in atypical skin infections via altering otherwise harmless bacteria.

The passage tells us that ingredient X is an “inactive ingredient” used only for shelf-stability in some antibacterial hand soaps.In other words, ingredient X is not an antibacterial agent.Therefore, the fact that almost all doctors agree that the use of any form ofantibacterial agentis likely to result in atypical skin infections via altering otherwise harmless bacteria does not strengthen this argument.

If you choose this answer as correct, you likely were not paying close enough attention to the details of the passage; you likely did not notice that ingredient X is an inactive ingredient.At all times, we must keep the straight the details provided in the passage.
(B) Most of the people who participated in the studies cited did not report needing to shake hands with many different people each day as a function of their professions.

The fact that most of the people who participated in the studies cited did not report needing to shake hands with many different people each day as a function of their professions does not strengthen the argument.

Let’s say that most of the people who participated in the studies cited DID report needing to shake hands with many different people each day as a function of their professions.So what? Does shaking hands many times for work make a person more susceptible to atypical skin infections? Without a PhD in microbiology, who knows right? Certainly, we would not be required to have this level understanding for a critical reasoning question.

Similarly, if these people did not shake hands with many people, would that fact strengthen or weaken the argument? Who knows, right? So, we really don’t know what this answer choice does to the argument; its effect on the argument is unclear.
It’s important to not make up unsubstantiated stories.
(C) The atypical skin infections associated with use of antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X tend to be resistant to many antibiotics.

The fact that the atypical skin infections associated with use of antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X tend to be resistant to many antibiotics does not give us more conviction that the use of ingredient X is the cause of these atypical skin infections.
If we were not being careful, we might choose this shiny object, this trap, as correct.After, all what this answer choice says is certainly pertinent to the topic of the argument.

However, the fact that these atypical skin infections associated with use of antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X are resistant to many antibiotics does not indicate that ingredient X is the cause of these infections.
The fact that the infections are resistant to antibiotics is a characteristic of the infections themselves, and it does not connect the infections to ingredient X.

To see why this choice does not connect the infections to ingredient X, we could consider the following.Many infections in the world are resistant to antibiotics.Are all of those infections caused by ingredient X?
(D) Most of the people who participated in the studies cited did not wash their hands more frequently than does the average user of soaps containing ingredient X.
If most of the people who participated in the studies cited DID wash their hands more frequently than does the average user of soaps containing ingredient X, what would that fact mean?
Would it mean that they were less susceptible to atypical skin infections or more susceptible to atypical skin infections? Who knows right?

Furthermore, we don’t even have information on the soaps used by the study participants.Maybe the participants used various types of soaps, some of which contained ingredient X and some of which didn’t.In that case, both the people who used soaps containing ingredient X and got the infections and the people who didn’t get the infections may have been washing their hands “more frequently than does the average user of soaps containing ingredient X.” If all of them were washing their hands that frequently, the frequency of hand washing would not be a differentiating factor.
Therefore, this answer choice has no clear effect on the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The study participants using antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X were not doing so because of their having poorly functioning immune systems.

What would happen to the argument if, contrary to what this choice says, the study participants using antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X WERE doing so because of their having poorly functioning immune systems?

In that case, the argument would be weakened because we’d have an alternative explanation for why the people using antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X were developing atypical skin infections – they had weakened immune systems.
Of course, it is common knowledge the people with weakened immune systems would be more susceptible to infections.

So, notice that, by saying that the study participants using antibacterial hand soaps containing ingredient X were NOT doing so because of their having poorly functioning immune systems, this answer choice serves to rule out that possible alternative explanation and, thus, strengthens the argument.
Remember, by ruling out an alternative explanation for a correlation between events, we can strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion about those events.

question.text: Whereas the majority of college-aged Arbanticans attend university, a much smaller percentage of college-aged Orlonticans do.This difference is often attributed to the fact that the per capita income of Arbantica is much higher than that of Orlontica, making university tuition more affordable for Arbanticans than for Orlonticans.However, the more likely reason is that, because families tend to be smaller in Arbantica than in Orlontica, the average child in Arbantica receives more parental attention than is received by the average child in Orlontica and, therefore, becomes more likely to pursue higher education and gain acceptance into a university.Of the following, which does the most to support the second conclusion over the first?

answers.text: If Orlonticans were to take full advantage of the country’s resources and trading relationships, the per capita income of Orlontica would be at least as high as that of Arbantica.
The percentage of college-aged Arbanticans who attend university on full scholarships is much greater than the percentage of college-aged Orlonticans who attend university on full scholarships.
A significant number of Orlonticans have incomes higher than the per capita income of Arbantica.
The average distance that Orlonticans have to travel in order to get from their homes to universities is no greater than the distance Arbanticans have to travel.
Families in Orlontica are larger on average than not only the average family in Arbantica but also the worldwide average.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Whereas the majority of college-aged Arbanticans attend university, a much smaller percentage of college-aged Orlonticans do.

Conclusion 1: This difference is often attributed to the fact that the per capita income of Arbantica is much higher than that of Orlontica, making university tuition more affordable for Arbanticans than for Orlonticans.

Conclusion 2: However, the more likely reason is that, because families tend to be smaller in Arbantica than in Orlontica, the average child in Arbantica receives more parental attention than is received by the average child in Orlontica and, therefore, becomes more likely to pursue higher education and gain acceptance into a university.
The passage states two conclusions about the cause of the difference between the percentage of Arbanticans who attend university and the percentage of Orlonticans who do.

Our job in answering this question to find a choice that supports the second conclusion over the first.So, we have not only to support the second conclusion but also to show why the second conclusion’s cause is more likely the actual cause than the first conclusion’s cause is.
(A) If Orlonticans were to take full advantage of the country’s resources and trading relationships, the per capita income of Orlontica would be at least as high as that of Arbantica.
This choice could be tempting because it supports a conclusion related to the one that we have to support.
However, this choice supports the wrong conclusion, and does not support either of the conclusions stated in the passage.

While we have to show that it’s not income but family size that is responsible the difference in university attendance, this choice supports the conclusion that the per capita income of Orlontica could readily be made the same as that of Arbantica.

Since showing that the per capita incomes of the two countries don’t have to be different is not the same as showing that the currently existing difference between the two per capita incomes is not what’s causing the difference in university attendance, this choice is not our answer.

YOUR CHOICE(B) The percentage of college-aged Arbanticans who attend university on full scholarships is much greater than the percentage of college-aged Orlonticans who attend university on full scholarships.
This choice shows that the first conclusion is likely not correct and, thus, provides some additional support for the second conclusion.

The way in which this choice shows that the first conclusion is not correct is by showing that, when we remove the first conclusion’s supposed cause, affordability, the effect, the difference in university attendance, is still there.
Here’s exactly how this choice shows that the first conclusion is not correct.

The first conclusion is that the reason why the percentage of Orlonticans who attend university is smaller than the percentage of Arbanticans who attend university is thatuniversity is more affordable for Arbanticans.

Notice what this choice does.It removes the cause, affordability of university attendance, by considering only those Arbanticans and Orlonticans who attend university on full scholarships.Students on full scholarships pay nothing to attend university.So, for these students, affordability is not an issue.

Now, notice what result we get when the first conclusion’s supposed cause is removed.This choice says, “The percentage of college-aged Arbanticans who attend university on full scholarships is much greater than the percentage of college-aged Orlonticans who attend university on full scholarships.”
So, without the supposed cause (affordability), the effect (the difference in the percentages attending university) is still there.

If the effect (difference in the percentages attending university) continues to exist when the supposed cause (affordability) is removed, we certainly have reason to believe that the supposed cause is not the actual cause of the effect.
So, this choice provides a clear reason to doubt that the first conclusion is correct.

Also, by serving to call into question the first conclusion, this choice makes it more likely that the second conclusion, that family size is responsible for the difference in percentages attending university, is correct.
(C) A significant number of Orlonticans have incomes higher than the per capita income of Arbantica.
This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as weakening the case for the first conclusion by attacking a premise that supports the first conclusion.

Because it says that a significant number of Orlonticans have incomes higher than the per capita income of Arbantica, this choice may seem to somehow undermine the premise that the per capita income of Arbantica is much higher than that of Orlontica.
Notice, however, that the premise that the per capita income of Arbantica is much higher than that of Orlontica is stated as fact in the passage and, therefore, cannot be undermined.

All this choice really tells us is that, within the set of data that goes into the calculation of the per capita income of Orlontica, there are some incomes that are higher than the others and higher than the per capita income of Arbantica.Be that as it may, it remains true that the per capita income (average income) of Orlontica is lower than that of Arbantica.
So, this choice does not really affect the support for the first conclusion.
(D) The average distance that Orlonticans have to travel in order to get from their homes to universities is no greater than the distance Arbanticans have to travel.
This choice is very tempting, because it does add support for the second conclusion.

However, this choice is not correct, because it supports the first conclusion as well, and our job in answering this question is not just to support the second conclusion but to support the second conclusionoverthe first.

By stating that the average distance that Orlonticans have to travel in order to get from their homes to universities is no greater than the distance Arbanticans have to travel, this choice tends to make clear that distance traveled to universities is not the reason for the difference between the percentage of Orlonticans and the percentage of Arbanticans who attend university.After all, if Orlonticans have no further to go to get to universities than Arbanticans have, then the variable, distance traveled to universities, is the same for people from both countries.

So, this choice supports both conclusions stated in the passage by serving to eliminate an alternative reason, distance traveled, for the difference between the percentage of Orlonticans and the percentage of Arbanticans who attend university.
(E) Families in Orlontica are larger on average than not only the average family in Arbantica but also the worldwide average.
This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as magnifying a premise that supports the second conclusion.

Since this choice says that Families in Orlontica are larger on average than not only the average family in Arbantica but also the worldwide average, this choice could seem to magnify the premise that families in Orlontica are larger than families in Arbantica.

However, as we know, premises of arguments in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions are taken to be factual, and, since there is no way to make a fact any truer, a choice that seems to magnify a premise has no effect on that premise or on the conclusion that that premise supports.

In this case, what we are seeking to confirm is that the relatively large size of families in Orlontica is responsible for the difference in university attendance.Of course, no matter how large families are in Orlontica, family size may or may not be responsible for the difference.Information that the families in Orlontica are very large does not clearly connect family size with the difference in university attendance.
So, this choice has no effect on the support for the second conclusion.

question.text: In Neurtai, a refreshing cold drink called hezocal is typically sold by sidewalk vendors.Since hezocal has psychoactive effects, when it was discovered that, on days when sidewalk vendors’ hezocal sales were relatively high, the rate at which fresh graffiti was painted onto buildings and other structures was also relatively high, the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, who are known to create the vast majority of the graffiti in Neurtai.Oddly though, after the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, the pattern involving the vendors’ hezocal sales and graffiti creation persisted.Which of the following, if true, best explains why the change made by the vendors did not result in a change in the observed pattern?

answers.text: In most cases, the consumption of hezocal results in people’s becoming more meditative and introspective.
A program designed to reduce use of psychoactive substances by youth in Neurtai was discontinued when it was found to be counterproductive.
The colder or rainier the weather is in Neurtai, the more likely it is that teenagers will remain indoors and read, exercise, or sleep.
Sales of hezocal were decreasing in Neurtai even before the sidewalk vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers.
There are in Neurtai a variety of substances to which teenagers have access that are known to have psychoactive effects.

explanation_parts.text: We are presented with an apparent paradox outlined by two statements connected by the contrast expression “oddly though.” The facts presented are the following:

Fact 1: In Neurtai, in response to a temporal correlation between relatively high rates of graffiti creation and relatively high sales by sidewalk vendors of hezocal, a refreshing cold drink with psychoactive effects, sidewalk vendors in Neurtai ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, who are known to create most of the graffiti in Neurtai.
Fact 2: After the sidewalk vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, the correlation between the vendors’ hezocal sales and graffiti creation persisted.

What Has to Be Explained: Picture it: Even after the sidewalk vendors who sell hezocal ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, the ones creating the vast majority of the graffiti in Neurtai, the correlation between hezocal sales and graffiti creation in Neurtai persisted.How is this situation possible?
(A) In most cases, the consumption of hezocal results in people’s becoming more meditative and introspective.
This choice would explain something other than what has to be explained.It would explain why ceasing to sell hezocal to teenagers would not result in a reduction in graffiti creation, and here’s why.

People who are meditative and introspective probably would not create graffiti.So, what this choice says indicates that consumption of hezocal probably would not result in teenagers’ creating graffiti.Of course, in that case, ceasing to sell hezocal to teenagers probably would not result in a reduction in graffiti creation, because hezocal consumption is probably not a cause of graffiti creation.

The issue with this choice is that what has to be explained is not why ceasing to sell hezocal did not result in a reduction in graffiti creation.What has to be explained is why, even after the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, there was still a correlation between hezocal sales and graffiti creation, and the fact that, in most cases, consumption of hezocal results in people’s becoming more meditative and introspective does not explain the persistence of that correlation.
(B) A program designed to reduce use of psychoactive substances by youth in Neurtai was discontinued when it was found to be counterproductive.

To correctly answer this question, we have to find a choice that explains one specific phenomenon, the persistence of the correlation between hezocal sales and graffiti creation even after the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers.Information on teenagers’ use of psychoactive substances in general does not help to explain that specific phenomenon.So, the fact that a program designed to reduce the use of psychoactive substances by teenagers was counterproductive does not help to explain the phenomenon that has to be explained.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The colder or rainier the weather is in Neurtai, the more likely it is that teenagers will remain indoors and read, exercise, or sleep.

This choice may seem to be the least relevant of all.The paradox involves sales of hezocal and graffiti creation, and this choice is about the effect of weather on the activities of teenagers.However, there is a logical connection that we can make via which this choice explains the phenomenon that the correct answer has to explain.

It’s reasonable to presume that, the colder or rainier the weather is, the lower are street vendors’ sales of the refreshingcolddrink hezocal.On relatively cold and rainy days, people would be unlikely to linger outdoors and buy cold drinks from street vendors.On the other hand, on days with more comfortable weather conditions, sales of hezocal would likely be relatively high.

Meanwhile this choice tells us that, the colder or rainier the weather is in Neurtai, the more likely it is that teenagers will remain indoors.So, it’s reasonable to presume that, the colder or rainier the weather is, the lower is the rate of teenagers’ graffiti creation, whereas, on days with more comfortable weather conditions, the rate of graffiti creation could be expected to be relatively high.

So, what we can see is that, even if teenagers are not able to purchase hezocal, it makes sense that street vendors’ sales of hezocal and the rate of graffiti creation would rise and fall together, because those two variables are similarly affected by a third variable, the weather.When the weather is nice, both sales of hezocal and the rate of graffiti creation are relatively high.When the weather is cold and rainy, sales of hezocal are relatively low, and teenagers stay indoors and don’t create graffiti.
So, what this choice says helps to explain why the pattern persisted even though the vendors had ceased selling hezocal to teenagers.
(D) Sales of hezocal were decreasing in Neurtai even before the sidewalk vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers.

Information about an overall trend in hezocal sales – they were decreasing – existing before the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers does not help to explain why the correlation between hezocal sales and the rate of graffiti creation persisted even after the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers.We need something that shows how the two variables were connected, rather than something that merely describes a trend in only one of the variables.
YOUR CHOICE(E) There are in Neurtai a variety of substances to which teenagers have access that are known to have psychoactive effects.
This choice might explain something other than what has to be explained.It might explain why ceasing to sell hezocal to teenagers would have no effect on the rate of graffiti creation.

After all, if teenagers’ consumption of substances with psychoactive effects does indeed result in teenagers’ creation of graffiti, and if teenagers have access to a variety of substances with psychoactive effects, ceasing to sell one of those substances, hezocal, might have no effect on the rate of graffiti creation.The teenagers could just consume other substances with psychoactive effects and continue to create graffiti.

The issue with this choice is that what has to be explained is not why ceasing to sell hezocal did not result in a change in the rate of graffiti creation.What has to be explained is why, even after the vendors ceased selling hezocal to teenagers, there was still a correlation between hezocal sales and graffiti creation, and the fact that teenagers in Neurtai have access to a variety of substances with psychoactive effects does not explain the persistence of that correlation.

question.text: Birds attracted by various types of food available in Farmer Jones’s fields typically feed in all of Farmer Jones’s fields but, in recent weeks, began feeding almost exclusively in his east fields.A few weeks ago, Farmer Jones applied a new soil treatment in his west fields.It must be true that the birds chose to feed in his east fields because they prefer to feed in fields that have not been treated with the new soil treatment.Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the above argument?

answers.text: After Farmer Jones started using the new soil treatment in all of his fields, the birds feeding in Farmer Jones’s fields reverted to feeding in all of his fields.
The birds feeding in Farmer Jones’s fields did not have any prior contact with the new soil treatment used by Farmer Jones.
In past years, birds that feed in Farmer Jones’s fields have not exhibited any aversion to soil treatments that Farmer Jones has applied to only the west fields or only the east fields.
Farmer Jones used the new soil treatment in his west fields because crop growth there was not as robust as crop growth in his east fields.
In applying the soil treatment to the west fields, Farmer Jones did not follow all of the guidelines recommended by the manufacturer of the treatment.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Birds attracted by various types of food available in Farmer Jones’s fields typically feed in all of Farmer Jones’s fields but, in recent weeks, began feeding almost exclusively in his east fields.
Premise: A few weeks ago, Farmer Jones applied a new soil treatment in his west fields.
Conclusion: It must be true that the birds chose to feed in his east fields because they prefer to feed in fields that have not been treated with the new soil treatment.

We have these birds that normally eat in all the fields, but recently these birds began eating almost exclusively in the east fields.According to the author, the reason for this change in eating habits is that, a few weeks ago, Farmer Jones applied a new soil treatment in his west fields, creating an area that the birds dislike (the west fields) and an area in which they prefer to eat (the east fields).In other words, a change occurred that produced a driving force that has motivated these birds to leave the west fields and move to the east fields.

One key thing that is important to see in this case is that the purported cause is not the soil treatment itself.Rather, the cause is the fact that the soil treatment in the west fields, combined with the absence of the treatment in the east fields, provided the birds with a driving force to move to the east fields.So, the author believes that thedifferencebetween the west fields and the east fields is the driving force that is motivating the birds to move to the east fields.

Why does he believe this? His evidence is simply that Farmer Jones applied the new soil treatment, and right afterwards, the birds began eating in the east fields.Of course, the fact that one event preceded another event is not solid evidence that the former caused the later.
So, in answering this question, we’ll be seeking evidence to strengthen the author’s cause-and-effect claim.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) After Farmer Jones started using the new soil treatment in all of his fields, the birds feeding in Farmer Jones’s fields reverted to feeding in all of his fields.
Many of you avoided this choice because you felt that it weakens the argument.
However, this choice says strengthens the argument by showing that, when the cause goes away, so too does the effect.
Let’s think about this.

The birds normally feed in all fields.Then, after the soil treatment is applied to the west fields, the birds feed almost exclusively in the east fields.So, as we mentioned, the cause of this eating migration seems to be that there is a difference between the fields: there is soil treatment on the west fields but no soil treatment on the east fields.

Now, what this answer choice tells us is that, after Farmer Jones started using the new soil treatment in all of his fields, the birds feeding in Farmer Jones’s fields reverted to feeding in all of his fields.What this shows is that when there is no longer a difference between fields (the cause), the birds do not show a preference for where they eat.In other words, when the birds do not have the option to move to fields that have not been treated with the soil treatment, they go back to feeding in all the fields.
In other words, when the cause (the west fields have the soil treatment, but the east fields do not) goes away, the effect also goes away.
Remember that we can strengthen a cause-and-effect argument by showing that, when the cause is taken away, so too is the effect.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The birds feeding in Farmer Jones’s fields did not have any prior contact with the new soil treatment used by Farmer Jones.
The fact that the birds feeding in Farmer Jones’s fields did not have any prior contact with the new soil treatment used by Farmer Jones does not strengthen the argument.
After all, regardless of whether the birds have had prior contact with the soil treatment, they may have moved to the fields without it because they prefer to eat in fields without the treatment.
So, the birds may have had prior contact, and the argument’s conclusion could be perfectly valid.Or, the birds may not have had prior contact, and the argument’s conclusion could be perfectly valid.
We have to be careful not to make up unsupported stories that make choices like this one seem to affect the strength of arguments.
(C) In past years, birds that feed in Farmer Jones’s fields have not exhibited any aversion to soil treatments that Farmer Jones has applied to only the west fields or only the east fields.

The fact that the birds that feed in Farmer Jones’s fields have not exhibited any aversion to other soil treatments that Farmer Jones has applied to only the west fields or only the east fields does not somehow confirm that the birds do have a strong aversion to the soil treatment described in this passage.
(D) Farmer Jones used the new soil treatment in his west fields because crop growth there was not as robust as crop growth in his east fields.
Why Farmer Jones used the new soil treatment in the west fields but not in the east fields does not strengthen the argument.

Regardless of the differences in crop growth in the fields, the birds were eating in all of the fields until Farmer Jones added the soil treatment.This difference in crop growth in the fields does not somehow give us confidence that the birds moved because they prefer fields without the treatment.
If you chose this choice, you may have made up an unsupported story that somehow seemed to make the difference in crop growth into a strengthener.
The truth is that we don’t even have any clear reason to believe that the birds are affected in any particular way by relatively weak or relatively robust crop growth.
(E) In applying the soil treatment to the west fields, Farmer Jones did not follow all of the guidelines recommended by the manufacturer of the treatment.
What does “did not follow all of the guidelines” mean exactly?
Does that mean that Farmer Jones used more of the treatment than the manufacturer recommends? Less than the manufacturer recommends?
Perhaps the manufacturer recommends applying the soil treatment on a dry day, and Farmer Jones applied it on a rainy day.
Notice that we can’t tell in what way Farmer Jones did not follow all of the guidelines or how Farmer Jones' not following all the guidelines would affect birds.
We must be careful of intelligent sounding choices that don’t actually take us in one direction or another.

question.text: In Bolivia, which has many cities located at altitudes greater than 12,000 feet above sea level, the coca plant is used to make cocaine, a street drug that is illegal there, and coca tea, which is legal in all parts of Bolivia and often used in the treatment of altitude sickness.While consumption of coca tea does not result in the euphoric or intoxicating effects that result from cocaine use, a person who consumes coca tea may generate a positive result on a test meant to detect cocaine use.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: People caught drinking coca tea in Bolivia can face harsh penalties.
Altitude sickness is a common problem for people in Bolivia.
The coca plant does not grow at altitudes below 12,000 feet.
People who might be subject to drug tests never drink coca tea.
There is at least one difference between cocaine and coca tea.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: In Bolivia, which has many cities located at altitudes greater than 12,000 feet above sea level, the coca plant is used to make cocaine, a street drug that is illegal there, and coca tea, which is legal in all parts of Bolivia and often used in the treatment of altitude sickness.
Fact 2: Consumption of coca tea does not result in the euphoric or intoxicating effects that result from cocaine use.
Fact 3: A person who consumes coca tea may generate a positive result on a test meant to detect cocaine use.
Given these three facts, what must be true?
(A) People caught drinking coca tea in Bolivia can face harsh penalties.
Notice that what this answer choice says is in contradiction to the information provided to us in the passage.The passage says “coca tea, which islegalin all parts of Bolivia.”
(B) Altitude sickness is a common problem for people in Bolivia.
Although what this answer choice says is true in reality, the passage does not provide any information about how common a problem altitude sickness is for people in Bolivia.

In order to avoid choosing a choice like this one we have to be careful to keep the facts provided straight.The passage says that coca tea is “often used in the treatment of altitude sickness.” That fact is not the same as what this choice says.
(C) The coca plant does not grow at altitudes below 12,000 feet.

Notice that the passage does not provide us with any information regarding the range of altitudes at which the coca plant grows.For all we know given what the passage says, it does grow at altitudes below 12,000 feet.

In order to avoid choosing a choice like this one we have to be careful to keep the facts provided straight.The passage mentions altitudes greater than 12,000 feet above sea level, and it mentions coca plants, but it does not connect altitude and coca plants.
(D) People who might be subject to drug tests never drink coca tea.
The passage tells us that a person who consumes coca tea may generate a positive result on a test meant to detect cocaine use.
Notice, however, that we do not know whether people who might be subject to drug tests drink coca tea.For all we know given what the passage says, many do.
YOUR CHOICE(E) There is at least one difference between cocaine and coca tea.
We’re told that “consumption of coca tea does not result in the euphoric or intoxicating effects that result from cocaine use.”
So, clearly there must be least one difference between cocaine and coca tea.

question.text: While farmers in Verdantia produce limfruit in limited quantities only, farmers in Hexacua produce limfruit in substantial quantities, and Hexacua exports limfruit to many other countries.However, because of the great distance between Verdantia and Hexacua, there is no way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantia such that limfruit exported from Hexacua could profitably be sold at affordable prices in Verdantia.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: None of the countries to which Hexacua exports limfruit get limfruit from Verdantia.
Any limfruit profitably sold at affordable prices in Verdantia did not come from Hexacua.
The level of the supply of limfruit in Hexacua has no effect on the price of limfruit in Verdantia.
All of the limfruit produced in Verdantia is consumed by people who live in Verdantia.
If there were a low-cost way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantia, farmers in Verdantia who produce limfruit would receive lower prices for limfruit.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Farmers in Verdantia produce limfruit in limited quantities only.
Fact 2: Farmers in Hexacua produce limfruit in substantial quantities, and Hexacua exports limfruit to many other countries.

Fact 3: Because of the great distance between Verdantia and Hexacua, there is no way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantia such that limfruit exported from Hexacua could profitably be sold at affordable prices in Verdantia.
(A) None of the countries to which Hexacua exports limfruit get limfruit from Verdantia.

The passage does not provide us with information regarding from where other countries get their limfruit.For all we know, many of the countries to which Hexacua exports limfruit get limfruit from Verdantia.

Notice that, while the passage indicates that Verdantia is too far from Hexacua for limfruit from Hexacua to be profitably be sold at affordable prices in Verdantia, there could very well be a countrylocated in betweenVerdantia and Hexacua in which limfruit from either Verdantia or Hexacua could be profitably sold at affordable prices.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Any limfruit profitably sold at affordable prices in Verdantia did not come from Hexacua.
Notice that the passage says, “there is no way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantiasuch that limfruit exported from Hexacua could profitably be sold at affordable prices in Verdantia.”
Given that fact, we can tell that there must not ever be any limfruit from Hexacua profitably sold at affordable prices in Verdantia.
Therefore, we can infer what this answer choice says, which is that any limfruit profitably sold at affordable prices in Verdantia did not come from Hexacua.
(C) The level of the supply of limfruit in Hexacua has no effect on the price of limfruit in Verdantia.

We are not provided with any information on how supply and demand of limfruit in Hexacua and Verdantia affect each other’s limfruit prices.For all we know, the level of the supply of limfruit in Hexacua has a big effect on the price of limfruit in Verdantia.

We have to consider the possibility that, even though there is no way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantia such that limfruit exported from Hexacua could profitably be sold at affordable prices in Verdantia, the markets for limfruit in Verdantia and Hexacua are part of one larger marketplace.In that case, even though limfruit from Hexacua may not go to Verdantia, if the level of supply of limfruit in Hexacua would affect prices in that larger marketplace, the level of supply of limfruit in Hexacua could indirectly affect prices in Verdantia.
(D) All of the limfruit produced in Verdantia is consumed by people who live in Verdantia.

This choice could be tempting, because the passage says that farmers in Verdantia produce limfruit in limited quantities only, and one could perceive that fact as indicating that all limfruit produced in Verdantia is consumed in Verdantia.In other words, one might feel that it makes sense to keep in Verdantia the limited quantities of limfruit produced there, and that, what seems to make sense is what actually occurs.

However, the truth is that we are not provided with any information about where the limfruit produced in Verdantia is consumed.For all we know, some of the limfruit produced in Verdantia is consumed by people who live outside of Verdantia.
(E) If there were a low-cost way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantia, farmers in Verdantia who produce limfruit would receive lower prices for limfruit.
We are not provided with any information regarding what would happen in Verdantia if there were a low-cost way to transport limfruit from Hexacua to Verdantia.
We may feel that availability of Hexacuan limfruit in Verdantia would have a downward effect on prices, but we really don’t know much about the situation.

For instance, we have to consider the possibility that farmers in both Verdantia and Hexacua already export limfruit to a third country.In that case, limfruit from Verdantia and limfruit from Hexacua would already be sold in what is essentially the same marketplace and, thus, the supply of limfruit from Hexacua would already be indirectly affecting the supply of limfruit in Verdantia.In such a case, the existence of a low-cost way of transporting limfruit directly from Hexacua to Verdantia might not make much difference in the supply of or price of limfruit in Verdantia.

question.text: Many exams no longer have to be taken at monitored test centers.These exams can now be taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home.To prevent cheating, software powered by artificial intelligence uses the test-taker’s computer camera, analyzing, among other things, the test-taker’s eye and head movements, the room in which the test-taker is sitting, and the pen with which the test-taker is writing.Research shows that this software can detect a person’s cheating 99 times out of 100.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: No person has ever cheated on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at the person’s home.
Most people who take exams on computers connected to the Internet at their homes score lower on the exams than they would have had they taken them at test centers.
It is possible to cheat on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home.
An exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home is easier than a similar exam taken at a monitored test center.
It is much harder to cheat on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home than on one taken at a monitored test center.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Many exams no longer have to be taken at monitored test centers.These exams can now be taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home.

Fact 2: To prevent cheating, software powered by artificial intelligence uses the test-taker’s computer camera, analyzing, among other things, the test-taker’s eye and head movements, the room in which the test-taker is sitting, and the pen with which the test-taker is writing.
Fact 3: Research shows that this software can detect a person’s cheating 99 times out of 100.
Given these three facts, what must be true?
(A) No person has ever cheated on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at the person’s home.
The passage does not give us any information regarding how many people have or have not cheated on a computer connected to the Internet at the person’s home.
(B) Most people who take exams on computers connected to the Internet at their homes score lower on the exams than they would have had they taken them at test centers.

The passage does not give us any information regarding how most people score when they take exams on computers connected to the Internet at their homes relative to how they would score if they had taken them at test centers.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It is possible to cheat on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home.

Notice that we’re told that research shows that this software can detect a person’s cheating 99 times out of 100.In other words, the software is about 99 percent effective at catching cheaters, meaning that, in the other 1 percent of cases, a person could cheat.
Therefore, it must be true that, as this choice says, it is possible to cheat on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home.
Thus, what this choice says can be inferred from what the passages says.
(D) An exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home is easier than a similar exam taken at a monitored test center.

Notice that the passage never provides us with any information comparing the difficulty of an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home with the difficulty of a similar exam taken at a monitored test center.
(E) It is much harder to cheat on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home than on one taken at a monitored test center.

Notice that we don’t have any information regarding how hard it is to cheat on an exam taken at a monitored test center.For all we know, as hard as it is to cheat on an exam taken on a computer connected to the Internet at a test-taker’s home, it is even harder to cheat on an exam taken at a monitored test center.

question.text: At least 1.6 billion people live without electricity.Even as most countries have experienced continued economic development and as the world’s population has continued to grow, the number of people living without electricity has remained virtually unchanged.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred on the basis of them?

answers.text: The people who do not have access to electricity are all located in less developed countries.
The proportion of the world population living without electricity has been decreasing over time.
The countries in which the greatest proportion of people have access to electricity are those that have experienced the greatest rates of economic growth.
Economic development has not resulted in an increase in the number of people who have access to electricity.
Unless the rate of economic development increases, it will continue to be the case that approximately 1.6 billion people live without electricity.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: At least 1.6 billion people live without electricity.

Fact 2: Even as most countries have experienced continued economic development, and as the world’s population has continued to grow, the number of people living without electricity has remained virtually unchanged.
(A) The people who do not have access to electricity are all located in less developed countries.
We cannot infer that the people who do not have access to electricity are all located in less developed countries.Such a fact is not supported by anything said in the passage.
We must resist the urge to choose as correct an answer that is not supported by the passage but sounds good because it is very probably true in the real world.

Notice also that this choice is tempting because “less developed” in the choice sounds like and seems related to “economic development” in the passage.However, as you know, we don’t merely match words to answer Critical Reasoning questions.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The proportion of the world population living without electricity has been decreasing over time.

It must be true that the proportion of the world population living without electricity has been decreasing over time because we’re told that the world’s population has continued to grow while the number of people living without electricity has remained virtually unchanged.
So, for example, let’s say that the following has occurred.
Proportion Ten Years Ago: 1.6 billion people without electricity / 6 billion people on Earth
Proportion Today: 1.6 billion people without electricity / 7 billion people on Earth
We can clearly see that, according to what the passage says, the proportion would be smaller today than at some point in the past (for example, ten years ago).
In other words, 1.6 billion / 7 billion is smaller than 1.6 billion / 6 billion.
(C) The countries in which the greatest proportion of people have access to electricity are those that have experienced the greatest rates of economic growth.

We cannot infer that the countries in which the greatest proportion of people have access to electricity are those that have experienced the greatest rates of economic growth.This answer represents an excellent opportunity to improve your skills.Go back to the passage.Take an hour if you need to.Find one thing, just one thing, that would allow us to infer what this answer says.You will not find anything supporting what this answer choice says …
(D) Economic development has not resulted in an increase in the number of people who have access to electricity.
We cannot infer that economic development has not resulted in an increase in the number of people who have access to electricity.There are a few things to notice here.
First, given what the passage says, it must be true that the number of people with electricity has increased over time.
We are told by the passage that the number of people without electricity has remained virtually unchanged and that the world’s population has continued to grow.
Let’s consider a scenario that involves those facts.Here’s our first stage of the scenario:
Stage One: 4.6 billion people - 1.6 billion people without electricity =3 billion people with electricity
Now, we know that the world’s population has continued to grow.So, lets increase the world population to 7.6 billion people for the next stage:
Stage Two: 7.6 billion people - 1.6 billion people without electricity =6 billion people with electricity
So, we see that, since the total population has increased, while the number without electricity has remained unchanged, the number of people with electricity must have increased.

Now, was economic development responsible for this increase? We don’t know for sure.Economic development very well could have been responsible, but, given the information presented, we just can’t say.
(E) Unless the rate of economic development increases, it will continue to be the case that approximately 1.6 billion people live without electricity.

The choice could be tempting, because the passage says that, even as most countries have experienced continued economic development, the number of people living without electricity has remained virtually unchanged.Those facts could be perceived as indicating that economic develop occurring at the rate at which economic development has been occurring just isn’t sufficient to result in a reduction in the number of people living without electricity.
However, the truth is that we cannot infer that, unless the rate of economic development increases, it will remain the case that approximately 1.6 billion people will live without electricity.

What has been the case in the past need not be the case in the future.For instance, for all we know, at some point in the future, there will be a technological development such that the number of people living without electricity will quickly decrease regardless of what the rate of economic development is at that time.

question.text: Five years ago, 500 amateur chess players began training for the national chess championships without the assistance of professional chess coaches.Since then, four of those players have won the national chess championships.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: No amateur chess players train for the national chess championships with the assistance of professional chess coaches.
A person need not be a professional chess player to win the national chess championships.
The national chess championships are not very difficult to win.
No more than 500 people train for the national chess championships during a given year.
Professional chess coaches can never be helpful to people training for the national chess championships.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: Five years ago, 500 amateur chess players began training for the national chess championships without the assistance of professional chess coaches.
Fact 2: Since then, four of those players have won the national chess championships.
Given these two facts, we are seeking something that must be true.
(A) No amateur chess players train for the national chess championships with the assistance of professional chess coaches.

Notice that this passage is about 500 amateur chess players; it is not about all amateur chess players in the entire world.So, for all we know given what the passage says, there could be 10,000 more amateur chess players, all of whom do use professional chess coaches.In that case, of course, what this choice says would not be true.
So, what this choice says cannot be properly inferred from what the passage says.
We must take care to not make a broad generalization from a limited sample.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A person need not be a professional chess player to win the national chess championships.
What this answer choice says must be true given what the passage says.
The passage says that 500 amateur chess players trained for the national chess championships, and that four of those players won the national chess championships.

Of course, since an amateur chess player would not be a professional chess player, the passage says essentially that four players who were not professional chess players won the national chess championships.
Therefore, we can see that, given what the passage says, it must be true that, as this choice says, a person need not be a professional chess player to win the national chess championships.
Thus, this choice can be properly inferred from what the passage says.
(C) The national chess championships are not very difficult to win.

This choice could be tempting because we could see that the passage says that amateur players won the national chess championships and be tempted to decide that that information indicates that it is easy to win the national chess championships.
However, the truth is that the fact that amateurs have won the championships does not really indicate that it is easy to win them.
So, for all we know given what the passage says, they are extremely difficult to win.
Thus, what this choice says does not have to be true given what the passage says.
(D) No more than 500 people train for the national chess championships during a given year.

Notice that the passage is about a sample of 500 amateur chess players.We certainly don’t have any reason to believe that these 500 amateur chess players represent all the amateur chess players in the entire world.So, for all we know given what the passage says, many other players train for the championships and what the choice says is not true.
(E) Professional chess coaches can never be helpful to people training for the national chess championships.

The fact that this sample of 500 amateur chess players didn’t use the assistance of professional chess coaches certainly does not indicate that professional chess coaches can never be helpful to people training for the national chess championships.For all we know given what the passage says, professional chess coaches can be extremely helpful to people training for the national chess championships.

question.text: When stored properly, white wine can be safely consumed for up to two years past the expiration date found on the bottle, whereas, when stored properly, red wine can be safely consumed for up to three years past the expiration date found on the bottle.In general, wines with relatively high acidity, relatively low alcohol levels, and relatively high sugar levels will store for longer periods of time than their counterparts will store for.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: Even when not stored properly, red wine usually lasts past the expiration date found on the bottle.
If a wine has a relatively high sugar level, then that wine can be consumed long past the expiration date found on the bottle.
White wine is generally less expensive than red wine.
Some wine can be safely consumed past the expiration date found on the bottle.
Wines that have relatively high alcohol levels generally cannot be safely consumed past the expiration dates found on their bottles.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: When stored properly, white wine can be safely consumed for up to two years past the expiration date found on the bottle.
Fact 2: When stored properly, red wine can be safely consumed for up to three years past the expiration date found on the bottle.
Fact 3: In general, wines with relatively high acidity, relatively low alcohol levels, and relatively high sugar levels will store for longer periods of time than their counterparts will store for.
Given these three facts, what must be true?
(A) Even when not stored properly, red wine usually lasts past the expiration date found on the bottle.

Notice that the passage tells us that “When stored properly, white wine can be safely consumed for up to two years past the expiration date found on the bottle, whereas,when stored properly, red wine can be safely consumed for up to three years past the expiration date found on the bottle.”
Notice that we don’t have any information about what will happen to red wine when it isnotstored properly.
Thus, this choice cannot be inferred from what the passage says.
(B) If a wine has a relatively high sugar level, then that wine can be consumed long past the expiration date found on the bottle.

Notice that we are told “In general, wines with relatively high acidity, relatively low alcohol levels, and relatively high sugar levels will store forlongerperiods of time than their counterparts will store for.”

This information simply provides a comparison between wines with relatively high acidity, relatively low alcohol levels, and relatively high sugar levels and those without those characteristics.The passage does not tell us how long any wines last.
So, we don’t have any information about whether wine that has a relatively high sugar level can be consumed long past the expiration date found on the bottle.
(C) White wine is generally less expensive than red wine.
Notice that the passage does not provide us with any information about the cost of red wine or white wine.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Some wine can be safely consumed past the expiration date found on the bottle.

Notice that we are told “When stored properly, white wine can besafely consumed for up to two years past the expiration date found on the bottle, whereas, when stored properly, red wine can besafely consumed for up to three years past the expiration date found on the bottle.”
Of course, from this information we can infer that some wine can be safely consumed past the expiration date found on the bottle.
(E) Wines that have relatively high alcohol levels generally cannot be safely consumed past the expiration dates found on their bottles.

Notice that what this answer choice says is not supported by the passage.For all we know, wines that have relatively high alcohol levels generally CAN be safely consumed past the expiration dates found on their bottles.Nothing in the passage tells us otherwise.

question.text: In a recent study, 31 randomly selected, high performing professional athletes were scanned with MRI machines.In 28 of the 31 cases, the scans revealed significant abnormalities in and damage to the tendons and cartilage of the athletes.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: Playing sports at a professional level tends to result in abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.
Abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage usually go undetected unless found via MRI.
It is possible to perform at a high level in professional sports with significant abnormalities in and damage to one’s tendons and cartilage.
Most professional athletes will eventually need medical treatment for abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.
Most professional athletes would benefit from undergoing MRI scans more often than they currently do.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: In a recent study, 31 randomly selected, high performing professional athletes were scanned with MRI machines.
Fact 2: In 28 of the 31 cases, the scans revealed significant abnormalities in and damage to the tendons and cartilage of the athletes.
(A) Playing sports at a professional level tends to result in abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.
We can’t infer that playing sports at a professional level tends to result in abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.
Remember, the fact that two events occur simultaneously does not necessarily indicate that one caused the other.

So, the fact that, in 28 of 31 cases, scans revealed significant abnormalities in and damage to the tendons and cartilage of professional athletes does not mean that playing sports at a professional level tends to cause abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.The significant abnormalities in and damage to the tendons and cartilage could have resulted from something other than playing sports.
We must always be skeptical of cause-and-effect reasoning.

Also, notice, what this choice says may seem to be a main conclusion that follows from what the passage says, making this answer choice like a shiny object that we are tempted to pick it up, but that characteristic of this choice makes this choice a trap, because what it says is not a valid inference.
(B) Abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage usually go undetected unless found via MRI.

We can’t infer that abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage usually go undetected unless found via MRI.Of course, there could be many other ways in which abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage are detected.Some actual examples are ultrasounds, manual tests of the joints by a doctor or physical therapist, and instability in joints.

The fact that this passage discusses detection via MRI of these abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage does not allow us to infer that such abnormalities and damage usually go undetected unless found via MRI.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It is possible to perform at a high level in professional sports with significant abnormalities in and damage to one’s tendons and cartilage.
Given what the passage says, it must be true that it is possible to perform at a high level in professional sports with significant abnormalities in and damage to one’s tendons and cartilage.
After all, the passage says that 28“high performing professional athletes”were found to have significant abnormalities in and damage to their tendons and cartilage.
(D) Most professional athletes will eventually need medical treatment for abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.
We can’t infer that most professional athletes will eventually need medical treatment for abnormalities in and damage to tendons and cartilage.

The fact that the professional athletes mentioned in the passage had such abnormalities and damage does not allow us to conclude with certainty that those athletes or any other athletes will need medical treatment for such abnormalities and damage.For all we know, these professional athletes, and most others, will continue doing their things very well for their entire careers with no complaints, or need for medicate treatment, at all.
If you picked this answer as correct, you must be careful to not choose answers that say things that seem likely or could be true in reality yet are not fully supported by the passage.
(E) Most professional athletes would benefit from undergoing MRI scans more often than they currently do.
We can’t infer that most professional athletes would benefit from undergoing MRI scans more often than they currently do.

The passage never makes any claims about the value of professional athletes being scanned with MRI machines.Yes, the scans mentioned in the passage revealed abnormalities and damage, but did finding those abnormalities and damage somehow benefit the athletes? The passage does not indicate that it did.

Furthermore, even if professional athletes do benefit from undergoing MRI scans, it may not be the case that they would benefit from undergoing MRI scans more often than they currently do.The passage says nothing about how often such athletes undergo MRI scans.For all we know from what the passage says, they currently do so quite often, or even too often.
If you picked this answer as correct, you must be careful to not choose answers that say things that could be true in reality yet are not fully supported by the passage.

question.text: A multi-drug resistant fungus,Candida Auris, is spreading at an alarming rate across the globe.Most fungi require warm, damp places to live, butCandida Auriscan thrive on cooler surfaces, such as skin, enabling it to spread more like a bacterium, which can rapidly move from person to person, than like a fungus, which typically is not easily spread.Further complicating matters, expensive blood or bodily fluid tests are necessary to identify the presence ofCandida Auris, which can live in, and be spread by, a host for many months before causing symptoms that would prompt a physician to perform diagnostic tests.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: Candida Aurisspreads from person to person more easily than do many other types of fungi.
Identification of most bacterial infections does not require expensive blood tests.
