Candida Aurisspreads from person to person as fast as do bacteria living on skin.
Some infections withCandida Aurisare spread from person to person by way of bodily fluids.
Candida Auriswill not thrive in warm, damp locations.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: A multi-drug resistant fungus,Candida Auris, is spreading at an alarming rate across the globe.

Fact 2: Most fungi require warm, damp places to live, butCandida Auriscan thrive on cooler surfaces, such as skin, enabling it to spread more like a bacterium, which can rapidly move from person to person, than like a fungus, which typically is not easily spread.

Fact 3: Further complicating matters, expensive blood or bodily fluid tests are necessary to identify the presence ofCandida Auris, which can live in, and be spread by, a host for many months before causing symptoms that would prompt a physician to perform diagnostic tests.
YOUR CHOICE(A)Candida Aurisspreads from person to person more easily than do many other types of fungi.

Notice that the passage says,“Most fungi require warm, damp places to live, butCandida Auriscan thrive on cooler surfaces, such as skin, enabling it to spread more like a bacterium.”Also notice that it says,“a bacterium, which can rapidly move from person to person, and says, “a fungus, which typically is not easily spread.”
So, we see thatCandida Aurisspreads more like a bacterium than like a fungus, and that bacteria move more rapidly from person to person than fungi move.
So, it must be true thatCandida Aurisspreads from person to person more easily than do many other types of fungi.
(B) Identification of most bacterial infections does not require expensive blood tests.

The passage does not mention how most bacterial infections are identified.For all we know from what the passage says, the identification of most bacterial infections does require expensive blood tests.

If you chose this answer as correct, you may have done so because what is says seems to be true in reality.Remember, though, we are not seeking something that could very well be true in reality.We are seeking something that is fully supported by the information presented in the passage.
(C)Candida Aurisspreads from person to person as fast as do bacteria living on skin.

The passage says, “Candida Auriscan thrive on cooler surfaces, such as skin, enabling it to spreadmore likea bacterium, which can rapidly move from person to person, than a fungus, which typically is not easily spread.”

Notice, however, that the passage does not make a comparison of the rate at whichCandida Aurisspreads from person to person and the rate at which bacteria living on the skin spread from person to person.
We must be careful not to over-infer.
(D) Some infections withCandida Aurisare spread from person to person by way of bodily fluids.
This answer is seductive because it uses wording that appears also in the passage, “bodily fluids,” and because we know that, in real life, many diseases are spread by way of bodily fluids.
However, the passage does not say that some infections withCandida Aurisare spread from person to person by way of bodily fluids.

The passage says that, “expensive blood or bodily fluid tests are necessary to identify the presence ofCandida Auris, which can live in, and be spread by, a host for many months before causing symptoms.”

The fact that expensive blood or bodily fluid tests are necessary to identify the presence ofCandida Aurisdoes not allow us to infer that some infections withCandida Aurisare spread from person to person by way of bodily fluids.
Remember, we’re not seeking something that could very well be true in reality.We are seeking something that is fully supported by the information presented in the passage.
(E)Candida Auriswill not thrive in warm, damp locations.
The passage says, “Most fungi require warm, damp places to live, butCandida Auriscan thrive on cooler surfaces.”

Notice that the above statement from the passage does not say that “Candida Auriswill not thrive in warm, damp locations.” For all we know,Candida Auriswill thrive in warm, damp locations as well as cooler surfaces.

If you choose this answer as correct, you likely were not paying close enough attention to the details.The fact thatCandida Auriscan thrive on cooler surfaces does not allow us to infer thatCandida Auriswill not thrive in warm, damp locations.After all, why couldn’t it do both?

question.text: Last year, the average price at which a house in the center of Oakville sold was $270,000, and the average price at which a house in Oakville’s coastal area sold was $320,000.During the same period, the average price at which a house in any area of Oakville sold was $340,000.If the information above is correct, which of the following can be properly concluded about the sales of houses in Oakville last year?

answers.text: No house in the coastal area sold for less than the average price at which a house in the center of Oakville sold.
More of the houses sold in Oakville were located in the coastal area than were located in the center.
Some houses in Oakville sold for a price higher than the price at which any house in the center of Oakville area sold.
At least one house sold in Oakville sold for a price higher than the average of all the prices at which houses in the coastal area sold.
Most of the houses sold in Oakville were located neither in the center of Oakville nor in the coastal area.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Last year, the average price at which a house in the center of Oakville sold was $270,000.
Fact 2: Last year, the average price at which a house in Oakville’s coastal area sold was $320,000.
Fact 3: During the same period, the average price at which a house in any area of Oakville sold was $340,000.
To correctly answer this question, we have to keep in mind that the prices that go into the calculation of an average price can be much larger or smaller than the average price.
(A) No house in the coastal area sold for less than the average price at which a house in the center of Oakville sold.
We cannot infer that no house in the coastal area sold for less than the average price at which a house in the center of Oakville sold.We know that:
Avg.Oakville’s coastal area: $320,000
Avg .center of Oakville: $270,000

Remember that an average is often not a useful statistic because it represents the “average” value, but unless all the values are the same, most of the values will not actually be the average value.So, the information provided by an average value is limited.

For example, take the average of 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5.Of course, the average is 3.However, only one of the numbers is actually 3.Furthermore, it’s important to see that to get an average of 3, since some of the values are greater than 3, some of the values must be less than three.

Similarly, if last year, the average price at which a house in Oakville’s coastal area sold was $320,000, then unless all homes sold for the same price, there must have been at least one value less than $320,000.We don’t know what this one value is.However, it could have been less than $270,000.
So, contrary to what this choice says, it is quite possible that one or more houses in the coastal area sold for less than the average price at which a house in the center of Oakville sold.
(B) More of the houses sold in Oakville were located in the coastal area than were located in the center.
We cannot infer that more of the houses sold in Oakville were located in the coastal area than were located in the center.

The fact that the coastal area average price is higher than the center of Oakville average price certainly does not mean that more of the houses sold in Oakville were located in the coastal area than were located in the center.
The averages alone do not tell us how many data points were used to produce those averages, and the passage does not provide information on numbers of houses sold in the two areas.
(C) Some houses in Oakville sold for a price higher than the price at which any house in the center of Oakville area sold.
We cannot infer that some houses in Oakville sold for a price higher than the price at which any house in the center of Oakville area sold.

While we know the average price at which a house sold in the center of Oakville, $270,000, and the average price at which a house in any area of Oakville sold, $340,000, we don't have information that indicates what makes up those averages.The averages could result from a mix of very high and very low prices.

Thus, even though the average price at which houses sold in the center was lower than the average price for all houses sold, it could be the case that one or more of the houses that sold for the highest prices of all happened to be located in the center of Oakville.

So, while it could be the case that some houses in Oakville sold for prices higher than the price at which any house in the center of Oakville area sold, it also could be the case that one or more houses in the center of Oakville sold for prices higher than those at which any other houses in Oakville sold.
YOUR CHOICE(D) At least one house sold in Oakville sold for a price higher than the average of all the prices at which houses in the coastal area sold.
It must be true that at least one house sold in Oakville sold for a price higher than the average of all the prices at which houses in the coastal area sold.Why? Consider the following:
Average price center of Oakville: $270,000
Average price Oakville’s coastal area: $320,000
Average price any area of Oakville: $340,000
Notice that, when we consider the average price at which a house in any area of Oakville sold, $340,000, this amount is greater than $270,000 and $320,000.

To bring the average for any area of Oakville to $340,000, there must have been at least one house that sold for a price higher than the average of all the prices at which houses in the coastal area sold.The coastal area average is $320,000.If no house in Oakville sold for more than $320,000, we could not have an average price of $340,000 for any area of Oakville.
So, it must be the case that at least one house sold in Oakville sold for a price higher than the coastal area average of $320,000.
(E) Most of the houses sold in Oakville were located neither in the center of Oakville nor in the coastal area.

We cannot infer that most of the houses sold in Oakville were located neither in the center of Oakville nor in the coastal area.The averages alone do not tell us how many data points were used to produce those averages, and the passage provides no information on how many houses were sold in the center of Oakville, in the coastal area, or in Oakville as a whole.

question.text: Marvelo Chocolatiers was unprofitable during the first half of the year.Therefore, Marvelo reduced the prices at which it sold its various products, so that all products that Marvelo sold during the second half of the year were sold at prices lower than those at which Marvelo sold the same products during the first half of the year.However, even though Marvelo’s revenue from product sales for the second half of the year was greater than its revenue from product sales for the first half of the year, Marvelo was not profitable during the second half of the year.Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?

answers.text: Marvelo was more profitable during the first half of the year than it was during the second half of the year.
Marvelo was more profitable during the second half of the year than it was during the first half of the year.
Marvelo sold more of at least one of its products during the second half of the year than Marvelo sold of that product during the first half of the year.
Marvelo will not become profitable unless it succeeds in reducing the costs that it incurs in producing the products that it sells.
Marvelo sold in the second half of the year greater quantities of all of its products than it sold in the first half of the year.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Marvelo Chocolatiers was unprofitable during the first half of the year.

Fact 2: Therefore, Marvelo reduced the prices at which it sold its various products, so that all products that Marvelo sold during the second half of the year were sold at prices lower than those at which Marvelo sold the same products during the first half of the year.
Fact 3: Marvelo’s revenue from product sales for the second half of the year was greater than its revenue from product sales for the first half.
Fact 4: Marvelo was not profitable during the second half of the year.
(A) Marvelo was more profitable during the first half of the year than it was during the second half of the year.

This choice could be tempting, because the fact that all products that Marvelo sold during the second half of the year were sold at prices lower than those at which Marvelo sold the same products during the first half of the year could be perceived as indicating that Marvelo must have been more profitable during the first half of the year than it was during the second half of the year.

However, the truth is that price is not the only factor that affects profitability.Changes in revenue or expenses also affect profitability, and, in fact, the passage says that Marvelo’s revenue for the second half of the year was greater than its revenue for the first half.So, we know that price was not the only variable that changed from the first half to the second half of the year.Revenue increased.

So, for all we know, even though Marvelo’s prices were lower during the second half of the year than they were during the first half of the year, Marvelo could have been more profitable during the second half of the year.
Thus, what this choice says is not supported by the information given.
(B) Marvelo was more profitable during the second half of the year than it was during the first half of the year.

This choice could be tempting, because the fact that Marvelo’s revenue from product sales for the second half of the year was greater than its revenue for the first half could be perceived as indicating that Marvelo must have been more profitable during the second half of the year than it was during the first half of the year.

However, the truth is that revenue is not the only factor that affects profitability.Price levels and expenses also affect profitability, and, in fact, the passage says that, during the second half of the year Marvelo’s prices were lower than Marvelo’s prices were during the first half.So, we know that revenue was not the only variable that changed from the first half to the second half of the year.Prices decreased.

So, for all we know, even though Marvelo’s revenue was greater during the second half of the year than it was during the first half of the year, Marvelo could have been less profitable during the second half of the year.
Thus, what this choice says is not supported by the information given.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Marvelo sold more of at least one of its products during the second half of the year than Marvelo sold of that product during the first half of the year.
It must be true that Marvelo sold more of at least one of its products during the second half of the year than Marvelo sold of that product during the first half of the year, and here’s why.
Here’s a simple formula for the revenue of a company that sells products, such as Marvelo:
(Revenue) = (Price) x (Quantity of Products Sold)
We know that “all products that Marvelo sold during the second half of the year were sold at prices lower than those at which Marvelo sold the same products during the first half of the year.”
We also know that “Marvelo’s revenue from product sales for the second half of the year was greater than its revenue for the first half.”

If the prices at which Marvelo’s products sold were lower during the second half of the year than they were during the first half of the year, in order for Marvelo’s revenue for the second half of the year to have been greater than its revenue for the first half of the year, Marvelo must have sold a greater quantity of products during the second half of the year than it sold during the first half of the year.After all, if during the second half of the year, Marvelo’s prices had been lower, and Marvelo had not sold a greater quantity of products that it sold during the first half of the year, then Marvelo’s revenue would have been lower during the second half.

Of course, it does not have to be the case that Marvelo sold greater quantities ofallof its products during the second half of the year.It could have been the case that Marvelo achieved the increase in revenue by selling during the second half of the year more ofoneof its products than it sold during the first half of the year.
So, since we know that, during the second half of the year,
- Marvelo’s revenue was greater.
- Marvelo’s prices were lower.
Then we also know that, during the second half of the year,
- Marvelo sold more of at least one of its products during the second half of the year than Marvelo sold of that product during the first half of the year.
(D) Marvelo will not become profitable unless it succeeds in reducing the costs that it incurs in producing the products that it sells.

Notice that the passage does not provide information regarding why Marvelo was not profitable or under which circumstances Marvelo would become profitable.Thus, we cannot infer that Marvelo will not become profitable unless it succeeds in reducing the costs that it incurs in producing the products that it sells.It could very well be that there is another way in which Marvelo could become profitable.
(E) Marvelo sold in the second half of the year greater quantities of all of its products than it sold in the first half of the year.

We know that “all products that Marvelo sold during the second half of the year were sold at prices lower than those at which Marvelo sold the same products during the first half of the year.” We also know that “Marvelo’s revenue from product sales for the second half of the year was greater than its revenue for the first half.”

As outlined in the explanation of choice (C), if all of the products sold for less in the second half of the year, yet the revenue for the second half of the year was greater, then it must be true that Marvelo sold more of at least one of its products during the second half of the year than Marvelo sold of that product during the first half of the year.

Notice, however, that it need not be true that Marvelo sold in the second half of the year greater quantities of ALL of its products than it sold in the first half of the year.As long as ONE or SOME of the products were sold in greater quantities in the second half of the year than in the first half of the year, Marvelo’s revenue for the second half of the year could have been greater than its revenue for the first half.

question.text: The floor space of the average apartment in Percingham is half that of the average house in Percingham.Meanwhile, household energy use per square foot of floor space of apartments in Percingham is less than household energy use per square foot of floor space of houses in Percingham.However, household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person who lives in a house.If the information given above is true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: The average number of people living in a house in Percingham is more than twice the average number living in an apartment in Percingham.
People who live in houses in Percingham are more concerned about the environmental effects of energy use than are people who live in apartments in Percingham.
The sources of energy used in apartments in Percingham are more expensive than the sources of energy used in houses in Percingham.
Most energy use in apartment buildings in Percingham is regulated by the residents of the buildings rather than by the owners of the buildings.
People who live in apartments in Percingham use more energy consuming appliances than are used by people living in houses in Percingham.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: The floor space of the average apartment in Percingham is half that of the average house in Percingham.
Fact 2: Household energy use per square foot of floor space of apartments in Percingham is less than household energy use per square foot of floor space of houses in Percingham.
Fact 3: Household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person living in a house.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) The average number of people living in a house in Percingham is more than twice the average number living in an apartment in Percingham.
The passage tells us that floor space of the average apartment in Percingham is half that of the average house in Percingham.
It also says that the household energy use per square foot of floor space of apartments in Percingham is less than household energy use per square foot of floor space of houses in Percingham.
Therefore, it must be true that the total household energy use for the average apartment is less than half the total household energy use of the average house.
In other words, half the floor space using less than half the energy per square foot of floor space results in use of less than half the total energy.
So, we have the following:
Household Energy Use Per Apartment < 1/2 x Household Energy Use Per House
What that also means, of course, is that the household energy use per house is more than twice the household energy use per apartment.
So, we have the following:
2 x Household Energy Use Per Apartment < Household Energy Use Per House
The passage also says that household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person living in a house.
Of course, household energy use per person is the following:
Household Energy Use Per Person = Household Energy Use / People in Household
So, household energy use per person depends on how much energy the household uses and how many people are in the household.
For a given amount of energy used by a household, the more people in the household, the lower the energy use per person of that household.

So, how could it be that, even though the average apartment uses less than half the energy used by the average house, household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person living in a house?
What must be the case is that, as this choice says, the average number of people living in a house in Percingham is more than twice the average number living in an apartment in Percingham.

If, on average, more than twice as many people live in a house, then that all that energy that a house household uses, which is more than twice what an apartment household uses, is distributed over more than twice as many people.
In that way, the energy use per person living in a house could be less than the energy use per person living in an apartment.
If that is hard to see, consider the following example scenario:
Average household energy use of an apartment: 150 units.
Average household energy use of a house: 320 units (more than twice that of the apartment)
Average number of people in apartment: 1.5 (Some have 1; others have 2.)
Average people in house: 4 (more than twice as many as are in apartment)
Energy use per person in apartment: 150/1.5 = 100 units per person
Energy use per person in house: 320/4 = 80 units per person
We can see that, since there are more than twice as many people in the house, the greater household energy use of the house is also less energy use per person in the house.

Given what the passage says, having more than twice as many people per house is the only way in which the energy use per person living in a house could be lower than the energy use per person living in an apartment.
So, given what the passage says, what this choice says must be true.
(B) People who live in houses in Percingham are more concerned about the environmental effects of energy use than are people who live in apartments in Percingham.

The passage does not give us information on whether people who live in houses in Percingham are or are not more concerned about the environmental effects of energy use than are people who live in apartments in Percingham.

While the passage does say that household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person who lives in a house, there is no clear reason to believe that the reason for the difference in energy use per person is that people who live in apartments are less concerned about the environmental effects of energy use than are people who live in houses.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The sources of energy used in apartments in Percingham are more expensive than the sources of energy used in houses in Percingham.
Notice that the passage is about units of energy, not the cost of energy.The passage does not tell us anything about how expensive the energy is.
(D) Most energy use in apartment buildings in Percingham is regulated by the residents of the buildings rather than by the owners of the buildings.
The passage does not give us information on who regulates most energy use in apartment buildings in Percingham.The passage provides information on only how much energy is used.

Going from the fact that household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person who lives in a house to the conclusion that most energy use in apartment buildings in Percingham is regulated by the residents of the buildings is taking a leap greater than the one Evel Knievel took when he jumped over 14 Greyhound buses.
(E) People who live in apartments in Percingham use more energy consuming appliances than are used by people living in houses in Percingham.

This choice could be tempting, because the passage says that household energy use per person who lives in an apartment in Percingham is greater than household energy use per person who lives in a house.Given that information, we might be tempted to decide that the reason why the energy use per person who lives in an apartment is higher is that people who live in apartments use more energy consuming appliances than are used by people who live in houses.

However, the truth is that that story goes way beyond what is supported by the passage.The passage does not say anything that clearly supports any conclusion about the appliance use of people living in Percingham.

question.text: In remote areas on Earth, satellite phones, phones that utilize electromagnetic waves broadcast in straight line paths to and from satellites orbiting in space 22,300 miles above Earth’s equator, are among the few long distance communication options available.All electromagnetic waves travel at a speed of about 186,282 miles per second through space but travel more slowly once they penetrate Earth’s dense atmosphere.Although making phone calls from satellite phones is very expensive, these devices help ensure that communication is possible almost anywhere on the globe, even when no cellular phone service is available.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: Satellite phones are more commonly used in areas without cellular phone service than in areas with cellular phone service.
In the most remote areas on Earth, making phone calls would be impossible without the use of satellite phones.
Without satellite phones, rescue workers who assist after natural disasters that have taken down cellular service would not be able to make phone calls.
If an electromagnetic wave traveled between two objects in under one-tenth of a second, those objects were not a satellite phone on Earth and a satellite 22,300 miles above Earth.
Earth’s dense atmosphere limits the range over which a user of a satellite phone can communicate with people in non-remote locations.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:

Fact 1: In remote areas on Earth, satellite phones, phones that utilize electromagnetic waves broadcast in straight line paths to and from satellites orbiting in space 22,300 miles above Earth’s equator, are among the few long-distance communication options available.
Fact 2: All electromagnetic waves travel at a speed of about 186,282 miles per second through space but travel more slowly once they penetrate Earth’s dense atmosphere.

Fact 3: Although making phone calls from satellite phones is very expensive, these devices help ensure that communication is possible almost anywhere on the globe, even when no cellular phone service is available.
(A) Satellite phones are more commonly used in areas without cellular phone service than in areas with cellular phone service.

This choice is seductive because what it says is very likely true in the real world.However, in answering an inference question, we must stick to what’s been provided to us by the passage.In other words, we are not seeking something that is true in reality.We are seeking something that must be true given what the passage says.
The passage does not provide information on how commonly satellite phones are used in areas without cellular phone service or in areas with cellular phone service.
(B) In the most remote areas on Earth, making phone calls would be impossible without the use of satellite phones.

The passage never discusses what options are available for making phone calls in the “most remote areas on Earth.” For all we know, there are other options available.What this answer choice says sounds plausible because of what the passage tells us about satellite phones and how they are used, but what it says is not inferable.

Don’t fall for the time-worn trap of presenting us with information that seems plausible or jives with what we know about reality yet is not inferable from the information presented in the passage.Remember, an inference must be fully supported by the passage.
(C) Without satellite phones, rescue workers who assist after natural disasters that have taken down cellular service would not be able to make phone calls.
There are a few reasons why what this answer choice says is not inferable.

First, where are these natural disasters occurring? Don’t natural disasters, such as hurricanes, occur in, for example, Miami? Miami is not a remote area of Earth.There could be, and most likely are, numerous other ways rescue workers could make phone calls in many areas.

Second, even if these natural disasters were in remote areas of Earth, the passage says: “In remote areas on Earth, satellite phones are among the few long-distance communication options available.” So, we see that there are other communication options available.We must remember to pay close attention to each piece of information presented to us in the passage or in the answer choices.
YOUR CHOICE(D) If an electromagnetic wave traveled between two objects in under one-tenth of a second, those objects were not a satellite phone on Earth and a satellite 22,300 miles above Earth.

The passage says that satellite phones utilize electromagnetic waves broadcast in straight line paths to and from satellites orbiting in space 22,300 miles above Earth’s equator, and that all electromagnetic waves travel at a speed of about 186,282 miles per second through space but travel more slowly once they penetrate Earth’s dense atmosphere.
Given those facts, we can see that an electromagnetic wave traveling between a satellite phone on Earth and a satellite in space would take the following:
22,300 miles / 186,282 miles per second = about 22/186 or a little over 1/10 of a second
Therefore, if an electromagnetic wave traveled between two objects in under one-tenth of a second, those objects were “not” a satellite phone on Earth and a satellite 22,300 miles above Earth.

Notice that this inference is not the conclusion of the argument, nor is it the main point of the argument, nor is it even – at least on our opinion – an interesting facet of the argument.However, an inference need not be any of those things; an inference simply must be true.
(E) Earth’s dense atmosphere limits the range over which a user of a satellite phone can communicate with people in non-remote locations.
The passage tells us that “all electromagnetic waves travel at a speed of about 186,282 miles per second through space but travel more slowly once they penetrate Earth’s dense atmosphere.”
However, the passage never says that that slowing of the electromagnetic waves affects the range over which a user of a satellite phone can communicate with people in non-remote locations.

If you chose this answer as correct, you likely assumed that, because the waves slow once they penetrate Earth’s dense atmosphere, it must also be true that that slowing limits the range over which a user of a satellite phone can communicate with people in non-remote locations.
Of course, we don’t have information to support that idea.

question.text: In 2014, the Beckford Automobile Company had 1,500 employees who worked for a total of 300,000 employee days and produced 8,000 automobiles.During the same year, the Fiorello Automobile Company had 2,000 employees who worked for a total of 390,000 employee days and produced 12,000 automobiles.Which of the following can be properly concluded about the performance of the two companies in 2014 from the information provided by the statements above?

answers.text: On average, each Fiorello Automobile Company employee was more productive in terms of cars produced in a given amount of time than each Beckford Automobile Company employee.
The average employee income at the Beckford Automobile Company exceeded the average employee income at the Fiorello Automobile Company.
The cars produced by the Fiorello Automobile Company were lower in quality than the cars produced by the Beckford Automobile Company.
Most employees at the Beckford Automobile Company worked for more days than most employees at the Fiorello Automobile Company worked for.
Total revenue from selling cars of the Fiorello Automobile Company exceeded the total revenue from selling cars of the Beckford Automobile Company.

explanation_parts.text: YOUR CHOICE(A) On average, each Fiorello Automobile Company employee was more productive in terms of cars produced in a given amount of time than each Beckford Automobile Company employee.

Notice that Beckford produced 8,000 automobiles with 300,000 employee days, whereas Fiorello produced 12,000 automobiles with 390,000 employee days.In other words, Fiorello produced 50 percent more automobiles with only 30 percent more employee days.

Therefore, it must be true that, on average, each Fiorello Automobile Company employee was more productive in terms of cars produced in a given amount of time than each Beckford Automobile Company employee.
(B) The average employee income at the Beckford Automobile Company exceeded the average employee income at the Fiorello Automobile Company.
This choice could be tempting because of the following facts:
Beckford: 300,000 Employee Days / 1,500 Employees = 200 Days per Employee
Fiorello: 390,000 Employee Days / 2,000 Employees = 195 Days per Employee
We can see that, on average, the employees at Beckford worked for more days in 2014 than employees at Fiorello worked.

As a result of the foregoing, we might be tempted to decide that we can infer what this choice says, that the average employee income at the Beckford Automobile Company exceeded the average employee income at the Fiorello Automobile Company.

Notice, however, that the passage provides no information on how the employees at each company are paid, how much they are paid per day, or anything else that we can use to determine what their incomes were.The number of days an employee works is not information sufficient for determining what that employee’s income is, of course.
So, the passage does not clearly support what this choice says about incomes.
We have to be careful not to jump to conclusions from information that does not really support those conclusions.
(C) The cars produced by the Fiorello Automobile Company were lower in quality than the cars produced by the Beckford Automobile Company.

Notice that the passage never provides us with information regarding the quality of the cars produced.We have only information on the numbers of cars produced and how many employees and days producing those cars took.Therefore, we can’t make this inference.
We have to be careful not to take information provided and use it to make up a story that that information does not clearly support.
(D) Most employees at the Beckford Automobile Company worked for more days than most employees at the Fiorello Automobile Company worked for.
This choice could be tempting because of the following facts:
Beckford: 300,000 Employee Days / 1,500 Employees = 200 Days per Employee
Fiorello: 390,000 Employee Days / 2,000 Employees = 195 Days per Employee
We can see that, on average, the employees at Beckford worked for more days in 2014 than employees at Fiorello worked.

As a result of the foregoing, we might be tempted to decide that we can infer what this choice says, that most employees at the Beckford Automobile Company worked for more days than most employees at the Fiorello Automobile Company worked for.
However, the truth is that we cannot draw that inference from information on average days worked.

Remember, there are many ways to arrive at averages.In this case, the higher average days worked of Beckford employees does not have to result frommostBeckford employees having worked for more days thanmostemployees at the Fiorello Automobile Company worked for.

It could have been the case that most Beckford employees did not work for as many days as most Fiorello employees worked for, butsomeBeckford employees worked for so many days that they drove the average days worked by Beckford employees higher than the average days worked by Fiorello employees.
So, we can see that the aggregate information provided by the passage does not clearly support what this choice says.
(E) Total revenue from selling cars of the Fiorello Automobile Company exceeded the total revenue from selling cars of the Beckford Automobile Company.

The choice could be tempting, because the passage says that the Fiorello Automobile Company sold 12,000 cars in 2014, while the Beckford Automobile Company sold 8,000 cars.Given that information, we might be tempted to decide that, as this choice says, total revenue from selling cars of the Fiorello Automobile Company exceeded the total revenue from selling cars of the Beckford Automobile Company.
However, the fact that Fiorello sold more cars than Beckford sold does not necessarily mean that Fiorello’s total revenue exceeded Beckford’s total revenue.

A company’s revenue is affected not only by volume of products sold but also by the prices of products sold, and the passage provides no information on the prices at which the companies sold the cars they sold.So, for all we know from what the passage says, Beckford’s prices and revenue greatly exceeded those of Fiorello.
Therefore, we cannot infer what this choice says.

question.text: Cacao beans, from which come the main ingredients in chocolate, are produced by cacao trees.90 percent of all cacao trees are grown on small family farms between 2 and 5 hectares in size.Despite growing demand and higher global chocolate prices, farmers operating such farms have experienced declining incomes as more of the profits from chocolate go to chocolate wholesale companies, which purchase cacao beans, at decreasing prices, from these farmers.The situation has raised alarm as some farmers have been resorting to unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing, to grow enough cacao trees to earn sufficient income.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: Farmers who grow cacao trees on small family farms between 2 and 5 hectares in size have experienced an increase in expenses in recent years.
The ratios of some global chocolate prices per pound to the prices received by some cacao farmers per pound of beans have increased.
As global chocolate prices increase, most cacao farmers will resort to unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing.
Unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing, allow cacao farmers to increase the number of cacao trees that they grow without the farmers increasing their expenses.
No more than 10 percent of all cacao beans purchased by chocolate wholesale companies come from cacao farms larger than 5 hectares in size.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Cacao beans, from which come the main ingredients in chocolate, are produced by cacao trees.
Fact 2: 90 percent of all cacao trees are grown on small family farms between 2 and 5 hectares in size.

Fact 3: Despite growing demand and higher global chocolate prices, farmers operating such farms have experienced declining incomes as more of the profits from chocolate go to chocolate wholesale companies, which purchase cacao beans, at decreasing prices, from these farmers.
Fact 4: The situation has raised alarm as some farmers have been resorting to unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing, to grow enough cacao trees to earn sufficient income.
(A) Farmers who grow cacao trees on small family farms between 2 and 5 hectares in size have experienced an increase in expenses in recent years.
We can’t infer that farmers who grow cacao trees on small family farms between 2 and 5 hectares in size have experienced an increase in expenses in recent years.
If you chose this answer as correct, you might have made the unwarranted assumption that because “farmers operating such farms have experienced declining incomes,” their expenses must have increased.

However, the passage clearly says that the farmer’s incomes have gone down because more of their profits from chocolate go to chocolate wholesale companies which purchase cacao beans, at decreasing prices, from these farmers.There is a big difference between income going down because of a farmer’s earning a lower price for a product and income going down because of an increase in expenses.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The ratios of some global chocolate prices per pound to the prices received by some cacao farmers per pound of beans have increased.
The ratio of chocolate prices per pound to prices received by cacao farmers per pound of beans is basically the following:
Price Per Pound of Chocolate / Price Per Pound of Beans
So, let’s see what the passage says about the price of chocolate and the price of beans.
Regarding the price of chocolate, the passage says, “higher global chocolate prices.”
Regarding the price of beans, the passage says, “wholesale companies, which purchase cacao beans, at decreasing prices.”
Of course, if the prices of chocolate are “higher,” and if the prices of beans are “decreasing,” the ratio Price Per Pound of Chocolate / Price Per Pound of Beans must be increasing.

Now, granted, the passage does not say that all prices of all forms of chocolate are “higher” or that the prices that all cacao farmers receive for beans are “decreasing.” At the same time, neither does this choice.This choice is about the ratios ofsomeglobal chocolate prices per pound to the prices received bysomecacao farmers per pound of beans.

Since we can infer from what the passage says that at least some chocolate prices have increased and that at least some farmers have experienced decreases in the prices they receive for cacao beans, we can infer what this choice says.
(C) As global chocolate prices increase, most cacao farmers will resort to unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing.
We can’t infer that, as global chocolate prices increase, most cacao farmers will resort to unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing.

Notice that the passage clearly says, “assomefarmers resort to unsustainable farming techniques.” Of course, there is a big difference between the words “some” and “most.” “Some farmers” could be a very small percentage of the farmers.Thus, what this choice says does not fully support the prediction that “most cacao farmers will resort to unsustainable farming techniques.”
As always, we must pay close attention to the details of the passage and of the answer choices.
(D) Unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing, allow cacao farmers to increase the number of cacao trees that they grow without the farmers increasing their expenses.

We can’t infer that “Unsustainable farming techniques, such as rain-forest clearing, allow cacao farmers to increase the number of cacao trees that they grow without the farmers increasing their expenses.”

Notice that the passage says, “The situation has raised alarm as some farmers resort to unsustainable farming techniques, such as rainforest clearing, to grow enough cacao trees to earn sufficient income.”

We see that we are not provided with any information regarding how unsustainable farming techniques affect a farmer’s expenses.It could very well be that a farmer’s use of these techniques does result in an increase in that farmer’s expenses.
Remember, we’re not seeking something that could very well be true in reality.We’re seeking something that must be true given the information presented in the passage.
(E) No more than 10 percent of all cacao beans purchased by chocolate wholesale companies come from cacao farms larger than 5 hectares in size.

We can’t infer that no more than 10 percent of all cacao beans purchased by chocolate wholesale companies come from cacao farms larger than 5 hectares in size.Notice that the passage says that “90 percent of allcacao treesare grown on small family farms between 2 and 5 hectares in size.” Of course, there is a big difference between “90 percent of all cacao trees” and “10 percent of all cacao beans.”

As you can imagine, each cacao tree produces more than one cacao bean, and it could very well be that some cacao trees produce many more cacao beans than are produced by other cacao trees.So, the farms that have 90 percent of the cacao trees may not produce 90 percent of the cacao beans purchased by wholesalers.

Furthermore, for all we know, the wholesalers prefer to buy from larger farms, or farmers who own small cacao farms consume most of their crops themselves.It doesn’t have to be the case that the farms with 90 percent of the trees sell all the beans that those trees produce to wholesalers.

The expressions “cacao trees” and “cacao beans purchased by chocolate wholesale companies” are not synonymous.As always, we must pay close attention to all of the details provided in the passage and in the answer choices.

question.text: The number of rent-controlled housing units in Millville has not increased since the city council relaxed rent control regulations eleven years ago so that landlords would better maintain the housing stock in Millville.Since the point in time when rent control regulations in Millville were relaxed, the percentage change in the number of housing units in Millville has matched the percentage change in the population of Millville.If the information provided by the statements above is accurate, which of the following can be properly concluded from it?

answers.text: The percentage of housing units in Millville that are rent-controlled has decreased from the level at which it was eleven years ago.
The average rent for a housing unit in Millville is higher today that it was eleven years ago.
Had the city council of Millville not relaxed rent control regulations, there would now be a shortage of housing in Millville.
The number of housing units per resident in Millville is the same as it was when the city council relaxed rent control regulations.
In Millville, the housing units that are not rent-controlled are generally better maintained than the housing units that are rent-controlled.

explanation_parts.text: (A) The percentage of housing units in Millville that are rent-controlled has decreased from the level at which it was eleven years ago.
The percentage of housing units in Millville that are rent controlled equals the following:
Number of Rent Controlled Housing Units / Total Number of Housing Units

So, to determine whether the percentage of housing units in Millville that are rent-controlled has decreased from the level at which it was eleven years ago, we would need to have information on what has happened with both the number of rent-controlled units and the total number of units.
We do have the information that the number of rent-controlled housing units in Millville has not increased since the city council relaxed rent control regulations eleven years ago.

However, the only information the passage provides about the total number of housing units is that the percentage change in the number of housing units in Millville has matched the percentage change in the population of Millville, and we don’t know what the percentage change in the population of Millville has been.
Since the population of Millville could have increased, decreased, or remained the same, the total number of housing units in Millville could have increased, decreased, or remained the same.
So, the passage does not provide information sufficient to for determining that the percentage of housing units that are rent-controlled has decreased.
(B) The average rent for a housing unit in Millville is higher today that it was eleven years ago.

The passage provides no information on average rents or changes in rent levels.So, while it may be likely that, since the city council of Millville relaxed rent control regulations, the average rent for a housing unit in Millville is higher today that it was eleven years ago, the passage does not support that conclusion.
For all we know from what the passage says, factors other than rent control regulations caused rents to remain the same or even to decrease.
(C) Had the city council of Millville not relaxed rent control regulations, there would now be a shortage of housing in Millville.
Notice that nothing in the passage would lead us to infer that, had the city council of Millville not relaxed rent control regulations, there would now be a shortage of housing in Millville.

While the passage provides information on what happenedafterrent control regulations were relaxed – the percentage change in the number of housing units in Millville has matched the percentage change in the population of Millville – the passage does not indicate that that pattern has existedbecauserent control regulations were relaxed.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The number of housing units per resident in Millville is the same as it was when the city council relaxed rent control regulations.
Notice that we are told that “the percentage change in the number of housing units in Millville has matched the percentage change in the population of Millville.”

While we are not told how many housing units there are in Millville or what the population if Millville is, we can tell from the above statement that these two numbers have moved together.When one has gone up or down by a certain percentage, the other has as well.
Therefore, it must be true that the number of housing units per resident in Millville is the same as it was when the city council relaxed rent control regulations.
To see why, let’s consider a simple example.
Let’s start off with the following:
Number of Housing Units: 100
Population: 200
Number of Housing Units per Resident: 0.5
Now, let’s increase both the number of housing units and the number of residents by 50 percent.
We get the following:
Number of Housing Units: 150
Population: 300
Number of Housing Units per Resident: 0.5
We can see that, because both the number of housing units and the population increased by 50 percent, the number of housing units per resident remained the same.
As long as both the number of housing units and the population increase or decrease by the same percentage, the number of housing units per resident will remain the same.
(E) In Millville, the housing units that are not rent-controlled are generally better maintained than the housing units that are rent-controlled.

Of course, what this answer choices says is certainly plausible, and it matches what has actually occurred in real-world cities in which some housing is rent-controlled.However, we are not seeking something plausible or something that matches what has actually occurred; we’re seeking an inference fully supported by what the passage says.

While the passage does say that the Millville city council relaxed rent control regulations so that landlords would better maintain the housing stock in Millville, that statement does not fully support any conclusion about how well maintained any part of the housing stock is currently.For all we know from what the passage says, landlords in Millville maintain rent-controlled units just as well as they maintain other units.

question.text: Because of their high metabolisms, for the first weeks of their lives, baby hummingbirds, which cannot feed themselves, rely completely on their mothers to feed them every 15 minutes for the 14 consecutive hours of the day during which they are awake and will starve to death within an hour of missing a feeding.However, when food is not available, or when they deem necessary, adult hummingbirds can enter a torpid state of relatively low metabolism, decreasing their heart rates from 1,200 beats per minute to about 50 beats per minute.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: A birdwatcher watching a hummingbird nest during some newborn babies’ 14 waking hours and claiming that they were crying because their mother had been away for hours would be incorrect.
If a baby hummingbird goes for over 15 minutes without a meal, it will enter a reduced metabolism torpid state in order to conserve precious energy until a meal arrives.
Upon becoming more self-sufficient, a baby hummingbird will not find it necessary to utilize torpid states because its energetic needs can be met through hunting and feeding itself.
When not in a torpid state, adult hummingbirds have caloric needs much greater than those of baby hummingbirds.
When she has babies, a mother hummingbird will spend most of her time in the nest, watching over them to ensure that they are well cared for.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:

Fact 1: Because of their high metabolisms, for the first weeks of their lives, baby hummingbirds, which cannot feed themselves, rely completely on their mothers to feed them every 15 minutes for the 14 consecutive hours of the day during which they are awake and will starve to death within an hour of missing a feeding.

Fact 2: When food is not available, or when they deem necessary, adult hummingbirds can enter a torpid state of relatively low metabolism, decreasing their heart rates from 1,200 beats per minute to about 50 beats per minute.

CORRECT ANSWER(A) A birdwatcher watching a hummingbird nest during some newborn babies’ 14 waking hours and claiming that they were crying because their mother had been away for hours would be incorrect.

Notice that the passage tells us that “baby hummingbirds, which cannot feed themselves, rely completely on their mothers to feed them every15 minutes for the 14 consecutive hours of the day during which they are awake and will starve to death within an hour of missing a feeding.”

Therefore, it must be true that a birdwatcher watching a hummingbird nest during some newborn babies’ 14 waking hours and claiming that they were crying because their mother had been away for hours would be incorrect.After all, we are told that these babieswill starve to death within an hour of missing a feeding.So, it cannot be correct that baby hummingbirds “were crying because their mother had been away forhours.”
(B) If a baby hummingbird goes for over 15 minutes without a meal, it will enter a reduced metabolism torpid state in order to conserve precious energy until a meal arrives.

Notice that the passage says, “when food is not available, or when they deem necessary,adult hummingbirdscan enter a torpid state of relatively low metabolism, decreasing their heart rates from 1,200 beats per minute to about 50 beats per minute.”
The fact that “adult hummingbirds” can enter a torpid state does not indicate that baby hummingbirds can do so also.
Of course, at all times, we must pay attention to each of the details of the passage and of the answer choices.
If you chose this answer as correct, you likely mixed up the facts pertaining to adult hummingbirds and baby hummingbirds.
(C) Upon becoming more self-sufficient, a baby hummingbird will not find it necessary to utilize torpid states because its energetic needs can be met through hunting and feeding itself.

What does it mean to become “more self-sufficient?” Notice that the passage never uses or defines this criterion.In fact, the passage seems to contradict what this answer choice says by saying that “…when food is not available, or when they deem necessary,adult hummingbirdscan enter a torpid state of relatively low metabolism, decreasing their heart rates from 1,200 beats per minute to about 50 beats per minute.”

It would seem logical that a baby hummingbird learning to be able to meet its energetic needs by itself would also be learning to do other things that adult hummingbirds do, such as enter a torpid state.

Thus, we would expect that, contrary to what this choice says, a hummingbird would still find it necessary to utilize torpid states even once its energetic needs could be met through hunting and feeding itself.
(D) When not in a torpid state, adult hummingbirds have caloric needs much greater than those of baby hummingbirds.

Although what this answer choice says may seem likely to be true in reality, the passage does not present us with a comparison between the energy needs of adults and those of babies.For all we know from what the passage says, babies require considerably more energy than adults.
Remember, we are seeking an answer that is clearly supported by what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(E) When she has babies, a mother hummingbird will spend most of her time in the nest, watching over them to ensure that they are well cared for.

The passage does not present us with information on the amount of time that a mother hummingbird will spend in the nest, caring for her babies.Yes, the passage says that the babies must be fed every 15 minutes, but it’s possible that the mother bird spends, for instance, two minutes out of every fifteen in the nest.
If you choose this answer as correct, you likely did so because what it says is probably true in reality and therefore appealed to your sensibilities.
However, we must remember that, when solving Inference questions, we are seeking an answer that must be true given what the passage says, not an answer that is likely true in reality.

question.text: In order to maximize performance, a company must hire from the pool of available people those most qualified to work in the positions for which the company is hiring.However, in Wedania, many employers have been engaging in discriminatory hiring, defined as hiring, on the basis of characteristics other than degree of qualification for positions, applicants other than those most qualified.Some Wedanian legislators have proposed banning discriminatory hiring in Wedania.If the statements above are true, which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn on the basis of them?

answers.text: In Wedania, only those legislators not requiring the support of business leaders in order to be reelected would support a ban on discriminatory hiring.
Many employers in Wedania are not aware that discriminatory hiring results in their not hiring the people most qualified to work in the positions for which the employers are hiring.
Even if discriminatory hiring were to be banned in Wedania, some companies would continue to engage in discriminatory hiring.
Successful enforcement of a ban on discriminatory hiring in Wedania would result in more companies in Wedania maximizing performance than currently do so.
Any employer engaging in discriminatory hiring in Wedania is not concerned with maximizing company performance.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: In order to maximize performance, a company must hire from the pool of available people those most qualified to work in the positions for which the company is hiring.

Fact 2: In Wedania, many employers have been engaging in discriminatory hiring, defined as hiring, on the basis of characteristics other than degree of qualification for positions, applicants other than those most qualified.
Fact 3: Some Wedanian legislators have proposed banning discriminatory hiring in Wedania.
(A) In Wedania, only those legislators not requiring the support of business leaders in order to be reelected would support a ban on discriminatory hiring.
This answer is seductive because what it says matches things that go on in real life.However, we must remain vigilant to separate what goes on in real life and what is supported by the passage.

We can’t infer that, in Wedania, only those legislators not requiring the support of business leaders in order to be reelected would support a ban on discriminatory hiring, because the passage does not provide any information on legislators other than that some have proposed banning discriminatory hiring.
It’s very important NOT to make up stories that are not supported by the passage.
(B) Many employers in Wedania are not aware that discriminatory hiring results in their not hiring the people most qualified to work in the positions for which the employers are hiring.

This choice could be tempting because one might be tempted to believe that employers would not engage in discriminatory hiring if they were aware that doing so would result in their not hiring the people most qualified to work in the positions for which the employers are hiring.

However, we are not given any facts about what employers in Wedania are or are not aware of, and it’s entirely possible that employers who engage in discriminatory hiring are aware that discriminatory hiring results in their not hiring the people most qualified to work in the positions for which the employers are hiring.
So, we can’t infer what this choice says.

Perhaps the phenomenon mentioned in this answer choice is true in reality of employers who engage in discriminatory hiring, but, as always, we can’t be seduced by statements that match reality yet are not supported by the passage.
(C) Even if discriminatory hiring were to be banned in Wedania, some companies would continue to engage in discriminatory hiring.
This answer is seductive because what it says matches things that go on in real life.However, we must remain vigilant to separate what goes on in real life and what is supported by the passage.

We can’t infer that, even if discriminatory hiring were to be banned in Wedania, some companies would continue to engage in discriminatory hiring, because the passage provides no information clearly indicating what companies would do if discriminatory hiring were banned.
For all we know from what the passage says, if discriminatory hiring were banned in Wedania, all companies that are currently engaging in discriminatory hiring would cease doing so immediately.
It’s very important to NOT make up stories that are not supported by the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Successful enforcement of a ban on discriminatory hiring in Wedania would result in more companies in Wedania maximizing performance than currently do so.

The passage says that, in order to maximize performance, a company must hire from the pool of available people those most qualified to work in the positions for which the company is hiring.It also says that, many employers in Wedania have been engaging in discriminatory hiring, which is hiring on the basis of characteristics other than degree of qualification for positions, applicants other than those most qualified.

So, given those two facts, we can infer that many companies in Wedania are not maximizing performance, because rather than hiring the most qualified people, they are engaging in discriminatory hiring.

Of course, if a ban on discriminatory hiring were successfully enforced in Wedania, companies that are currently engaging in discriminatory hiring and, therefore, are not maximizing performance, would cease engaging in discriminatory hiring and, therefore, maximize performance.
Thus, if a ban on discriminatory hiring were successfully enforced, more companies in Wedania would maximize performance than currently do so.
(E) Any employer engaging in discriminatory hiring in Wedania is not concerned with maximizing company performance.

We can’t infer that any employer engaging in discriminatory hiring in Wedania is not concerned with maximizing company performance.The passage never tells us what employers in Wedania are or are not concerned with.

It could be true that many employers engaging in discriminatory hiring in Wedania are concerned with maximizing company performance, but they just don’t understand that discriminatory hiring can be a drag on performance.

question.text: Roasting coffee beans at high altitudes, those above 3,000 feet, must occur at relatively low temperatures, for the prevention of scorching, which occurs when beans are roasted at too high a temperature, and must occur for relatively short periods of time, for the prevention of baking, which occurs when beans are roasted for too long.Scorching and baking both invariably cause lack of flavor development in coffee beans, which results in an undesirable wild and woody flavor in coffee produced from those beans.Research suggests that, for the achievement of optimal coffee flavor, coffee beans should be roasted at the altitude at which they were grown.Which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn from the information above?

answers.text: If coffee has a wild and woody flavor, then the beans from which the coffee was made were either scorched or baked.
Coffee beans that are grown at a higher altitude produce coffee of higher quality than is produced by those grown at a lower altitude.
If coffee has a desirable flavor, the coffee beans from which it was produced were neither scorched nor baked.
If a coffee lacks flavor development, it was roasted at an altitude greater than 3,000 feet.
If coffee beans were roasted at the altitude at which they were grown, the coffee they produce will be of optimal flavor.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:

Fact 1: Roasting coffee beans at high altitudes, those above 3,000 feet, must occur at relatively low temperatures, for the prevention of scorching, which occurs when beans are roasted at too high a temperature, and for relatively short periods of time, for the prevention of baking, which occurs when beans are roasted for too long.
Fact 2: Scorching and baking both invariably cause lack of flavor development in coffee beans, which results in an undesirable wild and woody flavor in coffee produced from those beans.
Fact 3: Research suggests that, for the achievement of optimal coffee flavor, coffee beans should be roasted at the altitude at which they were grown.
YOUR CHOICE(A) If coffee has a wild and woody flavor, then the beans from which the coffee was made were either scorched or baked.

Notice that the passage says that “Scorching and baking both invariably cause lack of flavor development in coffee beans, which results in an undesirable wild and woody flavor in coffee produced from those beans.”

Of course, that sentence does not allow us to infer that, if coffee has a wild and woody flavor, then the beans from which the coffee was made were either scorched or baked.There could be many other reasons why coffee has a wild and woody flavor.

It’s helpful to remember that “If/then” statements do not necessarily retain their validity when reversed.For example, let’s use a simple quant example.We know that, if we have an evenly spaced set of integers, the mean of the set is the same as the median of the set.However, the fact that a set of integers has a mean equal to the median does not allow us to conclude that set of integers is evenly spaced.
(B) Coffee beans that are grown at a higher altitude produce coffee of higher quality than is produced by those grown at a lower altitude.

This choice could be tempting, because it seems similar to what the passage says, “Research suggests that,for the achievement of optimal coffee flavor, coffee beans should be roasted at the altitude at which they were grown.”
Notice, however, that the passage never offers us any information about the quality of coffee grown at high altitudes relative to the quality of coffee grown at low altitudes.
We must vigilantly pay attention to the details of the passage and of the answer choices.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) If coffee has a desirable flavor, the coffee beans from which it was produced were neither scorched nor baked.

Notice that the passage says, “Scorching and baking both invariably cause lack of flavor development in coffee beans, which results in an undesirable wild and woody flavor in coffee produced from those beans.” In other words, scorching or bakingalwayscauses an undesirable flavor.

Therefore, it must be true that, if coffee has a desirable flavor, the coffee beans from which it was produced were neither scorched nor baked.After all, if the coffee beans from which the coffee was produced were either scorched or baked, then the coffee would not have a desirable flavor.
(D) If a coffee lacks flavor development, it was roasted at an altitude greater than 3,000 feet.
It’s important to notice that the information presented in the passage does not allow us to make this claim.

While the passage provides some reasons why coffee would lack flavor development after being roasted at an altitude greater than 3,000 feet, it never says that, for instance, scorching and baking cannot occur at altitudes lower than 3000 feet.

We have to be careful not to misperceive the fact that lack of flavor developmentcanresult from roasting done at an altitude greater than 3000 feet as indicating that lack of flavor developmentcannotresult from roasting done at altitudes lower than 3000 feet.
(E) If coffee beans were roasted at the altitude at which they were grown, the coffee they produce will be of optimal flavor.
The passage says, “Research suggests that, for the achievement of optimal coffee flavor, coffee beans should be roasted at the altitude at which they were grown.”

However, does what that sentence says imply that “If coffee beans were roasted at the altitude at which they were grown, the coffee they producewillbe of optimal flavor.” No – of course it does not.After all, even if coffee beans were roasted at the altitude at which they were grown, there could be many reasons why the coffee they produce will not be of optimal flavor.For example, what if dirty swamp water is used to make the coffee? How’s that cup going to taste?

question.text: Each year, about 31 billion European honeybees are transported in boxes, each containing as many as 19,200 bees, to be used as pollinators on California’s almond farms, which cannot maximize profits by relying on wind pollination alone.Once released, the bees fly among the more than 90 million almond trees, carrying pollen to more than 2.5 trillion almond flowers, each of which produces an almond after being successfully pollinated.Biologists claim that this practice is contributing to the decline of honeybee populations in the United States.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: The use of European honeybees is the most effective way for farmers to pollinate their almond trees.
If a California almond farm has maximized its profits, it has used European honeybees as pollinators.
The use of European honeybees as pollinators will eventually lead to a global decline in bee populations.
Wind pollination is not an effective pollination method for California almond farms.
Not every European honeybee released on California almond farms pollinates only one almond flower.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:

Fact 1: Each year, about 31 billion European honeybees are transported in boxes, each containing as many as 19,200 bees, to be used as pollinators on California’s almond farms, which cannot maximize profits by relying on wind pollination alone.

Fact 2: Once released, the bees fly among the more than 90 million almond trees, carrying pollen to more than 2.5 trillion almond flowers, each of which produces an almond after being successfully pollinated.
Fact 3: Biologists claim that this practice is contributing to the decline of honeybee populations in the United States.
(A) The use of European honeybees is the most effective way for farmers to pollinate their almond trees.
The passage never says that the use of European honeybees is the most effective way for farmers to pollinate their almond trees.

We can’t assume that the fact that European honeybeesare usedindicates that the use of European honeybees is themosteffective way for farmers to pollinate their almond trees.There could be many reasons, besides effectiveness, why farmers use European honeybees.

For example, perhaps the European honeybee is one of the few types of bees that will not attack each other in boxes.Or, perhaps European honeybees are the only bees that can be legally used in such operations.
(B) If a California almond farm has maximized its profits, it has used European honeybees as pollinators.

Notice that the passage says, “California’s almond farms cannot maximize profits by relying on wind pollination alone.” However, it does not say that, if a California almond farm has maximized its profits, it has used European honeybees as pollinators.All we can infer is that, if a California almond farm has maximized profits, it has not done so by relying on wind pollination alone.
We must be careful not to over-infer.
(C) The use of European honeybees as pollinators will eventually lead to a global decline in bee populations.

Notice that the passage says, “Biologists claim that this practice is contributing to the decline of honeybee populations in the United States.” The passage does not mention what will happen to bee populations around the globe.

If you chose this choice as correct, you likely did so because you were not paying close enough attention to the passage or to what this answer choice says; you likely missed that one discusses bee population in the United States, while the other discusses global bee populations.
(D) Wind pollination is not an effective pollination method for California almond farms.

Notice that the passage says, “California’s almond farms cannot maximize profits by relying on wind pollination alone.” The passage does not say that wind pollination is not an effective pollination method for California almond farms.After all, wind pollination may be an effective pollination method on California almond farms, just not one that maximizes their profits.
As always, we must pay close attention to each detail of the passage and of the answer choices.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Not every European honeybee released on California almond farms pollinates only one almond flower.

The passage says,“about 31 billion European honeybees are released on California’s almond farms.” It also says that the bees carry pollen to “more than 2.5 trillion almond flowers.” If there are about 31 billion European honeybees and more than 2.5 trillion almond flowers, then many of the European honeybees pollinate more than one almond flower.In other words,“not every European honeybee released on California almond farms pollinates only one almond flower.” In other words, some European honeybee released on California almond farms pollinate more than one almond flower.

question.text: Whereas earlier TechnoStyle phones required the phone’s owner’s thumbprint for gaining access to the phones, last year’s model required the owner’s face.However, because in some cases the faces of identical twins were similar enough that one twin could unlock the other twin’s phone, TechnoStyle redesigned their phones so that the standard unlocking procedure now requires the owner’s thumbprint and face.As a result, no identical twins have succeeded in unlocking their twins’ latest model TechnoStyle phones.If the above is true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: Only identical twins were able to gain unauthorized access to phones of the model offered by TechnoStyle last year.
Fewer people will gain unauthorized access to the latest model phones from TechnoStyle than gained unauthorized access to previous TechnoStyle phones.
Identical twins who have gained unauthorized access to their twins’ phones have not done so by using the standard unlocking procedure if the phones were of the latest TechnoStyle model.
TechnoStyle’s latest model phones are the phones to which it is most difficult to gain unauthorized access.
No people who are identical twins share the same thumbprint with their twins.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Unlocking earlier TechnoStyle phones required the owner’s thumbprint.
Fact 2: Unlocking last year’s TechnoStyle phone required the owner’s face.
Fact 3: In some cases, identical twins could use their faces to unlock their twins’ phones that required the owners’ faces.
Fact 4: The latest model TechnoStyle phone requires both thumbprint and face.
Fact 5: No twins have been able to unlock their twins’ latest model TechnoStyle phones.

Notice, the information is very specific and tells us only about what identical twins have been able to do and have not been able to do with regards to the unlocking procedure of TechnoStyle phones only.We are not given information on other phones or on what people other than identical twins have been able to do.

In order to correctly answer this question, we have to find the answer choice that states something that must be true if what the passage says is true.A choice that is not fully supported by the statements in the passage will not be the correct answer.
(A) Only identical twins were able to gain unauthorized access to phones of the model offered by TechnoStyle last year.

What this choice says is not supported by what is said in the passage.The passage tells us only what identical twins were able to do.It does not tell us that nobody else was able to gain unauthorized access to a TechnoStyle phone.There could be ways for gaining unauthorized access to a TechnoStyle phone other than fooling the facial recognition feature.
In general, we must be careful not to over infer.
(B) Fewer people will gain unauthorized access to the latest model phones from TechnoStyle than gained unauthorized access to previous TechnoStyle phones.

While the latest model of TechnoStyle phone seems to be more secure than previous model phones since unlocking it requires both the owner’s face and the owner’s thumbprint, for all we know the latest model phone is easier to get into by means other than the standard unlocking procedure than previous phones were.For instance, the latest model could be easier to hack into than the previous model.
