Alternatively, it could be that, for some reason, more people will seek to gain unauthorized access to latest model TechnoStyle phone than have sought to gain unauthorized access to previous model TechnoStyle phones.

Since the unlocking procedure is only one variable of many determining how many people will succeed in gaining unauthorized access to a phone, information on only the unlocking procedure is insufficient for coming to a firm conclusion regarding how many people will gain unauthorized access to latest model TechnoStyle phones.

CORRECT ANSWER(C) Identical twins who have gained unauthorized access to their twins’ phones have not done so by using the standard unlocking procedure if the phones were of the latest TechnoStyle model.
The passage says that no identical twins have been able to unlock their twins’ latest model TechnoStyle phones by using the standard unlocking procedure.
Therefore, we can be sure that, as this choice says, any twins whohavegained unauthorized access to their twins’ TechnoStyle phones have not done so by using the standard unlocking procedure.
(D) TechnoStyle’s latest model phones are the phones to which it is most difficult to gain unauthorized access.

While the latest model TechnoStyle phones seem to have the most security features had by any model of TechnoStyle phone, there are many variables that go into determining the degree of difficulty of gaining unauthorized access to a phone.

Furthermore, while the security features of the latest model TechnoStyle phones seem quite robust, the passage provides no information on phones other than TecnoStyle phones, and other phones could be much more secure than any TecnoStyle phone.

Since we are only aware of one of variable, the steps necessary for unlocking the latest model TechnoStyle phone, and since we have no information at all on phones that are not TechnoStyle phones, we do not have information sufficient for determining whether the latest model TechnoStyle phones are the phones most difficult to gain unauthorized access to.
(E)Incorrect.While what this choice says may be true in reality, it is not the case that what this choice says has to be true given the information provided in the passage.

Given what the passage says, it could be that there are twins who share identical thumbprints but their faces are not similar enough for them to unlock their twins phones, or that there are twins who share identical thumbprints, but none of those twins even have TechnoStyle phones.In the latter case, even though, theoretically, such twins might be able to unlock each other’s TechnoStyle phones, that fact would not affect the truth of the statement made by the passage, which is about only twins who have TechnoStyle phones.
We have to be careful not to make the mistake of deciding that we can infer something from what the passage says just because we understand it to be true in reality.

question.text: An entire orange crop can be destroyed by cold air temperatures because, once oranges freeze, they lose their pleasant taste and texture.Counterintuitively, to prevent such damage, when air temperatures drop below freezing, farmers spray their orange trees with water, allowing a coat of ice to encapsulate the outside of the oranges for a limited time period, typically overnight.When liquid water changes to ice, which is the solid form of water, energy in the form of heat is released into the surroundings.This released heat is absorbed by the oranges themselves, allowing them to remain just above the freezing point even when air temperatures drop below freezing.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: If air temperatures drop below freezing for an extended period, oranges on trees will lose their pleasant taste and texture.
Oranges on trees that are encapsulated in ice will not freeze regardless of how long air temperatures remain below freezing.
When the outsides of wet cherries are exposed to air temperatures below zero, causing them to freeze and become encapsulated in ice, heat is released in the process.
When ice encapsulating oranges melts as air temperatures rise above freezing, heat is not removed from the oranges to drive the melting of the ice, causing their temperatures to drop.
No orange farmers experience economic losses due to damage of their orange crops caused by air temperatures’ dropping below freezing.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: An entire orange crop can be destroyed by cold air temperatures because, once oranges freeze, they lose their pleasant taste and texture.

Fact 2: Counterintuitively, to prevent such damage, when air temperatures drop below freezing, farmers spray their orange trees with water, allowing a coat of ice to encapsulate the outside of the oranges for a limited time period, typically overnight.
Fact 3: When liquid water changes to ice, which is the solid form of water, energy in form of heat is released into the surroundings.
Fact 4: This released heat is absorbed by the oranges themselves, allowing them to remain just above the freezing point even when air temperatures drop below freezing.
(A) If air temperatures drop below freezing for an extended period, oranges on trees will lose their pleasant taste and texture.
The passage says, “An entire orange crop CAN be destroyed by cold air temperatures because, once oranges freeze, they lose their pleasant taste and texture.”

However, this choice says, “If air temperatures drop below freezing for an extended period, oranges on trees WILL lose their pleasant taste and texture.” Of course, the words “can” and “will” are not interchangeable.
If you choose this answer as correct, you almost certainly did not pay close enough attention to the key details of the passage and of this answer choice.Remember that every word matters.
(B) Oranges on trees that are encapsulated in ice will not freeze regardless of how long air temperatures remain below freezing.
We don’t have information to make this inference.
In fact, Fact 2 includes “allowing a coat of ice to encapsulate the outside of the oranges for alimited time period, typically overnight.”

Notice that the passage discusses the oranges being frozen for only a limited period of time.We don’t know what will happen to these oranges if they are covered in ice for an extended period of time.For all we know, they will freeze.
We must be careful not to over-infer.
YOUR CHOICE(C) When the outsides of wet cherries are exposed to air temperatures below zero, causing them to freeze and become encapsulated in ice, heat is released in the process.
Notice that the passage tells us that,when liquid water changes to ice, which is the solid form of water, energy in form of heat is released into the surroundings.

Notice that this fact is a general rule; it is not a rule that applies to oranges only.It is a rule of physics.It is a statement of fact.Therefore, we can infer that, when the outsides of wet cherries are exposed to air temperatures below zero, causing them to freeze and become encapsulated in ice, heat is released in the process.In fact, if we put anything in in place of oranges or cherries, the inference will still hold.

If you missed choosing this answer choice, you probably reasoned (incorrectly), that cherries have absolutely nothing to do with the passage you just read, which is about oranges.We must be very careful not to rule out answers because they don’t seem connected to the passage.Some of you who are new to TTP might have even termed this answer choice “out of scope.” However, we don’t use that term at TTP because it’s very important not to use the ineffective crutch of calling an answer choice “out of scope.”
Often, correct answers seem, on initial inspection, out of scope.We must resist the urge to quickly eliminate choices that seem to be out of scope and must instead think carefully.
(D) When ice encapsulating oranges melts as air temperatures rise above freezing, heat is not removed from the oranges to drive the melting of the ice, causing their temperatures to drop.

In looking at the facts presented, we see nothing that would allow us to make this inference.The passage says nothing about what occurs when ice melts, and provides no clear reason to believe that the melting of ice on oranges does not involve removal of heat from oranges.

In fact, as a side note, what this choice describes does occur in reality, but the removal of heat from the oranges that occurs as the ice melts does not injure the oranges because the higher air temperature buffers the effects of the heat removal.
(E) No orange farmers experience economic losses due to damage of their orange crops caused by air temperatures’ dropping below freezing.
There could be multiple reasons why orange farmers experience economic losses due to damage of their orange crops caused by air temperatures’ dropping below freezing.

Yes, farmers can temporarily protect oranges by spraying them with water, but what if the roots of their orange tress freeze and their trees die? What if the freezing weather causes branches of the trees to break?
The passage does not address all possible scenarios that could result from freezing temperatures.
We must be careful not to over-infer.

question.text: The Model-M SUV has self-driving capabilities.On the highway, the SUV will completely relieve the driver of driving responsibilities.On city streets, however, the SUV will only partially relieve the driver of driving responsibilities, handling all aspects of driving the car, such as avoiding other cars, braking, negotiating obstacles, and changing lanes, with the exception that, at this time, neither the car’s cameras nor its software interpret stop signs or the red and green colors of stop lights not located on highways, and so, the driver must remain vigilant and control the SUV as necessary at city intersections.Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?

answers.text: If a Model-M SUV negotiated an obstacle without the driver’s intervention, then the SUV was driving on the highway.
With the self-driving mode engaged, a driver of a Model-M SUV traveling on the highway would not need to press the brake for the SUV to stop at a stop sign.
A driver of a Model-M SUV has a lower chance of being involved in a collision while traveling in the SUV on a highway than while traveling in it on a city street.
If an SUV stopped automatically, without a driver’s intervention, at an intersection of city streets, then that SUV was not a Model-M SUV.
If a Model-M SUV stopped at a red light at a highway intersection, then the car’s driver was controlling the SUV.

explanation_parts.text: (A) If a Model-M SUV negotiated an obstacle without the driver’s intervention, then the SUV was driving on the highway.

Notice the passage tells us that, on city streets, the SUV handles all aspects of driving the car, such as avoiding other cars, braking, negotiating obstacles, and changing lanes, with the exception that, at this time, neither the car’s cameras nor its software interpret stop signs or the red and green colors of stop lights not located on highways, and so, the driver must remain vigilant and control the SUV as necessary at city intersections.

Given that fact, we see that what this answer choice says must not be correct, because the Model-M SUV could have negotiated an obstacle without the driver’s intervention when the SUV was driving on city streets.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) With the self-driving mode engaged, a driver of a Model-M SUV traveling on the highway would not need to press the brake for the SUV to stop at a stop sign.

It must be true that with the self-driving mode engaged, a driver of a Model-M SUV traveling on the highway would not need to press the brake for the SUV to stop at a stop sign because the passage tells us that, on the highway, the SUV will completely relieve the driver of driving responsibilities.If a driver is “completely relieved” of driving responsibilities while on the highway, then it must be true that a driver of a Model-M SUV traveling on the highway would not need to press the brake for the SUV to stop at a stop sign; the vehicle would stop itself.
(C) A driver of a Model-M SUV has a lower chance of being involved in a collision while traveling in the SUV on a highway than while traveling in it on a city street.

Notice that what this answer choice says seems quite plausible in reality.After all, many automated systems do a significantly better job, a safer job, than is done by their human counterparts.Therefore, we might be tempted to believe that a driver in a Model-M SUV controlling itself on the highway is safer than a driver in Model-M SUV partly under the driver’s control in the city.

However, because this is an Inference question, we are not seeking something that is plausible; we are seeking something that is fully supported by the passage, and we have no evidence from the passage to support what this answer choice says.

For all we know, a driver of a Model-M SUV has a higher chance of being involved in a collision while traveling in the SUV on a highway than while traveling in it on a city street.Even if we were to assume that the automation is safer than a human, driving on the highway could, for some reason, still put a driver at a higher risk of being involved in a collision while traveling in the SUV.
(D) If an SUV stopped automatically, without a driver’s intervention, at an intersection of city streets, then that SUV was not a Model-M SUV.

What this answer choice says presents us with a tempting trap.The key to not picking this answer as correct is to see that, on city streets, the Model-M SUV will partially relieve the driver of driving responsibilities, handling all aspects of driving the car, such as avoiding other cars, braking, negotiating obstacles, and changing lanes, with the exception that, at this time, neither the car’s cameras nor its software interpret stop signs or the red and green colors of stop lights not located on highways, and so, the driver must remain vigilant and control the SUV as necessary at city intersections.

So, imagine this scenario: a Model-M SUV is approaching an intersection on a city street.Stopped just into the intersection is a car or another obstacle.Will the Model-M SUV stop without the driver’s assistance? Yes, it will, because we know that can respond to cars and obstacles on city streets, just not to stop signs or the red and green colors of stop lights.So, in the situation we just described, a Model-M SUV would have stopped at an intersection on a city street without the driver’s intervention.
Therefore, we see that what this answer choice says is not an inference.
YOUR CHOICE(E) If a Model-M SUV stopped at a red light at a highway intersection, then the car’s driver was controlling the SUV.
Notice that we’re told “The Model-M SUV has self-driving capabilities.On the highway, the SUV will completely relieve the driver of driving responsibilities.”
Thus, given what the passage says, it may be that a Model-M SUV stopped at a red light at a highway intersection is controlling itself, in which case the driver would not be controlling the SUV.
Therefore, what this answer choice says does not match the facts provided in the passage.

question.text: The bearings in wind turbines are subject to extreme mechanical pressures that eventually cause breakdown.Proper lubrication of bearings extends the life of the bearings, and the better the lubricant used, the more the lubricant extends the life of the bearings in which it is used.A new lubricant is the best lubricant currently available, and Pacifico Power has decided to use this new lubricant in all of the bearings in its new turbines.Atlantico Power, on the other hand, is still using a previous generation lubricant in the bearings in all of its turbines.If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

answers.text: All of the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines will outlast any of the bearings in Atlantico Power’s turbines.
If Atlantico Power’s bearings were lubricated with the lubricant used in the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines, Atlantico Power’s bearings would last longer than they currently last.
Were Pacifico Power to use in its new turbines’ bearings the lubricant used in Atlantico Power’s bearings, the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines would not outlast Atlantico Power’s bearings.
If any of Atlantico Power’s bearings were lubricated with the new lubricant, those bearings would last as long as the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines.
None of Pacifico Power’s bearings that were lubricated with the previous generation lubricant will last as long as will any of the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: The bearings in wind turbines are subject to extreme mechanical pressures that eventually cause breakdown.
Fact 2: Proper lubrication of bearings extends the life of the bearings, and the better the lubricant used, the more the lubricant extends the life of the bearings in which it is used.
Fact 3: A new lubricant is the best lubricant currently available, and Pacifico Power has decided to use this new lubricant in all of the bearings in its new turbines.
Fact 4: Atlantico Power, on the other hand, is still using a previous generation lubricant in the bearings in all of its turbines.
(A) All of the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines will outlast any of the bearings in Atlantico Power’s turbines.
We cannot infer that all the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines will outlast any of the bearings in Atlantico Power’s turbines.

There could be many reasons why bearings in turbines fail.For example, maybe Pacifico Power’s new turbines are located in areas characterized by extremely high air temperatures, which cause erosion of bearings, whereas Atlantico Power’s turbines are located in temperate areas.In that case, even though Pacifico Power’s bearing are lubricated with the best lubricant and Atlantico Power’s bearings are lubricated with a previous generation lubricant, Atlantico Power’s bearings may last longer, because Atlantico Power’s bearings aren’t operating in conditions characterized by extremely high air temperatures.

So, since the passage addresses only one variable affecting bearing life – the lubricant used – the passage doesn’t provide information sufficient for determining whether Pacifico Power’s bearings will outlast Atlantico Power’s bearings.

YOUR CHOICE(B) If Atlantico Power’s bearings were lubricated with the lubricant used in the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines, Atlantico Power’s bearings would last longer than they currently last.

From the facts given, we know that proper lubrication of bearings extends the life of the bearings, and that,the better the lubricant used, the more the lubricant extends the life of the bearings in which it is used.

We also know that Pacifico Power will use in all the bearings in its new turbinesthe best lubricant currently available, while Atlantico Power is still using a previous generation lubricant in the bearings in all its turbines, one that isnot as goodas the one used by Pacifico Power.

Clearly then, we can infer that, if Atlantico Power’s bearings were lubricated with the lubricant used in the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines, Atlantico Power’s bearings would last longer than they currently last.

(C) Were Pacifico Power to use in its new turbines’ bearings the lubricant used in Atlantico Power’s bearings, the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines would not outlast Atlantico Power’s bearings.

We cannot infer that, were Pacifico Power to use in its new turbines’ bearings the lubricant used in Atlantico Power’s bearings, the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines would not outlast Atlantico Power’s bearings.

There could be many factors affecting how long bearings last.Maybe Pacifico Power has an outstanding maintenance program, whereas Atlantico Power’s maintenance program is lacking.In that case, even if Pacifico Power and Atlantico power were using the same lubricant, Pacifico Power’s bearings would likely still last longer than Atlantico Power’s bearings.

So, since the passage and this answer choice address only one variable affecting bearing life – the lubricant used – the passage and this answer choice do not provide information sufficient for determining whether Pacifico Power’s bearings will outlast Atlantico Power’s bearings.
(D) If any of Atlantico Power’s bearings were lubricated with the new lubricant, those bearings would last as long as the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines.

We cannot infer that, if any of Atlantico Power’s bearings were lubricated with the new lubricant, those bearings would last as long as the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines.After all, even if the companies’ bearings were all lubricated with the same lubricant, there could still be reasons why Atlantico Power’s bearings would wear out faster than Pacifico Power’s bearings will.
(E) None of Pacifico Power’s bearings that were lubricated with the previous generation lubricant will last as long as will any of the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines.

We can’t infer that none of Pacifico Power’s bearings that were lubricated with the previous generation lubricant will last as long as will any of the bearings in Pacifico Power’s new turbines.After all, there could be many factors other than lubricant used that affect how long bearings last, and those other factors could somehow cause some bearings lubricated with the previous generation lubricant to last just as long as Pacific Power’s new bearings lubricated with the new lubricant.

question.text: When global calamities, such as economic collapses or natural disasters, strike, investors, along with foreign governments, invest in United States Treasury Bonds, which are seen as ultra-safe investments, increasing demand for these bonds.As the demand for these bonds increases, interest rates on a wide range of financial products from home mortgage loans to automobile loans decrease, making it more affordable for consumers in the United States to borrow money by taking out such loans.Increases in affordability of borrowing money push prices of purchases that United States consumers make higher.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: If consumers in the United States are finding borrowing money more affordable, then either investors or foreign governments must be purchasing United States Treasury Bonds.
When a global calamity strikes, more U.S.consumers will begin to pay for the purchases of new homes with home mortgage loans than would during times of global stability.
Whenever demand for U.S.Treasury Bonds increases, automobiles become more affordable for consumers in the United States but not for consumers in other countries.
For a period of time after a global calamity occurs, more U.S.consumers are able to afford to make large purchases than were able to before that calamity occurred.
If, by reducing U.S.interbank rates, the U.S.government were to make borrowing money more affordable, U.S.consumers would make purchases at prices higher than they would otherwise have paid.

explanation_parts.text: (A) If consumers in the United States are finding borrowing money more affordable, then either investors or foreign governments must be purchasing United States Treasury Bonds.

The passage outlines one way via which borrowing can become more affordable for consumers in the United States: investors, along with foreign governments, invest in United States Treasury Bonds, increasing demand for these bonds and causing in interest rates to decrease.

However, given that information, we can’t infer what this choice says, because the fact that borrowingcanbecome more affordable that way does not mean thatany timeborrowing becomes more affordable it has become more affordable in the way outlined in the passage.There could be many other ways via which borrowing could have become more affordable.
(B) When a global calamity strikes, more U.S. consumers will begin to pay for the purchases of new homes with home mortgage loans than would during times of global stability.

This choice could seem to follow from what the passage says about global calamities and their effects on interest rates.Yes, the passage does support the idea that global calamities can cause borrowing to become more affordable for U.S.consumers.

However, this choice goes beyond what the passage supports.What the passage supports stops at affordability.This choice goes beyond affordability to saying what U.S.consumers will do: “more U.S.consumers will begin to pay for the purchases of new homes with home mortgage loans.”
Of course, what’s quite likely is that, in the midst of a global calamity, even though interest rates may decrease, fewer, rather than more, people will purchase new homes.
The passage does not clearly support the idea that, in the midst of a global calamity more U.S.consumers will use mortgage loans to run out to buy new homes than would during times of stability.
(C) Whenever demand for U.S. Treasury Bonds increases, automobiles become more affordable for consumers in the United States but not for consumers in other countries.
This choice could seem correct, because of what the passage says about demand for U.S.Treasury Bonds, interest rates, and affordability for U.S.consumers.
However, the truth is that this choice goes beyond what is supported by the passage.
The passage discusses whatdoeshappen to affordability for U.S.consumers but does not say anything about whatdoes nothappen to affordability for other consumers, as this choice does.

For all we know from what the passage says, automobile purchases become more affordable for people in places other than the United States as a result of increased demand for U.S.Treasury Bonds.It could be, for instance, that, when U.S.rates decrease, some rates in some other countries do too.
So, what this choice says about what does not happen to affordability for consumers in other countries is not supported by the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(D) For a period of time after a global calamity occurs, more U.S. consumers are able to afford to make large purchases than were able to before that calamity occurred.

This choice could seem to follow from what the passage says about global calamities and their effects on interest rates since the passage does support the idea that global calamities can cause borrowing to become more affordable for U.S.consumers.So, we could get the impression that what the passage says means that, when calamities occur, more consumers can afford to make large purchases.

However, this choice goes beyond what the passage supports.What the passage supports stops ataffordability of borrowingand the general effects of interest rate decreases onpricesof purchases.It does not say that calamities causemore consumersto be able to afford to make purchases.
For all we know from what the passage says, borrowing becomes more affordable, and prices go up, but no more consumers are able to make purchases than would be without the occurrence of a calamity.

CORRECT ANSWER(E) If, by reducing U.S. interbank rates, the U. S. government were to make borrowing money more affordable, U.S. consumers would make purchases at prices higher than they would otherwise have paid.

Since the passage is largely about the effects of global calamities, whereas this choice is about the effects of the U.S.government reducing U.S.interbank rates, this choice may not seem correct at first glance.
Notice, however, that even though the passage does not mention interbank rates, it does support what this choice says.
The passage says the following: “Increases in affordability of borrowing money push prices of purchases that United States consumers make higher.”

The key thing to notice is that that statement is true regardless of what causes the “increases in affordability of borrowing money.” It’s true if a global calamity causes an increase in affordability of borrowing, and it’s true if a reduction in interbank rates causes an increase in affordability of borrowing.According to what the passage says, anything that causes “increases in affordability of borrowing money” will push “prices of purchases that United States consumers make higher.”
So, the passage supports what this choice says about the effects of the U.S.government making borrowing money more affordable.

question.text: Wildston Orchards produces and sells three kinds of fruit: apples, pears, and peaches.Last year, apple sales revenue was 10 percent greater than it was the year before last year.However, last year, the percentage of total revenue from sales of fruit represented by apple sales revenue was lower than the same percentage for the year before last year.Which of the following can be properly inferred about the fruit sales of Wildston Orchards from the statements above?

answers.text: If last year’s pear sales revenue was 10 percent greater than the preceding year’s, then last year’s peach sales revenue was more than 10 percent greater than the preceding year’s.
Apple sales revenue increased less than pear sales revenue and less than peach sales revenue increased from the year before last year to last year.
If apple sales revenue had not increased, total revenue from fruit sales would have increased less than ten percent from the year before last year to last year.
Last year, apple sales revenue represented less than one third of total revenue from fruit sales.
The year before last year, apple sales revenue increased from that of the year two years before last year more than total fruit sales revenue sales did.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Wildston Orchards produces and sells three kinds of fruit: apples, pears, and peaches.
Fact 2: Last year, apple sales revenue was 10 percent greater than it was the year before last year.
Fact 3: However, last year, the percentage of total revenue from sales of fruit represented by apple sales revenue was lower than the same percentage for the year before last year.

Notice that, in order for the percentage of total revenue from fruit sales represented by apple sales revenue to have decreased, the total revenue from fruit sales must have increased more than apple sales revenue did.Here’s what we can understand from the statements in more mathematical terms:
Apples Sales Revenue + Pear Sales Revenue + Peach Sales Revenue = Total Sales Revenue.
Apple Sales Revenue increased 10 percent.
Apple Sales Revenue/Total Sales Revenue (which is the percentage of Total Sales Revenue represented by Apples Sales Revenue) decreased.

In order for Apple Sales Revenue/Total Sales Revenue to have decreased even though Apple Sales Revenue increased by 10 percent, the denominator of the fraction, Total Sales Revenue, must have increased by more than the numerator increased, in other words, by more than 10 percent.
So, given what the passage says, Total Sales Revenue must have increased by more than 10 percent.
YOUR CHOICE(A) If last year’s pear sales revenue was 10 percent greater than the preceding year’s, then last year’s peach sales revenue was more than 10 percent greater than the preceding year’s.

As explained in the introduction to this explanation, we know that Total Sales Revenue increased more than Apple Sales Revenue did.So, we know that Total Sales Revenue increased by more than 10 percent, which is how much Apple Sales Revenue increased by.
Total Sales Revenue is composed of Apple Sales Revenue, Pear Sales Revenue, and Peach Sales Revenue.

If Apple Sales Revenue increased 10 percent, then, in order for Total Sales Revenue to have increased by more than 10 percent, either Pear Sales Revenue or Peach Sales Revenue or both Pear and Peach Sales Revenue must have increased bymorethan 10 percent.

Therefore, given what the passage says, it must be true that, as this choice says, if last year’s Pear Sales Revenue was exactly 10 percent greater than the preceding year’s, then last year’s Peach Sales Revenue wasmorethan 10 percent greater than the preceding year’s.
(B) Apple sales revenue increased less than pear sales revenue and less than peach sales revenue increased from the year before last year to last year.

As explained in the introduction to this explanation, given what the passage says, it must be the case that Apple Sales Revenue increased less than Total Sales Revenue did from the year before last year to last year.However, it does not have to be the case that Apple Sales Revenue increased less than Pear Sales Revenue AND less than Peach Sales Revenue increased from the year before last year to last year.

It could be that the increase in Total Sales Revenue was greater than the increase in Apple Sales Revenue because revenue from sales of JUST ONE of the other two types of fruit increased much more than Apple Sales Revenue increased while revenue from sales of the second of the other two types of fruit did not increase more than Apple Sales Revenue did.
(C) If apple sales revenue had not increased, total revenue from fruit sales would have increased less than ten percent from the year before last year to last year.

The passage does not tell us how much Pear Sales Revenue or Peach Sales Revenue increased and does not provide an upper limit on how much Total Sales Revenue increased.The only constraints provided by the passage are (1) that Apple Sales Revenue increased by 10 percent, and (2), as explained in the introduction to this explanation, that Total Sales Revenue increased by more than 10 percent.

So, given what the passage says, it could be that Pear Sales Revenue or Peach Sales Revenue increased so much that, even if Apple Sales Revenue had not increased, Total Sales Revenue would have increased by more than 10 percent.
(D) Last year, apple sales revenue represented less than one third of total revenue from fruit sales.
All we know from the passage is that the percentage of Total Sales Revenue represented by Apple Sales Revenuedecreased.
The passage never indicates what the percentage of Total Sales Revenue represented by Apple Sales Revenue was in any year.
Last year, Apple Sales Revenue could have represented much more than a third of Total Sales Revenue even after the percentage of Total Sales Revenue represented by Apple Sales Revenue decreased.

For example, Apple Sales Revenue could have gone from representing 80 percent of Total Sales Revenue to representing 60 percent of Total Sales Revenue, and still have represented much more than a third, or 33.3 percent, of Total Sales Revenue.

Notice that we could get trapped into choosing this choice if we were to consider it only intuitively, rather than consider exactly what it says and what the passage says.If we were to consider this choice only intuitively, we might perceive the fact that Apple Sales Revenue decreased in relationship to Total Sales Revenue along with the fact thatthreekinds of fruit are sold as indicating that Apple Sales Revenue represents less than a third of Total Sales Revenue.We have to be careful not to end up choosing incorrect choices via using vague thinking rather than carefully determining whether what a choice says is truly supported by the passage.
(E) The year before last year, apple sales revenue increased from that of the year two years before last year more than total fruit sales revenue sales did.

The passage does not provide any information on revenues of the year two years before last year or any information on any differences between revenues of the year two years before last year and revenues of the year before last year.So, we have no support at all for what this choice says.

question.text: When demand for agave, which is the primary ingredient used in the production of tequila, increases, so does the price of agave.When the price of agave increases, so does the price of tequila.Over the past five years, the price of agave has increased sixfold because of increased demand for it.Agave grows in only a few small regions of Mexico, and agave plants require about seven years to reach the mature stage at which agave to be used in tequila production can be harvested from them.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: Unless demand for tequila decreases significantly, the price of tequila will not decrease significantly within the next seven years.
Producers of tequila cannot respond in a timely fashion to increased demand for tequila.
If demand for agave were to drop significantly, the cost of tequila would decrease rapidly.
If, on an occasion on which tequila makers suddenly demanded significantly more agave, the price of agave did not increase, some other agave consumer must have reduced its agave demand.
If the price of agave were to double, the price consumers pay for tequila would also double.

explanation_parts.text: (A) Unless demand for tequila decreases significantly, the price of tequila will not decrease significantly within the next seven years.

We know that, over the past five years, the price of agave has increased sixfold because of increased demand for it.We also know that agave plants require about seven years to reach the mature stage at which agave to be used in tequila production can be harvested from them.Given that information, we might get trapped into thinking that, unless demand for tequila decreases significantly, the price of tequila will not decrease significantly within the next seven years.

However, some careful consideration will allow us to see that there might be reasons why the price of tequila will, in fact, decrease sometime in the next seven years even if demand for tequila does not decrease.For example, perhaps agave plants that were planted seven years ago will be coming online soon, allowing for an increased supply of agave for tequila production.In that case, the cost of agave for making tequila could decrease, and, in turn, the cost of tequila could decrease within the next seven years.
What this answer choice says is not an inference because what it says may not turn out to be true.
(B) Producers of tequila cannot respond in a timely fashion to increased demand for tequila.

If we are not careful, we might fall for this tempting trap because what it says could very well be true in reality.Sometimes producers have difficulty responding to an increase in demand for a product, and furthermore, in this case in particular, we see that increasing the supply of agave, a key input in the making of tequila, could take years.

If we think carefully, however, we see that what this answer choice says, that, producers of tequila cannot respond in a timely fashion to increased demand for tequila, need not be true, even though what the passage says is true.
For all we know from what the passage says, producers of tequila have stockpiles of agave or stockpiles of tequila ready for meeting increased demand.The passage does not say that they don’t.
In other words, the information in the passage does not truly support what this answer choice says.
(C) If demand for agave were to drop significantly, the cost of tequila would decrease rapidly.

We’re told that when demand for agave, which is the primary ingredient used in the production of tequila, increases, so too does the price of agave.When the price of agave increases, so too does the price of tequila.

However, we certainly don’t have enough information to make the inference that, if demand for agave were to drop significantly, the cost of tequila would decrease rapidly.After all, the passage never makes any claims about the speed at which the cost of tequila would decrease following a decrease in demand for agave.

Furthermore, we don’t even know that, if demand for agave were to drop significantly, the cost of tequila would decrease at all.Yes, we do know that, when demand for agave increases, so does the price of agave, but from that information alone, we can’t conclude that the reverse is also true.

In you choose this answer choice as correct, you might not have been paying close enough attention to what it says; you might have missed the word “rapidly.” Also, you may have failed to realize that what the passage says about an increase in demand for agave does not necessarily have to work in reverse if demand for agave decreases.

YOUR CHOICE(D) If, on an occasion on which tequila makers suddenly demanded significantly more agave, the price of agave did not increase, some other agave consumer must have reduced its agave demand.

The passage says that, when demand for agave increases, so does the price of agave.Of course, what that means also is that, if the price of agave has not increased, demand for agave has not increased.

Given that information, it must be true that, if, on an occasion on which tequila makers suddenly demanded significantly more agave, the price of agave did not increase, demand for agave did not increase.

Of course, if tequila makers were demanding more agave, but demand for agave did not increase, it must have been the case that some decrease in demand must have offset the increase in tequila makers’ demand for agave.
In other words, in the situation described by this choice, it must be the case that, as this choice says, some other consumer of agave reduced its demand for agave.
(E) If the price of agave were to double, the price consumers pay for tequila would also double.

Notice that we are told that, when the price of agave increases, so too does the price of tequila.However, that statement does not allow us to conclude that the price of tequila moves in a one-to-one ratio with the price of agave.Such a conclusion would be an over-inference.
For example, for all we know from what the passage says, if the price of agave doubles, the price of tequila increases by half (or triples or quadruples).We just can’t say right?
Therefore, what this answer choice says is not an inference that can be made.

question.text: Currently, the Boselton subway system’s expenses are 20 percent greater than its revenue.A rider on Boselton’s subways pays the same fare regardless of the length of the ride taken.Consultants hired by the Boselton Transit Authority have determined that, if the fare for using the subways is reduced by 20 percent, Boselton’s subway system will experience increased ridership such that the system will cover its current expenses.Which of the following can be properly inferred about the consultants’ conclusions about the Boselton subway system?

answers.text: The consultants do not believe that there is any practical way for the Boselton Transit Authority to reduce the expenses associated with operating the subway.
The consultants expect the fare reduction to result in at least a 50 percent increase in use of Boselton’s subway system.
The consultants predict that a 20 percent fare reduction would not result in crowding on the subways that would cause some people to reduce their use of the subway system.
The consultants do not believe that the subway system could cover its expenses by increasing rather than decreasing the fare.
The consultants have determined that reducing the fare for using the subway by less than 20 percent would result in the system’s continuing to operate at a loss.

explanation_parts.text: (A) The consultants do not believe that there is any practical way for the Boselton Transit Authority to reduce the expenses associated with operating the subway.

The consultants have determined that, if the fare for using the subways is reduced by 20 percent, Boselton’s subway system will experience increased ridership such that the system will cover its current expenses.
Notice that what this answer choice provides us with is a different plan.

The fact that the consultants have come up with a plan does not allow us to infer that the plan mentioned in this answer choice is not practical.In other words, in proposing their plan, the consultants are not ruling out the practicality or the viability of any other plans.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) The consultants expect the fare reduction to result in at least a 50 percent increase in use of Boselton’s subway system.

According to the passage, at the moment, the Boselton subway system’s expenses are 20 percent greater than its revenue.To make working with these facts easy, let’s say that the revenue is $100 million, and that the expenses are $120 million.

Now, the plan is to decrease the fare for using the subways by 20 percent.So, upon implementation of the plan,if ridership were to remain the same, the new revenue would be $100 million - $20 million = $80 million.
However, the passage says that the consultants believe that Boselton’s subway system will experienceincreased ridershipsuch that the system will cover its current expenses.
In other words, they feel that Boselton’s subway system’s revenue will increase to $120 million.
$120 million is 50 percent greater than $80 million.So, in order for the subway system’s revenue to increase to $120 million after the fare is reduced, ridership has to increase by 50 percent.
Therefore, we can infer that the consultants expect the fare reduction to result in at least a 50 percent increase in use of in Boselton’s subway system.
(C) The consultants predict that a 20 percent fare reduction would not result in crowding on the subways that would cause some people to reduce their use of the subway system.

The consultants never make the claim that their plan will not cause some people to reduce their use of the subway.In fact, their plan leaves open the possibility that some people will stop using the subway.

All they are claiming is that, if the fare for using the subways is reduced by 20 percent, Boselton’s subway system will experience increased ridership such that the system will cover its current expenses.

In fact, if we really think about it, if the 20 percent fare reduction DOES result in crowding on the subways that causes some people to reduce their use of the subway system, then the subways are crowded with people, meaning that the plan is probably working.
(D) The consultants do not believe that the subway system could cover its expenses by increasing rather than decreasing the fare.

The consultants have determined that, if the fare for using the subways is reduced by 20 percent, Boselton’s subway system will experience increased ridership such that the system will cover its current expenses.

Notice that what this answer choice provides us with is a different plan.The fact that the consultants have come up with a plan other the plan mentioned in this answer choice does not allow us to infer that the plan mentioned in this answer choice is not practical.In other words, in proposing their plan, the consultants are not ruling out the practicality or the viability of any other plans.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The consultants have determined that reducing the fare for using the subway by less than 20 percent would result in the system’s continuing to operate at a loss.

The consultants have determined that, if the fare for using the subways is reduced by 20 percent, Boselton’s subway system will experience increased ridership such that the system will cover its current expenses.
This fact does not allow us to infer that consultants have determined that reducing the fare for using the subway by less than 20 percent would result in the system’s continuing to operate at a loss.

In other words, the fact that the consultants chose 20 percent as the number does not mean that they feel that choosing a different number would result in the system’s continuing to operate at a loss.That is, we do not have information about any other numbers.

question.text: Because heat dissipates at known rates, hackers can use a “FLIR,” a device that detects heat released from objects, to determine the passcode to a user’s smartphone.As a user presses a numerical digit key on the device’s touch screen, heat from the user’s finger is conducted into the screen.Once the user’s finger leaves the screen, that heat begins to dissipate from the screen into the surrounding air.When a FLIR is used to view the smartphone’s screen, hotter areas of the screen will show in red, orange, or yellow, and cooler areas in purple, blue, or black, depending on how much heat they contain.Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?

answers.text: If a FLIR showed only yellow over the number three and blue over the number two on a smartphone’s just used passcode screen, the phone’s passcode isn’t an even number.
If a smartphone user were to run her hands under cold water before entering her passcode, a hacker using only a FLIR would not be able to determine the passcode.
A smartphone that utilizes facial recognition technology but not a touchscreen passcode would be more difficult for hackers to break into than one that utilizes only a typical touchscreen passcode.
Smartphone makers should develop mechanisms that would allow users to use their fingers to enter passcodes without leaving heat signatures that could be leveraged by hackers with FLIRs.
Hackers would have more difficulty hacking into a smartphone using a FLIR if the smartphone were being operated in a lighted area than if it were being operated in darkness.

explanation_parts.text: YOUR CHOICE(A) If a FLIR showed only yellow over the number three and blue over the number two on a smartphone’s just used passcode screen, the phone’s passcode isn’t an even number.

We are told that, when a user presses a numerical digit key on a device’s touch screen, heat from the user’s finger is conducted into the screen.Once the user’s finger leaves the screen, that heat begins to dissipate from the screen into the surrounding air.When a FLIR is used to view the smartphone’s screen, hotter areas of the screen will show in red, orange, or yellow, and cooler areas in purple, blue, or black, depending on how much heat they contain.

Notice, then, that the first key on the screen to be touched will be the one that contains the least amount of heat at some time in the future.Similarly, the final key on the screen to be touched will be the key that contains more heat, just after it is pressed, than any of the keys that were pressed prior.

So, if a FLIR showed color over only the number two and the number three on a smartphone’s just used passcode screen, those two numbers must have been the only numbers or the last two numbers entered, since the FLIR would show color over any number that contains heat.

Also, if a FLIR showed yellow over the number three and blue over the number two, then the number three must have been pressed after the number two (hotter areas of the screen will show in red, orange, or yellow, and cooler areas in purple, blue, or black).

Therefore, we can infer that the number three was the final number pressed.Because a number is even when its units digit, the last digit, is even and odd when its units digit is odd, this number must not be an even number; the number must be odd.Therefore, we can infer that the phone’s passcode isn’t an even number.
(B) If a smartphone user were to run her hands under cold water before entering her passcode, a hacker using only a FLIR would not be able to determine the passcode.
Because we are told that the FLIR device detects heat released from objects, this answer choice presents us with a tempting trap.

It seems possible that, if a smartphone user were to run her hands under cold water before entering her passcode, a hacker using only a FLIR would not be able to determine the passcode.However, because this is an Inference question, we are not seeking something that is possible; we are seeking something that must be true, and the passage does not fully support what this answer choice says.

For all we know from what the passage says, even after running her hands under cold water, a smartphone user would still, by touching the screen of her smartphone, conduct heat that a FLIR could then detect.After all, running one’s hands under cold water does not removeallheat from one’s hands.
Be careful not to choose as correct a choice that says something possibly true in reality but not supported the passage.

(C) A smartphone that utilizes facial recognition technology but not a touchscreen passcode would be more difficult for hackers to break into than one that utilizes only a typical touchscreen passcode.

Like what choice (B) present to us, what this answer choice says is also a well designed trap because the passage does describe how a smartphone touchscreen passcode could be hacked.So, after reading all that, we might decide that, yeah, of course, a smartphone that utilizes facial recognition technology but not a touchscreen passcode would be more difficult for hackers break into than one that utilizes only a typical touchscreen passcode.That idea seems plausible – right?

However, because this is an Inference question, we are not seeking something that is plausible; we are seeking something that must be true, and the passage does not fully support what this choice says.

For all we know from what the passage says, even though smartphones with a touchscreen passcode may be hacked via the use of FLIR devices, touchscreen passcode systems are significantly more difficult for hackers break into than those that utilize facial recognition technology but not a touchscreen passcode.
(D) Smartphone makers should develop mechanisms that would allow users to use their fingers to enter passcodes without leaving heat signatures that could be leveraged by hackers with FLIRs.

After reading the passage on how FLIRs can be used to hack into smartphones, we might be tempted to conclude that smartphone makers should develop mechanisms that would allow users to use their fingers to enter passcodes without leaving heat signatures that could be leveraged by hackers with FLIRs.

However, if we look at the passage closely, we see that the author never provides any guidance on what should be done.For all we know, nothing should be done because, although smartphone devices with touchscreen passcodes are susceptible to FLIR hacking, the incidence of this type of hacking is extremely low.
In other words, although what this answer choice says sounds quite plausible in reality, it is not fully supported by what the passage says.
(E) Hackers would have more difficulty hacking into a smartphone using a FLIR if the smartphone were being operated in a lighted area than if it were being operated in darkness.

After reading the passage on how FLIRs can be used to hack into smartphones, we might be tempted to conclude that hackers would have more difficulty hacking into a smartphone using a FLIR if the smartphone were being operated in a lighted area than they would if it were being operated in darkness.

After all, we might reason that a FLIR would be less effective in picking up on differences in heat or that it's more difficult to see the colors that appear on a FLIR in a lighted area than in darkness.

However, that reasoning is not supported by the information provided by the passage.The passage says that a FLIR detects heat, not visible light.Also, it does not say that the colors that appear on a FLIR are more difficult to see in a lighted area than in darkness.So, while light and heat are related phenomena, the passage provides no clear reason to believe that it’s more difficult to use a FLIR in a lighted area than in darkness.

question.text: It is impossible to complete Marvel’s course in wizardry without developing strong magical skills, and the Wizardry Exam tests magical skills.All the same, some people who have completed Marvel’s course have not passed the Wizardry Exam.Furthermore, some people who have failed to complete Marvel’s course have nevertheless passed the Wizardry Exam.Which of the following conclusions can most properly be drawn from the information above?

answers.text: Sometimes, people who take the Wizardry Exam become too anxious to pass the exam even though they possess the magical skills necessary for doing so.
It is possible to pass the Wizardry Exam without having magical skills as strong as those developed by people who complete Marvel’s course in wizardry.
It would be wrong to believe a person’s being strongly skilled in magic to be a guarantee that that person will pass any test of skill in magic.
No matter how strong the magical skills a person develops are, those skills may fade if the person who has developed them does not use them regularly.
The magical skills tested by the Wizardry Exam and the magical skills developed through taking Marvel’s course in wizardry are not the same skills.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: It is impossible to complete Marvel’s course in wizardry without developing strong magical skills.
Fact 2: The Wizardry Exam tests magical skills.
Fact 3: Some people who have completed Marvel’s course have not passed the Wizardry Exam.
Fact 4: Some people who have failed to complete Marvel’s course have nevertheless passed the Wizardry Exam.
In order to answer this question correctly, we have to be careful not to confuse one thing with another.

For instance, the fact that it is impossible to complete Marvel’s course in wizardry without developing strong magical skills means that completing the course is sufficient for developing strong magical skills.

However, we have to be careful not to decide that completing the course and having strong magical skills are the exact same thing.It could be that someone who has not completed the course nevertheless has strong magical skills.
(A) Sometimes, people who take the Wizardry Exam become too anxious to pass the exam even though they possess the magical skills necessary for doing so.

This choice is tempting because it could seem to follow from what the passage says about people who have completed Marvel’s course not passing the Wizardry Exam.If people who have completed the course have developed strong magical skills, and if the exam tests magical skills, then it is plausible that people who have completed the course yet not passed the exam have become too anxious to pass.

However, we are not looking for something that is plausible.We are looking for something fully supported by what the passage says, and the passage does not say anything that clearly supports what this choice says about anxiety.
YOUR CHOICE(B) It is possible to pass the Wizardry Exam without having magical skills as strong as those developed by people who complete Marvel’s course in wizardry.
This choice is an extremely tempting trap, because we see that the passage says that some people who have failed to complete Marvel’s course have nevertheless passed the Wizardry Exam.

Seeing that statement, we might make the erroneous move of deciding that it is possible to pass the Wizardry Exam without having magical skills as strong as those developed by people who complete Marvel’s course.“After all,” we may think, “people are passing the exam even though they have failed to complete the course.”
Why is that move erroneous? Because, in making that move, we would be confusing completing the course with having the skills.
Given what the passage says, it is clear that anyone who has completed the course must have developed strong magical skills.

However, we have to remember that completing the course and having strong magical skills are two different things.So, it’s quite possible that some people who fail to complete the course develop magical skills just as strong as those developed by people who do complete the course, and that some people who have failed to complete the course have passed the exam only because they have developed strong magical skills.

In that case, it would be that, even though some who have failed to complete the course have passed the exam, it is not possible to pass the exam without skills as strong as those developed by those who complete the course.
So, what this choice says is not actually supported by what the passage says.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) It would be wrong to believe a person’s being strongly skilled in magic to be a guarantee that that person will pass any test of skill in magic.
This choice is purposely written to seem poorly connected to what the passage says.
The passage mentions Marvel’s course and the Wizardry Exam and uses the wording “strong magical skills.”

This choice uses slightly different wording, “strongly skilled in magic,” and does not mention Marvel’s course or the Wizardry Exam, instead saying something about what “it would be wrong to believe” and “any test of skill in magic.”
So, if we were to seek to arrive at the correct answer by merely matching wording and topic, we might eliminate this choice without thinking about what it says.
Meanwhile, the truth is that this choice is fully supported by the passage.

What we know from the passage is that some people who are strongly skilled in magic, having completed Marvel’s course, do not pass the Wizardry Exam, which is a test of magical skill.So, those people’s being strongly skilled in magic did not guarantee that they would pass a test of magical skill.

So, of course, what the passage says clearly supports the conclusion that it would be wrong to believe a person’s being strongly skilled in magic to be a guarantee that that person will pass any test of skill in magic.
(D) No matter how strong the magical skills a person develops are, those skills may fade if the person who has developed them does not use them regularly.

This choice is tempting because it could seem to follow from what the passage says about people who have completed Marvel’s course in wizardry not passing the Wizardry Exam.If people who have completed the course have developed strong magical skills, and if the exam tests magical skills, then it is plausible that the magical skills of people who have completed the course yet not passed the exam have faded.

However, we are not looking for something that is plausible.We are looking for something fully supported by what the passage says, and the passage does not say anything that clearly supports what this choice says about skills fading.
(E) The magical skills tested by the Wizardry Exam and the magical skills developed through taking Marvel’s course in wizardry are not the same skills.

This choice is tempting because it could seem to follow from what the passage says about people who have completed Marvel’s course in wizardry not passing the Wizardry Exam.If people who have completed the course have developed strong magical skills yet not passed an exam that tests magical skills, then it is plausible that the magical skills tested by the exam and the magical skills developed through taking the course are not the same skills.

However, we are not looking for something that is plausible.We are looking for something fully supported by what the passage says, and the passage does not say anything that clearly supports what this choice says.For all we know from what the passage says, the skills people develop through taking the course and the skills the exam tests are exactly the same, and yet people still don’t pass the exam.

question.text: Newspaper Editorial: Given that, at the upcoming meeting, all council members must vote either for or against the proposed increase in education spending, any council members in favor of the increased spending must be in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate, the sales tax rate, or both.After all, only through increasing the real estate tax rate or increasing the sales tax rate can the town increase education spending.We know that at least some of the council members are in favor of increasing education spending and that some are against increasing the real estate tax rate.Reader Comment: I know for a fact that only a small minority of council members are in favor of increasing the sales tax rate.If the statements in the editorial and the reader comment are all accurate, then which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: At least some of the council members are opposed to increasing education spending.
If none of those in favor of increasing education spending are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate, then most council members are against increasing education spending.
Not all of the council members in favor of increasing education spending agree on how to finance the increase.
A large majority of the council members are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate.
If those against increasing the real estate tax rate represent a majority of the council members, then a minority of the council members are in favor of increasing education spending.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:

Fact 1: Any council members in favor of increasing education spending must be in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate, the sales tax rate, or both, because, only through increasing the real estate tax rate or increasing the sales tax rate can the town increase education spending.
Fact 2: At least some of the council members are in favor of increasing education spending
Fact 3: Some of the council members are against increasing the real estate tax rate.
Fact 4: Only a small minority of council members are in favor of increasing the sales tax rate.
The statements seem to say a lot, but really there are just a few things going on.
- Council members can be for or against education spending.
- Council members can be for or against increasing the real estate tax rate.
- Council members can be for or against increasing the sales tax rate.
- Increasing education spending requires increasing the real estate tax rate, the sales tax rate, or both.
(A) At least some of the council members are opposed to increasing education spending.
There are two ways to determine with certainly that some council members are against education spending.
One way would be to see a statement that says that some are against education spending.If we go through the facts, we see that there is no such statement among the facts.

The other way would be to use evidence to show that some council members are both against increasing the real estate tax rate and against increasing the sales tax rate, because any council member completely against increasing either of the rates must be against education spending.

Only Fact 3 and Fact 4 tell us about council members’ positions for or against raising tax rates, and they don’t tell us that any council members are both against increasing the real estate tax rate and against increasing the sales tax rate.
So, we have no support for what this choice says.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) If none of those in favor of increasing education spending are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate, then most council members are against increasing education spending.
Since, to be in favor of increasing education spending, a council member must be in favor of increasing at least one of the tax rates, what this choice says must be true, and here’s why.
Fact 4 tells us that only a small minority of the members are in favor of increasing the sales tax rate.

That means that only a small minority of the members would favor increasing education spending by increasing the sales tax rate, meaning thatmost council members are against increasing education spending by increasing the sales tax rate.
So, what if no council members were in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate?

Then, the only members in favor of increasing education spending would be those in favor of increasing the sales tax rate, which, as the passage tells us, are only a small minority of the council members.So, in that case, most council members would be against increasing education spending.

So, the passage indicates that, as this choice says, if none of those in favor of increasing education spending are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate, then most council members are against increasing education spending.
(C) Not all of the council members in favor of increasing education spending agree on how to finance the increase.
We don’t know what percentage of the council members are in favor of increasing education spending.That percentage could be very small, and all of them could agree on how to finance the increase.

Also, we aren’t told whether any council members who are for increasing education spending are for or against increasing either tax rate.So, we have no way of determining which tax rate those in favor of increasing education spending would increase.

So, for all we know from what the passage says, it could be the case that, contrary to what this choice says, all of the council members in favor of increasing education spending agree on how to finance the increase.
We have no evidence to back up what this choice says.
(D) A large majority of the council members are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate.
We have no way of determining what this choice says.The passage does not tell us the percentage in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate.

Furthermore, the passage does not tell us even the percentage in favor of increasing education spending.That percentage could be a small minority, rather than a large majority, of the council members.
So, the passage provides no clear reason to believe that a large majority of the members are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate.
YOUR CHOICE(E) If those against increasing the real estate tax rate represent a majority of the council members, then a minority of the council members are in favor of increasing education spending.
What this choice says does not have to be true.
A minority, for instance 30 percent, plus another minority, for instance 40 percent, can equal a majority, for instance 30 + 40 = 70 percent.
We already know from what the passage says that a minority of the members are in favor of increasing the sales tax rate.

So, if a minority of the members are in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate, the two minorities in favor of increasing the two tax rates could represent a majority in favor of increasing education spending.

So, it is not the case that, as this choice says, if thoseagainstincreasing the real estate tax rate represent a majority of the council members, then a minority of the council members are in favor of increasing education spending.
We need only a minority of the council members to be in favor of increasing the real estate tax rate in order for it to be possible that the majority are in favor of increasing education spending.

question.text: The move to use of self-driving vehicles is expected to result in a reduction in traffic collisions.However, when faced with an imminent crash, a self-driving vehicle may have to choose between two evils – for example, running over pedestrians or crashing into a building.The greatest level of safety for all people comes through using vehicles designed to make such decisions.However, since in crashing into a building or taking another similarly self-sacrificing measure, a vehicle would be putting its occupants at risk of injury, most people say that they would continue to use vehicles that they themselves drive unless they could use self-driving vehicles designed to choose to protect their own occupants at all costs.Which of the following can be inferred from the statements above?

answers.text: Self-driving vehicles that protect their own passengers at all costs present a danger to pedestrians greater than that presented by vehicles that people drive themselves.
In enacting a law requiring that all self-driving vehicles be designed to choose between two evils, a government could make people in general less safe than they otherwise would be.
Self-driving vehicles cannot be designed in ways that make them both relatively safe for pedestrians and acceptable to most people who would use them.
Even when self-driving vehicles are widely available, it will continue to make sense to require anyone seeking to use a vehicle on public roads to pass a driving skills test.
Most people driving vehicles today will never choose to use self-driving vehicles.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: The move to use of self-driving vehicles is expected to result in a reduction in traffic collisions.
Fact 2: Self-driving vehicles sometimes have to choose between two evils.
Fact 3: The greatest level of safety comes from using vehicles designed to choose between two evils.
Fact 4: In taking a self-sacrificing measure, a vehicle would be putting its occupants at risk of injury.
Fact 5: Most people say that they would continue to use vehicles that they themselves drive unless they could use self-driving vehicles designed to choose to protect their own occupants at all costs.

A key thing to notice about this passage is that, while it states that the greatest level of safety comes from using vehicles designed to choose between two evils, it also says that the move to use of self-driving vehicles in general is expected to result in a reduction in traffic collisions.To arrive at the correct answer to this question, we have to be careful to differentiate between thegreatestlevel of safety and thegreaterlevel of safety that would result from a reduction in traffic collisions.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Self-driving vehicles that protect their own passengers at all costs present a danger to pedestrians greater than that presented by vehicles that people drive themselves.
We cannot infer that self-driving vehicles that protect their own passengers at all costs present a danger to pedestrians greater than that presented by vehicles that people drive themselves.

Notice that the passage never makes the comparison between the danger to pedestrians posed by self-driving vehicles that protect their own passengers at all costs and the danger to pedestrians posed by vehicles that people drive themselves.

Furthermore, the passage does say that the move to self-driving vehicles is expected to result in a reduction in traffic collisions.The implications of that statement are that the use of self-driving vehicles of either type, those that choose between two evils and those that protect their occupants at all costs, will result in increased safety for many people, including pedestrians.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) In enacting a law requiring that all self-driving vehicles be designed to choose between two evils, a government could make people in general less safe than they otherwise would be.
The passage tells us the following:
The move to use of self-driving vehicles is expected to result in a reduction in traffic collisions.
- So, people’s switching to using self-driving vehicles would result in a reduction in traffic collisions.
The passage also tells us the following:
Most people say that they would continue to use vehicles that they themselves drive unless they could use self-driving vehicles designed to choose to protect their own occupants at all costs.

- So, it’s likely that, if a government requires that all self-driving vehicles be designed to choose between two evils, most people won't switch to using self-driving vehicles, and the reduction in collisions that would have occurred if they had switched won't happen.

As a result, it must be true that, in requiring that all self-driving vehicles be designed to choose between two evils, a government could make people in general less safe than they otherwise would be.
(C) Self-driving vehicles cannot be designed in ways that make them both relatively safe for pedestrians and acceptable to most people who would use them.
We cannot infer that self-driving vehicles cannot be designed in ways that make them both relatively safe for pedestrians and acceptable to most people who would use them.

We might be tempted to believe that we can infer what this choice says, because the passage says that “greatest level of safety for all people comes through using vehicles designed to” “choose between two evils,” and the passage implies that most people would not find using such vehicles acceptable.

Notice, however, we really don’t know from what the passage says that an “acceptable” vehicle, one that is designed to protect its occupants at all costs, could not also be designed in a way such that that vehicle is relatively safe for pedestrians.In fact, it’s quite possible that even a self-driving vehicle designed to protect its own occupants at all costs is very safe for pedestrians.
The “greatest level of safety” and “relatively safe” are not the same.A vehicle could be relatively safe for pedestrians even if its use does not result in the greatest possible level of safety.
(D) Even when self-driving vehicles are widely available, it will continue to make sense to require anyone seeking to use a vehicle on public roads to pass a driving skills test.
If you chose this answer as correct, you likely did so because what this answer choice says is likely to be true in reality.However, what this answer choice says is not supported by the passage.

We cannot infer from what the passage says that, even when self-driving vehicles are widely available, it will continue to make sense to require anyone seeking to use a vehicle on public roads to pass a driving skills test.The passage says nothing about limits of capabilities of self-driving vehicles or anything else that would clearly indicate that people will still need driving skills when self-driving vehicles are widely available.

For all we know from what the passage says, it could very well be the case that self-driving vehicles will do such a good job driving that people operating them will have absolutely no use for driving skills.In that case, why would we need to make anyone seeking to use a vehicle on public roads pass a driving skills test?
(E) Most people driving vehicles today will never choose to use self-driving vehicles.
We cannot infer that most people driving vehicles today will never choose to use self-driving vehicles.

The passage says something different from what this choice says.It says, “…most people say that they would continue to use vehicles that they themselves driveunless they could use self-driving vehicles designed to choose to protect their own occupants at all costs.” Of course the implications of that statement are that some people may be willing to use self-driving vehicles that do not protect their own occupants at all costs, and that most may be willing to use self-driving vehicles if those vehicles do protect their own occupants at all costs.

If you choose this choice as the correct answer, you were not paying close enough attention to the details of the passage.We must relentlessly focus on and take into account the details of every Critical Reasoning passage.

question.text: People driving cars with tinted windows are twice as likely to be stopped for speeding by the police as people driving cars without tinted windows.The only valid explanation for this phenomenon is that people who drive cars with tinted windows are much more likely to speed than other drivers.If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred from them?

answers.text: Speeding cars with tinted windows do not have a higher chance of being stopped by the police than other cars.
The probability that a car with tinted windows will be justifiably stopped for speeding is greater than the probability that a car without tinted windows will be.
Drivers of cars without tinted windows are unjustifiably stopped by the police less often than drivers of cars with tinted windows.
