Fewer animals are killed in the process of farming fruit than are killed in the process of farming grains.

explanation_parts.text: The information in this passage is restricted to some simple facts about harvesting machines used in harvesting grains’ killing small animals and reasons why these machines are used on relatively large farms but not on relatively small farms.

To avoid choosing a trap choice and arrive at the correct answer, we have to make sure that we don’t get the impression that these facts support conclusions about related topics that these facts don’t really support.
(A) In order to produce food in such a way as to minimize harm to small animals, one must operate a farm that is relatively small.
This choice distorts the implications of what the passage says.

The passage says that “the majority of farming is done by individuals who operate farms that are too small for the justification of the use of large harvesting machines,” and that, “as a result, on the majority of farms on which grains are grown, relatively few animals are killed during the harvesting process.”

What we have to notice in order to avoid choosing this trap choice is that, while the result “relatively few animals are killed” does occur on small farms, this result could occur onanyfarm on which large harvesting machines are not used.It’s the use of the machines, not the farm size, that’s responsible for the killing of the animals.

So, the passage supports the conclusion that, contrary to what this choice says, one does not have to operate a farm that is relatively small in order to produce food in such a way as to minimize harm to small animals.
(B) Farmers who operate relatively small farms are more likely to consider cultural traditions when choosing tools to use for harvesting grains than are farmers who operate relatively large farms.
Notice that the passage says two things about farmers’ choosing which tools to use.

It says that “most farmers would prefer to use large harvesting machines,” and that “the majority of farming is done by individuals who operate farms that are too small for the justification of the use of large harvesting machines, which are quite expensive.”

While those facts may indicate that farmers who operate relatively small farms may use tools that are less advanced than large harvesting machines, those facts indicate that farmers would beconsidering expensein choosing to use those less advanced tools.

The passage does not say anything that supports the conclusion that farmers who operate small farms are more likely toconsider cultural traditionsin choosing tools than farmers who operate relatively large farms.
(C) Large harvesting machines cannot be operated in ways such that fewer small animals are killed than are killed by such machines currently.
What this choice says is related to what the passage says but does not follow logically from what the passage says.
Notice that the passage says, “Large machines used in harvesting grains grown on farms often kill small animals.”
To avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to notice the difference between that statement, which is about what does occur, and this choice, which is about what is possible.
The fact that, as the passage says, large harvesting machinesdokill small animals does not support this choice, which says essentially that large harvesting machinesmustkill small animals.

For all we know from what the passage says, contrary to what this choice says, large harvesting machines CAN be operated in ways such that fewer small animals are killed than are killed by such machines currently.
YOUR CHOICE(D) If relatively small grain farms are combined to create more relatively large grain farms, more animals will be killed in the process of harvesting grain.

The passage says that most farmers would prefer to use large harvesting machines but don’t use them because they operate farms that are too small for the justification of the use of large harvesting machines.

What follows from those facts is that, if relatively small grain farms are combined to create more relatively large grain farms, more harvesting machines will be used, because farmers prefer to use them and because their use will be justified, because, the new “large grain farms” will not be too small for the justification of the use of large harvesting machines.

Of course, since large harvesting machines are more harmful to small animals than are tools commonly used on relatively small farms, if more large harvesting machines are used, more small animals will be killed.

Thus, what the passage says supports what this choice says, that, if relatively small grain farms are combined to create more relatively large grain farms, more animals will be killed in the process of harvesting grain.
(E) Fewer animals are killed in the process of farming fruit than are killed in the process of farming grains.
What the passage says about large harvesting machines used in the harvesting of grains’ killing animals indicates that small animals are killed in the process of harvesting grains.

However, since the passage provides no information on whether or how many small animals are killed in the process of harvesting fruit, the passage does not support the comparison expressed by this choice.
In order to support what this choice says, we would need additional information not included in the passage.

question.text: Workplace wellness programs designed to help employees improve their health and less frequently miss workdays because of illness have become commonplace.Results of a recent study raise questions about such programs’ effectiveness, indicating that, while offering such programs results in increased frequency of some healthy behaviors, such as exercise and meditation, other behaviors, including consumption of high calorie diets, were, on average, unchanged.In addition, the study found that employees at companies offering wellness programs did not, on average, have lower blood pressures or cholesterol levels than were had by employees at companies without such programs, and that both groups missed about the same number of workdays.The statements above, if true, best support which of the following conclusions?

answers.text: Exercise and meditation alone are generally not effective in helping employees miss fewer workdays because of illness.
No employees who utilized workplace wellness programs had lower blood pressures than were had by employees who did not utilize such programs.
Companies that offer wellness programs for their employees do not experience any benefits from having done so.
Employees would be better off creating and following their own wellness programs, rather than following ones designed by their employers.
Eating a high calorie diet alone is enough to prevent employees from lowering their blood pressures or cholesterol levels.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:
Fact 1: Workplace wellness programs designed to help employees improve their health and less frequently miss workdays because of illness have become commonplace.

Fact 2: Results of a recent study raise questions about such programs’ effectiveness, indicating that, while offering such programs results in increased frequency of some healthy behaviors, such as exercise and meditation, other behaviors, including consumption of high calorie diets, were, on average, unchanged.

Fact 3: In addition, the study found that employees at companies offering wellness programs did not, on average, have lower blood pressures or cholesterol levels than employees at companies without such programs, and that both groups missed about the same number of workdays.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Exercise and meditation alone are generally not effective in helping employees miss fewer workdays because of illness.
The passage says that the study results indicate that companies’ offering wellness programs results in increased frequency of some healthy behaviors, such as exercise and meditation.

The passage also says that the study found that employees at companies that offered wellness programs missed about the same number of workdays as were missed by employees at companies without such programs.
So, an overall increase in exercise and meditation is associated with an absence of change in the number of workdays missed.
This information supports the conclusion that exercise and meditation alone are generally not effective in helping employees miss fewer workdays because of illness.
(B) No employees who utilized workplace wellness programs had lower blood pressures than were had by employees who did not utilize such programs.

The only information about employee blood pressures provided by the passage is that the study found that employees at companies offering wellness programs did not, on average, have lower blood pressures than were had by employees at companies without such programs.

Of course, averages are generally midpoints of values above and below the averages.Thus, the fact that the average blood pressure of employees at companies offering wellness programs was not lower than the average blood pressure of employees at companies without such programs does not mean that some individual employees at companies offering wellness programs did not have blood pressures lower than those of individual employees at companies without such programs.
So, this choice is not supported by the passage.
(C) Companies that offer wellness programs for their employees do not experience any benefits from having done so.
Notice that this answer choice says that companies that offer wellness programsdo not experience any benefitsfrom having done so.
Of course, from the information presented in the passage, we can’t conclude that the companies “do not experience any benefits.”
There could be many benefits that the companys experience that the passage does not address.
(D) Employees would be better off creating and following their own wellness programs, rather than following ones designed by their employers.

We can’t conclude that employees would be better off creating and following their own wellness programs, rather than following ones designed by their employers.Notice that the passage does not provide us with any information about what employees could come up with on their own.Perhaps wellness programs they would come up with would be far worse than the workplace wellness programs.
(E) Eating a high calorie diet alone is enough to prevent employees from lowering their blood pressures or cholesterol levels.
We can’t conclude that eating a high calorie diet alone is enough to prevent employees from lowering their blood pressures or cholesterol levels.

While the passage does say that the study results indicate that, when wellness programs were offered, certain behaviors, including consumption of high calorie diets, were, on average, unchanged, and while the passage does say that employees at companies offering wellness programs did not, on average, have lower blood pressures or cholesterol levels than were had by employees at companies without such programs, the passage never provides us with any evidence that clearly connects eating a high calorie diet with employees inability to lower their blood pressures or cholesterol levels.

For all we know from what the passage says, the employees engaged in a variety of unhealthy behaviors including eating high calorie diets, and it was the combination of all of those behaviors that prevented them from lowering their blood pressures and cholesterol levels, not just their eating high calorie diets.

question.text: In academic settings, such as universities, there exists a mentality of publish or perish, in that professors are expected to publish their research in scholarly journals on a regular basis.The reasoning is that worthy professors should always be making progress in their fields.However, carefully writing, and then submitting, papers for scholarly journals is time and energy consuming.Furthermore, the number of publications attributed to a professor has never been a perfect indicator of his or her professional impact on a field; some of the most famous of all professors have published only a single, seminal paper that has changed the world forever.Each of the following conclusions is supported by the information above, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Some college professors can be impactful in their chosen fields without regularly publishing their research papers in scholarly journals.
Generally, a professor who publishes a single, seminal paper will have more impact on a field than a professor who publishes research in scholarly journals on a regular basis.
Professors’ feeling compelled to write research papers may result in their missing out on opportunities to conduct research that could positively impact their fields.
Professors should be evaluated on the bases of the contributions to and positive impacts they have on their fields, not solely on the regularity with which they publish papers.
A professor who does not publish research in scholarly journals on a regular basis is unlikely to meet the expectations of his or her colleagues.

explanation_parts.text: Notice that this is a Conclusion EXCEPT question.
So, four of the choices state conclusions supported by the passage.These four are incorrect choices.
The correct answer is the one that does not state a conclusion supported by the passage.
(A) Some college professors can be impactful in their chosen fields without regularly publishing their research papers in scholarly journals.
The passage says that some of the most famous of all professors have published only a single, seminal paper that has changed the world forever.

Clearly, publishing “only a single, seminal paper” does not involve regularly publishing research papers in scholarly journals, and, meanwhile, changing the world forever is certainly being “impactful.”
So, we can see that the passage supports what this choice says, that professors can be impactful in their chosen fields without regularly publishing research papers in scholarly journals.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Generally, a professor who publishes a single, seminal paper will have more impact on a field than a professor who publishes research in scholarly journals on a regular basis.
Notice that what the passage says is the following: “someof the most famous of all professors have published only a single, seminal paper that has changed the world forever.”

It does not say thatallof the most famous of all professors have published only a single, seminal paper, or that professors who have published only a single paper generally havemoreimpact than is had by professors who have published research in scholarly journals on a regular basis.
All the passage really supports about professors who publish research regularly and professors who don’t is that a professordoes nothave topublish research regularly in order to have an impact.
Thus, the passage does not support what this choice says.
So, since this question is a Conclusion EXCEPT question, this choice is the correct answer.
Conclusion supported by the passage? No.
(C) Professors’ feeling compelled to write research papers may result in their missing out on opportunities to conduct research that could positively impact their fields.
The passage says that carefully writing, and then submitting, papers for scholarly journals is time and energy consuming.

Since professors’ engaging in an activity that consumes their time and energy could result in their missing out on opportunities to conduct research that could positively impact their fields, this choice is supported by the passage.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.
(D) Professors should be evaluated on the bases of the contributions to and positive impacts they have on their fields, not solely on the regularity with which they publish papers.

The passage says that the number of publications attributed to a professor has never been a perfect indicator of his or her professional impact on a field; some of the most famous of all professors have published only a single, seminal paper that has changed the world forever.
That information indicates that evaluating professors solely on the bases of the regularity with which those professors publish papers may, in many cases, result in rather inaccurate evaluations.
Of course, that information supports what this choice says.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.
(E) A professor who does not publish research in scholarly journals on a regular basis is unlikely to meet the expectations of his or her colleagues.

The passage says, “In academic settings, such as universities, there exists a mentality of publish or perish, in that professors are expected to publish their research in scholarly journals on a regular basis.”

If professors are expected to publish their research in scholarly journals on a regular basis, then a professor who does not publish research in scholarly journals on a regular basis is likely not meeting expectations of his or her colleagues.
Thus, the passage supports what this choice says.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.

question.text: Facial recognition software is increasingly used by police in determining people’s identities and in convicting criminals.Those in favor of its use claim that its accuracy is well-supported by scientific research.However, when such software is tested in the controlled setting of a laboratory, clear, well-focused, high resolution images of faces are used in the experiments used to support its use, but the streets that police patrol are far from controlled settings.Images of suspects often are blurry or low resolution or are “corner cases,” captured at unusual angles.If the statements above are true, which of the following is most strongly supported by them?

answers.text: If facial recognition software is ever designed to handle corner cases, the results of using such software on the streets will match those of using it in laboratory settings.
Facial recognition software in its current form, when used by police patrolling streets, is just as apt to incorrectly identify a person as it is to correctly identify a person.
Members of a jury should view with skepticism evidence presented by police against a defendant that was generated via the use of facial recognition software.
Facial recognition software will never become as accurate when used by police patrolling streets as when used in laboratory experiments.
Until it can be made more reliable, facial recognition software should not be used by police patrolling the streets.

explanation_parts.text: (A) If facial recognition software is ever designed to handle corner cases, the results of using such software on the streets will match those of using it in laboratory settings.
This choice conflicts with what the passage says.

The passage says that images used in laboratory settings are “clear, well-focused, high resolution images,” whereas, when taken on the streets, “images of suspects are often ‘blurry or low resolution or are “corner cases.”’

We can see that, contrary to what this choice says, addressing the issue of corner cases will not cause the results of using such software on the streets to match those of using it in laboratory settings, because addressing the issue of corner cases will not make the often blurry or low resolution “on the streets” images clear and well-focused like the ones used in laboratory settings.
In answering Conclusion questions, we must ensure that a choice that we are choosing does not conflict in some way with what the passage says.
(B) Facial recognition software in its current form, when used by police patrolling streets, is just as apt to incorrectly identify a person as it is to correctly identify a person.
This choice goes beyond what is supported by the passage.

Yes, the passage indicates that the images taken “on the streets” are not as high in quality as those used in laboratory settings and, thus, supports the conclusion that facial recognition software is not as reliable as laboratory experiments may seem to indicate that it is.

However, just how unreliable the use of facial recognition software is is not made clear by the facts provided by the passage.Given what the passage says, the use of facial recognition software could be rather reliable, somewhat reliable, or, as this choice basically says, not reliable at all.

So, the passage does not really support the particular conclusion that facial recognition software in its current form, when used by police patrolling streets, isjust as apt to incorrectly identify a person as it is to correctly identify a person.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) Members of a jury should view with skepticism evidence presented by police against a defendant that was generated via the use of facial recognition software.

The passage says that those in favor of the use of facial recognition software by police in determining people’s identities and in the conviction of criminals base their support of its use on scientific research.

Then, the passage shows that there are issues involved with using scientific research to support the use of facial recognition software in police work, namely that the images used in laboratory settings are of much higher quality than those often used when police use facial recognition software.
Thus, the passage indicates that the support for using facial recognition software in convicting criminals is questionable.

The job of members of a jury is to decide whether to convict people.Thus, by indicating that the support for the use of facial recognition software in convicting criminals is questionable, the passage supports the conclusion that members of a jury should view with skepticism evidence presented by police against a defendant that was generated via the use of facial recognition software.
(D) Facial recognition software will never become as accurate when used by police patrolling streets as when used in laboratory experiments.

The passage provides information about currently existing differences between the use of facial recognition software in laboratory settings and the use of facial recognition software by police patrolling the streets.
This choice, on the other hand, is about future developments in the use of facial recognition software.

The fact that, currently, there are differences between the use of facial recognition software in laboratory settings and the use of facial recognition software on the streets does not mean that facial recognition software willneverbecome as accurate when used by police patrolling streets as when used in laboratory experiments.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Until it can be made more reliable, facial recognition software should not be used by police patrolling the streets.

The passage indicates that there are issues with the use of facial recognition software by police, namely that support for the reliability of its use comes from scientific experiments that use “clear, well-focused, high resolution images,” whereas police on the streets using facial recognition software often use images that are ‘blurry or low resolution or are “corner cases.”’
What this information indicates is that, when used by police, facial recognition software may not be completely reliable.

OK, but not completely reliable is not the same as completely unreliable or useless.There could be many reasons to use facial recognition software even if the results of using it in practice are not as good as the results of using it in a laboratory setting.In other words, while what the passage says indicates that the use of facial recognition software may not be ideal, facial recognition software may still be quite helpful, and there may be good reasons to use it.
So, by saying that, until it can be made more reliable, facial recognition software should not be used by police patrolling the streets, this choice goes beyond what is supported by the passage.

question.text: Devices designed to protect occupants of automobiles in the event of a crash have been shown to reduce the likelihood of an automobile’s occupants’ being injured should they be involved in a crash.However, it has also been found that drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants in the event of a crash have been installed often increase the riskiness of their driving behavior as a result of their feeling protected by these devices.Of the following, which is best supported by the above statements?

answers.text: The installation in automobiles of devices designed to protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash does not have any effect other than increasing the costs of automobiles.
While automobile passengers become safer as the result of installation in automobiles of devices that protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash, automobile drivers do not.
The installation in automobiles of devices designed to protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash results in increased likelihood of harm to people not occupying those automobiles.
The only way to reduce with certainty the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes is to get automobile drivers to reduce the riskiness of their driving behavior.
The installation in automobiles of devices that protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash does not reduce the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes.

explanation_parts.text: (A) The installation in automobiles of devices designed to protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash does not have any effect other than increasing the costs of automobiles.

This choice is tempting, because we could perceive the finding that drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants have been installed often increase the riskiness of their driving behavior as indicating that any protection that the devices provide is offset by an increase in danger resulting from the increased riskiness of driving behavior.Thus, we could perceive the devices as making no difference, i.e, having no effect.
Of course, we don’t know that the increased riskiness of driving behavior completely offsets any protection offered by the devices.

Furthermore, even if the increased riskiness of driving behavior does completely offset any protection offered by the devices, that fact is not the same as the installation of the devices’ not having any effect other than increasing the costs of automobiles.

For one thing, as we know from the passage, drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants have been installed tend to increase the riskiness of their driving behavior.So, we know for sure that there is an effect, increases in riskiness of driving behavior.
So, while, yes, some of the protective effects of the devices are offset by increases in riskiness of driving behavior, what this choice says goes too far.
(B) While automobile passengers become safer as the result of installation in automobiles of devices that protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash, automobile drivers do not.
The passage says that the devices “have been shown to reduce the likelihood of an automobile’s occupants’ being injured should they be involved in a crash.”

Both passengers and drivers are occupants of automobiles.So, even though it has been found that drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants have been installed often increase the riskiness of their driving behavior, drivers and passengers should be affected in the same way by installation in automobiles of devices that protect automobile occupants.

So, given what the passage says, and unlike what this choice says, if passengers become safer as the result of installation in automobiles of devices that protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash, drivers should become safer too.
So, what this choice says conflicts with what the passage says.

YOUR CHOICE(C) The installation in automobiles of devices designed to protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash results in increased likelihood of harm to people not occupying those automobiles.

Notice that the passage says that it has been found that drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants have been installed often increase the riskiness of their driving behavior.

Of course, if drivers of automobiles in which these devices been installed increase the riskiness of their driving behavior, then the risk of harm to people not in these automobiles, such as people in other automobiles or pedestrians, increases.
So, we can see that what this choice says is supported by what the passage says.
(D) The only way to reduce with certainty the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes is to get automobile drivers to reduce the riskiness of their driving behavior.
What this choice says goes beyond what is supported by the passage.

What the passage says, that it has been found that drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants have been installed often increase the riskiness of their driving behavior, seems to indicate that installing these devices in automobiles may not make the occupants of automobiles safer.The devices themselves may make the occupants safer, but then, the increase in riskiness of driving behavior makes the occupants less safe.So, the two factors – the installation of the devices and the increased riskiness of driving behavior – may offset each other.

However, the foregoing does not mean that theonlyway to reduce with certainty the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes is to get automobile drivers to reduce the riskiness of their driving behavior.There are many other approaches that may result in a reduction in the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes, such as designing cars so that they don’t go faster than a certain speed or changing the layouts of roads.
So, what this choice says is not supported by what the passage says.
(E) The installation in automobiles of devices that protect automobile occupants in the event of a crash does not reduce the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes.

Notice that the passage says that it has been found that drivers of automobiles in which devices designed to protect occupants have been installedoftenincrease the riskiness of their driving behavior.
Often is not the same as always.

So, what the passage says does not indicate that drivers of automobiles in which such devices have been installedalwaysincrease the riskiness of their driving behavior.Rather, given what the passage says, it may be that, in some cases, drivers of such automobiles do not increase the riskiness of their driving behavior.

Of course, in cases in which automobiles have the devices that protect occupants, and in which the drivers of those automobiles do not increase the riskiness of their driving behavior, the presence of the devices could very well result in decreases in likelihoods of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes.

Thus, while drivers of vehicles that have these devices often increase the riskiness of their driving behavior, it could be that, contrary to what this choice says, sometimes, or overall, installation in automobiles of these devicesdoesreduce the likelihood of automobile occupants’ being injured in crashes.
So, what this choice says is not supported by the passage.

question.text: When the incidence of a particular type of illness is unusually high in the members of an extended family, people often conclude that the factor shared by the family members that underlies the manifestation of the illness in the family members is genetic makeup.What the people arriving at this conclusion often fail to consider is that, like the language that all members of an extended family may speak, habits and lifestyle choices may be shared in common by members of an extended family, and habits and lifestyle choices can underlie the manifestation of a particular type of illness.Which of the following is best supported by the information presented above?

answers.text: Generally, an unusually high incidence of a particular illness in members of an extended family is underlied by a combination of genetic makeup and shared habits and lifestyle choices.
People who speak the same language are more likely than people who do not speak the same language to experience the same illnesses.
An unusually high incidence of a particular type of illness in an extended family is more likely underlied by habits or lifestyle choices than by genetic makeup.
By focusing exclusively on a family’s members’ genetic makeup, a researcher may reduce the likelihood of determining what underlies the unusually high incidence of an illness in that family.
Sometimes, it is not possible to determine what underlies the unusually high incidence of a particular type of illness in an extended family.

explanation_parts.text: (A) Generally, an unusually high incidence of a particular illness in members of an extended family is underlied by a combination of genetic makeup and shared habits and lifestyle choices.
This choice could be tempting because what it says sounds plausible and may match what we have heard from sources other than the passage in this question.

Notice, however, that, while the passage mentions genetic makeup and shared habits and lifestyle choices as factors that could underlie the manifestation of a particular type of illness in members of an extended family, the passage does not support what this choice says.

The passage never says anything about how commonly any of these factors underlie illnesses, and it certainly does not provide information that supports the specific conclusion stated by this choice about “a combination of genetic makeup and shared habits and lifestyle choices.”

In answering a Conclusion question, we have to be careful not to get tempted into choosing an incorrect choice by the fact that what the choice says matches what goes on in reality.The correct answer has to be supported by the passage, not by information from other sources.
(B) People who speak the same language are more likely than people who do not speak the same language to experience the same illnesses.
This choice could be tempting because it mentions something that the passage mentions, people who speak the same language, and because what it says seems plausible.
However, the truth is that the passage provides absolutely no information that supports the comparison expressed by this choice.
Does the passage say anything about how often people who do not speak the same language experience the same illness?
Does it say anything about how often people who do speak the same language experience the same illness? No.

For all we know from what the passage says, there is no difference between the likelihood that people who speak the same language will experience the same illnesses and the likelihood the people who do not speak the same language will experience the same illnesses, and the passage provides no indication that what this choice says is true.
In answering a Conclusion question, we have to avoid being tempted into choosing a choice that says something that seems logical or plausible yet is not supported by what the passage says.
(C) An unusually high incidence of a particular type of illness in an extended family is more likely underlied by habits or lifestyle choices than by genetic makeup.
To avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to notice the key difference between what the passage says and what this choice says.

The passage says that people often conclude that the factor shared by the family members that underlies the manifestation of the illness in the family members is genetic makeup, and then says that shared habits and lifestyle choicescanunderlie the manifestation of a particular type of illness.

So, notice that the passage has merely offered some factors thatcouldunderlie incidences of illnesses in extended families.The passage never indicates that one of the factors, shared habits and lifestyle choices ismore likelyto underlie the high incidence of a particular type of illness in an extended family.
To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to distort in our minds what the passage says.

YOUR CHOICE(D) By focusing exclusively on a family’s members’ genetic makeup, a researcher may reduce the likelihood of determining what underlies the unusually high incidence of an illness in that family.

The passage says that habits and lifestyle choices may be shared in common by members of an extended family, and that habits and lifestyle choices can underlie the manifestation of a particular illness.
What follows from those statements is that the members of an extended family could experience the same illness as a result of sharing habits and lifestyle choices.

Of course, then, if a researcher seeking to determine what underlies the high incidence of a particular illness in a family were to focus exclusively on the family members’ genetic makeup, the researcher might, in doing so, reduce the likelihood of that researcher’s determining what’s actually going on, because the researcher would not be considering shared habits and lifestyle choices, which could be what actually underlies the high incidence of the illness in that family.
So, we can see that the passage supports what this choice says.
(E) Sometimes, it is not possible to determine what underlies the unusually high incidence of a particular type of illness in an extended family.
This choice could be tempting because what it says sounds somewhat plausible.

Notice, however, that, while the passage says that people often fail to consider that habits and lifestyle choices may be shared in common by members of an extended family, that information certainly does not mean what this choice says, that sometimes, it isnot possibleto determine what underlies the unusually high incidence of a particular type of illness in an extended family.
What this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.

question.text: BMI is an inexpensive and easy-to-perform method of screening for weight category - for example, underweight, normal weight, or overweight.While BMI is simply a person’s weight in kilograms divided by the square of the person’s height in meters, research has shown that BMI is moderately correlated with more exact measures of body fat obtained via the use of methods that are more expensive and complex to use than is calculating BMI.Furthermore, BMI appears to be as strongly correlated with various body fat related health outcomes as are the other measures of body fat.Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the statements above?

answers.text: There is no practical reason for using more complex and expensive methods for measuring a person’s body fat rather than just using BMI.
The more expensive and complex a method for measuring body fat is, the more exact are the measurements that it produces.
BMI tends to be higher for people whose heights are relatively low.
Generally, spending more than the cost of calculating BMI to measure a person’s body fat will not increase the accuracy of predictions about that person’s health.
The more body fat a person has, the more important it is to use for measuring that person’s body fat a method more complex and expensive than calculating BMI.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: BMI, is an inexpensive and easy-to-perform method of screening for weight category - for example underweight, normal weight, and overweight.

Fact 2: While BMI is simply a person’s weight in kilograms divided by the square of the person’s height in meters, research has shown that BMI is moderately correlated with more exact measures of body fat obtained via the use of more complex and expensive methods.
Fact 3: Furthermore, BMI appears to be as strongly correlated with various body fat related health outcomes as are the other measures of body fat.
(A) There is no practical reason for using more complex and expensive methods for measuring a person’s body fat rather than just using BMI.

This choice is tempting because the passage says that research has shown that BMI is moderately correlated with more exact measures of body fat obtained via the use of methods that are more expensive and complex to use than is calculating BMI, and that BMI appears to be as strongly correlated with various body fat related health outcomes as are the other measures of body fat.So, it could seem to follow that there is no practical reason for using more complex and expensive methods for measuring a person’s body fat rather than just using BMI.
However, the truth is that what this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.

While the above facts from the passage support the idea that BMI can, in many cases, be just as useful as more complex and expensive methods for measuring body fat, the passage doesn’t support the specific and extreme conclusion that there isno practical reasonfor using more complex and expensive methods for measuring a person’s body fat rather than just using BMI.
For all we know given what the passage says, there may be many practical reasons to use more complex and expensive methods for measuring a person’s body fat.

Also, notice that the passage says, “research has shown that BMI is moderately correlated with more exact measures of body fat obtained via the use of more complex and expensive methods.” Moderately correlated is not “very closely” correlated or “perfectly” correlated.So, it must be true that, in at least some cases, more complex and expensive methods do a better job.After all, they generate “more exact measures.” So, in situations calling for “more exact measures,” there could be practical reasons for using more complex and expensive methods for measuring a person’s body fat rather than just using BMI.
(B) The more expensive and complex a method for measuring body fat is, the more exact are the measurements that it produces.

This choice is tempting because the passage mentions “more exact measures of body fat obtained via the use of methods that are more expensive and complex to use than is calculating BMI.” So, we could get the impression that the passage supports the conclusion that the more expensive and complex a method for measuring body fat is, the more exact are the measurements that it produces.
Notice, however, that all the passage indicates is that there aresomemethods that are more expensive and complex to use than calculating BMI that are also more exact than calculating BMI.

The passage never says or implies that,generallyorin all cases, the more expensive and complex a method for measuring body fat is, the more exact are the measurements that it produces.In fact, for all we know from what the passage says, there are very expensive and complex ways of measuring body fat that produce measurements that are not exact at all.
In answering a Conclusion question, we have to be careful not to make generalizations that are not actually supported by what the passage says.
(C) BMI tends to be higher for people whose heights are relatively low.

This choice could be tempting, because we could see that the passage says that BMI is simply a person’s weight in kilograms divided by the square of the person’s height in meters (weight/height2), and decide that, since the square of the person’s height is the denominator of the fraction used in calculating BMI, what the passage says indicates that BMI will be greater for people whose heights are relatively low.

However, the truth is that that reasoning would ignore the fact that height is not the only variable in the formula for BMI.Weight matters too, and, of course, it is the case that, generally, the taller people are, the more they weigh.So, generally, as height (the denominator) increases, weight (the numerator) does too,

So, for all we know given what the passage says, it could be the case that increases in height tend to be offset by increases in weight, and that, therefore, contrary to what this choice says, BMI tends to be the same across different heights.
In answering a Conclusion question, we have to be careful not to jump to an illogical conclusion that actually conflicts with or doesn’t match what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Generally, spending more than the cost of calculating BMI to measure a person’s body fat will not increase the accuracy of predictions about that person’s health.

The passage mentions BMI, “an inexpensive and easy-to-perform method of screening for weight category,” and also mentions other methods for measuring body fat “that are more expensive and complex to use than is calculating BMI.”
The passage then says, “BMI appears to be as strongly correlated with various body fat related health outcomes as are the other measures of body fat.”

Well, if BMI is as strongly correlated with various body fat related health outcomes as are other measures of body fat, such as more expensive measure of body fat, it follows that using a measure of body fact more expensive than BMI wouldn’t result in predictions about body fat related health outcomes any more accurate than the predictions that would result if BMI were used.

So, the conclusion that spending more than the cost of calculating BMI to measure a person’s body fat will not increase the accuracy of predictions about that person’s health is well supported by what the passage says.
(E) The more body fat a person has, the more important it is to use for measuring that person’s body fat a method more complex and expensive than calculating BMI.
This choice is tempting because what it says sounds somehow logical or plausible.

However, the truth is that the passage provides absolutely no information that indicates that how much body fat a person has affects which method would be best to use for measuring that person’s body fat.In fact, for all we know from what the passage says, the opposite of what this choice says is true, and the more body fat a person has the better BMI works.
So, this choice is not supported by what the passage says.

question.text: The star Betelgeuse has recently become very dim, causing speculation regarding whether it will soon explode in spectacular supernova fashion.A star going supernova will first shed debris, forming massive amounts of dust around itself, causing its light to become very dim.Stars fluctuate and change all the time, as does Betelgeuse, which dims and brightens in somewhat regular cycles.Therefore, an alternative possible cause of this extreme dimming is that two of these dimming cycles have converged.If the statements above are all true, which of the following is most strongly supported by them?

answers.text: If Betelgeuse is expelling massive amounts of debris, its dimming cycles have not converged.
The convergence of a star’s dimming cycles tends to prevent that star from expelling massive amounts of debris and exploding.
Detailed understanding of the timing of a star’s dimming cycles could be useful in determining whether that star is about to explode.
The greater the volume of debris a star is expelling, the more likely it is that that star will soon go supernova.
If Betelgeuse is not about to go supernova, it is the case that two of its dimming cycles have converged.

explanation_parts.text: Correctly answering this question requires noticing the difference between what must be the case given what the passage says and what is merely possible given what the passage says.

Some of the answer choices in this question make statements that convey that thingsare for surethe case.The passage does not support those choices because it supports only that those thingsmay bethe case.
(A) If Betelgeuse is expelling massive amounts of debris, its dimming cycles have not converged.
Given what the passage says, it is possible that Betelgeuse is expelling massive amounts of debris, and that its dimming cycles have not converged.
However, what this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.
In other words, the passage does not support the conclusion that, if Betelgeuse is expelling massive amounts of debris, then, for sure, its dimming cycles have not converged.

Notice that the passage never says that expelling debris and convergence of dimming cycles cannot occurat the same time.So, given what the passage says, it could be the case that, contrary to what this choice says, Betelgeuse is expelling massive amounts of debrisandits dimming cycles have converged.
(B) The convergence of a star’s dimming cycles tends to prevent that star from expelling massive amounts of debris and exploding.
What this choice says is not at all supported by what the passage says.
The passage mentions convergence of a star’s dimming cycles and a star’s expelling massive amounts of debris and exploding as two possible developments.
The passage does not say that convergence wouldpreventexpelling debris and exploding.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Detailed understanding of the timing of a star’s dimming cycles could be useful in determining whether that star is about to explode.
The passage says that the star Betelgeuse has recently become very dim.
It then implies that Betelgeuse may have become dim because it is about to explode, in which case it may have become dim because it has expelled debris that has surrounded it.
Next the passage says that, alternatively, Betelgeuse may have become very dim because two of its dimming cycles have converged.
Notice that the passage indicates that it is not clear that these dimming cycles have converged, and notice that the passage terms the dimming cycles “somewhat regular.”

So, we can tell that part of the reason why it is not clear whether Betelgeuse is about to explode or two of its dimming cycles have converged is that people are not really sure about the timing of the dimming cycles.After all, if people understood exactly the timing of Betelgeuse’s dimming cycles, then, of course, they would know whether two of them have converged.

The passage also says that “stars fluctuate and change all the time, as does Betelgeuse,” suggesting that the other stars also have dimming cycles and that much of what the passage says about Betelgeuse is also true of other stars.

So, we can see that the passage supports the conclusion that, if someone really understood the timing of a star’s dimming cycles, the person could use that information to determine whether a star that has become extremely dim has likely done so because of the convergence of dimming cycles.

Of course, what follows, then, is that knowing whether a convergence of dimming cycles is responsible for a star’s having become very dim could help a person determine whether that star has become dim because it is about to explode.
So, the passage supports what this choice says, that detailed understanding of the timing of a star’s dimming cycles could be useful in determining whether that star is about to explode.
(D) The greater the volume of debris a star is expelling, the more likely it is that that star will soon go supernova.

The passage says only that a star going supernova will first shed debris.It does not say that stars that are not going supernova do not shed debris or shed less debris than a star that is about to explode in supernova fashion.For all we know from what the passage says, stars often shed lots of debris and shed no more debris when they are about to go supernova than they do at certain other times.
(E) If Betelgeuse is not about to go supernova, it is the case that two of its dimming cycles have converged.

Given what the passage says, it is possible that Betelgeuse is not about to go supernova, and that two of its dimming cycles have converged.However, what this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.
In other words, the passage does not support the conclusion that, if Betelgeuse is not about to go supernova, then, for sure, its dimming cycles have converged.

Notice that the passage never says that expelling debris before going supernova and convergence of dimming cycles are theonlypossible causes of Betelgeuse’s having become very dim.For all we know from what the passage says, there could be many other reasons why Betelgeuse has become so dim.

So, given what the passage says, and contrary to what this choice says, it could be the case that Betelgeuse is not about to go supernova, that its dimming cycles have not converged, and that something other than expelling debris or convergence of dimming cycles is responsible for Betelgeuse’s having become very dim.

question.text: Townsend Paint Supply sells paint and also sells supplies used for painting such as paintbrushes, rollers, and tarps.During recent years, Townsend Paint Supply’s profit margin on paint sales has consistently hovered around 30 percent, while its profit margins on supplies sales have consistently hovered at an average of around 40 percent.Last year, Townsend Paint Supply’s total sales revenue was the same as its total sales revenue was the year before last year, but its profit on sales was significantly lower than its profit on sales was the year before last year.Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the information presented above?

answers.text: The costs to Townsend Paint Supply of some of the painting supplies sold by Townsend Paint Supply were lower last year than they were the year before last year.
The volume of paints of exotic colors sold by Townsend paint supply was not as great last year as it was the year before last year.
The costs to Townsend Paint Supply of many of the paints sold by Townsend Paint Supply were much higher last year than they were the year before last year.
The average retail prices of painting supplies sold by Townsend Paint Supply were significantly lower last year than they were the year before last year.
Last year Townsend Paint Supply sold more paint than it sold the year before last year.

explanation_parts.text: Correctly answering this question requires avoiding jumping to conclusions that seem to be supported by part of what the passage says but that are such that we can see that they are not supported at all once we consider the entire passage.
(A) The costs to Townsend Paint Supply of some of the painting supplies sold by Townsend Paint Supply were lower last year than they were the year before last year.
The passage provides no support at all for this choice.
The passage says that Townsend Paint Supply’s profit margins on supplies sales have consistently hovered at an average of around 40 percent.

All we can tell from that information is that the difference between the cost to Townsend Paint Supply of supplies and the prices at which the supplies are sold has remained around the same in percentage terms.
That information certainly does not indicate that, as this choice says, there has been a decrease in the costs to Townsend Paint Supply of some of the painting supplies sold by Townsend Paint Supply.
(B) The volume of paints of exotic colors sold by Townsend paint supply was not as great last year as it was the year before last year.

The only change from one year to next that the passage mentions is that, last year, Townsend Paint Supply’s total profit on sales was significantly lower than its profit on sales was the year before last year.
The passage provides no information connecting that decrease in profit on sales to a change in the volume of paints of exotic colors sold by Townsend Paint Supply.
Supporting this conclusion requires information not included in the passage.
(C) The costs to Townsend Paint Supply of many of the paints sold by Townsend Paint Supply were much higher last year than they were the year before last year.

What this choice says may seem to be consistent with something the passage says, that, last year, Townsend Paint Supply’s profit on sales was significantly lower than its profit on sales was the year before last year.
It may seem logical that, if Townsend Paint Supply’s profit on sales was lower, the cost to Townsend Paint Supply of paint likely increased.
Notice, however, that something else that the passage says means that the passage does not support this choice at all.
The passage says that Townsend Paint Supply’s profit margin on paint sales has consistently hovered around 30 percent.

If the profit margin on paint sales has consistently hovered around 30 percent, then the reason for the decrease in profits would not be that the cost to Townsend Paint Supply of paints has increased, because the profitability of selling paint has not changed.
So, when we consider all of the information provided by the passage, we see that the passage does not support what this choice says.
(D) The average retail prices of painting supplies sold by Townsend Paint Supply were significantly lower last year than they were the year before last year.

What this choice says may seem to be consistent with something the passage says, that, last year, Townsend Paint Supply’s profit on sales was significantly lower than its profit on sales was the year before last year.
It may seem logical that, if Townsend Paint Supply’s profit on sales was lower, the average retail prices of painting supplies sold by Townsend Paint Supply likely decreased.
Notice, however, that something else that the passage says means that the passage does not support this choice at all.
The passage says that Townsend Paint Supply’s profit margins on supplies sales have consistently hovered at an average of around 40 percent.

If the profit margins on supplies sales have consistently hovered at an average of around 40 percent, then the reason for the decrease in profits would not be that the retail prices of at which Townsend Paint Supply sells supplies have decreased, because the profitability of selling supplies has not changed.
So, when we consider all of the information provided by the passage, we see that the passage does not support what this choice says.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Last year Townsend Paint Supply sold more paint than it sold the year before last year.

We see that the passage says, “Last year, Townsend Paint Supply’s total sales revenue was the same as its total sales revenue was the year before last year, but its total profit on sales was significantly lower than its profit on sales was the year before last year.”
If revenue remained the same, but profit on sales decreased, then margins must have decreased.
The passage says that the margins on sales of paint have hovered at around 30 percent, while margins on sales of supplies have hovered at around 40 percent.
So, the way margins would have decreased is via a decrease in sales of the relatively high margin supplies and an increase in sales of the relatively low margin paint.
Thus, the passage provides support for the conclusion that, as this choice says, last year Townsend Paint Supply sold more paint than it sold the year before last year.

question.text: VO2 max is the maximum amount of oxygen that an athlete can utilize per unit of time.As an athlete’s VO2 max increases, so too does his or her ability to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the molecule that powers each of the cells in our bodies.The higher a person’s cellular levels of ATP are, the more work per unit of time that person’s muscles can perform.All professional endurance athletes have VO2 maxes much greater than the VO2 max of the average person.Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the information above?

answers.text: In most cases, people who have become professional endurance athletes have had to increase their VO2 maxes in order to do so.
If a person’s VO2 max is much greater than that of the average person, that person is probably a professional endurance athlete.
The more a professional endurance athlete trains, the higher that athlete’s V02 max will be.
In at least some cases, it would be possible to determine whether a person is a professional endurance athlete by determining the person’s VO2 max.
If an athlete’s VO2 max is equal to the average person’s, that athlete’s muscles can perform no more work per unit of time than the average person’s muscles can perform.

explanation_parts.text: (A) In most cases, people who have become professional endurance athletes have had to increase their VO2 maxes in order to do so.
The passage says, “All professional endurance athletes have VO2 maxes much greater than the VO2 max of the average person.”

Notice that the passage does not say why professional endurance athletes have VO2 maxes much higher than the VO2 max of the average person.For all we know from what the passage says, most professional endurance athletes have had VO2 maxes greater than the average person’s even without the athletes’ doing anything to increase their VO2 maxes.
Thus, what this answer choice says, that in most cases, people who have become professional endurance athletes have had toincreasetheir VO2 maxes in order to do so, is not supported.
(B) If a person’s VO2 max is much greater than that of the average person, that person is probably a professional endurance athlete.

The passage says, “All professional endurance athletes have VO2 maxes much greater than the VO2 max of the average person” It’s not logical to simply twist that fact around to arrive at “If a person’s VO2 max is much greater than that of the average person, that person is probably a professional endurance athlete.”

After all, there could be many more people whose VO2 maxes are much greater than the average person’s who are not professional endurance athletes than there are professional endurance athletes.For instance, there could be 40 million people whose VO2 maxes are much greater than the average person’s but only 4000 professional endurance athletes.In that case, a person whose VO2 max is greater than average probably would not be a professional endurance athlete.
If the foregoing is hard to see, think of another example.We know that dogs have fur.Right? Can we therefore say that, if something has fur, then it is probably a dog? Of course not.
(C) The more a professional endurance athlete trains, the higher that athlete’s V02 max will be.
This choice could be tempting because what it says is likely to be true in reality.

Notice, however, that nothing said (or implied) by the passage would support what this answer choice says about the results of training.Everything the passage says is about how things are, and the passage says nothing abouthow things became the way they are.
To support this conclusion, we would need information other than the information provided by the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(D) In at least some cases, it would be possible to determine whether a person is a professional endurance athlete by determining the person’s VO2 max.
The doctor states that all professional endurance athletes have VO2 maxes that are much higher than the average person’s.

If that statement is true, then at least in some cases, we know that a person with an average or below-average VO2 max is not a professional endurance athlete.After all, in many cases, if that person were a professional endurance athlete, he or she would have a much higher than average VO2 max.
So, this choice is supported by what the passage says.
(E) If an athlete’s VO2 max is equal to the average person’s, that athlete’s muscles can perform no more work per unit of time than the average person’s muscles can perform.
Let’s look at what the passage says.
The passage says that, as an athlete’s VO2 max increases, so too does his or her ability to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the molecule that powers each of the cells in our bodies.
The passage also says that, the higher a person’s cellular levels of ATP are, the more work per unit of time that person’s muscles can perform.
It follows, then, that, in general, the greater a person’s VO2 max is, the more work per unit of time that person’s muscles can perform.

However, in order to avoid choosing this incorrect answer, we have to notice that the passage does not say that a person’s VO2 max is theonly factorthat affects how much work per unit of time that person’s muscles can do.There could be many other factors that affect how much work per unit of time a person’s muscles can do.

So, it could be the case that,because of factors other than VO2 max, the muscles of one person with the same VO2 max that another person has can do much more work than the muscles of the other person can do.

So, for all we know from what the passage says, the muscles of a well-trained athlete with the same VO2 max as the average person can do much more work per unit of time than the muscles of the average person can do.
Thus, the passage does not really support what this choice says.

question.text: Mature rainforests are close to equilibrium in terms of their effects on atmospheric carbon dioxide, absorbing about the same amount of carbon dioxide as they release.However, the growing trees of new rainforests do remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, storing absorbed carbon dioxide in their woody biomass as they grow.In its first 20 years of growth, an average acre of new rainforest will remove a total of 75 tons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, and the biomass of an average acre of mature rainforest stores the equivalent of 109 tons of carbon dioxide.Which of the following is most strongly supported by the statements given?

answers.text: Any mature trees living in an area of mature rainforest have little to no effect on the atmosphere.
An average acre of rainforest in which tree growth has been going on for 10 years holds around 37.5 tons of carbon dioxide.
The older a tree, the less carbon dioxide that tree absorbs over the course of a year.
Within 20 years, the average acre of rainforest growing today, if allowed to grow naturally, will have had most of the effect on atmospheric carbon dioxide that that acre will ever have.
If percentage of the atmosphere made up of carbon dioxide were higher, trees in the rainforest would mature faster.

explanation_parts.text: (A) Any mature trees living in an area of mature rainforest have little to no effect on the atmosphere.

Although we are told that mature rainforests are close to equilibrium in terms of their effects on atmospheric carbon dioxide, absorbing about the same amount of carbon dioxide as they release, that statement does not allow us to conclude that any mature trees living in an area of mature rainforest havelittle to no effect on the atmosphere.

After all, these trees could have many effects on the atmosphere.In other words, notice that the passage speaks of the mature rainforests as being “close to equilibrium in terms of their effects on atmospheric carbon dioxide.” That statement is a specific one.Right? It’s about effects on atmospheric carbon dioxide only.

However, what this answer speaks about, “little to no effect on the atmosphere,” is a much more general statement.Of course, there are many other effects that mature trees could have on the atmosphere other than those related to carbon dioxide.

In answering a Conclusion question, we have to be careful to notice whether a choice is truly supported by the passage or says something that is much more general than the information provided by the passage.
(B) An average acre of rainforest in which tree growth has been going on for 10 years holds around 37.5 tons of carbon dioxide.

This answer choice tempts us into concluding that there is a linear relationship between the age of an acre of new rainforest and the amount of carbon dioxide that that acre of rainforest has absorbed.
However, if we look closely and carefully at the passage, we can see that the existence of such a relationship is never established.

In other words, if you chose this choice as correct, you probably saw the statement “In its first 20 years of growth, an average acre of new rainforest will remove a total of 75 tons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.” From there, you assumed that if, in the first 20 years of growth, 75 tons is absorbed, then in the first 10 years of growth, half of 75 tons, or 37.5 tons, is absorbed.
However, the passage does not say that there is a linear relationship between the age of an acre of new rainforest and the amount of carbon dioxide that that acre of rainforest has absorbed.

For all we know from what the passage says, an average acre of rainforest in which tree growth has been going on for 10 years can be expected to be storing much less than 37.5 tons of carbon dioxide.After all, the trees would start off small.So, during the first 10 years of its development, an acre of new rainforest might not absorb as much carbon dioxide as it does during the second 10 years of its development.
As always, we must avoid making unwarranted assumptions.
(C) The older a tree, the less carbon dioxide that tree absorbs over the course of a year.

The passage never provides us with enough information to conclude that, the older a tree, the less carbon dioxide that tree absorbs over the course of a year.Notice that information on the relationship between the age of a tree and the amount of carbon dioxide that that tree absorbs over the course of a year is not provided.
Yes, the passage says that mature rainforests are close to equilibrium in terms of their effects on atmospheric carbon dioxide, absorbing about the same amount of carbon dioxide as they release.

Notice, however, that a tree takes years to become mature.So, during some of those years, a tree may absorb more carbon dioxide when it is older than it did when it was younger.For instance, a relatively large, five-year-old tree may absorb more carbon dioxide than it absorbed when it was a tiny, six-month-old tree.
As always, we must avoid making unwarranted assumptions.

YOUR CHOICE(D) Within 20 years, the average acre of rainforest growing today, if allowed to grow naturally, will have had most of the effect on atmospheric carbon dioxide that that acre will ever have.

Notice that we are told that, in its first 20 years of growth, an average acre of new rainforest will remove a total of 75 tons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, and that the biomass of an average acre of mature rainforest stores the equivalent of 109 tons of carbon dioxide.
Some very simple math shows us that 75 is considerably more than half of 109.
Meanwhile, “most of” means “more than half of.”

Therefore, we see that it is reasonable to conclude that, within 20 years, the average acre of rainforest growing today, if allowed to grow naturally, will have had most of the effect on atmospheric carbon dioxide that that acre will ever have.
(E) If percentage of the atmosphere made up of carbon dioxide were higher, trees in the rainforest would mature faster.

This choice could be tempting because the passage says that growing trees remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.So, we might be tempted to believe that, the more carbon dioxide there is available, the faster trees mature.
Also, this choice could be tempting because what it says may be true in reality, since carbon dioxide is a molecule essential to the growth of trees.
However, the truth is that the passage never says anything that indicates that, if the percentage of the atmosphere made up of carbon dioxide were higher, trees in the rainforest would mature faster.
So, this statement is not a conclusion supported by the passage.
In answering a Conclusion question, we must be careful to not choose a choice that says something that is likely true in reality yet is not supported by the passage.

question.text: Because students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school are constantly surrounded by younger students who are not as far along in their development as the older students, students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school tend to become more self-confident than other students.The more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college.The statements above, if true, most strongly support which of the following?

answers.text: The proportion of college students significantly older than average for their classes will likely be greater than the proportion of grade school students significantly older than average for their grades.
The academic performance in grade school of students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school will typically be higher than average.
People who are younger than average for their grade levels in grade school are less likely to go on to attend college than the average person.
Given two applicants with similar academic records but different levels of self-confidence, a college is more likely to admit the more self-confident applicant.
In some cases, students who are significantly older than average for their grades were entered into school late by parents hoping to improve the students’ chances of attending college.

explanation_parts.text: Information given:

Fact 1: Because students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school are constantly surrounded by younger students who are not as far along in their development as the older students, students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school tend to become more self-confident than other students.
Fact 2: The more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college.

YOUR CHOICE(A) The proportion of college students significantly older than average for their classes will likely be greater than the proportion of grade school students significantly older than average for their grades.

The passage says that students who are older than average for their grade levels in grade school tend to become more self-confident than other school children, and that, the more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college.
What follows, then, is that students who are significantly older than average for their grades are more likely to attend college than other students are.
So, what we would expect is that the proportion of older-than-average-for-their-grades students who attend college will be greater than the proportion of other students who will attend college.

The logical result of this pattern is that, as this choice says, the proportion of college students significantly older than average for their classes will likely be greater than the proportion of grade school students significantly older than average for their grades.
To see how this dynamic works, consider the following.
Older-Than-Average Grade School Students:10
Other Grade School Students:90
Total Grade School Students:100
Older-Than-Average Grade School Students/Total Grade School Students:10/100 = 1/10
Now, let’s say that 60 percent of the older-than-average students attend college and 40 percent of the other students attend college.We get the following outcome.
Older-Than-Average College Students:10 x 0.6 = 6
Other College Students:90 x 0.4 = 36
Total College Students:6 + 36 = 42
Older-Than-Average College Students/Total College Students:6/42 = 1/7

We can see that since the proportion of older-than-average students who attend college, 0.6, is greater than the proportion of other students who attend college, 0.4, the proportion of college students older than average for their classes, 1/7, ends up being greater than the proportion of grade school students older than average for their grades, 1/10.
(B) The academic performance in grade school of students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school will typically be higher than average.
Notice that the passage never makes mention of how academic performance in grade school is linked to age or self-confidence.
What the passage says about age and self-confidence links them only to the likelihood of a student’s attending college.
Therefore, what this statement says is not supported by the passage.
(C) People who are younger than average for their grade levels in grade school are less likely to go on to attend college than the average person.

This choice is tempting, because what the passage says tends to indicate that people who are older than average for their grade levels are more likely than average to attend college.So, we might be tempted to decide that the passage also supports the conclusion that the reverse is true, in other words, that students who are younger than average for their grade levels are less self-confident than average and are less likely than average to attend college.

However, the passage doesn’t say anything about students who are younger than average, and concluding what this choice says would require making unwarranted assumptions.After all, for all we know from what the passage says, students who are younger than average for their grade levels are more likely than average to attend college.

Perhaps students who are younger than average for their grade levels get extra attention, or perhaps they are younger than average for their grade levels because they are especially motivated and, therefore, are ahead of where they would otherwise be in school and, thus, rather likely to attend college.
So, we can see that what the passage says about older-than-average students doesn’t really support what this choice says about younger-than-average students.
(D) Given two applicants with similar academic records but different levels of self-confidence, a college is more likely to admit the more self-confident applicant.

Notice that what the passage says about self-confidence and attending college is specific and limited.It says only that, the more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college.

The passage does not say why it is that, the more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college, and it certainly does not say what this choice says, which is basically that colleges consider self-confidence in making admissions decisions.

There are many possible reasons for the fact that, the more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college.So, it does not have to be the case that what underlies that pattern is that colleges admit relatively self-confident applicants over other applicants.

Of course, it is quite possible that self-confidence is a factor in college admissions, but, to correctly answer this question, we have to choose a choice that is supported by what the passage says rather than just possible.
(E) In some cases, students who are significantly older than average for their grades were entered into school late by parents hoping to improve the students’ chances of attending college.

The passage says, “students who are significantly older than average for their grade levels in grade school tend to become more self-confident than other students,” and “The more self-confident people are, the more likely they are to attend college.”

So, it’s plausible that, in some cases, parents who are aware of these facts have done what this choice describes, entered their children into school late, hoping to improve their children’s chances of attending college.
