However, while it’s plausible that some parents have done what this choice describes, that some parents have done so is basically a guess on our part, and is not clearly supported by anything that the passage says.The passage says nothing about why some students are significantly older than average for their classes, and certainly does not say anything that clearly indicates that, in some cases, students are significantly older than average for their classes because their parents started them in school late for the purpose of improving their chances of attending college.

In answering a Conclusion question, we must be careful not to get fooled into choosing a choice that merely sounds plausible or matches something that we are aware goes on in reality but is not supported by anything that the passage says.

question.text: Smartphones contain sensors essential to their operation, such as accelerometers, which enable devices to sense steps taken by users, and magnetometers, which allow devices to orient users’ positions on map applications.Because smartphone applications do not need special permissions to access these sensors, hackers can use sensor-generated data for malicious purposes.For example, data from smartphone accelerometers can be used to acquire passwords, because each user impact on a character on a device’s screen results in a predictable acceleration of the device.If the statements above are true, which of the following is most strongly supported by them?

answers.text: By putting into place security protocols that recognize and block malicious use of sensor-generated data, smartphone manufactures could improve the security of phones.
Manufacturers of smartphones should improve the security of their devices by installing fewer sensors that collect data.
Once smartphones require applications installed in them to have special permissions before accessing data from the phone’s sensors, there will be fewer breaches of people’s data.
To protect their passwords and other sensitive information, smartphone users must limit the number and variety of applications that they install on their smartphones.
The sensors installed on smartphones should be designed to encrypt the data that they generate so that only the owners of the devices can access the data collected by them.

explanation_parts.text: YOUR CHOICE(A) By putting into place security protocols that recognize and block malicious use of sensor-generated data, smartphone manufactures could improve the security of phones.

The passage says that, currently, because smartphone applications do not need special permissions to access sensors in smartphones that generate data, hackers can use sensor-generated data for malicious purposes.That information indicates that currently, smartphones are vulnerable to being breached by hackers who use that data.

What follows, then, is what this choice says, that, by putting into place security protocols that recognize and block malicious use of sensor-generated data, smartphone manufactures could improve the security of phones.
So, this choice is supported by what the passage says.
(B) Manufacturers of smartphones should improve the security of their devices by installing fewer sensors that collect data.

While what the passage says supports the conclusion that manufacturerscouldimprove the security of devices by installing fewer sensors that collect data, the conclusion that the manufacturersshoulddo so does not follow from what the passage says.

After all, the passage says that the sensors are “essential to their (smartphones’) operation.” So, what follows from what the passage says is that, by installing fewer sensors, manufacturers would reduce the functionality of smartphones.
So, should manufacturers install fewer sensors and reduce the functionality of smartphones? The passage does not indicate one way or another what the manufacturersshoulddo.
(C) Once smartphones require applications installed in them to have special permissions before accessing data from the phone’s sensors, there will be fewer breaches of people’s data.
The information provided by the passage does not support the prediction stated by this choice.

The passage does not provide information on how many breaches of user data result currently from the sensors’ not requiring applications to have special permission to access the data.For all we know from what the passage says, no breaches of user data currently result from the sensors’ not requiring applications to have special permission to access the data, in which case there could not be fewer breaches.

Furthermore, even if, currently, some breaches of data do result from the sensors’ not requiring applications to have special permission to access the data, for all we know from what the passage says, if hackers could not breach user data by accessing the sensor-generated data, hackers would readily get user data in another way, in which case requiring applications to have special permissions before accessing data from the phone’s sensors would not cause there to be fewer breaches of people’s data.

So, while, given what the passage says, what this choice says is possible, what this choice says is not supported by what the passage says, because the passage does not provide the types of information necessary for supporting this prediction.
(D) To protect their passwords and other sensitive information, smartphone users must limit the number and variety of applications that they install on their smartphones.

The passage does not support the conclusion that smartphone users “must” take the action of limiting the number and variety of applications that they install on their smartphones in order to protect their passwords and other sensitive information.
For one thing, the passage never says that a smartphone with a greater number of applications is more accessible to hackers than one with a limited number of applications.
Furthermore, there could be many ways for users to protect sensitive information other than limiting the number and variety of applications that they install on their smartphones.
So, what this choice says, that users “must” take this particular action, is not supported.
(E) The sensors installed on smartphones should be designed to encrypt the data that they generate so that only the owners of the devices can access the data collected by them.
This choice does not follow from what the passage says.

The passage does not say that owners of smartphones have any use for or ever access the sensor-generated data.So, the passage does not support the conclusion that, through encryption, the data should be made available only to the owners of the phones.

Furthermore, what the passage says indicates that the phones use the sensor-generated data.Since that information indicates that making the data available only to the owners, and not to the phones themselves, would interfere with the functioning of the phones, if anything, what the passage says tends to conflict with what this choice says.

question.text: In some areas, the presence of gold becomes apparent through the testing of surface soil to assess its composition.In other areas, conclusively determining whether gold is present requires testing soil from deep beneath the surface for gold, a process that can be relatively costly, since deeply buried soil can be more costly to acquire than soil near the soil surface.However, since, in order to find water when conditions are dry, termites often dig deep into the soil and then use soil from deep levels for building mounds on the soil surface, miners can discover deeply buried gold by testing soil in termite mounds.All of the following are conclusions supported by the statements above EXCEPT:

answers.text: Testing of deeply buried soil for gold is unlikely to be undertaken unless there is reason to believe that that deeply buried soil may differ in composition from surface soil.
Testing termite mounds for the presence of gold is the most cost-effective method for determining whether gold lies deep beneath an area’s soil surface.
A lack of gold in the composition of a termite mound that was constructed when conditions were wet does not indicate that there is no gold deep beneath that mound.
Tests of the composition of a termite mound constructed of soil from an area at a significant horizontal distance from the mound could produce results that could mislead miners.
In areas with abundant and consistent rainfall, testing termite mounds for the presence of gold is unlikely to generate results significantly different from those generated via testing surface soil.

explanation_parts.text: Fact 1: In some areas, the presence of gold becomes apparent through the testing of surface soil to assess its composition.
Fact 2: In other areas, conclusively determining whether gold is present requires testing soil from deep beneath the surface for gold.
Fact 3: Testing soil from deep beneath the surface for gold can be relatively costly, since deeply buried soil can be more costly to acquire than soil near the soil surface.

Fact 4: Since, in order to find water when conditions are dry, termites often dig deep into the soil and then use soil from deep levels for building mounds on the surface, miners can discover deeply buried gold by testing soil in termite mounds.

Since this is a Conclusion EXCEPT question, our job is to find the one answer choice that is a statementnotsupported by the facts.The other four answer choices, the incorrect choices, state conclusions supported by the facts provided.
(A) Testing of deeply buried soil for gold is unlikely to be undertaken unless there is reason to believe that that deeply buried soil may differ in composition from surface soil.
The passage describes testing soil from deep beneath the surface as “relatively costly, since deeply buried soil can be more costly to acquire than soil near the soil surface.”
It also says that, in some areas, the presence of gold becomes apparent through the testing of surface soil to assess its composition.

What follows, then, is that, because of the relative costliness of testing deeply buried soil, such testing would be unlikely to be undertaken unless there is reason to believe that that deeply buried soil may differ in composition from surface soil.

After all, since testing of surface soil can reveal the presence of gold, if there were no reason to believe that the soil deep beneath the surface may differ in composition from the surface soil, there would be no reason to undertake the relatively costly testing of the deeply buried soil.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Testing termite mounds for the presence of gold is the most cost-effective method for determining whether gold lies deep beneath an area’s soil surface.

The passage says that “deeply buried soil can be more costly to acquire than soil near the soil surface.” So, we might be tempted to believe that the passage supports the conclusion that testing termite mounds, which often contain soil from deep beneath the surface, is the most cost-effective method for determining whether gold lies deep beneath an area’s soil surface.After all, testing a mound built on the soil surface is likely more cost-effective than acquiring and testing soil from deep beneath the surface.

However, even if it is true that testing termite mounds for gold is more cost effective than testing soil acquired from deep beneath the soil surface, the passage does not support the conclusion that testing termite mounds is themostcost-effective method for determining whether gold lies deep beneath an area’s soil surface.

After all the passage never provides us with information regarding all of the methods, and their associated costs, for determining whether gold lies deep beneath an area’s soil surface There could be other methods not mentioned that are more cost effective than testing termite mounds.
So, in saying that testing termite mounds for the presence of gold is themostcost-effective method, this choice goes beyond what the passage supports.
Conclusion supported by the passage? No.
(C) A lack of gold in the composition of a termite mound that was constructed when conditions were wet does not indicate that there is no gold deep beneath that mound.
Notice that the passage says that, in order to find water when conditions are dry, termites often dig deep into the soil and then use soil from deep levels for building mounds on the soil surface.

What follows is that, when conditions arenotdry, i.e., when conditions arewet, termites would not have a reason to dig deep into the soil, in which case they would not use soil from deep levels for building mounds.They would use soil from close to the surface.

Of course, then, as this choice says, a lack of gold in the composition of a termite mound that was constructed when conditions were wet does not indicate that there is no gold deep beneath that mound, because a mound constructed when conditions were wet would not be composed of soil from deep levels.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.
(D) Tests of the composition of a termite mound constructed of soil from an area at a significant horizontal distance from the mound could produce results that could mislead miners.

What this choice says is supported by the passage.Tests of the composition of a termite mound constructed of soil from an area at a significant distance from the mound could produce results that could mislead miners for sure.

Imagine if some termites dug deep into the soil and brought that soil to the surface.Then, these termites walked 100 yards to where their termite mound was.If we were to test the soil used to make the termite mound, wouldn’t there be a strong possibility that we would be misled? After all, what if there were a considerable quantity of gold in the soil where the termites dug, but no gold in the soil below the termite mound?
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.

(E) In areas with abundant and consistent rainfall, testing termite mounds for the presence of gold is unlikely to generate results significantly different from those generated via testing surface soil.

The passage says that the reason why miners can discover deeply buried gold by testing soil in termite mounds is that, when conditions are dry, termites often dig deep into the soil and then use soil from deep levels for building mounds on the soil surface.

Given that information, we can see that what follows is that, if conditions are not dry, termites will not dig deep into the soil, in which case their mounds will not include soil from deep beneath the surface.

Of course, in an area with “abundant and consistent rainfall,” conditions may almost never be dry enough to cause termites to dig deep beneath the soil surface, in which case their mounds will likely be built entirely or almost entirely of surface soil.In such a situation, testing termite mounds for the presence of gold is unlikely to generate results significantly different from those generated via testing surface soil.
So, we can see that what this choice says is supported by the passage.
Conclusion supported by the passage? Yes.

question.text: As the proportion of parents in Percingham who are highly educated has been increasing, the percentage of Percingham high school students who graduate has also been increasing.Also, since more educated parents tend to be more aware of the environmental consequences of helium balloon use, sales of helium balloons for children’s birthday parties in Percingham have been decreasing steadily.Recently, a group of Percingham parents succeeded in raising awareness in Percingham children of the environmental consequences of helium balloon use, with the result that very few children in Percingham want helium balloons at their birthday parties.Which of the following is most strongly supported by the above statements?

answers.text: Only by selling balloons not filled with helium will Percingham businesses that currently sell helium balloons be able to avoid significant decreases in annual revenue.
It is currently the case that very few parents of children in Percingham are not highly educated.
As the children currently living in Percingham grow up, helium balloon sales in Percingham will rebound.
Any correlation that has existed between trends in helium balloon sales and future high school graduation rates in Percingham is likely to cease to exist.
In Percingham, sales of helium balloons for purposes not related to children’s birthday parties are likely to increase.

explanation_parts.text: (A) Only by selling balloons not filled with helium will Percingham businesses that currently sell helium balloons be able to avoid significant decreases in annual revenue.

Since the passage says, “Recently, a group of Percingham parents succeeded in raising awareness in Percingham children of the environmental consequences of helium balloon use, with the result that very few children in Percingham want helium balloons at their birthday parties,” it seems likely that the number of helium balloons sold by Percingham businesses will be significantly lower going forward than it was until recently.
So, it makes sense to conclude that the amount of revenue that these businesses generate annually by selling helium balloons will be lower than what it was in the past.

However, concluding from that information that, only by selling balloons not filled with helium, will Percingham businesses that currently sell helium balloons be able to avoid significant decreases in annual revenue does not make sense.

For all we know from what the passage says, there could be many ways other than selling balloons not filled with helium for these businesses to make up for decreases in revenue resulting from decreases in helium balloon sales and, thus, avoid significant decreases in annual revenue.
So, this choice is not supported by the passage.
(B) It is currently the case that very few parents of children in Percingham are not highly educated.

The passage says that the number of parents in Percingham who are highly educated has been increasing.However, it does not provide any information indicating that the number of parents in Percingham who are highly educated has increased so much that, as this choice says, currently, very few parents in Percingham are not highly educated.
(C) As the children currently living in Percingham grow up, helium balloon sales in Percingham will rebound.

The passage provides two pieces of information about or closely related to helium balloon sales: that sales of helium balloons for children’s birthday parties in Percingham have been decreasing steadily, and that very few children in Percingham want helium balloons at their birthday parties.

While it is possible that, as this choice says, as the children currently living in Percingham grow up, helium balloon sales in Percingham will rebound, the information provided by the passage does not clearly indicate that helium balloon sales will rebound.They could continue to decrease, or they could rebound, and which they will do is not clearly indicated by the information provided.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Any correlation that has existed between trends in helium balloon sales and future high school graduation rates in Percingham is likely to cease to exist.
To see why this choice is correct, let’s consider what has been going on in Percingham.
The passage says that, as the proportion of parents in Percingham who are highly educated has been increasing, the percentage of Percingham high school students who graduate has also been increasing.
So, we have two related trends: the proportion of parents who are highly educated has been increasing, and the proportion of high school students who graduate has been increasing.

The passage also says that, since more educated parents tend to be more aware of the environmental consequences of helium balloon use, sales of helium balloons for children’s birthday parties in Percingham have been decreasing steadily.

Given that information, and the information about the proportion of parents who are highly educated increasing, we can see that there have been related trends: the proportion of parents who are highly educated has been increasing, and sales of helium balloons for children’s birthday parties have been decreasing.

Now, here’s the thing.If, as the proportion of highly educated parents has been increasing, sales of helium balloons have been decreasing, and the proportion of high school students who graduate has been increasing, then there has been a negative correlation between helium balloon sales and high school graduation rate, i.e., helium balloon sales and the high school graduation rate have been going in opposite directions.

OK, but what will happen now that the parents in Percingham have convinced all children there that helium balloons are terrible for the environment, and so, helium balloon sales for children’s parties have ceased almost entirely? In that case, the trend of the decrease in helium balloon sales will cease to exist, because helium balloon sales can’t decrease much more.They are basically over.

In that case, even if the proportion of parents who are highly educated continues to increase, and the graduation rate continues to increase, helium balloon sales won’t decrease.So, the negative correlation between helium balloon sales and high school graduation rates is likely to cease to exist.
So, we can see that this choice is indeed supported by the passage.
(E) In Percingham, sales of helium balloons for purposes not related to children’s birthday parties are likely to increase.

The passage provides two pieces of information about or closely related to helium balloon sales: that sales of helium balloons for children’s birthday parties in Percingham have been decreasing steadily, and that very few children in Percingham want helium balloons at their birthday parties.

The passage provides no other information about helium balloon sales or any other trend related information that supports the conclusion that sales of helium balloons for purposes not related to children’s birthday parties are likely to increase.
Simply put, the fact that very few children want helium balloons at their birthday parties does not support the conclusion that sales of helium balloons for other purposes will increase.

question.text: The deer carrying capacity of an area of land is the number of deer that that area can sustain in the long term.The carrying capacity is constrained by characteristics of resources available in that area, such as the area’s plant growth, as deer eat only plant matter.If the number of deer inhabiting an area is at or below the carrying capacity, the plants in the area can replenish themselves as fast as the deer eat them.If the number of deer exceeds the carrying capacity, the deer will eat the plants faster than the plants can replenish.If the statements above are accurate, which of the following conclusions is supported by them?

answers.text: If the number of deer inhabiting an area exceeds the deer carrying capacity of that area, some of them will migrate to another area.
A certain number of deer can sustainably inhabit an area as long as there is sufficient plant growth to support that number of deer.
By counting the deer inhabiting an area, one can determine the carrying capacity of that area.
Whenever the number of deer in an area exceeds that area’s carrying capacity, the deer run out of food.
One could confirm that the deer inhabiting an area do not exceed the number of deer that area can sustainably feed by analyzing trends in the presence of plants there.

explanation_parts.text: Information we are given:
Fact 1: The deer carrying capacity of an area of land is the number of deer that that area can sustain in the long term.
Fact 2: The carrying capacity is constrained by the resources available in that area, such as the area’s plant growth, as deer eat only plant matter.
Fact 3: If the number of deer inhabiting an area is at or below the carrying capacity, the plants in the area can replenish themselves as fast as the deer eat them.
Fact 4: If the number of deer exceeds the carrying capacity, the deer will eat the plants faster than the plants can replenish.
(A) If the number of deer inhabiting an area exceeds the deer carrying capacity of that area, some of them will migrate to another area.
The passage does not support the conclusion that, if the number of deer inhabiting an area exceeds the deer carrying capacity of that area, some of them will migrate to another area.

Look carefully at the facts presented.Notice we have no information indicating that the deer will move to a different area.In fact, it may be true that the deer cannot migrate to a new area because of roads or mountains.Alternatively, perhaps, it’s not in their nature to migrate to another area.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A certain number of deer can sustainably inhabit an area as long as there is sufficient plant growth to support that number of deer.

That a certain number of deer can sustainably inhabit an area as long as there is sufficient plant growth to support that number of deer implies that the ONLY thing deer need to sustainably inhabit an area is sufficient plant growth.
Of course, such an idea is not supported by the passage.

Even in thinking about it logically, we see that what this choice says does not make much sense.After all, what if an area had lots of plant growth but was so hot that deer could not survive there? What if an area had lots of plant growth but no level spots where deer could sleep? Would deer be able to sustainably inhabit such areas? Highly unlikely …
In answering a Conclusion question, we have to be careful not to distort facts to arrive at unsupported conclusions.

In this case, we have to be careful not to distort the fact that there must be sufficient plant growth in order for an area to sustain a certain number of deer to arrive at the conclusion that, if there is sufficient plant growth in an area, that area can support a certain number of deer.
(C) By counting the deer inhabiting an area, one can determine the carrying capacity of that area.

The passage never gives us any indication that, by counting the deer inhabiting an area, one can determine the carrying capacity of that area.Look back at the passage.Spend two hours if you need to.Try to find even of modicum of evidence to support what this answer choice says.You will not find such evidence.
Remember, we must always be laser-focused on the fine details presented in Critical Reasoning passages.

The passage discusses what happens if number of deer inhabiting an area “is at or below the carrying capacity” of the area and what happens if the number of deer “exceeds the carrying capacity” of the area.

So, the passage seems to support the conclusion that the number of deer inhabiting an area may or may not be in line with the carrying capacity of the area, a fact that tends to indicate that, contrary to what this choice says, onecannotdetermine the carrying capacity of an area by counting the deer inhabiting that area.
(D) Whenever the number of deer in an area exceeds that area’s carrying capacity, the deer run out of food.

The passage says, “The deer carrying capacity of an area of land is the number of deer that that area can sustainin the long term,” and “If the number of deer exceeds the carrying capacity, the deer will eat the plants faster than the plants can replenish.” So, the passage may seem to support what this choice says.
However, notice that this choice says, “Wheneverthe number of deer in an area exceeds that area’s carrying capacity, the deer run out of food.”
The word “whenever” is synonymous with “every time.”
So, this choice says, essentially, “Every timethe number of deer in an area exceeds that area’s carrying capacity, the deer run out of food.”
There is a big difference between “in the long term” and “whenever.”

For instance, some deer could migrate through an area that is already close to its deer carrying capacity, and it could be that, while those migrating deer are in that area, the number of deer in that area exceeds the area’s deer carrying capacity for a few days.Will the deer run out of food during those few days? Probably not.
We could come up with other scenarios in which the number of deer in an area would temporarily exceed the area’s deer carrying capacity and yet the deer would not run out of food.
So, we can see that what this choice says, thatwheneverthe number of deer in an area exceeds that area’s carrying capacity, the deer run out of food, is not supported by what the passage says.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) One could confirm that the deer inhabiting an area do not exceed the number of deer that area can sustainably feed by analyzing trends in the presence of plants there.
The statements in the passage support what this choice says.

The passage says that, if the number of deer inhabiting an area is at or below the carrying capacity, which is the number of deer that that area can sustain, the plants in the area can replenish themselves as fast as the deer eat them.If the number of deer exceeds the carrying capacity, the deer will eat the plants faster than the plants can replenish and run out of food.

Given those statements, we can tell that one could confirm that the deer inhabiting an area do not exceed the number of deer that area can sustainably feed by analyzing trends in the presence of plants there.

question.text: A group of surfers were chosen by Olympic surfing team organizers to train for possible participation in the Olympics under the leadership of one of the world’s most successful surfers.While all of the surfers had tremendous skill, not all made the team.Only those who were able to master their fear and anxiety were able to perform well enough during the training to be chosen for the team.If the above statements are true, which of the following is most strongly supported by them?

answers.text: Ability to master fear and anxiety is the most important ingredient for success in Olympic surfing.
It is possible to develop the level of surfing skill necessary for being considered for an Olympic surfing team without being able to master fear and anxiety.
Any person who has been chosen to compete in the Olympics must have successfully eradicated fear.
In order to be competitive in Olympic surfing, one must undergo coaching in mental preparedness as well as physical training.
Considering someone’s level of surfing skill is usually insufficient for determining whether that person would make an Olympic surfing team.

explanation_parts.text: In answering this question, we’re seeking the answer choice that is most strongly supported by the passage.Remember that “most strongly supported” does not necessarily mean “must be true.” Rather, it means the answer choice that most logically follows from the information presented.

In choosing the choice that is most strongly supported, we have to be careful not to choose a choice that says something that is related to what the passage says but isn’t really supported by what the passage says.So, as we go through the choices, we have to be aware of why we are considering choosing a choice.Are we attracted to a choice because it says something merely related to what the passage says, or does the choice we are considering logically follow from what the passage says?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Ability to master fear and anxiety is the most important ingredient for success in Olympic surfing.
What this choice says goes beyond what the passage tells us.
The passage says that “only those who were able to master their fear and anxiety were able to perform well enough during the training to be chosen for the team.”

Notice that the passage does not say that the ability to master fear and anxiety isthe most important ingredient.The passage indicates only that the ability to master fear and anxiety isnecessary for success.
Somethings’ being “necessary” is not the same as it’s being “most important.”
Many things, such as surfing skills and the ability to get along with others, are necessary for success in Olympic surfing.They are not all “the most important ingredient.”
We must be careful to not choose as correct a choice that says something that goes beyond what is supported by the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) It is possible to develop the level of surfing skill necessary for being considered for an Olympic surfing team without being able to master fear and anxiety.
Notice that the passage says that “A group of surfers were chosen by Olympic surfing team organizers to train for possible participation in the Olympics.”

Presumably, all of those chosen by Olympic surfing team organizers to train for possible participation in the Olympics had developed “the level of surfing skill necessary for being considered for an Olympic surfing team.”
The passage says also that “not all made the team.Only those who were able to master their fear and anxiety were able to perform well enough during the training to be chosen for the team.”
So, we can see that “not all” were “able to master their fear and anxiety.”

So, what the passage says supports the conclusion that it is possible to develop the level of surfing skill necessary for being considered for an Olympic surfing team without being able to master fear and anxiety.
(C) Any person who has been chosen to compete in the Olympics must have successfully eradicated fear.
Notice that the passage discusses only surfers and only one particular occasion on which surfers were training for the Olympic surfing team.
Now, notice what this choice is about: any person who has been chosen to compete in the Olympics.
The information we have about what happened involving surfers on that one occasion does not support a general conclusion about anyone who ever has been chosen to compete in the Olympics.
(D) In order to be competitive in Olympic surfing, one must undergo coaching in mental preparedness as well as physical training.
The passage says that only those surfers who were able to master their fear and anxiety were able to perform well enough to be chosen for the Olympic team.

Notice that the passage does not say anything about how those surfers who made the team became able to master their fear and anxiety, and it certainly does not say that those surfers underwent “coaching in mental preparedness.”
For all we know what from the passage says, some or all of the surfers who were able to master their fear and anxiety did so without any coaching in mental preparedness.
So, the passage does not really support what this choice says.
(E) Considering someone’s level of surfing skill is usually insufficient for determining whether that person would make an Olympic surfing team.
Notice that the passage discusses just one group of athletes training to surf in the Olympics, so a relatively small sample size.

While the passage does say that all of these surfers had “tremendous skill” and that not all made the Olympic team, we have to be careful not to make a broad generalization from what occurred in this one instance, with this one group of surfers.
After all, the passage does not indicate what "usually" happens or whether the circumstance with this particular group of surfers was typical or atypical for the sport.
Thus, this choice is not supported by the passage.

question.text: Concerned Citizen: The department of education would have us believe that the fact that our region’s elementary school students’ average standardized test scores have increased indicates that our region’s school system is more effective at educating students than it was in the recent past.The truth is that the teachers in our region’s schools have been told that their pay and promotions will be based in large part on their students’ test scores, and they are focusing on the material they know will appear on standardized tests, while teaching little to nothing else.Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the statements made?

answers.text: At the completion of their elementary education, the children currently in the region’s elementary schools are likely to be much better at rote memorization than at critical thinking.
Currently, the quality of elementary education in the region depends on the quality of the design of standardized tests given to elementary school students.
The region’s elementary school teachers care more about pay and promotion than they do about ensuring that the students they teach become well educated.
The elementary school system in the region is, at best, no more effective at educating students than it was in the recent past.
Currently, the region’s elementary school teachers are covering in class a range of topics narrower than is ideal for the education of elementary school students.

explanation_parts.text: Notice that the passage says only four things:

Fact 1: The department of education would have us believe that the fact that our region’s elementary school students’ average standardized test scores have increased indicates that our region’s school system is more effective at educating students than it was in the recent past.
Fact 2 (within Fact 1): Our region’s elementary school students’ average standardized test scores have increased.
Fact 3: The teachers in our region’s schools have been told that their pay and promotions will be based in large part on their students’ test scores.
Fact 4: The teachers are focusing on the material they know will appear on standardized tests, while teaching little to nothing else.
To arrive at the correct answer to this question, we have to be careful not to put our own spin on what the passage says or go beyond what’s supported.
The passage does not say anything specific about what the students are learning or how they are learning it.So, we have to make sure not to decide that the passage does say things about those topics.
YOUR CHOICE(A) At the completion of their elementary education, the children currently in the region’s elementary schools are likely to be much better at rote memorization than at critical thinking.

This choice could be tempting, because we could see that the passage says that teachers in the region “are focusing on the material they know will appear on standardized tests, while teaching little to nothing else” and decide that that information indicates that the students are memorizing lots of information and not learning to do critical thinking.
But does it really indicate that?
Does the choice or the passage say what the standardized tests test? No.

So, the passage does not support the line of thinking that the students are preparing for the standardized tests by memorizing lots of information and that, therefore, at the completion of their elementary education, the children currently in the region’s elementary schools are likely be much better at rote memorization than at critical thinking.
In fact, for all we know from the information given, the students are preparing for the standardized tests by learning to think critically.
We have to be careful not to read into what the passages say things that the passages don’t actually say.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) Currently, the quality of elementary education in the region depends on the quality of the design of standardized tests given to elementary school students.

The passage says, “the teachers in our region’s schools have been told that their pay and promotions will be based in large part on their students’ test scores, and they are focusing on the material they know will appear on standardized tests, while teaching little to nothing else.”

What can be reasonably concluded on the basis of that information is that the regions’ students’ educations depend on what the tests test, because that’s all the teachers are teaching.If the tests test useless things, the students will learn useless things.If tests are well designed and test useful things, the students will learn useful things.

So, the passage supports what this choice says, that, currently, the quality of elementary education in the region depends on the quality of the design of standardized tests given to elementary school students.
(C) The region’s elementary school teachers care more about pay and promotion than they do about ensuring that the students they teach become well educated.

This choice could be tempting because we could see that the passage says that “the teachers in our region’s schools have been told that their pay and promotions will be based in large part on their students’ test scores, and they are focusing on the material they know will appear on standardized tests, while teaching little to nothing else” and decide that it makes sense to conclude that, as this choice says, the region’s elementary school teachers care more about pay and promotion than they do about ensuring that the students they teach become well educated.

However, the truth is the passage alone does not support what this choice says.In order to conclude what this choice says, we need the additional information that, by focusing on what they know will appear on the standardized tests, the teachers are not ensuring that the students they teach will become well educated.

Since we don’t have that additional information, for all we know, by focusing on what they know will appear on the standardized tests, the teachersareensuring that the students they teach will become well educated.
(D) The elementary school system in the region is, at best, no more effective at educating students than it was in the recent past.
The passage provides no information to support what this choice says.
This choice states a comparison between how effective at educating students the elementary school system is now and how effective it was in the past.

The only information the passage provides that supports any comparison of what’s occurring currently with what occurred in the past is that the region’s elementary school students’ average standardized test scores have increased.
That information does not support what this choice says.
If anything, that information supports the conclusion that, contrary to what this choice says, the elementary school system is more effective in educating students than it was in the past.
(E) Currently, the region’s elementary school teachers are covering in class a range of topics narrower than is ideal for the education of elementary school students.

This choice could be tempting, because we could see that the passage says, “the teachers are focusing on the material they know will appear on standardized tests, while teaching little to nothing else,” and decide that that statement supports what this choice says.

However, the truth is that the passage does not indicate that what the teachers are covering, which is “the material they know will appear on standardized tests” is “a range of topics narrower than is ideal for the education of elementary school students.” For all we know from what the passage says, the material that will appear on standardized tests comprises a range of topics that isnotnarrower than is ideal for the education of elementary school students.
Thus, the information in the passage does not support what this choice says.

question.text: It had always been the case that the seaside town of Mergate experienced the largest influx of visitors during the week in which the nation’s birthday fell.Therefore, the people of Mergate had attributed the size of the influx to the celebration of that holiday.However, after the Mergate Arts Festival, which traditionally had been held during the week of the nation’s birthday, began to be held during another particular summer week, the number of visitors in Mergate during the nation’s birthday week became no greater than the number there during other summer weeks in which holidays fall.The statements above most strongly support which of the following conclusions?

answers.text: The seaside town of Mergate and its beaches are not on their own very attractive to potential visitors.
The most popular reason why people choose to visit the seaside town of Mergate during the summer is the existence of the Mergate Arts Festival.
More people visit the seaside town of Mergate during summer weeks in which holidays fall than do during any other week of the year.
The celebration of the nation’s birthday has no effect on the number of visitors to the seaside town of Mergate during the week in which the nation’s birthday falls.
During the particular week during which the Arts Festival is now held, there are in Mergate more visitors than there would be were the festival held during another week.

explanation_parts.text: Correctly answering this question requires differentiating between incorrect choices that seem supported by the passage but go beyond what is supported by the passage and the correct answer, which is clearly supported by what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The seaside town of Mergate and its beaches are not on their own very attractive to potential visitors.

What the passage says, that, after the Mergate Arts Festival, which traditionally had been held during the week of the nation’s birthday, began to be held during another particular summer week, the number of visitors in Mergate during the nation’s birthday week became no greater than the number there during other summer weeks in which holidays fall, indicates that the Arts Festival attracts people to Mergate.

However, that information does not indicate that nothing else about Mergate, such as the beaches, is very attractive to visitors.For all we know from what the passage says, both the Arts Festival and the beaches are attractive to potential visitors.
(B) The most popular reason why people choose to visit the seaside town of Mergate during the summer is the existence of the Mergate Arts Festival.

Since the passage says that, after the Mergate Arts Festival was moved to another summer week, the number of visitors in Mergate during the nation’s birthday week became no greater than the number there during other summer weeks in which holidays fall, it is reasonable to conclude that at least some people go to Mergate because of the Arts Festival.

However, concluding that some people choose to go to Mergate because of the festival is much different from concluding that the existence of the festival is “the most popular reason” why people visit Mergate during the summer.
What this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.
(C) More people visit the seaside town of Mergate during summer weeks in which holidays fall than do during any other week of the year.
This choice is tempting because what it says is plausible and matches what goes on in reality.
However, the truth is that the passage does not support what this choice says.

The only thing the passage says about holiday weeks is that the number of visitors in Mergate during the nation’s birthday week became no greater than the number there during other summer weeks in which holidays fall.

That statement does not indicate that holiday weeks are the most popular weeks.For all we know from what the passage says, those weeks are the slowest weeks, or there is another week, such as the week of the Arts Festival, that is the week when the most people visit Mergate.
The correct answer to a Conclusion question won’t be merely plausible.It will be clearly supported by the passage.
(D) The celebration of the nation’s birthday has no effect on the number of visitors to the seaside town of Mergate during the week in which the nation’s birthday falls.
What this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.

It is true that the passage says that, after the Mergate Arts Festival was moved to another summer week, the number of visitors in Mergate during the nation’s birthday week became no greater than the number there during other summer weeks in which holidays fall.

However, it could still be the case that the celebration of the nation’s birthday has some effect on the number of visitors to the seaside town of Mergate during the week in which the nation’s birthday falls.After all, maybe many people who would not otherwise go to Mergate do go to Mergate that week because the nation’s birthday falls during that week.Maybe, if the nation’s birthday did not fall during that week, the number of visitors in Mergate that week would be fewer than the number there during other summer holiday weeks.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) During the particular week during which the Arts Festival is now held, there are in Mergate more visitors than there would be were the festival held during another week.

The passage says that, after the Mergate Arts Festival, which traditionally had been held during the week of the nation’s birthday, was moved to another particular summer week, the number of visitors in Mergate during the nation’s birthday week became no greater than the number there during other summer weeks in which holidays fall.
What follows from that information is that it’s quite likely that many people who had been going to Mergate during the nation’s birthday week had been going because of the festival.
It’s reasonable to conclude, then, that the festival attracts people to Mergate or that people choose to go to Mergate when the festival is going on rather during other weeks.

Thus, it’s also reasonable to conclude what this choice says, that during the particular week during which the Arts Festival is now held, there are in Mergate more visitors than there would be were the festival held during another week.
Remember, the correct answer to a Conclusion question does not have to be a certainty, just a reasonable conclusion.

question.text: Beth: Legalizing heroin was supposed to result in a reduction in the number of heroin overdoses experienced by people in our country annually.However, after heroin was legalized, the number of people admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses annually did not change significantly.Ariane: But, generally, when people overdose on illegal drugs, they are reluctant to go to a hospital, because they are concerned about running into legal trouble if they do.Ariane’s response to Beth’s statements leads to which of the following conclusions?

answers.text: Since heroin was legalized in the country, there has been a reduction in the annual number of heroin overdoses in the country.
Drugs other than heroin are still illegal in the country.
In the country in question, generally, heroin overdoses experienced by people since heroin was legalized have not been as severe a heroin overdoses were generally before heroin was legalized.
It would have been impossible to arrive at a relatively accurate estimate of how many people overdosed on heroin in the country annually before heroin was legalized.
Before the country’s legalization of heroin, in the country, only people who were unconcerned about running into legal trouble were admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses.

explanation_parts.text: It is not easy to see that the correct answer to this question follows from what the statements say.So, arriving at the correct answer may require eliminating the four incorrect choices that are clearly not supported by the statements in the passage and, then, figuring out how it could possibly be that the remaining choice is supported by what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Since heroin was legalized in the country, there has been a reduction in the annual number of heroin overdoses in the country.
Beth says (1) that heroin was legalized, and (2) that the number of people admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses annuallydid not changesignificantly after heroin was legalized.
Now, to see why this choice follows from what the passage says, we have to notice the implication of what Ariane says.

The implication of what Ariane says is that, if the number of overdoses per year remained the same, we would have expected the number of people going to hospitals for treatment for overdoses each year to increase, and here’s why.

Ariane says that, generally, when people overdose on illegal drugs, they are reluctant to go to hospitals, because they are concerned about running into legal trouble if they do.What follows, then, is that, when heroin was legalized, people would have become less reluctant to go to hospitals when they overdosed on heroin, because they would no longer have been concerned about getting into legal trouble.

Thus, after heroin was legalized, people who overdosed on heroin would be more likely to go to hospitals than people who overdosed on heroin were before legalization, because people would no longer would have legality related reasons not to go.So, presumably, with the legalization of heroin, the percentage of people who overdosed who would go to hospitals for treatment would increase.

In that case, we can see that, if the number of heroin overdoses per year remained the same, the number of people going to hospitals for treatment for heroin overdoses per year would increase, because, even though the total number of people overdosing would not change, the percentage of people overdosing who go to hospitals would increase.
However, as Beth says, the number of people admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses annuallydid not changesignificantly after heroin was legalized.
That fact supports the conclusion that the number of people who overdosed annuallydecreased, and here’s how.
(Number Going to Hospitals) = (Percentage Going to Hospitals) x (Number Who Overdose).
Thenumber of people going to hospitalsfor treatment for heroin overdoses annually hasremained the same.
The percentageof people overdosingwho go to hospitals has increased.
Thus, the totalnumber of people who overdoseannually must havedecreased.
(B) Drugs other than heroin are still illegal in the country.

It is possible that the reason why, after heroin was legalized, the number of people admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses did not change significantly has something to do with other drugs’ remaining illegal in the country.
However, the truth is that the passage does not clearly support any connection between the events described and anything having to do drugs other than heroin.
So, the passage does not support this choice.
(C) In the country in question, generally, heroin overdoses experienced by people since heroin was legalized have not been as severe a heroin overdoses were generally before heroin was legalized.

The passage provides no information that supports a conclusion about a change in severity of heroin overdoses.All we know about heroin overdoses in the country in question are that the number of them occurring annually was expected to decrease, but the number of people admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses annually did not change significantly.
So, the passage provides information on numbers of overdoses, but a conclusion about severity would require information other than the information provided by the passage.
(D) It would have been impossible to arrive at a relatively accurate estimate of how many people overdosed on heroin in the country annually before heroin was legalized.

This choice could seem to follow from what the passage says, because, if before heroin was legalized, people who had overdosed were reluctant to go to hospitals for treatment, then it seems likely that, before heroin was legalized, it would have been difficult to arrive at a relatively accurate estimate of how many people overdosed on heroin in the country annually.

However, the truth is that what this choice says goes too far.Difficult is not the same as impossible, and, it could have been the case that, even if people were hiding the fact that they had overdosed on heroin, there were ways to arrive at a relatively accurate estimate of how many overdosed.
What this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.
(E) Before the country’s legalization of heroin, in the country, only people who were unconcerned about running into legal trouble were admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses.

This choice could seem to follow from what the passage says, because, if before heroin was legalized, people who had overdosed were reluctant to go to hospitals for treatment, then it’s possible that, before heroin was legalized, only people who were unconcerned about running into legal trouble were admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses.
However, the truth is that, while what this choice says is possible, it’s not supported by the passage.

It’s one thing for people to be, as the passage says, “reluctant” to go to a hospital and another to conclude that only people who were unconcerned about running into legal trouble were admitted to hospitals because of heroin overdoses.What’s more likely is that some people extremely sick because of heroin overdoses went to hospitals, or were carried to hospitals, even though they were concerned about running into legal trouble.
So, what this choice says goes beyond what the passage supports.

question.text: A concern associated with proposed minimum wage increases is that employers faced with paying a higher minimum wage will reduce employee headcounts, with a decrease in employment therefore resulting from a minimum wage increase.However, employees paid higher minimum wages tend to stay in jobs for longer periods of time.Therefore, minimum wage increases can cause reductions in employee turnover, which in turn result in reductions in employers’ hiring costs, which reductions can offset cost increases associated with minimum wage increases and allow employers to employ the same numbers of employees at a higher minimum wage without experiencing overall cost increases.Which of the following conclusions is most strongly supported by the information presented above?

answers.text: When employers are unable to offset increases in the minimum wage by reducing hiring expenses, increases in the minimum wage inevitably lead to decreases in employment.
Generally, the higher the wages paid by an employer, the lower is the employee turnover experienced by that employer.
Only workers who respond to minimum wage increases by increasing the lengths of time they remain in jobs will truly benefit from an increase in the minimum wage.
Increases in hourly wages generally do not result in increases in employers’ overall costs.

Employment decreases resulting from minimum wage increases are more likely to occur in business sectors normally characterized by very low minimum wage employee turnover than in other business sectors.

explanation_parts.text: To correctly answer this question, we have to avoid choosing a trap choice that says something that is related to what the passage says but is not supported by the statements in the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(A) When employers are unable to offset increases in the minimum wage by reducing hiring expenses, increases in the minimum wage inevitably lead to decreases in employment.
What the passage says indicates that, when employers are unable to offset increases in the minimum wage by reducing hiring expenses, theymayreduce employee headcounts.
However, what the passage says does not indicate that, when employers are unable to offset increases in the minimum wage by reducing hiring expenses, they willinevitablyreduce employee headcounts.
Common sense tells us that there could be circumstances in which employers are unable to offset increases in the minimum wage by reducing hiring expenses but do not reduce employee headcounts.

For instance, there could be cases in which minimum wage increases and increases in profits occur simultaneously, in which cases employers may decide not to reduce headcounts even though they are unable to offset increases in the minimum wage by reducing hiring expenses.
This choice may seem to be supported by what the passage says, but, in using the word “inevitably,” this choice goes beyond what the passage says.
(B) Generally, the higher the wages paid by an employer, the lower is the employee turnover experienced by that employer.

In order to avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to notice that all of the information in the passage is specifically about increases in the minimum wage and reductions in turnover that occur when the minimum wage is increased, whereas this choice is about wages and turnover in general.

The fact that increases in the minimum wage, a specific wage with certain characteristics,canresult in reductions in employee turnover does not mean that,in generalthe higher the wages paid by an employer, the lower is the turnover experienced by that employer.

Certainly it is plausible that the higher the wages an employer pays, the lower will be the employee turnover experienced by that employer, but that conclusion is not supported by the facts stated by the passage.
(C) Only workers who respond to minimum wage increases by increasing the lengths of time they remain in jobs will truly benefit from an increase in the minimum wage.
This choice could seem to be somehow correct, because, like the passage, it mentions workers’ responding to minimum wage increases by increasing the lengths of time they remain in jobs.
However, the truth is that the passage provides no information about workers benefiting from remaining in jobs.
So, while this choice isrelated towhat the passage says, it is notsupported bywhat the passage says.
(D) Increases in hourly wages generally do not result in increases in employers’ overall costs.

In order to avoid choosing this incorrect choice, we have to notice that all of the information in the passage is specifically about increases in the minimum wage and what can occur when the minimum wage is increased, whereas this choice is about hourly wages in general.

Whatcanoccur when the minimum wage, a specific wage with certain characteristics, is increased may be quite different from whatgenerallyoccurs when hourly wages are increased, and, of course, we can think of many scenarios in which increases in wages would, contrary to what this choice say, result in increases in employers’ overall costs.

For instance, we can see that, if a union were to negotiate a wage increase at a company with low employee turnover, that company’s overall costs would likely increase as a result of that wage increase.
So, although this choice is related to what the passage says, the passage does not support what this choice says.

CORRECT ANSWER(E) Employment decreases resulting from minimum wage increases are more likely to occur in business sectors normally characterized by very low minimum wage employee turnover than in other business sectors.
The passage says that “a concern associated with proposed minimum wage increases is that employers faced with paying a higher minimum wage will reduce employee headcounts.”
The passage then presents the fact that “minimum wage increases can cause reductions in employee turnover, which in turn result in reductions in employers’ hiring costs.”

Finally the passage says that it can be that, as a result of the aforementioned reductions in employee turnover and employers’ hiring costs, employers are able to “employ the same numbers of employees at a higher minimum wage without experiencing overall cost increases.”

Of course, if an increase in the minimum wage werenotto result in a decrease in employee turnover, then an increase in the minimum wage would not result in a reduction in hiring costs, and employers would not be able to employ the same numbers of employees at a higher minimum wage without experiencing overall cost increases.
OK, now let’s consider what this choice says.
This choice mentions business sectors “normally characterized by very low minimum wage employee turnover.”

Of course, if minimum wage employee turnover is normally very low in a business sector, then an increase in the minimum wage would likely cause little to no reduction in employee turnover in that sector because the turnover is already very low.

So, if the minimum wage were increased, the cost offsetting effect of turnover reduction would not occur in such a business sector, and thus, employers in such a sector would not be able to employ the same numbers of employees at a higher minimum wage without experiencing overall cost increases.

What follows then is that, employers in a sector normally characterized by very low minimum wage employee turnover are more likely than other employers to be faced with increasing costs if the minimum wage is increased, and that, therefore, such employers would be more likely than other employers to lay off employees as a result of a minimum wage increase.
Thus, we can see that the passage supports what this choice says.

question.text: Miller Corp’s customer service statistics indicate that, while, for several years, it had taken the customer response team at Miller Corp an average of 14 minutes to fix a critical software problem reported by a customer, since the company replaced its European customer response team with a team from India a year ago, the average time taken to fix a critical software problem has dropped to 9 minutes.The CEO of Miller Corp.believes that the switch to the Indian team is directly responsible for the decrease.In evaluating the accuracy of the CEO’s belief, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether, during the past year, the Indian customer response team handled a greater number of customer problems than were handled each year by the European team.
Whether 9 minutes is considered an acceptable length of time in which to solve a customer’s critical software problem.
Whether, a year ago, Miller Corp redefined the definition of a critical software problem to exclude the most challenging software problems.

Whether, during the past year, Miller Corp’s customers were as satisfied with service provided by the company’s customer response team as the company’s customers were during the preceding several years.
Whether critical software problems are less likely to develop in the products that Miller Corp sold to customers during the past year than in products the company sold in prior years.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: For several years, it had taken the customer response team at Miller Corp an average of 14 minutes to fix a critical software problem reported by a customer.
Premise: Since the company replaced its European customer response team with a team from India a year ago, the average time taken to fix a critical software problem has dropped to 9 minutes.
Conclusion: The CEO of Miller Corp.believes that the switch to the Indian team is directly responsible for the decrease.

Any time an author claims that there is a cause-and-effect link between variables, we should be skeptical regarding whether a cause-and-effect link actually exists.The fact that two variables occur together does not provide us with sufficient evidence to conclude that one of the variables causes the other.

As we consider this question, the question in our minds should be, “Is the change from the European customer response team to the Indian team actually what caused the average time taken to fix a critical software problem to drop from 14 minutes to 9 minutes?”
Could there be an alternative explanation for the drop? What else might we want to know in order to properly evaluate this argument?
(A) Whether, during the past year, the Indian customer response team handled a greater number of customer problems than were handled each year by the European team.

Information on whether, during the past year, the Indian customer response team handled a greater number of customer problems than were handled each year by the European team does not affect the argument.
Be careful not to decide that such information strengthens or weakens the argument.

The reality could be that more issues were solved BECAUSE the time went down.Or it could be that there were just more issues to solve.The team’s solving more issues does not affect the claim that it was the switch to the Indian team that caused the decrease.
Let’s apply the yes/no test to this statement.
Yes, during the past year, the Indian customer response team handled a greater number of customer problems than were handled each year by the European team.
We are still left wondering whether it was in fact the switch that caused the decline in time spent per problem, right?
No, during the past year, the Indian customer response team did not handle a greater number of customer problems than were handled each year by the European team.
Again, we are still left wondering whether it was in fact the switch that caused the decline, right?
(B) Whether 9 minutes is considered an acceptable length of time in which to solve a customer’s critical software problem.

The answer to this question does not affect the argument.We are not concerned with whether 9 minutes is considered an acceptable length of time in which to solve a customer’s critical software problem.Rather we are concerned with whether the switch to the Indian team is directly responsible for the decrease.
Let’s apply the yes/no test to this statement.
Yes, 9 minutes is considered an acceptable length of time in which to solve a customer’s critical software problem.
So what, right?
No, 9 minutes is not considered an acceptable length of time in which to solve a customer’s critical software problem.
So what, right?
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether, a year ago, Miller Corp redefined the definition of a critical software problem to exclude the most challenging software problems.
The answer to this question does affect the argument.Let’s apply the yes/no test to this statement.
Yes, The Miller Corp redefined the definition of a critical software problem to exclude the most challenging software problems.

This statement would weaken the argument.After all, if the company redefined the definition of a critical software problem to exclude the most challenging software problems, then of course the average time taken to fix a critical software problem would likely drop from 14 minutes to 9 minutes.After all, the amounts of time spent solving the most difficult, and most time consuming problems, have been removed from the data.Since we have a plausible alternative explanation, we are less certain that it was actually the switch in teams that created the change.
No, The Miller Corp “did not” redefine the definition of a critical software problem to exclude the most challenging software problems.

This statement would strengthen the argument because it serves to rule out the alternative explanation that the company redefined the statistic.But ruling out this alternative explanation, we have more conviction that it was in fact the switch from the European team to the Indian team that is responsible for the change.

(D) Whether, during the past year, Miller Corp’s customers were as satisfied with service provided by the company’s customer response team as the company’s customers were during the preceding several years.

The answer to this question does not affect the argument.We are not concerned with whether, during the past year, Miller Corp’s customers were as satisfied with service provided by the company’s customer response team as the company’s customers were during the preceding several years.Rather we are concerned with whether the switch to the Indian team is directly responsible for the decrease.If this is hard to see, apply the yes/no test.
(E) Whether critical software problems are less likely to develop in the products that Miller Corp sold to customers during the past year than in products the company sold in prior years.
The answer to this question does not affect the argument.

Information on whether critical software problems are less likely to develop in the products that Miller Corp sold to customers during the past year than in products the company sold in prior years does not affect the strength of the argument.After all, what does such information have to do with the speed of addressing problems that did develop? If this is hard to see, apply the yes/no test.

question.text: Company X sells a natural supplement called NewVigor, which is composed of a proprietary blend of organic dried fruits and vegetables.Because NewVigor facilitates the repopulation of healthy gut bacteria, which then trigger increases in neurotransmitters, low levels of which are linked to depression, the company claims that NewVigor is an effective treatment for depression.As further evidence, the company cites one of its recent studies in which 100 people experiencing depression took NewVigor for 30 days, with 87 of the people reporting significant relief of their symptoms by the study’s end.In order to evaluate the argument, it would be useful to determine each of the following, EXCEPT:

answers.text: What percentage of the 87 people also engaged in other treatment therapies known to improve depression symptoms.
How many of the 87 people were experiencing a depression-inducing life event at the beginning of the study that was resolved during the study.
What percentage of people experiencing depression and taking no steps to alleviate their symptoms typically see significant improvement in their symptoms within 30 days.
Whether Company X can explain how the blend of organic dried fruits and vegetables in NewVigor facilitates the repopulation of healthy gut bacteria.
What proportion of the 87 people were abusing prescription drugs at the beginning of the study but had eliminated this habit by the study’s end.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Company X sells a natural supplement called NewVigor, which is composed of a proprietary blend of organic dried fruits and vegetables.
Premise: NewVigor facilitates the repopulation of healthy gut bacteria, which then trigger increases in neurotransmitters, low levels of which are linked to depression.

Premise: The company cites one of its recent studies in which 100 people experiencing depression took NewVigor for 30 days, with 87 of the people reporting significant relief of their symptoms by the study’s end.
Conclusion: The company claims that NewVigor is an effective treatment for depression.

We see that a cause-and-effect claim is made in the argument.The company claims that “NewVigor is an effective treatment for depression.” The evidence for this claim comes in the form of a company-led study in which 100 people experiencing depression took NewVigor for 30 days, with 87 of the people reporting significant relief of their symptoms by the study’s end.

Does the fact that 87 of the 100 people reported significant relief of their symptoms allow us to conclude that the NewVigor was responsible for this improvement? Might there be another explanation for the observation? What might we want to know in order to better evaluate the claim being made?
Because this is an “EXCEPT” question, our job is to find four answer choices that would be useful to determine in order to evaluate the argument; these will be incorrect answer choices.
Then, the answer choice that remains, the one that would not be useful to determine in order to evaluate the argument, will be the correct answer.
(A) What percentage of the 87 people also engaged in other treatment therapies known to improve depression symptoms.
What this answer choice says would be helpful to determine in order to evaluate the argument.

If the answer to this question is “a high percentage,” then the argument is weakened.After all, if a high percentage of the 87 people also engaged in other treatment therapies known to improve depression symptoms, then we can’t really say what alleviated the depression symptoms.

For example, let’s say that most of the people, in addition to taking New Vigor, also began, for the first time, meditating.In this situation, it would be unclear to us whether it was the NewVigor or the meditation that helped improve the depression symptoms.

On the other hand, if the answer is “a very small percentage,” then the argument is stronger because we are ruling out the alternative explanation that it might actually be the other treatment modalities that are responsible for the improvement of symptoms.
Useful? Yes.
(B) How many of the 87 people were experiencing a depression-inducing life event at the beginning of the study that was resolved during the study.
What this answer choice says would be helpful to determine in order to evaluate the argument.

If “many” or “most” of the 87 people were experiencing a depression-inducing life event at the beginning of the study that was resolved during the study, then we have a very plausible alternative explanation for why the depression symptoms improved – the depression-inducing life event resolved, and hence, so did the depression.In other words, in this situation we’re less sure that it was in fact the NewVigor that caused the improvement in the depression symptoms; the improvement could very well have been from the resolution of the depression-inducing life event.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “few,” then the argument is strengthened because we’re largely able to rule out an alternative explanation that might have explained why the depression symptoms resolved.
(C) What percentage of people experiencing depression and taking no steps to alleviate their symptoms typically see significant improvement in their symptoms within 30 days.
What this answer choice says would be helpful to determine in order to evaluate the argument.

If a “high” percentage of people experiencing depression and taking no steps to alleviate their symptoms typically see significant improvement in their symptoms within 30 days, then the argument is weakened because NewVigor may not actually be what is causing an improvement in these depression symptoms; it might just be that these depression symptoms improve on their own, without outside treatment, in a very high percentage of people.

On the other hand, if a “small” percentage of people experiencing depression and taking no steps to alleviate their symptoms typically see significant improvement in their symptoms within 30 days, then the argument is stronger because we’re largely able to rule out an alternative explanation that might have explained why the depression symptoms resolved.
Useful? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Whether Company X can explain how the blend of organic dried fruits and vegetables in NewVigor facilitates the repopulation of healthy gut bacteria.

Company X need not be able explain how the blend of organic dried fruits and vegetables in NewVigor facilitates the repopulation of healthy gut bacteria in order to support the claim that NewVigor works.

Of course, it would be nice for the company to know precisely how the product works, but if the product works, then it works.Similarly, if the product does not work then it does not work, regardless of whether the company can clearly explain the products biochemical mechanism sin the body.

In general, we don’t need to know the exact mechanism by which something works in order to conclude that it works.After all, to this day we are still not too sure how the force of gravity works, but we can be sure that if we push a book off of a desk that book will fall to the ground.
(E) What proportion of the 87 people were abusing prescription drugs at the beginning of the study but had eliminated this habit by the study’s end.
What this answer choice says would be helpful to determine in order to evaluate the argument.
