If a “large” proportion of the 87 people were abusing prescription drugs at the beginning of the study but had eliminated this habit by the study’s end, then the argument is weakened because we have an alternative explanation as to why the people’s depression symptoms improved.In other words, perhaps these depression symptoms did not improve because of NewVigor at all but rather because people ended drug habits.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “a small proportion,” then the argument is stronger because we’ve largely able to rule out an alternative explanation that might have explained why the depression symptoms resolved.

question.text: Most proposed strategies for reducing global temperatures involve the reduction of CO2 levels.However, a new approach involves the use of zeolites to convert atmospheric methane to CO2, along with other gaseous byproducts, all of which would be released back into the atmosphere.Preliminary research shows that the temperature delta, which is the difference between current atmospheric temperature and the ideal atmospheric temperature, could be reduced by one-sixth with each removal of three billion tons of methane, creating a volume of CO2 equivalent to only about six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions in the process.In evaluating whether conversion of methane to CO2 via the use of zeolites is a viable way to solve global warming, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether the gaseous byproducts that result from using zeolites to convert three billion tons of methane to CO2 would cause the temperature delta to increase by at least one-sixth.
Whether, at this point, atmospheric chemists precisely understand the chemical reactions that occur when methane is converted to CO2 via the use of zeolites.
Whether there are technologies available that could reduce the temperature delta by an amount greater than one-sixth.
Whether volumes of other greenhouse gases would also decrease in the process of using zeolites to convert methane to CO2.
Whether the amount of CO2 equivalent to six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions is considered by scientists a large amount of CO2.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Most proposed strategies for reducing global temperatures involve the reduction of CO2 levels.
Premise: However, a new approach involves the use of zeolites to convert atmospheric methane to CO2, along with other gaseous byproducts, all of which would be released back into the atmosphere.

Premise: Preliminary research shows that the temperature delta, which is the difference between current atmospheric temperature and the ideal atmospheric temperature, could be reduced by one-sixth with each removal of three billion tons of methane, creating a volume of CO2 equivalent to only about six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions in the process.
The conclusion is found in the question stem:
Conclusion: Conversion of methane to CO2 via the use of zeolites is a viable way to solve global warming.

Notice that we have a plan proposed to use these zeolites to take methane from the atmosphere and convert that methane to CO2.The author believes that the temperature delta could be reduced by one-sixth with each removal of three billion tons of methane, and in the process, create a volume of CO2 equivalent to only about six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions.
Is the plan sound? Might there be factors that would cause the plan to fail? What might we want to know in order to evaluate the validity of the plan?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whether the gaseous byproducts that result from using zeolites to convert three billion tons of methane to CO2 would cause the temperature delta to increase by at least one-sixth.

To evaluate this plan, it would be important to know whether the gaseous byproducts that result from using zeolites to convert three billion tons of methane to CO2 would cause the temperature delta to increase by at least one-sixth.

After all, if they would, then the plan is not going to succeed because, although the process of converting methane to CO2 would decrease the temperature delta by one-sixth, the gaseous byproducts resulting from the process would negate that decrease by themselves increasing the temperature delta by one-sixth, resulting in a net zero improvement.
On the other hand, if they will not, then the argument is stronger because we are able rule out a possible reason why the plan will not succeed.
(B) Whether, at this point, atmospheric chemists precisely understand the chemical reactions that occur when methane is converted to CO2 via the use of zeolites.

Although it would be good if atmospheric chemists precisely understood the chemical reactions that occur when methane is converted to CO2 via the use of zeolites, this understanding is not necessary for the plan to work.After all, if the plan will work, it will work, regardless of whether the scientists understand how exactly the plan works.
(C) Whether there are technologies available that could reduce the temperature delta by an amount greater than one-sixth.

The conclusion of the argument is not that there are no technologies available that could reduce the temperature delta by an amount greater than one-sixth.In other words, the author is not making the claim that the process described is the best possible process.Rather, she is making the claim, instead, that this new approach involving the use of zeolites to convert atmospheric methane to CO2, along with other gaseous byproducts, all of which would be released back into the atmosphere could reduce the temperature delta by one-sixth with each removal of three billion tons of methane, creating a volume of CO2 equivalent to only about six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions in the process.In other words, she is simply making the claim that this is one process that could work; she is not making the claim that this process is the best possible process.We need to be skeptical of answer choices that claim to have a different plan or a better plan.After all, if there is a better plan out there, the great, but that fact will not help us in evaluating the validity of our own claim.
(D) Whether volumes of other greenhouse gases would also decrease in the process of using zeolites to convert methane to CO2.

Greenhouse gases are never good thing, and yes, it would be great if volumes of other greenhouse gases would also decrease in the process of using zeolites to convert methane to CO2.However, let’s focus on the conclusion we have to evaluate, which is that “conversion of methane to CO2 via the use of zeolites is a viable way to solve global warming.” So, notice that the claim is not that many different types of greenhouse gas volumes will decrease.Rather it is that the method described is a viable approach.So, although it would be great if other greenhouse gasses decreased, the success of the plan does not depend on a decrease in these other greenhouse gasses.
(E) Whether the amount of CO2 equivalent to six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions is considered by scientists a large amount of CO2.

Regardless of whether the amount of CO2 equivalent to six months’ worth of industrial carbon dioxide emissions is considered by scientists a large amount of CO2, if the plan works it works.What this answer choice says would not be helpful evaluating whether conversion of methane to CO2 via the use of zeolites is a viable way to solve global warming.

question.text: The city of Oakmont plans to foster the regrowth of the population of the near-endangered wolves that make their homes in the city’s wooded hills.To do so, the city plans to make it illegal to hunt wolves in approximately 50 percent of the city’s wooded hills, designating this area the conservation zone.Officials contend that, in doing so, the city will protect at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently living in the city’s wooded hills.In evaluating the accuracy of the contention made, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Will an increased number of hunters in the non-conservation zone force the wolves there to begin to move to wooded areas in cities outside of Oakmont?
Could the city protect a greater percentage of its wolves by raising public awareness on the critically important role that wolves play in the ecosystem?
Will hunters seeking to exploit wildlife for profit begin more aggressively hunting wolves in wooded areas outside of Oakmont?
Are there characteristics of the conservation zone that make it significantly less habitable for and attractive to wolves than areas in the non-conservation zone?
Are the wolves in the Oakmont area subject to more intense hunting than wolves in other regions?

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: The city of Oakmont plans to foster the regrowth of the population of the near-endangered wolves that make their homes in the city’s wooded hills.
Premise: To do so, the city plans to make it illegal to hunt wolves in approximately 50 percent of the city’s wooded hills, designating this area the conservation zone.
Conclusion: Officials contend that, in doing so, the city will protect at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently living in the city’s wooded hills.

We are presented with a plan, which is that by making it illegal to hunt wolves in approximately 50 percent of the city’s wooded hills, the city will protect at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently living in the city’s wooded hills.

Any time an author proposes a plan, she is making the assumption that the plan will work as expected.In other words, she is assuming that there will not be unforeseen circumstances that will arise and derail the plan.
Might the plan’s creators have missed something? What else might we want to know in order to properly evaluate the argument.
(A) Will an increased number of hunters in the non-conservation zone force the wolves there to begin to move to wooded areas in cities outside of Oakmont?

It’s important to see that the plan hinges on the fact that the city will protect at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently living in the city’s wooded hills.In other words, the essential location is the conservation area in which hunting will be banned.Therefore, an increased number of hunters in the non-conservation zone forcing the wolves there to begin to move to wooded areas in cities outside of Oakmont, does not affect the validity of the plan.After all, in this plan all that matters is what happens inside of conservation area.
(B) Could the city protect a greater percentage of its wolves by raising public awareness on the critically important role that wolves play in the ecosystem?

The city is not making the claim that there is not a better plan that could be executed, one that could protect a greater percentage of its wolves by raising public awareness on the critically important role that wolves play in the ecosystem.Rather, the city is simply proposing a plan that “it will protect at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently living in the city’s wooded hills.” Therefore, it would not be useful to determine whether the city could protect a greater percentage of its wolves by raising public awareness on the critically important role that wolves play in the ecosystem.Always be careful of the “different plan / better plan” answer choice.Review these answer choices with a skeptical eye.
(C) Will hunters seeking to exploit wildlife for profit begin more aggressively hunting wolves in wooded areas outside of Oakmont?

To not choose this answer as correct, we need to pay careful attention to the argument’s conclusion, which is that “the city will protect at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently livingin the city’s (Oakmont’s) wooded hills.” Therefore, that hunters seeking to exploit wildlife for profit begin more aggressively hunting wolves in wooded areas outside of Oakmont will not affect the validity of this plan.Of course, had the answer choice said that the hunters would be doing so in the conservation area, then the validity of the plan would be diminished.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Are there characteristics of the conservation zone that make it significantly less habitable for and attractive to wolves than areas in the non-conservation zone?

What this answer choice says would be most useful to determine in evaluating the accuracy of the contention made.Let’s think about the plan that is being proposed – no hunting in 50 percent of the city’s wooded hills, and as a result, it is expected that at least 50 percent of the endangered wolves currently living in the city’s wooded hills will be protected.In other words, if 50 percent of the land is made into conservation land, then at least 50 percent of the wolves will be saved.An assumption made in making this argument is that at least 50 percent of the wolves will actually be found in the 50 of the city’s wooded hills where hunting is banned.After all, if at least 50 percent of wolves do not live in this conservation zone, then the city will not protect at least 50 percent of the wolves, and the plan will fail.For example, imagine if 90 percent of the wolves felt that the characteristics of the conservation zone make it significantly less habitable for and attractive than areas in the non-conservation zone? In this case, it might just be that only 10 percent of the wolves spend their time in the conservation zone.Of course, in such a situation, then at least 50 percent of the wolves would not be protected.
(E) Are the wolves in the Oakmont area subject to more intense hunting than wolves in other regions?

Notice that regardless of whether the wolves in the Oakmont area are subject to hunting that is less intense, just as intense or more intense than wolves in other regions are subject to, the plan could work as intended.The point is to project 50 percent of the wolves by designating 50 percent of the wooded hills a conservation zone.Theoretically no hunting at all will occur in that zone.
So, the answer to this question is not useful for evaluating the accuracy of the contention made.

question.text: Clearly, one way to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide levels is to plant new forests of trees.After all, in a process known as photosynthesis, trees take in carbon dioxide from the air and release oxygen.In fact, research has shown that one acre of forest could remove 3 million metric tons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere each year.In evaluating the validity of the conclusion drawn, it would be most useful to answer which of the following?

answers.text: Would the vehicles and equipment necessary for planting new forests create and emit as much carbon dioxide as the forests would absorb?
Is a decrease in atmospheric carbon dioxide alone sufficient to result in a decrease in Earth’s average temperature?
If timber were harvested from the land on which the new forests were planted, would the rate at which the forests removed carbon dioxide from the atmosphere be significantly reduced?
Are there ways to remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere at rates greater than could be achieved via planting of new forests?
Could significant quantities of land on which to plant new forests be procured without a resulting reduction in the world’s food supply?

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: In a process known as photosynthesis, trees take in carbon dioxide from the air and release oxygen.
Premise: Research has shown that one acre of forest could remove 3 million metric tons of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere each year.
Conclusion: Clearly, one way to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide levels is to plant new forests of trees.
The author claims that one way to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide levels is to plant new forests of trees.
Of course, she is proposing a plan.In proposing this plan, she assumes that the plan will work as expected and that there will not be unforeseen circumstances that arise that act to derail the plan.
Is her assumption a good one? Are there other factors that she may have failed to consider? What else might we want to know in order to properly evaluate the validity of the conclusion she draws?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Would the vehicles and equipment necessary for planting new forests create and emit as much carbon dioxide as the forests would absorb?

Imagine if the answer to this question is that the vehicles and equipment necessary for planting new forests WOULD create and emit as much carbon dioxide as the forests would absorb.In this case, the plan would almost certainly fail because carbon dioxide levels would not fall; they would remain the same.

On the other hand, if vehicles and equipment necessary for planting new forests WOULD NOT create and emit as much carbon dioxide as the forests would absorb, then the argument is stronger because we are able to rule out one possible fatal flaw that could derail the plan.
So, answering this question would certainly be useful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion.
(B) Is a decrease in atmospheric carbon dioxide alone sufficient to result in a decrease in Earth’s average temperature?
Let’s revisit the argument’s conclusion, which is “one way toreduce atmospheric carbon dioxide levelsis to plant new forests of trees.”

We see that the prediction is that planting forests of trees will result in a reduction in “atmospheric carbon dioxide levels.” It’s not that planting forests of trees will result in a reduction in “Earth’s average temperature.”
Therefore, determining whether a decrease in atmospheric carbon dioxide alone is sufficient to result in a decrease in Earth’s average temperature is not going to help us in evaluating this argument.
(C) If timber were harvested from the land on which the new forests were planted, would the rate at which the forests removed carbon dioxide from the atmosphere be significantly reduced?
Notice that the conclusion is simply that one way to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide levels is to plant new forests of trees.

That conclusion would still be true even if timber harvesting would cause reductions in the rates at which forests remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.After all, as long as forests are removing carbon dioxide at some rate, even a reduced rate, they are still removing it.
So, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” that answer has essentially no effect on the support for author’s conclusion.
(D) Are there ways to remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere at rates greater than could be achieved via planting of new forests?
To avoid being fooled into choosing this choice, we have to keep in mind that the author’s conclusion is simply that one way to reduce atmospheric carbon dioxide levels is to plant forests of trees.
The author does not make the claim that her way is the best possible way to remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
So, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” that answer has no effect on the support for the author’s conclusion.
(E) Could significant quantities of land on which to plant new forests be procured without a resulting reduction in the world’s food supply?

Information on whether significant quantities of land on which to plant new forests could be procured without a resulting reduction in the world’s food supply would not help us in evaluating this argument.

After all, the author does not claim that her plan is a perfect one, one that would not have any unintended consequences.Her claim is simply that a certain action, planting forests, would have a certain result, a reduction in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels.

question.text: The government of Cazacuna is proposing to provide its citizens with subsidized health insurance, enabling them to access medical care provided by family doctors.The goal is to avoid the paying of unusually high prices that both the citizens and the government currently pay when citizens must seek treatment in hospital emergency rooms for routine health issues, such as colds and minor burns, that could be effectively treated by family doctors at lower cost.However, because most citizens have little income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses, it is unlikely that many would be able to afford the health insurance.In order to evaluate the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether many citizens of Cazacuna generally spend significant portions of their incomes on seeing doctors in hospital emergency rooms for routine health issues
Whether citizens of Cazacuna are content being seen by doctors in the emergency rooms of hospitals
Whether, by traveling to the nearby country of Calazadonia, citizens of Cazacuna could access family doctors who provide routine medical services for nominal fees
What proportion of all emergency room visits in Cazacuna are for routine health issues, such as minor burns and colds
Whether the proposed subsidized health insurance would be accepted by all family doctors

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: The government of Cazacuna is proposing to provide its citizens with subsidized health insurance so they can see family doctors, at a lower cost, for routine health issues instead of paying high prices to been seen in emergency rooms.
Premise: Most citizens have little income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses
Conclusion: It is unlikely that many in Cazacuna would be able to afford the health insurance.
Some of the citizens of Cazacuna are going to emergency rooms to be seen by doctors for routine health issues.The problem is that seeing doctors in emergency rooms is expensive.

The government is planning to offer subsidized health insurance to help in preventing this phenomenon from occurring.However, the author believes that many will not be able to afford the insurance because they have little extra income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses.

Is the author’s logic solid? Is he making any errors in reasoning? Is he making any unwarranted assumptions? Could there be more to the story? What else might we want to know to evaluate the validity of his argument?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whether many citizens of Cazacuna generally spend significant portions of their incomes on seeing doctors in hospital emergency rooms for routine health issues

Notice that the author makes the claim that because “most citizens have little income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses,” “It is unlikely that many in Cazacuna would be able to afford the health insurance.”

Great, but what if in that figure, whatever their current expense number is, is included the high prices they pay to see doctors in the emergency room, and what if this cost represents a significant proportion of their incomes? In this case, the author’s argument would be weakened because these people could stop using this money to pay the emergency room fees and instead use it to pay the cost of the subsidized health insurance, which would allow them to see family doctors.

On the other hand, if the money they are spending in emergency rooms represents a small fraction of their incomes, then the argument is stronger because the author is probably correct that it would be unlikely for the people to be able to afford the health insurance.
So, the answer to this question would certainly be useful.
(B) Whether citizens of Cazacuna are content being seen by doctors in the emergency rooms of hospitals
The citizens of Cazacuna may or may not be content being seen by doctors in the emergency rooms of hospitals and either way the strength of the argument is unaffected.

Let’s say that the citizens are very content.Fine, but the fact of the matter remains that most citizens have little income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses.So, the argument is unaffected.

Similarly, what if the citizens are not content at all? Still, the fact of the matter remains that most citizens have little income in excess of what they need in order to cover their current expenses.So, the argument is unaffected.
(C) Whether, by traveling to the nearby country of Calazadonia, citizens of Cazacuna could access family doctors who provide routine medical services for nominal fees
Let’s look back to the argument’s conclusion, which is “it is unlikely that many would be able to afford the health insurance.”

Notice that, the answer to the question of whether, by traveling to the nearby country of Calazadonia, citizens of Cazacuna could access family doctors who provide routine medical services for nominal fees, has no impact on whether people can or cannot afford health insurance.

In other words, let’s say that the answer is “Yes,” and the people can travel to get medical care.Does that strengthen or weaken the argument? No, right? Because these people may be able to travel to get medical care, and at the same time they may not be able to afford health insurance.

Similarly, if the answer to this question is “No,” there is no effect on the argument because these people may not be able to travel to get medical care, and at the same time they may not be able to afford health insurance.
(D) What proportion of all emergency room visits in Cazacuna are for routine health issues, such as minor burns and colds
Let’s look back to the argument’s conclusion, which is that “it is unlikely that many would be able to afford the health insurance.”
What proportion of all emergency room visits in Cazacuna are for routine health issues, such as minor burns and colds has no impact on whether people can or cannot afford health insurance.
(E) Whether the proposed subsidized health insurance would be accepted by all family doctors
The answer to the question of whether the proposed subsidized health insurance would be accepted by all family doctors would not be helpful in evaluating this argument.
After all, information on whether the insurance would be accepted by all doctors does not help us to determine whether citizens can afford to pay for the insurance.

question.text: To better prepare its students for a rigorous exit exam, two years ago, the Lighthouse School switched its teaching methodology for some classrooms from traditional classroom, in which a teacher lectures during class and then students practice what they’ve learned for homework, to “flipped classroom,” in which students interact with video-based lessons for homework and then, during class, practice what they’ve learned.Since the switch, the percentage of students in the flipped classrooms passing the exam has been significantly greater than the percentage in the traditional classrooms passing.Clearly, the new teaching methodology should be adopted school wide.In order to evaluate the force of the evidence presented, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: The percentage of the school’s students who passed the exit exam before the school switched to the new teaching methodology
The percentage of students in the flipped classrooms who have passed the exam in the two years since the switch began
Whether teachers in the flipped classrooms were given better training in best practices for preparing their students to pass the exam than was given to teachers in the traditional classrooms
The pass rate of Lighthouse School students who have taken the exam over the two years since the switch
Whether more Lighthouse School students would pass the exam than currently do if students at the school were, on average, more motivated to become educated

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Background: To better prepare its students for a rigorous exit exam, two years ago, the Lighthouse School switched its teaching methodology for some classrooms from traditional classroom, in which a teacher lectures during class and then students practice what they’ve learned for homework, to “flipped classroom,” in which students interact with video-based lessons for homework and then, during class, practice what they’ve learned.
Premise: Since the switch, the percentage of students in the flipped classrooms passing the exam has been significantly greater than the percentage in the traditional classrooms passing.
Conclusion: Clearly, the new teaching methodology should be adopted school wide.

The author concludes that there is a cause-and-effect link between the flipping of the classroom and the success of the school’s students.In making this cause-and-effect claim, the author is assuming that there is not an alternative explanation for the observed pattern involving the classrooms and the percentages of students passing.
What if this assumption is incorrect? What if there actually is another explanation for the observed pattern? Could the author be missing something? What might we want to know?
(A) The percentage of the school’s students who passed the exit exam before the school switched to the new teaching methodology

A key premise of the argument is that “Since the switch, in the flipped classrooms, the percentage of students passing the exam has been significantly greater than the percentage passing in the traditional classrooms.”

Notice that this comparison between the performance of students in the flipped classrooms and that of students in the traditional classrooms would support the conclusion regardless of what percentage of the school’s students passed the exit exam before the school switched to the new teaching methodology.
After all, the author is not making a comparison between students now and students in the past.
So, establishing the percentage mentioned by this choice would not be useful.
(B) The percentage of students in the flipped classrooms who have passed the exam in the two years since the switch began

A key premise in the argument is that “Since the switch, in the flipped classrooms, the percentage of students passing the exam has been significantly greater than the percentage passing in the traditional classrooms.”

Notice that this comparison between the performance of students in the flipped classrooms and that of students in the traditional classrooms would support the conclusion regardless of what percentage of students in the flipped classrooms have passed the exam in the two years since the switch began.After all, whether the percentage of students in the flipped classrooms who have passed is 10 percent or 100 percent, it is still the case that the percentage of flipped classroom students passing is higher than the percentage of traditional classroom students passing.
So, establishing the percentage mentioned by this choice would not be useful.

YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether teachers in the flipped classrooms were given better training in best practices for preparing their students to pass the exam than was given to teachers in the traditional classrooms

As we know, the author concludes that there is a cause-and-effect link between the flipping of the classroom and the success of students.In making this cause-and-effect claim, the author is assuming that there are not alternative explanations for the cause and effect observed.
What if that assumption is incorrect?

For example, what if, yes, teachers in the flipped classrooms were given better training in best practices for preparing their students to pass the exam than was given to teachers in the traditional classrooms? In this case, we would have less faith in the argument’s conclusion, because we’d be presented with a plausible alternative explanation for the observed pattern.After all, if teachers in the flipped classrooms were given better training in best practices for preparing their students to pass the exam than was given to teachers in the traditional classrooms, then maybe the student’s newfound success was actually a result of the better teacher training.Right?

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No, teachers in the flipped classrooms were not given better training,” then the argument is stronger because an alternative explanation for the observed pattern has been ruled out.
Remember, when we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim, if we can rule out an alternative explanation for the observed pattern, then the argument is strengthened.
So, establishing whether teachers in the flipped classrooms were given better training than was given to teachers in the traditional classrooms would certainly be useful.
(D) The pass rate of Lighthouse School students who have taken the exam over the two years since the switch

A key premise in the argument is that “Since the switch, in the flipped classrooms, the percentage of students passing the exam has been significantly greater than the percentage passing in the traditional classrooms.”

Notice that this comparison between the performance of students in the flipped classrooms and that of students in the traditional classrooms would support the conclusion regardless of what the pass rate of Lighthouse School students who have taken the exam over the two years since the switch has been.After all, whether the pass rate of Lighthouse School students is 5 percent or 95 percent, it is still the case that the percentage of flipped classroom students passing is higher than the percentage of traditional classroom students passing.
Therefore, it would not help to know the pass rate of students who have taken the exam over the two years since the switch.
(E) Whether more Lighthouse School students would pass the exam than currently do if students at the school were, on average, more motivated to become educated

It’s important to see that the argument is simply that the school should adopt the flipped classroom teaching methodology since the percentage of students in the flipped classrooms passing the exam has been significantly greater than the percentage in the traditional classrooms passing.
Of course, what the author is implying is that the use of the flipped classroom approach, rather than the traditional classroom approach, is a factor that causes a higher pass rate.

Notice that the author is not suggesting that there are no other factors or plans that would cause the pass rate to increase.Also, notice that, regardless of whether other factors, such as student motivation to become educated, could cause the pass rate to increase, it could still be the case that the use of the flipped classroom approach results in a pass rate higher than the pass rate that results from using the traditional classroom approach.

Thus, regardless of whether it is the case that, yes, more Lighthouse School students would pass the exam than currently do if students at the school were more motivated to become educated or, no, no more students would pass the exam, the conclusion that the school should adopt the flipped classroom methodology could still be supported by the premises.
Therefore, it would not help to establish whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No.”

question.text: The Sky Jungle, a trampoline park, recently swapped the metal trampoline springs that it had used for many years with new, plastic trampoline springs.Since the change, the percentage of Sky Jungle patrons who are injured while using the park’s trampolines has decreased by 20 percent.Managers at the Sky Jungle attribute this decrease to the switch to the new trampoline springs.In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether management at the Sky Jungle informed patrons that the trampolines now used plastic springs in place of the metal springs that patrons were used to
Whether the new plastic springs are considerably more elastic than the metal springs that they replaced
Whether the rules governing the conduct of patrons at the Sky Jungle have changed since its managers changed the trampoline springs
Whether many of the people who were injured before management changed the springs were injured because of collisions with the ground
Whether the 20 percent reduction in people injured represents a substantial change in terms of actual numbers of Sky Jungle patrons

explanation_parts.text: Notice that the managers at the Sky Jungle are making the cause-and-effect claim that the decrease in injuries at the Sky Jungle is attributable to the switch to the new trampoline springs.What is their evidence? Notice that the evidence is simply that the Sky Jungle recently swapped the metal trampoline springs that it had used for many years with new, plastic trampoline springs and since that time the injuries at the Sky Jungle have decreased by 20 percent.

It’s important to see that the managers essentially believe that, because one event (the replacement of the old metal springs with the new plastic springs) came before a second event (the 20 percent decrease in injuries), the first event caused the second event.In making a cause-and-effect argument, the management assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for why the injuries at the Sky Jungle decreased by 20 percent.
What might we want to know to help us to evaluate whether that assumption is a good one? Might there be an alternative explanation?
(A) Whether management at the Sky Jungle informed patrons that the trampolines now used plastic springs in place of the metal springs that patrons were used to

Whether management at the Sky Jungle did or did not inform patrons that the trampolines now used plastic springs in place of the metal springs that patrons were used to the switch to, the new trampoline springs could certainly have been the cause of the reduction in injuries.After all, it would not be necessary for patrons to know that the springs were changed for the spring to reduce injuries.
(B) Whether the new plastic springs are considerably more elastic than the metal springs that they replaced

The passage tells us that, since the change in springs, the percentage of Sky Jungle patrons who are injured while using the park’s trampolines has decreased by 20 percent.We don’t need to know whether the new plastic springs are considerably more elastic than the metal springs that they replaced.

If you choose this answer choice as correct, you probably assumed that, if the new springs were more elastic than the old metal springs, then the new springs are safer than the old springs.However, the truth is that we don’t know what it means for these springs to be more elastic.For all we know more elastic springs are more dangerous than less elastic springs, and we can’t make any unwarranted assumptions.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether the rules governing the conduct of patrons at the Sky Jungle have changed since its managers changed the trampoline springs
In making their cause-and-effect argument, the management assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for why the injuries at the Sky Jungle decreased by 20 percent.

However, what if it were true that the rules governing the conduct of patrons at the Sky Jungle have changed since its managers changed the trampoline springs.Wouldn’t that fact weaken the argument? It would because if the rules have changed, then these changes in the rules might have been the cause of the 20 percent reduction in injuries at the sky jungle.

On the other hand, it were true that the rules governing the conduct of patrons at the Sky Jungle have NOT changed since its managers changed the trampoline springs, then the argument is stronger because we are able to rule out an alternative explanation for the 20 percent reduction in injuries at the sky jungle.
(D) Whether many of the people who were injured before management changed the springs were injured because of collisions with the ground

The passage tells us that, since the change in springs, the percentage of Sky Jungle patrons who are injured while using the park’s trampolines has decreased by 20 percent.Whether many of the people who were injured before management changed the springs were injured because of collisions with the ground would not change that fact.In other words, what this answer choice could very well be true, but this being true does not change the fact that since the change in springs, the percentage of Sky Jungle patrons who are injured while using the park’s trampolines has decreased by 20 percent.
(E) Whether the 20 percent reduction in people injured represents a substantial change in terms of actual numbers of Sky Jungle patrons

The passage tells us that since the change in springs, the percentage of Sky Jungle patrons who are injured while using the park’s trampolines has decreased by 20 percent.A percent decrease is more than sufficient to support the argument’s conclusion.We need not know whether the 20 percent reduction in people injured represents a substantial change in terms of actual numbers of Sky Jungle patrons to effectively make this argument.

question.text: Many cases of diabetes can be attributed to poor diet.Public health officials have determined that about 15 percent of the people living in West Virginia are experiencing diabetes.To combat this epidemic, officials plan to make it mandatory that all cities in West Virginia offer healthy markets at which are sold fresh fruits, vegetables, and other health-promoting foods, but not diabetes-promoting food.Officials feel that implementing the plan will result in a significant decrease in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia.For evaluating the likelihood of success of this initiative, it would be useful to determine each of the following, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Whether public educational programs could be successfully used to get citizens of West Virginia to more regularly engage in exercise, which is shown to help to solve diabetes
Whether the types of health-promoting foods that would be sold at the healthy markets are already widely available in stores in West Virginia
The percentage of people experiencing diabetes in West Virginia who regularly consume diabetes-promoting food
Whether, for convenience, people in West Virginia generally choose to buy food where they can also buy a wide variety of non-food items
Whether there are powerful cultural factors causing people in West Virginia to eat diabetes-promoting foods.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many cases of diabetes can be attributed to poor diet.
Premise: Public health officials have determined that about 15 percent of the people living in West Virginia are experiencing diabetes.

Premise: To combat this epidemic, officials plan to make it mandatory that all cities in West Virginia offer healthy markets at which are sold fresh fruits, vegetables, and other health-promoting foods, but not diabetes-promoting food.
Conclusion: Officials feel that implementing the plan will result in a significant decrease in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia.

Notice that officials are proposing a plan.Any time a plan is proposed, an inherent assumption is that the plan will work as expected.In other words, the officials are assuming that there will not be unforeseen circumstances that will arise and derail the plan.What might we want to consider? Might there be some malicious variables that will spoil the plan?
Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful in evaluating the plan; each of these will be an incorrect answer.
Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful in evaluating the plan will be the correct answer.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whether public educational programs could be successfully used to get citizens of West Virginia to more regularly engage in exercise, which is shown to help to solve diabetes
First, let’s focus on the argument’s conclusion, which is, “implementing the plan will result in a significant decrease in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia.”

Now, notice that this answer choice says asks whether “public educational programs” could be successfully used to get citizens of West Virginia to more regularly engage in exercise, which is shown to help to solve diabetes.

It’s important to see that the answer to this question would be helpful in evaluating the likelihood of a different plan, one that centers on education, or lack thereof, but not the plan presented in the argument.After all, how would knowing more how public educational programs could be used to get these citizens moving more help us in evaluating a plan regarding the implementation of these healthy markets?
Useful? No.
(B) Whether the types of health-promoting foods that would be sold at the healthy markets are already widely available in stores in West Virginia
Determining the answer to this question would be useful in evaluating the likelihood of success of this initiative.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the likelihood of the plan succeeding is reduced.After all, if people in West Virginia already have access to the types of health-promoting foods that would be sold at the healthy markets, then we have less reason to believe that these healthy markets will drive any substantial change in the dietary habits of the people living in West Virginia.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then we’ve ruled out something that could indicate that the plan will not work, and in ruling that out, we’ve strengthened the argument.If these people do not already have access to the fresh fruits, vegetables, and other health-promoting foods, then maybe providing them with such access will result in a significant decrease in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia.
Useful? Yes.
(C) The percentage of people experiencing diabetes in West Virginia who regularly consume diabetes-promoting food
The answer to this question would be helpful for evaluating the likelihood of success of this initiative.

If the answer to this question is “a big percentage,” then we have more faith that the plan could achieve its intended goal.After all, most of the people in West Virginia who are experiencing diabetes consume diabetes-promoting food, then it’s likely that getting these people to eat healthier food will result in a reduction in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “a small percentage,” then we have less faith that the plan will achieve its intended goal.After all, if most people experiencing diabetes in West Virginia already don’t eat diabetes causing food, then offering healthy markets without diabetes causing food will not make much difference.
Useful? Yes.
(D) Whether, for convenience, people in West Virginia generally choose to buy food where they can also buy a wide variety of non-food items
The answer to this question would be helpful for evaluating the likelihood of success of this initiative.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the likelihood of the plan’s succeeding is reduced.After all, if people in West Virginia generally choose to buy food where they can also buy a wide variety of non-food items, then it could very well be the case that there will not be a significant decrease in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia because the people living there will not, in general, purchase food from the healthy markets.

On the other hand, if the answer to the question is “No,” then we have more reason to believe that this initiative will succeed.After all, if the answer to the question is “No,” then we’ve ruled out a factor that could derail the plan, and in ruling out that factor that could derail the plan, we’ve strengthened the argument.
Useful? Yes.
(E) Whether there are powerful cultural factors causing people in West Virginia to eat diabetes-promoting foods.
The answer to this question would be helpful for evaluating the likelihood of success of this initiative.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the likelihood of the plan’s succeeding is reduced.After all, if there are powerful cultural factors causing people in West Virginia to eat diabetes-promoting foods, then even if officials make it mandatory that all cities in West Virginia offer healthy markets at which are sold fresh fruits, vegetables, and other health-promoting foods, but not diabetes-promoting food, it could very well be the case that there is not a significant decrease in the rate of diabetes in West Virginia because the people living there will continue to regularly consume diabetes-promoting food.

On the other hand, if the answer to the question is, “No,” then we have more reason to believe that this initiative will succeed.After all, if the answer to the question is “No,” then we’ve ruled out a factor that could derail the plan, and in ruling out that factor that could derail the plan, we’ve strengthened the argument.
Useful? Yes.

question.text: In Chilcohochee, there is a popular nature preserve, and many visitors to Chilcohochee spend some time in that nature preserve during their stays in the area.Meanwhile, soon, a second nature preserve will open in Chilcohochee.It is expected that, after the second nature preserve opens, 20 to 30 percent of the time spent in nature preserves by visitors to Chilcohochee will be spent in the second nature preserve.Therefore, the first nature preserve will become less crowded once the second preserve opens.In assessing the accuracy of the prediction made, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether visitors to Chilcohochee commonly visit the first nature preserve because they perceive that there are health benefits to doing so
The percentage increase in the number of visitors to Chilcohochee that can be reasonably expected to result from the opening of the second nature preserve
The percentage of visitors to Chilcohochee who typically spend some time in a nature preserve during their stays in the area
How many times, on average, visitors to Chilcohochee will spend time in a Chilcohochee nature preserve after the second nature preserve opens
Whether there is in the Chilcohochee area any land that would be suitable for the creation of a third nature preserve

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Background: In Chilcohochee, there is a popular nature preserve, and many visitors to Chilcohochee spend some time in that nature preserve during their stays in the area.Meanwhile, soon, a second nature preserve will open in Chilcohochee.
Premise: It is expected that, after the second nature preserve opens, 20 to 30 percent of the time spent in nature preserves by visitors to Chilcohochee will be spent in the second nature preserve.
Conclusion: Therefore, the first nature preserve will become less crowded once the second preserve opens.

Will the first nature reserve really become less crowded once the second nature preserve opens? Might the author be missing something? Might there be more to the story? What else might we want to know in order to properly evaluate this argument?
(A) Whether visitors to Chilcohochee commonly visit the first nature preserve because they perceive that there are health benefits to doing so
The answer to this question would not be helpful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction made.

Notice that a “Yes” answer or a “No” answer to this question would be rather limited information that would not indicate whether visitors would keep going to the first nature preserve once the second one opens.

After all, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” for all we know, visitors will feel that the same benefits can be gained by spending time in the second nature preserve.

In other words the information that visitors do or do not perceive there to be health benefits to spending time in the first nature preserve is not a basis for comparing the two nature preserves or predicting which one people will spend time in, because it’s information on only one of the preserves.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The percentage increase in the number of visitors to Chilcohochee that can be reasonably expected to result from the opening of the second nature preserve

If the answer to this question is “a 1,000 percent increase,” for example, then we have a serious reason to believe that the prediction may not be accurate.After all, in a scenario involving such extreme percentage growth in the number of visitors to the Chilcohochee area, all of the parks may see, for example, a 700 percent increase in the number of visitors to each park.

Have you ever noticed that when a second (or third) mall opens next to an original mall, the number of people shopping in the original mall tends to substantially increase? Maybe the same effect could be at play in these nature preserves.

However, if the answer to the question is “1 percent growth,” then the argument is strengthened, because there will now be two parks and only a very small percentage increase in the number of visitors to the area.

So, establishing the percentage increase in the number of visitors to Chilcohochee that can be reasonably expected to result from the opening of the second nature preserve would certainly be helpful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction made.
(C) The percentage of visitors to Chilcohochee who typically spend some time in a nature preserve during their stays in the area
We have to be careful not to be fooled into choosing this choice by the fact that it uses wording that also appears in the passage.

Notice that, regardless of whether we were to establish that 10 percent, 50 percent, or 100 percent of visitors to Chilcohochee typically spend some time in a nature preserve during their stays in the area, that information would not be useful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction made.
After all, simply knowing the percentage of visitors who spend time in a nature preserve does not allow us to determine whether the first nature preserve will become less crowded.

For instance, if 100 percent of visitors to Chilcohochee spend time in a nature preserve, they could either keep going to the first preserve after the second one opens, keeping the first one just as crowded as it has been, or decide that they all like the second one and leave the first one less crowded.
Information on the percentage who visit a nature preserve doesn’t by itself indicate which of those outcomes will occur.
(D) How many times, on average, visitors to Chilcohochee will spend time in a Chilcohochee nature preserve after the second nature preserve opens

Notice that the passage provides no information on how many times visitors to Chilcohochee currently spend time in Chilcohochee nature preserves.Therefore, information on only how many times visitors will spend time in nature preserves after the second preserve opens would provide us with no basis for comparing the current situation with the situation that will exist once the second nature preserve opens.
So, information on how many times visitors will spend time in nature preserves after the second preserve opens would not be at all helpful.
(E) Whether there is in the Chilcohochee area any land that would be suitable for the creation of a third nature preserve
The answer to this question would not be helpful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction made.
Notice that the prediction made is about what will happen “once the second preserve opens.” It’s not about what could happen if a third nature preserve were to be created.
So, information indicating whether there is any land that would be suitable for the creation of a third nature preserve would not be useful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction.

After all, regardless of whether, yes, there is in the Chilcohochee area land that would be suitable for the creation of a third nature preserve, or, no, there isn’t, the effect of opening the second nature preserve will remain the same.

question.text: Ministry of Travel records show that the ElectricAir 552, a 25-passenger electric commercial airplane, has the fewest injuries per accident of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers.Therefore, it must be true that the ElectricAir 552 is the safest of all the commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.In order to evaluate the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether the other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers have unusually high accident rates.
Whether the ElectricAir 552 has an accident rate that is higher than the accident rates of other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.
Whether the ElectricAir 552 has been in use by airlines for longer than the other airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.
Whether the ElectricAir 552 would be considered relatively safe if it were compared with all commercial airplanes rather than only with airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.
Whether pilots of the ElectricAir 552 have positive things to say about the safety features found on the plane.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: Ministry of Travel records show that the ElectricAir 552, a 25-passenger electric commercial airplane, has the fewest injuries per accident of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers.
Conclusion: Therefore, it must be true that the ElectricAir 552 is the safest of all the commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.

Notice the logic of this argument.First the Ministry of Travel cites the “the fewest injuries per accident” of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers.And from this statistic “the fewest injuries per accident,” the Ministry of Travel arrives at the conclusion that the ElectricAir 552 is the safest of all the commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.Of course, in making this argument the Ministry of Travel of travel is assuming that the injuries per accident statistic is the appropriate statistic to cite as evidence.
Is the injuries per accident statistic the correct one to use? Is there more to the story? What might we like to know to better evaluate the argument?
(A) Whether the other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers have unusually high accident rates.

Information on whether the other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers have unusually high accident rates does not affect the strength of this argument, because such information about the other commercial airplanes does not allow us to make any comparison with the ElectricAir 552.

For all we know, all commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers, including the ElectricAir 552, do or do not have unusually high accident rates.Information on only “the other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers” does not allow us to determine whether the ElectricAir 552 is safer than the others.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether the ElectricAir 552 has an accident rate that is higher than the accident rates of other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.

What this answer says would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate the argument.The Ministry of Travel cites the “fewest injuries per accident” of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers.Does the story begin and end with this one statistic? Very likely not, right? Let’s say that the ElectricAir 552 DOES have an accident rate that is higher than the accident rates of other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.In this case, the argument would be weakened, because although the ElectricAir 552 has the “fewest injuries per accident” of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers, it also, unfortunately, has an accident rate that is higher than the accident rates of other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.Therefore, the ElectricAir 552, with its accident rate that is higher than the accident rates of other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers, might actually be less safe than other planes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.

Now, on the other hand, if it is true that the ElectricAir 552 DOES NOT have an accident rate that is higher than the accident rates of other commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers, then the argument is stronger, because we are ruling out a fact that could weaken the argument, and in ruling out this fact that could weaken the argument, we are increasing the chances that the conclusion of the argument is correct.
(C) Whether the ElectricAir 552 has been in use by airlines for longer than the other airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.

Let’s say that the ElectricAir 552 has been in use by airlines for longer than the other airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.This fact does not affect the strength of the argument, which rests on the fact that ElectricAir 552 has the fewest injuries per accident of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers.This plane could have been in use for 200 years, and the truth is that that fact does not change the claim made by the ministry.

Similarly, if the ElectricAir 552 has not been in use by airlines for longer than the other airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers, this fact does not affect the fact that ElectricAir 552 has the fewest injuries per accident of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers, which is the keys statistic upon which the argument rests.
(D) Whether the ElectricAir 552 would be considered relatively safe if it were compared with all commercial airplanes rather than only with airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.

It’s important to see that this argument is focused on safety of all the commercial airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers.Therefore, whether or not the ElectricAir 552 would be considered relatively safe if it were compared with all commercial airplanes rather than only with airplanes that carry fewer than 30 passengers is not a question that would be useful to have answered.Of course, the answer to this question would help us in evaluating the conclusion of a different argument, one making the claim that the ElectricAir 552 is among the safest ofallcommercial airplanes.
(E) Whether pilots of the ElectricAir 552 have positive things to say about the safety features found on the plane.

Of course, the pilots of the ElectricAir 552 can say positive things (or not) about the safety features found on the plane, and these things they say will not alter the key fact (premise) provided, which is that the ElectricAir 552 has the fewest injuries per accident of any commercial airplane that carries fewer than 30 passengers.

question.text: The director of a study that compared the lifespans of two groups of people – those who exercised regularly and those who did not – is claiming that regular exercise does not help to increase people’s lifespans.He is pointing to the fact that, on average, the study participants who did not exercise regularly had longer lifespans than the study participants who did exercise regularly had.In evaluating the validity of the director’s claim, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether, at the time of the commencement of the study, the study participants who did not exercise regularly were, on average, younger than the participants who did
Whether, the number of study participants who exercised regularly was as large as the number of study participants who did not exercise regularly
Whether all of the study participants lived longer, on average, than the average lifespan of people in the general population
Whether most of the study participants who exercised regularly did so in order to cope with the stresses of unusually stressful occupations
Whether the people who did not exercise regularly experienced health problems that the people who did exercise regularly did not experience

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: On average, the study participants who did not exercise regularly had longer lifespans than the study participants who did exercise regularly had.
Conclusion: Regular exercise does not help to increase people’s lifespans.

In evaluating this argument, it’s important to consider how the study director has supported his conclusion.Is he correctly interpreting the data? Might there be hidden flaws in the data that are leading him to an incorrect conclusion? Might there be more to the story? Why would those who did not exercise regularly live longer than those who did?
What might we want to know to help us in properly evaluating the argument?
(A) Whether, at the time of the commencement of the study, the study participants who did not exercise regularly were, on average, younger than the participants who did

What this answer choice says does not affect the argument.A person’s lifespan is how many years he or she lives.Therefore, the age of the people at the time of the commencement of the study would not affect their lifespans.
Let’s apply the yes/no test:
Yes, at the time of the commencement of the study, the study participants who did not exercise regularly were, on average, younger than the participants who did.

So what.Right? The fact remains that a person’s lifespan is a person’s lifespan.That statistic is not affected by the ages of the people in the study, each of whom lived or will live some number of years.
No, at the time of the commencement of the study, the study participants who did not exercise regularly were not, on average, younger than the participants who did.

So, what.Right? The fact remains that a person’s lifespan is a person’s lifespan.That statistic is not affected by the ages of the people in the study, each of whom lived or will live some number of years.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether, the number of study participants who exercised regularly was as large as the number of study participants who did not exercise regularly

The study director’s conclusion is based on two averages: the average lifespan of the study participants who did not exercise regularly and the average lifespan of the study participants who did exercise regularly.

With that in mind, what we have to notice in order to avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice is that an average will not be higher or lower simply because it is based on a larger or smaller number of data points.

So, in this case, information on whether the number of study participants who exercised regularly was as large as the number of study participants who did not exercise regularly would not affect what the averages mean.After all, regardless of whether one group was larger or smaller than the other, the average lifespans of the people in the groups are still going to be the midpoints of the lifespans of the people in those groups, and those midpoints are still going to support the conclusion.
(C) Whether all of the study participants lived longer, on average, than the average lifespan of people in the general population
Information on whether the study participants lived longer, on average, than the average lifespan of people in the general population does not affect the support for the conclusion.

After all, regardless of whether, on average, the study participants lived longer than the average lifespan, some of the study participants did not exercise regularly, while some did, and difference between the average lifespans of the study participants who did not and did exercise still supports the conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) Whether most of the study participants who exercised regularly did so in order to cope with the stresses of unusually stressful occupations
A “Yes,” or “No,’ answer to the question presented by this choice does affect the argument.

It’s common knowledge that stress will shorten a person’s lifespan.So, if those who exercised regularly did so in order to cope with the stresses of unusually stressful occupations, stress could be the cause of their shorter lifespans.In that case, it could very well be that, contrary to the study director’s conclusion, regular exercise does help to increase people’s lifespans, but in this particular case, the effects of exercise were more than offset by the effects of stress.
Let’s apply the yes/no test:
Yes, most of the study participants who exercised regularly did so in order to cope with the stresses of unusually stressful occupations.

What that statement says weakens the argument by bringing up an alternative explanation for the shorter lifespans of the people who exercised: it’s not that exercise does not help to increase lifespans; it’s that these particular people were so stressed out that they had shorter lifespans even though they exercised.
No, most of the study participants who exercised regularly did not do so in order to cope with the stresses of unusually stressful occupations.

What that statement says strengthens the argument by eliminating a plausible alternative explanation for why the people who exercised had shorter lifespans.In other words, that information gives us more conviction that exercise did not help to increase the people’s lifespans.
(E)Incorrect.The answer to this question does not affect the argument.

We can get the impression that, if the answer is “Yes,” that information serves to call the conclusion of the argument into question, but actually, that information doesn't affect the correct conclusion.

The conclusion is that regular exercise does not help to increase people's lifespans.It’s not that regular exercise has no benefits.So, to avoid being fooled into choosing this choice, we have to be sure to keep the argument’s conclusion in mind as we consider this choice.
Let’s use the yes/no test:
Yes, the people who did not exercise regularly experienced health problems that the people who did exercise regularly did not experience.
Remember that the conclusion is not about health problems.It’s that regular exercise does not help to increase people's lifespans.So, this information has no effect on the argument.
No, the people who did not exercise regularly did not experienced health problems that the people who did exercise regularly did not experience.
Once again, this information does not affect this particular argument.

question.text: To raise additional revenue, the Mayor of Urbina is considering implementing a $12 fee per car for the use of its main bridge, which connects the city’s downtown area, where almost all of Urbina’s businesses are located, with the city’s regions in which most of its citizens reside.Members of the city council are expressing concerns that the new fee would substantially harm the downtown economy, claiming that it would encourage a large percentage of the downtown workforce to seek employment in nearby Leesville in order to avoid the bridge.The answer to each of the following questions would be useful for evaluating the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Could Urbinians traveling to or from downtown Urbina easily use alternative routes that would allow them to avoid using the main bridge without adding significantly to their travel time?
What fraction of the total population of Urbina uses the main bridge to enter and leave downtown each day?
Are wages paid in Leesville significantly lower than wages paid in Urbina for similar work?
Could citizens of Urbina conveniently utilize forms of transportation other than automobiles to get from the city’s regions in which most of its citizens reside to downtown?
What percentage of the downtown Urbina workforce uses the bridge to enter and leave downtown each day?

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Premise: To raise additional revenue, the Mayor of Urbina is considering implementing a $12 fee per car for the use of its main bridge, which connects the city’s downtown area, where almost all of Urbina’s businesses are located, with the city’s regions in which most of its citizens reside.

Conclusion: Members of the city council are expressing concerns that the new fee would substantially harm the downtown economy, claiming that it would encourage a large percentage of the downtown workforce to seek employment in nearby Leesville in order to avoid the bridge.

The mayor wants to charge each car $12 to use the bridge that connects the downtown with the areas where most citizens live.However, Members of the city council believe that the fee would harm the economy downtown because people would find work elsewhere in order to avoid the bridge.What might we want to know to better evaluate the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council? Is the City Council making any unwarranted assumptions?

Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful in evaluating the claim made; each of these will be an incorrect answer.Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful in evaluating the claim will be the correct answer.
(A) Could Urbinians traveling to or from downtown Urbina easily use alternative routes that would allow them to avoid using the main bridge without adding significantly to their travel time?

What this answer choice says would be useful for evaluating the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council.If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the claim made by the members of the city council is weakened because although there is now a $12 fee per car to use the bridge, people can easily take alternative routes into the city.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the claim made by the members of the city council is strengthened because without these alternative routes that drivers could utilize, they may very well be forced to use the bridge.As a result, to avoid the bridge, they make seek employment in nearby Leesville.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) What fraction of the total population of Urbina uses the main bridge to enter and leave downtown each day?

As always, it’s very important to clearly identify the conclusion of the argument, which in this case is that “new fee would substantially harm the downtown economy, claiming that it wouldencourage a large percentage of the downtown workforceto seek employment in nearby Leesville in order to avoid the bridge.”

Notice that the conclusion is regarding the downtown workforce; it is these people that the members of the city council are concerned about.Therefore, what this answer choice asks, “What fraction of thetotal population of Urbinauses the main bridge to enter and leave downtown each day?” is not helpful in evaluating the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council.The answer to the question could be “a big percentage” or the answer to the question could be “a small percentage,” and either way we’d be asking a question about the wrong population of people.In other words, this population of people – the total population of Urbina – is not the population germane to the conclusion.
As always, we must pay close attention to the details provided to us in the passage as well as in the answer choices.
(C) Are wages paid in Leesville significantly lower than wages paid in Urbina for similar work?
What this answer choice says would be useful for evaluating the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the claim made by the members of the city council is weakened because, although there is now a $12 fee per car to use the bridge, people may still continue to work in the city’s downtown area instead of seeking employment in nearby Leesville because the wages paid in Leesville are significantly lower than those paid in Urbina for similar work.In other words, even after considering the $12 per car fee to use the bridge, people might actually be worse off going to work in Leesville because the wages paid there are much lower than those paid in Urbina; the people may just be better off paying the $12 per car fee to use the bridge and staying in Urbina to work.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the claim made by the members of the city council is strengthened because people may very well seek employment in nearby Leesville.After all, if the wages are about the same for similar work in Leesville as they are in downtown Urbina, but people can avoid paying the $12 per car fee to use the bridge, these people may very well seek employment in nearby Leesville.
(D) Could citizens of Urbina conveniently utilize forms of transportation other than automobiles to get from the city’s regions in which most of its citizens reside to downtown?
What this answer choice says would be useful for evaluating the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the claim made by the members of the city council is weakened because, although there is now a $12 fee per car to use the bridge, citizens of Urbina could conveniently utilize forms of transportation other than automobiles to get from the city’s regions in which most of its citizens reside to downtown.In other words, if they chose to, they could avoid the bridge and its $12 per car fee and still get into downtown Urbina to work.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “no,” then the claim made by the members of the city council is strengthened because, without these alternative forms of transportation that drivers could utilize, they may very well be forced to use the bridge.As a result, to avoid the bridge, and its $12 per car fee, they may seek employment in nearby Leesville.
YOUR CHOICE(E) What percentage of the downtown Urbina workforce uses the bridge to enter and leave downtown each day?
What this answer choice says would be useful for evaluating the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council.

If the answer to this question is “a lot,” then the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council is strengthened because a high percentage of the downtown workforce would be affected by the $12 fee to use the bride.Let’s revisit the argument’s conclusion: “Members of the city council are expressing concerns that the new fee wouldsubstantially harm the downtown economy, claiming that it would encourage a large percentage of the downtown workforce to seek employment in nearby Leesville in order to avoid the bridge.” Notice the words “substantially harm the downtown economy.” Of course, the larger the percentage of the downtown economy that would be affected, the more substantial the damage would be.

Of course, if instead the answer to this question were “not much,” then the accuracy of the claim made by the members of the city council would be weakened because, although there might be a new $12 fee per car to use the bridge, and although as a result some of the downtown workforce may go to nearby Leesville to seek employment, the fraction of the downtown workers doing so would be small.After all, imagine if just 1% of the downtown workforce went to work in nearby Leesville? In that case, the impact may be immaterial, right?
