question.text: The two main companies that produce xanthafiers are Xanthaco and Xanthatech.While Xanthaco has always been the industry leader, designers at Xanthatech have made significant improvements to Xanthatech’s xanthafiers over the past year.Recently, Xanthaco reduced, for the first time in its 20-year history, the number of people working in its xanthafier manufacturing plant.Clearly, it would be prudent to sell the stock of Xanthaco, as it must be the case that increased competition resulting from Xanthatech’s improvements is beginning to negatively affect demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers.Establishing which of the following would be most useful in determining whether it makes sense to follow the recommendation made?

answers.text: Xanthaco’s average profit margin on the sale of xanthafiers over the past five years
The proportion of all new xanthafiers produced and sold over the past year that were produced and sold by Xanthaco
Whether Xanthaco has already manufactured enough xanthafiers to fill the orders for xanthafiers it expects to receive over the next six months
Whether during the past few months Xanthaco has made any investments in equipment that increases the number of xanthafiers it can manufacture per worker-hour
Whether Xanthatech has been manufacturing and selling xanthafiers for as long as Xanthaco has

explanation_parts.text: In order to answer the question, we have to evaluate the following recommendation:

Conclusion: It would be prudent to sell the stock of Xanthaco, as it must be the case that increased competition resulting from Xanthatech’s improvements is beginning to negatively affect demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers.
That recommendation is based on two facts:
Premise: Designers at Xanthatech have made significant improvements to Xanthatech’s xanthafiers over the past year.
Premise: Recently, Xanthaco reduced, for the first time, the number of people working in its xanthafier manufacturing plant.

OK, there is certainly reason to wonder whether Xanthaco has lost its edge, but we don’t know for sure that it has.After all, there could be an explanation for Xanthaco’s workforce reduction other than that increased competition from Xanthatech is beginning to negatively affect demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers.
What else would we want to know in order to evaluate the recommendation?
(A) Xanthaco’s average profit margin on the sale of xanthafiers over the past five years

Notice that, in evaluating the recommendation, what we are essentially seeking to do is determine whether something has changed, whether competition is beginning to negatively affect demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers.So, information on Xanthaco’s average profit margin over the past five years would not help us to evaluate the recommendation, because that information does not help us to figure out whether something has changed.

So, whether we have the information that the average profit margin has been 50 percent, or we have the information that the average profit margin has been 5 percent, we still have no idea whether things have changed and competition is beginning to negatively demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers.
So, the answer to this question would not help us to evaluate the recommendation.
(B) The proportion of all new xanthafiers produced and sold over the past year that were produced and sold by Xanthaco
Since this choice mentions a proportion rather than presents a question that can be answered “Yes,” or “No,” let’s use some extreme values for that proportion.
For the low extreme value, let’s use20 percent.
For the high extreme value, let’s use90 percent.
When we use 20 percent, we get the following:
20 percentof all new xanthafiers produced and sold over the past year were produced and sold by Xanthaco.
Does this information indicate whether competition is beginning to negatively demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers? No, this information does not help at all.
When we use 90 percent, we get the following:
90 percentof all new xanthafiers produced and sold over the past year were produced and sold by Xanthaco.
OK, that’s a huge percentage, but it could still be the case that competition is beginning to negatively affect demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers.
So, the answer to this question would not help us to evaluate the recommendation.
(C) Whether Xanthaco has already manufactured enough xanthafiers to fill the orders for xanthafiers it expects to receive over the next six months
Notice that the passage says, “Recently, Xanthaco reduced, for the first time in its 20-year history, the number of people working in its xanthafier manufacturing plant.”
This information tends to indicate that something has changed at Xanthaco.After all, Xanthaco is doing something, reducing its plant workforce, that it has never done before.
So, in order to evaluate the recommendation, we need to determine whether that change is that demand has decreased.

Notice that the answer to the question presented by this choice does not help us to make that determination.After all, regardless of whether Xanthaco has or has not already manufactured enough xanthafiers to fill the orders for xanthafiers it expects to receive over the next six months, that information doesn’t tell us what has changed at Xanthaco.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Whether during the past few months Xanthaco has made any investments in equipment that increases the number of xanthafiers it can manufacture per worker-hour
The answer to this question would certainly be useful in evaluating the recommendation.

Notice that, if the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have reason to believe that there is an alternative explanation for Xanthaco’s having reduced the number of people working in its plant: it has invested in equipment that increases the number of xanthafiers it can manufacture per worker-hour, so it doesn’t need as many workers as it used to need.In that case, the recommendation could very well be faulty, because it could very well be that demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers has not been negatively affected by competition.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then that information serves to rule out a possible alternative explanation for Xanthaco’s having reduced its plant workforce, making us a bit more confident that competition is beginning to negatively affect demand for Xanthaco’s xanthafiers, and that the recommendation is sound.
(E) Whether Xanthatech has been manufacturing and selling xanthafiers for as long as Xanthaco has

The recommendation that we have to evaluate involves what’s going on in the present and what will occur in the future.Thus, information on whether Xanthatech has been in business as long as Xanthaco has, which is information about the past, has no bearing on the value of the recommendation.
So, the answer to this question would not be useful in determining whether it makes sense to follow the recommendation.

question.text: Recently, a study of cow’s milk consumption found that, the greater the average number of glasses of milk a study participant consumed daily, the greater was the body fat percentage of that person at the end of the period of the study.Cow’s milk contains a number of hormones intended to help baby cows pack on as much body fat as possible over a very short period of time.Therefore, it must be true that these hormones are to blame for the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk.In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether the study participants exercised on a regular basis.
Whether all the study participants’ daily caloric intakes were the same.
The average body fat percentage of the study participants at the beginning of the study.
Whether it is important for a baby cow's survival that it pack on a significant amount of body fat.
Whether the study participants consumed, on average, more cow’s milk per day than is consumed by most people.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Background: Recently, a study of cow’s milk consumption found that, the greater the average number of glasses of milk a study participant consumed daily, the greater was the body fat percentage of that person at the end of the period of the study.
Premise: Cow’s milk contains a number of hormones intended to help baby cows pack on as much body fat as possible over a very short period of time.
Conclusion: Therefore, it must be true that these hormones are to blame for the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk.

Notice that we have a cause-and-effect argument.The author believes that the hormones found in milk are to blame for the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk.
As we know, we must always be skeptical of cause-and-effect arguments.Remember, the fact that two variables are found together is not solid evidence that one causes the other.

Are the hormones really what is to blame for the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk? Could there be an alternative explanation? What else might we want to know in order to evaluate this argument?
(A) Whether the study participants exercised on a regular basis.

Information on whether the study participants exercised on a regular basis does not help us to evaluate the argument, whose conclusion rests on the fact that cow’s milk contains a number of hormones intended to help baby cows pack on as much body fat as possible over a very short period of time.
Let’s say that the participants did exercise regularly.What does that tell us about the effect that these hormones have on the body fat of these who drink milk?
Conversely, let’s say that the participants did not exercise regularly.What does that tell us about the effect that these hormones have on the body fat of these who drink milk?
In either case, are we more or less sure about the effect that the hormones found in milk have on a person’s body fat? No, we are not.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether all the study participants’ daily caloric intakes were the same.

Notice that the author claims that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the hormones found in cow’s milk and the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk.In making such a claim, the author assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not another explanation for the results from the study.
Therefore, in evaluating the efficacy of this argument, it would be important to know whether all the study participants’ daily caloric intakes were the same.

After all, imagine if the caloric intakes of the participants in the study were not the same? In other words, for example, imagine if the people in the study who consumed a greater number of glasses of milk each day also consumed far more calories than did those who consumed fewer glasses of milk each day.In this situation, the argument would be weakened because we’d have less conviction that it's the hormones in milk that explain (that are the cause of) the higher body fat percentages.After all, if the people drinking more milk are also consuming more calories than those drinking less milk, couldn’t those higher calories be to blame for the higher body fat percentages? For sure, they could.In such a case, the argument is weakened.

On the other hand, if it is true that all the study participants’ daily caloric intakes were the same, then the argument is stronger because we can rule out this alternative explanation (differences in caloric intake) for why the people who drank more milk also had higher body far percentages at the end of the study.
(C) The average body fat percentage of the study participants at the beginning of the study.

It would not be helpful for us to know average body fat percentage of the study participants at the beginning of the study.Let’s say that the average body fat percentage was “very high.” Would that fact make us any more or less certain about the link between the hormones found in milk and the greater body fat percentage? No, it would not.

What if the average body fat percentage was “very low.” Would that fact make us any more or less certain about the link between the hormones found in milk and the greater body fat percentage? No, it would not.

A key move to not choosing this answer choice as correct (as expected) is to focus carefully on the details provided in the passage.What did the study find? It found that “the greater the average number of glasses of milk a study participant consumed daily,the greater was the body fat percentage of that person at the end of the period of the study.” The study participants could begin the study with any average body fat percentage and we would still be able to look at their ending body fat percentages of the participants.In other words, regardless of the average body fat percentage of the people at the beginning of the study, we could still ask “Did the body fat percentage increase or decrease?”
(D) Whether it is important for a baby cow's survival that it pack on a significant amount of body fat.

It’s important to remember that the premises presented to us in a passage are considered factual.In this argument, we’re told that “Cow’s milk contains a number of hormones intended to help baby cows pack on as much body fat as possible over a very short period of time.” Also, notice that the argument rests on this fact.

Now, notice that what this answer choice says takes us in a different direction from the direction in which we need to go.Why would it be important to know whether it is important for a baby cow's survival that it pack on a significant amount of body fat.What if it is important for a baby cow's survival that it pack on a significant amount of body fat? Would that fact help us in evaluating the argument? No, it would not, because the argument’s conclusion is based on the fact that cow’s milk contains a number of hormones intended to help baby cows pack on as much body fat as possible over a very short period of time.

On the other hand, what if it is not important for a baby cow's survival that it pack on a significant amount of body fat? Would that fact help us in evaluating the argument? No, it would not, because the argument’s conclusion is based on the fact that cow’s milk contains a number of hormones intended to help baby cows pack on as much body fat as possible over a very short period of time.
(E) Whether the study participants consumed, on average, more cow’s milk per day than is consumed by most people.
It is not relevant whether the study participants consumed, on average, more cow’s milk per day than is consumed by most people.

Let’s say that they did.Does that strengthen or weaken the conclusion that “It must be true that these hormones are to blame for the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk?” No, it does not.

On the other hand, let’s say that they did not.Does that strengthen or weaken the conclusion that “It must be true that these hormones are to blame for the higher body fat percentages of the study participants who consumed greater amounts of milk?” No, it does not.

question.text: For years, ElectronStar has produced and profitably sold lithium batteries for use in electric cars.However, starting next month, electric car manufacturers will be required to pay a significant per-battery tax to cover expenses related to the disposal of worn out batteries.The car manufacturers will certainly pass much of this new cost onto ElectronStar and similar companies, thus rendering ElectronStar’s business unprofitable.Therefore, management at ElectronStar is considering switching to producing electric airplane batteries, for which there currently is no disposal tax.Management believes that the move will allow ElectronStar to continue to operate profitably.Useful for evaluating the validity of the belief of ElectronStar’s management is the answer to each of the following questions EXCEPT:

answers.text: To what degree are ElectronStar’s existing plant and equipment suitable for producing batteries for use in electric airplanes?
Will electric airplane manufacturers be willing to pay for batteries a per unit price as high as that paid to ElectronStar by electric car manufacturers?
In the near future, will electric airplane manufacturers be required to pay a significant electric battery disposal tax?
Can ElectronStar avoid having to deal with the repercussions of the car battery disposal tax by using a material other than lithium as the basis of its batteries?
Do manufacturers of electric airplanes already have with suppliers contractual relationships structured to meet the manufacturers’ battery requirements for the foreseeable future?

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Background: For years, ElectronStar has produced and profitably sold lithium batteries for use in electric cars.However, starting next month, electric car manufacturers will be required to pay a significant per-battery tax to cover expenses related to the disposal of worn out batteries.
Background: The car manufacturers will certainly pass much of this new cost onto ElectronStar and similar companies, thus rendering ElectronStar’s business unprofitable.
Premise: Management at ElectronStar is considering switching to producing electric airplane batteries, for which there currently is no disposal tax.
Conclusion: Management believes that the move will allow ElectronStar to continue to operate profitably.

Notice that Management at ElectronStar is proposing a plan – they are considering switching to producing electric airplane batteries, for which there currently is no battery disposal tax.Is this a smart plan? A well thought out plan? Remember that, any time a plan is proposed, an inherent assumption is that the plan will work as expected.In other words, management is assuming that there will not be unforeseen factors that will derail the plan.What might we want to consider? Could there be some malicious variables that might derail the plan?
Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful in evaluating the plan; each of these will be an incorrect answer.
Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful in evaluating the plan will be the correct answer.
(A) To what degree are ElectronStar’s existing plant and equipment suitable for producing batteries for use in electric airplanes?
The answer to this question would be useful for evaluating the validity of the belief of ElectronStar’s management.

If the answer to this question is “a high degree,” then we have more faith in the management’s belief.After all, if ElectronStar’s existing plant and equipment are very suitable for producing batteries for use in electric airplanes, then the company could make this battery-production-pivot much, much more easily than if ElectronStar’s existing plant and equipment are very suitable for producing batteries for use in electric airplanes.Right?

After all, imagine that the answer to this question is “no.” Of course, in this case ElectronStar’s existing plant and equipment may need expensive, time-consuming-to-implement, changes and alterations.In such a scenario, ElectronStar’s switching to producing electric airplane batteries might very well not allow ElectronStar to continue to operate profitably.
Useful? Yes.
(B) Will electric airplane manufacturers be willing to pay for batteries a per unit price as high as that paid to ElectronStar by electric car manufacturers?
The answer to this question would be useful for evaluating the validity of the belief of ElectronStar’s management.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have more reason to believe that ElectronStar’s management has come up with a good plan.After all, if electric airplane manufacturers are willing to pay for batteries a per unit price as high as that paid to ElectronStar by electric car manufacturers, then the company will continue to enjoy a similar level of revenue.

On the other hand, if the answer is “No,” then we have a good reason to believe that the plan may not work out as intended.After all, if electric airplane manufacturers are not willing to pay for batteries a per unit price as high as that paid to ElectronStar by electric car manufacturers, then perhaps ElectronStar will not be able to continue to operate profitably, even after it moves to producing electric airplane batteries, for which there currently is no disposal tax.
(C) In the near future, will electric airplane manufacturers be required to pay a significant electric battery disposal tax?
The answer to this question would be useful for evaluating the validity of the belief of ElectronStar’s management.

Let’s say that the answer to the question is “Yes.” In this case, ElectronStar might make the switch to producing electric airplane batteries (because of the significant per-battery tax electric car manufacturers will be required to pay to cover expenses related to the disposal of worn out batteries) only to realize that in the near future, electric airplane manufacturers be required to pay a significant electric battery disposal tax.Of course, such a tax might create a undesirable situation similar to that created by the significant electric battery disposal tax currently in place on car batteries.

On the other hand, if the answer to the question is “No,” then we have more reason to believe that the plan is a good one since we can rule out the possibility that such a problematic situation will develop.
Useful? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Can ElectronStar avoid having to deal with the repercussions of the car battery disposal tax by using a material other than lithium as the basis of its batteries?

What isElectronStar’s plan? ElectronStar is considering “switching to producing electric airplane batteries, for which there currently is no disposal tax,” to avoid a significant per-battery tax to cover expenses related to the disposal of worn out car batteries.Again, the plan is “producing electric airplane batteries. ”

Notice that what answer choice (D) says would help us evaluate the validity of another plan, a different plan, but it does not help us in determining whether this plan will work.We need to be careful to not be seduced by the idea of “a better/different plan” when we are evaluating the efficacy of a plan presented in the passage.

If you have a hard time seeing why this answer is correct, we can use the “yes/no” test.What if the answer to this question is Yes? Well, ok, great, but we are not any closer to knowing how valid the plan presented in the passage is.Similarly, if the answer to the question is “No” we are not any closer to knowing how valid the plan presented in the passage is.
Useful? No.
(E) Do manufacturers of electric airplanes already have with suppliers contractual relationships structured to meet the manufacturers’ battery requirements for the foreseeable future?
The answer to this question would be useful for evaluating the validity of the belief of ElectronStar’s management.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have a good reason to believe that the plan may not work out as intended.After all, if manufacturers of electric airplanes already have with suppliers contractual relationships structured to meet the manufacturers’ battery requirements for the foreseeable future, then from where will ElectronStar get the business it needs to continue to operate profitably?

On the other hand, if the answer is “No,” then ElectronStar’s switching to producing electric airplane batteries might very well allow ElectronStar to continue to operate profitably because manufacturers of electric airplanes may purchase ElectronStar’s batteries.
Useful? Yes.

question.text: It was found that, during a certain period of time, of the applicants to the various graduate schools at a certain university, the percentage of the men who applied to the schools and were admitted was much smaller than the percentage of the women who applied and were admitted.Clearly, during the period of time in question, the admissions processes of that university’s graduate schools were biased against men.The answer to which of the following questions about the period of time in question would be most useful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion drawn?

answers.text: Did a greater proportion of men than of women receive scholarships to attend graduate schools at the university?
Did the acceptance rates of the university’s graduate schools decline over the period of time in question?
Did a greater proportion of the men than of the women apply to the university’s graduate schools with the lowest acceptance ratios?
Did a greater proportion of the accepted men than of the accepted women end up attending graduate schools at the university?
Did a greater proportion of the men than of the women apply to the graduate schools of the university with the most challenging curricula?

explanation_parts.text: To answer this question, we have to find the choice the answer to which helps us evaluate the validity of the following conclusion:
Conclusion: During the period of time in question, the admissions processes of that university’s graduate schools were biased against men.

The author of the argument has drawn that conclusion on the basis of the fact that the graduate schools of the university accepted a lower percentage of the men who applied than of the women who applied.
Of course, the author’s conclusion could easily be incorrect, because the difference in acceptance rates could have been caused by things other than biased admissions processes.
What information would help us to determine whether biased admissions processes were the cause for the difference in acceptance rates?
(A) Did a greater proportion of men than of women receive scholarships to attend graduate schools at the university?

Notice that, regardless of whether a greater proportion of men than of women received scholarships, the admissions processes of the schools could have been biased against men.After all, getting admitted and getting a scholarship are two different things.
So, the answer to this question does not help us evaluate the validity of the conclusion.
(B) Did the acceptance rates of the university’s graduate schools decline over the period of time in question?

Unless we make some unwarranted assumptions, information on whether the acceptance rates of the university’s graduate schools declined over the period of time in question does not help us to evaluate the validity of the argument’s conclusion.
After all, a decline in the acceptance rates should affect men and women applicants equally rather than cause a difference between the acceptance rates of men and women.
So, neither a “Yes” nor a “No” answer to this question affects the strength of the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Did a greater proportion of the men than of the women apply to the university’s graduate schools with the lowest acceptance ratios?
Let’s apply the yes/no test to this question.
Yes, a greater proportion of the men than of the women applied to the university’s graduate schools with the lowest acceptance ratios.

Notice what this “Yes” answer indicates.If a greater proportion of men than of women applied to the university’s graduate schools with the lowest acceptance ratios, then we have an alternative explanation for the men’s lower acceptance rate: the men tended to apply to the schools with the lowest acceptance ratios, while the women tended to apply to the schools with higher acceptance ratios.In that case, of course the proportion of women accepted was higher, but not because of bias, but because the men and women were applying in different proportions to schools with lower and higher acceptance ratios.
Of course then, a “No” answer to this question would strengthen the argument, by serving to rule out that alternative explanation, and make us a bit more convinced that the conclusion is correct.
(D) Did a greater proportion of the accepted men than of the accepted women end up attending graduate schools at the university?

Information on whether the proportion of accepted men who attended the schools was greater than the proportion of accepted women who attended the schools does not give us insight into why the proportion of men accepted was lower than the proportion of women accepted.
So, the answer to this question would not help us to evaluate the validity of the conclusion.
(E) Did a greater proportion of the men than of the women apply to the graduate schools of the university with the most challenging curricula?

This choice could be tempting, because we might get the impression that a “Yes” answer would indicate that the reason why the acceptance rate for men was lower was that a greater proportion of the men than of the women applied to the schools with the most challenging curricula.

However, notice that, unless we make some unwarranted assumptions, there is no reason to decide that the reason why the acceptance rate for men was lower was that a greater proportion of the men applied to the schools with the most challenging curricula.After all, the degree to which a school’s curriculum is challenging and the school’s acceptance rate are two different matters.
So, the answer to this question would not help us evaluate the validity of the conclusion.

question.text: A study included participants who were considered depressed and participants who were considered not depressed.When the participants were presented with a decision-making related task, the depressed participants were more thorough in their analysis than the non-depressed participants were in theirs, with the result that the depressed participants made better decisions than the non-depressed participants made.It seems clear that, while depression is believed to result in impairment of some forms of cognitive functioning, depression actually enhances people’s ability to reason analytically.In evaluating the validity of the conclusion, it would be most valuable to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether, through cognitive skills training, the non-depressed participants could develop analytical reasoning skills comparable to those exhibited by the depressed participants
Whether making optimal decisions is an important aspect of the process of recovering from depression
The degree to which the relative strength of the analytical reasoning skills of depressed participants made up for any impairment of other forms of cognitive functioning that they experienced
The percentage of the non-depressed participants who later became depressed
Whether it is the case that there is a positive correlation between a person’s tendency to carefully contemplate situations and the likelihood that that person will experience depression

explanation_parts.text: Notice that the author is making the following cause-and-effect claim:
Conclusion: Depression actually enhances people’s ability to reason analytically.

The evidence used to support the conclusion is simply that, when the participants in a study were presented with a decision-making related task, the depressed participants were more thorough in their analysis than the non-depressed participants were in theirs, with the result that the depressed participants made better decisions than the non-depressed participants made.

It’s important to see that the author believes that, because one variable (depression) appeared along with another variable (making better decisions), the first variable caused the second variable.In drawing the cause-and-effect conclusion, the author assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed pattern.
What might we want to know to help us to evaluate whether that assumption is a good one? Might there be an alternative explanation?
(A) Whether, through cognitive skills training, the non-depressed participants could develop analytical reasoning skills comparable to those exhibited by the depressed participants

Notice that the conclusion of the argument is not that depression is the only path to better decision making or that depressed people are always better at making decisions than other people.It’s simply that depression enhances people’s ability to reason analytically.

Of course, that conclusion could be supported by the study evidence regardless of whether there is another way, such as through cognitive skills training, in which people could enhance their ability to reason analytically.After all, there can be multiple different things (in this case, depression or training), that cause an effect, (in this case, enhanced reasoning skills).
So, determining the answer to this question would not be valuable in evaluating the validity of the conclusion.
(B) Whether making optimal decisions is an important aspect of the process of recovering from depression
We have to be careful not to be fooled into choosing this choice by the fact that it mentions decision making and depression, because what this choice says goes in the wrong direction.
The conclusion is that depression enhances people’s ability to reason analytically.
A “Yes” or “No” answer to the question presented by this choice would indicate whether ability to make optimal decisions causes recovery from depression.
In other words, this choice is about the effects of the decision-making skills of depressed people, not the cause of those skills.
So, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” we don’t have information indicating whether depression enhances people’s ability to reason analytically.
(C) The degree to which the relative strength of the analytical reasoning skills of depressed participants made up for any impairment of other forms of cognitive functioning that they experienced

This choice could be tempting, because the passage mentions impairment of cognitive functioning in depressed people.So, the question presented by this choice seems pertinent to what the passage discusses.

At the same time, this choice is not the correct answer because, regardless of whether the relative strength of the analytical reasoning skills of depressed participants did or did not make up for any impairment of other forms of cognitive functioning they experienced, that information does not indicate whether depression caused the depressed participants to have relatively strong analytical skills.
In other words, this choice is about the effects of the relatively strong analytical skills of the depressed participants, not the cause of those skills.
(D) The percentage of the non-depressed participants who later became depressed
Since this choice mentions a percentage rather than presents a question that can be answered “Yes,” or “No,” let’s use some extreme values for that percentage.
For the low extreme value, let’s use0 percent.
For the high extreme value, let’s use100 percent.
When we use 0 percent, we get the following:
0 percentof the non-depressed participants later became depressed.
Do we now know whether depression enhances people’s ability to reason analytically? No.
When we use 100 percent, we get the following:
100 percentof the non-depressed participants later became depressed.
Do we now know whether depression enhances people’s ability to reason analytically? No.
The answer to this question has no effect on the support for the conclusion.

YOUR CHOICE(E) Whether it is the case that there is a positive correlation between a person’s tendency to carefully contemplate situations and the likelihood that that person will experience depression
Determining the answer to this question is valuable.

Notice that what this question is asking is essentially whether people who carefully contemplate things are more likely to become depressed than other people.In other words, is depression likely tofollowcareful contemplation?

Thus, if the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have an alternative explanation for the relatively strong analytical reasoning skills of the depressed people.They were already thinking more carefully than other peoplebeforethey became depressed.So, a “Yes” answer to this question weakens the argument by providing reason to believe that it is not the case that depression enhances people’s ability to reason analytically, and that it is instead the case that, for some reason, people who reason carefully are more likely to become depressed.

On the other hand, a “No” answer to this question strengthens the argument by serving to rule out that alternative explanation for the relatively strong reasoning skills of the depressed participants.In other words, a “No” answer gives us more confidence that the author’s conclusion is correct.

question.text: For a study, researchers used a standardized written personality test to analyze the psychological characteristics of genetically identical twins who were reared apart by separate sets of adoptive parents.Because the test responses of the individuals within each genetically identical, though reared apart, twin pair were found to be significantly more similar than are the responses of people in general on the same test, the researchers concluded that genetics to a large degree underlie psychological differences between people.Which of the following, if true, is most important to take into account in evaluating the validity of the researchers’ conclusion?

answers.text: Identical twins who are reunited after being reared apart often exaggerate stories that seem to indicate that they are psychologically similar, making themselves seem more similar than they are.
In many cases, even though identical twins within a pair are genetically identical, when such twins are adults, people can readily tell the twins apart.
Some of the test responses included among the responses of people in general came from identical twins reared apart.
The standardized personality test used for the study was not developed for the purpose of demonstrating degrees of psychological similarity between individuals.
Adoption agencies often have policies involving placing adopted children into homes similar in certain ways to the homes into which the children were born.

explanation_parts.text: The researchers have concluded the following:
Conclusion: Genetics to a large degree underlie psychological differences between people.
The researchers have arrived at that conclusion because genetically identical twins who have been reared apart are more similar psychologically to each other than people normally are to each other.
Notice that what the researchers have concluded is essentially that genetics causes psychology.As usual, we must be skeptical of this cause-and-effect conclusion.

Since the answer choices in this Evaluate question are in the form of statements rather than questions, we have to find the one choice that either weakens or strengthens the support for the conclusion.
What information is important to take into account in evaluating the researchers’ cause-and-effect conclusion?
(A) Identical twins who are reunited after being reared apart often exaggerate stories that seem to indicate that they are psychologically similar, making themselves seem more similar than they are.
This choice could be tempting, because it could be perceived as indicating that the identical twins involved in the study weren’t really as psychologically similar to each other as they seemed.
Notice, however, that this choice is about something different from what the researchers’ based their conclusion on.

This choice is about stories twins tell, whereas the researchers based their conclusion on standardized personality test results.It’s one thing to exaggerate stories and another to respond similarly on a standardized personality test.
Thus, regardless of what this choice says, the researchers’ evidence could still provide solid support for their conclusion.
So, this choice is a pseudo-weakener that doesn’t really make any difference.
(B) In many cases, even though identical twins within a pair are genetically identical, when such twins are adults, people can readily tell the twins apart.

Regardless of whether people can tell genetically identical twins apart, it remains true that the twins are genetically identical and that the test responses of identical twins involved the study were unusually similar to each other.
So, what this choice says does not affect the premises of the argument or the support those premises provide for the conclusion.
(C) Some of the test responses included among the responses of people in general came from identical twins reared apart.
We have to be careful not to be fooled into choosing this choice by the fact that it mentions identical twins reared apart.

Of course, test responses of people in general would include responses from people with all kinds of backgrounds and characteristics, including the characteristic of being identical twins reared apart.

What we need to see about what this choice says is that it does not change the fact that responses of people in general would be a broad set of responses from a diverse group of people even if the responses included some responses from identical twins reared apart.
Thus, what this choice says does not affect the nature of the evidence or the implications of the evidence.So, it does not affect the support for the conclusion.
(D) The standardized personality test used for the study was not developed for the purpose of demonstrating degrees of psychological similarity between individuals.

Regardless of the purpose for which the test was developed, it remains the case that the twins’ test responses were relatively similar and that the similarity of the twins’ responses tends to indicate that the twins are psychologically similar.
So, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Adoption agencies often have policies involving placing adopted children into homes similar in certain ways to the homes into which the children were born.
Notice what the implications of this choice are.

If adoption agencies often have policies involving placing adopted children into homes similar in certain ways to the homes into which the children were born, then it’s likely that two identical twins who are placed into separate homes are placed into homes similar in certain ways to the home into which the twins were born.As a result, while such identical twins are reared in two separate homes, the twins are also likely reared in homes that are relatively similar to each other.

Of course, the fact that the twins are likely reared in relatively similar homes is an alternative explanation for the psychological similarity between such twins.After all, we would expect people who have been reared in relatively similar homes to be more psychologically similar to each other than people normally are to each other.

Thus, by indicating that there is a viable alternative explanation for the psychological similarity between the reared apart identical twins, this choice serves to call into question both the idea that the twins’ identical genetics are the cause of their psychological similarity and the more general conclusion that genetics cause psychology.
So, the information provided by this choice is clearly important to take into account in evaluating the validity of the researchers’ conclusion.

question.text: Currently, the government is looking for organizations to handle numerous large software development projects.However, TechInc, a civilian software development company, currently does not have enough skilled software developers to handle such large projects.Furthermore, it lacks sufficient revenue or assets to hire such developers.Therefore, to increase revenue and net income by taking on some of these large government projects, TechInc’s management is considering raising investment capital in order to hire additional software developers.Each of the following pieces of information would be important for management of TechInc to consider in evaluating their plan, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Whether the cost of the capital raised for hiring developers would make it unfeasible for TechInc to make other investments necessary for the operation of its business.
Whether there would be available for hiring by TechInc skilled software developers in numbers sufficient for performing the work necessary for the large government projects.
Whether management at TechInc has the operational experience necessary to effectively manage large government projects.
Whether large government projects are available only to non-civilian software development organizations.
Whether the government is looking for organizations to handle software development projects that are not larger than the projects that TechInc has been working on.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Currently, the government is looking for organizations to handle numerous large software development projects.
Premise: TechInc, a civilian software development company, currently does not have enough skilled software developers to handle such large projects.
Premise: Furthermore, it lacks sufficient revenue or assets to hire such developers.

Conclusion: Therefore, to increase revenue and net income by taking on some of these government projects, TechInc’s management is considering raising investment capital in order to hire additional software developers.
Management at TechInc is considering raising investment capital in order to hire additional software developers so that the company can increase revenue and net income.

As with any plan, the authors of the plan believe that the plan will work as they have designed it; they assume that there will not be other unexpected variables that arise that will negatively impact the plan.Are there other things that management needs to consider?

Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful for TechInc’s management to consider; each of these will be an incorrect answer.Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful for TechInc’s management to consider will be the correct answer.
(A) Whether the cost of the capital raised for hiring developers would make it unfeasible for TechInc to make other investments necessary for the operation of its business.
This information would be useful for TechInc’s management to consider.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then there is reason to believe that management’s plan will not work because, if the cost of the capital raised for hiring developers would make it unfeasible for TechInc to make other investments necessary for the operation of its business, then there is a good chance that the plan will fail.

On the other hand, if the answer is “No,” then we have an additional reason to believe that the plan will work because we see that, even after taking into account the cost of the capital raised for hiring developers, TechInc could continue to make other investments necessary for the operation of its business.
Useful? Yes.
(B) Whether there would be available for hiring by TechInc skilled software developers in numbers sufficient for performing the work necessary for the large government projects.
This information would be useful for TechInc’s management to consider.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have more faith in management’s plan because, if there would be available for hiring by TechInc skilled software developers in numbers sufficient for performing the work necessary for the large government projects, then TechInc would actually be able to hire, in sufficient quantities, the skilled software developers necessary to the success of the plan.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then we have less faith in management’s plan, because TechInc would not be able to hire, in sufficient quantities, the skilled software developers necessary for the success of the plan.
Useful? Yes.
(C) Whether management at TechInc has the operational experience necessary to effectively manage large government projects.
This information would be useful for TechInc’s management to consider.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have more faith in management’s plan because, if management at TechInc has the operational experience necessary to effectively manage large government projects, then there is reason to believe that the plan will succeed.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then we have less faith in management’s plan, because without the necessary operational experience to manage large government projects, management at TechInc may experience significant problems in executing its plan.
Useful? Yes.
(D) Whether large government projects are available only to non-civilian software development organizations.
This information would be useful for TechInc’s management to consider.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then we have less faith in management’s plan because, if large government projects are available only to non-civilian software development companies, then even if TechInc has an army of skilled software developers, management’s plan will fail.

However, if the answer to this question is “No,” then we have more faith in management’s plan because, if large government projects are available to civilian software development companies, then we have addressed a possible issue that could have interfered with the success of the plan.
Useful? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Whether the government is looking for organizations to handle software development projects that are not larger than the projects that TechInc has been working on.
This information would not be useful for TechInc’s management to consider.
Notice that TechInc’s management’s plan involves increasing revenue and net income by taking on some large government projects that are larger than any the company is currently set up to handle.

Thus, neither a “Yes” nor a “No” answer to this question would be useful for management to consider, because the success of their plan to take on those large government projects does not depend on whether the government is also looking for organizations to handle software development projects that are not larger than the projects that TechInc has been working on.
Useful? No.

question.text: Three years ago, the Hedge Corporation began using a new curriculum for preparing its associates for the FINENG exam, a difficult exam that tests principles of financial engineering.It must be the case that the new curriculum is better than any of the curricula that the company used previously in preparing its associates for the FINENG exam, as, since the company began using the new curriculum, the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether, during the past three years, the FINENG exam pass rate of Hedge Corporation associates has been higher than the pass rate of all people who have taken the exam
Whether the Hedge Corporation instructors who used the new curriculum to teach the associates received training on how to best teach using that new curriculum
Whether, ten years ago, the writers of the FINENG exam eliminated from the exam a number of the most difficult concepts that had been tested
Whether three years ago the Hedge Corporation, for the first time in its history, made it mandatory that employees take a course covering the best practices in teaching and learning
Whether the percentage of Hedge Corporation employees who have taken the FINENG exam in each of the last three years has been greater than it was in previous years

explanation_parts.text: Three years ago, the Hedge Corporation began using a new curriculum for preparing its associates for the FINENG exam, a difficult exam that tests principles of financial engineering.It must be the case that the new curriculum is better than any of the curricula that the company used previously in preparing its associates for the FINENG exam, as, since the company began using the new curriculum, the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.
(A) Whether, during the past three years, the FINENG exam pass rate of Hedge Corporation associates has been higher than the pass rate of all people who have taken the exam

What this answer choice says would not be helpful in evaluating the argument because the argument’s conclusion, that the new curriculum is better than any of the curricula that the company used previously in preparing its associates for the FINENG exam, is based upon the evidence that since using the new curriculum, the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.Notice that the evidence is a comparison between the pass rate of those at the company who used the new curriculum and that of those at the company who took the exam in the past without the new curriculum.Therefore, we need not be concerned with the pass rates of all people who have taken the exam.

After all, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” the strength of the argument would be unchanged because, in either case, it is true that the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.
(B) Whether the Hedge Corporation instructors who used the new curriculum to teach the associates received training on how to best teach using that new curriculum

Regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No” the strength of the argument would be unchanged because we know as fact that the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam after using the new curriculum has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.

So, regardless of whether instructors who used the new curriculum to teach the associates received training on how to best teach using that new curriculum the fact is that, as presented, the argument is sound.If the instructors were trained, then great; they were trained and the fact remains that the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam after using the new curriculum has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.

If the instructors were not trained, the pass rate of the associates who have taken the exam after using the new curriculum still has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.
(C) Whether, ten years ago, the writers of the FINENG exam eliminated from the exam a number of the most difficult concepts that had been tested

Notice that what this answer choice says describes a change to the exam that would have occurred ten years ago.However, the company began using the new curriculum three years ago.In other words, the company began using the new curriculum seven years after the exam change, and we know that the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam after using the new curriculum has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.

Imagine if, yes, for the first time ten years ago, the writers of the FINENG exam eliminated from the exam a number of the most difficult concepts that had been tested.Great, but the fact remains that associates who have taken the exam after using the new curriculum has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history (either before or after the change made to the exam).
YOUR CHOICE(D) Whether three years ago the Hedge Corporation, for the first time in its history, made it mandatory that employees take a course covering the best practices in teaching and learning
This argument makes the cause and effect claim that the new curriculum is better than any of the curricula that the company used previously in preparing its associates for the FINENG exam.

However, what if three years ago the Hedge Corporation, for the first time in its history, made it mandatory that employees take a course covering the best practices in teaching and learning? Wouldn’t that fact cast doubt on the validity of the argument? For sure it would because we’d now have an alternative explanation for why the pass rates improved.

Alternatively, if the Hedge Corporation did not make such a course mandatory three years ago then the argument is, then the argument is stronger because we have ruled out an alternative explanation for the improved pass rates.
(E) Whether the percentage of Hedge Corporation employees who have taken the FINENG exam in each of the last three years has been greater than it was in previous years

What if the percentage of Hedge Corporation employees who have taken the FINENG exam in each of the last three years has been greater than it was in previous years? Would the strength of the argument be affected? No, because the conclusion is based upon the evidence that since using the new curriculum, the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.

What if the percentage of Hedge Corporation employees who have taken the FINENG exam in each of the last three years has NOT been greater than it was in previous years? Would the strength of the argument be affected? No, because the conclusion is based upon the evidence that since using the new curriculum, the pass rate of its associates who have taken the exam has been significantly higher than the pass rate during any other time in the company’s history.

question.text: In a study of more than 1,000 women aged 40 to 50, it was determined that the greater the number of alcoholic drinks a woman consumed per week the greater was the likelihood that that woman had experienced a cardiac event, such as a heart attack.The researchers therefore concluded that a person’s drinking alcohol increases the likelihood that that person will experience a cardiac event.Determining which of the following wouldnotbe helpful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion arrived at by the researchers?

answers.text: Whether there was observed in the women studied a positive correlation between the number of alcoholic drinks a woman consumed weekly and the number of cigarettes she smoked daily
Whether a woman’s having a stressful job increases both the number of alcoholic drinks she consumes each week and the likelihood that she will experience a cardiac event
Whether suffering a cardiac event predisposes a woman to drink more alcoholic drinks per week than she otherwise would
Whether women with relatively healthy diets and lifestyles are more likely to avoid consumption of alcohol than those with less healthy diets and lifestyles
Whether there was observed in the women studied a positive correlation between the age of a woman and the likelihood that she had experienced a cardiac event

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Premise: In a study of more than 1,000 women aged 40 to 50, it was determined that, the greater the number of alcoholic drinks a woman consumed per week, the greater was the likelihood that that woman had experienced a cardiac event, such as a heart attack.
Conclusion: The researchers therefore concluded that a person’s drinking alcohol increases the likelihood that that person will experience a cardiac event.

The researchers make the cause-and-effect claim that a person’s drinking alcohol increases the likelihood that that person will experience a cardiac event.As we know well, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim we encounter.

What is the researchers’ evidence for their cause-and-effect conclusion? Their evidence is simply an observation: “it was determined that, the greater the number of alcoholic drinks a woman consumed per week, the greater was the likelihood that that woman had experienced a cardiac event, such as a heart attack.”

Remember, the fact that two variables are observed to move together does not prove that one of the variables caused the other variable.Couldn’t there be other possible explanations for the observed pattern?

Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful in evaluating the cause-and-effect claim made; each of these will be an incorrect answer.
Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful in evaluating the cause-and-effect claim will be the correct answer.
(A) Whether there was observed in the women studied a positive correlation between the number of alcoholic drinks a woman consumed weekly and the number of cigarettes she smoked daily
This information would be helpful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion arrived at by the researchers.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” the argument is weakened, because if the more a woman drinks, the more she smokes, then it could be that smoking is causing the cardiac events in the women who drink more, and their drinking more has nothing to do with the cardiac events.Rather, amount of drinking is just a variable that happens to correlate with amount of smoking, which is the real cause of the cardiac events.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” the argument is strengthened, because the answer serves to rule out the possibility that smoking rather than drinking is the cause of the higher rate of cardiac events in women who drink more.
Helpful? Yes.
(B) Whether a woman’s having a stressful job increases both the number of alcoholic drinks she consumes each week and the likelihood that she will experience a cardiac event
This information would be helpful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion arrived at by the researchers.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the argument is weakened because we have plausible evidence to believe that drinking alcohol might not cause cardiac events.Instead, it could be a third variable – having a stressful job – that increases both the number of alcoholic drinks a woman consumes each week and the likelihood that she will experience a cardiac event.In order words, it is not that drinking alcohol causes these cardiac events.Rather, it is having a stressful job that is causing these cardiac events and also causing the women to drink alcohol, but there is no cause-and-effect link between alcohol consumption and cardiac events.

If the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is strengthened because an alternative explanation for the observed pattern – that a third variable, having the stressful job is the cause of both drinking and cardiac events – is eliminated.In other words, when the answer is “No,” we can rule out the alternative explanation that it is a stressful job that increases the number of cardiac events in women, while drinking alcohol does not affect the number of these cardiac events.
Helpful? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether suffering a cardiac event predisposes a woman to drink more alcoholic drinks per week than she otherwise would
This information would be helpful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion arrived at by the researchers.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” then the argument is weakened because we have reason to believe that drinking alcohol might not cause cardiac events.Instead, it could be that suffering a cardiac event predisposes a woman to drink more alcoholic drinks per week than she otherwise would.In other words, we’d feel that the cause and effect are actually the reverse of what the researchers have concluded they are: after a women suffers a cardiac event, she has a tendency to consume more alcohol.

If the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is strengthened because the possibility that the cause-and-effect relationship is the reverse of what the researchers believe it to be is ruled out.

Remember that, whenever we have a cause-and-effect argument, that is, A causes B, it’s always possible that the author has misunderstood the cause and effect nature of whatever he is speaking about, and the truth of the matter is that B causes A (the cause and effect is actually reversed).
Helpful? Yes.
(D) Whether women with relatively healthy diets and lifestyles are more likely to avoid consumption of alcohol than those with less healthy diets and lifestyles
This information would be helpful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion arrived at by the researchers.

If the answer to this question is “Yes,” the argument is weakened, because if women with relatively healthy diets and lifestyles are more likely to avoid consumption of alcohol than those with less healthy diets and lifestyles, then it could be that less healthy diets and lifestyles are causing the cardiac events in the women who drink more, and their drinking more has nothing to do with the cardiac events.Rather, their drinking more is just a variable that happens to correlate with unhealthy diets and lifestyles, which are the real causes of the cardiac events.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” the argument is strengthened, because the answer serves to rule out the possibility that unhealthy diets and lifestyles rather than drinking are the causes of the higher rate of cardiac events in women who drink more.
When an argument involves cause-and-effect reasoning, ruling out an alternative explanation will strengthen the argument.
Helpful? Yes.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) Whether there was observed in the women studied a positive correlation between the age of a woman and the likelihood that she had experienced a cardiac event

A positive correlation between the age of a woman and the likelihood that she had experienced a cardiac event would indicate that older women experience a greater number of cardiac events than younger women experience.
However, this information would not be helpful in evaluating the validity of the conclusion arrived at by the researchers.

After all, the conclusion is not that drinking alcohol is theonlyfactor that causes cardiac events.It is simply that a person’s drinking alcohol increases the likelihood that that person will experience a cardiac event, and regardless of whether a person’s age is a factor that contributes to the likelihood that that person will experience a cardiac event, the conclusion could still be supported by the evidence provided.
Helpful? No.

question.text: The average human is bigger, faster, and stronger than at any time before.To see evidence of this fact, one need not look much further than sports.Swimmers and runners are faster than ever, powerlifters lift more weight than ever before, and golfers drive golf balls farther than ever.Each of the following would be important to determine in evaluating the force of the evidence presented, EXCEPT:

answers.text: Whether runners of the past ran exclusively on energy-absorbing tracks that, due to their softer consistency, were 1 to 3 percent slower on which to run than are tracks of today
Whether poolside gutters, which allow water to flow off the sides of pools, reducing the friction against which swimmers must swim, have been used only in recent years
Whether the characteristics of athletes of today are a good representation of the characteristics of the human race as a whole
Whether, in recent years, there have been improvements in golf club technology such that clubs deliver much greater performance today than at any time in the past
Whether people involved in sports today have better nutrition than people involved in sports had at any time in the past

explanation_parts.text: The argument’s conclusion is that “the average human is bigger, faster, and stronger than at any time before. ” The evidence that the author uses to support this claim is that “swimmers and runners are faster than ever, powerlifters lift more weight than ever before, and golfers drive golf balls farther than ever.”

Notice that the author assumes that athletes are a good representation of humans in general.She also assumes that there are no other reasons why the athletes of today seem bigger, stronger, and faster than they were in the past.What might we want to know in evaluating the argument?
Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful in evaluating the argument; each of these will be an incorrect answer.
Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful in evaluating the argument claim will be the correct answer.
(A) Whether runners of the past ran exclusively on energy-absorbing tracks that, due to their softer consistency, were 1 to 3 percent slower on which to run than are tracks of today

If runners of the past DID run exclusively on energy-absorbing tracks that, due to their softer consistency, were 1 to 3 percent slower on which to run than are tracks of today then the argument is weakened because we have an alternative explanation for why runners are faster today.

On the other hand, if runners of the past DID NOT run exclusively on energy-absorbing tracks that, due to their softer consistency, were 1 to 3 percent slower on which to run than are tracks of today, then the argument is stronger because we’ve ruled out an alternative explanation for why runners might actually be faster today.
Useful? Yes.
(B) Whether poolside gutters, which allow water to flow off the sides of pools, reducing the friction against which swimmers must swim, have been used only in recent years

If poolside gutters, which allow water to flow off the sides of pools, reducing the friction against which swimmers must swim, have been used only in recent years, then this fact would provide an alternative explanation for why swimmers are faster today than in the past, weakening the argument.

On the other hand, if poolside gutters, which allow water to flow off the sides of pools, reducing the friction against which swimmers must swim, have been used for many years, then the argument is stronger because we’ve ruled out an alternative explanation for why swimmers might actually be faster today.
Useful? Yes.
(C) Whether the characteristics of athletes of today are a good representation of the characteristics of the human race as a whole

If the characteristics of athletes of today are a good representation of the characteristics of the human race as a whole, then the argument is stronger because we see that one of the author’s assumptions is a fact.

On the other hand, if the characteristics of athletes of today are NOT a good representation of the characteristics of the human race as a whole, then the argument falls apart because the author’s evidence no longer supports her conclusion.
Useful? Yes.
(D) Whether, in recent years, there have been improvements in golf club technology such that clubs deliver much greater performance today than at any time in the past

If, in recent years, there have been improvements in golf club technology such that clubs deliver much greater performance today than at any time in the past, then the argument is weaker because we have an alternative explanation as to why golfers can hit the ball farther now than in the past.

On the other hand, if such improvements to golf club technology have not occurred, then the argument is stronger because we’ve ruled out an alternative explanation for why golfers can hit the ball father today than in the past.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Whether people involved in sports today have better nutrition than people involved in sports had at any time in the past

This choice is tricky because we could get the impression that, if the answer to this question is “Yes” and people involved in sports today DO have better nutrition than people involved in sports had at any time in the past, then we have additional support for the conclusion that the average human is bigger, faster, and stronger than at any time before.After all, we would expect people’s having better nutrition to result in their being bigger, faster, and stronger.

However, notice that a “Yes” answer to this question does not actually support the conclusion that the average human is bigger, faster, and stronger.Rather the fact that people involved in sports have better nutrition could explain why runners are faster than ever, powerlifters lift more weight than ever before, and golfers drive golf balls farther than ever.
Information that explains a premise does not strengthen the support provided by that premise.So, a “Yes” answer to this question does not add support for the conclusion.
Similarly, a “No” answer does not weaken the argument because, either way, it still may or may not be the case that the average human is bigger, faster, and stronger than at any time before.
So, the answer to this question doesn’t help us to evaluate the argument.
Useful? No.

question.text: 23 years after automobile makers began selling automobiles that burn leaded gasoline, violent crime rates began to increase.Then, 23 years after automobile makers stopped selling automobiles that burn leaded gasoline, violent crime rates began to decrease, and from that point in time, both use of automobiles that burn leaded gasoline and violent crime rates continued to decrease, with an approximately 23 year lag between decreases in the former and decreases in the latter.In determining whether the correlation between selling and using automobiles that burn leaded gasoline and rates of violent crime is the result of a cause-and-effect relationship, it would be most useful to establish which of the following?

answers.text: Whether, 23 years after automobile makers start selling automobiles that use leaded gasoline, the rates of some types of violent crime increased more than the rates of other types

Whether, after automobile makers stopped selling automobiles that use leaded gasoline, rates of violent crime decreased fastest in regions where weather conditions cause automobiles to wear out especially quickly
Whether automobile makers stopped selling automobiles that use leaded gasoline voluntarily or stopped selling them because such automobiles were banned
Whether makers of automobiles are expected to begin selling cars that burn leaded gasoline again in the future
Whether exposure to lead from automobile emissions ever causes illnesses so debilitating that people experiencing such illnesses would be unlikely to engage in violent behavior

explanation_parts.text: This question is structured differently from most Evaluate questions, in that the passage in this question does not include a conclusion or an outline of a plan.Rather, the conclusion whose validity we need to assess is found in the question stem.That conclusion is the following:
Conclusion: The correlation between selling and using automobiles that use leaded gasoline and rates of violent crime is the result of a cause-and-effect relationship.

Of course, since this question includes both a passage that presents factual information and a conclusion that may or may not be supported by that information, we can see that this question is not really that different from other Evaluate questions.

As usual, to correctly answer the question, we have to find the choice that highlights information that helps us to determine whether the statements in the passage support the conclusion, which is essentially that use of automobiles that burn leaded gasoline causes violent crime.
What information would help us to confirm that that cause-and-effect relationship exists?
(A) Whether, 23 years after automobile makers start selling automobiles that use leaded gasoline, the rates of some types of violent crime increased more than the rates of other types
Notice that all this choice is asking is whether the increases in rates of various types of violent crime were all the same or differed from each other.

What if the increases in rates of various types of violent crime were all the same? Would that information indicate that the use of cars that burn leaded gasoline caused the increases? No.Similarly, if the increases in the rates were different, that information would not indicate anything about the cause of the increases.

To avoid getting fooled into choosing this choice, we have to be careful not to get the impression that this choice says more than it actually says.The information highlighted by this choice is actually very limited.

YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether, after automobile makers stopped selling automobiles that use leaded gasoline, rates of violent crime decreased fastest in regions where weather conditions cause automobiles to wear out especially quickly

At first glance, this choice may appear irrelevant because what it mentions, weather causing cars to wear out, may seem unrelated to the conclusion that use of cars that burn leaded gasoline causes violent crime.
Notice, however, the following.

It is likely that, once automobile makers stopped selling cars that burn leaded gasoline, cars that burn leaded gasoline then disappearedmost quicklyin regions where weather conditions cause automobiles to wear outespecially quickly.In other words, in such regions, the supposed cause, cars that burn leaded gasoline, likely disappeared faster than it disappeared in other areas.
So, notice what happens if we answer “Yes” to the question presented by this choice.
In that case, it’s likely that both the supposed cause (use of automobiles that burn leaded gasoline), and the supposed effect, (violent crime), disappeared fastest in the same place.

So, essentially a “Yes” answer to this question would indicate that, the faster the supposed cause disappears, the faster the effect disappears.That information certainly tends to indicate that the first is indeed the cause of the second.

On the other hand, a “No” answer to this question would indicate that, in the areas where cars that burn leaded gasoline likely disappeared the fastest, the rates of violent crime did not decrease the fastest.While that information would not prove the conclusion incorrect, it would certainly serve to call the conclusion into question.
So, establishing whether the answer to the question presented by this choice is “Yes,” or “No” would certainly be helpful.
(C) Whether automobile makers stopped selling automobiles that use leaded gasoline voluntarily or stopped selling them because such automobiles were banned
Notice that the reason why automobile makers stopped selling cars that burn leaded gasoline does not affect the effects of cars that burn leaded gasoline.

So, knowing whether the answer to the question presented by this choice is that automobile makers stopped voluntarily or that automobile makers stopped because automobiles that burn leaded gasoline were banned does not help us to determine whether the use of cars that burn leaded gasoline causes violent crime.
(D) Whether makers of automobiles are expected to begin selling cars that burn leaded gasoline again in the future
What we are seeking to establish is whether there is a cause-and-effect relationship between use of cars that burn leaded gasoline and violent crime.
