Having information on whether the supposed cause, cars that burn leaded gasoline, will exist in the future does help us to determine whether that supposed cause actually does cause the effect, violent crime.

So, regardless of whether the answer to the question presented by this choice is “Yes,” or “No,” we won’t have information that we can use to establish whether there is a cause-and-effect relationship between use of cars that burn leaded gasoline and violent crime.
(E) Whether exposure to lead from automobile emissions ever causes illnesses so debilitating that people experiencing such illnesses would be unlikely to engage in violent behavior

This choice is a tempting trap.After all, we could perceive information indicating that exposure to lead from automobile emissions sometimes causes illnesses so debilitating that people experiencing such illnesses would be unlikely to engage in violent behavior as indicating that the use of cars that burn leaded gasoline prevents, rather than causes, violent crime, by making people too ill to engage in violent crime.

Notice, however, that it could be that lead from automobile emissions bothsometimesmakes people very ill and causes people to engage in violent crime.So, neither a “Yes” answer or a “No” answer to the question presented by this choice helps us to determine whether the use of automobiles that burn leaded gasoline causes violent crime.

question.text: The Jet-Thrust Corporation, a producer of jet engines, will transition from using the LZ4 laser to cut metals to be used in its engines to using the LZ3 laser for the same task, because the LZ3 laser demands significantly less electrical power per hour to operate than is demanded by the LZ4 laser.Company executives believe that, since Jet-Thrust currently runs many LZ4 lasers, by completely switching to using LZ3 lasers, the company will considerably reduce energy costs associated with engine production.In evaluating the conclusion arrived at by Jet-Thrust Corporation Executives, which of the following would be most useful to establish?

answers.text: Whether the Jet-Thrust Corporation expects to see an increase over the coming years in the number of jet engine orders it receives from airplane manufacturers
The proportion of the company’s total electricity usage that is used by the company’s LZ4 lasers.
Whether the speed at which the LZ3 laser cuts metal is not much less than the speed at which the LZ4 laser cuts metal.
Whether there is another laser that could be used to meet the company’s needs that would consume less electricity than does either the LZ3 or the LZ4.
Whether transitioning to the LZ3 lasers will require significant expenditures for purchase and installation of the LZ3 lasers and training operators in their use.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Premise: The Jet-Thrust Corporation, a producer of jet engines, will transition from using the LZ4 laser to cut metals to be used in its engines to using the LZ3 laser for the same task, because the LZ3 laser demands significantly less electrical power per hour to operate than is demanded by the LZ4 laser.

Conclusion: Company executives believe that, since Jet-Thrust currently runs many LZ4 lasers, by completely switching to using LZ3 lasers, the company will considerably reduce energy costs associated with engine production.

Notice that Jet-Thrust executives are proposing a plan – a complete switch to using LZ3 lasers.Why? To considerably reduce energy costs associated with engine production.Is this a smart plan? Remember that any time a plan is proposed, an inherent assumption is that the plan will work as expected.In other words, the executives are assuming that there will not be unforeseen circumstances that will arise and derail the plan.What might we want to consider? Might there be some malicious variables that could spoil the plan?
(A) Whether the Jet-Thrust Corporation expects to see an increase over the coming years in the number of jet engine orders it receives from airplane manufacturers

Information on whether Jet-Thrust Corporation expects to see an increase over the coming years in the number of jet engine orders it receives from airplane manufacturers is not helpful in evaluating his argument.After all, even the number of jet engine orders that the company receives stays the same, the plan could very well still work.

After all, if it is true that the LZ3 laser demands significantly less electrical power per hour to operate than is demanded by the LZ4 laser, then at any level of engine production the company will save money.In other words, if production increases then the company will save money, and if production remains constant then the company will save money.
(B) The proportion of the company’s total electricity usage that is used by the company’s LZ4 lasers.

Knowing the proportion of the company’s total electricity usage that is used by the company’s LZ4 lasers would not be helpful in evaluating the argument.Let’s focus carefully on the argument’s conclusion, which is that “…by completely switching to using LZ3 lasers, the company will considerablyreduce energy costs associated with engine production.” Notice the key detail “…associated with engine production.”

So, clearly then, this argument is not centered on company-wide electricity savings.Rather, it is focused on electricity savings from only engine production.Once we notice that key detail, we see that regardless of the proportion of the company’s total electricity usage that is used by the company’s LZ4 lasers, the plan could still work.After all, as long as the company saves energy from engine production by switching from the LZ4 to the LZ3, the argument is sound and logical.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Whether the speed at which the LZ3 laser cuts metal is not much less than the speed at which the LZ4 laser cuts metal.
In evaluating the argument, it would be useful to know whether the speed at which the LZ3 laser cuts metal is not much less than the speed at which the LZ4 laser cuts metal.

After all, imagine a scenario in which the LZ3 laser cuts metal much slower than does the LZ4 laser.In this case, it is plausible that the company would need to run the LZ3 lasers for much longer periods of time than they would have needed to run the LZ4 lasers.Of course, running the LZ3 lasers for a much longer period of time might very well create an electricity cost that exceeds what the cost would have been had the company stuck with the LZ4 lasers.In this case, the argument would be weakened.

On the other hand, if the LZ3 lasers are about as fast as, or even faster than, the LZ4 lasers, then the argument is strengthened because we are able to rule out one possible problem that might derail the plan (that because the LZ3 lasers are much slower than the LZ4 lasers, the LZ3 layers end up costing the company just as much, or more than, what the costs were with the LZ4).
(D) Whether there is another laser that could be used to meet the company’s needs that would consume less electricity than does either the LZ3 or the LZ4.

As always, we must pay close attention to the argument’s conclusion, which is that “since Jet-Thrust currently runs many LZ4 lasers, by completely switching to using LZ3 lasers, the company will considerably reduce energy costs associated with engine production.”

The evidence for this claim is that “the LZ3 laser demands significantly less electrical power per hour to operate than is demanded by the LZ4 laser.” So, notice that we have a plan that company executives think will work.To evaluate this plan, we don’t need to know whether there is a better laser that the company could use to save even more power.Rather, what we need to know is whether the plan proposed in the passage will be successful.

In general, we should be on the lookout for the “better plan / different plan” trap answer choices.In general, when we are trying to evaluate the efficacy of some plan, call it plan A, it’s not helpful to know whether there is some better plan, call it plan B, that is available to us.
(E) Whether transitioning to the LZ3 lasers will require significant expenditures for purchase and installation of the LZ3 lasers and training operators in their use.
It would not be helpful to know whether transitioning to the LZ3 lasers will require significant expenditures for purchase and installation of the LZ3 lasers and training operators in their use.

As always, we must pay close attention to the argument’s conclusion, which is that “since Jet-Thrust currently runs many LZ4 lasers, by completely switching to using LZ3 lasers, the company will considerably reduce energy costs associated with engine production.”

The executives are not making the claim that there will not be other expenses and costs that come with the switch from the LZ4 to the LZ3.Rather, they are making the claim that the company can reduce energy costs associated with engine production.

question.text: About 20 miles off the west end of Grand Bahama Island is an underwater sandbar known as Tiger Beach, named for the abundance of tiger sharks that frequent this area.Shark researchers believe that the reason for the abundance of tiger sharks in this area is that the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.As evidence, these researchers present the fact that multiple pregnant female tiger sharks have been observed in the area.In evaluating the argument, it would be useful to answer each of the following EXCEPT:

answers.text: At Tiger Beach, is there an unusual abundance of sea turtles, a favorite food of the tiger shark?
Is the proportion of all tiger sharks represented by pregnant females greater at Tiger Beach than in other areas in which tiger sharks are typically found?
Is Tiger Beach well situated to serve as a resting point between shark breeding grounds and the open ocean where sharks sometimes hunt?
Are tiger sharks attracted to the fish that scuba divers use as bait while diving in the waters at Tiger Beach?
Are tiger shark populations steadily increasing in the waters at Tiger Beach?

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: About 20 miles off the west end of Grand Bahama Island is an underwater sandbar known as Tiger Beach, named for the abundance of tiger sharks that frequent this area.
Conclusion: Shark researchers believe that the reason for the abundance of tiger sharks in this area is that the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.
Premise: As evidence, these researchers present the fact that multiple pregnant female tiger sharks have been observed in the area.

Researchers have made an observation – there are an abundance of tiger sharks that frequent the area known as Tiger Beach.More specifically, they’ve observed that multiple pregnant female tiger sharks have been observed in the area.From this observation, they conclude that the reason that there are so many female tiger sharks is that the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.

Are the researchers correct? Could there be another reason why there are multiple pregnant female tiger sharks in the area? Might there be more to the story? What else might we want to know in order to properly evaluate the argument?

Because this is an “EXCEPT question,” our job is to find four answer choices that say things that would be useful in evaluating the cause-and-effect claim made; each of these will be an incorrect answer.
Then the answer choice that does not say something that would be useful in evaluating the cause-and-effect claim will be the correct answer.
(A) At Tiger Beach, is there an unusual abundance of sea turtles, a favorite food of the tiger shark?
What this answer choice says would be useful in evaluating the argument.

Let’s say that the answer to this question is “Yes.” In this case, the argument would be weakened because we now have an alternative explanation for why there are an abundance of pregnant female tiger sharks in the area; they are here to eat turtles, one of their favorite foods.Now we are less sure that the reason the tiger sharks are at Tiger Beach is because the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is strengthened because we have ruled out an alternative explanation as to why the tiger sharks are at Tiger Beach, and by ruling out an alternative explanation, we are just a little be more sure that the author’s conclusion is correct.
Useful? Yes.
(B) Is the proportion of all tiger sharks represented by pregnant females greater at Tiger Beach than in other areas in which tiger sharks are typically found?

The argument is that shark researchers believe that the reason for the abundance of tiger sharks at Tiger Beach is that the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.

Their evidence is that “multiple pregnant female tiger sharks”have been observed in the area.A key detail to notice is the use of the word “multiple.” What does multiple mean? As used in the argument, we can’t really say for sure what it means.

Now, one thing is certain; if the researchers are correct that the abundance of tiger sharks at Tiger Beach is because the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area, then we would be reasonably justified in thinking that we would see a greater proportion of female tiger sharks there than in in other areas in which tiger sharks are typically found.In other words, if the answer to this question is, “Yes,” then the argument is stronger.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is weakened because, if there is not a greater proportion of female tiger sharks there than in in other areas, then it seems likely that the researchers are not correct in conclusion that the abundance of tiger sharks at Tiger Beach exists because the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.
Useful? Yes.
(C) Is Tiger Beach well situated to serve as a resting point between shark breeding grounds and the open ocean where sharks sometimes hunt?
What this answer choice says would be useful in evaluating the argument.

Let’s say that the answer to this question is “Yes.” In this case, the argument would be weakened because we now have an alternative explanation for why there are an abundance of pregnant female tiger sharks in the area; they are here because Tiger Beach is well situated to serve as a resting point between shark breeding grounds and the open ocean where sharks sometimes hunt.In other words, the tiger sharks are at Tiger Beach taking a siesta in between swimming from where they breed to where they hunt.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is strengthened because we have ruled out an alternative explanation as to why the tiger sharks are at Tiger Beach, and by ruling out an alternative explanation, we are just a little more sure that the author’s conclusion is correct.
Useful? Yes.
(D) Are tiger sharks attracted to the fish that scuba divers use as bait while diving in the waters at Tiger Beach?
What this answer choice says would be useful in evaluating the argument.

Let’s say that the answer to this question is “Yes.” In this case, the argument would be weakened because we now have an alternative explanation for why there are an abundance of pregnant female tiger sharks in the area; they are here because they are attracted to the fish that scuba divers use as bait while diving in the waters at Tiger Beach.In other words, the tiger sharks are at Tiger Beach for some free food.

On the other hand, if the answer to this question is “No,” then the argument is strengthened because we have ruled out an alternative explanation as to why the tiger sharks are at Tiger Beach, and by ruling out an alternative explanation, we are just a little be more sure that the author’s conclusion is correct.
Useful? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Are tiger shark populations steadily increasing in the waters at Tiger Beach?

Let’s say that the answer to this question is “Yes.” How does that fact affect the claim made in the argument? It does not affect that claim at all.That tiger shark populations steadily increasing in the waters at Tiger Beach does not mean that the reason that many tiger sharks are found there is because the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.After all, the tiger shark populations could be steadily increasing in the waters at Tiger Beach because, for example, tiger sharks are attracted to the fish that scuba divers use as bait while diving in the waters at Tiger Beach, Tiger Beach is well situated to serve as a resting point between shark breeding grounds and the open ocean where sharks sometimes hunt, there an unusual abundance of sea turtles, a favorite food of the tiger shark, or for any number of other reasons.

On the other hand, let’s say that the answer to this question is, “no.” In this case the strength of the argument is still unaffected.After all, it could very well be true that tiger shark populations are “not” steadily increasing in the waters at Tiger Beach, and, at the same time, the abundance of tiger sharks in this area is that the area is optimally suited to serve as a tiger shark gestation and birthing area.
Useful? No.

question.text: Over the past several years, the average probability that an applicant to a top 20 ranked university will be admitted must have decreased significantly.Over that time period, the total number of slots available to first-year students at top 20 ranked universities has remained about the same.Over the same time period, the number of applications received by those universities has increased significantly.In determining whether the conclusion is valid, it would be most useful to establish which of the following?

answers.text: Whether, over the past several years, the number of universities not ranked in the top 20 but comparable in quality to top 20 universities has increased
The proportion of the increase in top 20 university applications due to top 20 applicants applying to more top 20 universities
Whether the fraction of all applications to universities represented by applications to universities ranked in the top 20 has increased over the past several years
The fraction of those currently applying to top 20 ranked universities who are as qualified as the average applicant to top 20 ranked universities 20 several years ago
How many of the universities ranked in the top 20 several years ago are ranked in the top 20 currently

explanation_parts.text: The author concludes the following:
Conclusion: Over the past several years, the average probability that an applicant to a top 20 ranked university will be admitted to a top 20 ranked university must have decreased significantly.

As support for that conclusion, the author offers the facts that the number of slots at top 20 universities has remained about the same and that the number of applications received by those universities has increased significantly.
Notice that, if the number of slots has remained about the same, while the number of applications has increased significantly, then slots/applications must have decreased.
So, we can see that what the author is doing is using the fact that slots/applications has decreased to support the conclusion that the probability that an applicant will be admitted has decreased.
What information would help us to weaken or strengthen the support for the author’s conclusion?
(A) Whether, over the past several years, the number of universities not ranked in the top 20 but comparable in quality to top 20 universities has increased

In order to avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to keep the exact conclusion of the argument in mind.Notice that the conclusion of the argument is not about the probability that an applicant is admitted to a university “comparable in quality to” a top 20 ranked university.It’s about the probability that an applicant to a top 20 ranked university will be admitted to a top 20 university.
Of course, regardless of whether we answer the question presented by this choice “Yes,” or “No,” the answer does not change the number of top 20 universities out there.

So, regardless of whether we answer the question presented by this choice “Yes,” or “No,” the answer does not affect the support provided for the conclusion by the fact that the number of open slots at top 20 universities has remained about the same, while the number of applications has increased.
Thus, the answer to this question would not be helpful in determining whether the conclusion is valid.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The proportion of the increase in top 20 university applications due to top 20 applicants applying to more top 20 universities
Since this choice mentions a proportion rather than presents a question that can be answered “Yes,” or “No,” let’s use some extreme values for that proportion.
For the low extreme value, let’s use0 percent.
For the high extreme value, let’s use100 percent.
When we use 0 percent, we get the following:
0 percentof the increase in the number of top 20 university applications is attributable to an increase in the number of top 20 university applications per top 20 university applicant.
When we use 100 percent, we get the following:
100 percentof the increase in the number of top 20 university applications is attributable to an increase in the number of top 20 university applications per top 20 university applicant.
Now, notice the implications of establishing that the proportion is the extreme value 100 percent.

If 100 percent of the increase in the number of top 20 university applications is attributable to an increase in the number of top 20 university applications per top 20 university applicant, then the reason for the increase in the number of applications is not that more people are applying to top 20 universities.It’s thatthe same number of peopleare applying to more top 20 universitiesper person.

Of course, if the same number of people are applying, and, as the passage says, the total number of slots available to first year students at top 20 ranked universities has remained about the same, then we have the same number of applicants and the same number of slots as there were in the past.So, slots/applicants is the same as it was several years ago.

The probability that applicant to a top 20 university is admitted to a top 20 university must equal slots/applicants.Thus, in the 100 percent case, the probability that an applicant to a top 20 university is admitted to a top 20 university has remained the same, and the conclusion of the argument has been proved invalid.

On the other hand, if we use 0 percent, then we have a statement that rules out the possibility that the entire increase in the number of applications is attributable to an increase in number of applications per applicant, in which case, we can be confident that there are actually more applicants per slot.Of course, in that case, slots/applicants must have decreased, and thus, the probability that an applicant will gain admission must have decreased, just as the author has concluded.
So, by establishing the proportion mentioned by this choice, we can disprove or confirm the conclusion.
(C) Whether the fraction of all applications to universities represented by applications to universities ranked in the top 20 has increased over the past several years

The statistic citied in this answer choice is (number of applications sent to the top 20 universities / total number of applications sent to all universities).Whether this fraction has increased is not relevant to evaluating the argument presented in the passage.

After all, regardless of whether this fraction has increased, the number of open slots at top 20 ranked universities has remained about the same, while the number of applications has increased.Therefore, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes” or “No,” the strength of the support for the conclusion remains unaffected.
Thus, the answer to this question would not be helpful in determining whether the conclusion is valid.
(D) The fraction of those currently applying to top 20 ranked universities who are as qualified as the average applicant to top 20 ranked universities 20 several years ago

Notice that the argument is not affected by information about the qualifications of applicants to the top 20 universities.After all, regardless of what the qualifications of the applicants are, theaverageprobability that an applicant to a top 20 ranked university is admitted will remain the same, because that average probability is affected only by the number of applicants and the number of slots.

Therefore, regardless of what fraction of those currently applying to top 20 ranked universities are as qualified as the average applicant to top 20 ranked universities several years ago, the strength of the support for the conclusion remains unaffected.
Thus, establishing the value of that fraction would not be helpful in determining whether the conclusion is valid.
(E) How many of the universities ranked in the top 20 several years ago are ranked in the top 20 currently
In order to avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to keep the exact conclusion of the argument in mind.
The conclusion of the argument is about the probability that an applicant to a top 20 ranked university is admitted to a top 20 university.

That probability is affected by only two variables, the number of applicants to top 20 ranked universities and the number of slots available at top 20 ranked universities.It’s not affected by which universities are ranked in the top 20.

So, regardless of whether all of the universities ranked in the top 20 several years ago are ranked in the top 20 currently, or none of the universities ranked in the top 20 several years ago are ranked in the top 20 currently, the support for the conclusion remains the same.
Thus, establishing how many of the universities ranked in the top 20 several years ago are ranked in the top 20 currently would not be helpful in determining whether the conclusion is valid.

question.text: Many claim that the actions of humans, particularly their use of fossil fuels, which results in high levels of atmospheric CO2, are the cause of the global warming occurring currently.However, it’s difficult to draw a conclusive cause-and-effect link between increased fossil fuel use, rising CO2 levels, and increasing global temperatures.In fact, the same scientists who claim a fossil fuel-global warming link also concede that fossil records indicate that, on numerous past occasions before there were humans on Earth, the planet’s average temperatures increased to levels significantly greater than the historic mean temperature.In order to evaluate the validity of the claim that the actions of humans are the cause of global warming, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether CO2 levels existing during the times in the past when Earth’s temperature increased significantly were unusually high
Whether Earth’s current temperature is as high as the temperatures existing during past temperature spikes
What Earth’s mean temperature has been since the planet formed millions of years ago
Whether humans’ use of fossil fuels is currently increasing or decreasing
What levels of CO2 would be currently if humans were not using fossil fuels

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:
Background: Many claim that the actions of humans, particularly their use of fossil fuels, which results in high levels of atmospheric CO2, are the cause of the global warming occurring currently.

Background: The same scientists who claim a fossil fuel-global warming link also concede that fossil records indicate that, on numerous past occasions before there were humans on Earth, the planet’s average temperatures increased to levels significantly greater than the historic mean temperature.
Background: It’s difficult to draw a conclusive cause-and-effect link between increased fossil fuel use, rising CO2 levels, and increasing global temperatures.
The claim whose validity we have to evaluate is presented in the question stem:
Conclusion: The actions of humans are the cause of global warming.

We know from the passage that human use of fossil fuels results in high levels of atmospheric CO2.At the same time, the passage says also that fossil records indicate that, on numerous past occasions before there were humans on Earth, the planet’s average temperatures increased to levels significantly greater than the historic mean temperature.
What else might we want to know in order to evaluate the validity of the claim?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whether CO2 levels existing during the times in the past when Earth’s temperature increased significantly were unusually high
In evaluating the validity of the claim, it would be most useful to determine whether CO2 levels were unusually during the times in the past when Earth’s temperature increased significantly.
Why? Well, of course, the conclusion is that “The actions of humans (i.e., humans’ increasing CO2 levels) are the cause of global warming.”

Do you see the issue? The conclusion is about fossil fuel use, rising CO2 levels, and rising global temperatures, but all we know so far about the past is that there were temperature spikes.Did those temperature spikes have anything to do with CO2 levels?

Now, imagine if, during these past temperature spikes, CO2 levels were not unusually high? In this case, the validity of the claim would be called into question because there were these temperature spikes in the past, and higher than normal levels of CO2 almost certainly had nothing to do with the spikes.

On the other hand, imagine if during these past temperature spikes there were higher than normal levels of CO2 present? In this case, the argument would be stronger because we’d have some evidence that high levels of CO2 caused the spikes, and thus the high levels of CO2 caused by human use of fossil fuels may be the cause of the global warming occurring currently.
(B) Whether Earth’s current temperature is as high as the temperatures existing during past temperature spikes
We need not determine whether Earth’s current temperature is as high as the temperatures existing during past temperature spikes in order to evaluate the validity of the claim.

Regardless of whether Earth’s current temperature is as high as the temperatures existing during past temperature spikes, the conclusion, which is that the actions of humans are the cause of global warming, could still be perfectly sound.

Remember, any premise provided to us is assumed to be factually correct.Notice that the passage mentions “the global warming occurring currently.” So, we know that global warming is occurring, and the fact that global warming is occurring is sufficient for this argument to proceed.
(C) What Earth’s mean temperature has been since the planet formed millions of years ago

We need not determine what Earth’s mean temperature has been since the planet formed millions of years ago in order to evaluate this argument.Regardless of what Earth’s mean temperature has been throughout time, the fact remains that we the argument establishes that global warming does exist, and the argument establishes that the “planet’s average temperatures increased to levels significantly greater than the historic mean temperature.” We could pick any value we like for what Earth’s mean temperature has been, and the facts will still remain that we are seeing, and have seen in the past, temperatures significantly greater than that mean temperature.
(D) Whether humans’ use of fossil fuels is currently increasing or decreasing
The claim whose validity we have to evaluate is that the actions of humans (i.e., humans’ increasing CO2 levels) are the cause of global warming.
This claim is based on the facts that humans are using fossil fuels and that global warming is currently occurring.

Information on whether humans’ use of fossil fuels is currently increasing or decreasing would not affect these key facts.Regardless of whether use is increasing or decreasing, that use exists and could be causing the warming.
(E) What levels of CO2 would be currently if humans were not using fossil fuels

It’s not helpful to know what levels of CO2 would be currently if humans were not using fossil fuels because this argument is not about the effect on CO2 levels of the use of fossil fuels; it’s about whether the increases in CO2 levels caused by fossil fuel use are responsible for the global warming occurring currently.

CO2 levels could be very high, very low, or anywhere in between if humans were not using fossil fuels, and whatever they would be, that information would not help us to determine whether the CO2 levels that currently exist are responsible for the global warming occurring currently.

question.text: While pea protein has become a popular form of nutrition, many people have not been able to safely consume it because of its normally relatively high sodium levels.Pureness Foods has developed a method for manufacturing pea protein that, unlike other methods, does not involve using sodium.Therefore, one can expect that most people who, for health reasons, normally avoid high sodium foods will be able to consume Pureness Foods’ new pea protein without experiencing high sodium caused health issues.Determining which of the following would be most useful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction made?

answers.text: The percentage of people who, for health reasons, avoid consuming high sodium foods who would be interested in consuming pea protein if they could do so safely
Whether there are naturally present in all pea protein in significant quantities non-sodium substances that many people who avoid high sodium foods typically avoid for health reasons
The proportion of the sodium normally found in pea protein that is present as a result of the manufacturing process
Whether the quality of the protein in Pureness Foods’ new pea protein product is such that people consuming the product regularly will not experience nutritional deficiencies
Whether Pureness Foods uses in manufacturing pea protein substances to which some people have had allergic reactions

explanation_parts.text: The author makes the following prediction:

Conclusion: One can expect that most people who, for health reasons, normally avoid high sodium foods will be able to consume Pureness Foods’ new pea protein without experiencing high sodium caused health issues.
The support for that prediction is that Pureness Foods has developed a method for manufacturing pea protein that, unlike other methods, does not involve using sodium.
What else would we want to know in order to determine whether the prediction will prove to be correct?
(A) The percentage of people who, for health reasons, avoid consuming high sodium foods who would be interested in consuming pea protein if they could do so safely

Notice that, regardless of whether 0 percent, 50 percent, or 100 percent of people who avoid consuming high sodium foods wouldbe interested inconsuming pea protein, such information would not indicate whether they wouldbe able toconsume pea protein without experiencing health issues.
So, determining what percentage of these people would be interested in consuming pea protein would not be useful in assessing the accuracy of the prediction.
(B) Whether there are naturally present in all pea protein in significant quantities non-sodium substances that many people who avoid high sodium foods typically avoid for health reasons

To avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to notice that the prediction is specifically about “high sodium caused health issues,” whereas this choice is aboutnon-sodiumsubstances that people avoid for health reasons.

After all, pea protein could contain a variety of non-sodium substances that people avoid for health reasons, and still it could be the case that people will be able consume Pureness Foods’ pea protein without experiencing any high sodium caused health issues.
So, regardless of whether the answer to the question presented by this choice is “Yes,” or “No,” the answer has no effect on the support for the prediction.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The proportion of the sodium normally found in pea protein that is present as a result of the manufacturing process
Let’s use two extreme values here.
For the low extreme value, let’s use10 percent.
For the high extreme value, let’s use100 percent.
When we use 10 percent, we get the following:
10 percentof the sodium normally found in pea protein is present as a result of the manufacturing process.

In this case, since only 10 percent of the sodium is present as a result of the manufacturing process, even though Pureness Foods’ manufacturing process does not involve using sodium, presumably, 90 percent of the sodium that is normally present in pea will be present in Pureness Foods’ pea protein.Thus, in this case, we have reason to believe that even Pureness Foods’ pea protein will be high in sodium.So, the prediction will prove to be incorrect.
When we use 100 percent, we get the following:
100 percentof the sodium normally found in pea protein is present as a result of the manufacturing process.

In this case, since Pureness Foods’ manufacturing process does not involve using sodium, presumably, none of the sodium that is normally present in pea protein will be present in Pureness Foods’ pea protein.Thus, in this case, we have reason to believe that the prediction will prove to be correct.

So, establishing the proportion of the sodium normally found in pea protein that is present as a result of the manufacturing process is useful in assessing whether the prediction will prove to be correct.
(D) Whether the quality of the protein in Pureness Foods’ new pea protein product is such that people consuming the product regularly will not experience nutritional deficiencies

To avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to notice that the prediction is specifically about “high sodium caused health issues,” whereas this choice is about nutritional deficiencies.

After all, people consuming Pureness Foods’ pea protein could experience nutritional deficiencies, and still it could be the case that people will be able to consume Pureness Foods’ pea protein without experiencing any high sodium caused health issues.
So, regardless of whether the answer to the question presented by this choice is “Yes,” or “No,” the answer has no effect on the support for the prediction.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Whether Pureness Foods uses in manufacturing pea protein substances to which some people have had allergic reactions

To avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to notice that the prediction is specifically about “high sodium caused health issues,” whereas this choice is about substances to which some people have had allergic reactions.

After all, Pureness Foods’ pea protein could contain substances to which some people have had allergic reactions, and still it could be the case that people could consume Pureness Foods’ pea protein without experiencing any high sodium caused health issues.
So, regardless of whether the answer to the question presented by this choice is “Yes,” or “No,” the answer has no effect on the support for the prediction.

question.text: A recent study has called the existence of certain supposed benefits of meditation into question.People who self-reported experiencing high levels of stress were separated into two groups, the meditation group, in which the people were told to meditate each day for six months, and the control group, in which the people were not instructed to practice meditation.After six months, researchers determined that a full 40 percent of the people in the meditation group had followed instructions.At the same time, there was little difference in the average reported levels of stress experienced by individuals in the two groups.Clearly, the stress reducing benefits of meditation have been overstated.In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?

answers.text: Whether of all the people in the general population, typically around 40 percent choose to meditate daily to reduce levels of stress
Whether the people in the meditation group engaged in other forms of stress relieving activities, such as exercising each day
Whether more than 40 percent of people of the general population would self-report experiencing high levels of stress on a regular basis
Whether studies of other benefits attributed to meditation, such as increased self-awareness and attention span, will indicate that meditation does not generate those benefits
Whether people asked to self-report their levels of stress typically report levels of stress higher than experts in stress observing those people would determine them to be experiencing

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Background: A recent study has called the existence of certain supposed benefits of meditation into question.People who self-reported experiencing high levels of stress were separated into two groups, the meditation group, in which the people were told to meditate each day for six months, and the control group, in which the people were not instructed to practice meditation.

Premise: After six months, researchers determined that a full 40 percent of the people in the meditation group had followed instructions.At the same time, there was little difference in the average reported levels of stress experienced by individuals in the two groups.
Conclusion: Clearly, the stress reducing benefits of meditation have been overstated.

Notice that the argument’s conclusion rests upon evidence from a recent study.What the study properly conducted? Even if it were, does the author properly draw a conclusion from those study results? Might there be more to the story? What else might we want to know in order to evaluate this argument?
YOUR CHOICE(A) Whether of all the people in the general population, typically around 40 percent choose to meditate daily to reduce levels of stress

A key detail to notice is that 40 percent of the people in the meditation group had followed instructions.For example, out of, say, 100 people, 40 followed instructions.As we know, the author then makes the claim (conclusion) that the stress reducing benefits of meditation have been overstated.Notice that, of course, his reasoning rests on the assumption that the study was properly designed and executed.In other words, in drawing a conclusion based upon the results of the study, it must be assumed that that study was effectively designed.

Now, let’s say that, yes, it is true that, of all the people in the general population, typically 40 percent choose to meditate daily to reduce levels of stress.In that case, it could very well be that 40 percent of the people in the control group of people who were not instructed to meditate meditated anyway! After all, if 40 percent of people meditate, then it would be expected that 40 percent of the people in the control group would meditate.

Of course, in that case, we would have less faith in the argument’s conclusion because we’d see that the study was actually not really a study at all because, in terms of meditation, the experimental group (the people who were instructed to mediated) and the control group were likely the same.Remember, in a scientific study, we need to be able to isolate one variable of interest, in this case, meditation, but the information that 40 percent of people meditate would indicate that the study had not succeeded in isolating that variable.

On the other hand, if the answer to the question presented by this choice is that it is not the case that typically around 40 percent of people meditate daily, then that information serves to at least to some degree rule out the possibility that people in the two groups were doing the same amount of meditation, giving us a bit more confidence that the conclusion is valid.
(B) Whether the people in the meditation group engaged in other forms of stress relieving activities, such as exercising each day

We see that the argument’s conclusion, which is that “the stress reducing benefits of meditation have been overstated,” is based upon the study, in which some people meditated, while others supposedly did not.Therefore, we are not concerned with whether the people in the meditation group engaged in other forms of stress relieving activities, such as exercising each day.

For example, if the answer to this question is “yes, they did,” the author’s argument is still valid.After all, even after engaging in these other stress relieving activities, there was little difference in the average reported levels of stress experienced by individuals in the two groups.
Similarly, if the answer to the question were “no, they did not,” there was still little difference in the average reported levels of stress experienced by individuals in the two groups.
(C) Whether more than 40 percent of people of the general population would self-report experiencing high levels of stress on a regular basis

Let’s say that, yes, more than 40 percent of people of the general population would self-report experiencing high levels of stress on a regular basis.This fact would not impact the argument, which is based upon the stress levels of the participants in the study.

Similarly, what if the answer to the question were “No.” In this case, the strength of the argument would not be impacted.Again, the evidence used to support this arguments conclusion is the stress levels of the participants in the study.More than 40 percent of people in the general population may or may not self-report experiencing high levels of stress on a regular basis, and, either way, the study results would support the same conclusion.
(D) Whether studies of other benefits attributed to meditation, such as increased self-awareness and attention span, will indicate that meditation does not generate those benefits

The conclusion of this argument is that the stress reducing benefits of meditation have been overstated.The stress reducing benefits of meditation could be overstated or not regardless of whether meditation does or does not generate other benefits attributable.So, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” that answer does not help us to evaluate the argument.
(E) Whether people asked to self-report their levels of stress typically report levels of stress higher than experts in stress observing those people would determine them to be experiencing
This choice is tempting, because a “Yes” answer to this question could be perceived as indicating that the study results are suspect because people generally exaggerate their stress levels.

Notice, however, that the conclusion is based on the fact that the average levels of stress reported the participants were around the same.Of course, even if they all reported inflated stress levels, the fact that the averages are the same would still support the conclusion.So, regardless of whether the answer to this question is “Yes,” or “No,” the answer does not affect the strength of the argument.

question.text: Oil Company Representative: Recently, geologists drilled deep into Antarctic ice layers to analyze Earth’s past atmospheric temperatures and CO2 levels.Because CO2 gas becomes trapped in ice as liquid water freezes, high concentrations of atmospheric CO2 result in high concentrations of CO2 in ice formed when concentrations of atmospheric CO2 are high.Also, the hotter Earth’s atmosphere is when ice is formed, the greater the concentration of the isotope oxygen-18 in that ice.Since the geologists found that peak concentrations of CO2 in Antarctic ice were situated just above rather than along with peak concentrations of oxygen-18, high temperatures must have preceded, rather than been caused by, high concentrations of atmospheric CO2.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the oil company representative’s argument?

answers.text: Analysis of the ice indicated that, at all times in Earth’s past, around when the temperature of Earth’s atmosphere was the highest, so too was the atmospheric level of CO2.
Higher temperatures cause CO2 trapped in ice to become less dense, allowing it to move closer to the ice’s surface, but have no material effect on the density of oxygen-18.
The levels of CO2 and oxygen-18 in the ice layers indicated that low levels of atmospheric CO2 didn’t always come directly before low atmospheric temperatures.
Ice sheets in Antarctica, being considerably older than most other ice sheets on Earth, contain amounts of CO2 and oxygen-18 considerably greater than those contained in most other ice sheets.
The atmospheric levels of both CO2 and oxygen-18 have undergone significant fluctuations over the past five million years.

explanation_parts.text: We are told that CO2 gas becomes trapped in ice as liquid water freezes and that high concentrations of atmospheric CO2 result in high concentrations of CO2 in ice formed when concentrations of atmospheric CO2 are high.
We also know that, the hotter Earth’s atmosphere is when ice is formed, the greater the concentration of the isotope oxygen-18 in that ice.

The geologists found that peak concentrations of CO2 in Antarctic ice were situated just above rather than along with peak concentrations of oxygen-18.From that information, the representative concluded that high temperatures must have preceded, rather than been caused by, high concentrations of atmospheric CO2.
Is his reasoning sound? What information would point to a flaw in his argument?
(A) Analysis of the ice indicated that, at all times in Earth’s past, around when the temperature of Earth’s atmosphere was the highest, so too was the atmospheric level of CO2.
The argument says that peak CO2 follows high temperatures.It does not change anything to add that, at all times when temperatures were high, there was peak CO2.
What this answer choice says is in line with the author’s argument.Therefore, what this argument says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Higher temperatures cause CO2 trapped in ice to become less dense, allowing it to move closer to the ice’s surface, but have no material effect on the density of oxygen-18.

We know that times in which there were high concentrations of atmospheric CO2 result in high concentrations of CO2 in the ice that formed during those times.In addition, we know that the greater the concentration of the isotope oxygen-18 in a layer of ice, the hotter Earth’s atmosphere was when that ice was formed.
Now, the following point is crucial for getting this question correct:

“Since the geologists found that peak concentrations of CO2 in icewere situated just above rather than along withpeak concentrations of oxygen-18, high temperatures must have preceded rather than been caused by high concentrations of atmospheric CO2.”

Notice that the author assumes that the relative locations in the ice of peak concentrations of CO2 and peak concentrations of the isotope oxygen-18 are actually a valid indication of the relationship between atmospheric CO2 levels and temperature changes over time.In other words, he assumes that, since peak concentrations of CO2 in icewere situated just above rather than along withpeak concentrations of oxygen-18, high temperatures must have preceded rather than been caused by high concentrations of atmospheric CO2.

However, notice that this answer choices casts significant doubt on that assumption.After all, if higher atmospheric temperatures cause CO2 trapped in ice to become less dense, allowing it to move closer to the surface of that ice but these higher atmospheric temperatures have no material effect on the density of oxygen-18, then the layers in the ice of CO2 and oxygen-18 do not show the relationship claimed by the oil company representative.

Think about it – if the CO2 becomes less dense and moves closer to the surface of the ice, but the oxygen-18 does not move, then the relative positions of the CO2 layers compared to the oxygen-18 layers could indicate nothing.
(C) The levels of CO2 and oxygen-18 in the ice layers indicated that low levels of atmospheric CO2 didn’t always come directly before low atmospheric temperatures.

Notice that the argument is one about high levels of CO2 and increases in Earth’s atmospheric temperature.The oil company representative never makes the claim that levels of CO2 and oxygen-18 in the ice layers revealed that low levels of atmospheric CO2 always come directly before low atmospheric temperatures.
Therefore, what this choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.
(D) Ice sheets in Antarctica, being considerably older than most other ice sheets on Earth, contain amounts of CO2 and oxygen-18 considerably greater than those contained in most other ice sheets.

If we turn back to the argument, we see that it does not rest upon the concentrations of CO2 or oxygen-18 being greater or less in the Antarctic ice sheets than those of CO2 or oxygen-18 in other ice sheets, nor does the argument rest on the age of the Antarctic ice sheets being older or younger than other ice sheets.
Therefore, what this choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.
(E) The atmospheric levels of both CO2 and oxygen-18 have undergone significant fluctuations over the past five million years.
What this answer choice says is in line with the argument.After all, the argument discusses variations in CO2 levels and atmospheric temperatures.
Therefore, what this choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.

question.text: Neurologist: For some time, researchers have recommended that people experiencing symptoms of early dementia engage regularly in social interactions in order to reduce these symptoms.However, a recent study shows that people with such symptoms who exercised for at least 30 minutes on at least five days a week experienced symptom reduction equal to that experienced by people who regularly engage in social interactions.Therefore, I feel that any people experiencing symptoms of early dementia who exercise regularly would not experience any additional symptom reduction by also regularly engaging in social interactions.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the neurologist’s argument?

answers.text: Most people who are able to exercise for at least 30 minutes a day on at least five days a week have only the mildest of dementia symptoms.
Patients with dementia symptoms often forget conversations about treatment options that they have with their doctors shortly after those conversations occur.
The risk of injury associated with exercising is much higher than the risk of injury associated with engaging in social interactions.
In the recent study cited, the primary forms of exercise in which the research subjects engaged were group sports such as soccer, basketball, and tennis.
Dementia can be caused by heavy metals in the nervous system, levels of which will be permanently reduced only via dietary changes and are not significantly affected by exercise.

explanation_parts.text: We see that researchers have recommended that people experiencing symptoms of early dementia engage regularly in social interactions in order to reduce these symptoms.But now a study shows that people with such symptoms who exercised experienced symptom reduction equal to that experienced by people who regularly engage in social interactions.From that study, the neurologist claims that any people experiencing symptoms of early dementia who exercise regularly would not experience any additional symptom reduction by also regularly engaging in social interactions.

On face value, the argument seems sound.However, what might the neurologist have missed? Is there more to the story? What might be true about one or both of the studies that makes his conclusion suspect?
(A) Most people who are able to exercise for at least 30 minutes a day on at least five days a week have only the mildest of dementia symptoms.

As always, we must pay close attention to the argument’s conclusion, which is about the effects of exercise seen in those who do engage in exercise.Since the conclusion is about people who do exercise, the fact that only some people can engage in exercise does not change the effectiveness of exercise.
(B) Patients with dementia symptoms often forget conversations about treatment options that they have with their doctors shortly after those conversations occur.

The neurologist is saying that, if people WERE to exercise, this is what the situation would be.He is not saying that doctors can get people with dementia to exercise or that there are no issues or drawbacks to the approach.
So, this choice does not affect or point to any flaw in the argument.
(C) The risk of injury associated with exercising is much higher than the risk of injury associated with engaging in social interactions.
The conclusion is about the effects of exercise and social interaction on dementia symptoms.It is not about the overall positives and negatives of exercise and social interaction.

So, this choice simply brings up another fact about exercise and social interaction that does not affect the support for the conclusion about their effects on dementia symptoms and, thus, has no effect on the argument.

(D)Correct.The argument establishes that researchers have recommended that people engage regularly in social interactions to reduce symptoms of early dementia. The treatment variable here is regular social interaction.

Then, the author claims that a recent study shows that people with early dementia symptoms who exercised for a certain amount of time experienced symptom reduction equal to that experienced by people who regularly engaged in social interactions.The treatment variable here isbelieved to beexercise.From this study, the author concludes that people with early dementia symptoms who exercise won’t see any benefit from also engaging in social interactions.

However, if the primary forms of exercise in the study were group sports such as soccer, basketball, and tennis, then we have reason to think there is a flaw in the argument: the people in the study got exerciseandsocial interaction!

Because exercise and social interaction are both at play, we cannot determine which variable was responsible for the symptom reduction.Was it the exercise, the social interaction, or the combination of both? From the conclusion, we know that the author thinks that the exercise is responsible, but we now have reason to wonder whether it was actually the social interaction or the combination of exercise and interaction.
Since the evidence the author cites might not actually support the conclusion, this choice highlights a flaw in the argument.
(E) Dementia can be caused by heavy metals in the nervous system, levels of which will be permanently reduced only via dietary changes and are not significantly affected by exercise.
What this answer choice says indicates that exercise will not solve dementia caused by heavy metals.
However, the conclusion is not that exercise solves all problems associated with dementia.It is about whether someone exercising would benefit from social interaction.

Notice also, that what this choice says about “only via dietary changes” applies to social interaction as well.If levels of heavy metals will be permanently reduced only via dietary changes, then they won’t be reduced via social interaction any more than they will be reduce via exercise.
So, what this choice says has no effect on the support for the conclusion.

question.text: Six years ago, Fresh Mango Farms, a company that grows and sells mangos, went through the process of making its farm one of the few certified organic mango farms in the country of Mangovia.Since then, although the farm has seen its revenues increase because of the relatively high prices commanded in the marketplace by organic mangos, the farm has not been profitable; it has broken even year after year.Clearly, at least from a financial perspective, it has been the wrong move for the company to spend time and money to make and keep its farm organic certified.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the argument above?

answers.text: Mangos from organic certified farms will for the foreseeable future continue to sell for higher prices than will mangos from farms that are not organic certified.
In the last six years, all mango farms in Mangovia that are not organic certified have reported steep financial losses.
In the years to come, Fresh Mango Farms plans to increase the number of organic certified mango farms that it owns and operates.
Many consumers agree that Fresh Mango Farms’ mangos are of significantly higher quality than mangos grown by other farms and, therefore, are willing to pay a premium for its mangos.
Over the past six years, the few mango farms in Mangovia that are organic certified have seen their market share of mango sales in the country increase.

explanation_parts.text: Six years ago, Fresh Mango Farms made its farm one of the few certified organic mango farms in the country of Mangovia.Since then, although the farm has seen its revenues increase because of the relatively high prices commanded in the marketplace by organic mangos, the farm has not been profitable; it has broken even year after year.
Given these facts, the author concludes that, at least from a financial perspective, it has been the wrong move for the company to spend time and money to make and keep its farm organic certified.
Might the author have missed something? Could there be more to the story? Might the move to get organic certified have been a good one?
(A) Mangos from organic certified farms will for the foreseeable future continue to sell for higher prices than will mangos from farms that are not organic certified.

The fact that mangos from organic certified farms will for the foreseeable future continue to sell for higher prices than will mangos from farms that are not organic certified does not change the fact that Fresh Mango Farms has not been profitable; we are told that it has broken even year after year.In other words, for the past six years, Fresh Mango Farms has not been able to turn a profit, even with the relatively high prices commanded in the marketplace by organic mangos.Therefore, we would not expect what this answer choice says to affect the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In the last six years, all mango farms in Mangovia that are not organic certified have reported steep financial losses.
What this answer choice says tends to indicate that, contrary to the conclusion, Fresh Mango Farms has in fact made a good move in becoming organic certified.

After all, if in the last six years, all mango farms in Mangovia that are not organic certified have reported steep financial losses, but Fresh Mango Farms has broken even, then Fresh Mango Farms is doing better financially than all non organic certified mango farms in Mangovia.This tends to indicate that, in fact, the move to become an organic farm was a good one.

Notice that what this answer choice says indicates that the argument is flawed in that the author failed to consider that the fact that Fresh Mango Farms has not been profitable does not necessarily mean that making and keeping the farm organic certified was the wrong move.
(C) In the years to come, Fresh Mango Farms plans to increase the number of organic certified mango farms that it owns and operates.

Fresh Mango Farms’ becoming organically certified has not made it profitable to date, even after six years.So, what would make us think that opening many different farms would change the effect that becoming organically certified has had on this farm.Would the goal be to break even on many more farms too? In other words, if the company has not been able to turn a profit on its first farm, what makes us think that scaling up will make becoming organic certified not “the wrong move”?

Now, if you choose this answer choice as correct, you likely made the unwarranted assumption, that as the company increased the number of farms that it operates, making and keeping the farm organic certified will somehow pay off.However, we don’t have any evidence to indicate that that assumption is correct.In answering Critical Reasoning questions, we have to careful not to create unsubstantiated stories.
(D) Many consumers agree that Fresh Mango Farms’ mangos are of significantly higher quality than mangos grown by other farms and, therefore, are willing to pay a premium for its mangos.

The fact that many consumers agree that Fresh Mango Farms’ mangos are of significantly higher quality than mangos grown by other farms and, therefore, are willing to pay a premium for its mangos has not helped Fresh Mango farms operate profitably during the prior six years.So, what this answer choice says would certainly not point to a flaw in the argument.

To say that differently, many consumers CAN agree that Fresh Mango Farms’ mangos are of significantly higher quality than mangos grown by other farms and, therefore, ARE willing to pay a premium for its mangos, and at the same time, we know that Fresh Mango Farms has not been profitable.
So, what this answer choice says does not change the facts provided by the passage and does not point to any flaw in the argument.
(E) Over the past six years, the few mango farms in Mangovia that are organic certified have seen their market share of mango sales in the country increase.

The fact that over the past six years, the few mango farms in Mangovia that are organic certified have seen their market share of mango sales in the country increase, has not helped Fresh Mango farms operate profitably during the prior six years.So, what this answer choice says would certainly not point to a flaw in the argument.In other words, what this answer choice says does not change the facts provided to us in the passage.

question.text: To better understand the relationship between the consumption of fatty fish and the incidence of heart disease, researchers studied the people living in a small, long-isolated village in northern Alaska.The researchers found that almost all of the villagers had consumed fatty fish at least twice a day from a very young age and that the villagers had some of the lowest rates of heart disease on the planet.Therefore, the researchers concluded that consuming fatty fish twice a day is an effective way for people to reduce their risk of heart disease.The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on which of the following grounds?

answers.text: It fails to consider that not all of the villagers consumed fatty fish twice a day from a young age.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that the same fish that were consumed in the remote village could be found in other parts of the world.
It overlooks the possibility that people consuming fatty fish at least twice per day may develop non-heart health problems because of heavy metals found in the fish.
It neglects to consider that the people in the village might share characteristics, other than regular fatty fish consumption, that are very different from those of most other people.
It fails to establish the mechanism via which regular fatty fish consumption would result in protection against heart disease.

explanation_parts.text: We must be skeptical about any cause-and-effect claim made by an author, because these claims are often not well-supported by the evidence presented.

Notice that, in this argument, the researchers make the claim that consuming fatty fish twice a day is an effective way for people to reduce their risk of heart disease.In other words, the researchers claim that consuming fatty fish twice a day will cause a reduction in people’s risk of developing heart disease.

What’s the evidence supporting this cause-and-effect claim? Notice that the evidence is simply the observation that “almost all of the villagers had consumed fatty fish at least twice a day from a very young age and that the villagers had some of the lowest rates of heart disease on the planet.” It’s important to remember that such an observation is weak evidence to support a cause-and-effect claim.The fact that two variables occur together is not strong evidence that there exists a cause-and-effect relationship between those variables.

Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for the observation she seeks to explain.Often, we’ll see that this assumption is a bad one.

Is there more to the story? Is there something they may have missed? Is their analysis complete? Could there be an alternative explanation for why the villagers had some of the lowest rates of heart disease on the planet?
(A) It fails to consider that not all of the villagers consumed fatty fish twice a day from a young age.

Seeing that this answer choice is incorrect centers on seeing a key detail in the passage.Notice that we are told that “The researchers found thatalmost allof the villagers had consumed fatty fish at least twice a day from a very young age and that the villagers had some of the lowest rates of heart disease on the planet.” Notice that the author says flat out that “not all” of the villagers had consumed fatty fish at least twice a day.Furthermore, it need not be the case that all of the villagers consumed fatty fish twice a day from a young age.The fact that “almost all of them have” is more than sufficient data upon which base the author’s argument.
(B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that the same fish that were consumed in the remote village could be found in other parts of the world.

The researchers concluded that consuming fatty fish twice a day is an effective way for people to reduce their risk of heart disease.Notice that these researchers are not saying that consuming fatty fish twice a day is feasible for people in general.In fact, she is not even making the claim that a single person would or could follow her advice.She simply says that if people “were” to consume fatty fish twice a day, such consumption would be an effective way for people to reduce their risk of heart disease.As always, we must pay close attention to the conclusion of the argument.
(C) It overlooks the possibility that people consuming fatty fish at least twice per day may develop non-heart health problems because of heavy metals found in the fish.

The researchers are not claiming that eating these fatty fish twice a day will make people healthier overall or longer lived or anything else other than that consuming fatty fish twice a day is an effective way for people to reduce their risk of heart disease.So, even if, in consuming these fatty fish twice a day, people would become sick from heavy metal toxicity because of the heavy metals found in fish, the argument could still work.
YOUR CHOICE(D) It neglects to consider that the people in the village might share characteristics, other than regular fatty fish consumption, that are very different from those of most other people.

Notice that the researchers claim that consuming fatty fish twice a day is an effective way for people to reduce their risk of heart disease.Why do they claim this? Of course, their evidence is the study, which found that almost all the villagers had consumed fatty fish at least twice a day from a very young age and that the villagers had some of the lowest rates of heart disease on the planet.

At the same time, what is noteworthy about the village in which the research was conducted? Notice that the village was a “small, long-isolated village in northern Alaska.” In a small, long-isolated village, we would not expect to see large variations in the genetics of the people there; we’d expected the people to be very similar to each other.Because all the people would likely demonstrate stark similarities, it could be true that these people share some other trait, something genetic perhaps, that conveys to them protection against heart disease.Or, another consideration of a small, long-isolated village is that most, if not all, the people could share a similar lifestyle that itself conveys protection again heart disease.For example, imagine a situation in which the each of the villagers chopped wood for two hours each day or had to hike up a snowy hill ten times each day to procure water.Wouldn’t these cardiovascular activities help keep the villager’s heart’s healthy? They very likely would.

So, what this answer choice highlights is that there might be an alternative explanation that would explain the lower rates of heart disease; it shows that the researches may have incompletely analyzed the situation.
(E) It fails to establish the mechanism via which regular fatty fish consumption would result in protection against heart disease.

The conclusion is based on the results found by the researchers, which are sufficient to support the argument’s conclusion.We don’t need to have the exact mechanism for those results for them to adequately support the argument’s conclusion.

For instance, to use a real-life example, we are still not completely sure of the mechanism by which the force of gravity works.However, not a single person reading this would contest the fact that if a person were to drop a rock that it would fall to Earth.
We need not know the exact mechanism by which a process works to be certain that the process does in fact work.

question.text: Cheese Marketer: The citizens of France eat a diet exceptionally high in saturated fats, which many doctors and nutritionists claim to be a significant factor in the manifestation of heart disease, yet the French have one the lowest rates of heart disease seen in any group of people in the world.Therefore, those who claim that eating a diet high in saturated fats is unhealthy for the heart are clearly mistaken; it is clear that consuming saturated fats in fact prevents heart disease.Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning in the argument above?

answers.text: It uses as evidence rates of heart disease in France and other countries, rather than total numbers of people who experience heart disease in France and other countries.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that the only way to prevent heart disease is to follow a diet high in saturated fats.
It bases its conclusion on a sample that may be unrepresentative of the general population about whom the claim is drawn.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that the French choose what to eat on the basis of whether eating it prevents heart disease.
It overlooks the possibility that the observed correlation is not the result of the cause-and-effect relationship presented.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the argument:

Premise: The citizens of France eat a diet exceptionally high in saturated fats, which many doctors and nutritionists claim to be a significant factor in the manifestation of heart disease, yet the French have one the lowest rates of heart disease seen in any group of people in the world.

Conclusion: Therefore, those who claim that eating a diet high in saturated fats is unhealthy for the heart are clearly mistaken; it is clear that consuming saturated fats in fact prevents heart disease.

Notice that we have an argument that involves cause-and-effect reasoning.Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he makes the assumption that his cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there is not an alternative explanation for the observation she seeks to explain.
The cheese marketer makes the claim that consuming saturated fats in fact prevents heart disease.What is his evidence for this claim?

His evidence is the observation that “The citizens of France eat a diet exceptionally high in saturated fats, which many doctors and nutritionists claim to be a significant factor in the manifestation of heart disease, yet the French have one the lowest rates of heart disease seen in any group of people in the world.”

Of course, we must remember that the fact that someone makes an observation that two variables occur together does not allow him to conclude on that basis alone that a there is cause-and-effect relationship between those two variables.
Could the cheese marketer have misunderstood the real nature of what he has observed? Might there be more to the story? Perhaps there is an alternative explanation for the observed pattern.
(A) It uses as evidence rates of heart disease in France and other countries, rather than total numbers of people who experience heart disease in France and other countries.
It’s completely logical for the cheese marker to cite as evidence rates of heart disease in France and other countries.

The rate of heart disease in a country is a statistic that is essentially the same as the percentage of people in the country who experience heart disease.So, of course, by using rates of heart disease, the cheese marketer can make an apples-to-apples comparison of rates of heart disease in different countries.

Imagine if the cheese marketer had instead used total numbers.A larger country might have a much higher total number of people who experience heart disease even if that country has a much lower rate of heart disease, with the result that the statistics presented would be distorted by the sizes of the countries.
So, using rates, rather than total numbers, is a strength of the argument, not a flaw.

If you chose this answer as correct, you likely, without much thought, overplayed the “numbers versus percentages” concept.Remember that, when answering Critical Reasoning questions, we must resist the urge to engage in kneejerk reactions; we must take time and care in seeking the correct answer.
(B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that the only way to prevent heart disease is to follow a diet high in saturated fats.

The cheese marketer is not assuming that the only way to prevent heart disease is to follow a diet high in saturated fats.He is simply making the claim that consuming saturated fats prevents heart disease.The argument leaves open the possibility that there are other ways to prevent heart disease.
(C) It bases its conclusion on a sample that may be unrepresentative of the general population about whom the claim is drawn.

The people living in France are certainly not an unrepresentative sample of the general population about whom the claim is drawn.After all, millions of people from diverse backgrounds live in France.In other words, the sample size is large and has the same qualities that we would expect to see of people around the globe.If you chose this answer as correct, you likely overplayed the “flawed sample” concept.Do we often see flawed samples in the arguments presented to us in Critical Reasoning questions? Yes, for sure we do.Will all samples be flawed? No, they will not.
(D) It assumes without sufficient warrant that the French choose what to eat on the basis of whether eating it prevents heart disease.

In order to avoid choosing this choice as correct, we have to keep the exact conclusion of the argument in mind.The conclusion is simply that a cause, eating a diet high in saturated fats, has an effect, preventing heart disease.
