Notice that that cause could have that effect regardless of whether the people who choose to eat a diet high in saturated fats choose to do so because it prevents heart disease.In other words, why people choose to eat such a diet has no effect on the outcome of eating such a diet.
So, in arriving at his cause-effect conclusion the author does not have to assume anything about why the French choose to eat what they eat.
YOUR CHOICE(E) It overlooks the possibility that the observed correlation is not the result of the cause-and-effect relationship presented.

The cheese marketer observes that the citizens of France eat a diet exceptionally high in saturated fats, yet the French have one the lowest rates of heart disease seen in any group of people in the world.Does this fact alone allow us to conclude that eating saturated fats prevents heart disease? Of course not.The fact that two variables occur together (eating large amounts of saturated fat and the lowest rates of heart disease) does not on that basis alone allow us to conclude that one variable is the cause of the other.After all, there could be many other reasons why the French have such low rates of heart disease.Perhaps they walk more than most other people.Maybe the French have less stress than most people.Or, perhaps the genetics of people in France convey some natural protection against heart disease.And, yes, by the way, they also eat a diet high in saturated fat, but this diet has no cause-and-effect relationship with the low rates of heart disease.
So, the argument is flawed in that it overlooks the possibility that the observed correlation is not the result of a cause-and-effect relationship.

question.text: Pharmaceutical Company CEO: Although opponents of administering vaccines to children claim that vaccines are a primary cause of neurological illnesses, to date, there is no research that provides evidence of such a connection.Therefore, we can be sure that vaccine use in children is not a cause of neurological illness.Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning in the argument above?

answers.text: It assumes without warrant that there are not multiple different causes of neurological illness.
It fails to provide plausible causes of neurological illnesses other than administering vaccines to children.
It assumes without warrant that opponents of administering vaccines to children have taken that position without having any reason for doing so.
It relies on an arbitrary distinction between finding and not finding evidence of a connection.
It presumes that failure to find evidence of a connection is sufficient for concluding with certainty that that connection does not exist.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Opponents of administering vaccines to children claim that vaccines are a primary cause of neurological illnesses.
Premise: To date, there is no research that provides evidence of such a connection.
Conclusion: Therefore, we can be sure that vaccine use in children is not a cause of neurological illness.

Notice that the CEO reasons that the fact that the evidence has not been found allows us to conclude that that evidence does not exist.Such reasoning will always be flawed because as we say, “Failure to find evidence does not mean that the evidence does not exist.”

In other words, the fact that we have not yet found evidence does not allow us to conclude that that evidence does not actually exist.To say that differently, there is a major difference between “not having any evidence that something dangerous,” and “having evidence that something is safe.”

(A)Incorrect.The CEO is not making that claim that there are not multiple different causes of neurological illness.His argument is only that vaccine use in children is not a cause of neurological illness.His argument makes no claim about other potential causes of neurological illness.
(B) It fails to provide plausible causes of neurological illnesses other than administering vaccines to children.

In order to arrive at the conclusion that vaccine use in children is not a cause of neurological illness, the CEO does not have to provide another cause of neurological illness.After all, we could be quite confident that one factor does not cause neurological illness without knowing what does cause neurological illness.
For example, even without knowing what causes neurological illness, we could be rather confident that smiling or drinking pure water does not cause neurological illness.
So, the fact that the CEO has not provide plausible causes of neurological illnesses other than administering vaccines to children is not a flaw in his reasoning.
(C) It assumes without warrant that opponents of administering vaccines to children have taken that position without having any reason for doing so.

Notice that the CEO claims that there is, to date, no research that provides evidence of such a connection between vaccine use in children and the onset of neurological illness.He does not claim, or assume, that opponents of administering vaccines to children have taken that position without having any reason for doing so.In fact, there could be many reasons that these opponents believe in a connection between vaccine use in children and the onset of neurological illness, and at the same time, the CEOs evidence could still support his conclusion.
(D) It relies on an arbitrary distinction between finding and not finding evidence of a connection.

The CEO makes no arbitrary distinction between finding and not finding evidence of a connection.He is clear that “To date, there is no research that provides evidence of such a connection.” There is nothing arbitrary whatsoever about this statement.
YOUR CHOICE(E) It presumes that failure to find evidence of a connection is sufficient for concluding with certainty that that connection does not exist.

The CEO absolutely presumes that failure to find evidence of a connection is sufficient for concluding with certainty that that connection does not exist.Remember, the fact that the evidence has not yet been found does not allow us to conclude that the evidence does not exist.For all we know, there is a mountain of evidence that links vaccine use in children and neurological illnesses, yet that evidence has not yet been discovered.The fact that that link has yet to be discovered does not allow us to conclude that the link does not exist.

question.text: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that influences almost all of the cells in a human brain, including those related to mood and emotions.Levels of serotonin in the brain cannot be readily measured.However, levels of serotonin in the blood can be measured relatively easily.Since researchers theorize that relatively low levels of serotonin in the brain cause depression, measuring the levels of serotonin in their blood of people who believe they are suffering from depression would be an effective method for determining whether a person is suffering with depression.The argument above is most vulnerable to which of the following objections?

answers.text: It does not mention that levels of serotonin can be measured in organs of the body besides the brain.
It fails to mention symptoms that are reliable markers of depression observable without the use of blood tests.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that serotonin levels in the blood are related to those in the brain.
It neglects to consider whether levels of neurotransmitters other than serotonin can be measured via blood tests.
It fails to clearly define a level of serotonin in the blood that constitutes a relatively low level.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that influences almost all of the cells in a human brain, including those related to mood and emotions.
Premise: Levels of serotonin in the brain cannot be readily measured.However, levels of serotonin in the blood can be measured relatively easily.

Conclusion: Since researchers theorize that relatively low levels of serotonin in the brain cause depression, measuring the levels of serotonin in their blood of people who believe they are suffering from depression would be an effective method for determining whether a person is suffering with depression.

Notice that, in making this argument, the author assumes that there is a link between serotonin levels in the blood and serotonin levels in the brain.While it might seem reasonable to assume that the two will be linked in some way, it is not necessarily the case that serotonin levels in the blood can be used to accurately assess serotonin levels in the brain.

It might be the case that a low blood level of serotonin indicates a low brain level of the neurotransmitter, but it could also be true that a high blood level correlates with a low brain level.It is also possible that there is a more complex relationship, or even no reliable connection between blood and brain levels at all.Therefore, the strongest objection to the argument will highlight the lack of certainty about how blood and brain serotonin levels are linked.

(A)Incorrect.The argument claims only that people who believe they are suffering from depression should have their blood serotonin measured, as an indicator of brain serotonin levels.Whether serotonin in other organs of the body can be measured or not has no impact on this claim.
(B) It fails to mention symptoms that are reliable markers of depression observable without the use of blood tests.

The conclusion is concerned only with whether a measurement of serotonin in the blood can indicate levels in the brain.There may be other indicators of depression besides serotonin levels, but those are outside the scope of this particular serotonin/depression argument, and therefore do not affect this particular line of reasoning.

In general, we need to be careful of the “different plan / better plan” trap answer.Such a trap answer temps us into thinking that the fact that a different plan or approach exists somehow weakens the case for the plan presented in the passage.However, in general, that there is a different plan available (or a better plan available) does not by itself weaken an argument describing a particular plan.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It assumes without sufficient warrant that serotonin levels in the blood are related to those in the brain.

The argument assumes that serotonin levels in the blood are accurate indicators of serotonin levels in the brain, but it is possible that this assumption is incorrect.In the absence of any explanation of how the two levels may be linked, any assumption of linkage is speculative.While the assumption may prove to be true, it is vulnerable.

Notice that the author does not provides us with any evidence supporting the idea that the levels are in fact linked; she is merely assuming that there is a link.Therefore, it is possible that the assumption is not valid, and that blood serotonin levels cannot be accurately used to indicate brain serotonin levels.
Thus, the argument is vulnerable to the objection that it does what this choice says it does.
(D) It neglects to consider whether levels of neurotransmitters other than serotonin can be measured via blood tests.

The purpose of the argument is not to broadly advocate for blood testing as a means of determining levels of neurotransmitters.Rather, it is to provide a rationale for using blood testing to measure levels of serotonin specifically.Therefore, while the question of whether blood testing can be used to determine the levels of other neurotransmitters may be pertinent to medical practice in general, considering that question is not necessary for making this argument.
(E) It fails to clearly define a level of serotonin in the blood that constitutes a relatively low level.
This choice could be tempting because what it says is basically true.The argument does not clearly define a level of serotonin in the blood that constitutes a relatively low level.

However, the argument’s lacking a definition of that level is not a flaw in the argument.The argument is making a general case for using a particular method.Supporting the conclusion that the method would be effective does not require defining particulars, such as what constitutes a relatively low level of serotonin in the blood, involved in applying that method.
In answering Flaw in the Argument question, we must be aware of trap answers that make true statements about what an argument does or does not do, yet at the same time are not flaws in the argument.

question.text: In many metropolitan areas, there exist areas that health officials refer to as food deserts, which are so termed because such areas have within them no stores offering large selections of healthy food.Therefore, to foster the presence of stores that sell healthy food in these areas, officials are offering chains that operate such stores tax breaks associated with operating stores in areas currently considered food deserts, believing that, with lower expenses, such stores will be able to operate profitably in those areas.The reasoning that serves as the basis for the proposed strategy is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

answers.text: takes the offering of tax breaks to be a guarantee that healthy food stores will open in areas currently considered food deserts.
fails to consider that some people who live in food deserts are willing to travel to other areas to buy healthy food.
fails to consider the possibility that the current lack of stores selling healthy food in food deserts is due to lack of demand for such food there.
assumes without sufficient warrant that all officials utilize the same operational definition in determining what constitutes healthy food.
neglects to consider how many stores selling healthy food, on average, operate in metropolitan regions not considered food deserts.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: In many metropolitan areas, there exist food deserts, which are so termed because such areas have within them no stores offering large selections of healthy food.

Premise: To foster the presence of stores that sell healthy food in these areas, officials are offering chains that operate such stores tax breaks associated with operating stores in areas currently considered food deserts
Conclusion: With lower expenses, such stores offering large selections of healthy food will be able to operate profitably in those areas.
The speaker concludes that “With lower expenses, such stores offering large selections of healthy food will be able to operate profitably in those areas.”
Whenever someone presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work.In other words, he assumes that there are not malicious variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan.

What malicious variables might the officials have missed? Are lower expenses really going to be sufficient for these stores to operate profitably in the food desserts? Are expenses the only key variable in this profit equation? How might the officials’ reasoning be flawed?
(A) takes the offering of tax breaks to be a guarantee that healthy food stores will open in areas currently considered food deserts.

To not choose this answer as correct, we must, as always, pay careful attention to the argument’s conclusion, which is that with lower expenses, such stores offering large selections of healthy food will be able to operate profitably in the food desserts.

Notice that the argument does not make the claim that with these tax breaks, stores are guaranteed to open in the food desserts.In other words, there could be many other reasons, reasons that the argument does not address or consider, that could prevent the stores from opening in the food desserts.
All these officials are claiming is that, with lower expenses, such stores offering large selections of healthy food “will be able” to operate profitably in those areas.
So, their reasoning does not do what this choice describes.
(B) fails to consider that some people who live in food deserts are willing to travel to other areas to buy healthy food.

The fact that some people who live in food deserts are willing to travel to other areas to buy healthy food does not indicate that the plan will not work as expected.After all, if some people are willing to drive to other areas to buy healthy food, wouldn’t it be reasonable to expect that these people would also be willing to do their shopping for healthy food in the food desserts were these new stores to open and operate in these areas? For sure, it would be reasonable to expect them to do so.
So, failing to consider that possibility would not be a flaw in the officials’ reasoning.
YOUR CHOICE(C) fails to consider the possibility that the current lack of stores selling healthy food in food deserts is due to lack of demand for such food there.

The officials believe that offering tax breaks to stores that offer large selections of healthy food will allow these stores to operate profitably in the food deserts.In arriving at that conclusion, the officials assume that there are no other factors (no malicious variables) that could negatively impact the validity of this plan.

However, what if, as this answer choice mentions, there is a lack of demand for such healthy food? In that case, even with the tax breaks, the plan has a strong likelihood of failing because people in these food desserts will not be willing to purchase the healthy food that these stores sell.
Notice that this answer choice points to a malicious variable that the officials have failed to consider in making their plan; it points to a flaw in their argument.
(D) assumes without sufficient warrant that all officials utilize the same operational definition in determining what constitutes healthy food.

All officials need not utilize the same operational definition in determining what constitutes healthy food for this plan to work.For example, many, or most, officials could disagree on the operational definition of what constitutes healthy food, and still stores could profitably sell whatever does constitute healthy food in the food deserts.
So, the officials’ reasoning does not involve assuming that all officials utilize the same operational definition in determining what constitutes healthy food.
(E) neglects to consider how many stores selling healthy food, on average, operate in metropolitan regions not considered food deserts.

This is an argument about stores selling healthy food in food deserts.The plan proposed by officials centers on these food desserts.Therefore, the argument need not consider how many stores selling healthy food, on average, operate in metropolitan regions not considered food deserts.There could be many of these stores or few of these stores, and the plan proposed by officials could work perfectly.

question.text: Life Coach: Research unequivocally shows that people who are married are happier on average than people who are not married.Therefore, when my clients ask me about a practical step that they can take to increase their average levels of happiness, I point to marriage.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the life coach’s reasoning?

answers.text: Some people who are married report levels of happiness lower than the levels typically reported by people who are not married.
Many people who are very happy while they are not married are among those least likely to get married at any point in their lives.
Other research studies have found that, the later in life a person marries, the greater the happiness derived from the marriage.
Marriages in which the partners are not happy are less likely to last than marriages in which the partners are happy.
Many people who are not married report relatively high levels of average happiness.

explanation_parts.text: Some research shows that people who are married are happier on average than people who are not married.Being aware of that research, the life coach tells her clients that a practical step that they can take to increase their average levels of happiness is to get married.
Notice that the author must assume that marriage is a cause of increased levels of happiness.

Any time an author makes an argument that rests on cause-and-effect reasoning, we must be skeptical about the validity of the cause and effect claim as well as the soundness of the argument.Also, as we know, whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain.
Is the life coach’s argument sound? Might she have missed something of essence? Could there be an alternative explanation?
(A) Some people who are married report levels of happiness lower than the levels typically reported by people who are not married.

Notice that the coach says, “Research unequivocally shows that people who are married are happieron averagethan are people who are not married.” She is not saying that every single person who is married is happier than every single person who is not married.Rather, she is saying that people who are married are happier “on average” than are people who are not married.

Of course, the concept of an average is such that some people will be less happy than the average level of happiness and some people will be happier than the average level of happiness.In this case, regardless of the fact that some married people are less happy than the typical unmarried person, it remains the case that, on average, married people are happier.
So, what this answer choice says does not change the fact used to support the argument’s conclusion and does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.
(B) Many people who are very happy while they are not married are among those least likely to get married at any point in their lives.

Notice that what this answer choice says does not conflict with the argument made.After all, the coach makes the claim that research shows that people who are married are happier on average than are people who are not married.So, it could be true that there are a great many people who are very happy and will never get married at any point in their lives, but the evidence that the life coach has provided could still stand and still support the conclusion.

In other words, it could be true that there are many people who are very happy and will never get married, and at the same time, research could show that married people are, on average, happier than unmarried people.
So, what this answer choice says does not change the fact used to support the argument’s conclusion and does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.
(C) Other research studies have found that, the later in life a person marries, the greater the happiness derived from the marriage.
Notice that what this answer choice says is in line with the argument.

After all, the coach claims that people who are married are happier on average than people who are not, and although what this answer choice says is something a bit different from what the coach says, what it says is still in line with the idea that marriage can make people happier.
So, what this answer choice says would certainly not point to a possible flaw in the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Marriages in which the partners are not happy are less likely to last than marriages in which the partners are happy.
Notice that the life coach bases her conclusion on the fact that research shows that people who are married are happier on average than people who are not married.
However, what if it's true that marriages in which partners are not happy are less likely to last than those in which partners are happy? What occurs over time?

Well, when we looked at marriages, we would see a skewed sample, because many of the marriages in which the partners were not happy would not have lasted.In other words, the partners would have divorced.Then, the marriages that remain would be the ones in which the partners were happy.So, if we were to look at the existing marriages, of course we’d see that, on average, the people are happy.

At the same time, a conclusion about marriage that is based on the marriages that exist could be suspect because that conclusion would be based on a skewed sample of marriages, since only the ones in which the people were happy would remain.
So, this choice highlights a flaw in the argument by showing that the life coach has likely based her conclusion on the characteristics of a skewed sample.
Notice that, in general, this choice highlights that the author’s assumption that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observation she cites is a poor one.
(E) Many people who are not married report relatively high levels of average happiness.

What this answer choice says, that many people who are not married report relatively high levels of average happiness, does not conflict with the argument.After all, it could be true that many people who are not married report relatively high levels of average happiness, and at the same time, it could still be true that research shows that people who are married are happier on average than people who are not married.
So, what this choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.

question.text: Some people have suggested that the location and timing of our healing and wellness event are resulting in levels of attendance lower than they would be if the location of the event were different and the timing more convenient.Clearly, this suggestion is without merit.Year after year, in post event surveys, the vast majority of the attendees of the event indicate that they were satisfied with the location and that the timing of the event was well suited to their schedules.The author’s argument is flawed as a rebuttal to the suggestion made because the author

answers.text: provides as evidence for the conclusion information that merely reiterates the conclusion.
fails to consider the possibility that holding the event at a different location and time would be preferred by those organizing and presenting at the event.
assumes without warrant that event attendees who did not respond to post event surveys had positive opinions about the location and timing of the event.
cites as evidence opinions of individuals unlikely to be truly knowledgeable about the matter in question.
uses in supporting the conclusion a sample the composition of which renders it suspect as support for the conclusion drawn.

explanation_parts.text: (A) provides as evidence for the conclusion information that merely reiterates the conclusion.
Notice that the author does not support his conclusion by simply reiterating, i.e., repeating, his own conclusion.Rather, he provides as evidence for his conclusion the opinions of others.
(B) fails to consider the possibility that holding the event at a different location and time would be preferred by those organizing and presenting at the event.
This choice could be tempting because, in general, in determining when and where to hold an event, it would make sense to consider the preferences of those organizing and presenting at the event.
However, in this specific case, the author’s argument is a rebuttal to a suggestion about attendance and is not about those organizing and presenting at the event.

So, the fact that the author does not consider that possibility that holding the event at a different location and time would be preferred by those organizing and presenting does not make the argument flawed.
(C) assumes without warrant that event attendees who did not respond to post event surveys had positive opinions about the location and timing of the event.
Notice that the author says, “the vast majority of the attendees of the event indicate that they were satisfied with the location and that the timing of the event was well suited to their schedules.”

Of course, if the vast majority of the attendees have positive opinions of the location and timing of the event, then regardless of what the opinions of the other attendees are, it will remain the case that vast majority of the attendees have positive opinions.

So, in making the argument, the author does not have to be assuming that the attendees who did not respond to post event surveys had positive opinions about the location and timing of the event, because even if the attendees who did not respond did not have positive opinions, the vast majority of the attendees did have positive opinions, and that fact supports the author’s conclusion.
(D) cites as evidence opinions of individuals unlikely to be truly knowledgeable about the matter in question.
Notice that the author cites as evidence survey responses provided by “attendees of the event.”

Of course, attendees of the event are knowledgeable about the location and timing of the event and about their own opinions of the location and timing of the event.Thus, they are certainly knowledgeable about the matter in question.
So, the author does not do what this choice says the author does.
YOUR CHOICE(E) uses in supporting the conclusion a sample the composition of which renders it suspect as support for the conclusion drawn.

Notice that the author uses as evidence survey responses provided by “attendees of the event.” Of course, it’s not surprising attendees of the event were satisfied with the event’s location and found the timing convenient.After all, those people decided to attend the event.
So, the sample of people the author uses in supporting the argument is likely biased, because it’s made up only of people who were sufficiently happy with the location and timing to attend the event.

Of course then, that sample is suspect as support for the author’s conclusion that the suggestion about the location and timing of the event is without merit, because in using that sample to support his conclusion, the author is not citing opinions in general of the location and timing of the event.He’s citing only the opinions of people likely to have favorable opinions.
So, the author’s argument is flawed for the reason mentioned by this choice.

question.text: County Pest Control Officer: Rats and mice sometimes transmit disease to humans.Therefore, to control the sizes of local rat and mouse populations, I recommend that people regularly place rodenticides, poisons that kill rats and mice, in and around the outsides of their houses.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the pest control officer’s recommended approach?

answers.text: Raptors, such as hawks and owls, which naturally prey upon rats and mice, are poisoned and often die when they feed upon rats and mice that have consumed rodenticide.
More often than not, rats and mice remain outside of houses near which they live and, therefore, are unlikely to transmit diseases to the residents of those houses.
It is possible to reduce rat and mice populations in an area by reducing availability of sources of food for rats and mice.
Rat and mouse populations have been growing as global temperature increases have resulted in conditions better suited to such rodents’ breeding and survival than were past conditions.
Many of the more severe diseases associated with rats and mice are generally transmitted not directly by such rodents to humans but from the rodents to humans by insects.

explanation_parts.text: The county pest control officer recommends that people regularly place rodenticides in and around the outsides of their houses.The expected outcome or purpose of doing so is the control of rat and mouse populations.

Of course, any time someone presents a plan, he assumes that the plan will work.In other words, he assumes that there are not malicious variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan.
What malicious variable might the pest control officer have missed?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) Raptors, such as hawks and owls, which naturally prey upon rats and mice, are poisoned and often die when they feed upon rats and mice that have consumed rodenticide.

The county pest control officer claims that, to control the sizes of local rat and mouse populations, people should regularly place rodenticides, poisons that kill rats and mice, in and around the outsides of their houses.

This answer choice tells us that raptors, which prey upon rats and mice, are poisoned and often die when they feed upon rats and mice that have consumed rodenticide.Thus, this answer choice highlights a potential flaw in the pest control officer’s approach, in that rodenticides may actually have effects that ruin his plan.

After all, if raptors are poisoned and die, and raptor populations decrease, the county will lose a natural means by which rat and mouse populations are controlled.Thus, it’s possible that use of the approach will lead to increases, rather than decreases, in rat and mice populations.
Notice that, in general, what this answer choice indicates is that the author has neglected to account for a malicious variable that has the ability to derail his plan.
(B) More often than not, rats and mice remain outside of houses near which they live and, therefore, are unlikely to transmit diseases to the residents of those houses.

What this answer choice says could lead us into thinking that, if rats and mice, more often than not, remain outside of houses near which they live and, therefore, are unlikely to transmit diseases to the residents of those houses, the officer’s plan is flawed.However, the truth is that what this answer choice says is not a possible flaw in her argument.

For one thing, notice that the county pest control officer states that rats and micesometimestransmit disease to humans.What this choice says does not conflict with that statement or somehow render that statement less true.

Furthermore, the purpose of the pest control officer’s approach is to control the sizes of local rat and mouse populations.The use of the approach could serve that purpose even if what this choice says is true.
So, this choice does not highlight any flaws in the county pest control officer’s approach.
(C) It is possible to reduce rat and mice populations in an area by reducing availability of sources of food for rats and mice.
What this choice says, essentially, is that there is another way to control rat and mouse populations: reducing availability of food sources.
OK, great, but the fact that there is another way to control rat and mouse populations does not mean that the pest control officer’s recommended approach is flawed.
(D) Rat and mouse populations have been growing as global temperature increases have resulted in conditions better suited to such rodents’ breeding and survival than were past conditions.

What this choice says about rat and mouse populations does not point out a flaw in the pest control officer’s approach.It simply indicates that, while placing rodenticides around the outside of houses may serve to control rat and mouse populations, another factor, global temperature increases, will be working in the opposite direction, causing rat and mouse populations to increase.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Many of the more severe diseases associated with rats and mice are generally transmitted not directly by such rodents to humans but from the rodents to humans by insects.
Notice that what this choice says does not really change things, since even if what this choice says is true, what the pest control officer recommends could make sense.

After all, it’s not relevant how rats and mice transmit disease to humans; the argument simply centers on the fact that they can spread disease to humans.So even if it is true that many of the more severe diseases associated with rats and mice are generally transmitted not directly by such rodents to humans but from the rodents to humans by insects, rats and mice still transmit diseases to humans.
Also, regardless of how diseases are transmitted from rats and mice to humans, the approach of placing rodenticides around houses could be an effective way to control rat and mouse populations.
So, this choice does not point to any flaw in the pest control officer’s approach.

question.text: About 250 million years ago, nearly 90 percent of Earth’s species went extinct in what is known as the Permian-Triassic Extinction.Geologists believe that the extinction was caused by the impact of a large asteroid, which resulted in the creation of the Araguainha crater in what is now Brazil.The predominant theory holds that the impact site was atypical in that it was composed largely of shale in which oil existed in high concentrations, and that the energy of the impact was sufficient to ignite the oil, releasing into the atmosphere millions of tons of greenhouses gases, which catalyzed increases in temperatures, rendering Earth uninhabitable for many species.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the predominant theory for the Permian-Triassic Extinction?

answers.text: Between 300 and 240 million years ago, multiple asteroids at least as big as the one associated with the Araguainha crater impacted Earth, yet Earth’s species flourished during this period.
About 65 million years ago, after a large asteroid struck Earth by what is now Mexico, atmospheric temperatures declined precipitously.
Approximately 255 million years ago, when numerous large deposits of oil in shale were ignited by heat from massive volcanic eruptions, a very small percentage of Earth’s species went extinct.

The increase in Earth’s atmospheric temperature about 250 million years ago was far smaller than increases associated with some of Earth’s mass extinctions that followed the Permian-Triassic Extinction.
During the Permian-Triassic Extinction, a significantly greater number of marine species, those living in water, than terrestrial, or land dwelling, species went extinct.

explanation_parts.text: We know that about 250 million years ago, nearly 90 percent of Earth’s species went extinct likely due to the impact of a large asteroid.The predominant theory holds that the impact site was atypical in that it was composed largely of shale in which oil existed in high concentrations, and that the energy of the impact was sufficient to ignite the oil, releasing into the atmosphere millions of tons of greenhouses gases, which catalyzed increases in temperatures, rendering Earth uninhabitable for many species.

To get this question correct, we must pay close attention to key details provided about the event.Notice that the author goes out of her way to mention that the “impact site was atypical.” Why? Well, she says that it it was composed largely of shale in which oil existed in high concentrations, and that the energy of the impact was sufficient to ignite the oil, releasing into the atmosphere millions of tons of greenhouses gases, which catalyzed increases in temperatures, rendering Earth uninhabitable for many species.
(A) Between 300 and 240 million years ago, multiple asteroids at least as big as the one associated with the Araguainha crater impacted Earth, yet Earth’s species flourished during this period.
Although what this answer choice says is tempting, it’s a trap laid for those not paying close enough attention to the passage details.

Notice that the proposed theory for why nearly 90 percent of Earth’s species went extinct 250 million years ago goes beyond just an asteroid impact only.In fact, we're told that “The predominant theory holds that the impact site wasatypicalin that it was composed largely of shale in which oil existed in high concentrations, and that theenergy of the impact was sufficientto ignite the oil, releasing into the atmosphere millions of tons of greenhouses gases, which catalyzed increases in temperatures, rendering Earth uninhabitable for many species.”

So, according to this theory, there was an asteroid impact, which ignited oil deposits in the shale rock, releasing greenhouse gases in the process, leading to an increased temperature, which made it hard, according to the theory, for many species to survive.In other words, yes, the asteroid impact is of importance here.However, the true causative variable is the heat released from the ignition of large deposits of oil found in the shale, not just from an asteroid impact.

So, the reason that what this answer choice says does not point to a flaw in the argument is that it does not cast doubt on the essence of the predominant theory, which centers on the ignition of the oil deposits in the shale rock (something that the argument directly refers to as “atypical,” leading to a temperature spike as a result of the emissions of greenhouse gases.) This answer choice discusses only one component of the theory – the asteroid impact.

This answer choice is a seductive one, especially if you knew that one way we can weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to show that there are instances in which when the cause occurs, the effect does not.But as we discussed, what this answer choice says does address the essence of the cause-and-effect claim made, and thus it does not point to a flaw in the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(B) About 65 million years ago, after a large asteroid struck Earth by what is now Mexico, atmospheric temperatures declined precipitously.

The fact that, about 65 million years ago, after a large asteroid struck Earth by what is now Mexico, atmospheric temperatures declined precipitously, does not point to a possible flaw in the predominant theory for the Permian-Triassic Extinction.After all, notice that, like what we saw in answer choice (A), what this answer choice says leaves out crucial facets of the cause-and-effect relationship described in the passage, including, the “atypical” presence of the shale, the energy of the impact being sufficient for the ignition of the shale rock, and emissions of greenhouses gases, which, according to the passage, were what catalyzed the temperature increases on Earth.

So, the fact that temperatures declined after this impact is great and interesting, but this temperature decline does not weaken the predominant theory.After all, maybe this large asteroid that struck Earth 65 million years ago did not impact shale rock or have sufficient energy to ignite it if it did.

CORRECT ANSWER(C) Approximately 255 million years ago, when numerous large deposits of oil in shale were ignited by heat from massive volcanic eruptions, a very small percentage of Earth’s species went extinct.
One key factor in getting to this correct answer is noticing what is really of importance in this question.

As we’ve discussed, the predominate theory goes beyond just an asteroid impact only.In fact, we're told that “The predominant theory holds that the impact site wasatypicalin that it was composed largely of shale in which oil existed in high concentrations, and thatthe energy of the impact was sufficientto ignite the oil, releasing into the atmosphere millions of tons of greenhouses gases, which catalyzed increases in temperatures, rendering Earth uninhabitable for many species.” So, according to this theory, there was an asteroid impact, which ignited oil in the shale rock, releasing greenhouse gases in the process, leading to an increase in temperatures, which made it hard, according to the theory, for many species to survive.In other words, yes, the asteroid impact is of importance here.However, the true causative variable is the heat released from the ignition of large deposits of oil found in the shale.

Now, it’s easy to decide that what this answer choice says is “out of scope” but remember we don’t use that phrase here at TTP because, in saying that something is out of scope, what a person typically means is that he or she has no clue what is going on.And, furthermore, often correct answers sound “out of scope.” Take what this answer choice says, for example.It mentioned volcanoes.It would be easy to dismiss this answer choice if we felt that volcanoes were outside the scope of this argument.

However, notice that what this answer choice says shows that when numerous large deposits of oil in shale were ignited by heat from massive volcanic eruptions, a very small percentage of Earth’s species went extinct, showing that the purported cause occurred without the effect of numerous species dying off.In showing that the cause can occur without the effect, this, if true, would certainly point to a flaw in the argument.

(D) The increase in Earth’s atmospheric temperature about 250 million years ago was far smaller than increases associated with some of Earth’s mass extinctions that followed the Permian-Triassic Extinction.

That fact that the increase in Earth’s atmospheric temperature about 250 million years ago was far smaller than increases associated with some of Earth’s mass extinctions that followed the Permian-Triassic Extinction does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.It could have been that, although the temperature increase 250 million years ago was far smaller than other temperature increases that were associated with mass extinctions, the temperature increase 250 million years ago was still more than sufficient to create a spectacular mass extinction.In other words, a far smaller temperature increase does not equate to a temperature increase that could not have killed many species.
(E) During the Permian-Triassic Extinction, a significantly greater number of marine species, those living in water, than terrestrial, or land dwelling, species went extinct.

The fact that, during the Permian-Triassic Extinction, a significantly greater number of marine species, those species living in water, than terrestrial, or land dwelling, species went extinct does not point to a potential flaw in the argument.Remember that we don’t argue with premises of the passage, and we’re told that “nearly 90 percent of Earth’s species went extinct in what is known as the Permian-Triassic Extinction.” That is all we need to know.The ratio of marine species that went extinct to terrestrial species that went extinct does not change the facts provided, which could be sufficient to support the argument’s conclusion.

question.text: An art gallery proprietor noticed about the people who purchased expensive works of art in his gallery that, in general, the wealthier they were, the less interested in art they were.He therefore concluded that relatively wealthy people are, in general, less interested in art than are people who are not as wealthy.In arriving at his conclusion, the art gallery proprietor likely failed to consider the implications of the fact that

answers.text: the most expensive works of art he has sold have all been purchased by relatively wealthy people.
a person who is not relatively wealthy is not likely to purchase expensive works of art unless that person is unusually interested in art.
relatively wealthy people have the financial means to pursue many interests other than purchasing expensive works of art.
relatively wealthy people who patronize his gallery purchase more expensive works of art per person than are purchased by people who are not relatively wealthy.
the vast majority of the relatively wealthy people with whom the dealer interacts purchase expensive works of art.

explanation_parts.text: (A) the most expensive works of art he has sold have all been purchased by relatively wealthy people.

Regardless of what this choice says, the fact remains that the art gallery proprietor noticed about the people who purchased expensive works of art in his gallery that, in general, the wealthier they were, the less interested in art they were.In other words, the fact that the most expensive works of art the gallery proprietor has sold have all been purchased by relatively wealthy people does not change the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art, and regardless of what this choice says, the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art supports the gallery proprietor’s conclusion.
So, the gallery owner did not have to fail to consider what this choice says in order to arrive at his conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) a person who is not relatively wealthy is not likely to purchase expensive works of art unless that person is unusually interested in art.

Notice that the art gallery proprietor has used the characteristics of a sample, “the people who purchased expensive works of art in his gallery,” to arrive at a conclusion about people “in general.” Of course, in order for using that sample to support that conclusion to make sense, there has to be reason to believe that sample is representative of people in general.
With that in mind, let’s consider what this choice says.

This choice says that a person who is not relatively wealthy is not likely to purchase expensive works of art unless that person is unusually interested in art.Of course, what that means is that anyone not relatively wealthy who purchases an expensive work of art is likely unusually interested in art.

Thus, what this choice says indicates that, in comparing relatively wealthy people who purchase expensive works of art with other, not relatively wealthy, people who purchase expensive works of art, the art gallery proprietor has compared relatively wealthy people with peoplelikely to beunusually interested in art.

Of course, the fact that relatively wealthy people are less interested in art than people unusually interested in art does not mean that, in general, wealthy people less interested in art than other people.

So, in arriving at his conclusion, the art gallery proprietor likely failed to consider what this choice says, because what this choice says indicates that the sample of people that he has considered in arriving at his conclusion is not representative of people in general and, thus, that the characteristics of that sample do not effectively support his conclusion.
(C) relatively wealthy people have the financial means to pursue many interests other than purchasing expensive works of art.

Regardless of what this choice says, the fact remains that the art gallery proprietor noticed about the people who purchased expensive works of art in his gallery that, in general, the wealthier they were, the less interested in art they were.In other words, the fact that relatively wealthy people have the financial means to pursue many interests might explain why relatively wealthy people are not interested in art, but that fact does not change the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art.
Furthermore, regardless of what this choice says, the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art supports the gallery proprietor’s conclusion.
So, the gallery owner did not have to fail to consider what this choice says in order to arrive at his conclusion.
(D) relatively wealthy people who patronize his gallery purchase more expensive works of art per person than are purchased by people who are not relatively wealthy.

Regardless of what this choice says, the fact remains that the art gallery proprietor noticed about the people who purchased expensive works of art in his gallery that, in general, the wealthier they were, the less interested in art they were.In other words, the fact that wealthy people purchase more expensive works of art per person than are purchased by people who are not relatively wealthy does not change the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art, and regardless of what this choice says, the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art supports the gallery proprietor’s conclusion.
So, the gallery owner did not have to fail to consider what this choice says in order to arrive at his conclusion.
(E) the vast majority of the relatively wealthy people with whom the dealer interacts purchase expensive works of art.

Regardless of what this choice says, the fact remains that the art gallery proprietor noticed about the people who purchased expensive works of art in his gallery that, in general, the wealthier they were, the less interested in art they were.In other words, the fact that the wealthy people purchase expensive works of art does not change the fact that they were not interested in art, and regardless of what this choice says, the fact that the wealthy people were not interested in art supports the gallery proprietor’s conclusion.
So, the gallery owner did not have to fail to consider what this choice says in order to arrive at his conclusion.

question.text: Police statistics, in addition to data compiled over the last ten years by all major insurance companies, show that, of all cars sold in the country, more cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model.Therefore, prospective car buyers should realize that cars of Model X are the most likely of all cars in the country to be stolen.The reasoning of the argument is most vulnerable to criticism that the argument

answers.text: fails to consider that the most commonly stolen model of car is not necessarily the model of car that is most likely to be stolen.
fails to account for the possibility that the total number of cars stolen in the country each year has varied significantly.
assumes without sufficient warrant that there are not cases in which cars of Model X have been stolen that have gone unreported to police or insurance companies.
provides support for its conclusion based in part on the presupposition that the conclusion itself is indisputable.
neglects to present the characteristics of cars of Model X that cause cars of Model X to be stolen.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Premise: Police statistics, in addition to data compiled over the last ten years by all major insurance companies, show that, of all cars sold in the country, more cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model.
Conclusion: Therefore, prospective car buyers should realize that cars of Model X are the most likely of all cars in the country to be stolen.

Is the way in which the author has arrived at his conclusion sound? Has he properly used the evidence to arrive at an accurate, logical conclusion? Even if it is true that more (a greater number of) cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model, does that fact allow us to conclude that that cars of Model X are the most likely (have the greatest probability) of all cars in the country to be stolen? Are these two statistical metrics (a greater number vs.a greater probability) the same, or are they mathematically different concepts?

In considering arguments that mix up absolute numbers, for example, the number of cars stolen, with probability, for example, the likelihood of a certain car being stolen, we must be aware that absolute numbers are not the same type of statistic as probabilities.
YOUR CHOICE(A) fails to consider that the most commonly stolen model of car is not necessarily the model of car that is most likely to be stolen.

What this answer choice says is a flaw in the argument.Notice that the author is moving from numbers to probability in saying that the most commonly stolen model of car is also the model of car that is most likely to be stolen.We know that large numbers do not always translate to large probabilities.

For example, in real life, many Toyota Corollas are reported stolen every year simply because there are a lot of Toyota Corollas on the road.That is, many car owners own and drive them.Consider that because there are so many Toyota Corollas on the road, even if a high number are stolen, it could be true that only a small percentage of them are stolen (the probability of one being stolen is small).

For example, assume 50,000 stolen Toyota Corollas represents a very high number of stolen cars, more than any other car model.However, if there are 1 million Toyota Corollas on the road, then 50,000/1,000,000 = 5 percent of all Toyota Corollas are stolen.In other words, the probability of a Toyota Corolla being stolen is 5 percent.

Now consider a model of Aston Martin, a much rarer brand of car.Let's assume that there are 1,000 of these Astons on the road and that 100 of them are stolen each year.These stolen Astons represent 100/1,000 = 10 percent of all these Astons on the road.In other words, the probability of an Aston Martin’s being stolen is 10 percent.

We see that, since there are far fewer of this model of Aston Martin, a greater percentage of them are stolen, and thus the likelihood of having this model of car stolen is higher than the likelihood of having a Toyota Corolla stolen.At the same time, even if 99 percent the cars of this model of Aston Martin were stolen, it would not make the most commonly stolen list because there are just not that many of them.
(B) fails to account for the possibility that the total number of cars stolen in the country each year has varied significantly.

The total number of cars stolen in the country each year could vary significantly, and this fact would not impact, at all, the argument.After all, the author’s primary premise is that “Police statistics, in addition to data compiled over the last ten years by all major insurance companies, show that, of all cars sold in the country, more cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model.” That evidence could support the author’s conclusion regardless of whether the number of cars stolen each year has varied significantly.
So, the fact that the argument does not account for the possibility that the number of cars stolen each year has varied significantly is not a flaw in the argument.
(C) assumes without sufficient warrant that there are not cases in which cars of Model X have been stolen that have gone unreported to police or insurance companies.

What this answer choice says is not a flaw in the argument.Let’s say that there ARE cases in which cars of Model X have been stolen that have gone unreported to police or insurance companies.Wouldn’t this fact just take things in the same direction as the author’s logic?

So, the author is not assuming that there ARE NOT cases in which cars of Model X have been stolen that have gone unreported to police or insurance companies, because if there ARE such cases, then there is even more reason to believe the author’s conclusion.
(D) provides support for its conclusion based in part on the presupposition that the conclusion itself is indisputable.

What this answer choice says is not a flaw in the argument.In looking at the argument, we see that it doesn't somehow use its own conclusion for support.Instead, the conclusion rests upon the evidence that “Police statistics, in addition to data compiled over the last ten years by all major insurance companies, show that, of all cars sold in the country, more cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model.”
(E) neglects to present the characteristics of cars of Model X that cause cars of Model X to be stolen.

What this answer choice says is not a flaw in the argument.The author need not describe why more cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model to observe that more cars of Model X were stolen than cars of any other model.In other words, an author can construct an argument based upon an observation without also explaining the mechanism driving that observation.

question.text: Investment Analyst: Hedge Funds, financial companies that utilize high-risk strategies in seeking capital gains, self-report their investment performance on a quarterly basis to a common database, which aggregates key metrics, such as rates of return.Using information from that database, we see that, on average, hedge funds have earned unusually high rates of return over the past two decades.Therefore, many investors would do well to invest in hedge funds.The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

answers.text: assumes without warrant that investing in hedge funds is the only way for investors to earn unusually high rates of return.
overlooks the fact that hedge funds that fail during a quarter do not report their results to the database.
fails to take into account the fact that, because of government regulations, not all investors are legally able to invest in hedge funds.
neglects to consider the fact that hedge funds are not all similar but, rather, utilize a wide variety of different investment strategies.
incorrectly presupposes that hedge funds never experience poor investment performance.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Using information from a database to which hedge funds self-report, we see that, on average, hedge funds have earned unusually high rates of return over the past two decades.
Conclusion: Many investors would do well to invest in hedge funds.

It’s common to see authors in Critical Reasoning questions misinterpret or misunderstand statistical data.As you read the argument, did you see any issues with the way in which the hedge fund analyst arrived at the conclusion? Might there be some reasons why her conclusion might not be valid?
(A) assumes without warrant that investing in hedge funds is the only way for investors to earn unusually high rates of return.

The argument does not assume that investing in hedge funds is the only way for investors to earn unusually high rates of return.Rather, the argument is just about how hedge funds are a good mechanism for many investors in which to invest, not the best mechanism for those investors to invest.
YOUR CHOICE(B) overlooks the fact that hedge funds that fail during a quarter do not report their results to the database.

The major flaw in the reasoning of this argument centers on the concept of a biased sample, one type of which is the self-selected sample.In a self-selected sample, some characteristic or some trait appears to be significantly overrepresented.For example, imagine if we gave away basketballs to 100 people who allowed us to measure their heights.Our data would likely show an average height that is significantly taller than that of the general population since people who would want a basketball would probably be taller than most people in the general population.

So, if the hedge funds are “self-reporting” their results, we’re going to see a self-selected sample of hedge funds reporting, because the ones that fail will not be reporting their results to the database because these hedge funds will not be in business anymore.Thus, the data we see in the database would show an average return that is too high compared to reality.In other words, the true average return would be lower than what the database showed, maybe significantly lower, because the numbers in the database do not include the returns of hedge funds with the worst performance numbers, which, when added to the database, would pull the database average down.
So, the argument is flawed in the way described by this choice.
(C) fails to take into account the fact that, because of government regulations, not all investors are legally able to invest in hedge funds.

The conclusion of the argument is that “many” investors would do well to invest in hedge funds.“Many” never means “all.” Thus, the fact that not all investors are legally able to invest in hedge funds (because of government regulations) is not a flaw in the argument.
Regardless of whether all investors can legally invest in hedge funds, the argument works.
Pay close attention to words that limit the scope of an argument, such as “many,” “some,” “few,” and “most.”
(D) neglects to consider the fact that hedge funds are not all similar but, rather, utilize a wide variety of different investment strategies.

Hedge funds need not be the same, or utilize the same investment strategies, in order to conclude that many investors would do well to invest in hedge funds.After all, every hedge fund in operation could utilize a different strategy, and this fact would not imply that many investors would not do well to invest in hedge funds.In fact, for all we know, it could be this variation in strategies that is a source of strength for these hedge funds.
So, the analyst’s not considering the fact that hedge funds are not all similar but, rather, utilize a wide variety of different investment strategies is not a flaw in the argument.
(E) incorrectly presupposes that hedge funds never experience poor investment performance.

The argument does not make the presumption that hedge funds never experience poor investment performance.The author cites one key fact, that “using information from that database, we see that, on average, hedge funds have earned unusually high rates of return over the past two decades.” Of course, average performance could include low performance.
So, the author does not do what this choice says.

question.text: SolarSmart CEO: SolarSmart recently switched the protocols by which it collects data on customer satisfaction with SolarSmart products.In the past, the company, along with using other data collection methods, interviewed each quarter a randomized sample of its customers on their sentiments regarding SolarSmart products.From now on, the company will interview only customers who have used the company’s products for longer than seven years.Using the information gained during those interviews, the company will be able to make product-design decisions better than those it made in the past.Which of the following is the primary reason that the SolarSmart CEO’s reasoning is flawed?

answers.text: It fails to specify exactly what percentage of all SolarSmart customers have been using at least one of the company’s products for longer than seven years

It assumes without warrant that customers who have used SolarSmart products for over seven years provide information more detailed than that provided by those who became SolarSmart customers more recently.
It fails to provide evidence showing that SolarSmart’s being able to make better product-design decisions will result in increased customer satisfaction.
It neglects to consider that SolarSmart will be basing important decisions on information gained from a sample of customers who might not be representative of the company’s customer base.
It presupposes that SolarSmart’s gaining better information from its clients will be sufficient to increase the company’s profitability.

explanation_parts.text: In the past, the company interviewed each quarter a randomized sample of its customers on their sentiments regarding SolarSmart products.Now, the company will interview only customers who have used the company’s products for longer than seven years.
The CEO believes that, as a result of the change, the company will be able to make product-design decisions better than those it made in the past.

Might there be an issue with the data that the company will now collect? Is there a statistical issue with collecting data only from customers who have used the company’s products for longer than seven years?
(A) It fails to specify exactly what percentage of all SolarSmart customers have been using at least one of the company’s products for longer than seven years

The CEO need not specify exactly what percentage of all SolarSmart customers have been using at least one of the company’s products for longer than seven years to effectively make this argument, which is that, when interviewing only customers who have used the company’s products for longer than seven years, the company will be able to make product-design decisions better than those it made in the past.In other words, the company has some customers who have used the company’s products for longer than seven years.The company plans to interview them.It is not relevant what percentage of the total customers those customers represent.After all, they could represent 10 percent of the company’s customers, and the argument could be sound, or they could represent 90 percent of the company’s customers, and the argument could be sound.

(B) It assumes without warrant that customers who have used SolarSmart products for over seven years provide information more detailed than that provided by those who became SolarSmart customers more recently.

This argument is not about getting “more detailed” information.Rather, it is about getting “better” information.We can’t make the unwarranted assumption that more detailed information is synonymous with “better” information.As always, we must pay close attention to the details provided in the passage and in the answer choices.Detailed does not equate to better.
(C) It fails to provide evidence showing that SolarSmart’s being able to make better product-design decisions will result in increased customer satisfaction.
The conclusion of that argument is that “…the company will be able to make better product-design decisions than it made in the past.”
Notice that the argument never says anything about the effects on customer satisfaction of the company’s switching the protocols by which it collects data.

Being able to make “better product-design decision than it made in the past,” is not tantamount to increased customer satisfaction.” After all, the company could very well be able to make product-design decisions better than those it made in the past, and at the same time not see increased customer satisfaction.

To correctly answer Critical Reasoning questions, we have to be careful to notice that one thing, in this case “able to make better product design decisions,” is not the same as another thing, in this case, “increased customer satisfaction.”

YOUR CHOICE(D) It neglects to consider that SolarSmart will be basing important decisions on information gained from a sample of customers who might not be representative of the company’s customer base.

Notice that the company is moving away from interviewing each quarter a randomized sample of its customers to interviewing only customers who have used the company’s products for longer than seven years.

In interviewing only customers who have used the products for longer than 7 years, the company will likely be collecting data from a non-representative sample of customers.After all, when the company was looking at a random sample, the company would get a mix of opinions, some from customers who were very happy and some from customers who may have not been so happy.However, by looking only at the people who have used the products for more than 7 years, the company will collect data from customers who are clearly quite happy with the customers products (after all, these customers have been using the products for more than seven years).So, of course, what the company will begin to see is that people really enjoy these products, and unfortunately, the company stands a strong chance of not collecting the accurate data that it needs.
So, this choice captures exactly how the CEO’s reasoning is flawed.
(E) It presupposes that SolarSmart’s gaining better information from its clients will be sufficient to increase the company’s profitability.
The conclusion of that argument is that “the company will be able to make product-design decisions better than those it made in the past.”

Notice that the argument never says anything about the results of the decisions.In other words, the argument never makes the claim that the decisions will be sufficient to increase the company’s profitability.As always, we must pay close attention to the argument’s conclusion.

question.text: The results of a survey of five hundred billionaires, people who have at least one billion dollars in net assets, showed that the vast majority of them rarely bought coffee at coffee shops, never purchased lottery tickets, and rarely rode buses.Therefore, by skipping buying coffee at coffee shops, refraining from purchasing lottery tickets, and keeping use of buses to a minimum, a person could become a billionaire.Which of the following best describes the flaw in the argument above?

answers.text: It neglects to consider that other billionaires not surveyed may have habits different from the ones exhibited by those in the survey.
It takes a condition to be the effect of something that happened only after the condition already existed.
It assumes that one variable’s often being seen in conjunction with another variable indicates that one is the cause of the other.
It employs data unrelated to the conclusion made.
It presupposes without warrant that there are no ways to become a billionaire other than the ways listed.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:

Premise: The results of a survey of five hundred billionaires, people who have at least one billion dollars in net assets, showed that the vast majority of them rarely bought coffee at coffee shops, never purchased lottery tickets, and rarely rode buses.
Conclusion: Therefore, by skipping buying coffee at coffee shops, refraining from purchasing lottery tickets, and keeping use of buses to a minimum, a person could become a billionaire.

Notice that the author sees the habits of five hundred billionaires.From this observation, she argues that these habits directly contributed to these people becoming billionaires.As always, we must view cause-and-effect arguments with a skeptical eye.Are these habits really the driving force behind these people becoming billionaires, or might the author of the argument be misunderstanding, misconstruing, or misinterpreting the true facts of the matter?
(A) It neglects to consider that other billionaires not surveyed may have habits different from the ones exhibited by those in the survey.

The argument’s conclusion is based upon the results of the survey of five hundred billionaires.The fact that the survey did not go to every possible billionaire is not a flaw in the argument.After all, we need not survey the entire population of any group in order to make a cause-and-effect claim; a sample of that population is sufficient.In other words, the data from the survey could be enough to support the cause-and-effect claim made.
(B) It takes a condition to be the effect of something that happened only after the condition already existed.

This choice is temping, because we might be tempted to believe that it is clear that the billionaires surveyed rarely bought coffee at coffee shops, never purchased lottery tickets, and rarely rode buses only after they became billionaires.So, we might be tempted to believe that the argument is flawed because it takes becoming a billionaire to be the result of habits these people had only after becoming billionaires.

But do we really know when these people rarely bought coffee at coffee shops, never purchased lottery tickets, and rarely rode buses.We don’t.For all we know they adopted these habits long before they became billionaires.
So, we don’t really have a good reason to believe that the argument does what this choice says it does.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It assumes that one variable’s often being seen in conjunction with another variable indicates that one is the cause of the other.

The author assumes that one variable’s (being a billionaire) often being seen in conjunction with another variable (raring buying coffee at coffee shops, never purchasing lottery tickets, and rarely riding buses) indicates that one is the cause of the other.Remember, as we’ve said so many times before, a correlation between two variables does not necessarily imply cause and effect between those variables.In other words, the fact that we see two (or more) variables occurring together does not allow us to conclude on that basis alone that one of the variables is the cause of one of more of the other variables.Cause and effect relationships are far from simple, and cause and effect is not easy to determine.
(D) It employs data unrelated to the conclusion made.

In fact, the data used to support the argument’s conclusion is very related to the conclusion made.After all, the conclusion is that by skipping buying coffee at coffee shops, refraining from purchasing lottery tickets, and keeping use of buses to a minimum, a person could become a billionaire.Of course, the data upon which this conclusion rests is results of the survey of five hundred billionaires.
(E) It presupposes without warrant that there are no ways to become a billionaire other than the ways listed.

The argument never makes the claim, nor does it assume that there are no ways to become a billionaire other than the ways listed.The argument leaves open that there could be other ways to become a billionaire other than the ways listed.

question.text: Even though it is widely suspected that cellular phone towers cause health problems, ten years ago, some cellular phone towers were erected in close proximity to the neighborhood of West Elm.It must be true that waves emitted from those towers are causing people to develop cancer, as, since the erection of the towers, the rate of diagnosis of cancer in people living in the neighborhood of West Elm has been, on average, 300 percent higher than that in the general population of people.Which of the following, if true, points to a possible flaw in the above argument?

answers.text: Once a neighborhood is perceived as dangerous, housing prices there collapse, enabling lower income families that have poor access to healthy food and effective healthcare to move in.
Less than twenty percent of the people living in the neighborhood of West Elm have been diagnosed with any type of cancer over the past ten years.
Residents of the neighborhood of West Elm have undergone diagnostic cancer screenings no more regularly over the past ten years than have people on average.
To date, researchers have not been able to find clear a cause-and-effect link between exposure to waves emitted from cellular towers and the development of cancer in humans.
The waves emitted from the cellular towers near the neighborhood of West Elm are of considerably lower energy than the waves emitted from many other cellular towers.

explanation_parts.text: The author arrives at the following cause-and-effect conclusion:
Conclusion: Waves emitted from those (cellular phone) towers are causing people to develop cancer.

As is the case with any cause-and-effect argument, the author makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain.Is this assumption a good one?

Notice that her evidence is that, since the arrival of the cell towers, the rate of diagnosis of cancer in West Elm has been 300 percent higher than that in the general population of people.In other words, her reasoning is that since the rate increased right after the arrival of the cell towers, the cell towers must have been the cause.
Does the fact the one event immediately precedes another event allow us to conclude that the first event is the cause of the second? How might this argument be flawed?

CORRECT ANSWER(A) Once a neighborhood is perceived as dangerous, housing prices there collapse, enabling lower income families that have poor access to healthy food and effective healthcare to move in.
What this answer choice says would certainly point to a possible flaw in the argument.

If it is true that, once a neighborhood is perceived as dangerous, lower income families that have poor access to healthy food and effective healthcare begin to move into the neighborhood, the rate of cancer seen in the people in the neighborhood would likely increase, unfortunately, not necessarily because of the cell phone towers but perhaps because of the unhealthy food they are eating and their lack of effective healthcare, two factors that are widely-known to increase cancer rates.
