So, what this answer choice says casts doubt on the cause-and-effect claim advanced by the author by presenting a possible alternative explanation for the increase in the rate of diagnosis of cancer.In other words, she is asserting that it’s the waves from the cellular towers that are causing health problems, while this answer choice indicates that there may be an alternative reason for the increase: the families moving into the neighborhood are eating unhealthy food and do not have effective health care.
So, this choice points to the flaw that the author has relied on the assumption that there is not an alternative cause for the observed effect.
(B) Less than twenty percent of the people living in the neighborhood of West Elm have been diagnosed with any type of cancer over the past ten years.

The fact that fewer than 20 percent of the people living in the neighborhood have been diagnosed with any type of cancer over the past 10 years does not point to a flaw in this argument.Fewer than 20 percent could be 19 percent, which could translate into dozens or even hundreds of cases of cancer, even in a modest-sized neighborhood.In fact, the truth is that, even if one percent of the people in the neighborhood have been diagnosed with cancer, the author’s argument could still remain perfectly intact.
Keep in mind that we don’t need a high percentage of people to have been diagnosed with cancer for it to be true that waves emitted from the cellular towers are causing cancer.
So, regardless of what this choice says, the fact remains that, since the towers were installed, the rate of cancer has increased by 300 percent, and that fact still supports the conclusion.
(C) Residents of the neighborhood of West Elm have undergone diagnostic cancer screenings no more regularly over the past ten years than have people on average.
What this choice says strengthens the argument.

Imagine a situation in which the residents of West Elm have in fact undergone diagnostic cancer screenings more regularly than have people on average.That relatively high rate of diagnostic screenings would likely result in a relatively high rate of cancer diagnosis.So, in that situation, we would have an alternative explanation for the high rate of cancer diagnosis in West Elm.It might be that the explanation is not that the cell towers are causing cancer but that the residents have undergone diagnostic screenings more often than have people in general and thus more cancers are being diagnosed in West Elm residents, even though the rate of cancer there is no higher than average.

So, what this choice says, that West Elm residents have not undergone diagnostic cancer screenings more regularly than have people on average, serves to rule out the possibility we just described, and in serving to rule out that alternative possibility, what this choice says strengthens the argument.
Of course, a choice that strengthens the argument does not point out a flaw in the argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) To date, researchers have not been able to find clear a cause-and-effect link between exposure to waves emitted from cellular towers and the development of cancer in humans.
What this answer choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.

In general, remember that the failure to find sufficient evidence for something does not mean that such evidence does not exist.It could very well be that cellular waves do cause cancer in humans, but researchers have yet to design studies that support this cause-and-effect link.

So, regardless of whether researchers have been able to find a clear cause-and-effect link between exposure to waves emitted from cellular towers and the development of cancer, the fact remains that, after the towers were erected, the rate of cancer in the people living in West Elm increased by 300 percent, and that fact still supports the conclusion of the argument.
(E) The waves emitted from the cellular towers near the neighborhood of West Elm are of considerably lower energy than the waves emitted from many other cellular towers.
What this answer choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.

Even if the waves coming from the cellular towers in the West Elm neighborhood are of considerably lower energy than the waves emitted from many other cellular towers, there are still waves coming from the towers.

Remember, the concept of “lower” is one of relativity, of comparison.“Lower” does not mean “insufficient.” To give an extreme example, the deadliness of a 9 mm pistol bullet is considerably “lower” than the deadliness of a tank shell.At the same time, a 9 mm pistol bullet is sufficient to cause significant damage and even death to humans.

Thus, even though the waves coming from the cellular towers in the West Elm neighborhood are of considerably lower energy than waves emitted from many other towers, it’s still possible that the waves cause cancer.
So, this choice does not affect the support that the fact that the erection of the towers was followed by a 300 percent increase in the rate of cancer provides for the conclusion.

question.text: Two years ago, the town of Valencia passed a law that made it mandatory that all motorcycle riders wear approved motorcycle helmets.In the two years since, the fatality rate of motorcycle riders in Valencia has decreased by 20 percent.Therefore, it’s clear that mandatory motorcycle helmet laws can reduce motorcycle fatalities.The argument is vulnerable to each of the following criticisms, EXCEPT:

answers.text: It presupposes that habits of drivers of automobiles in Valencia have not changed in ways that would result in significantly increased safety of motorcycle riders.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that people riding motorcycles in Valencia have, since the helmet law was passed, ridden at least as safely as they did before the passage of the law.
It overlooks the possibility that, in the two years after the law was passed, the town of Valencia doubled the number of ambulances and emergency response personnel on its roads.
It takes as self-evident that motorcycle riders in Valencia actually obeyed the new motorcycle helmet law.
It fails to establish that, after the law was passed, motorcyclists in Valencia did not spend less time per person riding motorcycles than they spent before the law was passed.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: Two years ago, the town of Valencia passed a law that made it mandatory that all motorcycle riders wear approved motorcycle helmets.
Premise: In the two years since, the fatality rate of motorcycle riders in Valencia has decreased by 20 percent.
Conclusion: Mandatory motorcycle helmet laws can reduce motorcycle fatalities.

Notice that this argument involves a cause-and-effect claim.Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain.As we know, often that this assumption is a bad one.

It's instructive to see that the author’s evidence is weak because the fact that one variable precedes another variable doesn’t on that basis alone allow us to conclude that the first variable is the cause of the second variable.

In this question, we need to consider that perhaps it was not actually the new helmet laws that have caused the decline in the motorcycle fatality rate.Could there be an alternative explanation for this drop? Might the author have missed something?
Because this is an “EXCEPT” question, what four of the answer choices say will be flaws in the argument; these answers will be incorrect.
What one of the answer choices says will not be a flaw in the argument; this will be the correct answer.
(A) It presupposes that habits of drivers of automobiles in Valencia have not changed in ways that would result in significantly increased safety of motorcycle riders.

What this answer says is a flaw in the argument.Remember that in saying that the real cause of the drop was the helmet law, the author is assuming that there can be no other explanation for why the motorcycle fatality rate dropped.

However, if it’s true that habits of drivers of automobiles in Valencia have changed in ways that would result in significantly increased safety of motorcycle riders, then this argument is weakened because we have a plausible alternative explanation for the drop in the motorcycle fatality rate.

So, the argument is flawed in that it overlooks that possibility and presupposes that habits of drivers of automobiles in Valencia have not changed in ways that would result in significantly increased safety of motorcycle riders
Flaw? Yes.

YOUR CHOICE(B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that people riding motorcycles in Valencia have, since the helmet law was passed, ridden at least as safely as they did before the passage of the law.

What this answer says is a not flaw in the argument.The author need not assume that people riding motorcycles in Valencia have, since the helmet law was passed, ridden at least as safely as they did before the passage of the law.

Think about it.Let’s say that people riding motorcycles in Valencia have “not” since the helmet law was passed ridden at least as safely as they did before the passage of the law.In fact, let’s pretend that they’ve become maniacs on the road.Well, wouldn’t the author’s argument still be a good one? In fact, wouldn’t it even be a better one? If the riders were now maniacs on the road, but the motorcycle fatality rate has dropped since the passage of the helmet law, wouldn’t we really have faith that it was in fact the law that is causing this decline? Yes, we would.
Flaw? No.
(C) It overlooks the possibility that, in the two years after the law was passed, the town of Valencia doubled the number of ambulances and emergency response personnel on its roads.

What this answer says is a flaw in the argument.Remember that, in saying that the real cause of the drop was the helmet law, the author is assuming that there can be no other explanation for why the motorcycle fatality rate dropped.

However, if it is true that, in the two years after the law was passed, the town of Valencia doubled the number of ambulances and emergency response personnel on its roads, then we have a plausible alternative explanation for why there was a drop in the motorcycle fatality rate.As a result, we are less sure that it actually was the passage of the new helmet law that was the cause of the drop.
So, the argument is flawed in that it overlooks that possibility.
Flaw? Yes.
(D) It takes as self-evident that motorcycle riders in Valencia actually obeyed the new motorcycle helmet law.

What this answer says is a flaw in the argument.Remember that in saying that the real cause of the drop was the helmet law, the author is assuming that there can be no other explanation for why the motorcycle fatality rate dropped.

However, if could be true that motorcycle riders in Valencia actually did not obey the new motorcycle helmet law, in which case this argument would be weakened because we would have a plausible alternative explanation for the drop.
So, the argument is flawed in that it overlooks that possibility and takes as self-evident that motorcycle riders in Valencia actually obeyed the new motorcycle helmet law.
Flaw? Yes.
(E) It fails to establish that, after the law was passed, motorcyclists in Valencia did not spend less time per person riding motorcycles than they spent before the law was passed.

What this answer says points to a flaw in the argument.Remember that in saying that the real cause of the drop was the helmet law, the author is assuming that there can be no other explanation for why the motorcycle fatality rate dropped.

However, if it is true that after the law was passed, motorcyclists in Valencia DID spend less time per person riding motorcycles than they spent before the law was passed, then the argument’s conclusion is weakened because we now have a plausible alternative cause for the decline.After all, if motorcyclists in Valencia spent less time per person riding motorcycles than they spent before the law was passed, then we would expect the fatality rate to decline because of that factor alone.
So, the argument is flawed in that it overlooks the possibility that motorcyclists in Valencia spent less time per person riding motorcycles than they spent before the law was passed.
Flaw? Yes.

question.text: Avocados have been thought to be superfoods, foods that promote wellbeing.However, because of the results of a recent study, researchers have concluded that, rather than promote wellbeing, avocados cause heart disease.The researchers had tracked two groups of people, one in which each participant ate at least one avocado each day, the “avocado group,” and one in which each participant did not, the “no avocado group.” During the time period of the study, 99 members of the avocado group developed heart disease, while 33 members of the no avocado group developed heart disease.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the researchers’ argument?

answers.text: Nutritionists, researchers, and physicians have differing views on the criteria used to determine whether a food should be considered a superfood.
Overconsumption of food is known to result in the development of heart disease.
It is well established that avocados are high in monounsaturated fats, which are fats known to promote heart health.
There were substantial differences in the quantities of and intensity of daily exercise engaged in by the members of the avocado group and the members of no avocado group.
The avocado group was composed of 400 people between the ages of 20 and 30, while the no avocado group was composed of 100 people in the same age group.

explanation_parts.text: Because of the results of a recent study, researchers have concluded that avocados cause heart disease.What were the study results? Each participant in one group, the “avocado group,” consumed at least one avocado each day.Each participant in the other group, the “no avocado group,” did not.During the time period of the study, 99 members of the avocado group developed heart disease, while 33 members of the no avocado group developed heart disease.
Does the evidence effectively support the conclusion, or might the author have missed something material?
(A) Nutritionists, researchers, and physicians have differing views on the criteria used to determine whether a food should be considered a superfood.
This argument is not one about what should, and what should not be, considered a superfood.

Notice that the researchers’ conclusion is simply that, rather than promote wellbeing, avocados cause heart disease.So, the fact that nutritionists, researchers, and physicians have differing views on the criteria used to determine whether a food should be considered a superfood does not affect the support the evidence provides for the conclusion and does not highlight any flaw in the argument.
(B) Overconsumption of food is known to result in the development of heart disease.

Since we have no reason to believe that the people in the avocado group overconsumed food but the people in the no avocado group did not, what this answer choice says does not point to a possible flaw in the argument.

It’s important not to make unwarranted assumptions.The only difference between the two groups that we are aware of is that the participants in one group ate avocados, and the participants in the other group did not.
It’s also important not to confuse one thing for a different thing.In this case, eating avocados is not the same as overconsuming food.

In fact, for all we know from what the passage says, the percentage of people in the avocado group who overconsumed food was smaller than the percentage of people in the no avocado group who overconsumed food.
So, this choice does not present an alternative explanation for the difference in heart disease rates and, thus, does not highlight any flaw in the argument.
(C) It is well established that avocados are high in monounsaturated fats, which are fats known to promote heart health.
It is important not to “argue” with the facts provided in the passage.

We know that “During the time period of the study, 99 members of the avocado group developed heart disease while 33 members of the no avocado group developed heart disease.” It’s important to see that that fact remains true even if it is well established that avocados are high in monounsaturated fats, which are fats known to promote heart health.

So, regardless of the fact that avocados are high in facts known to promote heart health, the researchers can use the fact that 99 members of the avocado group developed heart disease, while 33 members of the no avocado group developed heart disease to support the conclusion that avocados cause heart disease.
After all, it could be that, although avocados are high in fats that promote heart health, there are other components of avocados that cause heart disease.
(D) There were substantial differences in the quantities of and intensity of daily exercise engaged in by the members of the avocado group and the members of no avocado group.
As always, we must be careful with bi-directional statements.
What does it mean that there were “substantial differences in the quantities of and intensity of daily exercise engaged in by the members of the avocado group and the members of no avocado group”?

Does that statement mean that the people in the avocado group engaged in a greater quantity of intense exercise than the members of the no avocado group engaged in? Or does it mean the reverse? Or does it mean something else entirely?
What this answer choice says could be interpreted multiple ways.Therefore, with no clear direction pointing to a flaw in the argument, we can easily eliminate this answer choice.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The avocado group was composed of 400 people between the ages of 20 and 30, while the no avocado group was composed of 100 people in the same age group.
Now, it’s easy to decide that what this answer choice says is “out of scope” but remember we don’t use that phrase here at TTP.Remember that correct answers often sound “out of scope.”

Notice that the passage states, “During the time period of the study, 99 members of the avocado group developed heart disease, while 33 members of the no avocado group developed heart disease.” The numbers mentioned are absolute numbers, and if we consider only the 99 versus the 33, we may get seduced into thinking that the argument is solid.

However, aren’t we missing some information that is essential to determining whether the argument is sound, such as the number of people in each group? Wouldn’t the number of people who were in each group be an essential thing to know?

This answer choice tells us that there were 400 people in the avocado group.Therefore, of the people in that group, 99 out of 400 developed heart disease.In other words, the rate of heart disease in the avocado group was 99/400 = 24.75/100.On the other hand, the rate of heart disease in the no avocado group was 33/100.So, the rate of heart disease of the the avocado group was actually lower than the rate of heart disease of the no avocado group.

Of course, the fact that the rate of heart disease in the avocado group was the lower rate conflicts with the conclusion that avocados cause heart attacks.So, if what this answer choice says is true, then the researchers have misinterpreted the data, and the evidence they have does not support their conclusion.

So, the fact presented by this choice highlights a flaw in the argument, the fact that the researchers compared the absolute numbers of heart attacks without considering how many people were in each group.

question.text: Toothpaste Company CEO: We recently sent questionnaires to all 1,000 dentists practicing in the state, asking them which toothpaste brands they recommend to patients.Results showed that 80 percent of the dentists practicing in the state who replied to the questionnaire recommend our brand of toothpaste to their patients.Therefore, it must be true that more of the dentists practicing in the state recommend our brand of toothpaste to their patients than recommend any other brand of toothpaste.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the Toothpaste CEO’s argument?

answers.text: The questionnaire was designed in a way such that dentists were instructed to list any toothpaste brands that they recommend to their patients.
Of the 1,000 dentists to whom the questionnaires were sent, only 700 dentists actually completed and returned the questionnaires.
Because general dentists often refer their patients to see specialist dentists, such as orthodontists, many of the dentists in the state have overlapping client bases.
Every dentist who completed and returned the survey was paid monetary compensation for his or her time.
Many of the dentists in the state who are not currently practicing, while practicing, consistently recommended a different brand of toothpaste to their patients.

explanation_parts.text: Background: We recently sent questionnaires to all 1,000 dentists practicing in the state, asking them which toothpaste brands they recommend to patients.
Premise: Results showed that 80 percent of the dentists practicing in the state who replied to the questionnaire recommend our brand of toothpaste to their patients.
Conclusion: It must be true that more of the dentists practicing in the state recommend our brand of toothpaste to their patients than recommend any other brand of toothpaste.

We must always be on the lookout for people misinterpreting statistical data.Does that fact that “80 percent of the dentists practicing in the state who replied to the questionnaire recommend our brand of toothpaste to their patients” allow the author to draw his conclusion that more of the dentists practicing in the state recommend her brand of toothpaste to their patients than recommend any other brand of toothpaste? What fact would highlight a flaw in this argument?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) The questionnaire was designed in a way such that dentists were instructed to list any toothpaste brands that they recommend to their patients.

Notice that 1,000 dentists were sent questionnaires that asked which toothpaste brands they recommended their patients.The CEO claims that the results show that 80 percent of the dentists who replied recommend her brand of toothpaste to their patients.From this information, she concludes that it must be true that more dentists practicing in the state recommend her brand than any other brand.

Notice, what she’s really saying is that her brand of toothpaste was recommended more often than other brands of toothpaste.However, what this answer choice says is that the dentists could choose on the survey whatever brands of toothpaste they recommend to their patients.If the survey were designed in this way, it could very well be true that some, or a large number, or even all the dentists who responded to the survey recommend, two, three, four, five, or even all of the toothpaste brands available.In other words, the dentists were not picking only one toothpaste brand.

So, it could be that, out of the 1,000 dentists, 800 recommended the CEO’s toothpaste brand, and all of those 800 dentists also recommended another five toothpaste brands.In other words, what if the 800 dentists recommended multiple toothpaste brands to their patients? In such a case it would not be true that more of the dentists practicing in the state recommend the CEO’s brand than recommend any other brand.
Notice that, as always, paying close attention to the argument’s conclusion is an essential thing to do; as soon as we lose track of the conclusion, we’re apt to fall for a trap choice.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Of the 1,000 dentists to whom the questionnaires were sent, only 700 dentists actually completed and returned the questionnaires.
The fact that, of the 1,000 dentists who received the questionnaires, only 700 actually responded with completed questionnaires is not a potential flaw this argument.

It would not be realistic to think that all 1,000 of the dentists who received the questionnaires would complete and respond to them.Furthermore, 700 responses is a large number of responses, one that is sufficiently large enough to be statistically valid.After all, it is not as if 8 dentists responded, right? 700 is a big number.
Furthermore, notice that 80 percent of the dentists who responded recommended the company’s toothpastes.
Using some simple math, we can see that 80 percent of 700 is 560.
560 dentists is most, i.e., more than half, of the 1000 dentists the passage says are practicing in the state.
Thus, rather than point out a flaw in the argument, this choice confirms that most of the dentists in the state recommend the company’s toothpaste.
Be careful not to overplay the “small sample size” concept.
(C) Because general dentists often refer their patients to see specialist dentists, such as orthodontists, many of the dentists in the state have overlapping client bases.
The fact that many of the dentists in the state have overlapping client bases would not point to a flaw in this argument.

After all, notice that the argument centers on the CEO’s belief that more dentists recommend her brand of toothpaste to their patients than recommend any other brand of toothpaste.So, this argument is not one about the number or the percentage of patients who use the CEO’s brand of toothpaste.Rather, this argument centers on the number of dentists who recommend his brand, according to the results of the questionnaires.

So, many of the dentists in the state could very well have overlapping client bases, and that fact wouldn’t change that the CEO is basing her argument on the results of the questionnaires, which show that 80 percent of the dentists replied stating that they used his brand toothpaste.After all, there is a difference between how many of the dentists recommend a certain brand and how many of their patients actually use the brand.
(D) Every dentist who completed and returned the survey was paid monetary compensation for his or her time.

The fact alone that every dentist who completed and returned the survey was paid monetary compensation for his or her time does not point to a potential flaw in the argument.That somebody has been paid to complete a survey does not necessarily lead us to the conclusion that that person’s responses to the survey questions are somehow invalid.For all we know, all the dentist’s responses were completely accurate, in spite of the fact that they were compensated.After all, all of the dentists who responded were paid, regardless of how they responded.

This answer choice is tempting because compensation could color someone’s opinion in a direction favorable to the person or entity compensating him or her, but the information presented in this the passage and in this choice provides no clear indication that the results of the survey were biased because of compensation.
(E) Many of the dentists in the state who are not currently practicing, while practicing, consistently recommended a different brand of toothpaste to their patients.

Notice that the CEO’s conclusion is that “Therefore, it must be true thatmore of the dentists practicing in the staterecommend our brand of toothpaste to their patients than recommend any other brand of toothpaste.” Her conclusion is about the dentists who are practicing in the state.Therefore, what non-practicing dentists do or feel or believe (even what they felt while they were practicing) will not affect the validity of her argument.As always, we must pay close attention to each detail provided in the passage and in the answer choices.

question.text: After some years of lackluster sales, the CEO of MillennialTech, a software development company, increased the price of the company’s bestselling software, for which its customers purchase a 6-month renewable license, by 500 percent but made no improvements to the software.In the quarters that followed the price increase, MillennialTech saw a 400 percent increase in the revenue generated by sales of that software.The board of directors applauded the CEO’s decision, claiming that the risky move of raising the software’s price certainly was the cause of the increase in revenue generated by sales of that software.The best criticism of the argument made by the board of directors is that it

answers.text: assumes without sufficient warrant that at least some of MillennialTech’s customers will be willing to pay the higher price for the bestselling software once they must renew their 6-month licenses.
ignores the fact that, often, suddenly increasing significantly the price of a product, especially software, does not increase, but rather decreases, the revenue generated by sales of that product.
fails to establish that none of the other software sold by MillennialTech saw a significant increase in sales revenue that began around when MillennialTech raised the bestselling software’s price.

overlooks the possibility that the economy of the country in which MillennialTech’s customers reside began to strengthen a few months before the company increased the price of its bestselling software.

does not include consideration of the possibility that, having increased by 400 percent, the revenue generated by sales of the bestselling software still represents relatively weak sales in dollar terms.

explanation_parts.text: Notice that the board of directors believes that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the 500 percent increase in the price of the company’s bestselling software and the 400 percent increase in the revenue generated by sales of that software.

Of course, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim made.After all, in this argument, couldn’t there be an alternative explanation for why the revenue generated by sales of the bestselling software increased by 400 percent? Might there be more to the story?

(A) assumes without sufficient warrant that at least some of MillennialTech’s customers will be willing to pay the higher price for the bestselling software once they must renew their 6-month licenses.

Notice that the board of directors “applaudedthe CEO’s decision, claiming that the risky move of raising the software’s price certainly was the cause of the increase in revenue generated by sales of that software.” We see that the board is pleased about an action that the CEO took in the past.In other words, the board is not making any assumptions about what customers will or will not do in the future; rather, the board is pleased about something that has already occurred.

(B) ignores the fact that, often, suddenly increasing significantly the price of a product, especially software, does not increase, but rather decreases, the revenue generated by sales of that product.

From the passage we know as fact that “In the quarters that followed the price increase, MillennialTech saw a 400 percent increase in the revenue generated by sales of that software.” Therefore, what happens often is irrelevant.We know that revenue has gone up 400 percent.

Remember that we never argue with the premises provided to us in Critical Reasoning questions.In other words, the premises provided to us are factual.What this answer choice says does not change the facts provided to us.

YOUR CHOICE(C) fails to establish that none of the other software sold by MillennialTech saw a significant increase in sales revenue that began around when MillennialTech raised the bestselling software’s price.

The passage says that “MillennialTech saw a 400 percent increase in the revenue generated by sales of that software.” This statement must be taken as fact.Therefore, it is irrelevant what has occurred with the revenue generated by the other products.

Remember that we never argue with the premises provided to us in Critical Reasoning questions.In other words, the premises provided to us are factual.What this answer choice says does not change the facts provided to us.

CORRECT ANSWER(D) overlooks the possibility that the economy of the country in which MillennialTech’s customers reside began to strengthen a few months before the company increased the price of its bestselling software.
As we know well, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim.

The board believes that raising the software’s price certainly was the cause of the increase in revenue generated by sales of that software.However, what this answer choice says points to a possible alternative explanation for why there was a 400 percent increase in the revenue generated by sales of the software: the economy began to boom just prior to the price increase.In other words, if the economy of the country in which MillennialTech’s customers reside began to strengthen a few months before the company increased the price of its bestselling software, it could be the case that the strengthening economy is the actual cause of the sales increase.After all, it has often been said that “a rising tide lifts all boats.”

What this answer choice says highlights the fact that the board of directors may have overlooked the real cause of the increase in sales.In other words, what this answer choices presents is an alternative explanation for why there was a 400 percent increase in the revenue generated by sales of the software.

(E) does not include consideration of the possibility that, having increased by 400 percent, the revenue generated by sales of the bestselling software still represents relatively weak sales in dollar terms.

The board is simply making a cause-effect-claim that the CEO’s decision has led to the increase in revenue.The board is not making the claim that the revenue increase represents a large dollar amount.If you choose this answer as correct you likely were not paying close enough attention to the conclusion or to the details of this answer choice.

question.text: People seeking to mitigate the effects of chronic stress can utilize meditation or benzodiazepines, which are medicines that suppress the effects of stress hormones.A study has found that people opting to utilize only benzodiazepines to address chronic stress are likely to have attention spans significantly shorter than the attention spans of people opting to utilize only meditation to address chronic stress.Apparently, benzodiazepines cause reductions in people’s attention spans.The argument is most vulnerable to criticism on which of the following grounds?

answers.text: It fails to consider the possibility that use of meditation often results in significant increases in a person’s attention span.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that the effects of stress hormones do not cause people to have relatively short attention spans.
It rules out the conclusion that relatively short attention spans are the cause of chronic stress without any reason for ruling out that conclusion.
It assumes without warrant that the people most apt to have relatively short attention spans are also those most apt to choose benzodiazepines over meditation.
It overlooks the fact that people who opt to use meditation to mitigate the effects of chronic stress might also utilize benzodiazepines.

explanation_parts.text: Notice that the author makes a cause-and-effect claim, saying that benzodiazepines likely cause significant reductions in people’s attention spans.

What’s the evidence upon which this conclusion rests? The evidence is a study that found that people opting to utilize benzodiazepines to address chronic stress are likely to have attention spans significantly shorter than the attention spans of people opting to utilize only meditation to address chronic stress.
It’s important to see that what the study found involves a comparison.

In other words, the study did not find that people who utilize benzodiazepines to manage stress have unusually short attention spans, nor did it find that people who utilize meditation to manage chronic stress have unusually long attention spans.

Rather what the study found involves a comparison of attention spans.The people who used only benzodiazepines were likely to have attention spansshorterthan the attention spans of people who used only meditation.
As always, we must be skeptical when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim.Could there be more to the story? What might the author have missed?
YOUR CHOICE(A) It fails to consider the possibility that use of meditation often results in significant increases in a person’s attention span.

As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, we must see that what the study found involves a comparison.The study found that the people who used only benzodiazepines were likely to have attention spans shorter than the attention spans of people who used only meditation.
Given that finding, the author concludes that “Apparently, benzodiazepines cause reductions in people’s attention spans.”
This answer choice points out a vulnerability in the argument by showing that there could be an alternative explanation for what the study found.

What this answer choice says underscores the idea that perhaps the explanation for the differences in attention spans found by the study is not that benzodiazepines cause reductions in attention spans but rather that meditation causes increases in attention spans.

In other words, this choice highlights the possibility that, when the study compared the attention spans of people who utilize benzodiazepines with the attention spans of people who meditate and found that attention spans of people who utilize benzodiazepines were shorter than the attention spans of the people who meditate, what was going on was not that the attention spans of people who utilize benzodiazepines are unusually short but rather that the attention spans of people who use benzodiazepines are short in comparison with the increased attention spans of people who meditate.

This concept of relatively bigger or smaller is an important one to master and be on the lookout for.Remember there is a difference between being unusually large (or small) and being large (or smaller) than something else.A rabbit is much larger than a mouse.However, a rabbit is not an unusually large animal.Right?
(B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that the effects of stress hormones do not cause people to have relatively short attention spans.
The passage describes benzodiazepines as medicines that suppress the effects of stress hormones.” In other words, the use of benzodiazepines results in reductions in the effects of stress hormones.

If the author is saying that the effects of stress hormones in the users of benzodiazepines are suppressed, then the author is certainly not making the assumption that the effects of stress hormones are the cause of the relatively short attention spans of people who utilize benzodiazepines.
(C) It rules out the conclusion that relatively short attention spans are the cause of chronic stress without any reason for ruling out that conclusion.
Let’s look again at the study.

We are told that “a study has found that people opting to utilize only benzodiazepines to address chronic stress are likely to have attention spans significantly shorter than the attention spans of people opting to utilize only meditation to address chronic stress.”

Notice that all of the people in both groups, those who utilize benzodiazepines and those who utilize meditation, are addressing chronic stress.Also, notice that we’re told that only those using benzodiazepines have the “shorter” attention spans.
So, the people using meditation are experiencing chronic stress but, apparently, do not have shortened attention spans.

Of course, the fact that the people using meditation are experiencing chronic stress but do not have “shorter” attention spans is a reason to rule out the conclusion that relatively short attention spans are “the cause” of chronic stress.

Thus, contrary to what this choice says, the argument does not rule out the conclusion that relatively short attention spans are the cause of chronic stress without any reason for ruling out that conclusion, because the argument does include a reason for ruling out that conclusion.
(D) It assumes without warrant that the people most apt to have relatively short attention spans are also those most apt to choose benzodiazepines over meditation.
The conclusion of the argument is that the use of benzodiazepines is the cause of the significantly reduced attention spans.

Notice that the idea that people most apt to have short attention spans are also those most apt to choose benzodiazepines over meditation reverses the factors, in that that idea suggests that people who choose benzodiazepines over meditation are most apt to have significantly reduced attention spansbeforethey choose benzodiazepines, rather thanafterorbecausethey choose benzodiazepines.
So, since this answer choice says that the argument assumes something that is in a way the opposite of the author’s conclusion, what this answer choice says is certainly not a flaw in the argument.
(E) It overlooks the fact that people who opt to use meditation to mitigate the effects of chronic stress might also utilize benzodiazepines.

To rule out this choice, we need only see the key detail that the author uses as evidence facts about “people opting to utilizeonlybenzodiazepines to address chronic stress” and “people opting to utilizeonlymeditation to address chronic stress.”
In other words, the author makes very clear that there is not any overlap between the two groups.That is, one group used only benzodiazepines, while the other group used only meditation.

So, clearly, the argument does not do what this choice says it does, because the argument involves facts about people who used one or the other and no facts about people who used one and also the other.

question.text: The hippocampus is a region of the brain.In a study of over 1,000 people experiencing depression, the percentage found to have hippocampuses that were smaller than normal given the size of their brains was significantly greater than the percentage of people in the general population with smaller than normal hippocampuses.Therefore, some people’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.The reasoning of the argument is most vulnerable to criticism that the argument

answers.text: draws a general conclusion about all people with hippocampus shrinkage from a limited sample of study participants.
considers the effects of depression on hippocampus shrinkage without also considering the effects of depression on other parts of the brain.
assumes without warrant that depression and shrinkage of the hippocampus are caused in the same way.
fails to consider that not all people will respond in the same way to a given treatment methodology.
overlooks the possibility that past negative experiences directly cause depression and directly cause hippocampus shrinkage.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Background: The hippocampus is a region of the brain.

Premise: In a study of over 1,000 people experiencing depression, the percentage found to have hippocampuses that were smaller than normal given the size of their brains was significantly greater than the percentage of people in the general population with smaller than normal hippocampuses.
Conclusion: Therefore, some people’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.

Notice that we have an argument based on cause-and-effect reasoning.The author makes the claim that some people’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.In order to make this claim, the author must assume that depression is the cause of these abnormally-sized hippocampuses.

What is her evidence for this claim? Her evidence comes in the form of a study, which found that, in depressed people, the percentage found to have hippocampuses that were smaller than normal given the size of their brains was significantly greater than the percentage of people in the general population with smaller than normal hippocampuses.Of course, we must remember that the fact that a study finds that two variables (being depressed and smaller than normal hippocampus) occur together does not on that basis alone allow us to conclude that a cause-and-effect relationship exists.

Also remember that whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain.
(A) draws a general conclusion about all people with hippocampus shrinkage from a limited sample of study participants.

A study of 1,000 people is a large study.Certainly, 1,000 people is not a limited sample of study participants.Be careful to not overplay the concept of the “small sample.” A study of 10 people might be a small sample; a study of 1,000 people is not.
(B) considers the effects of depression on hippocampus shrinkage without also considering the effects of depression on other parts of the brain.

The claim made by the author is that some people’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.Therefore, she need not consider the effects of depression on hippocampus shrinkage without also considering the effects of depression on other parts of the brain.In other words, her argument is a specific one, and she’s free to make specific argument about a specific brain structure without expanding the scope of her argument to a broad array of brain structures.
(C) assumes without warrant that depression and shrinkage of the hippocampus are caused in the same way.

The author’s claim is that some people’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.What this answer choice says seems to miss the point.The implication of what the passage says is that depression causes smaller than normal hippocampuses.So, the argument certainly does not involve assuming that depression and shrinkage of the hippocampus are caused in the same way.Rather it implies that something causes depression, and that depression in turn causes shrinkage of the hippocampus.
(D) fails to consider that not all people will respond in the same way to a given treatment methodology.

Notice that the author’s conclusion is that “somepeople’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.” To arrive at this conclusion, the author need not consider that not all people will respond in the same way to a given treatment methodology.
YOUR CHOICE(E) overlooks the possibility that past negative experiences directly cause depression and directly cause hippocampus shrinkage.

The author makes the claim that some people’s hippocampuses would be returned to normal size if their depression were treated successfully.Her evidence is the study, which found that, in depressed people, the percentage found to have hippocampuses that were smaller than normal given the size of their brains was significantly greater than the percentage of people in the general population with smaller than normal hippocampuses.

The fact that a study finds that two variables occur (being depressed and smaller than normal hippocampus) together does not on that basis alone allow us to conclude that a cause-and-effect relationship exists between those variables.

After all, it could very well be true that there is no cause-and-effect relationship at all between these variables, and the truth of the matter is, as this answer choice says, that past negative experiences directly cause depression and directly cause hippocampus shrinkage.

In other words, the author’s reasoning is flawed in that it overlooks the possibility that depression is not the cause at all of hippocampus shrinkage.Instead past negative experiences might actually be the cause depression and of hippocampus shrinkage.

question.text: The recent decisions by a number of the country’s provinces to offer incentives to induce cinematic production companies to operate in those provinces may make sense.All the same, the fact that the governor of Sartania has announced plans to offer such incentives to cinematic production companies indicates that he does not understand that, in order for a province’s economy to be stable, it must be diverse.The economy of Sartania is already dominated by only two industries, one of which is cinematic production.In making the above argument, the author fails to consider the possibility that

answers.text: the cinematic production industry represents a large percentage of the economy of Sartania.
the governor of Sartania’s purpose in offering the incentives is avoid a decrease in the degree of diversity by which Sartania’s economy is currently characterized.
companies in the other industry that dominates Sartania’s economy along with cinematic production will continue to operate in Sartania regardless of whether Sartania offers them incentives to do so.
the economies of some of the other provinces that are offering incentives to cinematic production companies are dominated by cinematic production even more than is the economy of Sartania.
the governor of Sartania is of the opinion that, at long as the province’s economy is growing, it is stable.

explanation_parts.text: (A) the cinematic production industry represents a large percentage of the economy of Sartania.

The author does not fail to consider what this choice says.Rather what this choice says is in line with what the author says about the economy of Sartania being dominated by only two industries, one of which is cinematic production.
YOUR CHOICE(B) the governor of Sartania’s purpose in offering the incentives is avoid a decrease in the degree of diversity by which Sartania’s economy is currently characterized.

The author sees that the governor has announced plans to offer incentives to cinematic production companies, which are in one of two industries that dominate the economy of Sartania.Having seen that, the author concludes that the governor does not understand that, in order for a province’s economy to be stable, it must be diverse.We can see why the author arrived at that conclusion.After all, an economy dominated by only two industries is not particularly diverse, and providing incentives to one of those two industries is not a path to making the economy more diverse.
That said, what possibility could the author have failed to consider?

The author could have failed to consider the possibility that Sartania is offering incentives to the cinematic production industry to keep the economy from becoming even less diverse.After all, if the cinematic production companies don’t keep operating in Sartania, it’s possible that Sartania’s economy will go from being dominated by two industries to being dominated by one industry.
Of course, in that case, the author’s conclusion about the governor would not be correct.
So, in making the argument, the author has failed to consider the possibility that what this choice says is true.

(C) companies in the other industry that dominates Sartania’s economy along with cinematic production will continue to operate in Sartania regardless of whether Sartania offers them incentives to do so.

Regardless of whether what this choice says is true, it remains the case that the governor is providing incentives to cinematic production companies, which are in an industry that already dominates Sartania’s economy, and that fact still provides effective support for the conclusion.
So, the author did not fail to consider what this choice says in making the argument.
(D) the economies of some of the other provinces that are offering incentives to cinematic production companies are dominated by cinematic production even more than is the economy of Sartania.

The fact that other provinces are doing the same thing Sartania is doing does not highlight a flaw in the argument.Those provinces could be making the same mistake the author says the governor of Sartania is making.
So, in making the argument, the author has not failed to consider this possibility, because, even if what this choice says is true, the argument still works.
(E) the governor of Sartania is of the opinion that, at long as the province’s economy is growing, it is stable.

What this choice says is in line with the conclusion that the governor does not understand that, in order for a province’s economy to be stable, it must be diverse.After all, the governor could be of the opinion that, at long as the province’s economy is growing, it is stablebecausethe governor does not understand that, in order for a province’s economy to be stable it must be diverse.So, the author’s argument would work just fine regardless of whether the author has considered what this choice says.

question.text: A recent study found that depression is significantly more common in those who abuse prescription pain medicine than it is in the general population of U.S.citizens.Given this evidence, the author of the study concluded that the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression.The reasoning of the study author is most vulnerable to criticism that the argument

answers.text: fails to consider the fact that most people who experience depression have not abused prescription pain medication.
overlooks the possibility that most people in the general U.S.population will neither experience depression nor abuse prescription pain medicine.
assumes that depression and prescription pain medicine abuse are not both induced by physical pain.
neglects to determine whether another psychological disorder, such as generalized anxiety, causes people to abuse prescription pain medicine.
overlooks factors other than abuse of prescription pain medicine that have been causally linked to depression.

explanation_parts.text: Breakdown of the Argument:
Premise: A recent study found that depression is significantly more common in those who abuse prescription pain medicine than it is in the general population of U.S.citizens.
Conclusion: Given this evidence, the author of the study concluded that the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression.
Notice that the author has presented us with a cause-and-effect argument.As we know well, we must be extremely critical of cause-and-effect arguments.

What is the author’s evidence for her cause and effect claim that the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression? Her evidence is that depression is significantly more common in those who abuse prescription pain medicine than it is in the general population of U.S.citizens.Notice that fact is just an observation, and an observation alone does not allow us, necessarily, to make a cause-and-effect claim.In other words, an observation is generally weak evidence upon which to base a cause-and-effect claim.

Remember that whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain.

However, couldn’t there be many other reasons, many other explanations, why these people are more likely to be depressed? Is abusing the prescription pain medicine really to blame? Is there more to the story? What might the author of the argument have missed?
(A) fails to consider the fact that most people who experience depression have not abused prescription pain medication.
Notice that the argument’s conclusion is that “the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression.”

Think about what the author is arguing.She’s not making the claim that there are not other causes of depression.In other words, it could very well be true that, for example, alcohol, lack of exercise, and poor diet all cause depression, and, at the same time, this fact would not conflict with her argument.

Therefore, in making her argument the author need not consider the fact that most people who experience depression have not abused prescription pain medication.After all, she is focused on the people who have abused prescription pain medication.
(B) overlooks the possibility that most people in the general U. S. population will neither experience depression nor abuse prescription pain medicine.

It’s important to see that this is not an argument about the likelihood of the general U.S.population experiencing depression or abusing prescription pain medicine.Rather, this argument makes the claim that the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression.In other words, the author is making a claim about the population of people who do abuse prescription pain medicine.Therefore, in making her argument, she need not examine the possibility that most people in the general U.S.population will neither experience depression nor abuse prescription pain medicine.
YOUR CHOICE(C) assumes that depression and prescription pain medicine abuse are not both induced by physical pain.

In making the cause-and-effect argument that the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression, the author assumes that there can be no other explanation for the observation made in the study.In other words, she assumes that her conclusion is correct and that there could not be any other explanation for the observation.

Such an assumption is probably a poor one at best.Almost certainly, it could be the case that depression and prescription pain medicine abuse are both induced by physical pain.After all, most people do not want to experience pain, and being in physical pain could very plausibly cause depression (in fact it often does).In addition, people experiencing physical pain would very plausibly be the people more apt to seek “relief” from prescription pain medicine, leading to abuse of this pain medicine.

Therefore, it could very well be true that the abuse of prescription pain medicine does not actually cause depression at all.Instead, it could be that the real cause of the depression is the physical pain.
So, we can see that the reasoning of the argument is flawed in that it assumes that depression and prescription pain medicine abuse are not both induced by physical pain.
(D) neglects to determine whether another psychological disorder, such as generalized anxiety, causes people to abuse prescription pain medicine.

It’s important to see that the author’s conclusion is that “the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression.” Notice that her argument is not concerned with causes of prescription pain medicine abuse.Therefore, she need not determine whether another psychological disorder, such as generalized anxiety, causes people to abuse prescription pain medicine.There could be many reasons why people abuse prescription pain medicine, and her argument would remain perfectly valid.
(E) overlooks factors other than abuse of prescription pain medicine that have been causally linked to depression.
It’s important to see that the author’s conclusion is that “the abuse of prescription pain medicine alone is enough to cause depression.”
Notice that she is not claiming that there are no other causes of depression.The scope of her argument is limited to concluding that pain medicine causes depression.

Therefore, what this answer choice says is not a valid criticism of the argument.After all, her argument could be complete valid, and, at the same time, there could be many other causes of depression.

question.text: The rate of lung cancer among residents of Lancaster is ten times as high as the rate in surrounding towns.Because of glacial activity thousands of years ago, large deposits of granite can be found in the earth under Lancaster.In addition to emitting beta and gamma radiation, both of which have the ability to cause health issues, granite is known to emit radon gas, which, when built up in sufficiently high concentrations in homes, is a well-demonstrated cause of lung cancer.Therefore, the town of Lancaster should immediately begin radon proofing all homes in Lancaster.Which of the following, if true, would point to a possible flaw in the author’s argument?

answers.text: The greatest concentrations of radon in structures are usually found in enclosed, below-ground areas, such as basements, crawl spaces, and tunnels.

The residents of the towns surrounding Lancaster are unusually health conscious and have significantly reduced their daily exposure to many carcinogens, including alcohol, fast food, and household cleaners.
Tests results indicate that, in most homes in Lancaster, the levels of beta and gamma radiation are equally as hazardous to human health as the levels of radon gas.
The cost to radon-proof all homes in Lancaster would far exceed the amount budgeted by the town for doing such work over the next year.
There are towns in the state in which Lancaster is located with as much granite under each as is under Lancaster with lung cancer rates 10 percent lower than Lancaster’s.

explanation_parts.text: Notice that we are presented with a cause-and-effect claim.The author believes that it is the radon that is the cause of the relatively high rate of lung cancer in the residents of Lancaster.Notice that her evidence is that granite rock, which is known to release radon, which is known to cause lung cancer, exists below the town.Is her reasoning sound?

Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she makes the assumption that her cause-and-effect claim is correct and that there cannot be an alternative explanation for her observation she seeks to explain.Is she considering all of the possible variables? Might there be another explanation for these relatively high rates of lung cancer seen in the residents of Lancaster?
(A) The greatest concentrations of radon in structures are usually found in enclosed, below-ground areas, such as basements, crawl spaces, and tunnels.

The passage states as fact that “… radon gas, which, when built up in sufficiently high concentrations in homes, is a well-demonstrated cause of lung cancer.” So, the thing to see is that we don’t care where the greatest concentrations of radon are.The argument is not saying that we need the “greatest concentrations" of radon to be found in the home for there to be a concentration of radon in the home sufficient enough to cause lung cancer.

It could be true that the highest concentrations of radon are in fact in the basements of homes, and at the same time, it could also be true that sufficiently high concentrations of radon do build up in homes, causing lung cancer.In other words, what this answer choices says does not conflict with the argument.

If you chose this answer choice, you likely made several unwarranted assumptions.For example, you might have assumed that, well, if the greatest concentrations of radon are found, for example, in the basement, then there would not be sufficient enough concentrations of radon in homes to cause lung cancer.However, let’s give that thought the greatest possible benefit of the doubt.Let’s say that 99 percent of radon stays in people’s basements, and 1 percent gets into homes.Is that 1 percent concentration of radon that is in people’s homes a concentration sufficient to cause lung cancer? Who knows, right? It could be the case that a 1 percent concentration is not enough to cause lung cancer.It could also be the case, on the other hand, that a 1 percent concentration of radon is a staggeringly high concentration, one more than sufficient to cause lung cancer.
Remember, it’s important that we do not make unwarranted assumptions.

CORRECT ANSWER(B) The residents of the towns surrounding Lancaster are unusually health conscious and have significantly reduced their daily exposure to many carcinogens, including alcohol, fast food, and household cleaners.

The author makes the claim that there is a cause-and-effect link between the radon gas that is emitted from granite rock and the relatively high rate of lung cancer in the town of Lancaster.As you know well at this point, we must be skeptical of any cause-and-effect claim.After all, perhaps there is an alternative explanation for why the lung cancer rate in Lancaster is ten times as high as in the surrounding towns, and what this answer choice suggests is that there may be an alternative cause for the differences in the rates of lung cancer.Notice that the author is not saying that the lung cancer rate in Lancaster is high or low.Rather, she is saying that the rate of lung cancer in Lancaster is ten times as high as the rate in the surrounding towns.The concept of “ten times as high” is one of relatively.In other words, the argument rests on a comparison.

Let’s think about how the rate of lung cancer could be “ten times as high as the rate in surrounding towns.” Of course, one way is that, yes, the rate of lung cancer in the surrounding towns is what would be considered “normal,” and the rate of lung cancer in Lancaster is exceptionally high compared to that normal rate of lung cancer.

However, another way for the rate of lung cancer in Lancaster to be ten times as high as the rate of lung cancer in the surrounding towns is for there to be something unique about those surrounding towns such that those residents have exceptionally low rates of lung cancer.Right? After all, if the residents in the surrounding towns have exceptionally low rates of lung cancer, the rate of lung cancer in the town of Lancaster could look exceptionally high, despite not being higher that what would be considered normal.

So, what this answer choice says, that the residents of the towns surrounding Lancaster are unusually health conscious and have significantly reduced their daily exposure to many carcinogens, including alcohol, fast food, and household cleaners, points to an alternative explanation for why there is such a large difference in the rates of lung cancer in the resident of Lancaster and the residents in the surrounding towns.

After all, these people in the surrounding towns, with their super healthy lifestyles lack of exposure to carcinogens, would have exceptionally low rates of lung cancer, making the rate of lung cancer in Lancaster appear to be exceptionally high.
In fact, given what this answer choice says, maybe there is not any Radon in the houses in Lancaster at all.
(C) Tests results indicate that, in most homes in Lancaster, the levels of beta and gamma radiation are equally as hazardous to human health as the levels of radon gas.

This is an argument about lung cancer.What does it mean to be hazardous to human health? We can’t assume that “hazardous to human health” means an alternative cause for lung cancer.There is no clear reason to believe that beta and gamma radiation are alternative causes of lung cancer.And even if they were, that fact would not lead us to believe that we should not radon proof homes.In other words, the fact that there could be other problems lurking does not lead us to believe that we should not try to fix the problem of Radon, if in fact a problem with Radon exists.What this answer says does not weaken the claim that radon is a primary cause of lung cancer.
(D) The cost to radon-proof all homes in Lancaster would far exceed the amount budgeted by the town for doing such work over the next year.

As always, to get Critical Reasoning questions correct, we must pay close attention to the conclusion.In this question, the conclusion is, “the town of Lancaster should immediately begin radon proofing all homes in Lancaster.” Notice that the argument does not say that all the homes must be radon proofed the first year.In addition, notice that this is not an argument about cost at all.This is an argument about what should be done.In other words, the author does not say “radon proof homes as long as we can do so in one year and within budget.” No, she says “the town of Lancaster should immediately begin radon proofing all homes in Lancaster.” Period.The fact that the cost would exceed the budget does not indicate that the plan should not be done.
YOUR CHOICE(E) There are towns in the state in which Lancaster is located with as much granite under each as is under Lancaster with lung cancer rates 10 percent lower than Lancaster’s.

This answer choice says that there are towns in the same state as Lancaster that have granite, and by the way, the rates of lung cancer in the residents of these town are 10 percent lower than the rate of lung cancer in the residents of Lancaster, meaning that the rates of lung cancer in these towns are nine times as high as the rates in the towns surrounding Lancaster.It is important that we can see this.Let’s break it down with some simple math.We’re told that the rate of lung cancer among residents of Lancaster is ten times as high as the rate in surrounding towns.Thus:
The rate of lung cancer in the towns surrounding Lancaster: x
The rate of lung cancer in Lancaster: 10x
These other towns with lung cancer rates 10 percent lower than Lancaster’s = 9x

So, what this answer choice really says that (1) there are other towns that also have granite below them, and (2) by the way the rate of lung cancer in these other towns is 9 times as high as the rate in the towns surrounding Lancaster.We see that what this answer choice says strengthens the argument by providing additional evidence that the granite, and the radon gas emitted from the granite, is in fact the cause of the lung cancer.Many people choose this answer as correct because they are not paying close enough attention to the details.They see “10 percent lower” and conclude that that statistic weakens the argument.However, as we just explained, 10 percent lower than in Lancaster in this case is nine times as high as in the surrounding towns.

question.text: The town of Barnaby Flats plans to begin spending 15 million dollars a year to improve its subway system in hopes of reducing traffic congestion in its downtown area.However, the town’s plan should be reconsidered.While improving the subway system is important, the town must also consider the many commuters who don’t use the subway but, rather, drive automobiles across the downtown bridge, driving across which can sometimes take up to 30 minutes.It is a known fact that only through expensive improvements to the bridge can bridge traffic congestion be reduced.Which of the following is the primary reason that the above argument is flawed?

answers.text: It fails to include among its considerations the fact that many people do not travel to the downtown area of Barnaby Flats.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that spending 15 million dollars a year to improve the downtown bridge would be sufficient to significantly reduce traffic congestion on the bridge.
It assumes without sufficient warrant that the town’s plan to reduce downtown traffic congestion by improving the subway system will not also significantly reduce downtown bridge traffic congestion.
It presupposes that allocating money to improving the subway system will leave insufficient funds available for improving the downtown bridge.
It fails to provide evidence showing that improvements made to the downtown bridge would result in a reduction of traffic congestion in the downtown area.

explanation_parts.text: The author concludes the following:
Conclusion: The town’s plan (to begin spending 15 million dollars a year to improve its subway system) should be reconsidered.

The support the author offers for that conclusion is basically that the town must also consider the many commuters who don’t use the subway and instead drive automobiles across the downtown bridge, traffic congestion on which can be reduced only through expensive improvements to the bridge.
Why would an argument that uses that premise to support that conclusion be flawed?
YOUR CHOICE(A) It fails to include among its considerations the fact that many people do not travel to the downtown area of Barnaby Flats.
The author’s focus is on the subway system and on improving the bridge.
So, why would he need to consider the fact that many people do not travel to the downtown area of Barnaby Flats? What difference would considering that fact make?

The author is basically advocating shifting spending from the subway to the bridge, and regardless of the fact that many people do not travel to the downtown area, given the argument’s premises having to do with the bridge, it could still make sense to conclude that the town should reconsider spending 15 million dollars a year on the subway.

So, while it is true that the argument does not include among its considerations the fact that many people do not travel to the downtown area of Barnaby Flats, that the argument does not include that fact among its considerations is not a flaw in the argument.
(B) It assumes without sufficient warrant that spending 15 million dollars a year to improve the downtown bridge would be sufficient to significantly reduce traffic congestion on the bridge.

The author does not say or assume that spending 15 million dollars a year will be enough to fix the traffic congestion on the bridge.The author simply says that the town’s plan to begin spending 15 million dollars a year to improve its subway system in the hopes of reducing traffic congestion in its downtown area should be reconsidered in light of the issues that drivers face while using the bridge.
In other words, we can see from his argument that he feels that all of part of the 15 million dollars, and perhaps even more, should be spent to fix the traffic congestion on the bridge.

Arriving at that conclusion does not require assuming 15 million dollars would be all the funding necessary to solve the issue on the bridge.After all, even if much more than 15 million dollars would be needed to reduce traffic on the bridge, it could still make sense to reconsider spending 15 million dollars to improve the subway system.
As always, we must pay close attention to the details of the passage.

(C) It assumes without sufficient warrant that the town’s plan to reduce downtown traffic congestion by improving the subway system will not also significantly reduce downtown bridge traffic congestion.
The argument never makes the assumption mentioned by this choice.
Notice that the passage says, “It is a known factthat only through expensive improvements to the bridge can bridge traffic congestion be reduced.”
Of course, if bridge traffic can be reducedonlythrough expensive improvements to the bridge, improving the subway system will not reduce downtown bridge traffic.

Therefore, we see that, contrary to what this choice says, the author does notassumethan that the town’s plan to reduce downtown traffic congestion by improving the subway system will not also significantly reduce downtown bridge traffic congestion.Rather, the authorstates a factthat indicates that improving the subway system will not also reduce downtown bridge traffic congestion.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) It presupposes that allocating money to improving the subway system will leave insufficient funds available for improving the downtown bridge.

Notice that, in making the argument, the author assumes that allocating money to improving the subway system will leave insufficient funds available for improving the downtown bridge.Otherwise, why would he be against the subway improvement plan? He’s against the plan to improve the subway because he feels that if this plan were to be set in motion, there wouldn’t be funding sufficient for making the “expensive improvements to the bridge” that are required for reducing bridge traffic.

However, his assumption is vulnerable to attack.We don’t know anything about the town’s budget.It could be the case that the town could spend the 15 million dollars a year to improve the subway and still have ample funding to fix the bridge.We can’t assume anything about the town’s budget.

As always, it’s important not to make unwarranted assumptions.It is also important to look up from battlefield.By this, we mean that we must not get hyper focused on details such that we neglect the big picture.It’s easy to focus on that 15-million-dollar number and somehow just assume that that number is the budget.Don’t do that.Instead, zoom out and view the battlefield on your own terms.What do you know? What do you not know? Try to view the situation holistically.
(E) It fails to provide evidence showing that improvements made to the downtown bridge would result in a reduction of traffic congestion in the downtown area.

Look at the first sentence of the passage, which says, “The town of Barnaby Flats plans to begin spending 15 million dollars a year to improve its subway system in hopes of reducing traffic congestion in its downtown area.” Notice that it’s the subway improvements that are supposed to improve the downtown traffic.The author never claims that the making changes to the bridge will reduce traffic congestion in the downtown area; the author claims that fixing the bridge will improve bridge traffic, not the downtown traffic.

Since the author never claims that improvements made to the downtown bridge would result in a reduction of traffic congestion in the downtown area, the fact that the author does not provide evidence to support that claim is not a flaw in the argument.
As always, we must pay close attention to the details of the passage and of the answer choices.

question.text: One theory about the people who lived on Easter Island is that their population was significantly reduced by wars between factions.However, that theory must be incorrect.Used for support for that theory has been the presence of the many sharpened stones found on the island.However, analysis of the characteristics of those sharpened stones has shown that they would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons and that it is likely that they were used as tools.The reasoning of the argument is most vulnerable to criticism that the argument

answers.text: proceeds as if lack of evidence that factions of the people on Easter Island warred among themselves proves that they did not.
explains why the sharpened stones were unlikely to have been used as weapons but does not explain how they would have functioned as tools.
does not take into account the variety of the shapes and sizes of the sharpened stones found on the island.
assumes without warrant that the people on Easter Island did not use tools as weapons of war.
fails to provide an alternate explanation for how the population on Easter Island decreased.

explanation_parts.text: Background: One theory about the people who lived on Easter Island is that their population was significantly reduced by wars between factions.
Premise: Used for support for that theory has been the presence of the many sharpened stones found on the island.

Premise: However, analysis of the characteristics of those sharpened stones has shown that they would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons and that it is likely that they were used as tools.
Conclusion: That theory (that war resulted in reduction in the population of the people who lived on Easter Island) must be incorrect.
YOUR CHOICE(A) proceeds as if lack of evidence that factions of the people on Easter Island warred among themselves proves that they did not.
