Notice that the conclusion of the argument is that the theory (that war resulted in reduction in the population of the people who lived on Easter Island)must be incorrect.
The statement that the theory must be incorrect is a strong statement that implies that the evidence providedprovesthat the theory is incorrect.

However, the only premise of the argument is that the sharpened stones were not likely used as weapons or parts of weapons.That premise does not prove that the theory is incorrect.It simply indicates that one thing used to support the theory, the presence of the sharpened stones, does not provide good support for the theory.
Of course, the fact that the presence of the sharpened stones does not provide good support for the theory does not prove that the theory must be incorrect.
So, the argument provides no evidence that proves that the theory is incorrect.It simply indicates that there is a lack of evidence supporting the theory.
So, this argument is flawed in that it proceeds as lack of evidence that factions of the people on Easter Island warred among themselves proves that they did not.
(B) explains why the sharpened stones were unlikely to have been used as weapons but does not explain how they would have functioned as tools.
The argument’s conclusion is that the theory that war resulted in reduction in the population of the people who lived on Easter Island must be incorrect.

Notice that the author offers as evidence the fact that “analysis of the characteristics of those sharpened stones has shown that they would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons and that it is likely that they were used as tools.” Ok, so these rocks would not be great for weapons, and it is more likely that they were tools.Isn’t that fact pretty good evidence to support the conclusion? Would the author also have to explain how the stones would have functioned as tools.
No, because, regardless of how the stones would have functioned as tools, the fact that they would have been ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons supports the conclusion.
So, while it is true that the argument does not explain how the stones would have functioned as tools, the fact that the argument does explain that is not a flaw in the argument.
(C) does not take into account the variety of the shapes and sizes of the sharpened stones found on the island.
The passage says that “analysis of the characteristics of those sharpened stones has shown that they would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons.”
Remember, we must take as fact what is said in the passage, and what the passage says implies that that “analysis” applies to all of the stones.

In fact, what makes sense is that the variety of the shapes and sizes of the sharpened stones found on the island was taken into account by whoever did the analysis of the stones that showed that the stones would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons.

So, what the argument says implies that taking into account the variety of shape and sizes of the stones has already been done.Thus, the fact that the argument does not bring up the variety of the shapes and sizes of the sharpened stones is not a flaw in the argument
(D) assumes without warrant that the people on Easter Island did not use tools as weapons of war.
Notice that the passage says, “analysis of the characteristics of those sharpened stones has shown that they would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons.”

That fact stated by the passage serves as evidence that the sharpened stones were not used as weapons of war.After all, it stands to reason that stones that would have beenineffectiveas weapons or parts of weapons were not likely used as weapons of war.In fact, if the stones would have been rather ineffective as weapons or parts of weapons, then using them as weapons of war would have been basically impossible.

Thus, contrary to what this choice says, the argument does not “assume without warrant” that tools, i.e., the sharpened stones, were not used as weapons of war.Rather, the argument includes evidence that clearly indicates that the stones were not used as weapons of war.
(E) fails to provide an alternate explanation for how the population on Easter Island decreased.

The argument need not provide an alternate explanation for how the population decreased in order to support the conclusion that the theory that war resulted in reduction in the population on Easter Island must be incorrect.After all, we can point out that a theory about something is incorrect without knowing what the correct theory is.

question.text: Three years ago, to deter drivers from speeding, the city of Montclair began using sophisticated HighwayCam camera systems to identify and ticket vehicles traveling over the speed limit.However, it is clear that Montclair’s camera systems are not effectively identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles, since, in the past six months, despite record numbers of vehicles using the highways, the numbers of vehicles ticketed by these camera systems have fallen to their lowest levels since the introduction of the cameras.Of the following, the best criticism of the argument above is that it overlooks the possibility that

answers.text: more vehicles using the highways have autonomous drive capabilities, which, among other things, allow a car to control its speed once that speed has been set by the driver.
greater numbers of vehicles on the highways make it more challenging for the camera systems to identify and ticket vehicles exceeding the speed limit.
the drivers of some speeding vehicles slow to under the speed limit when they approach the cameras.
HighwayCam cameras used by nearby towns have worked flawlessly, identifying and ticketing large numbers of speeding vehicles.
the camera systems on the highways are working exactly as planned.

explanation_parts.text: Three years ago, to deter drivers from speeding, the city of Montclair began using sophisticated camera systems to identify and ticket vehicles traveling over the speed limit.

In the past six months, record numbers of vehicles have used the highways, but the number of vehicles ticketed by the camera systems has fallen to its lowest level since the introduction of the cameras.
Given these facts, the author claims that Montclair’s camera systems are not effectively identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles.
What might the author have overlooked?
YOUR CHOICE(A) more vehicles using the highways have autonomous drive capabilities, which, among other things, allow a car to control its speed once that speed has been set by the driver.
Notice that what this choice says does not indicate that fewer cars are speeding.After all, drivers could presumably set the autopilots in their cars to go well above the speed limit.
At the same time, what this choice says could instead mean that drivers set the autopilots to speeds at or below the speed limit.

It’s important not to make unwarranted assumptions when reading statements that could be interpreted in multiple ways.In other words, this answer choices offers us a bi-directional statement.We just don’t know exactly how it impacts the argument.

So, while we might be tempted to decide that this choice indicates that the author has overlooked the fact that, because of the availability of autonomous driving capabilities, fewer drivers are speeding, this choice does not actually indicate that fewer drivers are speeding.
So, this choice does not highlight a flaw in the argument.
(B) greater numbers of vehicles on the highways make it more challenging for the camera systems to identify and ticket vehicles exceeding the speed limit.
What this answer choice says certainly does not point to a flaw in the argument.

After all, if the author’s conclusion that these camera systems are not effectively identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles is correct, what this answer choice says helps explain why it is the case that the cameras are not effectively identifying and ticketing speed vehicles: greater numbers of vehicles on the highways make it more challenging for the camera systems to identify and ticket vehicles exceeding the speed limit.
So, rather than point to a flaw in the argument, this choice explains why what the author concludes is true would be true.
(C) the drivers of some speeding vehicles slow to under the speed limit when they approach the cameras.
What this answer choice says certainly does not point to a flaw in the argument.

After all, if the author’s conclusion that these camera systems are not effectively identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles is correct, what this answer choice says helps explain why it is the case that the cameras are not effectively identifying and ticketing speed vehicles: the drivers of some speeding vehicles slow to under the speed limit when they approach the cameras.
So, rather than point to a flaw in the argument, this choice explains why what the author concludes is true would be true.
(D) HighwayCam cameras used by nearby towns have worked flawlessly, identifying and ticketing large numbers of speeding vehicles.
The fact that HighwayCam cameras used by nearby towns have worked flawlessly does not highlight a flaw in this argument.

Regardless of how well HighwayCam cameras have worked elsewhere, the author’s evidence, that, despite record numbers of vehicles using the highways, the numbers of vehicles ticketed by Montclair’s camera systems have fallen, supports the conclusion that Montclair’s camera systems are not effectively identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles.
After all, even if all other HighwayCam cameras are working flawlessly, something could be keeping Montclair’s camera systems from identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) the camera systems on the highways are working exactly as planned.
Notice that the passage says that the city of Montclair is using the camera systems “to deter drivers from speeding.”

Meanwhile, the author concludes that the cameras are not effectively identifying and ticketing speeding vehicles.In making the argument, the author is assuming that there could be no other explanation for the fact that the numbers of vehicles ticketed by the camera systems have fallen to their lowest levels since the introduction of the cameras.

However, what if the camera systems are working exactly as planned, deterring drivers from speeding? Couldn’t the cameras’ working as planned be a rather good reason why the number of vehicles ticketed by the camera systems has fallen to its lowest level since the introduction of the cameras? In other words, what if drivers of vehicles know that these camera systems were put in place, and because the drivers do not want to get tickets, they have stopped speeding? Couldn't this be a solid alternative explanation for why the cameras are not giving many tickets? For sure, it could.
Therefore, what this answer choice says points to something that the author may have overlooked, this possible alternative explanation for the observed decrease in the numbers of vehicles ticketed.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?In recent months, marathon running has come under fire after five runners have died during these long running races.Those against marathon running claim that running such long distances places an unusually large strain on the heart, leading sometimes to heart problems, which can lead to death during these races.In fact, these critics are advising that people rethink any plans to participate in marathons.However, the advice dispensed by these critics is quite misguided because __________.

answers.text: in any given year, less than 0.5 percent of the population will run a marathon
the five runners who died had been advised by their physicians not to partake in marathon running
many other sports carry with them risks to the heart significantly greater than those associated with marathon running
marathons always staff expert emergency services personnel who can respond to a wide range of race day problems
marathon runners are, on average, significantly more fit than people in general

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer to this question will answer the following question:
Why is the advice dispensed by these critics (rethink any plans to participate in marathons) quite misguided?
(A) in any given year, less than 0.5 percent of the population will run a marathon

We’re told that “five runners have died during these long running races.” We also know that “those against marathon running claim that running such long distances places an unusually large strain on the heart.” Would it make any difference that in any given year, less than 0.5 percent of the population will run a marathon? Of course not.What if 0.5 percent represents 100,000 people? If marathon running is dangerous, wouldn’t we still be concerned that these 100,000 people are putting themselves at risk?
CORRECT ANSWER(B) the five runners who died had been advised by their physicians not to partake in marathon running

If it is true that the five runners who died had been advised by their physicians not to partake in marathon running, then we have good reason to believe that the advice dispensed by these critics is quite misguided.After all, in this case, we’d have good reason to think that these five runners who died did not do so because of the dangers of running marathons and that, instead, they died because they were not well enough to be running marathons.
(C) many other sports carry with them risks to the heart significantly greater than those associated with marathon running

We’re seeking a reason why the advice dispensed by these critics (rethink any plans to participate in marathons) is quite misguided.What this choice says, that “many other sports carry with them risks to the heart significantly greater than those associated with marathon running,” does not provide such a reason.After all, the fact that there are other sports with more risks than running marathons does not mean that the critics’ advice is misguided.
(D) marathons always staff expert emergency services personnel who can respond to a wide range of race day problems
Even if what this answer choice says is true, the fact remains that five runners have died during marathons.

In other words, even given what this answer choice says, the critics of marathon running might still be correct because, regardless of the fact that there are emergency services personnel at marathons, five people have died.
So, the critics’ advice still seems to be supported by the facts.
YOUR CHOICE(E) marathon runners are, on average, significantly more fit than people in general
Even if what this answer choice says is true, the fact remains that five runners have died during marathons.

In other words, even given what this answer choice says, the critics of marathon running might still be correct because, regardless of the fact that marathon runners are, on average, significantly more fit than people in general, five people have died.
So, the critics’ advice still seems to be supported by the facts.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?For a study, some scientists compared the lifespans of people on restricted calorie diets with those of people not on such diets.Although most studies have shown that, on average, people who follow restricted calorie diets outlive people not on restricted calorie diets by 9 years, this study found that, on average, participants on restricted calorie diets lived 2 years less than those not on restricted calorie diets.Given these results, these scientists are recommending against following a restricted calorie diet.However, following the recommendation of these scientists is not advisable because __________.

answers.text: some people not on restricted calorie diets will outlive some of those on restricted calorie diets
the results of studies of the effects of dietary choices often seem to conflict with the results of similar studies
none of the study participants engaged in regular exercise, which is proven to increase longevity
many of the participants in the study were on restricted calorie diets for the purpose of managing health problems
2 years is not a large difference in lifespan in percentage terms, given that the average human lifespan is 79 years

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that most studies have shown that, on average, people who follow restricted calorie diets outlive people not on restricted calorie diets.What is surprising about this study is that it found that, on average, people on restricted calorie diets lived 2 years less than those not on restricted calorie diets.Given those results, the scientists are recommending against following a restricted calorie diet.
The blank at the end of the passage is preceded by “because.” So, we can see that the correct answer will answer the following question:
Why is following the recommendation of these scientists not advisable?
(A) some people not on restricted calorie diets will outlive some of those on restricted calorie diets

The scientists recommend against following a restricted calorie diet, and this choice says that some people who do not follow restricted calorie diets will outlive some of those on restricted calorie diets.Thus, what this choice says is essentially that some people who follow the scientists’ recommendation will outlive some who do not.
So, what this choice says is certainly not information indicating that following these scientists’ recommendation is not advisable.

In fact, this choice is a conclusion supported by the study results.After all, if the study participants on restricted calorie diets lived, on average, 2 years less than those not on restricted calorie diets, then it follows that some people not on restricted calorie diets will outlive some of those on restricted calorie diets.
Of course, since we are not looking for a conclusion supported by the study results, this choice is not the correct answer.
(B) the results of studies of the effects of dietary choices often seem to conflict with the results of similar studies

This choice is tempting because what it says both is plausible and follows from what the passage says.After all, the passage says that the results of this study of the effects of dietary choices are different from the results of similar studies.So, the passage provides an example of the phenomenon presented by this choice.

At the same time, this choice is not the correct answer, because the fact that the results of studies of the effects of dietary choices often seem to conflict with the results of similar studies does not indicate that following the recommendation based on the results of this study is not advisable.After all, the fact that results often seem to conflict indicates nothing about which results are correct.Maybe this study is the best one.
(C) none of the study participants engaged in regular exercise, which is proven to increase longevity

In order to avoid being fooled into choosing this choice, we have to consider whether this choice works with the entire passage, not just whether this choice seems to work in the sentence that ends with the blank.
Here’s what we get if we drop this choice into the sentence that ends with the blank:
However, following the recommendation of these scientists is not advisable because none of the study participants engaged in regular exercise, which is proven to increase longevity.
That sentence makes sense on its own.The issue is that it doesn’t make sense with the rest of the passage.

After all, the scientists’ recommendation is not to do exactly what the study participants did, i.e., not engage in regular exercise.The scientists’ recommendation is simply not to follow a restricted calorie diet, and the fact that none of the study participants engaged in regular exercise does not weaken the support that the study results provide for the scientists’ recommendation.
YOUR CHOICE(D) many of the participants in the study were on restricted calorie diets for the purpose of managing health problems

This choice provides a strong reason why following the recommendation of these scientists is not advisable, because, if many of the study participants were on restricted calorie diets for the purpose of managing health problems, then the study results are suspect.

After all, unfortunately, we would expect people using special diets to manage health problems to have reduced longevity.In other words, this answer choice indicates that the reason the study participants on restricted calorie diets lived, on average, 2 years less than those not on restricted calorie diets is likely that the participants on restricted calorie diets were sickbeforethey began following those diets, notbecausethey were on restricted calorie diets.

Thus, given what this choice says, the study results don’t really indicate that following a restricted diet results in a relatively short lifespan, and the scientists’ recommendation is based on a flawed study.Thus, following the scientists’ recommendation is not advisable.
(E) 2 years is not a large difference in lifespan in percentage terms, given that the average human lifespan is 79 years
The fact that 2 years is not a large difference in lifespan in percentage terms does not indicate why following the scientists’ recommendation is not advisable.
After all, 2 years is significantly more than nothing, and there’s no reason to believe that adding 2 years to one’s lifespan is “not advisable.”
Thus, the study results still support following the scientists’ recommendation even though 2 years is not a large difference in lifespan in percentage terms.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Although mayonnaise has long been a staple condiment in the United States, sales of mayonnaise have declined over the past decade.Food historians blame the decline in sales on characteristics of millennials, claiming that a product like mayonnaise has no place in the millennial diet because people of this generation have a strong desire to eschew the norms, both social and dietary, of prior generations, with the goal of demonstrating a rebel-like uniqueness of taste, form, and style.However, the conclusion of these food historians is incorrect.The actual reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales is that __________.

answers.text: millennials are known to eat foods with a wide spectrum of flavors, textures, and consistencies
mayonnaise was primarily used to disguise food flaws resulting from poor food standards, which have been replaced by more stringent ones over the past decade
members of generations older than the millennial generation have strong desires to live in ways different from ways in which previous generations have lived, but have continued to consume mayonnaise
mayonnaise has become increasingly marketed and advertised in all metropolitan areas of the United States over the past decade, with mayonnaise jars found in a high percentage of food ads
grocery stores catering to people who purchase high-end food products, such as cashew yogurt and kombucha tea, have continued to stock mayonnaise despite a decrease in purchases of mayonnaise

explanation_parts.text: The passage tells us that sales of mayonnaise have declined over the past decade.Food historians blame the decline in sales on characteristics of millennials, claiming that a product like mayonnaise has no place in the millennial diet because people of this generation have a strong desire to eschew the norms, both social and dietary, of prior generations.
But then the passage takes a turn and says “However, the conclusion of these food historians is incorrect.The actual reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales is __________.”
So, we’re seeking an answer choice that provides us with the actual reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales.In other words, the correct answer will answer the following question:
What is the actual reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales?
(A) millennials are known to eat foods with a wide spectrum of flavors, textures, and consistencies

Notice that this choice basically indicates that millennials are known to eat a wide variety of foods.Of course, the fact that millennials eat a variety of foods is not a reason why sales of mayonnaise have declined.After all, mayonnaise has its own unique flavor, texture, and consistency.So, given what this choice says, it would make sense that millennials would eat mayonnaise along with the other foods they eat.

If you chose this choice because you felt that the fact that millennials eat a wide variety of foods makes millennials different from members of preceding generations and, thus, somehow less likely to consume mayonnaise than members of preceding generations, remember that we have to be careful not to make up unsupported stories.Neither the passage nor this choice indicates that the fact that millennials eat a wide variety of foods makes them different from other generations.
YOUR CHOICE(B) mayonnaise was primarily used to disguise food flaws resulting from poor food standards, which have been replaced by more stringent ones over the past decade

If it is true that “mayonnaise was primarily used to disguise food flaws resulting from poor food standards, which have been replaced by more stringent ones over the past decade,” then this fact could certainly be the actual reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales.
After all, if there is no longer a need for the use of mayonnaise to disguise food flaws resulting from poor food standards, then we can see why mayonnaise sales have declined.

(C) members of generations older than the millennial generation have strong desires to live in ways different from ways in which previous generations have lived, but have continued to consume mayonnaise
This choice has the wrong effect.

This choice shows that members of other generations were similar to millennials in having strong desires to live in ways different from ways in which previous generations have lived, yet mayonnaise sales did not decline.So, this choice provides some confirmation that the conclusion of the food historians – that the decline in mayonnaise sales has been caused by millennials’ desire to be different – is incorrect.

That effect is not the effect we need.We need a reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales, and although it may be true that “members of generations older than the millennial generation have strong desires to live in ways different from ways in which previous generations have lived, but have continued to consume mayonnaise,” that fact would not provide the “actual reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales.”
After all, this answer choice explains that these older generations have continued to consume mayonnaise.We’re seeking the reason for the decline in mayonnaise sales.
(D) mayonnaise has become increasingly marketed and advertised in all metropolitan areas of the United States over the past decade, with mayonnaise jars found in a high percentage of food ads
Notice that what this answer choice says is certainly relevant to the topic of the passage but takes us in the direction opposite of where we need to go.

After all, if “mayonnaise has become increasingly marketed and advertised in all metropolitan areas of the United States over the past decade, with mayonnaise jars found in a high percentage of food ads” then why have mayonnaise sales declined?
Remember, to arrive at correct answers to Critical Reasoning questions, we have to go beyond considering topic and relevance to considering the effects on the arguments of what choices say.
(E) grocery stores catering to people who purchase high-end food products, such as cashew yogurt and kombucha tea, have continued to stock mayonnaise despite a decrease in purchases of mayonnaise

That fact that “grocery stores catering to people who purchase high-end food products, such as cashew yogurt and kombucha tea, have continued to stock mayonnaise despite a decrease in purchases of mayonnaise” does not explain why mayonnaise sales have declined.Rather, this answer choice describes what has occurred in the context of the decline in mayonnaise sales.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?One approach that a more developed country may attempt to use to reduce the flow of illicit drugs from less developed countries into the hands of its citizens is threatening to cease to provide financial aid for economic development to less developed countries in which significant production of illicit drugs occurs and the governments of which do not take significant action to reduce the domestic production of illicit drugs.However, following through on such threatening by withholding such aid is problematic, in that doing so can result in increased flow of illicit drugs from a less developed country because, __________.

answers.text: in many cases, the governments of less developed countries are significantly corrupted by the influences of illicit drug industries located in those countries
if they lack funds for starting new enterprises, citizens of less developed countries may increase their involvement in already ongoing enterprises
while foreign aid may facilitate a country’s economic development, it is possible for a country to develop without such aid
in some developing countries, the plants from which illicit drugs are produced have played significant parts in the countries’ cultures for centuries
even when a government of a developing country has the will to reduce production of illicit drugs, demand for such drugs may spur increased production

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer to this question will answer the following question:
Why can withholding financial aid for economic development result in increased flow of illicit drugs from a less developed country?
(A) in many cases, the governments of less developed countries are significantly corrupted by the influences of illicit drug industries located in those countries
Notice that this choice explains the wrong thing.

Whereas the correct answer has to explain why withholding financial aid for economic development can result inincreasedflow of illicit drugs from a less developed country, this choice explains in general why it might bedifficult to get governments of developing countries to reducethe domestic production of illicit drugs: the governments are so corrupted that they have no interest in reducing the production of illicit drugs.
The fact the governments are corrupted does not explain why withholding aid would cause an increase in flow of illicit drugs.
YOUR CHOICE(B) if they lack funds for starting new enterprises, citizens of less developed countries may increase their involvement in already ongoing enterprises

Financial aid for economic development is meant to help citizens of less developed countries start new enterprises.Of course, if such aid is withheld, citizens of less developed countries may lack funds for starting new enterprises.

Thus, if, as this choice says, citizens of less developed countries who lack funds for starting new enterprises may increase their involvement in already ongoing enterprises, then we can see why withholding financial aid for economic development can result in increased flow of illicit drugs from a less developed country.After all, in less developed countries in which significant production of illicit drugs occurs, production of illicit drugs is undertaken by already ongoing enterprises.So, by fostering a situation in which citizens of less developed countries lack funds for starting new enterprises, withholding financial aid for economic development can result in those citizens’ increasing their involvement in the production of illicit drugs.
So, this choice perfectly completes the passage.
(C) while foreign aid may facilitate a country’s economic development, it is possible for a country to develop without such aid
The fact that a country can develop without aid does not explain why withholding aid would result increased flow of illicit drugs from a country.
(D) in some developing countries, the plants from which illicit drugs are produced have played significant parts in the countries’ cultures for centuries
This choice explains the wrong thing.

What this choice might explain is why people in some less developed countries are involved in the production of illicit drugs: these people have been accustomed to using the plants from which the drugs are produced.
At the same time, what this choice says would not explain why withholding aid would result in anincreasein flow of illicit drugs from such a country.
(E) even when a government of a developing country has the will to reduce production of illicit drugs, demand for such drugs may spur increased production
Notice that what this choice does is bring up a different reason for an increase in the flow of illicit drugs: demand.
The fact that demand can spur production of illicit drugs does not explain why withholding aid can result in increased flow of illicit drugs.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?For decades, the financial statements of the Jetstar Corporation were produced by an in-house team of accountants.However, two years ago, the CFO outsourced the statements’ production to an outside team of accountants.Since then, the number of material errors found in the company’s financial statements has declined from an average of 7 per fiscal year, in the statements composed prior to the change, to an average of 4 per fiscal year, in the statements composed after the change.The CFO attributes this change to the greater skill of the outside accounting team.However, the CFO’s conclusion is likely incorrect because __________.

answers.text: the Jetstar CFO recently significantly improved the process via which the company checks for material errors in its completed financial statements
two years ago, the CEO of Jetstar redefined what the company considered a material accounting error with the result that, now, only the most egregious errors are considered material

since they work only for Jetstar, the in-house accountants have a deeper understanding of Jetstar's financial statements than is had by the outside accountants, who create statements for many companies
Jetstar’s previous average of 7 material errors per fiscal year set of financial statements is considerably lower than the industry average of 11 material errors per fiscal year set
the outsourced accounting team has been known to make mistakes in creating financial statements for companies large and small

explanation_parts.text: We see that, two years ago, the Jetstar CFO outsourced the statements’ production to an outside team of accountants.What happened then? We see that the number of material errors found in the company’s financial statements has declined from an average of 7 per fiscal year, in the statements composed prior to the change, to an average of 4 per fiscal year, in the statements composed after the change.
Then, the Jetstar CFO makes the cause-and-effect claim that this drop in material errors is attributable to the greater skill of the outside accounting team.
In making this cause-and-effect claim, the Jetstar CFO assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.
Is this assumption a good one? Might there be an alternative explanation for the observation made?
Notice that, in answering this question, we’re seeking to weaken the Jetstar CFO’s cause-and-effect argument.
(A) the Jetstar CFO recently significantly improved the process via which the company checks for material errors in its completed financial statements

It’s important to see that what this answer choice says is about what occursafterthe financial statements are produced.After all, the company would check for material errors in its “completed financial statements” only after the team of accountants has produced them.
Thus, what this choice says has no impact on what we know about the process ofproducingthe statements.
So, this choice does not cast any doubt on the CFO’s conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(B) two years ago, the CEO of Jetstar redefined what the company considered a material accounting error with the result that, now, only the most egregious errors are considered material
The Jetstar CFO has made the cause-and-effect claim that the drop in material errors is attributable to the greater skill of the outside accounting team.

However, if what this answer choice says is true, then we have an alternative explanation as to why the number of material errors declined after the switch.After all, if the CEO of Jetstar redefined the definition of a material error in the way described by this choice, we would certainly expect to see a decline in the number of material errors, because many errors that previously would have been considered material errors will not be considered material errors now.
Notice that what this answer choice says attacks an assumption made by the Jetstar CFO.

(C) since they work only for Jetstar, the in-house accountants have a deeper understanding of Jetstar's financial statements than is had by the outside accountants, who create statements for many companies

The passage tells us that “the number of material errors found in the company’s financial statements has declined from an average of 7 per fiscal year, in the statements composed prior to the change, to an average of 4 per fiscal year, in the statements composed after the change.” In other words that statement is a fact.

Notice that what regardless of what this choice says about the in-house accountants understanding of Jetstar's financial statements, the fact about material errors remains true, and the support it provides for the conclusion is unaffected.
Be careful not to choose as correct an answer choice that seem to argue with the facts provided in the passage.
(D) Jetstar’s previous average of 7 material errors per fiscal year set of financial statements is considerably lower than the industry average of 11 material errors per fiscal year set

Notice that the key comparison in this argument is between the number of material errors per fiscal year made before the switch from the in-house team of accountants to the outside team of accountants and the number made after the switch.
What this answer choice says focuses on a different, unrelated comparison between Jetstar’s average and the industry average.
So, this answer choice certainly does not weaken the argument.
(E) the outsourced accounting team has been known to make mistakes in creating financial statements for companies large and small

Notice that what this answer choice says does not change the fact that, two years ago, the CFO outsourced the statements’ production to an outside team of accountants, and since then, the number of material errors found in the company’s financial statements has declined from an average of 7 per fiscal year, in the statements composed prior to the change, to an average of 4 per fiscal year, in the statements composed after the change.
Be careful not to choose as correct an answer choice that seem to argue with the facts provided in the passage.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?Two years ago, Oaktree High School began offering an optional critical thinking class.Since that time, the exam scores of the school’s students who have completed the class have been, on average, significantly higher than those of the students who have not completed the class.Having observed that pattern, the school’s principal plans to make the class mandatory for all students starting next year, to improve the exam performance of all students at the school.However, the principal is unlikely to achieve the objective by implementing that plan because __________.

answers.text: none of the school’s teachers plan to change their exams in response to the school’s making the critical thinking class mandatory
many of the school’s current students will have completed most of high school by the time they complete the critical thinking class
ideally, a person would develop his or her critical thinking skills on an ongoing basis, while the class lasts for only one school semester
critical thinking skills have always been necessary to Oaktree High School students’ scoring relatively high on exams
at Oaktree High School, especially academically motivated students generally make up the vast majority of the students who sign up for optional classes

explanation_parts.text: Two years ago, Oaktree High School for the first time began offering an optional critical thinking class to its students.Having noticed that the exam scores of the students who have completed the class have been significantly higher than those of the students who have not completed the class, the principal plans to make the class mandatory for all students with the following objective:
Objective: The exam performance of all students at the school will improve.

Notice that the principal is essentially making the cause-and-effect claim that the critical thinking class is responsible for the relatively high exam scores of the students who have completed the class.
So, in coming up with the plan, the Oaktree High School Principal assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed pattern.
Is this assumption a good one? Might there be an alternative explanation for the observation made?
The correct answer will answer the following:
Why is the principal unlikely to achieve the objective by implementing that plan?
(A) none of the school’s teachers plan to change their exams in response to the school’s making the critical thinking class mandatory

Notice that the principal’s expectation that the students’ exam performance will improve is based on the fact that students who completed the critical thinking class scored higher on exams than other students scored.

Since, presumably, all students took the same exams, the factor that caused the higher scores of the students who completed the critical thinking class must not have been changes to the exams.It must have been something else, such as the students’ having completed the critical thinking class.
So, the fact that the teachers don’t plan to change their exams does not tell us why the principal is unlikely to achieve the objective.

In fact, if anything, it makes us more confident that the principal will achieve the objective, by confirming that the teachers won’t make the exams more difficult in response to what’s going on with the critical thinking class.
(B) many of the school’s current students will have completed most of high school by the time they complete the critical thinking class

Notice that, regardless of whether many of the school’s current students will have completed much of high school by the time they take the critical thinking class, those students’ exam performance during the remainder of high school could improve as a result of their completing the critical thinking class.
So, what this choice says has no impact on the expectation that the principal will achieve the objective.
(C) ideally, a person would develop his or her critical thinking skills on an ongoing basis, while the class lasts for only one school semester

Notice that what this choice says suggests that there is a better plan: developing critical thinking skills on an ongoing basis.In doing so, it tempts us to say to ourselves, “Well, the class is not a great idea after all because, ideally, people would be developing their critical thinking skills on an ongoing basis.”

However, as you know well at this point, such logic is flawed.The fact that there is a better plan or an alternative plan out there does not mean that the plan proposed by the Oaktree High School principal is not a good one.After all, regardless of what this choice says, it still makes sense that making the class mandatory could result in improvement in the students’ exam performance.
As always, be vigilant for the “better plan / alternative plan” trap choices.
(D) critical thinking skills have always been necessary to Oaktree High School students’ scoring relatively high on exams
Notice that this choice matches the passage in wording and topic but does not logically complete the passage.
Yes, this choice is about the effect of critical thinking on exam performance, just as the passage is.At the same time, this effect of this choice is the opposite of the effect we need.
The correct answer will show why the principal is unlikely to achieve the objective.

However, by confirming that critical thinking skills are necessary for scoring relatively high on exams, this choice tends to confirm that having students take a critical thinking class will result in improvement in student exam performance.
So, rather than indicate that the plan will not work, this choice indicates that it will.
YOUR CHOICE(E) at Oaktree High School, especially academically motivated students generally make up the vast majority of the students who sign up for optional classes

If, at Oaktree High School, especially academically motivated students generally make up the vast majority of the students who sign up for optional classes, then we have an alternative reason why students who have completed the optional critical thinking class have scored higher on exams than other students: the students who have completed the class are especially academically motivated.

In other words, given what this choice says, it seems likely that some students’ being especially academically motivated has caused those students both to sign up for the critical thinking class and to score higher than other students on exams.

Of course, the fact that motivation, rather than completing the critical thinking class, is likely the cause of the higher scores of the students who have completed the class is certainly a reason why the principal is unlikely to achieve the objective.After all, if motivation, rather than the critical thinking class, is likely the cause of the higher scores, then having all students take the critical thinking class is not likely to have any effect at all on student exam performance.
So, this choice effectively answers the question “Why is principal unlikely to achieve the objective?”

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Over the past few decades, houses in Strenland have, on average, become much more energy efficient, in that the quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent.However, on average, household energy intensity, in other words, the units of energy used per household per year, is approximately the same in Strenland as it was a few decades ago, since __________.

answers.text: population growth has resulted in there being in Strenland many more households now than there were a few decades ago
the size of the average house in Strenland has increased substantially over the past few decades
the rate at which the cost of fuel used by households in Strenland has increased over the past few decades has been greater than the overall rate of price inflation
the average number of people per household in Strenland has not changed substantially over the past few decades
many people in Strenland are not aware that houses there are, on average, much more energy efficient than houses there were a few decades ago

explanation_parts.text: Notice, that although this question is a Complete the Passage question, in answering it, what we’re really seeking to do it so resolve the paradox presented to us.So, the correct answer will explain why (1) the quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent, but at the same time, (2) the units of energy used per household per year is about the same as it was a few decades ago.
In other words, if homes are more energy efficient now then why are they using the same amount of energy?
(A) population growth has resulted in there being in Strenland many more households now than there were a few decades ago
Notice that the statistic household energy intensity (the units of energy usedper householdper year) is not affected by the total number of houses in Strenland.

So, even with that this answer choice says, we still don’t know why the quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent, yet the units of energy used per household per year has remained unchanged.
In other words, what this answer choice says provides us with no new information that could resolve the paradox.
YOUR CHOICE(B) the size of the average house in Strenland has increased substantially over the past few decades

Notice that we are told that the quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent.At the same time, the units of energy used per household per year is about the same as it was a few decades ago.

The explanation for this outcome could certainly be that the size of the average house in Strenland has increased substantially over the past few decades because although these homes have become more energy efficient, they have also become larger, and larger homes burn larger amounts of energy.
In other words, yes, the homes have become more energy efficient, but if they have also become larger, then it makes sense that the amount of energy each house uses has not changed.
(C) the rate at which the cost of fuel used by households in Strenland has increased over the past few decades has been greater than the overall rate of price inflation

Notice that what this answer choice describes is a price increase.However, the passage is about units of energy themselves.In other words, the passage is about units of energy, not the cost of that energy.So, this answer choice discusses the wrong concept.

As a result, even with what this answer choice says, we still don’t know why quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent, yet the units of energy used per household per year has remained unchanged.
(D) the average number of people per household in Strenland has not changed substantially over the past few decades

Even with what this answer choice says, we still don’t know why quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent, yet the units of energy used per household per year has remained unchanged.

Notice that the fact that “the quantity of energy used per square foot of house floor space per year has decreased by close to 30 percent” would remain true regardless of how many people live in these houses.In other words, if two people live in each house, that statistic is a fact, and if 200 people live in those houses that statistic is a fact.
(E) many people in Strenland are not aware that houses there are, on average, much more energy efficient than houses there were a few decades ago

In answering this question, we’re seeking an explanation for the phenomenon presented in the passage.Of course, that many people in Strenland are not aware that houses there are, on average, much more energy efficient than houses there were a few decades ago does not provide that explanation.After all, what the people are or are not aware of does not impact the situation presented.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?Over the last two years, the finance department of the Veggie Burger Company has grown by a number of employees greater than the number by which any other department at the company has grown.As a result, the company comptroller is claiming that the salaries of members of the finance department now account for the largest percentage of the total employee salary costs at the company accounted for by any single department.The company CEO is in agreement with the comptroller because __________.

answers.text: the finance department now also has the greatest number of company employees and the highest average salary per employee
the median employee salary of all the other employees at the company is considerably less than the median salary of the employees in the finance department
the finance department has the top ten most highly paid employees at the company
the total number of employees at the company has decreased over the past two years
the average salary of all employees at the company is currently the highest it has been at any time in the company’s history

explanation_parts.text: We know that, over the last two years, the finance department has grown by a number of employees greater than the number by which any other department at the company has grown.Because of that growth, the comptroller is claiming that the salaries of the finance department now account for the largest percentage of the total employee salary costs at the company accounted for by any single department.We now are seeking a reason for why the CEO would be in agreement with the comptroller.
YOUR CHOICE(A) the finance department now also has the greatest number of company employees and the highest average salary per employee

Given what this choice says, we can certainly see why the CEO would agree with the comptroller that the salaries of members of the finance department now account for the largest percentage of the total employee salary costs at the company accounted for by any single department.

After all, if the finance department has the greatest number of company employees and the highest average salary per employee, it also must have the highest salary total.After all, average = total/number.So, if the number is the highest, in order for the average to be the highest, the total must be the highest.
(B) the median employee salary of all the other employees at the company is considerably less than the median salary of the employees in the finance department

Notice that this information on median salaries does not enable us to determine the total of the salaries of members of the finance department or any other department, because we still don’t know how many members any department has.
So, even if what this choice says is true, some other department could have many more members and thus a much higher salary total.
(C) the finance department has the top ten most highly paid employees at the company

Notice that we need information that indicates that the finance department as a whole accounts for the largest percentage of the total employee salary costs at the company accounted for by any single department.

The fact that ten individuals in the finance department are the most highly paid employees at the company does not tell us about the department as a whole.After all, there could still be a department that is much bigger and paid much more in total than the finance department.
(D) the total number of employees at the company has decreased over the past two years

We know from the passage that the finance department has grown by a number of employees greater than the number by which any other department at the company has grown.What this choice say indicates only that some other department or departments must have shrunk, rather than grown.

OK, so a department or some departments shrank.That’s not a reason to believe that the salaries of members of the finance department now account for the largest percentage of the total employee salary costs at the company accounted for by any single department.After all, there could still be a department that is much bigger and much more highly paid than the finance department.
(E) the average salary of all employees at the company is currently the highest it has been at any time in the company’s history

What this choice says has no bearing on the percentage of the total employee salary costs at the company accounted for by the finance department.This choice tells us that salaries at the company have increased overall.It does not provide information on any particular department.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Research shows that increased government investment in early childhood education results in increased educational achievement and productivity of children and adults.Meanwhile, because people who have experienced early childhood education tend to as adults be healthier and less likely to be involved in crime than are those who have not experienced such education, governments that invest in early childhood education generally recoup the costs over time.Even so, elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education because __________.

answers.text: even taxpayers who do not have children can benefit extensively from the results of investments in early childhood education
a person’s having experienced early childhood education does not serve as a guarantee that that person will be healthy and will not become involved in crime
the benefits of an official’s having invested in early childhood education are not expected to become apparent until long after the next time that official is up for reelection
governments often spend taxpayer funds in ways such that the costs will never be recouped in financial terms
it is possible for a person to be productive, healthy, and not involved in crime without having gone through an early childhood education program

explanation_parts.text: Notice that although this question is in the form of a Complete the Passage question, in answering it, we’re seeking to resolve the paradox presented to us.

If (1) increased government investment in early childhood education results in increased educational achievement and productivity of children and adults, and if people who experience early childhood education tend to as adults be healthier and less likely to be involved in crime than are those who did not experience such education, governments that invest in early childhood education generally recoup the costs over time, then why is it the case that (2) elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education?
(A) even taxpayers who do not have children can benefit extensively from the results of investments in early childhood education
Notice that we are seeking a reason why elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education in light of the facts presented.

What this answer choice says, that even taxpayers who do not have children can benefit extensively from the results of investments in early childhood education, would provide a reason why officialswouldinvest taxpayer funds in early childhood education in light of the facts presented.In other words, this answer choice presents an additional reason why officials would want to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education.

Of course, what this answer choice says goes in the wrong direction; we are seeking to understand why officials do not want to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education.It’s important not to choose as correct an answer choice that deepens the paradox.
(B) a person’s having experienced early childhood education does not serve as a guarantee that that person will be healthy and will not become involved in crime

What this answer choice says, that a person’s having experienced early childhood education does not serve as a guarantee that that person will be healthy and not become involved in crime, would not explain why elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education because the passage already tells us that (1) people who experience early childhood education tend to as adults be healthier and less likely to be involved in crime than are those who did not experience such education.
Notice that what this answer choice says does not change the facts provide in the passage.In other words, even with that this answer choice says, the paradox remains unresolved.
YOUR CHOICE(C) the benefits of an official’s having invested in early childhood education are not expected to become apparent until long after the next time that official is up for reelection
Notice that what this answer choice says would definitely explain why elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education in light of the facts presented.

Even though research shows that increased government investment in early childhood education results in increased educational achievement and productivity of children and adults, and even though people who experience early childhood education tend to as adults be healthier and less likely to be involved in crime than are those who did not experience such education, if it is true that the benefits of an official’s having invested in early childhood education are not expected to become apparent until long after the next time that official is up for reelection, that fact would be good reason why elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education.

After all, if what this answer choice says is true, then the elected officials would not benefit from the investment of taxpayer funds in early childhood education, even though that investment would be good for society.
(D) governments often spend taxpayer funds in ways such that the costs will never be recouped in financial terms

While what this answer choice says may seem to be a reason why an official would be reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education, the fact is that the passage has already stated that governments that invest in early childhood education generally recoup the costs over time.

Remember that we are seeing a reason why an official would be reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education despite of the fact that governments that invest in early childhood education generally recoup the costs over time.It’s important not to choose as correct an answer choice that attempts to argue with the premises provided to us in the passage.

Another way to think about why this answer choice is wrong is that we don’t care about what “often” happens when governments spend taxpayer funds.Rather what we are about are the facts provides to us about the specific situation described in the passage.
(E) it is possible for a person to be productive, healthy, and not involved in crime without having gone through an early childhood education program

The fact that it is possible for a person to be productive, healthy, and not involved in crime without having gone through an early childhood education program would not explain why an official would be reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education.

After all, the passage tells us that research shows that increased government investment in early childhood education results in increased educational achievement and productivity of children and adults.In addition, we are told that because people who experience early childhood education tend to as adults be healthier and less likely to be involved in crime than are those who did not experience such education, governments that invest in early childhood education generally recoup the costs over time.
So then why are elected officials are often reluctant to invest taxpayer funds in early childhood education?

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the reasoning presented?When teenagers first receive licenses to drive automobiles, even though most have spent many hours training, most of them are still relatively unskilled at handling an automobile.Over the six months following receiving their licenses, most become much more skilled at handling an automobile, through spending more hours behind the wheel in a variety of situations.However, this fact should not be taken to mean that, six months after a teenager becomes licensed to drive an automobile, that teenager is likely much safer when driving than that teenager was when that teenager first received the license, since __________.

answers.text: licenses are given to all teenagers who fulfill licensing requirements, even though some are much more fully prepared to drive safely than are others
people generally do not fully develop skill in judging risk until they reach their mid-twenties in age
as teenagers become more comfortable with driving unsupervised, many become less averse to taking unwarranted risks while driving
some teenagers receive their licenses before they build their driving skills by taking formal driver education classes
in spite of the known dangers of texting while driving an automobile, many teenagers regularly do so

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that, over the six months following receiving their licenses, most teenagers become much more skilled at handling an automobile.
The correct answer will answer the following question:

Why should this fact (over the six months following receiving their licenses, most teenagers become much more skilled at handling an automobile) not be taken to mean that, six months after a teenager becomes licensed to drive an automobile, that teenager is likely much safer when driving than that teenager was when that teenager first received the license?
(A) licenses are given to all teenagers who fulfill licensing requirements, even though some are much more fully prepared to drive safely than are others

To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to notice that regardless of what this choice says, we would still expect teenagers who have become more skilled at handling an automobile to have become safer when driving.After all, even though some teenagers who have received licenses are much more fully prepared to drive safely than are others, all of them should improve their driving over the first six months of licensed driving.
So, why shouldn’t this fact be taken to mean that, six months after they receive their licenses, teenagers are likely much safer when driving than they were when they first received their licenses?
YOUR CHOICE(B) people generally do not fully develop skill in judging risk until they reach their mid-twenties in age

Notice that what we have to explain is not why teenagers are not great at judging risk.What we have to explain is why the fact that, over the six months following receiving their licenses, most teenagers become much more skilled at handling an automobile should not be taken as indicating that they are safer when driving than they were when the first received their licenses.
This choice does not explain why.After all, even if teenagers have not fully developed skill in judging risk, their becoming more skilled at driving would be expected to make them safer.
So, why doesn’t it make them safer?
CORRECT ANSWER(C) as teenagers become more comfortable with driving unsupervised, many become less averse to taking unwarranted risks while driving

This choice explains why the fact that, over the six months following receiving their licenses, most teenagers become much more skilled at handling an automobile should not be taken as indicating that they are safer when driving than they were when the first received their licenses.

After all, if, as teenagers become more comfortable with driving unsupervised, many become less averse to taking unwarranted risks while driving, then even as teenagers’ driving skills are improving, they may be increasingly taking unwarranted risks.Of course, in that case, six months after receiving their licenses, teenagers may be less safe rather than safer than they were when the first received their licenses.
So, this choice answers the question and logically completes the passage.
(D) some teenagers receive their licenses before they build their driving skills by taking formal driver education classes

Notice that what we have to explain is not why teenagers are not good at driving.What we have to explain is why the fact that, over the six months following receiving their licenses, most teenagers become much more skilled at handling an automobile should not be taken as indicating that they are safer when driving than they were when the first received their licenses.

This choice does not explain why.After all, even if some teenagers have not built their driving skills by taking formal driver education classes, their becoming more skilled at driving would be expected to make them safer.
So, why doesn’t it make them safer?
(E) in spite of the known dangers of texting while driving an automobile, many teenagers regularly do so

Notice that what we have to explain is not why teenagers are dangers to themselves and others on the roads.What we have to explain is why the fact that, over the six months following receiving their licenses, most teenagers become much more skilled at handling an automobile should not be taken as indicating that they are safer when driving than they were when the first received their licenses.
This choice does not explain why.After all, even if many teenagers regularly text while driving, teenagers becoming more skilled at driving would be expected to make them safer.
So, why doesn’t it make them safer?

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Many scientists are convinced that the effects of an impact of an asteroid that crashed into Earth 65 million years ago resulted in the immediate extinction of cretaceous plankton.Seeming to contradict this position is the fact that fossil remains of cretaceous plankton have been found in sediments deposited on top of glass pellets that were created by and distributed by the impact of the asteroid.This fact is not, however, clear evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth for a period of time after the asteroid impact since __________.

answers.text: scientists estimate that the effects of the asteroid’s impact resulted in the extinction of up to 70 percent of all species living on the planet at the time of impact
dust clouds generated by the asteroid blocked the sun’s rays from reaching Earth for months, preventing photosynthesis, the method by which cretaceous plankton created food
the impact of the asteroid stirred up sea floor sediments, causing previously buried fossils to rise into the ocean water and resettle later
evidence indicates that, at some point after the asteroid impact, microorganisms, such as cretaceous plankton, largely disappeared from the oceans
fossil evidence seems to indicate that bryozoans, animals that survived by eating plankton, did not die off at the time of the impact of the asteroid

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that, scientists are convinced that the impact of an asteroid resulted in the immediate extinction of cretaceous plankton.At the same time, sediments deposited on top of glass pellets that were created by and distributed by the impact of the asteroid contain remains of cretaceous plankton.

The fact that these sediments, which must have been deposited after the impact of the asteroid, contain remains of cretaceous plankton seems to indicate that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.
The correct answer will answer the following question:

Why is the fact that fossil remains of cretaceous plankton have been found in sediments deposited on top of glass pellets that were created by and distributed by the impact of the asteroid not clear evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth for a period of time after the asteroid impact?
(A) scientists estimate that the effects of the asteroid’s impact resulted in the extinction of up to 70 percent of all species living on the planet at the time of impact

Regardless of what this choice says, the fact that remains of cretaceous plankton have been found in sediments deposited on top of the glass pellets created and distributed by the asteroid impact still seems to be evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.

After all, even if most other species went extinct after the asteroid impact, cretaceous plankton could have continued to live on Earth, and we don’t have any other explanation for the presence of cretaceous plankton remains in the sediments on top of the glass pellets.
(B) dust clouds generated by the asteroid blocked the sun’s rays from reaching Earth for months, preventing photosynthesis, the method by which cretaceous plankton created food

Regardless of what this choice says, the fact that remains of cretaceous plankton have been found in sediments deposited on top of the glass pellets created and distributed by the asteroid impact still seems to be evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.

After all, even if dust clouds generated by the asteroid blocked the sun’s rays from reaching Earth for months, preventing photosynthesis, it’s possible that some cretaceous plankton could have survived and continued to live on Earth, and we don’t have any other explanation for the presence of cretaceous plankton remains in the sediments on top of the glass pellets.
YOUR CHOICE(C) the impact of the asteroid stirred up sea floor sediments, causing previously buried fossils to rise into the ocean water and resettle later

This choice provides an answer to the question of why the presence of cretaceous plankton remains in the sediments on top of the glass pellets is not clear evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.The answer could be that the cretaceous plankton remains found in the sediments were created before the asteroid impact, were stirred up by the asteroid impact, and later settled on top of the glass beads.

So, by providing an alternative explanation for the presence of the cretaceous plankton remains in sediments on top of the glass beads, this choice explains why the presence of the cretaceous plankton remains in sediments on top of the glass beads is not clear evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.
(D) evidence indicates that, at some point after the asteroid impact, microorganisms, such as cretaceous plankton, largely disappeared from the oceans

The fact that “at some point after the asteroid impact” microorganisms, such as cretaceous plankton, largely disappeared from the oceans, does not mean that cretaceous plankton did not continue to live on Earth for a period of time after the asteroid impact.After all, cretaceous plankton could have both continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact and eventually disappeared.

So, regardless of what this choice says, the fact that remains of cretaceous plankton have been found in sediments deposited on top of the glass pellets created and distributed by the asteroid impact still seems to be evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.
(E) fossil evidence seems to indicate that bryozoans, animals that survived by eating plankton, did not die off at the time of the impact of the asteroid

Notice that, if anything, the fact that fossil evidence seems to indicate that bryozoans, animals that survived by eating plankton, did not die off at the time of the impact of the asteroid tends to indicate that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact, and served as a source of food for bryozoans.

So, what this choice says certainly does not indicate that the presence of cretaceous plankton in the sediments is not evidence that cretaceous plankton continued to live on Earth after the asteroid impact.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?Farm subsidies are monies given by the government to farmers to help them offset monetary business losses and continue to operate their farms even when they would not earn profits if market forces were to prevail.The justification for these subsidies is that they help in keeping food prices at affordable levels.For many decades, the government has supported the use of farm subsidies, claiming that these subsidies have met their objective.However, when farm subsidies are more closely examined, it becomes clear that they are not the remedy that the government claims them to be because __________.

answers.text: economists have shown time and again that the government has available mechanisms substantially better than farm subsidies for keeping food prices at affordable levels
there have been numerous times over the past few decades during which farmers have made handsome profits selling their goods and services in the open market without receiving farm subsidies
some farmers, even with the help of farm subsidies, have been unable to keep their farms in operation because of high interest rates on farm loans demanded by banks
the passing of the subsidies’ costs directly from the government to taxpayers makes the real cost of food unaffordable
most watch-dog groups that examine farm subsidies agree that these subsidies exist almost solely because of lobbying by strong pro-farming lobbyists that lobby the government to keep them in place

explanation_parts.text: In answering this Complete the Passage question, we’re seeking an answer choice that provides a reason why, when farm subsidies are more closely examined, it becomes clear that they are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.
(A) economists have shown time and again that the government has available mechanisms substantially better than farm subsidies for keeping food prices at affordable levels
We’re seeking an answer choice that provides a reason for why when farm subsidies are more closely examined, it becomes clear that they are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.

Notice that this choice brings up “better plans” than farm subsidies to keep food prices at affordable levels.Of course, the fact that there are better plans out there would not explain why farm subsidies are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.
(B) there have been numerous times over the past few decades during which farmers have made handsome profits selling their goods and services in the open market without receiving farm subsidies

Notice that we’re told that “Farm subsidies are monies given by the government to farmers to help them offset monetary business losses and continue to operate their farms even when they would not earn profits if market forces were to prevail.” In other words, these farm subsidies are supposedly important when “farms would not earn profits if market forces were to prevail.”

However, what this answer choice says, that “there have been numerous times over the past few decades during which farmers have made handsome profits selling their goods and services in the open market without receiving farm subsidies” describes the times when it would appear that farm subsidies are not needed.
The fact that farmers have made profits at times when farm subsidies were not needed is not a reason why farm subsidies are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.
(C) some farmers, even with the help of farm subsidies, have been unable to keep their farms in operation because of high interest rates on farm loans demanded by banks
