The fact that “some farmers, even with the help of farm subsidies, have been unable to keep their farms in operation because of high interest rates on farm loans demanded by banks” does not mean that farm subsidies have not helped these farmers.After all, it could be the case that farm subsidies have helped these farmers a lot, but even with the help that farm subsidies have provided, these farmers have been unable to keep their farms in operation because of high interest rates on farm loans demanded by banks.
Therefore, what this answer choice says would not provide a reason why farm subsidies are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.
YOUR CHOICE(D) the passing of the subsidies’ costs directly from the government to taxpayers makes the real cost of food unaffordable

We’re told that the justification for farm subsidies is thatthey help in keeping food prices at affordable levels.In other words, farm subsidies are considered a “remedy” used to help in keeping food prices at affordable levels.

We know that the government has supported the use of farm subsidies, claiming that these subsidies have met their objective.At the same time, in answering this question, we’re seeking a reason why when farm subsidies are more closely examined, it becomes clear that they are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.

What this answer choice says, that “the passing of the subsidies’ costs directly from the government to taxpayers makes the real cost of food unaffordable” would certainly provide such a reason, right? After all, if the passing of the subsidies’ costs directly from the government to taxpayers makes the real cost of food unaffordable, then these farm subsidies certainly are not helping to keep food prices at affordable levels.

(E) most watch-dog groups that examine farm subsidies agree that these subsidies exist almost solely because of lobbying by strong pro-farming lobbyists that lobby the government to keep them in place

Remember that, in answering this question, we’re seeking an answer choice that provides a clear reason for why, when farm subsidies are more closely examined, it becomes clear that they are not the remedy that the government claims them to be.The fact that most watch-dog groups that examine farm subsidies agree that these subsidies exist almost solely because of lobbying by strong pro-farming lobbyists that lobby the government to keep them in place does not provide such a reason.

Notice that what this answer choice provides is an opinion on why farm subsidies exist.We’re not seeking such an opinion.We’re seeking a logical reason to believe that the subsidies don’t do what they are claimed to do.

question.text: In the country of Azulistan, statisticians determined that, in general, the greater the number of churches in a city, the greater tends to be the number of murders in that city.Since this information was presented to the country’s government, its leaders have begun passing laws that limit the number of churches that can simultaneously operate in any of Azulistan’s cities, believing that, in doing so, they will achieve a reduction in murders.However, the reasoning of the leaders is most likely flawed because __________.

answers.text: the numbers of churches in cities in Azulistan are closely correlated to the sizes of the cities' populations
no murder has even been committed inside a church in Azulistan
it overlooks the fact that the murder rate in Azulistan has decreased without there being any limits on the numbers of churches in cities
there are other, more impactful initiatives that the leaders of Azulistan could undertake to reduce the number of murders in its cities
reductions in the number of churches in cities are likely to inconvenience many people, who will have to travel long distances to attend church because of the reductions

explanation_parts.text: Notice that the argument has one premise, which is essentially that, in Azulistan, the greater the number of churches in a city, the greater tends to be the number of murders in that city.

After seeing that statistic, the leaders of Azulistan have begun passing laws that limit the number of churches that can simultaneously operate in any of Azulistan’s cities, believing that, in doing so, they will achieve a reduction in murders.So, these leaders clearly believe that there is a cause-and-effect link between churches and the number of murders in its cities.That is, the leaders believe that churches cause murders.
In believing that that cause-and-effect relationship exists, the leaders of Azulistan assume that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed pattern.
Is this assumption a good one? Might there be an alternative explanation for the observation made?
Notice that, in answering this question, we’re seeking to weaken the case for the leaders’ cause-and-effect belief.
YOUR CHOICE(A) the numbers of churches in cities in Azulistan are closely correlated to the sizes of the cities' populations

If it is true that “the number of churches in cities in Azulistan is closely correlated to the size of the cities’ populations,” then we have a plausible alternative explanation for why, in Azulistan, the greater the number of churches in a city, the greater tends to be the number of murders in that city.

After all, if the number of churches in cities in Azulistan is closely correlated to the size of the cities’ populations, then as the number of citizens living in a city increases, so too does the number of churches in that city.In other words, more churches = more people.When there are more people in a city, we, sadly, expect to see a greater number of murders in that city than if there were fewer people in that city.

Therefore, we can see that, given what this answer choice says, as the number of churches increases in a city, the murder rate might be higher simply because there are more people in that city.In other words, perhaps there is no cause-and-effect relationship between the number of churches and the number of murders; perhaps the leaders are simply observing a correlation.
So, this choice explains why the reasoning of the leaders is most likely flawed.
(B) no murder has even been committed inside a church in Azulistan

Notice that the leaders are not making the claim that the murders are taking place within the churches.They are simply making the claim that the churches are the cause of these murders.Therefore, what this answer choice says does not highlight a flaw in the reasoning of the leaders.
(C) it overlooks the fact that the murder rate in Azulistan has decreased without there being any limits on the numbers of churches in cities

The fact that the murder rate has decreased without there being any limits on the number of churches does not mean that churches don’t cause murders.After all, for all we know, the number of churches has decreased without laws limiting the number of churches.
Alternatively, churches are still causing murders, but something else that causes murders has changed.The leaders are not claiming that churches are the only factor causing murders.
So, what this choice says does not cast doubt on the leaders’ cause-and-effect belief or highlight a flaw in their reasoning.
(D) there are other, more impactful initiatives that the leaders of Azulistan could undertake to reduce the number of murders in its cities

Notice that what this answer choice says is essentially that there are better plans for reducing the number of murders in Azulistan.The fact that there might be a better plan or an alternative plan does not weaken the case for the plan set forth by the leaders of Azulistan.After all, the leaders are not claiming that there is not a better way to reduce the number of murders.The leaders are simply proposing one plan that they believe will help to reduce the number of murders.
(E) reductions in the number of churches in cities are likely to inconvenience many people, who will have to travel long distances to attend church because of the reductions

What this answer choice says would not weaken the argument because the leaders never make the claim that their plan won’t inconvenience people.In fact, the leaders never say that the citizens will like the plan or that the plan won’t have some problems or that the plan won’t cause some friction.The leaders are claiming simply that the plan will reduce the number of murders.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Because the climate related benefits of reducing the production of greenhouse gases by using such approaches as improving cycling infrastructure, reducing meat production, and increasing the percentage of power produced via sustainable methods will not become apparent until after the passage of multiple decades, many elected officials are not motivated by these benefits to call for the implementation of such approaches.Nevertheless, those same officials have pushed for the implementation of such approaches because __________.

answers.text: any negative consequences for economic growth of implementing approaches to reduce greenhouse gas production tend be short term and quickly fade
many private sector managers would not without government involvement do anything to reduce greenhouse gas production
fully implementing approaches that result in reduced production of greenhouse gases can take many years
many voters are not convinced that human caused production of greenhouse gases is actually having significant effects on global temperatures
improvements in the health of citizens resulting from implementing greenhouse gas reduction approaches will quickly result in financial benefits for governments

explanation_parts.text: (A) any negative consequences for economic growth of implementing approaches to reduce greenhouse gas production tend be short term and quickly fade

Notice that the passage tells us essentially that the officials are not motivated by the long-term climate related benefits of the approaches.However, even in light of that fact, the officials are pushing for them anyway.Notice that what this answer choice says does not provide an explanation for why the officials are pushing for them anyway.

After all, if the officials are not motivated to push for the approaches, why would the fact that any negative consequences for economic growth of implementing approaches to reduce greenhouse gas production tend be short term and quickly fade be a reason why they would be pushing for the approaches?
The fact that negative consequences tend to be short term might clear the way for the officials to implement the approaches, but it would not motivate them to implement the approaches.
(B) many private sector managers would not without government involvement do anything to reduce greenhouse gas production

The passage states clearly that the climate related benefits will not become apparent until after the passage of multiple decades.As a result, many elected officials are not motivated by these benefits to call for the implementation of such approaches.
What this answer choice says, that many private sector managers would not without government involvement do anything to reduce greenhouse gas production, does not change the facts presented to us.
(C) fully implementing approaches that result in reduced production of greenhouse gases can take many years
Notice that what this answer choice says is bi-directional; we could make up a story to explain why they would or would not push for the implementation of such approaches.

After all, it might be true that, because fully implementing approaches that result in reduced production of greenhouse gases can take many years, the officials need to get started on the approaches right away.Or, it could be that, because fully implementing approaches that result in reduced production of greenhouse gases can take many years, the officials are just not interested in pushing for them.
(D) many voters are not convinced that human caused production of greenhouse gases is actually having significant effects on global temperatures

Notice that what this answer choice says would explain why officials wouldnot havepushed for the implementation of such approaches.However, our goal in answering this question is to explain why theyhavepushed for the implementation of such approaches.
YOUR CHOICE(E) improvements in the health of citizens resulting from implementing greenhouse gas reduction approaches will quickly result in financial benefits for governments

In answering this question, we are seeking to explain why officials have pushed for the implementation of such approaches even though the climate related benefits will not become apparent until after the passage of multiple decades.
What this answer choice says would provide an explanation.

After all, if improvements in the health of citizens resulting from implementing greenhouse gas reduction approaches will quickly result in financial benefits for governments, then that information is a good reason for officials to push for the implementation of such approaches even though the climate related benefits will not become apparent until after the passage of multiple decades.

question.text: Which most logically completes the passage?To combat air pollution, the government of Derask instituted a program through which people who purchase new electric cars, which produce essentially no air pollution, get subsidies that cover part of the cost of the cars.As a result, sales of electric cars soon outpaced sales of gasoline powered cars.However, the program has not reduced the amount of air pollution in Derask but rather merely moved its greatest concentrations from some areas to others __________.

answers.text: as, while many people who live in urban areas have been quick to take advantage of the subsidies and buy electric cars, most people in more rural areas continue to prefer gasoline powered cars
since, because electric cars have to be charged repeatedly during long trips, electric car owners making long trips often rent gasoline powered cars to use for making those trips
because most people in countries surrounding Derask still drive gasoline powered cars
because the vast majority of the electricity in Derask is produced through the use of coal powered generators located in sparsely populated regions of the country
as many people in Derask are still driving relative old cars that they purchased long before the electric car subsidy program was instituted

explanation_parts.text: In this question, the word “as,” “since,” or “because” appears at the beginning of each choice rather than before the blank.Nevertheless, since all the choices begin in essentially the same way, we can see that the correct answer will answer the following question that begins with “why.”
Why has the program not reduced the amount of air pollution in Derask but rather merely moved its greatest concentrations from some areas to others?
(A) as, while many people who live in urban areas have been quick to take advantage of the subsidies and buy electric cars, most people in more rural areas continue to prefer gasoline powered cars

Notice that what this answer choice says is that some people (those in urban areas) have been quick to advantage of the subsidies and buy electric cars.And, yes, at the same time, some other people (those in more rural areas) continue to prefer gasoline powered cars (there has been no change here).

Since some people have taken advantage of the subsidies, we would expect to see a decrease in the amount of air pollution in Derask.Thus, what this answer choice says cannot explain why the program has not reduced the amount of air pollution in Derask.
(B) since, because electric cars have to be charged repeatedly during long trips, electric car owners making long trips often rent gasoline powered cars to use for making those trips
We are told that the sale of electric cars soon outpaced sales of gasoline powered cars.Therefore, the fact is that some people now own and operate electric cars.

What this answer choice says, that since, because electric cars have to be charged repeatedly during long trips, electric car owners making long trips often rent gasoline powered cars to use for making those trips, does not change the fact that more people own and operate electric cars now than did in the past.

So then, why has the program has not reduced the amount of air pollution in Derask but rather merely moved its greatest concentrations from some areas to others? What this answer choice says cannot resolve the paradox.
(C) because most people in countries surrounding Derask still drive gasoline powered cars
We know from the passage that sales of electric cars soon outpaced sales of gasoline powered cars.In other words, we know that some people in Derask are now driving electric cars.
What this answer choice says tells us that the driving habits of most people in countries surrounding Derask have not changed; they still drive gasoline powered cars.
OK, but in Derask more people are driving electric cars.So, why did the program not reduce the amount of air pollution in Derask? What this answer choice says cannot resolve the paradox.
YOUR CHOICE(D) because the vast majority of the electricity in Derask is produced through the use of coal powered generators located in sparsely populated regions of the country

If it is true that sales of electric cars soon outpaced sales of gasoline powered cars and also that the vast majority of the electricity in Derask is produced through the use of coal powered generators in sparsely populated regions of the country, then we see why the program has not reduced the amount of air pollution in Derask but rather merely moved its greatest concentrations from some areas to others.
Even though there are more electric cars on the roads of Derask, charging these cars requires electricity, which is produced through the use of coal powered generators, which produce pollution.

Of course, if these coal powered generators are located in sparsely populated regions of the country, then it is safe to assume that there relatively few cars in these regions.As a result, we can see why the pollution has moved.It has moved from the urban areas where the most cars are to the areas in which the coal plants are located.
(E) as many people in Derask are still driving relative old cars that they purchased long before the electric car subsidy program was instituted

Notice that, regardless of what this answer choice says, in saying that “As a result, sales of electric cars soon outpaced sales of gasoline powered cars” the passage clearly tells us that some people have switched to electric cars.

If it is true that some people have switched to electric cars, then why has the program not reduced the amount of air pollution in Derask but rather merely moved its greatest concentrations from some areas to others?

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Automobile enthusiasts often claim that cars that were manufactured in the mid-1900s were built with higher levels of craftmanship and quality than cars manufactured today are built with.As evidence, they cite the fact that, in just about any town in America, cars of many car models from that era, such as the VW Minibus, Ford Mustang, and Cadillac Eldorado, are still driving the streets in pristine mechanical and cosmetic condition, whereas cars manufactured more recently typically last under 15 years.However, before they make such a claim, these automobile enthusiasts should consider that __________.

answers.text: there are also many cars of car models that were manufactured during the past few years that are driving the streets in pristine mechanical and cosmetic condition
most cars of most car models manufactured in the mid-1900s have long ago rusted-out in junkyards
a car built with higher levels of craftmanship and quality than another car was built with is not necessarily better overall than the other car
a significantly greater number of car models are designed and manufactured today than were in the mid-1900s
cars manufactured more recently are heavier on average than cars manufactured in the mid-1900s

explanation_parts.text: The passage says that automobile enthusiasts often claim the following:
Conclusion: Cars that were manufactured in the mid-1900s were built with higher levels of craftmanship and quality than cars manufactured today are built with.

As support for their claim, the automobile enthusiasts cite the fact that cars of many car models from that era are still driving the streets in pristine mechanical and cosmetic condition, whereas cars manufactured more recently typically last under 15 years.
In reading the sentence that ends with the blank, “before they make such a claim, these automobile enthusiasts should consider,” we can tell that the correct answer will cast doubt on the claim.
So, this Complete the Passage question is essentially a Weaken question, and the correct answer will answer the following question:
What should automobile enthusiasts consider before making such a claim?
(A) there are also many cars of car models that were manufactured during the past few years that are driving the streets in pristine mechanical and cosmetic condition
The fact that cars manufactured “during the past few years” are in pristine condition has no effect on the automobile enthusiasts’ argument.

The support for the automobile enthusiasts’ claim is that many cars manufactured in the mid-1900s, i.e., long ago, are in pristine condition, while cars manufactured more recently typically last under 15 years.

The fact that cars manufactured in the last few years are in pristine condition changes nothing about that argument.After all, even a car that will last under 15 years would likely be in pristine condition a year or two after it was manufactured, and a car manufactured during the past few years’ being in pristine condition is much different from a car manufactured in the mid-1900s’ being in pristine condition.
YOUR CHOICE(B) most cars of most car models manufactured in the mid-1900s have long ago rusted-out in junkyards
What this choice says is certainly something the automobile enthusiasts should consider before making their claim.

Notice that the support for their claim is based on a comparison that doesn’t quite make sense, because they compare the condition ofsomecars manufactured in the mid-1900s with how long cars manufactured today “typically last.” In other words, it seems likely they are comparing the condition ofextreme examples of carsmanufactured in the mid-1900s withthe average lifespan of all carsmanufactured today.

Thus, what this choice does by saying that most cars of most car models manufactured in the mid-1900s have long ago rusted-out in junkyards is make the comparison fairer, by showing that, even though some cars from the mid-1900s are in pristine condition, cars from the mid-1900s are not “typically” in pristine condition today.
By showing that the examples cited by the auto enthusiasts are not typical examples of cars manufactured in the mid-1900s, this choice casts doubt on their claim.
(C) a car built with higher levels of craftmanship and quality than another car was built with is not necessarily better overall than the other car
Notice that the automobile enthusiasts do not claim that cars manufactured in the mid-1900s are “better overall” than cars manufactured today.Their claim is restricted to craftsmanship and quality.
So, in making their claim, they don’t have to consider what this choice says.
(D) a significantly greater number of car models are designed and manufactured today than were in the mid-1900s

The number of cars manufactured does not change the facts about the craftsmanship and quality with which the cars were built.After all, regardless of whether one million or one hundred million cars are manufactured, the craftmanship and quality with which some cars are built are of a higher level or lower level than the craftmanship and quality with which other cars were built.
So, this choice has no effect on the argument.
(E) cars manufactured more recently are heavier on average than cars manufactured in the mid-1900s

In order avoid being fooled into choosing this choice, we have to be careful not to decide that being heavier and being built with higher levels of craftmanship and quality are essentially the same thing.After all, a car could be much heavier than another car and yet of much lower quality than the other car.

The truth is that, since we have no clear reason to believe that the fact that a car is heavier than another car means anything about which car was built with higher levels of craftsmanship and quality, this choice has no effect on the argument.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage below?The salt clock method of estimating the age of Earth is based on the fact that rivers are constantly depositing dissolved salts into the oceans.In theory, via determining how long it would take for Earth’s rivers to deposit into the oceans the amount of salt the oceans currently contain, one could arrive at a somewhat accurate estimate of the age of Earth.However, estimates made via the use of the salt clock method have drastically understated the age of Earth because __________.

answers.text: over time significant quantities of salt are removed from the oceans as seabeds rise and as ocean spray deposits salt on land
the rate at which rivers deposit salt into the oceans has not been constant
in estimating Earth’s age, those using the salt clock method rely on the unproven assumption that, initially, ocean water contained virtually no salt
along with rivers’ depositing salts into the oceans, rain causes airborne salt particles to fall into the oceans and dissolve
the levels of dissolved salts are not the same in all locations in Earth’s oceans

explanation_parts.text: We are told that the salt clock method involves arriving at an estimate of the age of Earth by determining how long it would take for rivers to deposit into the oceans the salt that the oceans contain.
We are then told that estimates made via the use of the salt clock method have drastically understated the age of the Earth.
The blank at the end of the passage is preceded by “because.” So, the correct answer will answer this question:
Why have estimates made via the use of the salt clock method drastically understated the age of Earth?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) over time significant quantities of salt are removed from the oceans as seabeds rise and as ocean spray deposits salt on land

Notice that, if it is true that, over time, significant quantities of salt are removed from the oceans as seabeds rise and as ocean spray deposits salt on land, then the amount of salt that the oceans currently contain is significantly less than the amount of salt the rivers have deposited into the ocean.Of course then, if, in using the salt clock method, someone considers only the salt that is in the oceans, that person is not considering all the salt that rivers have deposited into the oceans, because much of that salt has been removed.

Now, if a person using the salt clock method is basing the resulting estimate on less salt than the rivers have actually deposited, then that person will underestimate the age of Earth.After all, the estimate is based on how long the rivers would take to deposit the salt.So, if less salt than was actually deposited is used in arriving at the estimate, then the estimated time spent depositing will be less than the actual time spent depositing salt.
So, this choice explains why estimates made via the use of the salt clock method have drastically understated the age of Earth.
(B) the rate at which rivers deposit salt into the oceans has not been constant

Does the fact that the rate at which rivers deposit salt into the oceans has not been constant mean that the current levels of salt in the oceans are higher or lower than what the salt clock method would calculate them to be? We don’t have any way of knowing.
Therefore, what this answer choice says cannot explain why estimates made via using the salt clock method have drastically underestimated the age of Earth.
(C) in estimating Earth’s age, those using the salt clock method rely on the unproven assumption that, initially, ocean water contained virtually no salt

If it is true that, in estimating Earth’s age, those using the salt clock method rely on the unproven assumption that, initially, ocean water contained virtually no salt, then, if anything, those using the method would overestimate the age of Earth, and here’s why.
If that assumption is correct, then relying on that assumption would lead to a correct estimation of Earth’s age, not to an underestimation of Earth’s age.

On the other hand, if that assumption is incorrect, then those using the salt clock method would overestimate Earth’s age.They would overestimate because if they assume that there were virtually no salt in the ocean water initially, but there actually was salt in the ocean water initially, then the starting value of salt in the ocean is higher than what they believe it to have been.After all, they believe that the level was zero, but it was higher.

In that case, those using the salt clock method would count the salt that the oceans began with as salt that the rivers deposited and, thus, overestimate the amount of time over which the rivers have been depositing salt.
YOUR CHOICE(D) along with rivers’ depositing salts into the oceans, rain causes airborne salt particles to fall into the oceans and dissolve

Notice that, if along with rivers’ depositing salts into the oceans, rain causes airborne salt particles to fall into the oceans and dissolve, then the levels of salt in the oceans would be higher than they would be if the salt in the oceans were coming only from rivers.

In that case, those using the salt clock method would count the airborne salt particles that fell into the oceans as salt that the rivers deposited and, thus, overestimate the amount of time over which the rivers have been depositing salt.

Therefore, what this answer choice says takes us in the direction opposite from the one in which we want to go.After all, we’re seeking to explain why estimates made via using the salt clock method have drastically underestimated the age of Earth.We are not seeking to explain why the age of Earth has been overestimated.
(E) the levels of dissolved salts are not the same in all locations in Earth’s oceans

Notice we are seeking to explain why estimates made via using the salt clock method have drastically underestimated the age of Earth.This answer choice might lead us to assume that, because the levels of dissolved salts are not the same in all locations in Earth’s oceans, the salt clock method might not lead us to an accurate age of Earth.

First that assumption would not be warranted.If what this answer choices says is known fact, those using the salt clock method may take that fact into account and adjust the calculations so that they are accurate.

Furthermore, even if we made the unwarranted assumption that what this answer choice says would in fact lead to a miscalculation, in what direction would that miscalculation be? What this answer choice says is bi-directional.Would the fact that the levels of dissolved salts are not the same in all locations in Earth’s oceans lead to an overestimate or an underestimate of the age of Earth? There is no way to tell.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?In Itrazia, people who have completed college are, on average, twice as productive in terms of annual economic output as those who have not completed college.Even so, because of the high cost of college education in Itrazia, only 20 percent of adults in the workforce have completed college.If a recently announced proposal to have the government cover the college expenses of all who wish to attend is implemented, some of the remaining 80 percent will complete college, but it is unlikely that, on average, the productivity of those who do will double, as __________.

answers.text: many of them will have already worked for years without the benefit of college education
fewer than 15 percent of the adults who have not already completed college are expected to attend with the help of the government program
while the government will cover the expenses of attending college, taking time off from work to attend college will still result in a temporary reduction in earnings
there are people who have not completed college whose levels of productivity are as high as the average of those who have completed college
all of the adults who are most motivated to be productive in terms of economic output have already completed college

explanation_parts.text: Notice that, although this question is a Complete the Passage question, in answering it, what we’re really seeking to do is resolve the paradox presented to us.

The correct answer will explain why it is true (1) people who have completed four years of college are on average twice as productive in terms of annual economic output as those who have not completed college, and at the same time, (2) people who take advantage of the program and complete college will be unlikely - on average – to double their productivity.
(A) many of them will have already worked for years without the benefit of college education

Regardless of for how long people have worked without the benefit of college education, the passage tells us that people who have completed college are, on average, twice as productive in terms of annual economic output as those who have not attended college.Therefore, even if a person has already worked for years without a college education, if those people were to earn a college degree, we would, on average, expect that their average productivity would double.

In other words, an easy way to see that what this answer choice says is incorrect is to see that it is not relevant what people have done before they complete college; all that matters is that they do complete college.What the people have done prior is immaterial.
(B) fewer than 15 percent of the adults who have not already completed college are expected to attend with the help of the government program

This passage is about the effect on the individuals who do take advantage of the program.Therefore, that less than 15 percent of the adults who have not already attended college are expected to attend with the help of the government program does not explain why it is unlikely that on average the productivity of those who do take advantage of the program will double.
In other words, even if 1 percent of the people without a college degree take advantage of the program, it would be that 1 percent that we are interested in.
(C) while the government will cover the expenses of attending college, taking time off from work to attend college will still result in a temporary reduction in earnings

The point the passage is what happens to the annual economic output of the people who take advantage of the program and complete college.In other words, the defining point in time is the completion of college.What happens prior to that date is not relevant.

Notice that this answer choice is about the temporary reduction in earnings while attending college (prior the date at which college is completed).Therefore, the temporary reduction in earnings while attending college would not explain why it is unlikely that on average the productivity those who do take advantage of the program will double.
(D) there are people who have not completed college whose levels of productivity are as high as the average of those who have completed college
Notice that this choice doesn’t significantly change what we already know.

The passage says that, in Itrazia, people who have completed college are,on average, twice as productive in terms of annual economic output as those who have not completed college.Of course, among the values that are used in calculating an average can be values that are much higher or lower than that average.

Thus, all this choice really says is that some people who have not completed college are much more productive than the average person who has not completed college.Of course, what that information indicates also is that some people who have not completed college are much less productive than the average person who has not completed college.

So, given what this choice says, we are still left with essentially the same facts, and no explanation for why it is unlikely that, on average, the productivity of those who take advantage of the program will double.
YOUR CHOICE(E) all of the adults who are most motivated to be productive in terms of economic output have already completed college

Notice that, if all of the adults who are most motivated to be productive in terms of economic output have already completed college, then, even if the government covers the college expenses of all who wish to attend, the people remaining who would take advantage of the program would not be as motivated to be productive in terms of economic output as those who have already completed college

So, what this answer choice says resolves the paradox by highlighting the fact that that there is another component to economic output: motivation to produce economic output.If it is true that the people most motivated to be productive in terms of economic output have already completed four years of college, then we can easily understand why it is unlikely that on average the productivity of those who take advantage of the program will double.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?In many states around the country, recently enacted environmental regulations have resulted in situations such that more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.Nevertheless, while the Blitz Automobile Company is the clear electric car industry leader in the country, these developments are not expected to result, over the coming year, in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through the stores that Blitz uses for selling vehicles because __________.

answers.text: a production bottleneck caused by a shortage of the lithium used in electric car battery production is expected to continue to exist
regulations designed to protect traditional car dealers limit the number of stores that Blitz Automobile Company can open in each state
the salespeople who work in Blitz’s Automobile Company’s stores are paid by the hour, not on the basis of how many cars they sell
any change that benefits the Blitz Automobile Company benefits the company’s competitors as well
the distances that Blitz Automobile Company’s cars can travel between charges are not expected to increase significantly in the near future

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that, in many states around the country, recently enacted environmental regulations have resulted in situations such that more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.
This information would lead us to believe that sales of electric cars will increase.

Then the passage says that these developments are not expected to result in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through the stores that Blitz, the electric car industry leader, uses for selling vehicles.
The correct answer will answer the following question:
Why are these developments not expected to result, over the coming year, in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through the stores that Blitz uses for selling vehicles?
YOUR CHOICE(A) a production bottleneck caused by a shortage of the lithium used in electric car battery production is expected to continue to exist

The passage says that that recently enacted environmental regulations have resulted in situations such that more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.In such a situation, we would expect the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores to increase.

However, what if, as this choice says, there is a production bottleneck caused by a shortage of the lithium used in electric car battery production? In that case, regardless of how interested people are in purchasing electric cars, the volume of cars sold may not increase, because Blitz doesn’t have the lithium it needs to produce cars.

So, we can see that this choice logically completes the passage by explaining why the developments mentioned are not expected to result in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.
(B) regulations designed to protect traditional car dealers limit the number of stores that Blitz Automobile Company can open in each state

In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to notice the following.The passage has made clear that recently enacted environmental regulations have resulted in situations such that more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.In such a situation, even if, as this choice says, regulations limit the number of stores that Blitz Automobile Company can open, we would still expect to see an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.After all, even if the number of stores is limited, Blitz can sell more vehicles through the stores it already has.
So, this choice does not explain why these developments are not expected to result in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.
(C) the salespeople who work in Blitz’s Automobile Company’s stores are paid by the hour, not on the basis of how many cars they sell

In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to notice the following.The passage has made clear that recently enacted environmental regulations have resulted in situations such that more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.In such a situation, even if the salespeople who work in Blitz’s Automobile Company’s stores are paid by the hour, we would still expect to see an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.After all, while the salespeople are paid by the hour, more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.So, sales at the stores should increase.
So, this choice does not explain why these developments are not expected to result in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.
(D) any change that benefits the Blitz Automobile Company benefits the company’s competitors as well

Notice that the fact that the developments described in the passage benefit Blitz’s competitors does not mean that the developments do not benefit Blitz.The developments should still benefit Blitz, the electric car industry leader.So, regardless of what this choice says, we would still expect the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores to increase.
So, this choice does not explain why these developments are not expected to result in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.
(E) the distances that Blitz Automobile Company’s cars can travel between charges are not expected to increase significantly in the near future

Notice that, regardless of what this choice says, it remains the case that recently enacted environmental regulations have resulted in situations such that more people than ever are interested in purchasing electric cars.In such a situation, even if the distances that Blitz Automobile Company’s cars can travel between charges are not expected to increase significantly in the near future, we would still expect the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores to increase.
So, this choice does not explain why these developments are not expected to result in an increase in the volume of vehicles sold through Blitz’s stores.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?In recent years, the Town of Santa Colorada has experienced on its only two-lane highway heavy automobile traffic volume causing serious delays and slowdowns.City engineers have proposed addressing the problem via adding to the highway a third lane, which would have a speed limit 25 miles per hour faster than that of the other two lanes.They claim that, with the additional lane, traffic on the highway would move steadily, without delays or slowdowns.However, these engineers are mistaken in their assessment of the effects of adding such a lane because __________.

answers.text: reliable projections indicate that, by the time the third lane would become operational, the number of automobiles using the highway will have substantially decreased
the cost to build the third lane would exceed the amount of money that the Town of Santa Colorada has available to spend on highway improvements
the Town of Santa Colorada could more efficiently eliminate the serious delays and slowdowns on its highway by charging a toll for using the highway
the imposition of relatively low highway speed limits tends to result in significant reduction in numbers of automobile crashes, which almost always result in serious delays and slowdowns

Santa Colorada officials are unlikely to approve a major highway construction project, because such construction, while underway, would result in significant traffic related problems on the town’s streets

explanation_parts.text: We are told that engineers have proposed addressing the problem of heavy automobile traffic volume causing serious delays and slowdowns by adding to the town’s highway a third lane with a speed limit 25 miles per hour faster than that of the other two lanes.
The engineers have made the following claim:
Conclusion: With the additional lane, traffic on the highway would move steadily, without delays or slowdowns.

Since the sentence that ends with a blank says, “these engineers are mistaken in their assessment of the effects of adding such a lane because,” we can tell that we have to find a choice that weakens the case for the engineers’ assessment.
The correct answer to the question will answer the following question:
Why are these engineers mistaken in their assessment of the effects of adding such a lane?
(A) reliable projections indicate that, by the time the third lane would become operational, the number of automobiles using the highway will have substantially decreased
Notice that what this choice says does not cast doubt on the engineers’ assessment.

After all, if these projections are correct, and, by the time the third lane would become operational, the number of automobiles using the highway will have substantially decreased, that information is even more reason to believe the engineers’ claim that, with the additional lane, traffic on the highway would move steadily.After all, the fewer cars there are on the highway, the less volume there will be to cause delays and slowdowns.
So, this choice certainly does not say why the engineers are mistaken.
(B) the cost to build the third lane would exceed the amount of money that the Town of Santa Colorada has available to spend on highway improvements

Notice that the engineers’ claim is simply that, with the additional lane, traffic on the highway would move steadily.This choice, on the other hand, tells us that the cost of the third lane would exceed what the town has available to spend.

This fact about the cost has no effect on the claim about how traffic would move if the lane were built.After all, regardless of whether the town can afford to build the lane, the lane would have certain effects if it were built.
So, this choice does not say why the engineers are mistaken.
(C) the Town of Santa Colorada could more efficiently eliminate the serious delays and slowdowns on its highway by charging a toll for using the highway

To avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to keep in mind that the engineers’ claim is not that adding a third lane is the only way or the best way to address the traffic problem.So, the fact that the Town of Santa Colorada could more efficiently eliminate the serious delays by using an approach other than the approach of adding a third lane has no effect on the engineers’ argument.
After all, regardless of whether the town could more efficiently eliminate the delays by using a different approach, the approach of adding a third lane could still work quite well.
So, this choice does not say why the engineers are mistaken.

YOUR CHOICE(D) the imposition of relatively low highway speed limits tends to result in significant reduction in numbers of automobile crashes, which almost always result in serious delays and slowdowns

If what this choice says is true, then adding a third lane with “a speed limit 25 miles per hour faster than that of the other two lanes” could result in an increase in automobile crashes, which almost always result in serious delays and slowdowns.

After all, if having a relatively low highway speed limits tends to result in significant reduction in numbers of automobile crashes, then having a relatively high speed limit should have the opposite effect.
Of course, in that case, the engineers’ claim that, with the additional lane, traffic on the highway would move steadily, without delays or slowdowns is likely incorrect.
So, this choice answers the question, telling us why the engineers are mistaken.

(E) Santa Colorada officials are unlikely to approve a major highway construction project, because such construction, while underway, would result in significant traffic related problems on the town’s streets
To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to keep in mind that the engineers’ claim is not that the third lanewillbe added.It’s thatifthe lane were added, traffic would move steadily.
So, the fact that Santa Colorada officials are unlikely to approve a major highway construction project has no effect on the support for the engineers’ claim.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?To get people to change their habits, organizations post signs with messages such as “Don’t drink and drive,” or “Just say ‘No’ to drugs,” and to prevent certain behaviors in particular situations, organizations post signs with messages such as “No Smoking” or “No Consumption of Alcoholic Beverages.” Generally, people who see such signs while going about their business barely notice the signs, but the presence of the signs may result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce because __________.

answers.text: people unconsciously processing information presented in written form tend to fail to connect words expressing negation with the rest of the information
most people who see such signs don’t spend time reading and considering the value of the messages on the signs
people are so accustomed to seeing such signs that their seeing them has little impact on their thinking about the topics that are the subjects of the signs’ messages
people’s psychology is affected by the signs even if they don’t actively read and process the messages presented by the signs.
people who cope with issues by engaging in unhealthy behaviors often get caught up in behavioral cycles that lead to increased engagement in those unhealthy behaviors

explanation_parts.text: The passage mentions signs with messages such as “Don’t drink and drive,” “Just say ‘No’ to drugs,” “No Smoking,” or “No Consumption of Alcoholic Beverages.”
Then, since the word “because” precedes the blank at the end of the passage, we can tell that the correct answer will answer the following question:
Why may the presence of the signs result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce?
YOUR CHOICE(A) people unconsciously processing information presented in written form tend to fail to connect words expressing negation with the rest of the information

Notice that the passage says that, generally, people who see the signs while going about their business barely notice the signs.In other words, people who see the signs may be unconsciously processing the signs’ messages, by seeing the signs without paying attention to what the signs say.

Thus, if what this choice says is true, then people who see the signs likely fail to connect words expressing negation with the rest of the information and unconsciously perceive the signs to be saying things such as “Don’tdrink and drive,” “Just say‘No’to drugs,” “NoSmoking,” or “NoConsumption of Alcoholic Beverages.”

The fact that those are the types of messages that people who see such signs get from the signs would certainly explain why the presence of the signs may result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce.
(B) most people who see such signs don’t spend time reading and considering the value of the messages on the signs
Notice that this choice explains the wrong thing.
The correct answer will explain why the presence of the signs may result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce.

However, what this choice says would explain why the signs would not cause decreases in the behaviors.After all, if people don’t read or consider the value of the signs’ messages, then we would expect the signs not to have much of an effect.
Since not causing decreases and resulting in net increases are two different effects, this choice does not answer the question.
(C) people are so accustomed to seeing such signs that their seeing them has little impact on their thinking about the topics that are the subjects of the signs’ messages
Notice that this choice explains the wrong thing.
The correct answer will explain why the presence of the signs may result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce.

However, what this choice says would explain why the signs would not cause decreases in the behaviors.After all, if seeing the signs has little impact on people’s thinking about the topics that are the subjects of the signs’ messages, then we would expect the signs not to have much of an effect.
Since not causing decreases and resulting in net increases are two different effects, this choice does not answer the question.
(D) people’s psychology is affected by the signs even if they don’t actively read and process the messages presented by the signs.

Notice that what this choice says has an effect that is the opposite of the effect we need.We need a choice that explains why the presence of the signs may result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce, whereas what this choice says would explain why the signs cause decreases in those behaviors.

After all, if people’s psychology is affected by the signs even if they don’t actively read and process the messages presented by the signs, then even though, as the passage says, “people barely notice the signs,” we would expect them to reduce, not increase, the behaviors that the signs target.
(E) people who cope with issues by engaging in unhealthy behaviors often get caught up in behavioral cycles that lead to increased engagement in those unhealthy behaviors
Notice that this choice explains the wrong thing.
This choice provides one reason why people’s engagement in unhealthy behaviors often increases: people get caught up in behavioral cycles.

However, we don’t need a choice that explains why people’s engagement in unhealthy behaviors often increases.We need a choice that explains why the presence of the types of signs mentioned in the passage may result in net increases in the behaviors that the signs are meant to reduce.
The fact that people get caught up in behavioral cycles does not explain why the presence of the signs may cause increases in unhealthy behaviors.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage below?In general, the greater the number of versions of a particular type of item a consumer is offered, the smaller are the differences between versions, and the greater is the likelihood that the consumer will be able to choose a version well suited to that particular consumer.However, because much of the satisfaction a consumer derives from choosing an item comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer, in many cases, consumers offered greater numbers of versions of items __________.

answers.text: become so overwhelmed that they do not purchase any of the versions
have difficulty choosing between versions that do not have pronounced differences between them
do indeed choose versions well suited to their needs
are less satisfied with the choices they end up making than are consumers who are offered fewer versions with bigger differences between versions
choose versions no better suited to their needs than are the versions they would have chosen had there been fewer versions from which to choose

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer to this question will answer the following “what” question:

Because much of the satisfaction a consumer derives from choosing an item comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer, in many cases, consumers offered greater numbers of versions of items do what?
(A) become so overwhelmed that they do not purchase any of the versions
Notice that what this answer choice says is not supported by the information presented in the passage.

The fact that satisfaction comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer does not support the conclusion that consumers become overwhelmed.Being satisfied or unsatisfied and being overwhelmed are separate concepts.
(B) have difficulty choosing between versions that do not have pronounced differences between them

Notice that what this answer choice says is not supported by the information presented in the passage.The fact that satisfaction comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer does not support the conclusion that consumers have difficulty choosing.
(C) do indeed choose versions well suited to their needs

The passage says that the greater the number of versions of a particular type of item a consumer is offered, the greater is the likelihood that the consumer will be able to choose a version well suited to that particular consumer.Then, it says “however.” So, what follows will be a contrast to the first sentence.

Notice that what this answer choice says, that consumers offered greater numbers of versions of items do indeed choose versions well suited to each of their needs, is in line with what the first sentence says; what this answer choice says is not in contrast to what the first sentence says.
YOUR CHOICE(D) are less satisfied with the choices they end up making than are consumers who are offered fewer versions with bigger differences between versions

The fact that, as the passage says, much of the satisfaction a consumer derives from choosing an item comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer supports what this choice says.

After all, the greater the number of versions of a particular type of item a consumer is offered, and the smaller are the differences between versions, the less clear it will be that the version a consumer has chosen is the best version for that consumer.

After all, if there are only a few versions that are very different from each other, a consumer has big differences to consider and will likely feel that one of the versions is clearly the best one for that consumer.On the other hand, if there are many versions that are barely different from each other, after choosing one version, a consumer may not be sure that that version is actually the best one for that consumer.

So, we can see that, because much of the satisfaction a consumer derives from choosing an item comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer, in many cases, consumers offered greater numbers of versions of items are less satisfied with the choices they end up making than are consumers who are offered fewer versions with bigger differences between versions.
(E) choose versions no better suited to their needs than are the versions they would have chosen had there been fewer versions from which to choose
Notice that what this answer choice says is not supported by the information presented in the passage.

The fact that satisfaction comes from the consumer’s believing that the version chosen is clearly the best version for that consumer does not support the conclusion that consumers choose versions no better suited to their needs than are the versions they would have chosen had there been fewer versions from which to choose.
Regardless of how satisfaction may be affected, consumers could still be choosing the best versions for them.

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?One theory of why animals sleep is that, since they use less energy while sleeping than while awake, sleeping for a portion of each day allows animals to conserve energy by using less energy per day than they would use were they to stay awake continuously.However, this theory has been largely discredited by research indicating that __________.

answers.text: animals that are asleep are more vulnerable to attack than animals that are awake
some animals, such as bears and cats, spend sleeping a much greater portion of the day than is spent sleeping by other animals, such as deer and giraffes
an animal’s sleeping for a time period generally saves only a small fraction of the energy that that animal would generate via eating at a normal rate during that period
animals that hibernate, sleeping for extended periods of time, must first consume large amounts of food to meet the energy needs they will have while asleep
animals can reduce the quantities of energy that they consume while awake by reducing levels of activity

explanation_parts.text: To correctly answer this question, we have to find a choice that casts doubt on the theory that animals sleep because sleeping allows them to conserve energy.
(A) animals that are asleep are more vulnerable to attack than animals that are awake
The theory is that animals sleep because sleeping for a portion of each day allows animals to conserve energy by using less energy per day than they would use were they to stay awake continuously.
Notice that the theory does not say that there is no downside to sleeping.
In other words, what this answer choice says could be true and yet the theory could still be perfectly valid.
(B) some animals, such as bears and cats, spend sleeping a much greater portion of the day than is spent sleeping by other animals, such as deer and giraffes
Notice that the theory is about why animals sleep.

What this answer choice says would not at all affect the argument’s validity.After all, regardless of whether all animals sleep for the same portion of the day, they could all sleep to conserve energy.
YOUR CHOICE(C) an animal’s sleeping for a time period generally saves only a small fraction of the energy that that animal would generate via eating at a normal rate during that period
This choice casts doubt on the theory by showing that sleeping to conserve energy may not make sense.

After all, if what this choice says is true, then animals don’t conserve much energy by sleeping.In fact, they could have just as much energy by eating a small fraction of what they would normally eat if they were awake instead of sleeping, and if they were to eat normally instead of sleeping, they would have even more energy.

So, the fact that, rather than spend all that time sleeping, animals could just eat a small fraction of what they would normally eat if they were awake for that period of time serves to call into question the theory that animals sleep because sleeping allows them to conserve energy.
(D) animals that hibernate, sleeping for extended periods of time, must first consume large amounts of food to meet the energy needs they will have while asleep

The fact that animals that hibernate, sleeping for extended periods of time, must first consume large amounts of food to meet the energy needs they will have while asleep does not change the fact provided to us that, since they use less energy while sleeping than while awake, sleeping for a portion of each day allows animals to conserve energy by using less energy per day than they would were they to stay awake continuously.

After all, it might just be that these animals need to initially consume a bunch of food before they sleep, but still the sleeping allows them conserve energy by using less energy per day than they would use were they to stay awake continuously.
(E) animals can reduce the quantities of energy that they consume while awake by reducing levels of activity

Notice that we are told that sleeping for a portion of each day allows animals to conserve energy by using less energy per day than they would were they to stay awake continuously.What this answer choice says does not change that fact.So, what this answer choice says would not be research that has discredited the theory.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?When Wonder, an online magazine for parents of newborns, surveyed subscribers, the vast majority of subscribers who responded to the survey indicated that they were rather satisfied or completely satisfied with the magazine.However, a year later, most subscribers to the magazine had cancelled their subscriptions.Nevertheless, this pattern of cancellations does not indicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation because __________.

answers.text: Wonder did not change its format or its price during the time period in question
only a small percentage of the magazine’s subscribers had responded to the survey
few people in the countries where Wonder is available have more than one child
there are no other magazines with focuses and content similar to that of Wonder to which former Wonder subscribers could subscribe
new subscribers subscribed to Wonder over the time period in question

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that, while the vast majority of subscribers to Wonder who responded to a survey indicated that they were satisfied with the magazine, a year later, most subscribers to the magazine had cancelled their subscriptions.
The correct answer will answer the following question:
Why does this pattern of cancellations not indicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation?
(A) Wonder did not change its format or its price during the time period in question

One possible explanation for the fact that most subscribers cancelled only a year after the vast majority survey respondents said that they were satisfied with Wonder is that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation, and actually most subscribers were not satisfied.
Another possible explanation is that, after subscribers said they were satisfied with Wonder, Wonder changed its format or price, with the result that the satisfied subscribers became unsatisfied.
This choice serves to rule out the second explanation, making it more likely that the first explanation, that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation, is correct.

Of course, the correct answer will do essentially the opposite of what this choice does.The correct answer will show why the cancellations donotindicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation.
So, this choice is not the correct answer.
(B) only a small percentage of the magazine’s subscribers had responded to the survey
Notice that this choice has the wrong effect.

What this choice says could explain why the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation: only a small percentage of the magazine’s subscribers had responded to the survey, and therefore, the survey didn’t accurately capture what was going on.

However, we don’t need such an explanation.Instead, we need information indicating why the pattern of cancellations doesnotindicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation.
So, this choice is not the correct answer.
YOUR CHOICE(C) few people in the countries where Wonder is available have more than one child
Notice that the passage says that Wonder is a magazine for parents of newborns.Newborns, of course, remain newborn for only a short period of time, which is less than a year.
So, if parents have only one child, no matter how satisfied those parents are with Wonder, subscribing to Wonder for more than one year would not make sense.

Thus, what this choice says explains how it could be that satisfied subscribers to Wonder would cancel within one year: they have only one child and, a year after subscribing, no longer have any use for Wonder.

Of course, that information indicates how it could be that the pattern of cancellations does not indicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation.It indicates that the survey results provided an accurate picture of the situation, and then people who, though satisfied with Wonder, have only one child cancelled their subscriptions.
(D) there are no other magazines with focuses and content similar to that of Wonder to which former Wonder subscribers could subscribe
This choice does not have the effect we need.

After all, even if there are no magazines similar to Wonder to which the Wonder Magazine subscribers who cancelled could subscribe, the fact that most subscribers cancelled after the vast majority of survey respondents said that they were satisfied with Wonder still seems to indicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation.
So, this choice does not explain why the pattern of cancellations does not indicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation.
(E) new subscribers subscribed to Wonder over the time period in question

Notice that, regardless of the fact that some new subscribers subscribed to Wonder, it remains the case that most subscribers had cancelled a year after the vast majority of survey respondents said that they were satisfied with Wonder.

So, this choice does not somehow change the relevant facts, and it does not provide any reason to believe that the pattern of cancellations does not indicate that the survey results provided an inaccurate picture of the situation.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?During the coming year, farmers in Orlontica, along with farmers in other countries, are expected to produce the same number of kiwis that they produced during the past year.At the same time, demand for kiwis in Orlontica is expected to decrease.Nevertheless, Orlontican farmers are confident that they will be able to sell all of the kiwis that they produce without reducing the price because __________.

answers.text: demand for kiwis in the neighboring country of Arbantica is not expected to decrease
the average price of kiwis is already lower on an inflation-adjusted basis than it has been in decades
of improvements in farming methods, which will allow Orlontican farmers to reduce the cost of producing kiwis
people choose to eat kiwis because of their unique taste and their high nutrient content
total worldwide demand for kiwis is expected to remain the same

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that farmers in Orlontica, along with other farmers, are expected to produce during the coming year just as many kiwis as they produced during the past year, and that demand for kiwis in Orlontica is expected to decrease.

In that situation, we might expect the price Orlontican farmers receive for kiwis to decrease, because there would be just as many kiwis available as there were during the past year, but not as much interest in buying them.
The correct answer will answer the following question:
Why are Orlontican farmers confident that they will be able to sell all of the kiwis that they produce without reducing the price?
YOUR CHOICE(A) demand for kiwis in the neighboring country of Arbantica is not expected to decrease

Notice that, even if the demand for kiwis in the neighboring country of Arbantica is not expected to decrease, it still remains the case that demand for kiwis in Orlontica is expected to decrease.So, regardless of what this choice says, we still have reason to believe there will be an overall decrease in demand.
So, this choice does not really change what we know about the scenario.
(B) the average price of kiwis is already lower on an inflation-adjusted basis than it has been in decades

The fact that the price of kiwis is already lower than it has been in decades does not mean that it won’t go even lower, and decrease in demand would be expected to cause a decrease in price regardless of how low the price already is.
So, this choice does not explain why the farmers are confident that they will be able to sell all of the kiwis that they produce without reducing the price.
(C) of improvements in farming methods, which will allow Orlontican farmers to reduce the cost of producing kiwis
The fact that farmers can reduce the cost of producing kiwis may help the farmers operate profitably even if the price of kiwis decreases.
However, if demand for kiwis decreases, reducing the cost of producing kiwis would not hold the price up.
So, this choice does not explain why the farmers are confident that they will be able to sell all of the kiwis that they produce without reducing the price.
(D) people choose to eat kiwis because of their unique taste and their high nutrient content
To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to remember that we cannot argue with the facts presented in the passage.

The passage states as fact that demand for kiwis is expected to decrease, and what this choice says does not change that fact.In other words, as much as people may like kiwis because of their taste and nutrient content, demand is still expected to decrease.
So, this choice does not explain why the farmers are confident that they will be able to sell all of the kiwis that they produce without reducing the price.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) total worldwide demand for kiwis is expected to remain the same

The passage says that demand for kiwis in Orlontica is expected to decrease, a situation in which we might expect the price of kiwis to decrease if farmers everywhere produce just as many kiwis as they produced in the past year.

However, if, as this choice says, worldwide demand is expected to remain the same, and if, as the passage says, farmers in Orlontica, along with farmers in other countries, are expected to produce the same number of kiwis that they produced during the past year, then even if demand for kiwis in Orlontica is expected to decrease, farmers in Orlontica could reasonably expect the worldwide price of kiwis to remain the same.

So, this choice explains why the farmers are confident that they will be able to sell all of the kiwis that they produce without reducing the price.They expect the price of kiwis to remain about the same worldwide.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Some people assert that currently illegal hard drugs should not be legalized, claiming that legalization of such drugs would result in an increase in their use.However, even if these people’s assessment of the consequences of legalization of drugs is correct, legalization of drugs could result in a decrease in the percentage of people who experience significant neurological injury through the use of such drugs because __________.

answers.text: the purchase of such drugs could be taxed and the revenue from such taxation used to pay for programs designed to reduce use of such drugs
researchers able to legally acquire hard drugs that are currently illegal are likely to find ways to use some of them to treat neurological problems
many people are injured in the violence that results from the illegal nature of the business of supplying currently illegal hard drugs
many drugs that are currently illegal were in the past used for medicinal purposes by members of ancient cultures
in order to minimize the volumes of drugs illegally transported, hard drug producers often produce drugs of potencies significantly greater than those preferred by users

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer will answer the following question:

Why could legalization of drugs result in a decrease in the percentage of people who experience significant neurological injury through the use of such drugs even if it is correct that legalization of such drugs would result in an increase in their use?
YOUR CHOICE(A) the purchase of such drugs could be taxed and the revenue from such taxation used to pay for programs designed to reduce use of such drugs

Notice that the author’s claim is that,even if legalization of hard drugs would result in an increase in their use, legalization could result in a decrease in the percentage of people who experience significant neurological injury through the use of such drugs.

Thus, the author’s conclusion does not involve the possibility that taxation of drug purchases could be used to pay for programs designed to reduce use of such drugs.The author’s point is that, even in if drug use would increase, rather than be reduced, the percentage of people injured by drugs could decrease.
So, this choice does not tell us why the author’s conclusion is true.
(B) researchers able to legally acquire hard drugs that are currently illegal are likely to find ways to use some of them to treat neurological problems
The author’s conclusion is not about treating neurological problems.It’s about reducing the percentage of people who experience significant neurological injury.
Researchers’ finding ways to treat neurological problems would not prevent hard drugs from causing neurological injuries.
So, this choice does not tell us why the author’s conclusion is true.
(C) many people are injured in the violence that results from the illegal nature of the business of supplying currently illegal hard drugs

Notice that the author is not making any point about people injured in the violence that results from the illegal nature of the business of supplying currently illegal hard drugs.The conclusion is solely about the percentage of people injured by the drugs themselves.

So, while this choice indicates that making hard drugs legal may result in a reduction in the number of people injured in violence, this choice does not answer the question, because it indicates nothing about reducing the percentage of people injured by the drugs themselves.
(D) many drugs that are currently illegal were in the past used for medicinal purposes by members of ancient cultures

The fact that drugs that are currently illegal were used for medicinal purposes in the past does not explain why legalizing such drugs would result in a decrease in the percentage of people who experience significant neurological injury through the use of such drugs.After all, how such drugs were used in the past does not affect the number of people they will injure in the future.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) in order to minimize the volumes of drugs illegally transported, hard drug producers often produce drugs of potencies significantly greater than those preferred by users
