If what this choice says is true, then the fact that hard drugs are illegal is causing hard drug producers to produce drugs of potencies significantly greater than those preferred by users.Of course, such high potency drugs are more likely to cause neurological injuries than drugs of potencies preferred by users.

So, we can see what would likely happen if hard drugs were legalized.The potencies would come down to match the potencies preferred by users, and therefore, people who use hard drugs would become less likely to experience significant neurological injury.

In such a case, it could turn out that, even if legalization of hard drugs would result in an increase in their use, legalization could result in a decrease in the percentage of people who experience significant neurological injury through the use of such drugs.
So, this choice explains why the author’s conclusion is true.

question.text: Which most logically completes the argument in the passage?To promote environmental sustainability, the government of Tholesko plans to tax the carbon emissions of all companies located in Tholesko.While doing so may result in a reduction in carbon emissions attributable to manufacturing facilities in Tholesko, it will probably result in an increase in carbon emissions attributable to the manufacture of products purchased by people living in Tholesko, as __________.

answers.text: in order to avoid increased costs associated with the tax, manufacturing companies will move their operations from Tholesko to countries with fewer emissions regulations than Tholesko has now
Tholesko is coming out of a recession and the number of products that people living in Tholesko buy is expected to increase
significantly reducing carbon emissions from the already relatively low levels seen in Tholesko will not be possible without substantial investments in new technology
per capita income in Tholesko is increasing quickly, and therefore people in Tholesko can be expected to increase their purchases of large manufactured items such as automobiles
the government of Tholesko does not offer subsidies to help companies switch from using carbon producing energy sources to using more sustainable energy sources

explanation_parts.text: In the passage, the word “as,” which in this context means “because,” appears just before the blank.So, the correct answer will answer a question that begins with “why.”
Notice that, in answering that “why” question, we are basically resolving an apparent paradox that involves the following two facts.
Fact 1: The government’s taxing carbon emissions may result in a reduction in carbon emissions attributable to manufacturing facilities in Tholesko.
Fact 2: The government’s taxing carbon emissions will probably result in an increase in carbon emissions attributable to the manufacture of products purchased by people living in Tholesko.
So, the correct answer will explain how these two facts can coexist.
Notice also that we have to explain anincrease.

Why will taxing the carbon emissions of all companies located in Tholesko probably result in anincreasein carbon emissions attributable to the manufacture of products purchased by people living in Tholesko?

YOUR CHOICE(A) in order to avoid increased costs associated with the tax, manufacturing companies will move their operations from Tholesko to countries with fewer emissions regulations than Tholesko has now

If, as this choice says, in order to avoid increased costs associated with the tax, manufacturing companies will move their operations from Tholesko to countries with fewer emissions regulations than Tholesko has now, then it would be reasonable to predict that at least some of these companies will increase their carbon emissions once they are operating in countries with fewer emissions regulations than Tholesko has.

Thus, we can see that this choice explains why the government’s taxing carbon emissions will probably result in an increase in carbon emissions attributable to the manufacture of products purchased by people living in Tholesko.
(B) Tholesko is coming out of a recession and the number of products that people living in Tholesko buy is expected to increase
This choice explains the wrong thing.
We have to explain whytaxing carbon emissionswill probably result in an increase in carbon emissions.

What this answer choice says, that the number of products that people living in Tholesko buy is expected to increase, would explain why carbon emissions would increase, but it does not explain why taxing carbon emissions would result in an increase in carbon emissions.
After all, even if the number of products that people buy increases, taxing emissions would still be expected to cause emissions to be lower, not higher, than they would be without the taxing.
(C) significantly reducing carbon emissions from the already relatively low levels seen in Tholesko will not be possible without substantial investments in new technology
Notice that what we are trying to explain is why the tax on carbon emissions of all companies located in Tholesko will result in anincreasein carbon emissions.

On the other hand, what this answer choice says, that significantlyreducingcarbon emissions from the already relatively low levels seen in Tholeskowill not be possiblewithout substantial investments in new technology, could explain why carbon emissionswould not decrease.

Of course, “will result in an increase” and would not decrease are two different outcomes.So, this answer choice explains the wrong thing, since it does not explain why carbon emissions will increase.
(D) per capita income in Tholesko is increasing quickly, and therefore people in Tholesko can be expected to increase their purchases of large manufactured items such as automobiles
This choice explains the wrong thing.
We have to explain whytaxing carbon emissionswill probably result in an increase in carbon emissions.

What this answer choice says, that per capita income in Tholesko is increasing quickly, and therefore people in Tholesko can be expected to increase their purchases of large manufactured items, would explain why carbon emissions would increase, but it does not explain why taxing carbon emissions would result in an increase in carbon emissions.
After all, even if per capita income in Tholesko is increasing, taxing emissions would still be expected to cause emissions to be lower, not higher, than they would be without the taxing.
(E) the government of Tholesko does not offer subsidies to help companies switch from using carbon producing energy sources to using more sustainable energy sources
Notice that what we are trying to explain is why the tax on carbon emissions of all companies located in Tholesko will result in anincreasein carbon emissions.

On the other hand, what this answer choice says, that the government of Tholesko does not offer subsidies to help companies switch from using carbon producing energy sources to using more sustainable energy sources, could explain why carbon emissionswould not decrease.

Of course, “will result in an increase” and would not decrease are two different outcomes.So, this answer choice explains the wrong thing, since it does not explain why carbon emissions will increase.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Some consumers maintain that eating organically grown bananas can be little different from eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides, since pesticide spray from banana plantations that use synthetic chemical pesticides often drifts onto bananas on plantations that use organic growing methods.Ironically, that this contamination occurs is just another reason __________.

answers.text: why there is little need to be concerned about health effects of eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides, since bananas’ thick skins keep pesticides from the part people eat
to be concerned about the health of people involved in growing bananas, both on plantations that use synthetic chemical pesticides and on plantations that use organic methods
for purchasing organically grown bananas, as the greater the percentage of bananas grown organically the lower will be the amounts of drifting synthetic chemical pesticides
that runoff of synthetic chemical pesticides from banana plantations can be found in waterways long after the pesticides were sprayed on the bananas
why a label certifying that a food was grown organically never means anything

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that some consumers claim that eating organically grown bananas can be about the same as eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides because pesticide spray from banana plantations that use synthetic chemical pesticides often drifts onto bananas on plantations that use organic growing methods.
Then the passage says “Ironically, that this contamination occurs is just another reason ….”

So, we’re seeking something ironic in relation to the fact that some consumers claim that eating organically grown bananas can be about the same as eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides because pesticide spray often drifts onto bananas on plantations that use organic growing methods.
(A) why there is little need to be concerned about health effects of eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides, since bananas’ thick skins keep pesticides from the part people eat

What this answer choice says, that there is little need to be concerned about health effects of eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides, as banana’s thick skins keep pesticides from the part people eat, is in line with the consumers’ claim.
Since what this answer choice says is in line with the consumers’ claim, it is certainly not ironic.
(B) to be concerned about the health of people involved in growing bananas, both on plantations that use synthetic chemical pesticides and on plantations that use organic methods

Notice that the fact that pesticide sprays drifts “onto bananas” is not clearly a reason to be concerned about the health of people involved in growing bananas.After all, neither the passage nor this choice says that the people involved in growing bananas eat the bananas, and generally, it is not clear that this contamination of bananas is a reason to be concerned about the of health people involved in growing bananas.
Furthermore, even if it were true that this contamination of bananas is a reason to be concerned about the of health people involved in growing bananas, that fact would not be ironic.
YOUR CHOICE(C) for purchasing organically grown bananas, as the greater the percentage of bananas grown organically the lower will be the amounts of drifting synthetic chemical pesticides

We’re told that some consumers claim that eating organically grown bananas can be about the same as eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides because pesticide spray from banana plantations that use synthetic chemical pesticides often drifts onto bananas on plantations that use organic growing methods.

Then the passage says “Ironically, that this contamination occurs is just another reason ….” So, we’re seeking something ironic in relation to the claim that eating organically grown bananas can be about the same as eating bananas grown using synthetic chemical pesticides because pesticide spray from banana plantations that use synthetic chemical pesticides often drifts onto bananas on plantations that use organic growing methods.
Of course, what this answer choice says, that that claim isa reason for purchasing organically grown bananas, both logically completes the passage and is ironic in relation to the consumers’ claim.
(D) that runoff of synthetic chemical pesticides from banana plantations can be found in waterways long after the pesticides were sprayed on the bananas
What this answer choice says does not really follow from the fact that pesticide spray drifts “onto bananas” and is not ironic at all.
(E) why a label certifying that a food was grown organically never means anything
What this answer choice says is not ironic in light of the facts presented in the argument because what this answer choice says is in line with the consumers’ claim.

Also, the fact that bananas are contaminated does not mean that a label certifying that a food was grown organically “never means anything.” It might mean that such a label is suspect, but not that such a label never means anything.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?Since 1989, Mexico City has sought to achieve a reduction in air pollution by banning on certain days of the week the driving of cars with certain numbers on their license plates.For example, on Mondays, cars with license plates ending in 5 or 6 may not be driven during peak driving hours.Nevertheless, even on days on which this regulation has resulted in a reduction from past daily averages in the total numbers of miles cars are driven, levels of automobile produced air pollution often have been no lower than they were in the past because, in Mexico City, __________.

answers.text: the number of residents and, thus, the total number of cars owned by residents of the city have continued to increase
some people whose cars, because of the car ban, cannot be driven during peak hours drive during off peak hours
people whose cars cannot be driven because of the ban get rides from friends, causing their friends to drive much further than they would otherwise
in order to be able to drive on all days, many people purchase and register second cars, which are often older models with lower than average fuel economy
public transportation is so inconvenient to use that the car bans have resulted in virtually no shift to use of these less polluting means of travel

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that, since 1989, Mexico City has sought to achieve a reduction in air pollution by banning on certain days of the week the driving of cars with certain numbers on their license plates.
The correct answer will answer the following question:

Why is it that, even on days on which this regulation has resulted in a reduction from past daily averages in the total numbers of miles cars are driven, levels of automobile produced air pollution often have been no lower than they were in the past?

Notice that the correct answer will explain how it could be that, even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past.
(A) the number of residents and, thus, the total number of cars owned by residents of the city have continued to increase
What we have to explain is why there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past.

This choice says does not explain that.After all, on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, it doesn’t matter how many cars there are in Mexico City.Those cars are still being driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, in which case we would expect there to be less pollution than there was in the past.
But there isn’t less pollution.Why?
(B) some people whose cars, because of the car ban, cannot be driven during peak hours drive during off peak hours
What we have to explain is why there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past.

This choice does not explain that.After all, on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, it doesn’t matter that some people drive during off peak hours.People are still driving fewer miles in total than they drove in the past, in which case we would expect there to be less pollution than there was in the past.
But there isn’t less pollution.Why?
(C) people whose cars cannot be driven because of the ban get rides from friends, causing their friends to drive much further than they would otherwise
What we have to explain is why there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past.

This choice says does not explain that.After all, on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, it doesn’t matter that people are causing their friends to drive much further than they would otherwise.People, including those friends, are still driving fewer miles in total than people drove in the past, in which case we would expect there to be less pollution than there was in the past.
But there isn’t less pollution.Why?
YOUR CHOICE(D) in order to be able to drive on all days, many people purchase and register second cars, which are often older models with lower than average fuel economy
What we have to explain is why there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past.

Notice that what this choice says indicates that, because of the ban, people are driving older model cars with lower than average fuel economy.In other words, people are driving cars that burn more fuel than average per mile.

Of course, the fact that, because of the ban, people are driving cars that burn more fuel than average per mile would explain why there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past, even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past.The explanation is that, even though people are driving fewer miles, they are burning more fuel and, thus, creating more pollution per mile.In other words, they are offsetting the decrease in miles driven by creating more pollution per mile.
So, this choice perfectly answers the question and logically completes the passage.
(E) public transportation is so inconvenient to use that the car bans have resulted in virtually no shift to use of these less polluting means of travel
What we have to explain is why there’s just as much pollution as there was in the past even on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past.

This choice says does not explain that.After all, on days when cars are driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, it doesn’t matter that public transportation is so inconvenient to use that the car bans have resulted in virtually no shift to use of public transportation.Cars are still being driven fewer miles in total than cars were driven in the past, in which case we would expect there to be less pollution than there was in the past.
But there isn’t less pollution.Why?

question.text: Which of the following best completes the passage?Recently, a group of scientists proposed that a new epoch, the Anthropocene, be added to Earth’s geologic record to mark the profound ways in which humans have changed the planet.They propose beginning the Anthropocene in the mid-20th century, a time when the human population rose rapidly, atmospheric temperature increases accelerated, and radionuclides from atomic bomb blasts for the first time became a part of Earth’s geology.Other scientists don’t agree with adding this new Anthropocene epoch to Earth’s geologic time, claiming that the Anthropocene epoch does not have a definitive start date because __________.

answers.text: humans have made progressive and numerous impacts on the world since the advent of agriculture in prehistoric times
humans have, during the period in question, had positive impacts not considered by the scientists proposing the addition of the Anthropocene epoch
many significant developments that have occurred in the proposed era have not been attributed to human activity
radionuclides from atomic bomb blasts break down over time
before humans inhabited Earth, there were increases in atmospheric temperatures

explanation_parts.text: We see that the blank at the end of the passage is preceded by the word “because.” So, the correct answer to this Complete the Passage question will answer a question that begins with “why.”
Why does the Anthropocene epoch not have a definitive start date?
CORRECT ANSWER(A) humans have made progressive and numerous impacts on the world since the advent of agriculture in prehistoric times
What this choice says explains why the Anthropocene epoch does not have a definitive start date.
After all, if humans have made progressive and numerous impacts on the world since the advent of agriculture in prehistoric times, then it’s difficult to say when the Anthropocene epoch started.
Did it start with agriculture? Did it start when humans made another impact?
Given what this choice says, scientists could make the case that it is unclear which human impact is sufficiently significant to serve as the marker of the start of the Anthropocene epoch.
(B) humans have, during the period in question, had positive impacts not considered by the scientists proposing the addition of the Anthropocene epoch

The fact that humans have made other impacts during the same time period does not mean that there is no definitive start date to the period.The impacts considered by the scientists could still mark the start date of the Anthropocene epoch.
(C) many significant developments that have occurred in the proposed era have not been attributed to human activity
This choice supports the wrong claim.

What this choice says might support the claim that calling the epoch the Anthropocene epoch does not make sense, because significant developments other than humans’ impacts have occurred during the same time period.

Of course, we don’t need to support that claim, and regardless of what else has occurred during the time period that some scientists have proposed calling the Anthropocene epoch, that epoch could still have a definitive start date.
(D) radionuclides from atomic bomb blasts break down over time

The fact that radionuclides from atomic bomb blasts break down over time does not mean that their first becoming part of Earth’s geology does not mark a point in time.After all, even if radionuclides from atomic bomb blasts will eventually disappear completely, they came into existence at a certain point in time, which could be considered the definitive start date of the Anthropocene epoch.
YOUR CHOICE(E) before humans inhabited Earth, there were increases in atmospheric temperatures
The fact that there weretemperature increasesbefore humans inhabited Earth does not mean thathumans accelerating temperature increasesdoes not mark the start date of a new era.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the reasoning of the passage?As atmospheric carbon dioxide levels rise, the resulting temperature increases will cause the quantities of water available for growing wheat to decrease.Fortunately, it is the case that, when grown in conditions characterized by relatively high carbon dioxide levels, wheat requires lower than normal amounts of water for growth.However, whether the existence of this dynamic indicates that areas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food source will not experience food shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels remains unclear, because it is unknown whether __________.

answers.text: factors other than increases in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels will cause temperatures to rise
increases in carbon dioxide and temperature levels will lead to increased incidences of tropical diseases in people in such areas
people currently dependent on soybeans as a primary food crop will find it necessary to switch to growing wheat instead as carbon dioxide levels increase
the populations of such areas will increase to sizes such that they outstrip the areas’ food production capabilities
levels of nutrients in wheat grown under high carbon dioxide conditions are as high as those in wheat grown today

explanation_parts.text: We’re told that there are two offsetting factors:
As atmospheric carbon dioxide levels rise, the resulting temperature increases will cause the quantities of water available for growing wheat to decrease.
When grown in conditions characterized by relatively high carbon dioxide levels, wheat requires lower than normal amounts of water for growth.
The correct answer will answer the following question:

What must be known for making clear whether the existence of this dynamic indicates that areas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food source will not experience food shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels?
Notice that this question is essentially an Evaluate the Argument question in the form of a Complete the Passage question.
YOUR CHOICE(A) factors other than increases in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels will cause temperatures to rise
We need information necessary for determining whether areas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food source will not experience food shortages resulting fromincreases in carbon dioxide levels.

On the other hand, the information mentioned by this choice would help with determining whether factors other than increases in carbon dioxide levels will cause temperatures to rise and, perhaps, cause food shortages.

Since information on whether other factors will cause temperatures to rise and, perhaps, cause food shortages does not help us to determine whether increases in carbon dioxide levels will result in food shortages, this choice is not the correct answer.
(B) increases in carbon dioxide and temperature levels will lead to increased incidences of tropical diseases in people in such areas
We need information necessary for determining whether areas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food source will not experiencefood shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels.
On the other hand, the information mentioned by this choice would help with determining whether people will experiencetropical diseases resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels.

Since information on whether people will experience tropical diseases does not help with determining whether people will experience food shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels, this choice is not the correct answer.
(C) people currently dependent on soybeans as a primary food crop will find it necessary to switch to growing wheat instead as carbon dioxide levels increase
We need information necessary for determining whetherareas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food sourcewill not experience food shortages.
This choice is aboutpeople currently dependent on soybeans as a primary food cropswitching to growing wheat.

Information on whether people currently dependent on soybeans as a primary food crop will find it necessary to switch to growing wheat does not help with determining whether areas dependents on locally grown wheat will experience food shortages.After all, information on whether people will switch to growing wheat does not tell us whether people who already depend on wheat will have enough wheat.

In other words, it’s not clear what effect people’s switching from growing soybeans to growing wheat would have on the supplies of wheat in areas already dependent on wheat, and we have no clear reason to believe that there would be any effect at all.
So, this choice does not mention the information we need.
(D) the populations of such areas will increase to sizes such that they outstrip the areas’ food production capabilities
We need information necessary for determining whether areas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food source will not experience food shortagesresulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels.

The information mentioned by this choice, whether the populations of such areas will increase to sizes such that they outstrip the areas’ food production capabilities, would help in determining whether these areas will experience food shortagesresulting from population growth.

Since we are concerned not with whether these areas will experience food shortages resulting from population growth but with whether these areas will experience food shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels, the information provided by this choice does not have the effect we need.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) levels of nutrients in wheat grown under high carbon dioxide conditions are as high as those in wheat grown today
We need information necessary for determining whether areas dependent on locally grown wheat as a food source will not experience food shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels.

We already know that, although less water will be available, when grown in conditions characterized by relatively high carbon dioxide levels, wheat requires lower than normal amounts of water for growth.So, it may be that, because these two factors offset each other, these areas will not experience food shortages resulting from increases in carbon dioxide levels.
What else would we need to know?
Well, we would also need to know whether levels of nutrients in wheat grown under high carbon dioxide conditions are as high as those in wheat grown today.

After all, if the levels of nutrients in wheat grown under high carbon dioxide conditions are NOT as high as those in wheat grown today, then, even if wheat supplies will not decrease, the nutritional, i.e., food, value of wheat will decrease.In that case, there is reason to believe that these areas will experience food shortages.

On the other hand, if the levels of nutrients in wheat grown under high carbon dioxide conditions ARE as high as those in wheat grown today, then, the nutritional value of wheat grown in the high carbon dioxide conditions of the future will be the same as that of wheat grown today.In that case, there is reason to believe that these areas will not experience food shortages.
So, we can see that this choice answers the question and logically completes the passage.

question.text: Which of the following most logically completes the passage?In today’s market for employees, many candidates are recommended for positions in companies by friends or colleagues within the companies.When recommended for a position in this way, a candidate often has a greater incentive than would otherwise exist to perform well in an interview for the position, because the candidate wants to avoid embarrassing the person who made the recommendation.As a result of this dynamic, it is often the case that __________.

answers.text: people responsible for filling positions prefer to hire candidates who have not embarrassed company insiders who have recommended them
of people who perform equally well in interviews, those who were recommended by company insiders are the least well suited for the positions to be filled
people tend to recommend for positions in the companies for which they work only friends or colleagues unlikely to embarrass them in interviews
those who are best prepared for interviews rather than those who are most qualified are hired to fill positions in companies
people who have recommended friends or colleagues as candidates for positions at their companies are willing to coach those friends or colleagues to prepare them for interviews

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer to this question will answer the following question:

What is the case as the result of the dynamic that, when recommended for a position by a friend or colleague, a candidate often has a greater incentive than would otherwise exist to perform well in an interview for the position?
In other words, we are looking for a conclusion supported by the statements in the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(A) people responsible for filling positions prefer to hire candidates who have not embarrassed company insiders who have recommended them
In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to remember that we are not looking for something that’s plausible.We are looking for a conclusion supported by the passage.
Of course, what this choice says is plausible.

At the same time, the passage says nothing about the preferences of people responsible for filling positions.Also, the fact that a person recommended by a friend or colleague often has a greater incentive than would otherwise exist to perform well in an interview does not support any conclusion about how people responsible for filling positions feel about hiring candidates who have embarrassed company insiders.
So, this choice does not follow from what the passage says and, thus, is not the correct answer.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) of people who perform equally well in interviews, those who were recommended by company insiders are the least well suited for the positions to be filled

The passage tells us that, when recommended for a position by a friend or colleague, a candidate often has a greater incentive than would otherwise exist to perform well in an interview for the position.Of course, what that information means is that candidates who have been recommended by friends or colleagues are likely to perform better that other similar candidates who don’t have this “greater incentive.”
So, what would likely happen as a result of this “dynamic”?

Well, the people who have been recommended by friends or colleagues would interview better than is typical of people with their qualifications.In other words, people recommended by company insiders would tend to interview as well as people who are better suited for the positions for which these people are being interviewed.

Thus, we can see that what would happen.As this choice says, basically, of people who perform equally well in interviews, those who were recommended by company insiders would tend to be the ones least well suited for the positions to be filled.
So, this choice follows from what the passage says and logically completes the passage.
(C) people tend to recommend for positions in the companies for which they work only friends or colleagues unlikely to embarrass them in interviews
In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to remember that we are not looking for something that’s plausible.We are looking for a conclusion supported by the passage.
Of course, what this choice says is plausible.
At the same time, the passage says nothing about how people who recommend  friends or colleagues for positions choose whom to recommend.
So, this choice does not follow from what the passage says and, thus, is not the correct answer.
(D) those who are best prepared for interviews rather than those who are most qualified are hired to fill positions in companies
In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to remember that we are not looking for something that’s plausible.We are looking for a conclusion supported by the passage.
What this choice says is somewhat plausible.
At the same time, the passage tells us nothing about who is “best prepared for interviews,” who is most qualified, or who gets hired.
So, this choice does not follow from what the passage says and, thus, is not the correct answer.
(E) people who have recommended friends or colleagues as candidates for positions at their companies are willing to coach those friends or colleagues to prepare them for interviews
In order to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to remember that we are not looking for something that’s plausible.We are looking for a conclusion supported by the passage.
Of course, what this choice says is plausible.

At the same time, the passage indicates nothing about what people who recommend friends or colleagues are willing to do other than recommend friends or colleagues.In other words, the passage provides no clear reason to believe that people who have recommended friends or colleagues are willing to coach those friends or colleagues.
So, this choice does not follow from what the passage says and, thus, is not the correct answer.

question.text: Economist: Increased U.S.consumer demand has resulted in increases in the prices at which farmers in Mexico sell avocados and standard of living increases for such farmers.However, there is a dark side to this boom; Mexican criminal cartels have begun to extort avocado farmers for money.To stop this extortion, U.S.consumers should boycott Mexican avocados.Politician: A boycott by U.S.consumers of Mexican avocados would serve to hurt financially the farmers but would not affect the cartels, which would continue the extortion unfettered.To stop the extortion, U.S.consumers need to pressure Mexican politicians to deploy security forces to protect farmers.The politician responds to the economist by

answers.text: providing evidence that U.S consumers’ boycotting Mexican avocados as suggested by the economist would not have significant effects.
showing that the approach advocated by the economist is not the only way to achieve the stated goal.
demonstrating that boycotting avocados from Mexico is necessary, but likely not sufficient, for fixing the problem the economist cites.
undermining the force of the evidence that the economist uses by showing that a factor that the economist did not mention may be causing the problem he cites.
highlighting that the economist’s suggestion that a boycott of Mexican avocados by U.S.consumers would fix the problem cited is misguided.

explanation_parts.text: (A) providing evidence that U. S consumers’ boycotting Mexican avocados as suggested by the economist would not have significant effects.
What this choice says sounds something like what the politician does, since this choice says, “would not have significant effects,” and the passage says, “would not affect the cartels.”
However, the politician does not respond by “providing evidence.” Rather the politician simply makes some unsupported statements about the effects of a boycott.
Furthermore, the politician does not say that boycotting “would not have significant effects.” On the contrary, the politician says that a boycott “would serve to hurt financially the farmers.”
(B) showing that the approach advocated by the economist is not the only way to achieve the stated goal.
This choice is incorrect because it uses “the only way.”

The politician does not show that the approach advocated by the economist is not “the only way” to achieve the stated goal.Rather, by saying that a boycott “would serve to hurt financially the farmers but would not affect the cartels, which would continue the extortion unfettered” the politician shows that the approach advocated by the economist is simplynot a wayto achieve the goal.

To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to notice that, in mentioning the approach of pressuring Mexican politicians to deploy security forces to protect farmers, the politician is not suggesting an additional approach to achieving the goal.Rather, the politician is mentioning an approach that would work, unlike the approach advocated by the economist.
(C) demonstrating that boycotting avocados from Mexico is necessary, but likely not sufficient, for fixing the problem the economist cites.
This choice does not match at all what the politician does.

The politician does not demonstrate that boycotting avocados is necessary.The politician says outright that boycotting avocados would be counterproductive, in that it would “serve to hurt financially the farmers but would not affect the cartels.”
(D) undermining the force of the evidence that the economist uses by showing that a factor that the economist did not mention may be causing the problem he cites.
This choice may sound as if it says something interesting, but what it says clearly does not match what goes on in the passage.
The politician does not bring up anything that shows that something other than what the economist has mentioned is causing the extortion.

In other words, the economist mentions a chain of events involving U.S.consumer demand, increases in the prices at which farmers sell avocados, and extortion by Mexican criminal cartels, and the politician does not bring up anything that shows that some other factor is causing any of those events.
YOUR CHOICE(E) highlighting that the economist’s suggestion that a boycott of Mexican avocados by U. S. consumers would fix the problem cited is misguided.

By stating that a boycott “would serve to hurt financially the farmers but would not affect the cartels, which would continue the extortion unfettered” the politician indicates that the approach suggested by the economist, while seemingly logical, would actually cause new problems without solving the extortion problem.
Since an approach that may seem logical but actually would cause new problems is a misguided approach, this choice accurately describes what the politician does.

question.text: Board Member to Stadium Manager: Before the latest season began, you said that fan rowdiness could be reduced through restriction of sales of beer, but nothing changed.Beer sales were not restricted, and fan rowdiness was just as problematic during the latest season as it was during the previous season.So, clearly, your assessment of the situation was not accurate.Stadium Manager: My claim has been that taking a particular course of action would result in a particular outcome, and I still stand by that claim.The stadium manager responds to the board member by

answers.text: explaining that her claim was that a particular event would occur eventually, not that it would occur during the latest season.
challenging the validity of board member’s account of the events that occurred during the latest season.
highlighting the difference between a claim about past events and a claim about a possible future outcome.
emphasizing that her claim is about only the effect that would result from the implementation of a proposed strategy.
pointing out the difference between what the board member says that she said and what she actually claimed.

explanation_parts.text: Notice what’s goes on in the exchange between the board member and the stadium manager.The board member says that the stadium manager did not assess the situation accurately.Then the stadium manager responds by clarifying that the board member has missed the stadium manager’s claim, which was not simply that rowdiness would decrease but rather that taking a particular course of action, which we can presume to be restriction of beer sales, would result in a particular outcome, which we can presume to be a reduction in rowdiness.
(A) explaining that her claim was that a particular event would occur eventually, not that it would occur during the latest season.

This choice is tempting, because one could perceive what the stadium manager says as indicating that she made a prediction that was not restricted to a particular period of time, such as the latest season.

However, by carefully considering what the stadium manager says, we can see the following.The stadium manager’s point is that her conclusion is not a prediction but rather a claim that a particular effect would result from taking a particular course of action,ifthat course of action were to be taken.

In other words, the stadium manager’s claim was not that the rowdinesswoulddecrease during the latest season and is not that the rowdinesswilldecrease eventually.It has been that rowdiness “couldbe reduced” through restriction of sales of beer.
(B) challenging the validity of board member’s account of the events that occurred during the latest season.

The stadium manager does not challenge the board member’s account of the events that occurred during the season.Rather, the stadium manager’s response is essentially that, while that account of events may be correct, she still stands by what she has said.
(C) highlighting the difference between a claim about past events and a claim about a possible future outcome.

While the board member does mention past events, and the stadium manager does mention “a particular outcome,” the stadium manager does not highlight any difference between any claim about past events and a claim about a possible future outcome.

Rather, the stadium manager’s response is, essentially, to emphasize that there is a difference between a prediction and what she has claimed, which is that a particular effect would result from taking a particular course of action,ifthat course of action were to be taken.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) emphasizing that her claim is about only the effect that would result from the implementation of a proposed strategy.
This is exactly how the stadium manager responds.

In saying “My claim has been that taking a particular course of action would result in a particular outcome,” the stadium manager is emphasizing that her conclusion is about only the effect thatwouldresult from the implementation of the proposed strategy, i.e., course of action, which is restriction of beer sales,ifthat course of action were to be taken.
YOUR CHOICE(E) pointing out the difference between what the board member says that she said and what she actually claimed.

We can see that what the board member said the stadium manager said, “fan rowdiness could be reduced through restriction of sales of beer,” is logically similar to what the stadium manager says she claims, “taking a particular course of action would result in a particular outcome.”
So, the stadium manager is certainly not pointing out a difference between anything she has claimed and what the board member says she said.

question.text: Mike: Reducing pesticide use was supposedly going to help bee populations recover.However, since we reduced pesticide use, the size of the local honeybee population has not changed.Clearly, the pesticides weren’t really making any difference.Ike: Because of increases in the presence of invasive plants in the area, the quantity of sources of food for honeybees has decreased.So, the fact that the honeybee population has not decreased in the face of this decrease in food availability is a testament to the effectiveness of reducing pesticide use.The way in which Ike makes his case is most analogous to which of the following?

answers.text: A farmer shows that a biodynamic method for increasing crop yields is effective, using as evidence increases in yields without increases in fertilizer use.
An engineer believes that traffic rerouting has improved safety, because the number of cars involved in crashes has remained the same while streets have become more crowded.
An ecologist demonstrates that planting milkweed plants can help sustain butterfly populations by showing a correlation between sizes of milkweed plant populations and sizes of butterfly populations.
An educator shows that a music program has helped students academically, using as evidence the fact that graduation rates have increased even at the weakest schools in the area.
A manager makes the case that offering flexible scheduling improves productivity by showing that production increased even as expenses decreased.

explanation_parts.text: We see that Mike has concluded the following:
Mike’s conclusion: The pesticides weren’t really making any difference.
The basis for Mike’s conclusion is that “since we reduced pesticide use, the size of the local honeybee population has not changed.”
To answer the question correctly, we have to find the choice that matches how Ike responds.

Ike responds by showing that the fact that the size of the local honeybee population has not changed does not mean that the pesticides were not making a difference or that reducing pesticide use has not made a difference.

He does so by providing the information that, even though there has been a factor that would be expected to cause a decrease in the honeybee population, “the quantity of sources of food for honeybees has decreased,” the honeybee population has not decreased.

Of course, the fact that the honeybee population has not decreased even though there has been a factor that would be expected to cause a decrease is reason to believe that there is another factor that has offset the factor that would be expected to cause a decrease.
What could that offsetting factor be? Ike appears to believe that that offsetting factor is reducing pesticide use.
Now, let’s find a choice analogous to the way in which Ike makes his case.
(A) A farmer shows that a biodynamic method for increasing crop yields is effective, using as evidence increases in yields without increases in fertilizer use.
Ike’s reasoning involves using the fact that something (the bee population)has not changedto support the conclusion that something (reducing pesticide use) is making a difference.
On the other hand, this argument involves using the fact that something (yields,has increasedto support the conclusion that something (a biodynamic method) is making a difference.
So, this choice does not match Ike’s reasoning.
YOUR CHOICE(B) An engineer believes that traffic rerouting has improved safety, because the number of cars involved in crashes has remained the same while streets have become more crowded.
The logic of this choice is similar to Ike’s reasoning.

This choice presents a situation in which something has not changed, “the number of cars involved in crashes has remained the same,” even though there has been a factor that would be expected to cause a change, “streets have become more crowded.”
Having observed those events, the engineer concludes that there must have been a factor offsetting the streets’ having become more crowded, which factor is “traffic rerouting.”
So, the engineer supports her conclusion in a way similar to how Ike supports his conclusion, as presented in the introduction to this explanation.

(C) An ecologist demonstrates that planting milkweed plants can help sustain butterfly populations by showing a correlation between sizes of milkweed plant populations and sizes of butterfly populations.
Ike’s reasoning involves using the fact that something (the bee population)has not changedto support the conclusion thata changein something else (pesticide use) is making a difference.

On the other hand, since “a correlation between” two things means that one of the things changes when the other changes, this argument involves using the fact that something (sizes of butterfly populations) changes when something else (sizes of milkweed plant populations) changes to support the conclusion that the second affects the first.
So, this choice does not match Ike’s reasoning.
(D) An educator shows that a music program has helped students academically, using as evidence the fact that graduation rates have increased even at the weakest schools in the area.
Ike’s reasoning involves using the fact that something (the bee population)has not changedto support the conclusion that something (reducing pesticide use) is making a difference.
On the other hand, this argument involves using the fact that something (graduation rates)has increasedto support the conclusion that something (a music program) has made a difference.
So, this choice does not match Ike’s reasoning.
(E) A manager makes the case that offering flexible scheduling improves productivity by showing that production increased even as expenses decreased.

Ike’s reasoning involves using the fact that something (the bee population)has not changedeven though something (the quantity of food sources) has decreased, to support the conclusion that something else (reducing pesticide use) is an offsetting factor that is making a difference.

On the other hand, this argument involves using the fact that something (production)has increasedeven though something (expenses) has decreased, to support the conclusion that something (flexible scheduling) is making a difference.
So, this choice does not match Ike’s reasoning.

question.text: Trickle-down theory involves the idea that taxes on corporations and on the wealthy should be as low as possible so as to stimulate business investment, which, in theory, would benefit all of society in the long-term.However,this theory is flawedbecause it incorrectly assumes that investors, savers, and owners of companies are the drivers of economic growth, and becauseit therefore misses the fact that the real drivers of economic growth are the workers and employees at the lowest levels of business and commerce, those who run factories, erect skyscrapers, drive buses, and build roads.In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second provides support for the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion of the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is an observation for which the second provides an explanation; the second is the main conclusion but not the only conclusion.
The first and second each provide evidence for the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is an intermediate conclusion that supports the argument’s main conclusion; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.

explanation_parts.text: First let’s identify the main conclusion of the argument.
Conclusion:this theory is flawed
We can tell that that statement is the main conclusion because of the following:

It’s supported by two other statements, “it incorrectly assumes that …” and“it therefore misses the fact that ….”It does not support any other statements.The statement “Trickle-down theory involves …,” is a statement of fact and, thus, cannot be the main conclusion.
Now, having identified the main conclusion, we can see that the first boldfaced portion is the main conclusion, and that the second boldfaced portion provides support for that main conclusion.

One interesting thing to notice about this passage is that the two boldfaced portions can appear to be a premise and a conclusion that is supported by that premise, as we can more clearly see by putting them next to each other.

this theory is flawed…it therefore misses the fact that the real drivers of economic growth are the workers and employees at the lowest levels of business and commerce, those who run factories, erect skyscrapers, drive buses, and build roads.

So, we have to be careful to consider the boldfaced portions in the context of the entire passage in order to avoid misperceiving the two boldfaced portions as support for the argument’s main conclusion and the argument’s main conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second provides support for the argument’s main conclusion.
As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion is the argument’s main conclusion, and the second portion provides support for that conclusion.
(B) The first is a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion of the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.

To avoid choosing this choice, we have to notice that the first boldfaced portion is not a consideration in opposition to a conclusion that the main conclusion of the argument opposes; it is the main conclusion itself.

Also, we have to notice that, even though the second boldface portion begins with “it therefore” and appears at the end of the passage, it is not the main conclusion; it is a statement that supports the main conclusion.
(C) The first is an observation for which the second provides an explanation; the second is the main conclusion but not the only conclusion.
The first part of this choice is debatably correct, because the second boldfaced portion does in a way explain the first.
However, since, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is not the main conclusion of the argument, we can eliminate this choice.

Notice that, to avoid choosing this choice, we have to notice that, even though the second boldface portion begins with “it therefore” and appears at the end of the passage, it is not the main conclusion.
(D) The first and second each provide evidence for the argument’s main conclusion.

While it is true that the second boldfaced portion provides evidence for the argument’s conclusion, we can eliminate this choice, because the first boldfaced portion does not provide evidence for the main conclusion; it is the main conclusion.
(E) The first is an intermediate conclusion that supports the argument’s main conclusion; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
This choice is very tempting for the following reasons.

It’s easy to misperceive the first boldfaced portion as an intermediate conclusion that is supported by the statement “it incorrectly assumes that investors, savers, and owners of companies are the drivers of economic growth.”

Also, it’s easy to misperceive the second boldfaced portion as a main conclusion supported by the first boldfaced portion because the second boldfaced portion begins with “it therefore” and appears at the end of the passage.

However, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion does not support any other statement in the passage, and the second boldfaced portion is support for the main conclusion, not the main conclusion.

question.text: Health Commissioner: I see that, around when a reality TV show about the challenges faced by teenage mothers began to be broadcast, the decrease in the teen pregnancy rate in our country accelerated.I wonder whether there is a connection between the broadcasting of the show and the acceleration of the decrease.Analyst: In regions of the country where that show isn’t available on basic cable, the rates of decrease in teen pregnancy rates have barely changed since the show began to be broadcast.In responding to the Health Commissioner, the analyst accomplishes which of the following?

answers.text: Provides evidence that serves to call into question whether there truly has been an acceleration in the decrease in the country’s teen pregnancy rate.
Supports a conclusion by demonstrating what occurs when a variable is eliminated.
Provides reason to believe that, in some areas of the country, teen pregnancy rates are likely to remain approximately the same unless a new factor comes into play.
Uses a subset of data to demonstrate that any conclusion supported by only the aggregate data cited by the Health Commissioner is suspect.
Makes a convincing case for the government’s using its regulatory authority to cause cable television providers to bundle services rather than offer services on an à la carte basis.

explanation_parts.text: (A) Provides evidence that serves to call into question whether there truly has been an acceleration in the decrease in the country’s teen pregnancy rate.
We can see that the Health Commissioner has stated as fact that there has been an acceleration in the decrease in the country’s teen pregnancy rate.

What the analyst says, that, in certain regions of the country, rates of decrease in teen pregnancy rates have barely changed, does not conflict with or serve to call into question the fact stated by the Health Commissioner.

After all, there could very well have been a country-wide acceleration in the decrease in the teen pregnancy rate even if, in certain regions of the country, rates of decrease in teen pregnancy rates have barely changed.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Supports a conclusion by demonstrating what occurs when a variable is eliminated.
The Health Commissioner has noticed a correlation:
Around when a reality TV show about the challenges faced by teenage mothers began to be broadcast, the decrease in the teen pregnancy rate in our country accelerated.
Having noticed that correlation, the Health Commissioner is wondering whether it makes sense to conclude the following:
There is a connection between the broadcasting of the show and the acceleration of the decrease.
The analyst responds by saying what has occurred where the show is not available:
The rates of decrease in teen pregnancy rates have barely changed.
Notice that what the analyst has done is shown what occurs when the variable, the show, is not present:
There is barely any change.
By showing that there is barely any change when the show is not present, the analyst provides some confirmation that the show is the cause of the acceleration in the decrease in teen pregnancy rates.
Thus, this choice describes exactly what the analyst accomplishes in responding to the Health Commissioner.
(C) Provides reason to believe that, in some areas of the country, teen pregnancy rates are likely to remain approximately the same unless a new factor comes into play.

To avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to notice that what the analyst says is that, in areas of the country where the show isn’t available on basic cable,rates ofdecreasein teen pregnancy rates have barely changed.
Notice that “rates of decrease” are rates of change.So, the analyst is essentially saying that, where the show isn’t available on basic cable, the rates of change have not changed.

Of course, the fact that rates of change have not changed does not mean that change is not occurring.It means that change is occurring at the same rate at which it was occurring before the show began to be broadcast.So, given what the analyst says, it could very well be that teen pregnancy rates are decreasing and, thus, that teen pregnancy rates arenotlikely to remain the same.

So, actually, the analyst does not provide any reason whatsoever to believe that, in some areas of the country, teen pregnancy rates are likely to remain approximately the same unless a new factor comes into play.
(D) Uses a subset of data to demonstrate that any conclusion supported by only the aggregate data cited by the Health Commissioner is suspect.
This choice starts off matching how the analyst responds, because the analyst does bring up a subset of data.

Whereas the Health Commissioner mentions data on decreases in teen pregnancy rates “in our country,” the analyst mentions a subset of that data, data on decreases in teen pregnancy rates in certain regions of the country.

However, this choice is not correct, because the analyst does not use that subset of data to demonstrate that any conclusion supported by only the aggregate data cited by the Health Commissioner is suspect.Rather, as discussed in the explanation for (B), by showing what occurs when a variable, the show, is removed, the analyst provides additional support for a conclusion that the Health Commissioner is considering, the conclusion that there is a connection between the broadcasting of the show and the acceleration of the decrease in the teen pregnancy rate.
(E) Makes a convincing case for the government’s using its regulatory authority to cause cable television providers to bundle services rather than offer services on an à la carte basis.
What the analyst says does seem to indicate that there would be a benefit to making the reality TV show about the challenges faced by teenage mothers more widely available on basic cable.

However, indicating that there would be a benefit to making that one show more widely available on basic cable does not constitute making a convincing case for the government’s causing cable television providers to change how they offer services.Such a “convincing case” would have to take into account multiple factors, not just the value of one show that isn’t available on basic cable in some areas.

question.text: Because of a growing supply of PhDs competing for jobs, the number of adjunct professors – those teaching limited numbers of classes, not eligible for tenure, and costing far less than their full-time, tenure-track counterparts – working at universities has doubled, whereas the number of full-time, tenure-track professors has increased by only about ten percent.Many claim that this pattern represents ruthless labor exploitation by universities.However, when many PhDs compete for adjunct professor positions, making university staffing less expensive, universities can utilize their resources to acquire top minds in fields and reward them with tenure-track positions.The author develops the argument by

answers.text: questioning the support for a claim by showing that the data used to support the claim is biased.
casting doubt on a cause-and-effect conclusion by demonstrating that the observed pattern persists without the presence of the supposed cause.
describing certain consequences of current market conditions and outlining an approach for rectifying those conditions.
presenting his conclusion and then conceding that there is evidence in favor of an alternative conclusion.
countering a conclusion indirectly by providing information supporting an alternative perspective.

explanation_parts.text: (A) questioning the support for a claim by showing that the data used to support the claim is biased.

The claim that the author mentions is the claim that “this pattern,” which is a much larger increase in the number of adjunct professors than in the number of tenure-track professors, “represents ruthless labor exploitation by universities.”
We can rule out this choice because the author never indicates that those numbers are “biased.”
(B) casting doubt on a cause-and-effect conclusion by demonstrating that the observed pattern persists without the presence of the supposed cause.

We could perhaps consider the claim “this pattern represents ruthless labor exploitation by universities” a cause-and-effect conclusion, with the cause being “ruthless labor exploitation” and the effect being “this pattern.”

OK, but does the author demonstrate that “this pattern,” which is a much larger increase in the number of adjunct professors than in the number of tenure-track professors, persists without the presence of the supposed cause?
Where does the author show that the pattern persists without the presence of the supposed cause?

In saying that, because of the pattern, “universities can utilize their resources to acquire top minds in fields and reward them with tenure-track positions,” the author may be implying that “this pattern”wouldpersist without the presence of ruthless labor exploitation.
At the same time, the author does not provide an example of a situation in which ruthless labor exploitation clearly is not present, yet the pattern persists.
So, the author does not do what this choice says.
(C) describing certain consequences of current market conditions and outlining an approach for rectifying those conditions.

This choice starts off matching how the author develops the argument, because the author does describe certain consequences of market conditions in saying, “the number of adjunct professors … has doubled, whereas the number of full-time, tenure-track professors has increased by only about ten percent.”
However, this choice is not correct, because the author does not outline any approach for rectifying the conditions that have led to those consequences.
(D) presenting his conclusion and then conceding that there is evidence in favor of an alternative conclusion.

Notice that the author does not present “his conclusion” and then concede something about “an alternative conclusion.” Instead, the author presents what “many claim,” which does not seem to be “his conclusion,” and then presents an alternative perspective, which does seem to be the author’s perspective.
So, what this choice says is quite different from what the author does.
YOUR CHOICE(E) countering a conclusion indirectly by providing information supporting an alternative perspective.

The author presents a conclusion, what “many claim,” which is that “this pattern,” a much larger increase in the number of adjunct professors than in the number of tenure-track professors, “represents ruthless labor exploitation by universities.”

Then, the author counters that conclusion indirectly by saying, “However, when many PhDs compete for adjunct professor positions, making university staffing less expensive, universities can utilize their resources to acquire top minds in fields and reward them with tenure-track positions.”

That information provided by the author supports an alternative perspective, the perspective that universities are not simply ruthlessly exploiting labor but rather doing their best to staff optimally.
So, the author does exactly what this choice says.

question.text: Because people suffering from type II diabetes have blood sugar levels significantly higher than those of healthy people, for many years, doctors held the belief that consumption of sugary foods was the cause of this disease.Although that belief was plausible,those doctors were incorrect.Although consumption of sugary foods is generally not a healthy action, persistently high blood sugar levels tend to follow the onset of type II diabetes rather than precede it.In fact, numerous research studies have confirmed thattype II diabetes is actually caused by consumption, and later accumulation in muscle and liver cells, of animal fats.In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is the argument’s main conclusion but not its only conclusion; the second is an intermediate conclusion used to support the first.
The first is an assumption upon which support for the argument’s main conclusion depends; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is a premise supporting the argument’s main conclusion; so is the second.
The first is a conclusion for which the argument provides support; the second presents evidence in support of that conclusion.
The first is used to provide context; the second is a finding that the argument seeks to explain.

explanation_parts.text: First, let’s identify the main conclusion of the argument, which is a statement that is supported by other statements in the passage and that does not support another conclusion.
The main conclusion is the following:
those doctors were incorrect.
That conclusion is supported by all the sentences the follow it.
(A) The first is the argument’s main conclusion but not its only conclusion; the second is an intermediate conclusion used to support the first.
The first part of this choice is incorrect, because the first boldfaced portion is not only the argument’s main conclusion but also the argument’s only conclusion.

We might be tempted to believe that “consumption of sugary foods was the cause of this disease,” is another conclusion of the argument, but that conclusion is not a conclusion of this argument, it is the belief, or conclusion, of “many doctors.”

We also might be tempted to believe that“type II diabetes is actually caused by consumption, and later accumulation in muscle and liver cells, of animal fats,”is a conclusion of the argument, but it’s actually a premise of the argument that has been confirmed by study results.

So, given what we just discussed, we can see that the second part of this choice is also incorrect, because the second boldfaced portion is not an intermediate conclusion, though it is used to support the first boldfaced portion.
(B) The first is an assumption upon which support for the argument’s main conclusion depends; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.

This choice could seem to be correct, because we could perceive the first boldfaced portion to be an assumption upon which the second boldfaced portion depends and the second to be a conclusion, as we can see more easily by putting the two together.
those doctors were incorrect.type II diabetes is actually caused by consumption, and later accumulation in muscle and liver cells, of animal fats.
However, since actually the first boldfaced portion is a conclusion supported by the second boldfaced portion, this choice is incorrect.

To avoid being fooled into choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful to consider the boldfaced portions in the context of the entire passage, not just alone, so that we don’t get the impression that they are related to each other in a way in which they are not related.
(C) The first is a premise supporting the argument’s main conclusion; so is the second.

While the second boldfaced portion is a premise supporting the argument’s main conclusion, we can eliminate this choice because the first boldfaced portion is not a premise; it’s the argument’s main conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The first is a conclusion for which the argument provides support; the second presents evidence in support of that conclusion.

As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion is the main conclusion, i.e., a conclusion for which the argument provides support, and the second boldfaced portion presents evidence in support of that conclusion.
(E) The first is used to provide context; the second is a finding that the argument seeks to explain.

The first boldfaced portion may seem to be used to provide context since it appears toward the beginning of the passage along with other statements that appear to provide context for what follows.However, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion is actually the argument’s main conclusion.
Also, the argument says nothing that explains the second boldfaced portion.Rather, the argument simply presents the second boldfaced portion as a finding that supports the main conclusion.

question.text: While some fields are known hotbeds of corruption or incompetence, the medical field is perceived by many as a bastion of integrity and expertise.However, like those of professionals in other fields, the motivations and levels of knowledge and professionalism of medical professionals vary widely, and so, just as one would not trust the recommendations of a stockbroker merely because that individual is licensed and has a title, so one would do well to treat with some skepticism the pronouncements of physicians.The argument proceeds by

answers.text: establishing that the attitude commonly adopted in one situation and that commonly adopted in another situation are more similar than most people realize.
using information about the motivations and levels of expertise of professionals in different fields to support a conclusion about the limitations of the effectiveness of licensing.
drawing an analogy between two types of situations to underscore that a stance commonly adopted in one would be justifiably adopted in the other as well.
outlining why neither of two common contrasting perceptions of different professions is truly more accurate than the other.
calling into question the trustworthiness of members of a profession by demonstrating that their motivations are identical to those of members of a profession known for corruption.

explanation_parts.text: (A) establishing that the attitude commonly adopted in one situation and that commonly adopted in another situation are more similar than most people realize.

To avoid being fooled into choosing this choice, we have to notice that the similarities mentioned in the passage are between the medical professionals and professionals in other fields, not between attitudes.
Notice also that the argument does indicate that any particular attitudes are “commonly adopted.”
(B) using information about the motivations and levels of expertise of professionals in different fields to support a conclusion about the limitations of the effectiveness of licensing.

The passage does not state or support any conclusion specifically about the limitations of the effectiveness of licensing.The only conclusion stated is “one would do well to treat with some skepticism the pronouncements of physicians.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) drawing an analogy between two types of situations to underscore that a stance commonly adopted in one would be justifiably adopted in the other as well.

The author uses an analogy between licensed stockbrokers making recommendations and physicians making “pronouncements” to underscore that the skeptical type of stance commonly adopted toward the recommendations of stockbrokers would be justifiably adopted toward the pronouncements of physicians as well.
So, this choice accurately describes what the argument does.
(D) outlining why neither of two common contrasting perceptions of different professions is truly more accurate than the other.

This choice starts off seeming correct because the passage does mention two contrasting perceptions when it says, “While some fields are known hotbeds of corruption or incompetence, the medical field is perceived by many as a bastion of integrity and expertise.”

Notice, however, that the passage never explains why, or even says that, neither of these perceptions is more accurate than the other.In fact, the passage provides no information on the relative accuracy of these perceptions.
(E) calling into question the trustworthiness of members of a profession by demonstrating that their motivations are identical to those of members of a profession known for corruption.

This choice starts off resembling how the argument proceeds since the argument’s stating that “one would do well to treat with some skepticism the pronouncements of physicians” could be considered “calling into question the trustworthiness of members of a profession.”

However, this choice becomes clearly incorrect as it continues, because the passage does not “demonstrate” or even suggest that the motivations of physicians are “identical” to those of members of any other profession.

question.text: Marketing Director: We recently switched from emailing our customers 10-question satisfaction surveys after they purchase one of our motorboats to emailing them 50-question satisfaction surveys after such purchases.Since that change, we’ve seen a decline in the average product quality satisfaction level reported by customers who have completed and returned the surveys.It’s clear that we must immediately improve the quality of our motorboats.Chief Product Officer: Customers who are willing take the time to complete and return a 50-question survey are likely those who are unusually upset regarding some aspect of our motorboats.We can’t let the sentiments of these customers unduly impact the way we design, engineer, and build our products.The Chief Product Officer responds to the Marketing Director by

answers.text: showing that an assumption upon which the Marketing Director’s argument rests is invalid.
establishing that the company must collect more information before making important decisions.
pointing out that the Marketing Director has misunderstood the responses to the surveys.
demonstrating the importance of leaving product quality decisions to motorboat designers and engineers.
explaining why sentiments of customers are generally not reliable enough for basing product design decisions upon them

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer to this question does not jump out as an obvious match for how the Chief Marketing Officer responds.So, probably the best way to handle this question is to eliminate all the incorrect choices, all of which are clearly incorrect, leaving the correct answer as the only viable possibility.
YOUR CHOICE(A) showing that an assumption upon which the Marketing Director’s argument rests is invalid.
While this choice may not obviously resemble how the Chief Product Officer responds, let’s carefully consider how the Chief Product Officer responds.

The Chief Product Officer disputes the Marketing Director’s conclusion that “we must immediately improve the quality of our motorboats” by providing reason to believe that the survey results are from “unusually upset” customers and, thus, are not representative of the sentiments of the company’s customers in general.
