Notice that, in concluding that the company must immediately improve product quality, the Marketing Director seems to have assumed that the survey results are representative of the sentiments of more than a small, unusual segment of the company’s customers.Otherwise, responding to those results by “immediately” changing the products would not make sense.

So, by showing that the survey results are likely representative of the sentiments only a small, unusual segment of the company’s customers, the Chief Product Officer shows that an assumption upon which the Marketing Director’s argument rests is invalid.
(B) establishing that the company must collect more information before making important decisions.

While the Chief Product Officer discounts the survey results, the Chief Product officer does not establish anything about collecting more information.Rather, the Chief Product Officer provides reason to believe that the survey results, the already available information, are not representative of the sentiments of the company’s customers in general.
(C) pointing out that the Marketing Director has misunderstood the responses to the surveys.
This choice is tempting because the Chief Product Officer does point something out.So, this choice starts off correctly.
However, the Chief Product Officer does not point out that the Marketing Director hasmisunderstoodthe responses.

Rather, the Chief Product Officer points out that,even though the Marketing Director has correctly understood the responses, the responses should not be allowed to unduly impact the company’s product related decisions.
(D) demonstrating the importance of leaving product quality decisions to motorboat designers and engineers.

We have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing this choice by the fact that the wording of this choice resembles wording that appears in the passage, “the way we design, engineer, and build our products.”
After all, the Chief product officer does not say anything that clearly demonstrates the importance of “leaving product quality decisions to” anyone in particular.

In other words, the Chief Product officer says only that certain sentiments, the sentiments of the survey respondents, should not be allowed to “unduly impact” decisions, not that decisions should be left to the people mentioned by this choice.
(E) explaining why sentiments of customers are generally not reliable enough for basing product design decisions upon them
This choice is tempting because the Chief Product Officer does recommend not letting certain customer sentiments unduly impact the way the company designs products.
Notice, however, that the Chief Product Officer does not say that customer sentiments are generally not reliable, and does not say that design decisions should not be based on customer sentiments.

The Chief Product Officer’s point is about how the company should respond to the responses to one particular survey, which he characterizes not as unreliable but as not representative of the sentiments of the company’s customers in general, because these responses are from customers who are “likely those who are unusually upset regarding some aspect of our motorboats.”

question.text: In recent years, the health benefits of wine have become an important selling point for the wine industry.Gone are the days when wine marketers simply promoted wine as a beverage for the elite.However, the wine industry often fails to mention thatwine is more than just fermented grape juice; in addition to health promoting substances, often contained in wine are a number of additives, stabilizers, and preservatives that are probably less-than-health-promoting.Further research that investigates the healthfulness of drinking wine is necessary.In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is a fact used to provide context; the second is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
The first is a claim that the argument disputes; the second is a finding whose implications are at issue in the argument.
The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument rejects; the second is evidence used to support the author’s rejection of that conclusion.
The first mentions a conclusion that the argument’s author considers suspect; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument calls into question; the second is evidence used to support the main conclusion of the argument.

explanation_parts.text: First, let’s identify the main conclusion of the argument.
To identify the main conclusion, we have to find a statement that is supported by at least one other statement in the passage and does not support any other conclusion in the passage.

The statement“wine is more than just fermented grape juice,”could seem to be supported by “in addition to health promoting substances, often contained in wine are a number of additives, stabilizers, and preservatives that are probably less-than-health-promoting.”

However, upon further consideration, we can see that“wine is more than just fermented grape juice,”is not really a conclusion supported by the sentence that follows it.It’s a statement of fact that is followed by more details on the same topic.Furthermore,“wine is more than just fermented grape juice,”supports a conclusion further along in the passage.

That conclusion is “Further research that investigates the healthfulness of drinking wine is necessary.” Since that conclusion is supported by the statements that precede it, and since that conclusion does not support any other conclusion, that conclusion is the argument’s main conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The first is a fact used to provide context; the second is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
We can see that the first boldfaced portion is simply a statement of fact that is not supported by any other statement in the passage and is used to provide context.
Also, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
(B) The first is a claim that the argument disputes; the second is a finding whose implications are at issue in the argument.

In order to avoid falling for this trap choice, we have to see the following.While the argument provides reason to question the healthfulness of drinking wine, the argument does not in any way dispute what the first boldface portion says, which is not that drinking wine is healthful but that the health benefits of wine have become an important selling point for the wine industry.
Also, calling the second boldfaced portion a finding is a bit off, because it’s not a finding, i.e., something that has been discovered.It’s a simple fact.
Furthermore, the second boldfaced portion is not “at issue,” i.e., in dispute.It’s an undisputed fact.
(C) The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument rejects; the second is evidence used to support the author’s rejection of that conclusion.

The first boldfaced portion is not used to support any conclusion that the argument rejects.The argument does not reject any conclusion, and the first boldfaced portion is simply a fact that the argument uses to provide context.
As a result of the issues with the first part of this choice, the second part of this choice does not make sense either.
(D) The first mentions a conclusion that the argument’s author considers suspect; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.

In order to avoid falling for this trap choice, we have to see the following.While the argument provides reason to question the healthfulness of drinking wine, the argument does not consider suspect what the first boldface portion says, which is not that drinking wine is healthful but that the health benefits of wine have become an important selling point for the wine industry.
Also, we have to notice that the second boldfaced portion not the main conclusion of the argument but rather a fact that supports the main conclusion.
(E) The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument calls into question; the second is evidence used to support the main conclusion of the argument.
As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.

However, we can eliminate this choice, because the first boldfaced portion is not used to support any conclusion that the argument calls into question.It is simply a fact that the argument uses to provide context.

question.text: The Hubble constant, the relative rate of the expansion of the universe, has been calculated through the use of two techniques that produce differing values.The first technique uses the cosmic microwave background, electromagnetic radiation whose origin is the Big Bang, which created our known universe.The second technique uses the cosmological red shift, which occurs as galaxies move away from Earth as the universe expands.This divergence in the values of the constant could mean that there are undiscovered phenomena in our universe the discovery of which could radically alter our understanding of the universe’s development.The author proceeds by

answers.text: presenting a scientific theory, explaining some problems with that theory, and then making a claim about future discoveries related to that theory.
outlining an example of an instance in which a result of mathematical calculation is the indication of a possibility of existence of unknowns.
explaining why a scientific constant has been incorrectly calculated and then describing consequences resulting from that miscalculation.
undermining the force of the evidence supporting a common scientific understanding by providing two examples the results of which contradict each other.
highlighting issues related to a scientific theory and then describing what might occur if that theory were found to be incorrect.

explanation_parts.text: (A) presenting a scientific theory, explaining some problems with that theory, and then making a claim about future discoveries related to that theory.

This choice is designed to resemble what the author does: we could perceive the Hubble constant to be a “theory,” the differing values produced by the two techniques to be “problems,” and the implications of the divergence in the values to be “a claim about future discoveries.”

However, the truth is that the Hubble constant is not a theory.It’s simply a constant, i.e., a value, and the differing values are not “problems.” So, what this choice says differs significantly from what the author does.
YOUR CHOICE(B) outlining an example of an instance in which a result of mathematical calculation is the indication of a possibility of existence of unknowns.
The author outlines results of calculation of the Hubble constant.
When the Hubble constant is calculated with the use of one technique, one value is produced.When it is calculated via the use of another technique, a different value is produced.

The author also says that the fact that two different values are produced “could mean that there are undiscovered phenomena in our universe.” So, the author says, essentially, that a result of the calculation of the Hubble constant is the indication of a possbility of existence of unknowns.
So, this choice matches what the author does.
(C) explaining why a scientific constant has been incorrectly calculated and then describing consequences resulting from that miscalculation.

Notice that the author does not say that the Hubble constant has been “incorrectly calculated.” Rather the author says that calculation of the constant via the use of two different techniques produces two different values.
Furthermore, the author does not “explain why” the two techniques produce different values.
(D) undermining the force of the evidence supporting a common scientific understanding by providing two examples the results of which contradict each other.

This choice is tempting, because what it says resembles what the author does, in that what the author says about two techniques that produce different results is somewhat like what this choice says about two examples the results of which contradict each other.
Notice, however, that the author does not say that the values produced by the two techniques “contradict each other.” The author says only that values are “differing.”
Furthermore, in mentioning the two techniques and their differing results, the author does not undermine the force of any evidence supporting any particular understanding.
(E) highlighting issues related to a scientific theory and then describing what might occur if that theory were found to be incorrect.
Notice that the Hubble constant is not a theory; it’s a value, a constant, a number.So, this choice is way off.
Furthermore, the author does not describe anything that might occur if the Hubble constant “were found to be incorrect.”

question.text: Physicist: For hundreds of years, the predominant thinking in the field of cosmology was that, although black holes, extremely dense regions of space that exhibit strong gravitational fields, do exist,they are not of sufficient mass to bend electromagnetic waves passing close to them.However, Albert Einstein showed that, in fact, the gravitational effect that we observe between objects of mass, including black holes, results from those objects’ warping of spacetime.Because whenever spacetime becomes warped, electromagnetic waves will bend around that warping of spacetime, it must be true thatblack holes do in fact bend electromagnetic waves passing close to them.In the physicist’s reasoning, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is a claim that the physicist sets out to dispute; the second is evidence that the physicist offers in support of her position.
Both the first and the second provide evidence in support of the position that the physicist defends.
The first is a conclusion for which the physicist provides no support; the second is position taken by the physicist.
The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the physicist calls into question; the second is that conclusion.
The first is evidence that has been used in support of the position taken by the physicist; the second is a position about which the physicist has reservations.

explanation_parts.text: Let’s find the main conclusion, which must be a statement that is supported by other statements in the passage.
Going through the passage, we can see that the only statement in the passage that is supported by any other statement is the following:
it must be true thatblack holes do in fact bend electromagnetic waves passing close to them.
That statement is supported by two statements that precede it:
Albert Einstein showed that, in fact, the gravitational effect that we observe between objects of mass, including black holes, results from those objects’ warping of spacetime.
whenever spacetime becomes warped, electromagnetic waves will bend around that warping of spacetime
So, the main conclusion is “it must be true thatblack holes do in fact bend electromagnetic waves passing close to them.”
(A) The first is a claim that the physicist sets out to dispute; the second is evidence that the physicist offers in support of her position.
The first part of this choice is basically correct, because the physicist does present a position against the first boldfaced portion.

However, we can eliminate this choice because, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is not evidence that the physicist offers in support of her position.It is her position.
(B) Both the first and the second provide evidence in support of the position that the physicist defends.
This choice is completely incorrect.
The first boldfaced portion is not evidence in support of a position that the physicist defends.It a position that the physicist argues against.
Also, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is not evidence in support of a position.It is the physicist’s position.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The first is a conclusion for which the physicist provides no support; the second is position taken by the physicist.
The first boldfaced portion is a conclusion that was predominant in the field of cosmology, and the physicist provides no support for that conclusion.So, the first part of this choice is correct.
Also, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is the physicist’s position, i.e., the physicist’s conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the physicist calls into question; the second is that conclusion.
The first boldfaced portion is not evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the physicist calls into question.It is a conclusion that the physicist argues against.
The second boldfaced portion is not a conclusion that the physicist calls into question.It’s a conclusion for which the physicist provides support.
(E) The first is evidence that has been used in support of the position taken by the physicist; the second is a position about which the physicist has reservations.
The first boldfaced portion is not evidence that has been used in support of the position taken by the physicist.It’s a conclusion that the physicist argues against.
Also, while the second boldfaced portion is a position, the physicist does not express any reservations, i.e., doubts, about that position.

question.text: Sleep Medicine Specialist: Sleep is critical to our wellbeing, yet our research suggests that about 50 percent of all Americans are sleep-deprived, increasing their risk of developing a wide range of health issues, such as diabetes, heart disease, and depression.Although the persistent use of technology can’t bear all of the blame, it certainly takes ownership for more than just a few sleepless nights.Not only do mobile devices emit blue light, which interferes with the body’s production of melatonin, a hormone essential for proper sleep, but they also keep our brains in an “awake-type” state by offering up endless opportunities for reading, watching videos, and chatting with friends.For better sleep, people should avoid the use of technology within 90 minutes of bedtime.In the Sleep Medicine Specialist’s argument, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is an intermediate conclusion that supports the argument’s main conclusion; the second is that main conclusion.
The first is the argument’s main conclusion; the second supports that conclusion and is itself a conclusion for which support is provided.
The first is a position that the argument acknowledges but disagrees with; the second is the argument’s only explicit conclusion.
The first is a conclusion for which no support is provided; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is a premise supporting the argument’s only explicit conclusion; the second is that conclusion.

explanation_parts.text: To set ourselves up to arrive at the correct answer to this question, let’s find the main conclusion.
In going through the passage, we can see that there are two statements that are conclusions supported by other statements.
The first statement supported by another statement is:
Although the persistent use of technology can’t bear all of the blame, it certainly takes ownership for more than just a few sleepless nights.
It is supported by these statements:

Not only do mobile devices emit blue light, which interferes with the body’s production of melatonin, a hormone essential for proper sleep, but they also keep our brains in an “awake-type” state by offering up endless opportunities for reading, watching videos, and chatting with friends.
The second statement supported by other statements is:
For better sleep, people should avoid the use of technology within 90 minutes of bedtime.

That statement is supported by the statements that precede it, including the statement“Although the persistent use of technology can’t bear all of the blame, it certainly takes ownership for more than just a few sleepless nights.”

So, the first boldfaced statement is part of the support for the second boldfaced statement, and the second boldfaced statement is supported by other statements and does not support any other statement.

Therefore, we can consider the second boldfaced statement,“For better sleep, people should avoid the use of technology within 90 minutes of bedtime,”the main conclusion and consider the first boldfaced statement an intermediate conclusion that supports the main conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The first is an intermediate conclusion that supports the argument’s main conclusion; the second is that main conclusion.
As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion is an intermediate conclusion that supports the second boldfaced portion, which is the argument’s main conclusion.
(B) The first is the argument’s main conclusion; the second supports that conclusion and is itself a conclusion for which support is provided.
This choice is a little tricky, because the first boldfaced portion is a conclusion, but it is not the argument’s main conclusion.It is an intermediate conclusion that supports the main conclusion.

Also, the second boldfaced portion is a conclusion for which support is provided, but, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion does not support the first boldfaced portion.The second boldfaced portion is supported by the first boldfaced portion.
(C) The first is a position that the argument acknowledges but disagrees with; the second is the argument’s only explicit conclusion.

The argument does not disagree with the first boldfaced portion, and the second boldfaced portion is not the argument’s only explicit conclusion, since the first boldfaced portion is also a conclusion.
(D) The first is a conclusion for which no support is provided; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.

This choice is super tricky because the second boldfaced portion is the argument’s main conclusion and because the first boldfaced portion could appear to be a conclusion for which no support is provided.

All the same, the first part of this choice is incorrect, because the argument does provide support for the first boldfaced portion.As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the statements in the sentence that immediately follows the first boldfaced portion support the first boldfaced portion.
(E) The first is a premise supporting the argument’s only explicit conclusion; the second is that conclusion.
This choice is tricky because the first boldfaced portion does support the second, which does state a conclusion.

However, we can eliminate this choice because the conclusion stated by the second boldfaced portion is not the argument’sonlyexplicit conclusion.The first boldfaced portion expresses a conclusion as well.

question.text: Desert locusts, flying insects that can easily consume their weight in food each day, travel in swarms of upwards of hundreds of millions of locusts, which can devour thousands of square miles of agricultural crops in a week.Some entomologists who study the desert locust feel thatthe aerial spraying of insecticides is the only effective method to reduce their numbers;these entomologists are mistaken.Not only are such insecticides extremely harmful to the environment but also, they actually might contribute to the acceleration of locust population growth.Furthermore,there are available natural methods for effectively controlling locust populations, including the planting of new forests, the elimination of cattle grazing, and the reduction of greenhouse gas production.In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is a conclusion that is not the only conclusion present in the passage; the second is an example used to support the first.
The first and second are both used by the author to support the author’s main conclusion.
The first is a position undermined by information found in the passage; the second is evidence that the author uses to support the author’s position.
The first is a position that the author argues against; the second presents evidence that supports that position.
The first is an assumption upon which an argument that the author rejects depends; the second is the author’s main conclusion.

explanation_parts.text: Let’s find the argument’s main conclusion.
In this case, the main conclusion is short and inconspicuous.
Conclusion: these entomologists are mistaken.
We can tell that that statement is the argument’s main conclusion because it is the only statement in the passage that is supported by other statements.
(A) The first is a conclusion that is not the only conclusion present in the passage; the second is an example used to support the first.
The first part of this choice could be considered correct, because the first boldfaced portion is the position of “some entomologists” and, thus, could be considered a conclusion.

Nevertheless, we can confidently eliminate this choice because the second boldfaced portion is not an example used to support the first boldfaced portion.Quite the contrary, the second boldfaced portion is a reason to doubt the first.
(B) The first and second are both used by the author to support the author’s main conclusion.
While the second boldfaced portion is used by the author to support the author’s main conclusion, the first boldfaced portion is a position that the author argues against.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The first is a position undermined by information found in the passage; the second is evidence that the author uses to support the author’s position.
We can see that the first part of this choice is correct, because the argument proceeds by making statements that undermine the position stated by the first boldfaced portion.

Also, we can see that, by providing a reason to doubt the position stated by the first boldfaced portion, the second boldfaced portion supports the author’s conclusion, which, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, is “these entomologists are mistaken.”
(D) The first is a position that the author argues against; the second presents evidence that supports that position.

The first part of this choice is correct, because the first boldfaced portion is a position that the author argues against in concluding “these entomologists are mistaken,” and then supporting that conclusion.

However, we can confidently eliminate this choice because the second boldfaced portion does not present evidence that supports the first boldfaced portion.Quite the contrary, the second boldfaced portion presents a reason to doubt the first.
(E) The first is an assumption upon which an argument that the author rejects depends; the second is the author’s main conclusion.

The first part of this choice tries to confuse us by saying something that resembles what’s correct.What’s correct is that the first boldfaced portion is a position that the author rejects.Of course, since a position and an assumption upon which an argument for a position depends are not the same thing, what this choice says is incorrect.

Similarly, the second part of this choice tries to confuse us by saying something that resembles what’s correct.This choice says that the second boldfaced portionisthe author’s main conclusion, whereas what’s correct is that the second boldfaced portionis support forthe author’s main conclusion.

question.text: Mango Farmer: Although, over the last five years, our organic mango farms have experienced a sharp increase in consumer demand for our mangos,we’ve earned substantially lower profits per year than we earned during each year in the preceding five-year period.Despite those low profit figures, the outlook for our mango farms is as strong as ever.Our revenues are higher than they have ever been, the quality of our mangos is superior to the quality of the mangos grown on competing farms, and, because we have made a significant investment in advanced robot-assisted machinery,our farms can be expected to see over the coming decade a significant reduction in operating expenses allowing for increased profitability.In the mango farmer’s argument, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first presents a conclusion that the argument rejects; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is the argument’s main conclusion; the second is a premise used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is an observation for which an explanation is provided; the second is the main conclusion but not the only conclusion.
The first is a conclusion drawn to support the main conclusion of the argument; the second is that main conclusion.
The first is a fact that the author uses to provide context; the second is an intermediate conclusion used to support the author’s position.

explanation_parts.text: The passage contains multiple conclusions and support for those conclusions.To get this question correct, let’s get them straight.
We’ll start with first conclusion that appears in the passage:
Despite those low profit figures, the outlook for our mango farms is as strong as ever.
That conclusion is supported by all the statements that follow it.
The other conclusion that appears in the passage is the following:
our farms can be expected to see over the coming decade a significant reduction in operating expenses allowing for increased profitability.
That conclusion is supported by the statement that precedes it:
we have made a significant investment in advanced robot-assisted machinery
Since the second conclusion supports the first conclusion, the second conclusion is an intermediate conclusion, and the first conclusion is the main conclusion.
(A) The first presents a conclusion that the argument rejects; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.

To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to notice the following.The argument does not “reject” the first boldfaced portion.Rather, the argument states a conclusion that is true “despite” what the first boldface portion says.In other words, the argument acknowledges that what the first boldface portion says is true rather than “rejects” it as untrue.
Also, we have to notice that, while the second boldface portion is a conclusion, it is not the main conclusion of the argument.It is an intermediate conclusion that supports the main conclusion.
(B) The first is the argument’s main conclusion; the second is a premise used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The second part of this choice is correct, because the second boldfaced portion is a premise, in the form of an intermediate conclusion, used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
However, we can eliminate this choice because the first boldfaced portion is not a conclusion.It is a statement of fact that is not supported by any other statement in the passage.
(C) The first is an observation for which an explanation is provided; the second is the main conclusion but not the only conclusion.
The first boldfaced portion is an observation, but the passage provides no explanation for that observation.
Also, the second boldfaced portion is a conclusion, but it is not the main conclusion.It is an intermediate conclusion that supports the main conclusion.
(D) The first is a conclusion drawn to support the main conclusion of the argument; the second is that main conclusion.
The first boldfaced portion is not a conclusion.It is a statement of fact that is not supported by any other statement in the passage.

Furthermore, the first boldfaced portion does not support the main conclusion of the argument.Notice that the passage says that main conclusion is true “despite” what the first boldfaced portion says.
Also, the second boldfaced portion is not the main conclusion.It is an intermediate conclusion that supports the main conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The first is a fact that the author uses to provide context; the second is an intermediate conclusion used to support the author’s position.
As this choice says, the first boldfaced portion is a fact that the author uses to provide context.

Also, the second boldfaced portion is a conclusion supported by “we have made a significant investment in advanced robot-assisted machinery,” and the second boldfaced portion supports the argument’s main conclusion.So, as this choice says, the second boldfaced portion is an intermediate conclusion used to support the author’s position.

question.text: The newest wind turbines are taller than their predecessors and utilize much larger blades, features that make them more efficient in converting wind energy to electricity, yet this improved technology has had limited impact on energy generation in the field.Tall turbines with large blades can intrude into airspace, an issue making securing permits for such turbines difficult, and make considerably more noise than is made by smaller ones, an issue resulting in pushback to having large blade turbines near communities.However, it’s important for ecological reasons that this new technology make its way from the engineering table to the field.The author proceeds by

answers.text: outlining an engineering design challenge and elaborating on the reasons why that challenge exists.
describing a problem and then providing a plausible solution to that problem.
outlining a set of issues and explaining why those issues are not significant.
relating why potential benefits of developments in an industry sector have yet to materialize.
describing technological innovations and then establishing that wide adoption of these innovations is unlikely to ever occur.

explanation_parts.text: (A) outlining an engineering design challenge and elaborating on the reasons why that challenge exists.

What the author says about the issues associated with large wind turbines could be considered a challenge.However, those issues do not constitute an “engineering design challenge.” The issues make it challenging to adoptalready designedtechnology.
(B) describing a problem and then providing a plausible solution to that problem.

This choice starts off somewhat correctly, because the author does describe a problem, the problem that, because of issues associated with large turbines, “this improved technology has had limited impact on energy generation in the field.”
However, this choice is incorrect because the author does not provide any solution to that problem.
(C) outlining a set of issues and explaining why those issues are not significant.

This choice is very tempting, because the author does outline a set of issues with using large wind turbines, “Tall turbines with large blades can intrude into airspace, an issue making securing permits for such turbines difficult, and make considerably more noise than is made by smaller ones, an issue resulting in pushback to having large blade turbines near communities.”
However, this choice is not correct because the author does not explain why those issues are not significant.

Notice that, what the author says at the end of the passage, “it’s important for ecological reasons that this new technology make its way from the engineering table to the field,” does not explain why the issues are “not significant.” Rather it explains something else, such as why, even though the issuesaresignificant, they have to be addressed or overcome.
YOUR CHOICE(D) relating why potential benefits of developments in an industry sector have yet to materialize.
The author mentions developments in an industry sector, features of “the newest wind turbines.”
The author also indicates that these features have potential benefits, since these features make wind turbines “more efficient in converting wind energy to electricity.”

Then, the author indicates that these benefits have yet to materialize, i.e., “this improved technology has had limited impact on energy generation in the field,” and explains why these benefits have yet to materialize, “Tall turbines with large blades can intrude into airspace, an issue making securing permits for such turbines difficult, and make considerably more noise than is made by smaller ones, an issue resulting in pushback to having large blade turbines near communities.”
So, this choice matches exactly what the author does.
(E) describing technological innovations and then establishing that wide adoption of these innovations is unlikely to ever occur.
This choice starts off matching what the author does, as the author does describe innovations in wind turbine technology.

Notice, however, that the author does not establish that wide adoption of large wind turbines is “unlikely to occur.” Rather, the author explains why wide adoption of large wind turbines has yet to occur and then indicates that it’s important for ecological reasons that wide adoption does occur, leaving open the possibility that wide adoption will eventually occur.

question.text: Currently, approximately 70 percent of American adults are considered overweight.This statistic is amazing since never before have so many people spent time, energy, and money attempting to reduce body fat by consuming less food or fewer calories.However, if lasting weight loss were as simple as skipping meals, more people dieting would be lean.The truth is that diet-driven undereating is causing many people to remain overweight.Caloric and nutrient deprivation triggers people’s metabolisms into shutting down biochemical pathways involved in burning body fat, making weight loss challenging.The reasoning of the passage above proceeds by

answers.text: making the case that an approach should be rejected because there is no evidence supporting its use.
weakening the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion by bringing up an alternative reason for the observed pattern.
explaining key differences between what should be done versus what is actually being done by a large group of people attempting to achieve a goal.
undermining the force of the evidence supporting a common belief by providing a reasonable alternative interpretation of the same information.
explaining how the existence of certain countervailing factors results in unexpected outcomes.

explanation_parts.text: (A) making the case that an approach should be rejected because there is no evidence supporting its use.

This choice starts off matching the reasoning of the passage because the passage does make a case for rejecting the approach of consuming less food or fewer calories for the purpose of reducing body fat.

However, the passage does not say that the approach of consuming less food or fewer calories should be rejected simply “because there is no evidence supporting its use.” Rather, the passage supports the case for rejecting that approach by providinga reason not to usethat approach: “Caloric and nutrient deprivation triggers people’s metabolisms into shutting down biochemical pathways involved in burning body fat, making weight loss challenging.”
(B) weakening the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion by bringing up an alternative reason for the observed pattern.
The passage could be construed as making a case against the cause-and-effect conclusion that consuming less food or fewer calories causes weight loss.

However, even if we agree that the passage weakens the support for the cause-and-effect conclusion that consuming less food or fewer calories causes weight loss, this choice isn’t correct, because the passage does not weaken the support for that conclusion by providing an alternative reason for any pattern.The passage weakens the support for that conclusion by providing the facts that “70 percent of American adults are considered overweight,” that “never before have so many people spent time, energy, and money attempting to reduce body fat by consuming less food or fewer calories,” and that “caloric and nutrient deprivation triggers people’s metabolisms into shutting down biochemical pathways involved in burning body fat, making weight loss challenging.”
(C) explaining key differences between what should be done versus what is actually being done by a large group of people attempting to achieve a goal.

Notice that the passage does not say “what should be done” instead of “attempting to reduce body fat by consuming less food or fewer calories.” The passage simply explains why that approach does not work without saying what should be done instead and without “explaining key differences between what should be done versus what is actually being done.”
(D) undermining the force of the evidence supporting a common belief by providing a reasonable alternative interpretation of the same information.

While the passage does present a case against the common belief that one can reduce body fat by simply consuming less food or fewer calories, the passage does not present any evidence that supports that belief.Thus, of course, the passage does not provide an alternative interpretation of any such evidence either.
YOUR CHOICE(E) explaining how the existence of certain countervailing factors results in unexpected outcomes.

The passage indicates that “many people” have “spent time, energy, and money attempting to reduce body fat by consuming less food or fewer calories.” So, it’s reasonable to presume that, in not becoming lean, these “dieting people” are experiencing unexpected outcomes.
Thus, we can see that, in explaining why these people are not losing weight, the passage is doing what this choice says.

The passage is explaining how the existence of certain countervailing factors, “caloric and nutrient deprivation triggers people’s metabolisms into shutting down biochemical pathways involved in burning body fat,” results in unexpected outcomes.

question.text: It is well known that many organisms use magnetic fields for navigation.Now, a study has generated results involving some animals’ seeming to consider magnetic fields in determining in which direction to excrete bodily waste.Researchers observed 70 dogs over a two-year period and found that the dogs almost always excreted waste in alignment with north-south magnetic field lines, and almost never in alignment with east-west magnetic field lines.Evidence of important interaction between magnetic fields and living organisms now exists in a quantity sufficient that biologists should begin to probe more deeply the implications of such interaction.For example, how might a solar storm causing temporary yet significant alterations in Earth’s magnetic field lines affect the behavior of organisms?In the argument given, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second is an intermediary conclusion used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is a fact used to provide context; the second is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
The first is a claim for which support is provided; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
The first is general claim with which the argument agrees; the second is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
The first is a fact that supports the main conclusion of the argument; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.

explanation_parts.text: First, let’s find the argument’s main conclusion.
To find the main conclusion, we have to find a statement that is supported by at least one other statement in the passage.

One statement that may be supported by another statement in the passage is “Now, a study has generated results involving some animals’ seeming to consider magnetic fields in determining in which direction to excrete bodily waste.” That statement may be supported by the statement that follows it, “Researchers observed 70 dogs over a two-year period and found that ….”

However, upon further consideration, we can see that that statement is not really a conclusion supported by the statement that follows it.It’s more a statement of fact about the study results.Furthermore, that statement provides part of the support for another statement further along in the passage.

That other statement is“Evidence of important interaction between magnetic fields and living organisms now exists in a quantity sufficient that biologists should begin to probe more deeply the implications of such interaction.”In fact, that statement is supported by all the statements that precede it.

Since that statement, the one in boldface, is a conclusion supported by all the statements that precede it, and since the statement “For example, how might a solar storm …” provides an example and is clearly not a conclusion, that statement in boldface must be the main conclusion of the argument.
(A) The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second is an intermediary conclusion used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first boldfaced portion is not the main conclusion.It is a statement of fact not supported by any other statement in the passage.
Also, the second boldfaced portion does not support the first; it is supported by the first.
(B) The first is a fact used to provide context; the second is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
While the first boldfaced portion is a statement of fact, it does not simply provide context.It provides support for the main conclusion, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation.

Also, we have to be careful to notice that the second part of this choice is incorrect because the second boldfaced portion is not evidence that supports the main conclusion.It is the main conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The first is a claim for which support is provided; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
The second part of this choice is correct, because the second boldfaced portion is the main conclusion of the argument.
All the same, we can eliminate this choice because no support is provided for the first boldfaced portion.
(D) The first is general claim with which the argument agrees; the second is evidence that the argument uses to support the main conclusion.
The first part of this choice is a little off because, while the first portion is a general claim, the argument does not agree with that claim.Rather, the argument depends on that claim for support.
Also, the second part of this choice is incorrect because the second boldfaced portion is not evidence used to support the main conclusion.It is the main conclusion.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) The first is a fact that supports the main conclusion of the argument; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion supports the main conclusion of the argument, and the second is the main conclusion of the argument.

question.text: Conservationist: Four hippos were transported from Africa to a farm in Colombia in 1980.Although not native to the area, since escaping the farm, those four have proliferated to a herd of more than 80 hippos.Some ecologists claim that, sincethe herd’s fecal waste is fertilizing harmful algae, leading to blooms that can cause illness in native species,the herd is wreaking havoc on the local aquatic ecosystem.However, these hippos might actually be a less than perfect feature of, not a flaw in, the Colombian ecosystem, because they might be filling a niche left abandoned by the loss of native species that humans pushed to extinction thousands of years ago.We’re seeing these hippos increase vitality of lakes, reduce invasive grasses, and keep fish populations in check.In the conservationist’s argument, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is a claim that the argument disputes; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument rejects; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
The first is a cause-and-effect conclusion that the argument challenges; the second is evidence that serves to call into question validity of the first.
The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument considers; the second is evidence used to support the main conclusion of the argument.
The first is a finding whose accuracy is evaluated in the argument; the second is evidence presented to establish that that finding is not accurate.

explanation_parts.text: Let’s first identify the main conclusion of the argument.
The first conclusion that we see as we read the passage is what some ecologists claim:
the herd is wreaking havoc on the local aquatic ecosystem.
That claim is supported by the following premise:
the herd’s fecal waste is fertilizing harmful algae, leading to blooms that can cause illness in native species
Further along in the passage, we see a second conclusion:
these hippos might actually be a less than perfect feature of, not a flaw in, the Colombian ecosystem
That conclusion is supported by all the statements that follow it.

Since this second conclusion is the author’s conclusion, while the ecologists’ claim is a claim that the author mentions and contends might be incorrect, this second conclusion must be the main conclusion of the argument.
(A) The first is a claim that the argument disputes; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.

We have to be careful to notice that the first part of this choice is incorrect because the first boldfaced portion is not a claim that the argument disputes; it’ssupportfor a claim that the argument suggests might be incorrect.
Also, the second boldfaced portion is not the main conclusion of the argument.It’s support for the main conclusion.
(B) The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument rejects; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.

The first part of this choice is close to correct, because the first boldfaced portion is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument suggests might be incorrect.However, the first part is a little off, because the argument does not outright reject that conclusion but, rather, suggests that it might be incorrect.
Also, the second boldfaced portion is not the main conclusion of the argument.It’s support for the main conclusion.
(C) The first is a cause-and-effect conclusion that the argument challenges; the second is evidence that serves to call into question validity of the first.

We have to be careful to notice that the first part of this choice is incorrect because the first boldfaced portion is not a conclusion that the argument challenges; it’ssupportfor a conclusion that the argument suggests might be incorrect.

Also, the second part of this choice is incorrect, because both the first boldfaced portion and the second boldfaced portion state facts.So, the second boldfaced portion both does not and cannot serve to call into question the validity of the first.After all, the validity of a stated fact would not be called into question in a GMAT Critical Reasoning argument.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The first is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument considers; the second is evidence used to support the main conclusion of the argument.

Notice that, in saying, “these hipposmightactually be …,” the author is not stating a firm conclusion.Rather, the author is saying that something “might” be the case.So, in saying “these hipposmightactually be …,” the author is considering the alternative possibility that the hipposmight not be“a less than perfect feature of, not a flaw in, the Colombian ecosystem.”
In other words, the author is considering the possibility that the ecologists’ claim that the herd is wreaking havoc on the local aquatic ecosystem is correct.
Thus, the first part of this choice is correct, because the first boldfaced portion is evidence that has been used to support a conclusion that the argument considers, i.e., the ecologists’ claim.
Also, the second boldfaced portion is evidence used to support the main conclusion of the argument.
(E) The first is a finding whose accuracy is evaluated in the argument; the second is evidence presented to establish that that finding is not accurate.

We have to be careful to notice that the first part of this choice is incorrect because the first boldfaced portion is not a finding whose accuracy is evaluated in the argument; it’s a fact that the argument allows is valid support for a conclusion that the argument suggests might be incorrect.
Then, since the first part of this choice is incorrect, the second part of this choice does not make sense.

question.text: Many Cosmologists believe that, given the age and size of the universe, intelligent life almost certainly exists beyond planet Earth.However, in decades of searching, these same scientists have been unable to find strong evidence of such extraterrestrial life.One prominent theory explaining why, the Zoo Theory, posits that other forms of intelligent life are here on Earth and observing humans, just as we might observe animals in a zoo, but these lifeforms prefer to remain hidden from our awareness.Such a theory seems implausible until one considers that, in a universe as vast and as old as ours, anything is possible.The author proceeds by

answers.text: outlining a controversial theory and then providing evidence to support that theory over others.
showing that a scientific theory, if generally applied, would have wide-reaching consequences.
questioning the plausibility of a theory and then demonstrating with examples how that theory could be correct.
describing a hypothesis and then concluding that that hypothesis might be accurate.
using an analogy with a known phenomenon to support a conclusion about a theoretical phenomenon.

explanation_parts.text: (A) outlining a controversial theory and then providing evidence to support that theory over others.
This choice starts off seeming to match what the passage does, because, in outlining the Zoo Theory, the passage does seem to be “outlining a controversial theory.”
Notice, however, that, while the Zoo Theory may seem debatable or, at least, a bit strange or surprising, that it is “controversial” is not clear.

Furthermore, and more importantly, notice that the passage does not support the Zoo Theory “over” other theories.After all, the statement “anything is possible,” which is the only support of any kind the passage provides for the Zoo Theory, supports “anything,” such as any theory, not just the Zoo Theory.
(B) showing that a scientific theory, if generally applied, would have wide-reaching consequences.
This choice does not match at all what the passage does, because the passage does not show anything about consequences of the application of the Zoo Theory or any other theory.
(C) questioning the plausibility of a theory and then demonstrating with examples how that theory could be correct.

This choice to a degree resembles what the passage does, since the passage mentions the Zoo Theory, uses the wording “seems implausible,” and then provide a reason to believe that the Zoo Theory “could be correct.”

Notice, however, that the passage does not exactly “question” the Zoo Theory.It merely says that such a theoryseems implausibleuntilone considers that, in a universe as vast and as old as ours, anything is possible.
Furthermore, notice that the passage does not demonstrate anything via the use of examples.

It is so easy to be tempted into choosing a choice like this one that resembles what the passage does.At the same time, if we look carefully, we can clearly see that the passage simply does not use examples.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) describing a hypothesis and then concluding that that hypothesis might be accurate.

This choice matches what the passage does.The passage describes the Zoo Theory, which is a hypothesis, and then, in saying “anything is possible,” essentially concludes that the Zoo Theory is possibly correct, i.e., “may be accurate.”
YOUR CHOICE(E) using an analogy with a known phenomenon to support a conclusion about a theoretical phenomenon.

This choice is tempting because it starts off matching what the passage does, since the passage does use an analogy between what “other intelligent forms of life” are doing on Earth according to the Zoo Theory and what people do in a zoo.
However, this choice is not correct because this analogy is not used to support a conclusion.Rather this analogy is used for the purpose of explaining Zoo Theory.

question.text: Some people have made the claim that reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely not the cause of the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.Those making that claim take the fact that reading scores have been increasing nationwide as an indication that some cause present nationwide rather than reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely responsible for the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.They seem to have missed the fact that student-teacher ratios have been reduced nationwide.The author of the reasoning presented proceeds by

answers.text: outlining the basis for a claim made and then providing statistical information that conflicts with the information used to support that claim.
explaining the case for concluding that a certain change has not occurred and then providing information indicating that that change has indeed occurred.
outlining an argument and then providing information that undermines the force of the evidence used to support that argument’s conclusion.
presenting cases for supporting each of two opposing viewpoints and then demonstrating that there is no basis for one of the two viewpoints.
describing the reasoning leading to a conclusion and then providing additional evidence that supports that same conclusion.

explanation_parts.text: Answering this question correctly takes keeping straight what the passage says, which is the following.
Some people have made a claim:
Reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely not the cause of the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.
The support for that claim is the following:
Reading scores have been increasing nationwide.

Why would the fact that that reading scores have been increasing nationwide support the claim? Because the fact that reading scores have been increasing nationwide could be taken as an indication of the following:
Some cause present nationwide rather than reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely responsible for the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.

So, we can see that the reason why people are claiming that reduction of student-teacher ratios in “our town” is not the cause of the increases in reading scores is that the fact that reading scores have increased nationwide seems to indicate that there is analternative explanationfor the increases in the reading scores of children in “our town.” In other words, they think some other cause, “some cause present nationwide,” is the cause of the increases in reading score of the children in “our town.”
The passage then says, “They seem to have missed the fact that student-teacher ratios have been reduced nationwide.”
Why would the fact that student-teacher ratios have been reduced nationwide matter?

Well, if student-teacher ratios have been reduced nationwide, then the “cause present nationwide” could very well be reduction of student-teacher ratios! Of course, in that case, the claim that reduction of student-teacher ratios in “our town’s” schools is likely not the cause of the increases in the reading scores of children in “our town” is not supported by the fact that reading scores have been increasing nationwide.After all, in that case, the factor present nationwide and reduction of student teacher ratios are one and the same.

So, we can see that what the author does is (1) present a claim, (2) present the premise used to support that claim, and (3) present information that completely undermines the support provided by the premise.
Now, let’s find an answer choice that matches what the author does.
(A) outlining the basis for a claim made and then providing statistical information that conflicts with the information used to support that claim.

This choice starts off matching what the author does, because the author does outline the basis for the claim that reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely not the cause of the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.

However, this choice is incorrect because the author does not provide statistical information that conflicts with the information used to support that claim.After all, the information that the author presents is the fact that student-teacher ratios have been reduced nationwide, and that information does not “conflict with” the basis for the claim, which is that reading scores have been increasing nationwide.
(B) explaining the case for concluding that a certain change has not occurred and then providing information indicating that that change has indeed occurred.
To avoid being fooled into choosing this trap choice, we have to notice the key difference between what the passage says and what this choice says.

The author of the passage explains the case for the claim that reduction in student-teacher ratios is not the cause of increases in reading scores.Notice that the claim is not that a change “has not occurred.” Those making the claim are not arguing that the reduction in student-teacher ratios or the increases in reading scores have not occurred.They are arguing that the first did not cause the second.

So, in saying that the author proceeds by “explaining the case for concluding that a certain change has not occurred and then providing information indicating that that change has indeed occurred,” this choice completely misses what the author does.
YOUR CHOICE(C) outlining an argument and then providing information that undermines the force of the evidence used to support that argument’s conclusion.
As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the author does outline an argument, which includes the “claim” that some people have made and the support for that claim.

Then, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the author presents information that undermines the force of the evidence supporting the claim, which information is that student-teacher ratios have been reduced nationwide.
So, this choice matches exactly what the author does.
(D) presenting cases for supporting each of two opposing viewpoints and then demonstrating that there is no basis for one of the two viewpoints.

Notice that the author does not present the cases for two opposing viewpoints.The author presents only the case for the claim that “reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely not the cause of the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.”

Also, the author does not demonstrate that there is “no basis” for that claim.Rather the author presents the basis for that claim, the fact that “reading scores have been increasing nationwide,” though the author also presents information that serves to call the claim into question.
(E) describing the reasoning leading to a conclusion and then providing additional evidence that supports that same conclusion.

This choice starts off correctly, because the author does describe the reasoning leading to the claim that “reduction of student-teacher ratios in our town’s schools is likely not the cause of the increases in the reading scores of children in our town.”

However, this choice is not correct, because the author does not provide additional evidence that supports that claim but, rather, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, provides information that undermines the force of the evidence used to support that claim.

question.text: Legislator: Tests of the water in the river show that concentrations of industrial contaminants in the water are much lower than they were six months ago.Apparently, the industrial regulations enacted six months ago are working.Ecologist: Currently we are in the rainy season, when the volume of water flowing through the river is much higher than it was six months ago.So, even if the regulations were having no effect, the concentrations of industrial contaminants would be lower than they were then.The logic of the reasoning supporting the ecologist’s conclusion is most similar to that of which of the following?

answers.text: Recent improvements in the air quality of a city may be simply the result of higher than normal winds distributing airborne particles over a greater area.
Testing of the water in a lake reveals a decrease in levels of dissolved oxygen, but the decrease could be simply the result of a cyclical increase in algae growth.
A decrease in traffic congestion on a highway may have resulted not from people shifting to using a new train but from their remaining home because of rainy weather.
The percentage of athletes caught using banned substances may have decreased only because athletes are now using substances effective at very low body fluid concentrations.
A decrease in rain acidity may not indicate that factories are emitting less sulfur, since volcanic activity, which also acidifies rain, has decreased significantly over the past several months.

explanation_parts.text: There are two factors that determine the concentrations of contaminants in water:
the quantities of contaminantsthe quantity of water
So, there are two ways in which concentrations of contaminants in water can decrease:
The concentrations decrease if the quantities of contaminants present decrease.The concentrations decrease also if the quantity of water present increases.

So, in the scenario presented in this question, the concentrations of contaminants in the water could have decreased because the quantities of contaminants put into the river on an ongoing basis decreased or because the quantity of water flowing through the river increased.

The legislator, seeing that the concentrations of contaminants in the river water have decreased, concludes that the regulations are working.In other words, the legislator concludes that the quantities of contaminants put into the river on an ongoing basis have decreased.

The ecologist responds by suggesting that the concentrations of contaminants may have decreased for the other possible reason, the volume of water present has increased.In other words, the ecologist suggests that the concentrations of contaminants have decreased because the amount of water flowing through the river has increased.

So, the ecologist’s argument is essentially that it could be that the regulations are not working and that, instead, what is going on is that the same quantities of contaminants are present, but the concentrations are lower because those contaminants are distributed through a greater volume of water.
The logic of the correct answer will somehow resemble the logic involved in the ecologist’s response.
YOUR CHOICE(A) Recent improvements in the air quality of a city may be simply the result of higher than normal winds distributing airborne particles over a greater area.
The logic of this choice is similar to the logic of the ecologist’s response.

The ecologist’s point is that the concentrations of contaminants may have decreased not because the quantities of contaminants present have decreased but becausethe same quantities of contaminantsare distributed througha greater volume of water.

Similarly, this choice indicates that improvements in air quality may have resulted not from a decrease in the quantity of airborne particles but from high winds’ having distributedthe same quantity of airborne particlesthrougha greater volume of air.
(B) Testing of the water in a lake reveals a decrease in levels of dissolved oxygen, but the decrease could be simply the result of a cyclical increase in algae growth.
This choice tempts us by mentioning a “cyclical increase,” which is similar to what the ecologist says about “the rainy season.”
However, the logic of what’s described by this choice differs from the logic involved in the ecologist’s response.

Whereas the ecologist indicates that the decrease in levels of contaminants may have occurred without a decrease in the total quantity of contaminants, in the scenario described by this choice, the total quantity of oxygen seems to have decreased because of algae growth.
Furthermore, unlike the ecologist’s response, what this choice says does not involve an increase in the volume of water present.
(C) A decrease in traffic congestion on a highway may have resulted not from people shifting to using a new train but from their remaining home because of rainy weather.
This choice tempts us by mentioning “rainy weather,” which is similar to what the ecologist says about “the rainy season.”
However, the logic of what’s described by this choice differs from the logic involved in the ecologist’s response.

The ecologist’s point is that rain may have caused a decrease in concentrations of contaminants by causing the volume of water in the river to increase whilethe quantities of contaminants in the waterremained the same.

On the other hand, in the scenario described by this choice, rain may have caused a decrease in congestion, in other words, a decrease in the concentration of cars on the highway, by causingthe number of cars on the highway todecrease.
(D) The percentage of athletes caught using banned substances may have decreased only because athletes are now using substances effective at very low body fluid concentrations.
This choice tempts us by mentioning “body fluid concentrations,” which is similar to what the passage says about “concentrations of industrial contaminants.”
However, the logic of what’s described by this choice differs from the logic involved in the ecologist’s response.

The ecologist’s point is that rain may have caused a decrease in concentrations of contaminants by causing the volume of water in the river to increase whilethe quantities of contaminants in the waterremained the same.

On the other hand, what this choice says implies that the reason why the concentrations of banned substances in athletes’ body fluids have decreased is thatthe quantities of such substances used by athletes have decreased.
(E) A decrease in rain acidity may not indicate that factories are emitting less sulfur, since volcanic activity, which also acidifies rain, has decreased significantly over the past several months.

This choice tempts us by mentioning “rain” and “factories,” which are similar to what the passage says about “the rainy season” and “industrial contaminants.” Also, the decrease in volcanic activity “over the past several months” mentioned by this choice could be perceived as similar to “the rainy season” mentioned in the passage.
However, the logic of what’s described by this choice differs from the logic involved in the ecologist’s response.

The ecologist’s point is that rain may have caused a decrease in concentrations of contaminants by causing the volume of water in the river to increase whilethe quantities of contaminants in the waterremained the same.

On the other hand, what this choice says implies that the reason for the decrease in rain acidity is thatquantities of substances causing rain to be acidic have in fact decreased, even though factories may not be emitting less sulfur.

question.text: Every human action produces both positive and negative consequences.However,impact investors, those who invest in companies expected to generate beneficial social or environmental impacts, in addition to financial returns, often fail to consider negative effects, focusing only on the positive.For example, impact investors may be happy to provide capital to an electric airplane company, failing to take into account the fact that, in producing electric airplanes, the company generates copious amounts of greenhouse gases that have negative effects on the environment.Impact investors must begin to examine the net impact, the sum of the positives and negatives, of a company’s actions, not just the positive ones, because, in failing to consider negative effects, impact investors may make a show of investing for beneficial impacts without actually achieving that aim.In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is a judgment advanced in support of a position that the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s conclusion.
The first is evidence used to support a conclusion opposed by the argument; the second is an intermediate conclusion drawn to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is factual information from which support for the argument is developed; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is evidence used to support an intermediate conclusion of the argument; the second is that intermediate conclusion.
The first is evidence used to support the argument’s case against a position; the second is evidence used to support the argument’s conclusion.

explanation_parts.text: First, let’s find the author’s main conclusion.
To find the main conclusion, we have to find a statement that is supported by at least one other statement in the passage.

One statement that may be supported by another statement in the passage is“impact investors, those who invest in companies expected to generate beneficial social or environmental impacts, in addition to financial returns, often fail to consider negative effects, focusing only on the positive.”That statement may be supported by the statement that follows it, “For example, impact investors may be happy to ….”

However, upon further consideration, we can see that that statement is not really a conclusion supported by the statement that follows it.It’s a statement of fact about impact investors, and the statement that follows it does not provide support; it provides an example.

In fact, the only statement that is supported by other statements in the passage is“Impact investors must begin to examine the net impact, the sum of the positives and negatives, of a company’s actions, not just the positive ones.”That statement is clearly supported by the statement that follows it, “in failing to consider negative effects, impact investors may make a show of investing for beneficial impacts without actually achieving that aim.”

Also, notice that the first statement in boldface and the sentence that immediately follows the first statement in boldface contribute to the support for“Impact investors must begin to examine the net impact, the sum of the positives and negatives, of a company’s actions, not just the positive ones.”
So, the main conclusion is“Impact investors must begin to examine the net impact, the sum of the positives and negatives, of a company’s actions, not just the positive ones.”
(A) The first is a judgment advanced in support of a position that the argument opposes; the second is the argument’s conclusion.
The second part of this choice is correct, because the second boldfaced portion is the argument’s conclusion.

However, we can eliminate this choice, because the first boldfaced portion is not advanced in support of a position that the argument opposes.It contributes to the support for the argument’s conclusion.
(B) The first is evidence used to support a conclusion opposed by the argument; the second is an intermediate conclusion drawn to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first boldfaced portion is not used to support a conclusion opposed by the argument.The first boldfaced portion contributes to the support for the argument’s conclusion.
Also, the second boldfaced portion is not an intermediate conclusion.Rather, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, it is the argument’s final conclusion.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The first is factual information from which support for the argument is developed; the second is the argument’s main conclusion.

As discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the first boldfaced portion is factual information that contributes to the support for the main conclusion, i.e., is factual information from which support for the conclusion is developed.
Also, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldfaced portion is the main conclusion.
(D) The first is evidence used to support an intermediate conclusion of the argument; the second is that intermediate conclusion.
This choice is tricky, because the first boldfaced portion does support the second boldfaced portion.

All the same, this choice is incorrect, because the second boldfaced portion is not an intermediate conclusion.Rather, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, it is the argument’s final conclusion.
(E) The first is evidence used to support the argument’s case against a position; the second is evidence used to support the argument’s conclusion.
Since the argument does not involve a “case against a position,” the first part of this choice does not make sense.
Also, as discussed in the introduction to this explanation, the second boldface portion is not evidence used to support the argument’s conclusion; it is the argument’s conclusion.

question.text: The generation of power from nuclear fusion energy, the energy released when atomic nuclei fuse together, currently requires extremely high temperatures and pressures, conditions that maximize the probability of these isotopes’ coming in close enough proximity to one another to create a chain reaction.However,soon, we may no longer need such extreme temperature and pressure conditions to generate power from nuclear fusion energy.Scientists are working on triggering a nuclear fusion reaction using boron-11, an isotope of boron, and high energy lasers that are used to initiate the chain reaction necessary for fusion occur, at room temperature at sea level.In the argument above, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles?

answers.text: The first is a position that the argument acknowledges but disagrees with; the second is evidence used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first is an assertion for which the argument provides no support; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.
The first is a fact that the argument uses to provide context; the second is evidence used to support the argument’s main conclusion.
The first and second are opposing viewpoints that the argument considers in arriving at its final conclusion.
The first is a claim that the argument calls into question; the second is the main conclusion of the argument.

explanation_parts.text: Let’s start by finding the main conclusion.

The main conclusion must be supported by at least one other statement in the passage, and we can see that only one statement in the passage is supported by another statement.That one statement is the following:
soon, we may no longer need such extreme temperature and pressure conditions to generate power from nuclear fusion energy
That statement is supported by the following statement:
